categories in favor of arbitrary personal opinions without abolishing itself. In
order to be able to exist, it needs not only moral consensus but also a certain
minimum of logical unanimity, the limits of which are not to be crossed
arbitrarily.3
All of this is unquestionably true, but the real issue is whether these
categories are also biological facts, as has previously been believed, or purely
social ones, as the neopositivists claim, or—which is our contention—
whether we are dealing with facts of biosocial nature. The question thus
posed is in reality far more complicated than it may initially appear. In any
case, it requires a comprehensive discussion and cannot be resolved in so
many words. That is why we prefer to keep it out of this discourse altogether,
particularly since it is outside the limits of our present task. We can only
point out that individual experience must have definitely played a role in the
evolution of the above categories. Apparently, collective experience acquires
a special significance here, leading through socialization to the equalization
and softening of individual differences in the subsumed phenomena.
As for the evolution of the human personality, some believe that it created
society. There can be no doubt that a better-developed human personality
produces better social structures and that personality does, indeed, influence
society. But if we ask whether society is the result of personality
development, the answer can be only negative.
The first proof of this is the phenomenon of imitation. It is common
knowledge that personality development is to a great extent a product of
imitation, which plays an enormous role in upbringing as well as in all
aspects of social life in general. One can unequivocally state that without
imitation there would be no personality as a social form, inasmuch as the
main source of imitation is communication with one's own kind, among
whom mutual induction and suggestion are developed through cooperation.
Another proof is language, which owes its origins to imitation on the one
hand and to the instinctual need for communication on the other. Language
is, therefore, an undisputable product of socialization, since without
preliminary contact between individuals the emergence of human language is
inconceivable. On the other hand, we know that human thought evolved
mostly due to the word, and in such close and immediate association with it
that abstract thinking would seem impossible without the participation of
inner speech. And there is, indeed, evidence that savage prehistoric man was
to a significant extent ignorant of both thought and words, in place of which
there were only gestures, shrieks, exclamations and sound imitations.
Writing is another proof that the human personality evolves under the
influence of society. It allows the results of individual experience to be
widely circulated in the collective as well as to be recorded for the future and
preserved for posterity. It will be no exaggeration to state that writing
transformed man into an historical being.
The transition from pictorial (ideographic) to phonetic writing also marked
an enormous advance in the evolution of human personality, hardly less
significant than the transformation of gestures and exclamations into
articulate speech consisting of words. Indisputably, it was phonetic writing in
conjunction with oral speech that gave rise to the civilization of developed
nations, since it became possible for each generation of humanity to pass on
the totality of its achievement to all the generations to come.
Is, however, the evolution of the human personality exclusively due to
society? Heredity and what we relegate to the manifestations of instincts
undoubtedly represent a biological phenomenon, but is it possible to conceive
of personality development unaffected by heredity and instincts? The initial
stage of personality evolution may have been determined by the role of the
hand, which provided the primal language of gestures (from which human
speech developed), just as the later stage was determined by verbal language.
However, the evolution of the hand was the result of biological causes rather
than the social conditions. The hand developed mostly due to the fact that,
through natural selection or constant exercise, human beings stood up on
their feet and transformed themselves from four-legged, or rather four-
handed, creatures into two-legged ones.
Also, articulate speech as such could evolve only when the animal snout
became the facial part of the head, which may have resulted partly from
changes in nutrition and partly from the more sophisticated preparation of
food, which are again biological rather than social factors. As for the family
—that primal model of social relations—is it not a result of sexual attraction?
Even the social aspect of human life may be a consequence of natural
selection in the sense of the better survival of socially oriented beings, and
natural selection is again a biological rather than a social factor. We also
know from reflexology that higher, or associative, reflexes develop on the
basis of common reflexes which are purely biological phenomena.
Furthermore, what we define as "inborn inclinations" are determined by
heredity, which is yet another purely biological factor. And finally,
extraordinary giftedness, or genius, is to a significant degree produced by
fortunate hereditary circumstances; education and erudition can only provide
this inborn gift with the means and motivation to apply natural abilities. One
can state with confidence that, no matter how substantial the social
preparation for a certain discovery or invention might be, it is beyond the
shadow of doubt that a poorly endowed person is not suited for making great
discoveries, just as a retarded child is incapable of complex mathematical
computations.
This leads us to the conclusion that the early evolution of personality was
affected both by biological and social factors, while at the later stages it was
influenced predominantly, though not exclusively, by social factors, which is
why personality should be recognized as a phenomenon of biosocial origins.
This important thesis has been established only in recent times. It was
proposed as early as 1860 by Courneau (A Treatise on the Interrelations
between Seminal Ideas in Science and History) and the German sociologist
Lazarus (The Life of the Soul); somewhat later this view found its way into
English scientific literature in Lewis's Problems of Life and Spirit, 1874, and
The Physiological Foundations of the Spirit, 1877. But it is our compatriot
Professor of the Psycho-neurological Institute in Petrograd, the well-known
sociolo gist Evgenii de Roberti, who has undoubtedly been the great
champion of this proposition. He called it the "biosocial hypothesis" and used
it as a foundation for his sociological research.
According to this hypothesis, or theory, the human mind is a product of the
close union between the organic nature studied by biology and the
supraorganic nature, or social environment, which is the subject matter of
sociology. For that reason, de Roberti regarded psychology as a concrete
science (when this theory was elaborated reflexology had not yet come into
existence), as biosociology based both on biology and sociology. Therefore, it
cannot be an abstract science, nor can it be recognized as a major science in
the scientific hierarchy. However, we should not go as far as do some
neopositivists, who claim that human individuality, i.e., personality, has no
other root but the social one. Nor can we agree with Worms (The Philosophy
of Social Sciences, 1903-1907), who does not see a satisfactory resolution of
this problem and relegates its solution to the future. According to him, "No
one has proved these issues insoluble and no one can claim that advances in
knowledge will not shed a bright light on them with time" (vol. Ill, p. 95).
From our point of view, associative reflex theory also permits us to
establish that personality is not after all the exclusive product of social
interaction. In order to protect oneself from an attack by a wild animal it was
enough to form an associative reflex between the animal's roar and
appearance on the one hand, and the possibility of its attack on the other. The
only escape was based on individual experience—the reflex of defense.
Neither of those owe their existence to social interaction, although interaction
does disseminate, through imitation, useful individual experience. In order to
satisfy his hunger with shellfish or bananas, a savage did not have to possess
previous collective experience: all that was needed was an associative reflex
—born of the frequent search for food necessitated by hunger—between the
outward appearance of the shell or the fruit and the satisfaction of hunger. In
this and similar cases we deal with individual experience that subsequently
becomes universal and by itself cultivates personality—the more so since the
latter is manifest among uncivilized peoples far more strongly than is
generally supposed. Besides the conditions that influence personality
formation through individual experience from the very first days of its
existence, there also emerge conditions for cultivating the human personality
via games, cooperation, etc., through collective experience in which imitation
plays a special role as a reflex of exclusively social variety.
One should also bear in mind that a collective can act and express itself as
a whole, as a composite personality, which will be discussed below and
which is, in fact, the main subject of the present work.
As we know, there have already been attempts at creating so-called folk
psychology as well as social, or collective, psychology (Steinthal, Lazarus,
Wundt, McDougall, Kopel'man, and others), but these attempts, being based
on the subjective interpretation of facts, have not met with success.
As has been mentioned earlier, the aim of the present work is to exclude
subjectivism altogether from the issues of collective actions and reactions, as
it has been excluded from my concept of reflexology as the objective science
of the human personality.
While not permitting subjective interpretation of issues concerning the
study of the evolution of social or collective phenomena, and understanding
all such phenomena as collective or social reflexes, we recognize the
necessity to apply to their study the same strictly objective method that we
use when studying the individual personality. For this reason, the title chosen
for this work is "Collective Reflexology" rather than the commonly used
social, or collective, psychology.
It is hardly necessary to elaborate here on the fact that tackling the issues
related to the evolution of social phenomena, or social, i.e., collective,
reflexes, demanded from the author a new approach to the subject and its new
interpretation, since what has until now been called social psychology could
not yield much useful material in this respect because of its subjective
method. One should also mention that although psychologists and subjectivist
sociologists (since sociology still remains largely a subjective science, which
should not be the case) could stretch their subjective method to philosophize
about the crowd as an entity with a "single soul," it was far more difficult to
apply the subjective method to all the other forms of the collective. That is
why the crowd became the favorite object of study among psychologists and
subjectivist sociologists. We shall expose in due course the one-sidedness
apparent in the works of a number of authors when they describe the crowd
from a subjective point of view. Here we would like to point out a more
serious mistake made by psychologists and subjectivist sociologists in their
studies of the masses and their movements: some of them try to explain the
latter by a single common principle.
Thus, Tarde, for instance, sees this common principle in imitation, whereas
W. McDougall finds it in the instincts of human nature.
The obvious deficiency of these views proceeds from the lack of strict
objectivity in research that tolerates preconceived notions.
Tarde—that talented sociologist—proceeded from a preconceived notion
of the significance of imitation in social life and exaggerated its role in his
"laws of imitation," paying hardly any attention to individual initiative and
social creativity. When he does mention them, he, like other authors,
considerably limits their importance and plays down their role in social life.
At the same time, when he inevitably comes across some phenomena of
social life that have nothing to do with imitation, he is forced by virtue of the
preconceived nature of his theory, to incorporate them into his theory by
using an ingenious term, "counter-imitation."
On the other hand, Professor W. McDougall (The Main Problems of Social
Psychology), while emphasizing the nature of instincts in individual life,
transposes the entire concept of sociopsychological phenomena: "If it is true,"
he says, "that human nature everywhere has always had the same natural
foundation, this point of view will serve as the much-needed basis for human
societies and institutions."
He then discusses the issue of instincts, unresolved by psychologists, some
of whom pay it too little attention, while others, for example, Schneider and
William James, maintain that humans have the same number of instincts as
animals and attribute to them a corresponding role in determining human
behavior. The latter view is supported by McDougall without sufficient
grounds. At the same time, he admits that instinct is "of hereditary and inborn
psychophysical predisposition, which enables its owner to perceive certain
objects, to pay attention to them, to experience particular sensory excitement
when perceiving such an object and to perform the corresponding special
actions or at least feel the urge to perform them." It is on these instincts that
the author builds his social psychology, by analyzing in sequence the role of
the instincts of procreation, parenting, belligerence, of the herd instinct, and
the instincts through which religious conceptions influence social life, the
acquisitive instinct and of the creative instinct; as for the other factors that
regu late interpersonal relations in social life, the author discusses imitation,
play, and habit.
It is clear that, in developing his social psychology, W. McDougall was
inevitably forced to stretch his concept of instincts out of all proportion.
Suffice it to say that in addition to the above instincts, the author
distinguishes, among the primary inclinations of the human soul that
influence social life, the instincts of escape, repulsion, curiosity, self-
effacement, self-confidence and a number of other instincts manifested as
fear, submissiveness, etc. It is obvious that the recognition of all instincts is,
in this case, derived from a preconceived social psychological theory of the
"instinctual foundation" of all social phenomena. In a word, this is a case of
the artificial construction of a particular system, since the author begins his
study with a preconceived idea born of the subjective method instead of
objectively analyzing phenomena in question and constructing his system on
the basis of the facts thus revealed.
The application of a strictly objective method to the study of social
phenomena avoids such strained interpretations that lead to wrong
conclusions. Therefore, the same strictly objective viewpoint should also be
applied to various manifestations of the collective, or composite, personality.
Only this point of view can extricate this important realm of knowledge from
its infant state.
As we shall see later, a strictly objective examination of the subject leads
inevitably to the conclusion that manifestations of the composite personality
revealed through the analysis of social life are ruled by the same law that is
revealed through a strictly objective reflexological study of manifestations of
the individual personality. The very forms of this law turn out to be the same
for both the individual personality and the composite one; this should
certainly be recognized as an advance in knowledge, since the theory of
opposition between the personality and the crowd accepted by many authors
who write about the crowd is greatly exaggerated.
As will be shown below, one encounters among the manifestations of
social life essentially the same reflexes, in the form of universal, eternal
movements following the same path and evolution, as one finds in individual
activity. This is understandable if we bear in mind that a collective is nothing
other than a group of individuals interconnected through their various
interests and representing a single entity in the form of a collective, or
composite, personality.
That is why the laws that govern the manifestation of collective activity are
the same as those that rule the expression of individual activity. It cannot be
otherwise, for a collective that represents a composite personality acts as a
united group of individuals.
It follows from the above that a reflexology of the individual personality
should shed light on collective reflexology just as the latter should shed light
on the former, since both sciences are closely interrelated.
In conclusion, I would like to mention that the present work was begun as
long ago as 1910-11 when I mapped out its basic concepts. The first chapters
appeared partially in 1911 in my work The Subject Matter and the Goals of
Social Psychology as an Objective Science. That is why the text of this work
with the appropriate corrections has been incorporated into the corresponding
parts of this book.
V. Bekhterev
Notes
1 E. de Roberti, New Ideas in Sociology (in Russian), 2nd Collection, p. 6.
2 V. Bekhterev, General Principles of Human Reflexology (in Russian), chapter 2.
3 E. Durkheim, "Sociology and Theory of Cognition" (in Russian), in New Ideas in Sociology, 2nd
Collection, 1914, p. 40.
1
Introduction
Social events are molded by human hands, so whatever the other determining
influences are, the human factor as the agent of social events should in no
instance be ignored.
Here, however, the agent is not a single person but the whole society, a
crowd, a gathering or a collective—which is not the same. Consequently,
individual psychology is not suitable for understanding social movements and
the evolution of social events, since an individual personality cannot
personify a whole society or nation. These are the grounds for the attempts to
create a special so-called "social psychology."
As early as the 1860s, Steinthal and Lazarus made such an attempt to
create "folk psychology." They proceeded from the premise that the national
spirit is distinct from the individual soul. In their view, the existence of folk
art provided evidence, as it were, in favor of the existence of a unique,
suprapersonal consciousness, or a composite national spirit. Therefore, the
differences between the cultures of various peoples could be attributed to the
peculiarities of their national, or folk, spirit.
But this attempt failed to strike a sympathetic chord, since one can speak
of supra-individual consciousness or national spirit only as a figurative
comparison and not as a reality.
The fact is that if we understand consciousness as the inner content of the
self—which is universally accepted—it is then impossible to divide human
consciousness into an individual and a public, or national, consciousness
when the latter is understood as something special, as a single supra-
individual consciousness, rather than a composite consciousness of the
individual persons who make up the population of a nation and its
representatives. It is, therefore, natural that the attempt to build a social
psychology on such a foundation could not succeed and that it was met with
opposition from various quarters.
Further development of the same concept, but in a different way, occurred
in W. Wundt's work on "folk psychology." The object of his research
included language, myth, and tradition as products of the collective activity
of the national mind, and his purpose was to study the psychological laws
they reflected.
It is obvious that here we no longer deal with suprapersonal, or folk,
consciousness but with the study of collective creativity, and in this direction
W. Wundt undoubtedly made a big step forward, even though he failed to
produce a social, or public, psychology in the full sense of the term.
According to W. Wundt,1 the common products of creative activity are
determined by the fact that "creativity of one person can be acknowledged by
another as an adequate expression of the latter's concepts and feelings, which
is why large numbers of different persons can be creators of the same concept
to an unequal degree." The matter is thereby reduced to the following: in
social organizations there are individual mental2 processes which, inasmuch
as they correspond to similar processes in other individuals, are the collective
products of the mental activity of a certain number of people. But in such a
case there can be no social psychology, since under these circumstances it
will have no new goals except those in the province of individual psychology.
The Romanic nations have made a very considerable contribution to the
study of the characteristic manifestations of the crowd; one may only mention
the works of such authors as Tarde, LeBon, Sighele, and others.
In Russia, crowd psychology was studied by Mikhailovskii and
subsequently by a host of other authors. The crowd, however, represents only
one type of social form, and the most elementary one at that, with all the
features of the herd. Thus, the crowd is only one of the objects of study
pursued by a science focused on the social manifestations of human activity.
Issues of a social nature were also addressed in Suggestion and its Role in
Social Life,3 a work by the present author first published in 1898. It discussed
the significance of suggestion as an important factor in the manifestation of
social processes. Since then and until very recently there have been no
attempts by Russian scientists to comprehend so-called "psychic"
manifestations of the masses in their totality except a few pamphlets by
various authors. Abroad, a number of works were published during that
period by such authors as LeBon, Tarde, Baldwin, De la Grasserie, Ross,
Wundt, and others. Their common shortcoming is the subjective method used
in their approach to the issue.
Somewhat later, A. Kopel'man published a work4 in which he attempted to
establish a general point of view on this subject. His analysis led to the total
exclusion of folk spirit or supra-individual consciousness. According to the
author, "social spiritual products are based exclusively on individual mental
processes"; therefore the root of social phenomena is to be sought "nowhere
else except in individual consciousness-in an exclusively individual one-
which has no social aspects."
The author admits that, as creations of individual persons, social products
share the nature of the individual products they contain, but he does not think
it possible to use this fact as a proof of "the unity of consciousnesses of the
producers, the people."
He allows for social unity or—in civilized nations—numerous individual
unities under the general term "collective," construed by the author as "any
group united through the ongoing process of establishing mental unity" (p.
37). It is this kind of collective that Kopel'man recognizes as the subject
matter of collective psychology. Therefore, the latter, according to the author,
examines not a particular mental process but its relationship with processes
that occur in other members of the collective; it tries to define the essence of
this relationship, the interaction between these individual processes as well as
the regularity they manifest.
This point of view is undoubtedly more correct than the one postulated by
other writers. Unfortunately, the author did not go beyond the general
statement of the problem and was unable to renounce the subjective
standpoint in this field.
Besides, the author proceeds to discuss yet again "the unity of individual
consciousnesses" and the "soul of the collective" in addition to the individual
soul (p. 42), the "fusion of minds" that "form a single collective soul," etc.
We believe that in societies and gatherings one can talk about the
interaction or, to a certain degree, the standardization of the products of
individual associative (neuro-psychological) activity, but not about their unity
in the form of a "single collective soul," which is not very different from the
views of Steinthal and Lazarus opposed by Kopel'man himself.
The following quotations seem to provide the most complete
representation of Kopel'man's ideas (pp. 4 and 43): "We must conceive of an
individual psyche as converging to a greater or lesser degree with the psyche
of other individuals....The content of other consciousnesses is added to the
individual mental content, to the material which is related to, and developed
in, the individual brain."
Thus occurs to a greater or lesser extent the fusion of minds, all of which
form a single collective soul. It is in this single soul, in this amalgamated
though initially divers mental content, that a number of processes occur
which result in the erosion of diversity and its replacement by unity: it is here
that united collective creativity takes place.
In contrast to the above author, we believe that it is more correct to say that
in social groups we have gatherings of individuals, their interaction and
equalization which lead to an amalgamation of their creative output, rather
than a "single collective psyche" or a "single collective soul," which does not
exist in reality. In a word, the apparatus of association—the brain and its
associative activity5—of individual members of groups and gatherings
generates, as a result of interpersonal contact, the products of social creativity
without relinquishing its individuality or merging with others into a single
mind or a "single soul."
Furthermore, we are decisively against any subjectivism in the assessment
of social activity; this subjectivism is inevitable when "unity of
consciousness" is believed to be the basis of a collective.
Although consciousness has its place in individual psychology, we are
dealing here with studies conducted on oneself through self-observation and,
unfortunately, transferred by analogy to other persons without sufficient
justification; in our opinion, we cannot apply the method of self-observation
based on the analogy with one's own consciousness to a multitude of
individuals in social groups while accepting a hypothetical "unity of souls" or
"unity of consciousnesses" and simultaneously presupposing the unity of
their content, i.e., perceptions, conceptions, etc.
We believe that in all cases that involve the manifestation of mass
correlative activity we should completely renounce the subjective point of
view, since only in the figurative sense can we speak of a national soul,
national feeling, or conception, while what we actu ally mean is the feelings
or conceptions of many individuals, especially those from the ruling class of
the population, and not a monolithic feeling or conception shared by the
majority.
It follows from the above that in "collective reflexology"—as we shall call
this new discipline—we can speak of the manifestations of associative
activity of a whole group of people and their external responses under certain
conditions, but not of the subjective side of their psyches, which will remain
outside our field of study. In other words, collective reflexology should be an
exclusively objective science in the strict sense of the word and not a
subjective one.
Any social activity, irrespective of the circumstances that generated it, has
an element of the highest order, or, to be more precise, a reflexological
element.
The role this element plays in combat and war is common knowledge. The
experts are absolutely positive that success in combat depends on the "spirit"
of the troops rather than on their physical might.
According to Meillard, it is not the supremacy of weapons of destruction
that wins a war. There is a force as old as the world and yet eternally young, a
force more terrible than cannons and guns, which is capable of all kinds of
surprises, for it can instantly produce the most unexpected means for actions
both extremely divers and perfectly suited to the situation. It is moral force.
This force saturates, excites and inspires the masses, making them capable of
enormous sacrifices for the sake of victory. Moral force tips the scales of
victory in its favor. It was Napoleon who said that in a war the moral element
is three times more important than the physical one, and he was right.
This view is shared by the military historian S. Gershel'man.6 According to
him, the Napoleonic formula regarding the role of the moral element in a war
has not changed at all; on the contrary, this element has acquired even greater
significance.
The same opinion is held by Korf, 7Golovin8 and other authors. A detailed
discussion of this issue can be found in Dr. Shumkov's work.9
We would like to remark here that the role of combatant enthusiasm, which
often prevails over might, has been recognized since time immemorial. In this
connection, we would like to quote the German authority Bernhardi, an
advocate of a large army for Germany, who, according to Sheredam, said:
One should never forget that moral and intellectual factors always prevail
over others and within some limits—rather unstable, incidentally—they are
manifest far more strongly than the factor of sheer numbers. However, theory
and practice have demonstrated that in the face of overwhelming numerical
superiority even a genius is helpless, since a mass—if it is sufficiently large
and acts according to its nature—can totally wipe out any superiority of
intellect and morals.
We should not overlook the fact that in the latter case the moral factor is
also at work inspiring confidence in victory in one party while dispiriting the
other by paralysing its activity.
The importance of the same moral element in work becomes apparent from
the simple fact that the results of labor vary depending on whether it was
conducted "under duress" or of one's own free will, as well as on the system
of payment—daily wage, bonus distribution or piece-rate. Moreover, we all
know how important are one's mood and interest in work.
One of the reasons for decreased productivity is lack of confidence in the
success of an undertaking. In work more than in any other sphere we should
take into consideration the reflexological elements related to the work
collective.
The phenomenon of fashion is largely based on novelty and suggestion on
the one hand, and on the satisfaction of vanity of certain groups of the
population on the other.
In economic life, the law of supply and demand is not the only dominating
force. The factor of confidence also plays a part in all instances, especially
during times of economic upheaval, when confidence then acquires even
greater importance than economic factors. Let us assume that a company has
sustained losses in capital and property. If it retains its credibility, it will not
be declared bankrupt, but will be provided with all possible cooperation and
assistance towards finding its way out of the difficult situation and recovering
from the shock it has suffered. Nor can commerce survive without this
element of trust. Temptation and suggestion surely play a role in the traffic of
commodities. And finally, the whole of the economy and the financial sphere
cannot do without the above element. In the final analysis, the entire
monetary system is based on what we call confidence. As soon as confidence
in the stability of a financial system has been undermined, the value of its
paper money plummets.
On the other hand, this is what we find in Tugan-Baranovskii's writings on
money circulation.10 When there is a significant amount of money in
circulation due to greater purchasing power and higher demand for
commodities, it leads to price increases in general and, in particular those
goods in high demand. As a result of the over-accumulation of money in the
banks, interest rates fall, which, in turn, injects new life into free enterprise
and causes prices to rise, unevenly at first, although with time prices should
regain their equilibrium.
There is, however, another factor in price increases caused by the
augmentation of a country's money supply; this factor acts much faster and
leads to the proportional rise of all commodity prices throughout the whole
market.
This factor is of a totally psychological nature, in contrast to the first two
objective factors. The point is that a commodity price results from conscious
individual valuation, and the resulting monetary price represents two types of
valuation: that of the acquired commodity and that of the money paid for it as
the means of purchase. Under normal conditions of the financial market,
money is not an object of independent valuation. Every purchaser estimates
the relative importance for his needs of the commodities he can obtain in
exchange for money, but the question of how much this money is worth does
not trouble the purchaser; usually the value of money is subject least of all to
fluctuation, which is why it serves as a measure of value of all commodities
without being an object of measurement itself.
Similarly, when we want to describe the length of an object, we say that "it
is two feet long" without stopping to think how long the foot itself is.
There are periods of economic life when money—usually a very stable
measure of value of all commodities—loses its stability and starts changing
in value.
Public consciousness, accustomed to proceed from the unchanging value
of money as a universal measure of value of all commodities, finds it hard to
renounce its traditional view.
Facts, however, are capable of destroying the most deeply rooted
prejudices and of undermining the most persistent biases.
When prices of certain commodities begin to rise, public opinion normally
attributes this growth to causes rooted in market conditions rather than in the
realm of money.
But if the number of goods with rising prices keeps growing and the price
increase becomes of a lasting and stable nature, extending to the entire
market, public opinion capitulates before the facts, loses its former
confidence in the unchanging value of money and slowly begins to measure
the value of money itself.
"Is money falling in value?" the average person asks, and when the answer
is positive he changes his own prices accordingly. If, in his opinion, the value
of money has fallen to half of what it used to be, he will double the prices of
all his commodities.
In this case, the universal and proportional rise of all prices is due to public
consciousness rather than to the play of the unconscious natural forces of the
conflict between demand and supply.
It should be noted that the laws of reflexology are manifest in economic
life in the same way as in all other spheres of social activity. Thus, demand as
well as supply often obey the law of imitation and both—due to the law of
inertia—occur on a wider scale than circumstances call for. On the other
hand, supply and demand are subject to the law of differentiation as well as to
other laws, since initially, under the influence of need, demand acquires a
more general form and only later is it established as a demand for certain
objects. And since any action, everything else being equal, follows the line of
least resistance, the public need in any country is initially satisfied by means
of exchange for money, earned by habitual labor, and only with time is this
need, as a goal, met by the mining and the manufacturing industries.
Münsterberg says that
political desires had to take into account the fact that the whole of
economic life in the final analysis depends on economic man and that this
economic person possesses not only a body but a soul as well. The work of
his muscles is regulated by the volitional impulses of his consciousness,
while the wealth produced and distributed by the economy must affect human
consciousness through the senses. The pricing of manufactured goods or any
other economic element—no matter which—are psychological factors
demanding special attention. (Psychology and Economic Life, p. 22)
Whenever a buyer meets a seller or an employer meets a worker—in short,
whenever a particular economic process takes place in the surrounding
society, one person confronts another not as an object that requires
interpretation but as a subject that should be understood rather than
interpreted. It holds true for our everyday contact with other people, for every
conversation and every direct interpersonal relationship. (Ibid. p. 24)
We know that needs generated by habit, i.e., through repeated excitation of
the same combinations of reflexes, are conditioned by established taste,
fashion and imitation; does this not eventually boil down to associative
reflexes? And then, if this is how needs are determined, it is obvious that
supply should adapt to these needs. Thus, for instance, an American merchant
shipbuilder told de Tocqueville that it made more sense for him to build
flimsy boats, since clients' tastes in this matter were subject to change.
Similarly, when tradition has established a particular type of dress for a small
community occupying a relatively small area, it is obvious that industrial
production of this kind of clothing would prove unfeasible. Here again
tradition—that stable collective reflex—regulates economic life.
Generally, when prices are established for the first time, the dominating
tradition plays an important role since it determines the need. On the other
hand, according to all economists, supply and demand are the major factors
in setting the prices. But as we know, both demand and supply can and
should be regarded as collective reflexes.
Incidentally, one should point out that economics as a science has until
now paid little attention to the role of the factors that underlie the
circumstances of correlative activity. Thus, as we have already mentioned,
market prices are not always determined by supply and demand or, at least,
not exclusively by them, for much depends on so-called public "taste" and
"fashion" based on the conditions of associative activity, and lastly on
suggestion and the seller's ability to present his wares. Watch an expert
merchant: how he praises his goods and thrusts them on the customer. He
does it mostly through suggestion and persuasion, by convincing the
customer that his merchandise is of high quality. It all depends on the
personal characteristics of the merchant, i.e., on his skill and shrewdness as
well as on the level of his associative activity in general. Therein lies the
explanation of why a commodity of the same quality can fetch different
prices when sold by different merchants.
Even advertising cannot do without the help of this element and, as we
know, experiments carried out by the American psychologist Munsterberg on
the influence of certain forms of advertising on the public have provided
some interesting results, which would be inexpedient to discuss here. The
reflexological method of strictly objective study could yield considerable
results here, if this line of research were seen as worth pursuing.
It goes without saying that the role of the moral element in stocks and
currency speculation is well known and there is no need to elaborate on the
subject. It is enough to recall notorious stock exchange panics which are
typical of the periods of market fluctuations.
Another example from the economy and the financial sphere is lottery
bonds. Their attraction—as well as that of all lotteries in general—is common
knowledge, although everyone recognizes that as an investment they are not
profitable.
Everybody knows how the revolutionary process in Russia affected the
country's finances. Having restored the rights of the oppressed classes, the
revolution had to substantiate them accordingly in material terms, i.e., in
terms of their wages. However, the actualization of the rights of the working
population to live better was not accompanied by an increase in productivity,
which, on the contrary, sharply declined during the initial stages of the
revolution, leading to an extensive disruption of the country's finances. How
could the state cover the unprecedented growth of expenses due to the
extraordinary rises in wages? Obviously only by printing more paper money
which, in turn, undermined its credibility and brought about its rapid and
relentless depreciation.
Naturally, the moral element also plays an enormous role in the realm of
punishment and reward. The practice of capital punishment and severe
repression that was accepted as a necessity in former times when society was
less organized, crime prevention less feasible and mores rather crude, has
been gradually disappearing from the criminal codes of various countries.
In wartime conditions, however, the introduction of capital punishment is
recognized to be unavoidable as a preventive measure against desertion and
other serious crimes among the troops engaged in front-line operations.
There is hardly any need to discuss here the moral significance of rewards.
It is clear from all the above how important the moral element is in social
life.
It cannot be otherwise. The life of society is nothing more than the life of a
gathering or a collective of individual persons. And while individual
associative activity is the principal, though not single determinant of
individual behavior, it is evident that this element must also play a leading
role in the life of society or a collective.
Unfortunately, the study of society from the viewpoint of collective
reflexology has not yet commenced. Although there have been some very
impressive treatises on social psychology, they deal predominantly with
various aspects of social life and, being permeated with subjectivism, do not
cover the whole discipline. That is why we have every right to claim that
despite a number of works on various social psychological issues, collective
reflexology as an objective science in our understanding of the term does not
yet exist.
The question is, though, how this situation can be explained, particularly in
the context of the enormous and detailed research in the field of individual
psychology. It would seem that if certain advances have been made in
individual psychology they should merely be transferred to the composite
personality and this should result, it would seem, in the development of a
corresponding social science that would follow in the footsteps of individual
psychology. In reality, however, we observe something quite different,
because the psychological method, as the purely subjective method of self-
observation still used by individual psychology, is totally unsuitable for
studying a composite personality.
Although we do speak of public consciousness, public emotion, public
thought and public will, self-observation, which underlies these concepts
related to the individual personality, is not at all applicable to the composite
personality or a collective.
Regarding the individual personality, too, I have proved in my General
Principles of Human Reflexology the necessity of studying associative
activity, understood as the sum total of the higher reflexes, in a strictly
objective manner. One should admit that, if the objective study of individual
associative activity provides the foundation for a special scientific discipline
which I call reflexology, it is even more essential for the study of the higher
functions of the composite personality or the collective, where self-
observation is totally inapplicable; thus, there can be no subjective social
psychology reminiscent of subjective individual psychology.
Evidently, the exclusively objective method which we used as the basis of
reflexology can and should be applied to the study of the higher functions of
the composite personality. Therefore, to treat the manifestations of the
composite personality, or collective, in a strictly objective way we can speak
only of its correlative activity, while its manifestations in a collective, via
analogy with the manifestations of the individual personality, can be
designated by higher, or associative, reflexes. For this reason, we shall name
the science which is the object of our present investigation collective, or
social, reflexology.
We shall see later that by following this strictly objective path we can
arrive at many of the conclusions that have already been reached in the field
of individual reflexology and which make this discipline a well-proportioned
scientific structure.
While reflexology studies the manifestations of individual correlative
activity, collective reflexology studies the same activity of the collective. It
reveals, therefore, how this activity is manifested in the case of social
affiliation between several (two, three or more) individuals and to what extent
the characteristics of this correlative activity depend on the nature of the
social bond established between individual persons.
It is thus apparent that collective reflexology must also—and most
importantly—elucidate the mechanism of interpersonal relations in the
collective.
Relations between individual members of a collective, whatever their
outcome, first have to be established. This process is always based on the
principle of associative reflex formation. Every individual act that satisfies
one's own (as well as another person's) interests produces a reciprocal reflex
of the same kind or, at least, a reflex with analogous results, which stimulates
a corresponding reaction and, when repeated, leads to the establishment of
relations based on reciprocity. In contrast, every individual act adversely
affecting one's neighbor provokes a reflex of defensive nature on his part and
results in the establishment of interrelations based on confrontation and
revenge, which are subsequently even incorporated in the written law: "an
eye for an eye, a tooth for a tooth." But, of course, prior to all the written
laws, an unwritten law operates—the law of tradition instituted by life itself
or by collective experience. It is this existential experience which, in one
case, leads to mutually favorable relations between the cohabitants, while, in
another, to mutually unfavorable or hostile relations. In the long run, these
relationships become stable due to the repetition of the acts themselves,
which become part of a habit and, later, of a tradition handed down from
generation to generation.
Since the establishment of favorable interrelations finds more congenial
circumstances within one's family and among relatives, it is only natural that,
already in the prehistoric period, tribal groups were formed on the basis of
cooperation between their members, whereas the establishment of hostile
interrelations occurred naturally between different tribal groups. Hence the
continuous warfare between various tribal groups attacking one another under
conditions of prehistoric culture.
This is what happened with the establishment of collectives during the
initial prehistoric stage of human existence. Mutual action coordination by
individual members of a collective becomes even more reinforced due to
imitation and reflex contagion, on the one hand, and to the necessity of
collective defense and attack to secure the existence of the collective itself,
on the other.
It goes without saying that the need for collective defense and collective
attack, representing nothing other than collective defensive and offensive
reflexes, excites common interests to such a degree that it becomes the
connecting link between the members of a collective; but the same
conditions, by provoking hostility between various groups and collectives,
lead to the estrangement of these collectives.
Besides collective reflexes of a defensive and an offensive nature, there are
other forms of collective reflexes, such as collective observation, collective
instincts, collective emotions or mimico-so-matic reflexes, collective
concentration, etc., which make up the object of investigation of collective
reflexology.
Abundant material for the latter is provided by those periods in national
history which, accompanied by tumultuous social movements, lead to a coup
d'état. During a revolution, a country experiences in a month what, under
other circumstances, spans centuries of historical experience. In such
historical periods, all the manifestations of associative activity of a society
appear to be more distinct and prominent, supplying thereby an abundant
source of material for collective reflexology.
For this reason, much of the data used in this book as the basis for my
postulations have been drawn from the Russian Revolution, which I, together
with all Russian citizens, had to live through both in 1905 and from 1917
onward.
In conclusion, let us point out that social phenomena, being highly
complex, cannot be explained by only one regularity or one dependence, as
the well-known sociologist Tarde believed when postulating the law of
imitation. We deal here with undoubtedly more complex dependences that
can be revealed only by pursuing analysis of social phenomena in various
directions.
Since the analysis of a physical phenomenon such as a whirlwind shows
that the movement of soil and air particles is dominated by a whole variety of
dependences that characterize a certain phenom-enological regularity, such as
the law of action and equal reaction, the law of attraction and repulsion, the
law of inertia, the law of rhythm, etc., it is obvious that such complex
phenomena as social movements must also be a result of a large number of
dependences that should be elucidated through appropriate analysis.
The opportunity to reveal the regular development of individual expression
and actions presented itself when we started investigating human behavior
not from the standpoint of subjective causality, but from a strictly objective
point of view, recognizing the need to regard all actions of the human
personality as manifestations of higher, or associative, reflexes.11 The latter
are based on energy, and whether one calls it neurological or
neuropsychological energy does not really matter, for it is similar in its nature
to other kinds of energy. It is thus evident that there must also be a certain
developmental regularity in the manifestations of popular masses who
represent a composite personality.
In this respect, observation and experience lead us to infer that the basic
laws of correlative activity of the composite personality are the same as those
operating throughout the whole of the animate and inanimate world. Here we
disclose through analysis the same universal cosmic laws, such as the law of
energy conservation, of gravity, of repulsion, the law of action and equal
reaction, of similarity, rhythm, enthropy, evolution, differentiation,
integration or synthesis, the law of adaptation, of selection, inertia, etc.
We shall see that the world is ruled by the same basic laws common to all
phenomena, both inorganic and organic as well as supraorganic, or social,
which will be discussed in more detail below.
It cannot be otherwise. If the world of animate nature is the result of the
transformation and complication of inanimate nature through evolution—and
the natural sciences give us no grounds whatsoever to doubt it—then why
should there be one set of laws for the inorganic world, another for the
organic world, and a third one for the supraorganic world?
If this, indeed, were the case, we would have to accept the existence of two
or even three worlds that have nothing in common with one another, or to
admit that some miracle divided our planet Earth and the universe into two or
three incommensurable parts, and this would be an utter absurdity.
Notes
1. W. Wundt, Völkerpsychologie, part 2, p. 593.
2. Translator's note: we henceforth use the word "mental" where the Russian psikhicheskii might strictly
be translated as "psychical," because of the infrequent present use of the latter in English.
3. The 3rd edition was published in 1908.
4. What should be the function of collective psychology? Introduction to Works on Collective
Psychology (in Russian), Odessa, 1908.
5. The objective term I coined in reflexology instead of the subjective concept "mental activity."
6. S. Gershel'man, The use of the moral element by Suvorov (in Russian), 1900.
7. P. A. Korf, On cultivating will power in military leaders (in Russian), 1906.
8. K. N. Golovin, Study of combat (in Russian), St. Petersburg, 1907.
9. Dr. G. E. Shumko, Soldiers'mentality in combat (in Russian), Issue 1, St. Petersburg.
0. His book, Paper money and metal, and his article, "Should we be concerned about the increased
amount of paper money?"
1. See V. Bekhterev, General Principles of Human Reflexology, Petrograd.
2
Definition of Collective Reflexology
The above interpretation of collective reflexology cannot in any sense be
identified with Wundt's folk psychology. Collective reflexology dissociates
itself from the latter because its subject matter includes not only myths,
traditions and language but all manifestations of collective correlative activity
in general—and not only of such large tribal social groups as separate nations
but also of smaller social groups within a nation—as well as manifestations
of associative activity of groups formed by nations for the purpose of
carrying out joint international activity.
There is no doubt that myths, traditions and language reflect to a
significant degree national characteristics and therefore provide certain
material for the characterology of a nation. But valuable as this material may
be, it can never cover the whole subject. The reason is that other
manifestations of a nation's correlative activity such as science, art, religion,
literature, etc., being original products of this nation's activity, can also
contribute to national characterology. Although Wundt attributes these
manifestations of creative activity to the products of individual creativity in a
given nation and sets them in opposition, as it were, to such impersonal (in
his view) products of social creativity as myths, traditions and language, there
is no doubt that these products of national creative activity—inasmuch as
they reflect manifestations of the collective activity of the representatives of a
given nation, their views and opinions—cannot be excluded from national
characterology.
In any case, although the study of myths, traditions and language is
particularly important for a nation's characterology, it is far from being the
sole or even the principal source of research in collective activity. Besides,
the aim of collective reflexology is not so much the investigation of tribal
peculiarities of associative activity developed under the influence of certain
historical events, as well as the climatic and geographic conditions of a given
nation, but rather the definition of societal moods, creativity and actions.
Therefore, in collective reflexology, as we understand it, findings obtained
through the study of myths, language and traditions can occupy only a certain
place but not an exclusive or even a leading position.
First of all, myths and traditions, hailing from the days of yore and being
relics of the remote past, cannot characterize modern society. By the same
token, language, developed over the centuries, does not by itself provide all
the material for the reflexological investigation of contemporary social
groups. In this case, the content and the outward form of the declarations
made by a particular national assembly or representative body are just as
important for reflexological description of this nation as are, the various
aspects of its lifestyle and manifestations of popular movements.
Collective reflexology—as suggested by its very name—naturally
impinges upon the general reflexology of the individual personality but
differs from the latter in that it studies associative manifestations of social
groups rather than of individual persons.
In this sense, there is a certain correlation between individual reflexology
and collective reflexology. The former tries to discover the unique features of
the individual personality, to identify the differences in the individual make-
up of various persons and define the reflexological basis of these differences.
The aim of collective reflexology is to study mass, or collective,
manifestations of correlative activity and find out how its social results are
achieved through interpersonal relations within social groups and through the
smoothing out of individual differences.
There can be no doubt that manifestations of associative activity in social
groups possess certain characteristics, derived from the fact that this activity
is manifested not in individuals but in groups and gatherings welded together
through specific interests, and therefore acting in cooperation like a single
composite entity.
One can state with confidence that members of a gathering frequently
display distinctive aspects of their associative activity which are not
commonly revealed in their individual life.
For instance, when in a crowd or a gathering, a person is often transformed
to such a degree that he no longer resembles his own self, that is, the kind of
person he is when left to his own devices.
It also happens that a quiet and even somewhat apathetic person gets so
aroused by the speeches made in a crowd or a gathering that he is soon
infected by the general mood and falls into a fervor the like of which he
would never express under conditions of his life as an individual.
Due to a certain elevation in mood that finds favorable conditions in a
crowd, such persons by their passionate speeches sometimes sway a mass of
other people towards extreme decisions, which are, however, incompatible
with their quiet mode of action and demeanor in their individual life, where
they are usually cautious and careful.
It is clear from all of the above why collective reflexology, which studies
specific manifestations of mass associative activity, should be separated from
general reflexology with its focus on individual associative activity.
Collective reflexology impinges upon sociology, for it investigates the
foundations of joint activity in social groups. In this regard, we must say that
collective reflexology functions as a link connecting two sciences—
reflexology and sociology.
According to S. Sighele (77ie Criminal Crowd),
collective psychology has a distinct field of research and its development
follows paths never pursued by sociology: it takes over the territory vacated
by sociology and legally comes into power where the reign of sociology has
ended. The more transitory, incidental and unorganized the association of
entities is, the further it is removed from Spencerian laws and drawn into the
sphere of research pursued by collective psychology.
Therefore, the crowd, the jury, assemblies, the theatre audience, being
more or less accidental gatherings, should be removed "from the jurisdiction
of sociological laws and ruled by the laws of collective psychology."
That is why the crowd, as the most heterogeneous gathering, is the favorite
topic of authors dedicated to the study of the laws of collective psychology,
including Sighele himself.
Approximately the same definition of collective psychology is offered by
De la Grasserie.1 According to him, collective psychology should be
concerned with more or less accidental and unorganized aggregates, which
display neither differentiation, nor definite coordination, nor permanent
relationships. Organized aggregates are the object of sociological study.
Rossi's2 views, too, are close to this definition, as he assigns accidental
aggregates like the crowd to collective psychology and the national or racial
soul to social psychology (which is the same as folk psychology or
characterology), while the initially unconscious automatic interaction that
gradually becomes conscious is assigned to sociology.
These definitions, however, commit a sin primarily against sociology,
which does not let the degree of organization impose any restrictions on its
study of human interaction. They commit no lesser sin against collective
psychology, or reflexology (to use our terminology), by relegating it to an
obscure corner of human existence associated with accidental and
unorganized gatherings.
If collective reflexology, as we understand it, represents the body of
knowledge concerned with the manifestations of activity in integrated social
groups or collectives from the objective point of view, what is then left for
sociology? There is no doubt that these two scientific disciplines have not
only numerous points of contact but are also closely interrelated. While one
of them studies the emergence, development, and establishment of collective
manifestations or behavior of social groups, the other—sociology—
investigates the structure and the life of the composite personality itself, as
well as interaction and correlation between social forces, primarily in
connection with various conditions: racial, economic, political, geographic,
climatic, and others. Obviously these factors affect collective manifestations
of the composite personality, and their dominating influence on the structure
and the life of the composite personality itself as well as on the balance of
social forces is beyond any doubt. It is all this that makes up the province of
sociology as the science of the structure and life of society under given
conditions, just as the subject matter of the historical sciences is the study of
the life of society in time, i.e., during certain periods of the evolution of
civilization and culture.
From our viewpoint, sociology investigates the institution of social life
itself, as well as the causes and effects of social phenomena and interpersonal
relations, without attempting to penetrate the very mechanism of social
developments, which belongs to the realm of collective reflexology.
Sociology studies social institutions, facts, and phenomena, as well as their
interrelations, 
applying 
wherever 
necessary 
a 
psychological, 
i.e.,
reflexological, interpretation. However, the actual investigation of the
manifestations of mass associative activity is not one of its immediate goals.
It is merely an element of the interpretation of certain social phenomena,
which incidentally points to the special significance of collective reflexology
for sociology.
One of the essential tasks of sociology is undoubtedly the study of
cooperation and class struggle. The latter gradually leads the impoverished
classes to gain rights and, subsequently, power while weakening the social
role played by the previously dominating classes. Whereas cooperation and
agreement normally result in making the cooperating members stronger,
confrontation is associated with the mutual weakening of both sides of the
conflict, so that in some cases power is seized by a third element—such as an
aggressive outsider. This latter takes advantage of the weakening of a given
social organism and easily acquires power over it in his own interests. This is
what happened to some republican city states of the Appenine Peninsula
(e.g., Florence) and more recently to Poland. All these factors provide
material for sociology, just as the struggle for survival provides material for
biology. But the elucidation of the very process of struggle, its causes and
development in certain directions, is the subject matter of collective
reflexology. The emergence of social reflexes and their stimuli as well as
their direction and expression are the indisputable subject matter of social, or
collective, reflexology, while the results of this struggle and their
manifestations in the life of society belong to the province of sociology. A
country's socioeconomic conditions that lead to class struggle are also part of
sociological research. It is clear that both scientific disciplines merge together
to a considerable degree, but the objective of one of them is to study the
formation of collective reflexes, the causes, and the very process of
development of these reflexes, whereas the task of the other is to take into
account the consequences and circumstances of the balance of power between
social forces, their confrontations, struggles and reconciliations.
It follows from the above that collective reflexology should be separated
from sociology, since the latter studies social life as it evolves under the
influence of climatic, geographic, racial, legal, and economic conditions, as
well as the interplay of interests pursued by smaller collectives (e.g., the
struggle between parties). Although collective reflexology provides rich
material for sociology and, consequently, the two scientific disciplines have
numerous points of contact, there are no sufficient grounds to expect the
convergence of the two branches of knowledge as some sociologists do,
particularly those of neopositivist persuasion.
On the other hand, if we accept the popular view that sociology studies
aspects of human interaction, then collective reflexology as a scientific
discipline studies attitudes of human masses joined in collectives not only to
other human collectives or individual persons but also to animals and the
environment in general. Besides, there is another distinction to be made
between sociology and collective reflexology. Since the main task of
collective reflexology is to study the mechanism of collective reflex
formation and behavior, we are apparently dealing with the activity of social
groups possessing a certain degree of internal cohesion, since a collective
cannot exist without unity. Sociology, however, cannot confine itself to the
study of only those social phenomena where one or another kind of
cohesiveness is established. On the contrary, its area of investigation
encompasses the phenomena where one finds disunity, struggle, or open
warfare, even when only two persons are involved in it. Sociology is engaged
in the issues of social interrelations irrespective of whether the latter are
established between united groups or between individuals. And those
sociologists who classify as social phenomena only interrelations between
like individuals united in a common action (Guildings, Baldwin, and others)
are definitely wrong, while authors who believe that sociology should deal
with the entire hierarchy of social phenomena, irrespective of the content of
interpersonal or group relationships, are right. Where there is disunity and
hostility there is, certainly, interrelationship between social groups or
individual persons, but there is no collective.
While establishing connections between various social phenomena,
sociology by itself cannot in many cases provide an appropriate explanation
without turning to collective reflexology, since sometimes only the latter can
supply a more precise definition of inner relationships between different
social phenomena.
At the same time, collective reflexology does not regard the study of social
facts and phenomena as its immediate goal; it studies only the reflexological
mechanism of the evolution of social phenomena, their reflexological
preparation and the conditions under which these phenomena develop in a
number of individuals in the form of collective reflexes.
The difference between collective reflexology and sociology can best be
described in the following way. It is not essential for a sociologist to know
what changes take place in the activity and reactions of a collective as
compared to individual reactions and activity. He can certainly be interested
in it, but it is not his immediate goal, for he naturally accepts as his task the
elucidation of interrelations between social groups as well as the very fact of
the existence of social groups or collectives. Thus, the study of the ways in
which collective groups emerge and of the specific manifestations of
collective activity as compared to those of the individual variety is the task of
collective reflexology, whereas the number of collectives, their characteristics
and the interrelations within a particular nation should be investigated by
sociology.
Clearly, the method or mechanism of the emergence of collectives belongs
exclusively to the sphere of collective reflexology, since one cannot study the
specific manifestations of collective activity without first revealing how a
collective comes into being. It is precisely this field that Tarde called
"intermental psychology" and proposed it as the basis of sociology. "I mean,"
says Tarde,
not conventional psychology, which one can treat with the contempt it
deserves [ski], but what I call in the subsequent discussion "reciprocal
psychology" (Interpsychologie)...Interpsychology orinterlogic are essentially
elementary sociology, which is the only discipline capable of interpreting
complex sociology or sociology in the narrow sense of the term."3
It is clear from the above that formation of collectives as the subject matter
of "intermental psychology," or, to be more precise, "collective reflexology,"
simultaneously serves as the basis of sociology-
What we have said so far allows us to outline the objectives of collective
reflexology. They include on the one hand the study of the mechanism
governing the formation of a collective, and, on the other, the study of
manifestations of collective reflexes, which in their totality make up
collective activity, as compared to individual reflexes and individual activity.
In a word, sociology deals with social facts and phenomena and their
interaction; it interprets them from the reflexological or any other point of
view. Collective reflexology deals with the mechanism of integration or
socialization of individual reflexological phenomena; it explains how this
process generates collective reflexes and prepares the ground for a particular
social phenomenon; it also investigates manifestations of social group
behavior.
One should bear in mind that social phenomena that are the subject matter
of sociology cannot be reduced to reflexologically explicable phenomena
only, since as we have already mentioned, the evolution of social phenomena
is affected by a considerable number of factors (economic, legal, geographic,
climatic, etc.), whereas collective reflexology informs us only about the
reflexological mechanism of social phenomena.
We would also like to mention here that there are opinions according to
which collective psychology and social psychology, or in our terminology
collective and social reflexology, are not identical notions. Thus, Sighele
(The Criminal Crowd) and De la Grasserie (De la psycho-sociologie, Revue
de sociologie, 1912, No. 3, p. 163 and ff.) understand the former as a science
that investigates phenomena of human interaction when its elements are
"heterogeneous," "random," and form a "weak conscious" relationship,
whereas social psychology studies organized aggregates.
This view is shared by Rossi (Psichologia collectiva, Milano, 1900.
Sociologia e psichologia collectiva). We do not attach any particular
importance to this fine point, for we believe that a collective is a collective
both in the case of the crowd and in the case of an organized association,
such as a scientific or a commercial society, a cooperative, a nation, a state,
etc. For some authors, Le Bon,4 for instance, social psychology simply means
racial psychology. From our point of view it would be more appropriate to
call this subject of investigation "collective characterology" or social group
characterology, since the subject matter of this branch of knowledge is the
same as that covered by individual characterology but is concerned with
collectives rather than individuals.
We shall note in conclusion that, depending on their school of thought,
some authors incorporate the sphere of collective reflexology into sociology,
while others recognize sociology as the apex of the above fields of
knowledge. Thus, Rossi defines the subject matter of sociology as "initially
unconscious-automatic social interaction, which becomes more and more
conscious," whereas Sorokin (The System of Sociology, part 1, p. 20) regards
the same subject as only a part of sociology or allows for the possibility of
merging the two branches of knowledge into one. A considerable number of
other authors also hold diverging views on this issue. Their position
obviously depends on whether their interpretation of sociology as a science
concerned with the structure of society and the interaction between its
members is more exclusive or more inclusive:
Whatever the case might be, sociology cannot do without collective
reflexology. Attempts to found sociology directly on biology, while
bypassing psychology—i.e., to interpret society as a kind of organism
possessing a structure similar to that of an individual organism or to regard it
as a collective in which everything is reduced to the biological laws of the
species—were the results of that epoch in the history of natural sciences
when biology enjoyed the dominant position and set the tone for all the
sciences with the focus on human beings. Only psychology particularly in its
intermental aspect, which represents a transition from psychology to
sociology, if not the first part of the latter, can provide the true foundations of
sociology. The social sphere belongs to the realm of mental phenomena, even
though they serve as the basis for certain objective forms of social structure,
which give us the right to view society not only as a simple collective,
culturally united in a particular way, but also as a supra-individual entity
possessing a certain structure.
The above quotation is taken from the recently published book by N.
Kareev, General Laws of Sociology (Petrograd, 1919, pp. 1112). In a similar
manner, N. Kareev also dismisses the foundation of sociology exclusively on
the tenets of economic materialism based on the economic concept of
relations of production with the "psychic" element eliminated. "Even if this
theory were true," says the author,
relations of production, which are essentially nothing other than certain
interrelations between individual persons in the sphere of material relations,
become possible only due to the ability of individual members of a social
collective to engage in mental interaction. It is rather questionable (to put it
mildly) whether one can reduce the entire social structure and spiritual
culture of society to the status of mere superstructures over the purely
economic (material) basis.
As we know, at the end of his System of Logic, J. S. Mill had already
expressed a view of sociology as applied psychology. The psychology in
question was not social or collective—it was a psychology concerned with
the inner world of individual persons. Nowadays, however, when sociology is
—or at least should be—a strictly objective science, it is hard to see a
relationship between it and individual psychology. Collective reflexology,
which investigates in a strictly objective manner the formation of particular
social groups and collectives, is quite a different matter.
Although the biological view of society as an organism, supported by
Comte and Spencer and subsequently by René Worms, Novikov, and others,
has by now outlived itself, and although it becomes increasingly clear that
interpretation of the data supplied by linguistics, mythology, and political
economy is possible only through psychology, nevertheless, social, or
collective, psychology is not sociology in the narrow sense of the word, even
though it does explain many social phenomena. As a result, the psychological
trend supported by Tarde, Mikhailovskii, Simmel, Delovt, and others came
into being.
Obviously, just as there exist such schools of thought as the historical-
philosophical, founded by Condorcet and developed by Comte with his social
statics and dynamics; the historical-economic one founded by Marx and
Engels; the evolutionist trend started by Spencer and developed by a number
of authors (von Lilienfeld, Espinas, Letourneau, Vormeni, Sherle, and
others); the statistical-sociological one founded by Quetelet; the social-
biological direction of Benjamin Kidd and the ethnogeographic one of
Montesqueue and Bockle, so there may, and does indeed, exist a
psychological trend in sociology. But to say that the whole of social life can
be reduced to the psychological, i.e., reflexological, interrelations between
social groups means to negate all economic, marital, ethnographic and
geographic correlations between individual persons and groups, when it is
precisely these correlations which primarily and above all underlie the class
struggle and cooperation between individual persons and parties.
E. de Roberti, however, proposes the biosocial principle as the basis of
sociology as a science of human social behavior. When approached from this
direction, sociology undoubtedly absorbs to a significant extent social, or
collective, reflexology, but the biosocial principle cannot eliminate either
economic or ethnogeographic aspects of human interactions 
and
consequently is applicable only to some social phenomena, without
penetrating them all.
Thus, such phenomena as communication as the basis of social life, the
relationship between personality and society, as well as imitation and
repetition, which underlie the foundation of social life, are all psychological
factors, whereas everything related to the "self-sufficiency" of social groups
is not of a psychological—let alone an exclusively psychological—nature.
On the other hand, a social contact established through cooperation and
division of labor represents a phenomenon of an economic rather than a
psychological nature. No matter how we assess the importance of these
phenomena or, in other words, irrespective of whether we attach the primary
significance to division of labor, according to Comte or Durkheim, or to
cooperation, according to Spencer, or recognize that both are equally
important—which is a better approximation to reality—one thing is certain:
neither of the two can be explained exclusively in terms of communication as
a psychological factor. In any case, although stratification of society into
groups and classes is related in some degree to the purely psycho logical
characteristics of their members, it is a result of the division of labor, which is
a phenomenon of an economic nature. And, finally, social strata, which play
such a major role in social life, are not purely social-psychological groups,
but they often share common economic interests.
"It is absolutely useless to argue," says K.M. Takhtarev,
which aspect of social life—whether economic, sexual or mental—should
be regarded as primary. It is equally fruitless to try to establish which of the
basic human needs or forms of communication were the first to appear. At
the earliest stages of human evolution accessible to the modern scientific
inquiry, we find man as a social being, capable of feeling, thinking and
acting; involved from the very start in the economic, sexual and mental life in
its moral, intellectual and sensual aspects, all of which should be regarded
equally fundamental as are the corresponding human needs.
Saying subsequently that equal primacy of different social phenomena
does not imply their equivalence, the author remarks,
