Economic life has thus far almost always and everywhere prevailed over
mental and even sexual life. One can observe this not only among the peoples
at the earliest stages of evolution but also among the more advanced nations,
not only among the uneducated and most numerous classes of the population,
but also among the rich and the educated in whom the craving for the
material comforts of life replaces the motivation to provide the most basic
means of subsistence for the have-nots. Even among the rich, sexual life is
much more readily sacrificed to material gains than vice versa, and the same
is true for intellectual life. The energy to pursue it becomes available only
after all other needs deemed more essential have been satisfied.
Satisfaction of all needs in general, and therefore of the economic as well
as the sexual one, is mediated through associative activity. In both cases the
primary impulse is provided by organic needs, which underlie the
corresponding instincts, or the hereditary-organic reflexes. As a result, the
maturation and the satisfaction of these needs prevail over the satisfaction of
the demands of purely correlative activity.
Thus we cannot but agree that none of the existing sociological schools of
thought embraces the whole field of sociology as a science of social life, its
phenomena and laws. None of the sociological schools contains the whole
truth, i.e., the truth in all its totality, but on the other hand, none of them—no
matter how one-sided—is completely devoid of truth. Each sociological
school contains a certain degree of truth and in this respect it complements
the whole.5
Proceeding from the above, we cannot identify sociology with social, or
collective, reflexology; we admit that the latter is one of the cornerstones of
sociology, but not the whole of it.
There are quite a few points of contact between collective reflexology and
history, for history—if we ignore the issue of influence exercised by
individual persons on social events—is for the most part a history of
collective human deeds. And if the study of history were limited to gathering
only this kind of material, it would become a history of collective reflexes
and would, or should, consequently represent social, or collective,
reflexology in a historical light.
And, indeed, if we observe the history of nations not from the usual point
of view in terms of the relationship between persons and events, but in terms
of the movements occurring within a national collective, we shall encounter
sporadic waves of revival or stimulation of certain collective reflexes
generated by particular external influences and conditions. They spread
throughout the collective— which often transcends the limits of a nation or a
state—and are accompanied by a number of concomitant movements and
reactions, which may be in harmony with the first ones, yet intersect with
other movements and reactions which are in conflict with the first and inhibit
their proliferation. These inhibiting movements and reactions, in turn, spread
throughout the collective like rays and generate new movements and
reactions. Although the latter are in conflict with the first ones, they strike
sympathetic chords in other spheres and spark off new movements and
reactions, for which the first ones become the inhibiting factors. In other
words, in the historical perspective of social events we encounter mutually
conflicting collective reflexes. While generating congruent collective reflexes
they also exert a mutually inhibiting influence. Thus, the onward march of
history is determined by which of the two reflexes achieves final supremacy
and the most extensive proliferation.
What then is collective reflexology from a historical perspective? The
difference between it and proper history is the same as between social
reflexology and sociology, for history must take into consideration specific
characteristics of climate, geography, race, economy and other influential
factors determining the historical existence of a given nation, all of which
transcends the goals of collective reflexology.
But there is also a prehistoric period of human activity studied by
archeology through objects and relics preserved in the ground for many
centuries. Archeology does not limit its field to the prehistoric period
exclusively, but also gathers material related to the early epochs of human
history, supplementing thereby chronicles and other sources.
The specific nature of archeological research never focuses on the actions
and the reactions of ancient people but on the results of those actions and
reactions. Since the results enable us to ascertain the nature of activity, living
conditions, economic life, etc., archeology makes it possible to recreate the
collective reflexology of both the ancient and the prehistoric world. When
archeology recreates the activity of individual persons and whole societies, it
provides objective material for assessing individual or collective reflexology
in prehistoric or ancient times.
One should bear in mind that archeology is concerned with various aspects
of the ancient human collective, which yet again transcends the limits of
prehistoric collective reflexology, for nowadays we can speak not only of the
archeology of language, religions, mythologies and art but also of industrial,
political, and legal archeology. What is of particular value here is that
archeology enables us to trace the evolution of collective human reactions.
Comparative linguistics, which describes and interprets the origins and
destiny of each root, is nothing other than an elucidation of the evolution of
language— this most essential collective symbolic reaction. By the same
token, a comparative mythology concerned with the origins, evolution and
destiny of each myth is nothing other than an elucidation of the collective
reaction of prehistoric and ancient people to the manifestation of the
mysterious forces of nature. The same refers to the issues of behavior,
politics, exchange, law, and creative activity construed with the help of
archeology. In short, the key to the evolution of collective reflexes in the
human world of prehistory is to be found in archeo-logical investigations, just
as the key to the biological and anatomical evolution of human organs is to
be found in paleontology.
In this respect, it is of interest to compare the evolution of children's
language as it gradually transforms into adult language, the initial
development of children's drawings, which lay the foundation of adult
creativity, and the first interpersonal relations between children, which later
become the legal relations of the adult world, to what archeology reveals
about the life and activity of prehistoric man. These comparisons convince us
that individual evolution of human reflexes repeats, as it were, the evolution
of reflexes in prehistoric man.
Notes
1. DelaGrasserie,"Delapsychosociologie,"/fevi<eiieSoao/ogie, 1912,No. 3.
2. P. Rossi, Psichologia collectiva, Milano, 1900, p. 213. See also Sociologia e psychologia collectiva.
3. G. Tarde, "Psychology and Sociology" (in Russian), in New Ideas in Sociology, Collection No. 2, St.
Petersburg, 1914, p. 70.
4. Psychological laws of the evolution of nations (in Russian), 1906. See "The racial soul."
5. Takhtarev, Sociology and its concise history (in Russian), p. 13.
3
The Subject Matter and Method of
Collective Reflexology
It is obvious from the above that the subject matter of collective reflexology
is the study of the emergence, evolution, and activity of gatherings which,
due to the interpersonal communication between individual members,
manifest their communal associative activity as a single entity, due to the
interpersonal communication between individual members.
It is absolutely immaterial whether our object of observation is a crowd
gathered by chance in a street, attracted by some incident, or a public meeting
focused on an event that has disturbed society, or a public or even a
governmental assembly for a particular purpose-everywhere we shall
encounter manifestations of public mood, communal intellectual effort and
collective actions of many individuals interrelated through particular
interests. Therefore, all these manifestations should be investigated by
collective reflexology.
Thus, the subject matter of collective reflexology should be the
manifestations of associative activity of social groups in general, irrespective
of their nature and goals. Just as the subject of general reflexology is the
study of individual associative activity in light of the past and present
influences that have conditioned it,1 so the subject of collective reflexology
should be the products of the activity of certain social groups as entities
united through the cementing material of a political, legal, religious,
professional, academic or family nature in their relationship with the external
and the internal, the current and the past conditions they reflect.
Clearly, collective reflexology should deal with the same aspects of
associative activity as general reflexology does, but it investigates the activity
of many individuals interrelated through common circumstances. Under
given conditions of social activity they display manifestations of collective,
or composite, associative activity. Therefore, collective reflexology should
deal with the manifestations of public mood and mimico-somatic reactions in
general, as well as communal observation, concentration, creativity,
legislation, and action.
Irrespective of the external causes that bring social groups together,
collective manifestations become possible when a certain relationship of
greater or lesser coordination of various aspects of associative activity is
established between individual group members. The characteristic features of
these manifestations, as well as the conditions and laws of their development,
should be the object of special investigation by collective reflexology.
Thus, the subject matter of collective reflexology should include all
manifestations of associative activity in various social groups and gatherings,
from ordinary crowds, rallies and meetings, to scientific societies and
collegiate institutions of a social and administrative nature in the form of so-
called Soviets, committees, etc.
The size of a given group or its permanent or temporary nature are of no
consequence for collective reflexology, but it can and should take into
consideration the circumstances that unite this group as well as its past, since
both affect the nature and the characteristics of collective associative activity.
One should also bear in mind that collective activity develops along the
same lines and according to the same laws as individual activity. Therefore,
in the activity of every collective and every individual we can distinguish
hereditary-organic manifestations, greater or lesser excitability, collective
mood, collective ability to observe, collective concentration, collective
creativity and judgment, which, as one of its manifestations, leads to a certain
result in the form of a decision or a decree and, finally, to the execution of
this decision or decree in the form of a particular action.
When examining social phenomena in the process of their gestation,
development, and manifestation from a strictly objective point of view,
collective reflexology studies the emergence, evolution, and manifestation of
social activity or social movements as a kind of social reflex. Therefore,
collective reflexology should not use such subjective terminology as
collective consciousness, collective feelings, collective attention, collective
will, etc., which must be replaced by objective terminology.
A collective of people whose associative activity is investigated by
collective reflexology is always based on some unifying element such as
common mood, joint observation, collective concentration and discussion,
joint decision-making, and shared goals and activity.
A crowd can serve as an example of a collective united by a common
mood.
Theatres, concerts, entertainments in general and all kinds of performances
are examples of collectives united through joint observation and
concentration.
Soviets, conferences, committees, societies, congresses, etc., are examples
of collectives necessitated by the joint discussion of certain issues leading to
a particular decision.
Party meetings, professional and economic societies, cooperative
organizations, etc. provide a platform for discussing the goals pursued by
particular classes or certain sections of the public.
Collectives in the form of gymnastic and sport societies provide for the
physical development of the masses while workers' cooperatives serve for
organized production process.
Collectives in the form of army units and various patriotic organizations
serve for the purpose of organized defense and attack.
Bank collectives and gambling houses are examples of collectives bound
together by the goal of profit-making.
And, finally, scientific and other organizations are examples of collectives
based on the unity of specific achievements.
Thus, collectives always possess certain areas of a common goal. This
unity of interests can be either completely natural, resulting from daily
existence or occupation (farming, fishing, commerce, etc.) or it can be
formally organized and governed by certain regulations (unions,
cooperatives, etc). Finally, there can be collectives—such as Soviets,
committees, societies, commissions, etc.—established for the purpose of
accomplishing various legislatively determined tasks.
Common interests and goals bind individual members of a collective
together, and the more interests they share the more cohesive a collective is.
Clearly, it is only the unity of interests and goals that stimulates a collective
to undertake united actions and gives meaning to its very existence. In this
respect, a collective should be regarded as the best means of achieving a
particular goal. What is unfeasible for an individual person becomes feasible
for a concerted collective effort, and it is this circumstance that justifies the
necessity of forming a collective.
If the collective had no advantage over individual activity whatsoever
because of its lack of a positive contribution to the improvement of the
human condition, it would probably not exist. Every social phenomenon must
justify its existence, and the collective does so by accomplishing the tasks
that are unfeasible for individual persons if they act alone.
Irrespective of the importance of the collective, there may be different
reasons for its emergence and different conditions for its unity and
development.
Collectives can be organized or unorganized.
Collectives bound predominantly by mood or focus of observation have no
need of a specific internal organization as they have no predetermined goal.
Nor are there any predetermined aspirations or plans of action, making
internal organization unnecessary, especially since such collectives are short-
lived (a crowd, a theatre audience, etc.). They need only external stimulation
for the purpose of maintaining external order, so that the given collective
does not complicate the life of other collectives.
All other collectives that possess well-defined goals should certainly have
internal organization, the purpose of which is to maintain the regularity and
order of collective work as well as to establish relations between individual
members. To ensure coordination of joint actions, their organization into
collectives is carried out by an executive committee, a presidium, a
directorate, commanding officers, etc.
Generally, action coordination, planning of collective aspirations, and goal
achievement are accomplished by delegating power to one person or a small
collective—an executive—formed for that purpose. Internal order is also
achieved in this manner, even through coercive and repressive measures
when necessary.
These small collective institutions, endowed with power and the
appropriate means of coercion, represent the apparatus that participates in the
development and operation of collective reflexes regarding not only the
internal order of a given collective but also the achievement of its particular
goals.
Due to the special importance of internal organization for the existence and
functioning of a collective, the formation of every organized social unit or
collective starts first with internal organization, or inner order, and a certain
distribution of interrelations between individual members and their activities.
If a collective proves to be viable, it gradually attracts a growing number of
members, thus expanding in size and achieving greater internal unity. This is
usually accompanied by an internal process leading to considerable
specialization of the individual units of a given collective, i.e., to its
differentiation.
With time, separate entities develop in the collective itself as a
manifestation of the same differentiation that divides the expanded collective
into smaller ones.
Every newly developed collective discovers its relative position among
other collectives, elaborates its own plan or program of task implementation
and correlates its actions with the conditions of the surrounding reality.
Collectives may differ not only in their membership (colleagues in a
certain profession, etc.) and in the nature of their relations, but also in their
goals. Thus, we can distinguish a crowd, a theatre audience, a street
gathering, a party or class meeting, legislative bodies in the form of Soviets,
committees, conferences, cooperatives, congresses, associations, constituent
assemblies, etc.
Some of these collectives represent chance gatherings of individuals, as in
a crowd, while others emerge due to a particular need, such as the desire to be
entertained or to satisfy economic needs, etc.
Finally, there are collectives that in essence are not assemblages but
nevertheless form important groups of people bound by certain common
interests. Here collective activity is carried out via representation or the press.
Such collectives are the nation, the tribe, the state, an association of nations,
the whole of humanity.
The fact is that direct interaction for the purpose of collective activity has a
certain limit. The more elementary the goals of the collective, the greater size
it can achieve. Thus, a crowd can number several hundred thousand people.
A theatre audience can consist of several thousand spectators, whereas
collectives engaged in the exchange of opinion may consist of several
hundred individuals, seldom more than that. Collectives involved in
accomplishing some specific tasks—preparing legislation, for instance-
number no more than a few dozen, whereas collectives pursuing a still
narrower goal such as, say, editing a text, can have no more than a few
individuals.
Therefore, collectives whose membership exceeds the above numbers must
operate through their elected representatives.
Consequently, the means of communication between the members of a
collective depends on its size and nature, and cannot be the same for all
collectives. In some cases, there is direct interpersonal contact, while in
others communication occurs only via representation or by the written or
printed word.
But whichever route communication takes, a collective invariably implies a
certain unity. Therefore, those collectives where general assemblies do not
exist—e.g., nation, tribe, humanity—can be perceived as collectives only
inasmuch as they constitute a unity based on the elected or appointed
representation of individual assemblages and the printed word.
It goes without saying that the work productivity of a collective is not at all
related to its size. In this respect, one should bear in mind that in those
collectives from which more is expected than a mere expression of the
common mood—for instance, collective opinion and judgment—the larger
the collective itself the more slowly it works, but in this case the work of the
collective is more extensive, provided its membership consists of suitable
individuals. However, more narrow collective goals, such as the elaboration
of some details, are attained faster and better in a smaller assemblage.
A considerable number of authors, including Ostwald,2 Solvay,3 Voronov,4
Haret, and Partuendo y Barcelo, have tried to apply energy theory to social
phenomena, regarding the latter as physical/ chemical phenomena. "And to
what effect?" P. Sorokin queries. We have gained a few formulas of the type
"consciousness is the flow of the energy process," "war, crime and
punishment are related to a 'loss of energy'," "buying and selling constitute
the reaction of exchange" (Ostwald), "cooperation means addition of forces,
social strife is subtraction of forces, while social organization represents a
balance of forces," "degeneration is the disintegration of forces" and "law is a
correlate of forces," and so on and so forth. All this may well be true. But the
question is how much our understanding of social phenomena and
interpersonal interaction gains from the above analogies—in any one instance
not more than from the analogies of the unfortunate "organic school" in
sociology. All these formulas and analogies are truisms at best and inaccurate
comparisons at worst. One has to come to the same conclusion regarding the
attempts of creating a "social mechanics," which reduce the whole of human
behavior and interpersonal relations to the laws of general mechanics and
physical phenomena. Such attempts were made by Haret and A.P. y Barcelo.5
This approach to social phenomena means that human beings are studied in
the same manner as any other physical bodies. But people are people, i.e.,
they must be regarded as energy accumulators resulting from their past
individual experience and hereditary influences. Consequently, their
responses to external excitations cannot be assessed in the same way as are
reactions of physical bodies, for in this case a response often results not from
the effect of a given excitation but is to a far greater degree a consequence of
past excitations, while the immediate one merely provides an opportunity for
the manifestation of what is essentially the result of past influences. The same
is true for collectives.
Therefore, the question is not whether social phenomena are reduced to
physical/chemical substances or not, but whether their behavior displays any
peculiarities and how they respond to external stimulation—in other words,
whether these responses show the influence of established traditions and their
own structural characteristics.
It is thus clear that one can and should approach the study of social
phenomena using the methods of natural sciences with the purpose of
discovering the characteristics of more complicated social phenomena as
compared to more elementary physical or physical/chemical ones.6
Consequently, if social sciences—particularly, the one concerned with
collective activity—move forward using the methods of natural sciences,
there should be no subjectivism in their contents or their name.
However, such a newly established science as sociology has not yet settled
the argument between the objectivists and the subjectiv-ists.
In Durkheim, we constantly read about collective consciousness, collective
concepts, feelings, etc.
Comparatively recently, Boodin,7 proceeding from a theory of inter-
cerebral phenomena, went back to the social soul. "Instead of proceeding
from the postulate of the isolated spirit as psychology did in the past, trying
to explain interpersonal cognition by analogy, we should proceed from the
intersubjective spiritual continuum as an elementary fact." Other sociologists
are trying to approach the study of social phenomena using a more objective
method, but the majority have not succeeded in this. These divergences are
well represented in Sorokin's latest work, Sistema Sotsiologii (The System of
Sociology), vol. 1, p. 44-76. The author offers a positive solution in favor of
objectivism but, unfortunately, he makes some unwarranted (in our opinion)
concessions to subjectivism.
At this point, it is essential to find out whether such concessions are indeed
necessary, and since this methodological issue is also related to the volume of
knowledge we are interested in, we should resolve it once and for all. Let us
follow this controversy through in connection with the tendencies found in
Sorokin's work.
The author defines sociology as a science "of human behavior in the
process of interaction and the results of this behavior," having stated earlier in
the text that human interaction "takes place whenever individual behavior—
whether conscious or not—is a function of other people's behavior" (p. 50).
The author then analyzes the methods of investigation and poses the question
of whether interaction should be studied through external acts only or in
relation to the corresponding psychological experiences. He first of all draws
our attention to the vagueness of the very concept of the "mental" and its
synonyms (consciousness, spiritual phenomena, etc.) as well as to the
inaccessibility of the psyche to observation.
When turning to psychology for the definition of the mental, the author
finds there a peculiar circular argumentation, while the concept itself remains
undefined. According to Wundt, the common definition of psychology as a
science "of the state of consciousness" is circular, for if one subsequently
inquires what is meant by this consciousness whose state psychology should
be studying, the answer would be "consciousness represents the totality of the
state we are aware of."8 According to Gefding, it is impossible to define
consciousness and its elements.9 We find the same, or approximately the
same, views in other authors. Consciousness is frequently defined as a
spiritual or a mental process, while the term "spiritual" is replaced by the
term "conscious."10 Psychologists display no less conceptual confusion
regarding the notions of "mind," "reason," "thought," etc., and are probably
even more confused about the issue of which phenomena can or cannot be
described as mental. Thus, such authors as Haeckel, Le Dantec, Perti and
other exponents of monism talk about "cellular consciousness" and "atomic
soul," while Haekkel and De la Grasserie go as far as the psyche of atoms,
molecules, and even the "psychology of minerals." This school can be
described as "pan-psychism." Others (Wundt, Romaines, Letourneau,
Espinas, Jennings) attribute consciousness to organic nature only, still others
recognize the existence of consciousness only in those organisms that have a
nervous system, while some authors recognize it only in those higher animals
who have gray matter. Some contemporary physiologists (Loeb, to a degree
Bohn and others) throw overboard the concept of consciousness altogether
and obscure it with the unfortunate (from my point of view) term "associative
memory" (Loeb).
As works by Durkheim, Kost, Wachsweiler, and others show, there has
recently appeared in sociology a marked tendency towards dissociation from
psychology and subjectivism in general. However, even in these works one
comes across psychological, i.e., subjective, terms and concepts at every turn;
therefore we do not find here true objectivism either. In my Reflexology, I
came to the conclusion that it is necessary to study "associative activity" in
humans and animals as it is manifested in outward actions and reactions in
connection with external influences and impacts both past and present. I
attach particular importance to the latter fact, i.e., to the dependence of the
external expression of an individual on past influences. I discuss it
extensively both in General Principles of Human Reflexology and in my
earlier work Objective Psychology. To overlook this circumstance would lead
to some confusion, as will become apparent from what follows below.
I do not dismiss the study of the mental world or the processes in
consciousness, but relegate subjective psychology to the area of self-study,
through self-observation and experiments conducted with a detailed self-
analysis of one's subjective states, which can certainly produce some
generalizations regarding the subjective world in general and even
philosophical speculations on the subject of pan-psychism. Scientific thought,
however, should adhere to a strictly limiting rule when dealing with these
expansions of subjectivism, and instead of the so-called psyche or
consciousness in others, it should study the various correlations between the
organism and the environment by means of strictly objective observation and
experiment.
Recently, the realm of social problems has been entered by physiologists
who have eliminated "subjective" terminology from their investigations of
brain functioning and have spread this tendency to the study of interrelations
not only between animals but also between humans.
This viewpoint, which excludes all psychology from the social sciences, is
also shared by Bentley.11
In this controversy about objectivism, the physiologists, unfortunately,
proposed their own purely physiological point of view and extended it to a
number of social phenomena, which can on no account be a part of
physiology as the science of bodily functions. This false move met with
strong opposition from those engaged in pure sociology. I am referring to the
paper by Dr. Zelenyi (of the Pavlov school), according to whom the scientific
investigation of social phenomena should be limited to the objective study of
their physiological instead of their psychological aspect.12 And, indeed, one
should not forget that sociology is not social physiology and it is not without
reason that the organic theory of social phenomena has long been abandoned
and finds no support among present-day sociologists. It is, therefore, quite
natural that one of the prominent American sociologists, Ellwood, rebuffed
these physiological excursions into the realm of sociology, excursions that
weaken rather than strengthen the position of objectivism in sociology.13
Thus, when criticizing the Pavlovian school's misplaced attempt to introduce
physiological sociology, Ellwood remarks in passing that this method
may be good for studying the behavior of a rat or a rat society or at best the
behavior of a human group that lived half-a-million years ago in the
prehistoric world. Civilized man, however, lives in the world of ideas. For
him the world of real objects is to a significant degree replaced by the world
of ideas, symbols and values. These ideas, symbols and values have gradually
evolved and accumulated in the course of the whole human history. This
history itself can be perceived as a continuously evolving tradition or social
spirit, which cannot be understood outside of its context. Human culture as
such is essentially of a mental nature and this culture produced the society we
know.
Even if we disagree with the author's subjective interpretation of sociology,
we must admit that W. Wagner repudiated even more emphatically I.
Pavlov's proposal to abolish zoopsychology (or, more precisely, comparative
psychology) altogether by replacing it with a true physiology of the brain. I.
Pavlov makes the following comment about one of his experiments:
A zoopsychologist is someone who wants to penetrate into a dog's soul and
says that a dog should pay attention and remember that, as soon as it feels its
skin irritated in a particular spot, an acid solution is being administered;
therefore, when only its skin is irritated, it imagines, as it were, the
administered acid and responds correspondingly by salivating, etc.14
Regarding these views of academician I. Pavlov, Professor W. Wagner in
turn comments,
This is how Professor Pavlov recounts the words of representative
scientists, outstanding scientists and thinkers who have been the centre of
attention throughout the ages. After attributing to zoopsychologists the above
absurdities, Professor Pavlov commences to judge them, etc. In order to
disgrace the zoopsychologist once and for all, Professor Pavlov sets him a
task, the unsatisfactory resolution of which would "render their point of view
totally unscientific and useless for a profitable research." This "examination"
task consists of the following: having determined by the usual route the
coordination between salivary gland functioning and three skin areas,
Professor Pavlov found out that when the gland stops responding to the
irritation of one skin area, it resumes functioning with the same intensity
when the second area is irritated, etc. The answer to this question had been
given by zoopsychologists long before Professor Pavlov started his work on
the physiology of the canine gland: zoopsychologists draw a borderline
between the phenomena (actions) of bodily functions and the phenomena
(actions) of animal behavior. Physiology deals with the former,
zoopsychology with the latter. The facts presented by Professor Pavlov
pertain to animal functions, which means, naturally, that they are outside the
province of zoopsychology and that Professor Pavlov has not the slightest
understanding of modern, objective zoopsychology.15
From my point of view, every trace of subjectivism should be eliminated
from zoopsychology and comparative (as well as any other) psychology,
which should be replaced by the objective sciences of zoo- and comparative
reflexology. Physiology will naturally remain with its special goals and
methods, as will also remain zoo- and comparative reflexology with their
own goals and methods.16 Nor is the investigation of issues related to the
functioning of a particular gland in the form of conditioned and
unconditioned reflexes (or "associative" and "simple," in our terminology)
capable of embracing such vast areas of knowledge as, for instance,
comparative reflexology or even brain physiology itself, for in the latter it is
not solely the method of conditioned salivary reflexes that is important but
even more so our method of associative movement reflexes.17
As for sociology, Sorokin, who has followed the whole controversy
between the "objectivists" and the "subjectivists," has remained undecided
despite his obvious gravitation towards objectivism. "The data obtained by
the "objectivists" have so far been too inconclusive and insignificant," he
says,
for the sociologist to ignore the subjective-psychological aspect of human
activity altogether. Hence, there is only one route to follow: to minimize the
use of psychological terms; to turn only in extreme cases to the analysis of
subjective mental experiences in order to explain certain phenomena and to
introduce optimal precision into the study of such phenomena.18
Let us examine the way in which the author applies his half-way policy to
sociology.
First of all, he talks about "conscious" emotions and acts in terms of one's
individual experience, and admits that "their frequent and principal forms are
purposeful, volitional, and conscious acts under the influence of ideas."
While recognizing, in agreement with Wagner, three forms of mental
phenomena—reflexes, instincts, and reason—the author, unlike Wagner,
attributes only reason to mental phenomena rather than both reason and
instincts, for, according to Wagner, instincts are similar to reflexes in their
general nature while appearing more complicated than the latter.
Another difference of opinion between Wagner and Sorokin is in the
latter's refusal to identify reason and consciousness with "goal setting." The
fact is that there are acts (not only habitual ones, as Wagner believes) which,
although rational, i.e., conscious, are not motivated. A child, for instance,
consciously repeats his father's statement that "one should not tell lies," or
declares that "to torture a cat is wicked because daddy said so," and that is all
there is to it.
The author then poses the question whether emotional experiences should
be totally ignored; being inaccessible to us directly, they cannot be generally
understood, since any attempt at their interpretation through their external
manifestations (words, movements, facial expression, gestures, etc.)
inevitably leads to errors. His answer to this question is negative. According
to the author, if it were otherwise, people would not be able to understand
one another or read another person's mind:
However, we continually make diagnoses of the type: "X is out of sorts,"
"Y is feeling abit low," "L is angry," "A is delighted," "D is contemplating a
nasty deed," etc., and our diagnoses prove to be correct. We continually
discuss our emotional experiences with other people and in the majority of
cases we do understand one another. (I.e. p. 65)
The problem, however, is not whether we understand others or not but to
what extent we understand them. On the basis of the words and facial
expression of another person, we reproduce not his emotional states but our
own analogous emotions, if we ever experienced them. It goes without saying
that if one has never gone through similar inner experiences one cannot
reproduce them from another person's words. Someone deaf from birth is
unable to reproduce sounds in his imagination no matter how persuasively
they might be described. A child who has never tasted bananas is incapable
of recalling their taste no matter how much he might hear about it. But what
one has personally experienced can be reproduced by the listener on hearing
the corresponding description. This process, however, is based on one's own
past experience rather than on the current experience of the speaker. We can
thereby recognize another person's emotional state, albeit only to the degree
in which the analogous states we reproduce coincide in their external
manifestations with the emotional states of the other person. When we say "X
is out of sorts," "Y has been feeling low," "L is angry," "A is delighted," etc.,
it is our own indisposition, sadness, anger, or delight that we reproduce from
the corresponding external signs. And, since the states I experience are
similar in their overall pattern to those of other people, I am capable of
recognizing them in another individual; this is as far as it goes. As to any
attempts at more detailed diagnoses such as "It is the problem of
consciousness that B is contemplating" or "C is craving sweets" or "D is
definitely contemplating a nasty deed and not anything else," even if they are
possible at all, result from pure guesswork when these states are not
manifested, i.e., not expressed verbally. Words, however, will enable us to
reproduce our own, rather than another person's, contemplation of the
problem of consciousness, our own craving for sweets, our own nastiness,
etc. And what about simulation? I may be contemplating a nasty deed yet not
show any signs of scheming against another person; if so, is it right to use in
research the methods of subjective psychology (the methods of self-
observation and analogy, the combined methods of external and internal
observation according to Petrazhitskii, the methods of "empathy," "intuition,"
and "instinctive understanding of expressive movements" as supplementary
and auxiliary methods to those of the objective study of outward acts)? I do
not think it is right. And it is not right precisely because the investigation of
mental experiences does not add anything to the understanding and
elucidation of outward acts, contrary to what P. Sorokin thinks.
In the above case the respected author gives the following example to
make his point:
A postman delivers two telegrams to A. He opens the first one and reads
"Arriving today, B." Mr. A smiles, rushes off to call the servant and gives
orders to have his carriage ready by five o'clock. He then opens the second
telegram and reads: "Your wife was run over by a train. Come. Station master
X." A dies from a heart attack. Here we have two examples of interaction.
The sources of stimulation are similar (telegrams). The individual affected is
the same A. But the effects are totally different: in the first case a lively
response to stimulation, in the second—death. The question is whether we
can understand these two phenomena completely if we analyze them from a
strictly objective point of view. Will these facts be sufficiently clarified and
explicated if we exclude in advance from our field of vision all
manifestations of subjective states such as "love, attachment, the horror and
the magnitude of loss, profound grief, loneliness, etc? The answer seems to
be self-evident (l.c.p. 71).
One should not forget, however, that the words in the telegrams are not
significant in themselves as visual signs or sounds. They consist of symbols
which, although only slightly different, refer to completely opposite facts.
This alone explains the opposite effects in the two cases. Nor should we
forget that an effect depends not only on stimulation as a cause of action but
also on a number of preceding or past influences related to the current source
of stimulation and the response it generates. But when we take all this into
consideration, we have to interpret the second effect as the result of the
symbolic signs of the telegram, which tells A not only about the misfortune
with his wife but also about the disintegration—due to her death—of the
whole chain of circumstances attending their common existence, which
created a certain way of life that became part of the man himself, about the
irrevocable loss of someone dear with whom he shared the good and the bad
times, to whom he is attached and owes so much in his life, etc. We should
also bear in mind that someone who dies from a heart attack on receiving a
telegram with the news of the fatality with his wife must have suffered from a
bad heart condition.
Thus, the lethal effect of the telegraphed news of the wife's death becomes
quite clear and understandable from the standpoint of objective analysis. One
wonders, what can subjective analysis possibly add to this? It would tell us
about such hypothetical states as the recollection and experience at the
moment of receiving the telegram of the feelings of love, horror and
magnitude of loss, profound grief, loneliness, etc. However, all this
"subjectivity" that we would like to attribute to the man who died of heart
failure on reading the second telegram may prove to be pure nonsense; a man
suffering from a heart condition could, in fact, die without reproducing
anything at all in his consciousness, but simply from the shock imparted by
the telegram with the news of his wife's fatal accident. The same is true for
another of the author's examples when "one and the same action of
outstretching one's arm with a pointing finger can mean indicating a direction
or giving the command 'to go there' or ordering someone 'to get out of here'."
To understand this gesture we do not have to take into account the
inaccessible mental states of the person in question but need only to perceive
his tone of voice, the corresponding facial expression, and his relations with
the other person as they have evolved in the course of time. Let us take two
other examples offered by Sorokin. Everybody knows that the phrase "What
a pig you are" usually has a derogatory meaning, but sometimes it stands for
a somewhat affectionate form of address; similarly the expression, "Damn it,"
can indicate frustration, anger, or thrill. In both of these cases, however, we
should take into consideration not the inaccessible subjective states of the
person who pronounces the utterance—as Sorokin suggests—but only the
tone, the environment, the nature and the habitual manners of the person in
question as well as his past and present attitude to a given person or event.
Subjectivists, as we know, regard "goal" exclusively from the subjective
point of view and believe that it can be set only consciously by this or that
subject.19 A biologist, however, understands "goal" as something objective
and occasionally even designed by nature as, for instance, a goal carried out
by instincts as automatic acts. In acts of a personal nature, we understand
"goal" as the aspirations that the human personality or the human collective
manifest in their acts and behavior based on past individual experience as a
reproduction of these actions in the form of associative reflexes combined in
a particular way. Thus, even in this case there is no reason to turn to the
subjective method which gives rise to views that often have nothing in
common with scientific thought.
Therefore, there are no grounds whatsoever for supplementing the study of
interpersonal relations using the objective method, which records the outward
forms of human behavior as affected by various external influences, with the
analysis of subjective or mental states that we are unable to identify
accurately and that cannot add anything at all to the objective facts.
Thus, the method of collective reflexology, as well as of sociology, should
be exclusively objective. When studying the characteristics of the associative
activity of gatherings or groups of individuals, collective reflexology cannot
assume the viewpoint of an imaginary composite "self," or consciousness, of
a crowd that would recount everything this crowd or gathering has
experienced: this would be a mere reflection of the subjective viewpoint of an
individual "self." Collective reflexology, however, can make use of the
testimonies of those individuals who were present in the crowd or the
gathering, since they have an objective nature, interpret external events and
characterize the developments in social life. In any case, these testimonies
should be analyzed in a strictly objective, rather than subjective, way.
How little this truth has been absorbed by the appropriate scientific
literature is apparent from Tarde's writings. He reduces all social phenomena
based on "mental" human activity to two principal forces: desire and faith, or
confidence. Desire defines the aspiration while faith determines the
attainability of the desired. According to Tarde, it is by reducing social
phenomena to these simple forces that the issue of social facts is resolved.
On the other hand, while recognizing invention and imitation as principal
elementary social processes, Tarde queries
What is being invented and what is being imitated? That which is invented
and that which is imitated is nothing other than idea or desire, opinion or
intention. They manifest a certain degree of belief and desire, which make up
the true soul of any word in a language, of any prayer in religion, any
undertaking in a country, any work in industry and any activity in art. Belief
and desire are therefore the substance and the force, the two psychological
entities revealed by analysis to be at the base of all sensuous phenomena
combined with them. And when invention and imitation master them through
usage and organization, they become the two principal social entities.20
As far as its method is concerned, collective reflexology should follow in
the footsteps of individual reflexology. Therefore, it should observe and
record the outward aspect of mass behavior as well as determine its
relationship to the preceding external influences which provided the cause for
mass activity in any given case. The nature and membership of a gathering
should be taken into account, since the gathering itself represents a public
individuality, as it were, and just as individual reflexology is important in
regard to the manifestations of individual correlative activity, the nature and
the composition of a gathering exercise an enormous influence on the nature
and the direction of its activity.
Thus, collective reflexology ought to elucidate not only the mood or the
"emotional" aspect, or to be more precise, the mimico-so-matic response, of a
gathering and its specific sensitivity or impressionability, but also its
decisions and collective actions. It should examine all these mass
manifestations in relation to the nature of the gathering and its past as well as
to the diverse external influences that provided the main cause and the
conditions for the activity of a given gathering in each case.
Only this method can show in the right light the characteristic
manifestations of correlative activity of human masses united by particular
conditions.
Statistical method should undoubtedly be recognized as one of the
important research methods of collective reflexology. It makes it possible to
record the actions of human masses, but it should be applied with
competence. Statistics should be used for the tasks that correspond to a
particular goal, and should be collected in those areas where they can yield
appropriate results. Thus, if you wish to determine public temperament and
mood, you should gather data about the attendance at theatres, restaurants,
gambling houses, churches and about the number of suicides. If you are
interested in the orientation of public opinion, take the statistics of public
libraries and find out which books are in the greatest demand, collect data
regarding the popularity of various periodicals and journals, approach
publishers for the information on various genres of publications, etc. If you
want to find out the direction of public activity, you should gather data on the
number of young people entering higher educational institutions, on the
membership numbers of various professional associations and on the
proliferation of certain undertakings. If you want to know the direction of the
political views in a given society, you must count the membership numbers
of different political parties, the number of participants in various political
and public meetings, the number of votes for members of particular parties
during elections, etc. If you are interested in public behavior from the point of
view of its morality, you should collect data about the number and nature of
crimes committed within a certain period of time, etc. If you want to know
the needs and the tastes of society, you should gather information from
various companies and stores about the quantity and nature of merchandise
sold during a certain period of time.
It is clear from the above that statistics is of use for collective reflexology
only when it deals with the tasks of the latter. It is not enough, however, to
obtain the data: it is also necessary to discover the cause of the changes
occurring in public life, which can be achieved through correlation with
numerous other data gathered with the same statistical method. If we avoid a
subjective interpretation of the results obtained, we shall possess strictly
objective statistical material that will also enable us to correlate human
actions with various external causes. The realm of statistics includes all kinds
of actions and events that make up our existence: birth, marriage, death
(natural and violent, i.e., suicide); crime, various diseases; alcohol and drug
consumption; postal and other kinds of communications; development of
transport (by land, by rail, by air and underwater), agriculture, industrial
labor, various crafts and occupations, commerce and industry; dissemination
of books, development of religious and political teachings, establishment of
protective organizations in the form of insurance companies, etc. Statistics
determines the number of homogeneous acts committed in a collective,
recording thereby how a particular collective activity or state progresses—
what deviations or interruptions occur in their development—or decline. At
the same time, statistics also records biological (sex and age), individual
(origins 
and 
temperament), 
anthropological 
(race 
and 
character),
planetary/magnetic and meteorological (nature of locality, climate, season,
etc.) influences.
Besides the analysis of the curves representing the activity or state of a
collective, statistics enables us to correlate them with other states and
phenomena also represented by curves, establishing thereby the mutual
interrelations between them, which can also be verified by other methods. Let
us take an example from medicine. If we ascertain the number of patients
suffering from progressive paralysis in regional hospitals we shall have
figures representing the incidence of the disease in this region. In addition,
we may take figures representing alcohol consumption by the population.
And then it turns out that the curves of progressive paralysis and those of
alcohol consumption almost coincide (Dr. Vinogradov. Dissertation, St.
Petersburg). This gives every reason to conclude that there exists a direct
cause-and-effect dependence between alcohol consumption and progressive
paralysis. This, however, does not exclude other elements, such as the
influence of syphilis on progressive paralysis, which is proved by other
means. Yet another valuable feature of the statistical method is that it enables
us to determine not only the state of a collective in terms of its composition,
its members' health, growth, birthrate, ability to work, etc., but also their
political and religious views, moral qualities, economic status, level of
literacy, etc, as well as various collective needs, their growth and decline and,
finally, their dependence on various circumstances.
Thus, by means of the statistical method we ascertain the state of the
population concerning different types of education, particular forms of
upbringing, the need for books of a certain nature, the need for self-
preservation from natural disasters (fire, etc.) by insurance, the need for
communication, transport, particular clothes, foods and other consumer
goods, the need for stimulants, narcotics, etc. Incidentally, the whole of
commercial and industrial statistics indicates a number of needs generated by
the daily existence and way of life pursued by a country's inhabitants. There
is no doubt that statistics is one of the most important methods of collective
reflexology. Not an independent science, its importance lies in making the
conclusions of collective reflexology more precise.
The extent to which statistics is a method that enables us to deal with the
issues of collective reflexology is apparent from the fact that any human need
represents a habitual associative reflex, while a social need represents a
collective associative reflex. The evolution of this collective associative
reflex, just like that of the individual associative reflex, follows a certain
pattern that can be described as follows: initially, the given need goes through
a period of slow growth followed by a relatively rapid increase that reaches a
certain level. The growth then slows down again and proceeds along a
horizontal line until it comes to a temporary halt to be followed by a more or
less slow decline of the given need as it is being supplanted by other needs.
One should point out, however, that the above development of a need may
be disrupted by various attending circumstances and concurrent events, as a
result of which the distribution of any given need may show oscillations and
disturbances.
Thus, what happens after a social need has developed depends on
numerous conditions, such as competition and new inventions, which help to
supplant the need or, conversely, on all kinds of favorable circumstances.
It is apparent from the above that by using statistics it is also easy to follow
the action of the inhibiting influences on a given social need or social
movement as well as the substitution of this action by the opposite, so to
speak, favorable influences under different social conditions.
One example of both inhibiting and stimulating conditions (depending on
the force) in a collective is the competition due to the initiative of those
promoting a new invention or making a new discovery. It is these instances
of competition that disturb, as we have seen, the regular pattern of the
distributions representing various needs of a collective. To give an example,
let us take crime statistics, which record the negative need to live at the
expense of, and by inflicting harm on, other people in human society. Due to
the abnormal conditions of life in contemporary society, this specific need
displayed by some of its members is on the rise all over the world. In the
meantime, England has been trying to cope with child vagrancy by
"inventing" reformatories (which have not yet sufficiently spread to other
countries) and as a result the curve of the crime rate in England has started to
go downward. Another example is the funds allocated by the Russian
government for education. Their increase from one year to another had been
extremely slow until a sharp rise in 1906, when they began to grow rather
slowly at first and then faster. One can easily guess that this increase in
education funding in Russia is related to the establishment of legislative
institutions.
Since the October Revolution there has been a tremendous new rise in
government spending on education (of all kinds and levels) due to the new
social system. Now let us take France's expenses on public works. In the
period of 1843-1849, these figures increased because of the implementation
of a new invention in the form of railway construction.
Tarde says that "it is only imitation and not invention that is subject to laws
in the proper sense of the word" (Tarde, The Laws of Imitation, p. 147). The
question is, however, whether imitation can exist without invention. Is
invention not a kind of imitation transplanted into another area? In any case,
inventions, too, are subject to laws.
One should also bear in mind that statistics establishes primarily
quantitative relations, whereas every social phenomenon has, in fact, not only
a quantitative aspect but a qualitative one as well. Thus, the number of school
children may remain more or less the same, but the quality of education may
improve or decline depending on the competence of the teachers. Similarly,
the number of government supporters can remain unchanged while their
attachment to this form of government may progressively decrease and vice
versa.
These kinds of circumstances should never be overlooked when analyzing
statistical data. It certainly does not mean that such data cannot be used
numerically, but for this purpose another kind of material seems to be
necessary: that which would clarify precisely this aspect of the matter.
Statistical indications of certain correlations between social phenomena are
extremely valuable, since by understanding these correlations between
various social phenomena we can prevent the latter. Let us take medical
statistics. With its help it is not difficult to determine under which conditions
a particular epidemic disease develops and under which conditions it abates,
as well as how successful preventive measures are: e.g., to what extent the
rabies vaccination protects society against this disease, etc. In the same way,
we find out from statistics the degree to which marriage is related to lower
mortality rate; it tells us about the increasing number of suicides, crimes, and
personality aberrations in the urban environment as compared to the rural
one; to what extent the state's concern with homeless children limits criminal
behavior or how alcohol prohibition affects crime rate, suicides, and
personality aberrations. These and other numerous statistical findings leave
no doubt that statistics not only helps to elucidate various correlations but
also indicates how to change these correlations for the benefit of mankind.
In the final analysis, statistics is essentially mass observation which
provides material for collective reflexology. The latter, however, also needs
experiment for the verification of observation. The simplest form of
collective experiment can be carried out by means of so-called questionnaires
or inventories distributed in a considerable number of copies. The
disadvantage of these questionnaires is in that the number of responses is
much lower than the number of the questionnaires sent. Nevertheless, it is
possible with their help to gather sufficient material for the appropriate
conclusions. Besides, the formulation of the questions depends exclusively on
the author of the questionnaire that may be used for verification purposes.
The success of a questionnaire certainly depends greatly on the nature of
questions, their formulation and even on those who collect the data, which
facts should never be overlooked.
Another kind of collective experiment is the method of direct
experimentation with a whole collective, which can be achieved more
successfully in schools and universities. The disadvantage of this method,
however, is that the experiment can be conducted only with relatively limited
collectives where individual members function under unequal spatial
conditions, which leads to the experimenter's inability to affect every member
to the same degree. Besides, the fact that the subjects are aware of being
experimented on creates an artificial situation impinging on the members of
the collective. These circumstances are avoided by the natural experiment
devised by our school. The essence of this method, used by Dr. Lazurskii et
al. in schools and earlier in my work with infants (see Dr. Brazhas's paper in
Vestnik psikhologii), can be reduced to the following. The subjects are given
a lesson during which they perform as during any other lesson, unaware of
being involved in an experiment. The lesson, however, is conducted in such a
manner as to reveal individual characteristics of children's associative
activity. With the same degree of success one can use appropriately selected
games for preschoolers and occupations for adults.
In both cases, however, assessment of the results in terms of their meaning
in the context of the collective can be carried out only by comparing them
with the results of the same experiment conducted with individual members
of the same collective. Finally, the games and the occupations can be
organized in such a way as to involve the whole collective as a gathering of
individuals.
Until now, experimentation for the purposes of collective reflexology has
been almost nonexistent, although it must play the same role here as in other
branches of knowledge. The experiments with collectives conducted thus far
were done not for the purpose of investigating the role of the collective in a
particular process or of revealing personality changes in the collective, but for
other reasons, such as verification of witness accounts (Binet, Stern,
Claparede, and others).
Our so-called natural experiment used in schools identifies in the mass of
people, i.e., in the collective, certain members by their individual
characteristics, which is essentially an analysis of the collective in terms of its
composition.
For collective reflexology, however, a different kind of issue is of greater
significance: how personality manifestations change in a collective or, to be
more precise, what happens to personality when it takes part in the activity of
the collective. In this field, observation alone yields numerous data, but
experiment can undoubtedly produce valuable results here, as our
experiments with kindergarten children have shown. Another experimental
task is to find out the difference between the response to a certain event by
the collective personality and by the individual one under the same or similar
circumstances. The application of collective experiment in this direction is
the subject of future studies.
We can find only rather general information on the findings of
experimental research or, rather, observation conducted to ascertain the role
of collective associative activity. This is what Karl Marx stated in the chapter
on combined labor:
The sum total of the mechanical forces of individual workers differs from
the mechanical force generated by many hands simultaneously participating
in the same operation. The action developed during this combined labour is
either impossible to achieve by single-handed efforts of individual workers,
or it would take them a much longer time to perform it, or they could only
perform a minuscule part of it. We are dealing here not only with an increase
of an individual production force through cooperation but also with the
creation of a specific production force of the mass.
On the other hand, we have the dynamometric observations by Féré worthy
of our attention. First of all, this author proved that whether or not stimulation
reaches consciousness it is always manifest in the growth of a dynamic
equivalent or, in other words, in an increase of muscular force when
measured by a dynamometer. His experiments with individual persons and
with a crowd convinced the author that simply being present in a crowd
sharply increases one's dynamic force. The same can be observed in walking.
Clearly, a crowd creates conditions that not only stimulate mood but also
boost energy levels to a degree sufficient to invigorate motion impulses.
I have not found in the literature any other mention of experimental studies
of the collective for the above purposes. It is therefore clear that experimental
collective reflexology has yet to be established.
The question is, where and how should the collective experiment be
conducted? One must not recommend the use of a common laboratory set-up
in the sphere of collective reflexology. It is inexpedient to invite to a
laboratory a considerable number of people to conduct an experiment with
them as a collective, since we shall be dealing with several individuals rather
than a collective or a society united by certain goals and common conditions.
There is still no better place for conducting experiments on collective
reflexology than a school, where we have a society of peers at work.
Another setting suitable for observing collective personality is children's
playgrounds, where we actually have a proper crowd. Although it consists of
children, it is, nevertheless, a crowd in the true sense of the word, and with it
one can conduct a proper collective experiment. The results of such an
experiment can be compared with the results of studies carried out on an
individual basis with the same children (or with other children with similar
characteristics) under different conditions. Gymnastic and sport societies
provide a suitable setting for investigating the issues associated with
collective physical exercises, muscular effort, etc.
Such settings also make it easy to assess individual level fluctuations
depending on one's personal commitment as a member of a collective: e.g.,
during games or prize-winning exercises. Choral and orchestral societies can
be used for some specialized research.
There are obviously numerous other settings for experimental studies of
the composite personality, such as public lectures and other gatherings,
industrial enterprises, etc.
It is clear from the above that experimentation in collective reflexology
should proceed from a comparison with the data obtained from experiments
with individual persons. There is no other way, since the only measure to be
used for recording the influence of society on the activity of its members is
the activity of a separate personality in its habitual individual work? It is only
proceeding from this individual activity that we can discover how this
activity changes when it is performed in a collective.
Among the questions in need of experimental resolution, collective
reflexology should pose the following one: how do individual forces add
together during joint work in the same direction and during their
simultaneous application in the production process? Will the end result
correspond to the sum of individual forces applied by each worker or will it
be lesser or greater than the sum total? All these questions should be studied
in detail. For this purpose a device based on the principle of traction with a
dynamometer can be built. Application of force—both individual and
collective—should be designed in such a way that traction produced by a
number of people would most certainly occur in one and the same direction.
For this purpose, the principle of A. Mosso's device can be used with
appropriate modifications.
No less important are issues related to the inhibition of collective work:
inner inhibition in the form of fatigue or tiredness, as well as external
inhibition, which depends on external inhibiting circumstances leading to the
suppression of muscular efforts. These questions, too, can be resolved
experimentally in the process of collective labor performed under certain
circumstances on the appropriate equipment. It is easy to see that these
experiments must be extremely important for the solution of problems related
to the scientific organization of labor.
What role is played by collective work itself in this respect, in terms of
inhibiting or stimulating the energy of the workers, will be demonstrated by
all the experiments that compare collective and individual work. Some
special experiments, however, can be done to reveal the external conditions
of inhibition and de-inhibition, which should disclose the role of various
external influences in the work of a collective. Here one can expect an
unequal impact of different external influences on the work of a collective,
depending on the nature of the influence and the nature of the work. For
instance, one type of music facilitates physical work while another type
affects it adversely. The same music, however, which facilitates physical
work as an invigorating stimulus, as it were, may produce a negative effect
on mental collective activity by disrupting mental concentration.
Another important issue in collective reflexology which can be
experimentally resolved concerns the influence of induction and suggestion.
For this purpose, for instance, the subjects can be shown a few lines whose
length is progressively increased up to a certain limit. A group of subjects
must record in their notebooks how many times the experiment with the
lengthening lines has been done and on which line it has been interrupted.
Another procedure may consist of the verbal suggestion that the lines shown
are gradually lengthening, while, in fact, they are not. The number of subjects
who recorded the lengthening of the lines with a "+" will illustrate the effect
of the applied suggestion. In both cases, a comparison should be made
between group and individual suggestions. To trace the role of mutual
suggestion in a crowd, the same experiment could be modified as follows. A
group of subjects observes the lines and anyone who notices the interruption
in line lengthening says so, while the experimenter records the number of
people who have come to the same conclusion.
Similar experiments can be done on determining shades of color. One can
also conduct an experiment on the suggestion of collective illusions and
hallucinations. In one of the simple experiments of this kind the experimenter
suggests that there is the smell of something burning or some other smell in
the room where the subjects are.
Such statements can be made quite unexpectedly during mental exercise,
for instance, when pupils are involved in the solution of some problem, or it
can be done after a rest period. In this case, we shall see a difference in the
results depending on lesser or greater fatigue. It is also very important to
determine the role of attention on the success of suggestion. We should also
attach particular significance to the question of imitation in a crowd. For this
purpose, a number of people could be tested for the average speed of their
muscular movement by asking them, for instance, to draw lines on paper. The
subjects are then seated in such a way that everyone can observe the hand
movements of everyone else.
If, after a preliminary individual examination, we conduct this experiment
first with two subjects and then with several we shall be able to determine
whether imitation is related to a lesser or greater number of participants.
This type of experiment can be done with attention disruption by external
stimulation and without such disruption, thereby revealing the role of the
latter. Then an external light or sound stimulus applied with a certain
frequency can be used in these experiments to find out how this frequency
affects the curves of individual subjects working alone or together with
others.
Changes in the intensity and the frequency of external stimulation may also
indicate their effect on imitation.
In the future, I believe, it would be extremely valuable for the goals of
collective reflexology to determine the place of competition, which plays
such an important role in any collective in general. One can hardly doubt that
competition must increase work intensity, but the question is how it affects
the work quality.
For the above purpose there should be a special interest in work
performance, which can be provided by a mark for excellence, a prize, etc.
The object of work may vary considerably. For example, they could count
instances of a certain letter in a text, or count numbers, reproduce words or
fill in the blanks in a text, solve a problem, measure their strength with a
dynamometer, play a game, etc.
Naturally, these experiments, too, should be conducted both with a
collective and with individual members using the same set of instructions.
It would also be extremely interesting to study collective creativity in the
form of exchange of opinion required for the discussion of a specific task.
For experimental purposes, this task should be of the kind that could be
discussed and performed by a collective. This collective effort with the most
complete record of individual opinions and proposals should be compared to
the individual effort of the same type so as to reveal the characteristics of
collective and individual work.
In the latter case, the experiment should demonstrate how the collective
mind, as compared to the individual mind, functions in general from the
quantitative as well as the qualitative point of view. From our everyday
experience of participating in various meetings we know that life demands
collective decisions, but we also know that a considerable number of authors
claim that creativity of crowds and collectives is always inferior to individual
creativity and that even the gifted cannot fully express themselves in a crowd.
This should be verified in experiment.
There are certainly other possible ways of organizing collective activity in
experimental settings. All of them should provide valuable material for a
more detailed interpretation of various questions belonging to the province of
collective reflexology.
As well, various methods can be applied specifically to the char-acterology
of social groups. Here we should focus primarily on group attitudes toward
certain facts and phenomena of vital or social significance as well as on
professional skills, etc.

4
The Interrelation of Personality and
Society
The development of the personality as social formation is rooted primarily in
those special circumstances that evolve at the time of its birth due to a distinct
heredity and, consequently, to differences in temperament as well as in the
evolution and nature of individual inclinations. This determines unequal
abilities for skill acquisition, which in turn—combined with the external
circumstances surrounding the personality—results in its particular status in
society. Different personalities are born into different environments, which
generate great variety in the inner resources of each personality that consist of
acquiring skills or so-called associative reflexes and that maintain the various
conditions of its existence.
As we have already mentioned, the neopositivists admit that society is not
a product of personality but quite the opposite—personality is a product of
society. In this respect, E. Durkheim was particularly instrumental in
establishing this view. According to him, personality without society would
never have matured to the level of use of categories and general concepts and,
consequently, would have been unable to reach the state of the reasoning
personality.1 Dragichesko— another representative of the same school—
reduces the whole of the individual psyche to social life. For him, even the
evolution of consciousness is unimaginable without society. While
recognizing two processes as the actual conditions for the emergence of
consciousness—integration and differentiation, or combination and relativity
—and asking what the source of these two laws is, he concludes that the
physical environment, having remained unchanged in the course of history,
could not have been the source of changeable contrasts, novelty, and
complexity, and, consequently, could not have been the source of the
emergence of consciousness, since "the relative monotony of the cosmic
environment could not have served as the stimulating condition—or the
stimulus—that caused the emergence of consciousness." As opposed to this,
the social environment with its expansion of social groups leads to
specialization and the division of labor:
The simple fact of numerous small social circles and groups merging
together into a single society infinitely complicates internal social life: it
generates a great variety of work, behavior, mentality and religious
beliefs; all of these become accumulated, complicated and undergo
continuous fluctuations and changes that represent a complete contrast to
the immobility and relative uniformity of the cosmic environment.
Thus, the evolution of the social environment necessarily conditions and
causes personality differentiation, the mobility and instability of
interpersonal relations, the original nature of activity, etc., and most
importantly, an animated confrontation and irrepressible competition arise
between group members, which in turn awakens and strengthens
consciousness.2
In another place in the same work, the author formulates his idea in the
following manner: "consciousness emerges wherever the life phenomena
subordinated to the influence of the physical-chemical environment are
affected by social conditions" (p. 114). "Consciousness," says the author in
conclusion, "is a social phenomenon based on the experimentally proved
reality of society. One can conceive it as a social phenomenon embodied in
an organism and appropriately manifested in psychological language." This
extreme view, however, encountered certain objections in literature
elaborated particularly well by G. Palant in his book.3 He emphasizes the role
of physiology, heredity and race, and mentions animals with a social
organization whose intelligence is not superior to that of animals leading a
solitary life. Bees and ants, for example, developed a collective instinct, but
have no individual intelligence, display no initiative and are incapable of any
innovations. Social differentiation is the fruit of invention, while invention
itself is "primarily a biological and psychological fact, and only then a social
one." Nor is there any parallelism between social evolution and the degree of
intellectual progress. Contrary to Dragichesko's belief, the role of heredity is
not regressing, and, although due to intermixture, race is losing its
significance regarding mental ability, the yellow, the black, and the white
races do not exclude mental differences.
All of the above leads the author to the conclusion that, although the role
of society in personality development is enormous, the individual factor
underlying the biological nature of personality cannot be totally erased by the
influence of society, and therefore personality represents—and will always
represent—a biosocial phenomenon. Thus, the author arrives at the
conclusion established by the studies and penetrating analysis of our
compatriot, Professor E.V. de Roberti of the Petrograd Psycho-Neurological
