Institute, who founded the biosocial theory accepted nowadays by the
majority of prominent sociologists. According to this theory, human
intelligence, or reason, results from a combination of the organic properties
of matter that constitute the subject of biology with the supraorganic
properties studied by sociology. It can thus be considered a fact that the
personality is a biosocial phenomenon, its biological element consisting of
temperament, instincts, inclinations and abilities.
One should bear in mind that the preparation of the personality for social
activity and its adaptation to the social environment start at a very early age,
together with the development and regulation of its actions. These regulators
are, on the one hand, counteractions, prohibitions, and condemnations that
limit freedom of movement and act as inhibiting factors, and, on the other
hand, praise, encouragement and reward that stimulate and revitalize activity.
Direct observation can prove that both begin to affect the child from the first
days of his life through the appropriate treatment dispensed by his natural
mother or nurse. In time, the child is familiarized through experience with the
general social rules in his environment and he learns in the same way, as well
as through imitation, what is permitted and what is not, what is condoned and
what is not. Finally, familiarization with the laws that define certain
repressive measures for violating the minimal requirements of individual
behavior in a particular environment completes the regulation of his actions.
Various restrictive measures established by law represent, on the one hand,
an act of defense on the part of the collective, while, on the other, an inhibitor
of individual aspirations that transgress social rules. For their part, all
measures of encouragement stimulate individual persons to work for the
common weal. Step by step, individual behavior is thus oriented towards
collective interests, and a person gradually develops into a social personality.
In this case, the child repeats, as it were, the history of society, since the
development of the latter followed the path of a gradual subordination of
individual interests to the common interests of a collective.
At the highest rung of evolution of a human society with complex
organization, individual behavior is no longer regulated by measures of actual
or possible repression, but by the mere judgment of one's inner circle and
particularly by the judgment of public opinion. In this case, words of
condemnation—even of a hypothetical one—are enough to inhibit an action,
whereas words of encouragement act as an impulse that stimulates one's
activity. Apparently, at this stage of personality development, social skills
have already evolved and in certain cases need only the guiding influence of
approval or condemnation from one's intimate friends and family, from
public opinion or from one's own principles.
A well-developed social personality does not even need either praise or
disapproval from his family and friends or from society. It acts according to
an already developed habit, for the associative reflexes acquired through
upbringing and life experience become habitual and determine human
behavior under different circumstances. An army volunteer who is risking his
life is not at all motivated by approval or disapproval. His actions are
unavoidable, for his external circumstances are such that he cannot act
differently under given conditions. A person embarking on a political career,
which also involves certain risks, follows a path prepared by all his past life
in a particular social milieu. One way or another, social cooperation achieves
conformity of individual actions to its rules whenever it is a matter of
reaching collective goals. This, in turn, involves the individual personality in
public or collective life to such a degree that the person cannot but act in
accordance with his habits and traditions developed around the interests of
the collective as an entity.
Children in a collective are known to be far more cruel than adults, and this
is because they have not been socialized to a degree that would enable them
to restrain their individual desires and coordinate their actions with common
interests. Because of this, even in their collective behavior children manifest
inadequate restraint and are predisposed more than adults towards a harsher
condemnation of individual members. In this respect, women's collectives
possibly come somewhere between children's and men's collectives—at least
at present when women have not yet fully entered public life on an equal
footing with men.
One should add that different personalities exist under different conditions
in terms of both their contact with other people in their milieu and their
upbringing, which is of enormous consequence for the future development of
every personality. Therefore, personal individuality is in a significant
measure determined by society, its interests, traditions, and other social rules.
In other words, a personality is a product of the society that itself represents
millions of such personalities in a series of generations replacing one another.
It is the totality of those circumstances, which are different for each
personality, that make the latter characterologically different as a social
phenomenon from any other personality.
It goes without saying that professions and occupations naturally reflected
in the general development of associative activity of each personality are
tremendously important in this respect. Here one should take into particular
consideration division of labor—sometimes highly sophisticated—which
cannot but exercise a powerful influence on the development of individual
personalities. In exactly the same way, different levels of material well-being
must be somehow reflected in the makeup of individual personalities.
It is therefore clear that surrounding conditions exert an essential influence
on the development of the personality by reinforcing and maintaining its
various characteristics through the idiosyncratic features of various
professions or different living conditions.
In the same manner, the external effects of geographic and climatic
condition are necessarily reflected in personality development. When
assessing the latter, one should also take into consideration the state of
physical health, past ailments, and other organic conditions that affect the
nature and agility of movement.
Although each personality appears to be distinct from all others, being
characterized by its own combination of habits, functioning as an individual
and expressing itself according to its worldview, education, social status, etc.,
in point of fact the activity of each personality is in considerable measure
social rather than individual.4 Thus, first of all, the language used by a
personality belongs to the whole nation and not to the individual. At the same
time, individual behavior, too, is subordinate to the laws of society. Every
person is to a certain degree a slave of the tradition and form developed by a
society, as well as of its dominant prejudices and superstitions. Not only does
one use the language common to all, but one also wears the socially
sanctioned style of dress; follows the fashion; espouses a degree of
nationalism; abides by the generally accepted rules and regulations; shares a
common culture; has common legal and ethical concepts, a more or less
common worldview, common ideals; and evaluates both past and present
events in approximately the same way.
All of this is determined by the fact that, due to imitation, suggestion, and
persuasion, a personality absorbs, during its upbringing, prevalent habits and
views and becomes a typical representative of its own milieu.
Similarly, a person shares common beliefs, and repeats the statements
about the surrounding world and the creation of the Universe that were made
not by him but by others and were accepted by the whole society. He lives by
ideals shared with others, and, finally, his aspirations are also common to
many.
In a word, instead of pursuing unique self-expression, the personality—
through most of its actions and statements—represents society rather than its
own self. It is evident from the above that a personality is more of an imitator
than an individual creator. In other words, it is in considerable measure a
social product rather than a unique form.
Thus, a personality incorporated in society loses its own identity, becomes
part of the society itself, relinquishes in this sense a considerable portion of
its independent existence and becomes a practitioner of social customs. It is
clear from the above that society rules over personality, for it is in charge of
the complex correlations in which individual personalities act merely as
executive organs. In other words, the joint associative activity of society is
the resultant force, as it were, of individual activities pursued by its members,
and has its own laws of development based on the interaction between its
members.
A collective depersonalizes individuals by ensuring the prevalence of
quantity, whereas a personality represents qualitative characteristics by
revealing individual creative energy. Therefore, individual behavior in
isolation cannot be the same as in a crowd. G. Le Bon says in his Psychology
of Socialism, p. 54:
It seems, however—and this point is a most important one—that
individual behavior is completely opposite to one's behavior in a crowd.
An individual personality is preoccupied with its private interests only. A
person in a crowd is guided by common interests.
In a crowd, all national characteristics come to the surface, and when a
crowd consists of particular social groups, it is all the peculiarities of these
groups that come to the surface. According to Tarde (I.e., p. 132), "national
life will always require the individual to sacrifice his most cherished habits
and personal gains in the name of the public interest."
Although persons of different nationalities speak different languages, they
abide by universal social customs, have the same, or approximately the same,
ethical and aesthetic concepts, express the same views of an abstract nature,
proclaim more or less the same universal ideals, etc.
In contemporary society, we encounter different social strata, class
divisions, professional groups, and various circles that seem to divide
mankind into separate parts. These subdivisions, however, are not deep
enough to destroy the common element of the multiform external
manifestations of associative activity practiced by various persons.
Incidentally, one should mention that these subdivisions in their role of
minor societies have exactly the same power over their members, who for
this reason always act in conformity with the customs, views, and regulations
universally accepted in the given association.
So in this case, too, a personality proves to be a social being in the true
sense of the word: it reiterates generally accepted—rather than its own unique
—views, common customs and in some cases performs common actions.
In this case, all individual acts seem to be predetermined. A person in
society is unconditionally dominated by social requirements. His dress has
been defined beforehand, his treatment of others is dominated by certain
conventions, and his manner of action is bound by social form.
In a word, a person in a public place is an automaton, as it were, who
abides by the general rules and follows the generally accepted customs. One
can afford originality of self-expression in actions and speech only to a
certain extent—mostly in content and very insignificantly in form, for both
the nature of actions and the content of speech, let alone form, are to a
considerable degree determined by the membership of a gathering.
Under social pressure and under the overwhelming influence of social
statutes, a person involved in social activity has hardly any opportunity to
freely express his vital needs: to move spontaneously, to sit naturally, to relax
when necessary, to breathe freely, etc., since all of this is strictly regulated by
social customs, rules, etiquette, certain pre-established forms, and so on. As
soon as a person makes an attempt to transgress the rules established by
society, the latter grips him in a social vice, as it were, with all the force it can
muster, and brings him back into the fold. In case of sharper deviations,
society crushes him almost to the point of annihilation, in the social sense of
the word, as if taking revenge for the daring transgression of the norms
established by society.
When speaking about the role of society and its power over individuals,
one should not forget about the yoke of public opinion, against which even
the strongest personalities cannot fight. This is how Le Bon describes the
ancient world in this respect:
The Greeks who, according to ignorant rhetoricians enjoyed tremendous
freedom, were, in fact, under the yoke of public opinion and tradition.
Every citizen was enmeshed in a net of absolutely inviolable beliefs: no
one would ever dream of challenging the generally accepted ideas and
would abide by them without protest. The Greek world possessed neither
religious freedom, nor freedom in private life, no freedom of any kind.5
One should bear in mind that social manifestations of personality are also
passed on from generation to generation. In this case, the law of evolution is
certainly at work, with general, or social, manifestations of a particular
society being affected by continuous changes occurring with a greater or
lesser speed. Nevertheless, an individual personality is invariably dominated
by a given society's social manifestations, even though they constantly
change due to generational evolution.
We know that fashions, views, and theories sometimes change
extraordinarily fast, thus creating an impression of highly variable
phenomena. However, an individual personality follows all these changes,
submitting thereby to the constantly changing manifestations of social life.
It is apparent from the above that the very organization of society rests on
the principle of its domination of the personality. The customs and the laws
of society are categorical and demand an almost unconditional obedience. As
society continues to develop they become ever more differentiated,
constricting thereby individual expression in all spheres, limiting private
aspirations and destroying individuality. At the same time, with the evolution
of society its laws become both more complicated and more imperative
regarding common needs of a collective and, therefore, constrain the
personality even more.
In the final analysis, the development of social life, too, is expressed
through the establishment of a certain relationship between personality and
society. Interestingly, both the self-determination of a personality regulated
by the development of civic consciousness and the socialization of its various
manifestations appear to be highly mobile processes, whose resultant force is
continuously shifting from one direction to the other. Personal freedom and
social necessity; individualism and socialism—these are the two sides of the
social process that follow the path of social evolution. Nevertheless,
personality and society are not opposites, since the most perfect society can
consist only of the most perfect personalities. It is therefore in the interests of
society to encourage the development of, rather than to suppress, personality
as long as this development is not detrimental to society. Thus, society ought
to promote all-round personality development, while protecting itself from
disintegration by reinforcing solidarity between its members. This should be
the only goal of the organized society we call the state. But this type of
correlation between society and personality belongs to the future, because
until now society has been suppressing personality with its traditions,
customs, rituals, etiquette and other conventions and rules.
The fact that society has always suppressed the personality does not mean
that this is the most appropriate type of correlation between personality and
society. Continuous improvement and ordered freedom, the highest
development of creative activity and the socially accomplished personality,
equal rights for all, and the abolition of oppression and exploitation of one
person by another constitute goals of future society that have not yet been
achieved on this earth. In this case, the goal of producing the perfect
personality coincides with the actualization of the most perfect society. Here
personality represents the highest social value, and the suppression of its
valuable individual aspects by the social environment should be recognized
as a totally antisocial phenomenon.
However, one should not lose sight of the fact that personality will always
remain the product of social life. In contemporary society, first the family,
then the school or the factory, as well as the whole social milieu, creates the
personality. The latter thus becomes a direct product of society that, in turn,
developed on the basis of the given biological organization of its members.
At the same time, everything that constrains personality and represents social
standards is the product of individual social activity, but in this case we mean
the lower levels of individual activity, such habitual actions bordering on
automatic behavior.
Just as the actions of an individual become automatic and routine due to
their frequent practice and habit, so the above-mentioned social rules, too,
become firmly established, generally accepted, and habitual due to their
repetition and custom continued in every generation.
Regarding conditions of social relations, one should say that customs and
"social standards," in fact, constrain personality only in its more or less
automatic activity. They do not touch its individual manifestations and those
of its aspects that make the personality a unique entity with its own views and
aspirations, provided the latter do not lead to the violation of other people's
rights.
This individual aspect of personality must possess a special significance in
social life as well, for it is this aspect that mainly determines the progress of
society.
It is only due to its individual abilities that a personality can rise above the
masses and carry out the productive activity that ensures the onward march of
the intellectual evolution of mankind.
One may say that unique personality development is the basis of social
progress, and the best way to secure it is to grant total freedom of individual
views and aspirations as long as the latter do not interfere with the interests of
other individuals and especially of society as a whole.
It is therefore unjustified to hold the rather popular view of an interaction
between the personality and society, according to which the two are seen as
balance pans oscillating in opposite directions, so that the interests of society
benefit from the suppression of the personality, and vice versa.
On the contrary, one should recognize that, while properly looking after
public interests, a good social organization will also grant as much freedom
as possible to the unique development of personality, for it is only this
development of individual originality that guarantees the progress of nations.
The proof of this is provided by all great personalities whose significance
for history is indisputable. The role of great people is in their synthesis of
aspirations existing in a given race. According to G. Le Bon "their
discoveries always represent the result of a long series of previous
discoveries. They erect edifices from the stones slowly hewn by their
ancestors."6
While partially reflecting their own nation, great people undoubtedly
disrupt to a degree the regular course of the evolutionary process, although
they are unable to alter or halt its movement. However, even their mode of
expression is conditioned by external causes whose absence would render the
action itself impossible. According to Le Bon,
the truly great people in politics are those who foresee the germinating
needs and developments prepared by the past, and point at the path to
follow. Like the great inventors, they too synthesize the results of the long
preceding work.
One cannot lead a nation without embodying its dreams. For the Hebrew
slaves lashed by Egyptian whips, Moses personified their thirst for
freedom harbored for years in their souls. Buddha and Jesus were able to
understand the infinite sorrows of their time and to express in religion the
need for charity and compassion, which in the epochs of universal
suffering were beginning to make their appearance in the world. Through
religious unity, Muhammad achieved the political unity of a people
divided into thousands of hostile tribes. The artilleryman Napoleon
embodied the ideals of great glory, vanity and revolutionary propaganda
that were then the main characteristics of the nation that followed him for
fifteen years all over Europe in pursuit of the most reckless adventures.7
A leader is a leader only when his aspirations merge with those of his
army, in the same way a ruler is a ruler only when he expresses the
aspirations of his nation and identifies with it. "L'état, c'est moi," was the
well-known formula of an eminent French king.
On the other hand, the nation also identifies with its ruler. It is proud of his
glory and lives by his happiness. Taine is right when he describes a feudal
fief as a kind of fatherland
identified in the vassals' minds with their feudal lord and his family: they
take pride in him, tell one another about his war exploits, greet him with
loud cheers when he rides along the street with his retinue, and enjoy
through empathy the splendor that surrounds him.8
The fact that good social organization is not hostile to personality is proved
by the existence in a collective of certain persons who stand out due to their
outstanding individual characteristics. Far from being suppressed by the
collective, these individual characteristics, as well as the persons in question,
become the object of worship.
Deification of popular idols sometimes reaches extraordinary proportions;
one need only mention Robespierre, who was a veritable god of democracy
during the French Revolution, or Marat, who was compared to Christ in
funeral speeches. "Heart of Jesus and heart of Marat, you are equally worthy
of our veneration," were the actual words of one of the speakers. Another
continued, "Marat, like Jesus, passionately loved the people; like Jesus, he
hated the aristocracy, the priests, the rich and the dishonest; like Jesus he led
an austere and simple life."9
And finally, another example of deification is the great Bonaparte whom
all of France blindly venerated both during his lifetime and after his death.
The kind of charisma he emanated is conveyed by one of his contemporaries
who describes a ceremony during which the division generals, including
Augereau, were first presented to Bonaparte on his appointment as the
Commander of the Italian army. One may say that being an old soldier,
uncouth, heroic and proud of both his stature and courage, Augereau had
already been prejudiced against the "upstart" sent from Paris, and had been
incensed by his appointment. They were brought into the room. Bonaparte
made them wait. He finally appeared in full military dress, and having put on
his hat he explained to the generals his intentions, gave out his orders, and
dismissed them.
Augereau did not say a word, and only when they were out in the street did
he suddenly explode and start swearing in his usual fashion, agreeing with
Massena that the little general inspired fear in him, and wondering why on
earth he would feel so utterly insignificant under the gaze of His
Excellency.10
It goes without saying that Napoleon's charisma continued to grow with
each new success he scored, and finally equaled that of a divinity. The
bravest of people lost face in his presence and trembled like children when
they had to approach him.
Another proof of this charisma is his unprecedented escape from the island
of Elba, when within a few weeks and without bloodshed he overthrows all
the powers that be and becomes yet again the absolute ruler of the whole
country. The charm of his personality follows him into his grave, for his life
and glory have become a legend. In general, the charisma of great military
leaders like Napoleon, Suvorov, and others survives their death and comes
close to the charisma of a divinity. Here is another example: General
Vandamme, who himself had fought in many battles, was saying to a friend
of his on the way to the Tuileries Palace in 1815, when Napoleon's star was
no longer as bright as it used to be:
My dear, the charm this man exudes affects me so strongly that I cannot
explain it even to myself; its degree is such that I, who fear neither God
nor Devil, tremble like a child in front of him, and he can make me go
through the eye of a needle and send me into a fire.
The effect of this charm on the masses is shown in Count Leo Tolstoy's
masterful description of the crossing of the Vistula by a squadron of Polish
soldiers before Napoleon's eyes. '"Long live the Emperor!' the Poles were
cheering ecstatically, disrupting the front and crushing one another in order to
see him...They were ordered to find a ford and to cross to the other side by
wading." The commander of the Uhlans asked permission to swim across the
Vistula rather than look for a ford, and having obtained this permission, the
old mus-tached officer immediately threw himself into the river with a happy
face, shining eyes and a cheer on his lips:
Hundreds of Uhlans followed him. It was cold and terrifying in the middle
of the river, in the rapids. Although they were trying to hold onto one
another, the Uhlans were falling off their horses. The horses were
drowning, and so were the people; the rest were trying to swim in the
saddle or holding onto a horse's mane. They were trying to swim, and
were proud of swimming and drowning in this river before the eyes of the
man who was sitting on a log without even looking at what they were
doing. After crossing the river they cheered again, looking rapturously at
the place where Napoleon was standing.
Our own military leader, Suvorov, possessed the same kind of charisma.
We know that when his army was crossing the Alps the soldiers became so
exhausted that they could no longer move. When informed about this,
Suvorov ordered a grave to be dug for himself in the mountains. The effect of
this action on the army was such that the exhaustion was immediately
forgotten. The campaign continued and ended in a total victory.
Tarde is quite right when he says that
When the crowd admires its leader, or when the army adores its general, it
admires itself and appropriates its leader's high opinion of himself, which,
in the guise of pride in his origins or genius, shines forth from the face of
Louis XIV or Cromwell, Alexander the Great or Scipio, or even some
popular leader.
It goes without saying that the charisma of a military leader creates fertile
soil for suggestion, and the latter often works wonders and saves the
situation. The following is an example of Napoleon's magic effect on an
Austrian officer who had the force of a whole detachment behind him. This
episode was related by Dragomarov (I am quoting A.S. Rezanov):
Napoleon with his retinue and a small convoy came upon a four-thousand-
man column of Austrians. One of their officers approached Napoleon and
demanded his surrender. "Do you know to whom you are talking? I am
Commander-in-Chief. The whole army is behind me! How dare you!
Inform the commander of your column that I demand your immediate
surrender! If you fail to lay down your arms in five minutes I shall give
orders to shoot every one of you!" And they laid down their arms and
surrendered.
The explanation of charisma lies both in one's personal qualities and
certainly in success. In the above example, however, personal qualities were
apparently more important, since even in the absence of success—when one's
charismatic power usually runs out-Napoleon's charisma survived, and so did
that of another legendary hero—Charles XII. I have deliberately cited
examples of those historical figures, whose names retained their charismatic
appeal after their death, even though they were eventually defeated and the
cause they championed was not truly their people's cause, as were, for
example, the struggle of the Polish people for independence associated with
the memory of the great Kosciuszko, or the struggle for the liberation and
unity of the Italian nation associated with the memory of another remarkable
personality—Garibaldi. In the case of the last two examples, it is quite natural
that as long as the idea or the guiding principle championed by the people
survives, the hero who linked his name to this principle by personifying it, as
it were, will retain his charismatic power in the people's memory and will be
surrounded by the halo of glory bordering on deification.
All of the above leads us to the discussion of the art of controlling a
composite personality. This art should apparently take into consideration the
nature of the latter. Therefore, one set of methods may be appropriate for a
crowd, another for the organized masses, yet another for a theatre audience,
and finally some special methods of control may prove suitable for the
collectives whose tasks involve decision making.
Let us first examine the ways and means of guiding or controlling a crowd.
The power of the crowd lies, above all, in its unity and homogeneity,
characterized by a common ideology or a common goal. Dissent in this
regard is highly destructive. Therefore, the art of controlling a crowd consists
of maintaining the common ideology at all costs and of removing any
possibility of dissent by all available means. For this purpose one may use
various methods, which basically come down to isolating the crowd from
extraneous influences and increasing the homogeneity of the collective
through every means that supports and justifies the aspirations of the crowd.
Some authors (Tarde, Le Bon, Sighele, and others) agree that the crowd is
hopelessly obtuse. This is, however, a gross exaggeration. Giddings is right
when he claims that "the social mind does not differ too much from the
individual mind in its manifestations." It is beyond any doubt, however, that
the crowd is incapable of reasoning, and that reasoning has hardly any
meaning and value for the crowd. The latter is cemented into a single whole
mostly by mood; therefore, one should rely on fiery words rather than
persuasion in order to excite the crowd. Once this is achieved, all one has to
do is to rule, dispense orders and serve as an example, since the latter acts
like suggestion, and all famous military leaders use it.
In general, one should bear in mind that the more cohesive and especially
the more homogeneous the mass is, the easier psychic conduction becomes,
the faster social contagion spreads, and the more likely is an explosion of
enthusiasm under the influence of a fanatical sermon. Every individual
absorbed by the crowd experiences a weakening of the inhibiting influences
and an intensification of the associative reflexes of an imitative nature.
Due to suggestion, an individual in the crowd loses to a significant degree
his critical thinking in the general atmosphere of weakened and stunted moral
principles, heightened sensitivity, and astounding suggestibility.
It goes without saying that the crowd is not always capable of assessing
correctly even its own heroes, and often surrounds with slavish adoration
those who in reality are the evil geniuses of their nation and its destiny.
Another means of controlling the crowd is its organization. Only
spontaneous crowds have no organization of their own, which is why they are
capable of impulsive action. They are incapable, however, of practicing self-
restraint in any more or less complex collective action. Only organized
masses of people, like the army, are capable of it. On the one hand, the
organization of the crowd should consist of its correct differentiation into
separate parts, each performing its own functions, and on the other hand, it
should unite all these parts through their joint action and the principle of
solidarity, unity and mutual support.
Finally, the pursuit of particular goals in any struggle should be
comprehensible to the crowd and ought to inspire it. A.S. Rezanov says in
The Army and the Crowd that "the perfection of the external organization of a
regular army will not result in the desired advantages without an inspiring
moral force, for such an organism is a decaying wreck reminiscent of a dying
lion." The examples of this are the defeat of the Italians in Abyssinia and the
long unsuccessful operations of the British in the Transvaal. However,
organization and inspiration are not enough. There should also be faith in
one's leader, one's cause, and one's power. This faith should be total and
unshakable. It constitutes what one calls the morale of the army, without
which even the best organization is powerless. As soon as faith in the leader
and the cause is shaken, the crowd begins rapidly to disintegrate. This is what
happened to Napoleon's army after arriving in Moscow. The same was
observed in the Russian army during the World War, when the shortage of
armaments, the Tsar's inept military leadership, as well as rumors about the
treason of the incompetent, rude, and ignorant war minister, caused the troops
to lose faith in the possibility of their victory, which led inevitably to the
demoralization of the army, completed by revolutionary propaganda.
Faith in victory is undoubtedly of paramount importance to the crowd, so
particular foresight and caution should be exercised in order to avoid
straining this faith excessively, as was the case during the Russo-Japanese
war. According to G. Martynov,
for some reason the Russian army in Manchuria regarded deception as a
means of raising the spirit of the troops. For example, they would always
announce before battles that there would be no retreat, and then the very
same people would give the order to retreat; they would announce our
victories, which later proved to have been defeats; they would spread
rumors about the deaths of Kouroki and Oyama (the former managed to
die three times), about the incredible losses and the epidemic diseases in
the Japanese army, about the sinking of the enemy fleet, and about famine
and uprisings in Japan.
Obviously, deception will invariably be disclosed one way or another; it
cannot fail to undermine the moral authority of the commanding staff and to
result in a general disaffection followed by loss of energy. Clearly, faith in
victory should be sustained by all possible means, and good timing is
particularly important here. " 'A division is coming to our rescue!' one of
Sevastopol's heroes shouted, and the defenders of the hill repulsed with
renewed energy the enemy's mad attacks, while their actual reinforcement
consisted of the pitiful remnants of a few detachments."11
At the same time, one should not overlook the extraordinary gullibility of
the crowd and its predilection for exaggeration. That is why it is just as easy
for a popular leader to earn unmerited adoration as to completely lose the
people's trust.
Returning to the subject of collectives, one must further add that
collectives generally manifest less initiative than individual persons. The
latter, however, can better implement their intentions through a collective.
As we have already mentioned, alongside unity of interests, every
collective also presupposes a certain self-limitation of individual members in
the interests of the collective—in other words, an inhibition of individual
aspirations that go against the common interests. On the other hand, a
collective supports all individual aspirations that benefit the common weal.
In the course of history, the evolution of collective life originally led to the
suppression of personality by the family, by the clan and, subsequently, by
the state. However, the more recent period of human history has, until the
Great War, been gradually eroding those institutions which put excessive
constraints on an individual, and this erosion resulted in the liberation of the
personality from its fetters. With the progress of history the personality
gradually frees itself from the chains of the family, the tribe, and the state,
and embarks on the road to self-determination.
Undoubtedly, this is directly related to personality development and
progress. The truth is that human personality represents not only something
individual, but also something recurrent. Besides, a higher personality
development implies a more valuable contribution to a human collective.
That is why, when individual development was rather limited, the value of
the personality in the collective was reduced mostly to increasing the
collective in terms of its numbers, whereas the personality's characteristics
were not duly appreciated by the collective because of their insignificant
value in general.
At the most, an average individual can make his presence known during
voting by raising or not raising his hand, or by leaving through this or that
door. In any case, the minority must defer to the majority, and it is only in
highly exceptional cases that the minority protests publicly. Quite often this
protest is of purely theoretical significance— in other words, it is not
expressed in any action. The minority merely preserves its political or social
physiognomy, and falls away, as it were, from the majority, which
implements its policies unimpeded. Instances of resistance and confrontation
are certainly possible during particular periods, but only when the minority
can find support among some actual forces that would prove sufficient to
counteract the majority.
With the gradual development of the personality as a unit that contributes
its initiative and unique intellectual abilities to the activity of the whole
collective, its qualities become particularly important in the light of collective
interests.
For this reason, the role of the modern state amounts to facilitating
personality development, satisfying individual interests, and encouraging the
fusion and cohesiveness of individual members into the powerful collective
of the state.
However, parties themselves, as well as other collectives, including the
state, also strive for self-determination. This kind of self-determination aims
at establishing normal relationships between different collectives, and
basically should not violate the principles of individual self-determination:
e.g., personal immunity; sanctity of the home; freedom of speech, assembly,
and associations; freedom of conscience; national equality and free elections
of governing bodies.
Yelinek, as we know, recognized that the state wields absolute power over
the personality, and determines its own rights in regard to the personality.
However, Benjamin Constant and our M.M. Kovalevskii (Common
Constitutional Law IT) refute this theory. "I believe," says Kovalevskii,
that the presently dominant theory, supported by most German publicists
and claiming that any constitution is no more than a voluntary self-
restriction of state authority, cannot serve as a bulwark against the danger
to freedom represented by a doctrine that allows for unlimited state
sovereignty (p. 102).
In another place in the same work the author says,
From our point of view there is no need whatsoever to accept the state's
absolute power, which would enable it to revoke the freedom of personal
self-determination in the interests of some state necessity. No such
necessity exists or can ever exist—on the contrary, human solidarity
demands that we preserve and advance the freedom of personal self-
determination.
And later on: "The state should have only those rights, which, instead of
inhibiting, serve to protect and guard personal self-determination as an
indispensable condition of social solidarity" (pp. 103106).
It is thus apparent that governmental oppression in contemporary states
would be paralyzed by the decentralization of administration, by total
democracy, accountability of all civil servants to courts, and by inalienable
individual rights regarding the freedom of assembly, associations and
conscience, national equality, and the right to elect one's government.
Therefore, under normal conditions, a modern state collective presupposes a
governing body properly constructed on the principle of representation,
national self-determination, and local self-government elected by voting.
The quality of any collective always depends on its constituent units.
When discussing this question, Sighele says that "analogy between the
properties of the units and the aggregate is possible only when its parts are
somewhat similar," when its components are homogeneous. A cosmopolitan
action does not reflect the national goals of a particular people, and neither
does a group of individuals from this people: a hatter and a scholar in a jury
cannot express their opinion as correctly as would a gathering of experts. The
mere similarity of constituent elements, however, is not enough to establish
an analogy between the properties of the elements and those of the aggregate
they form. One also needs a connection between the components of the
aggregate. That is why spontaneous and unorganized gatherings, such as a
jury, a theatre audience and a crowd "cannot reproduce in their
manifestations the qualities of their component units," for there is no organic
connection between them as there is, for example, between the members of a
family or a social group. Sighele's conclusion that the properties of the
aggregate are determined by the properties of its component units is true for
aggregates that consist of homogeneous and organically interrelated units. It
is not accurate, however, for partly homogeneous and weakly organized
units, and it is wrong for the aggregate consisting of heterogeneous and
nonorganized components.
When a collective is homogeneous, its activity largely depends on whether
its members are educated or uneducated, whether they possess certain
technological knowledge and experience, whether they practice any
profession, or whether they belong to any social class. All of this
undoubtedly shows in one way or another in the activity of the collective,
makes a distinct imprint on its manifestations, and affects in a particular
manner its position on various issues.
As for the manifestation of collective activity, it depends mostly on the
cohesiveness and organization of the collective. The weaker its cohesiveness
and organization are, the less chance there is of achieving the goals that
brought this collective into existence. Thus, a crowd may become awesome
due to its cohesiveness. If you create division in a crowd regarding its object
of interest, it turns into a powerless rabble.
Any army is held together solely by its cohesiveness and organization. If
you divide the troops and disorganize them by relaxing the discipline based
on blind obedience, you will transform an army into a rabble incapable of
decent behavior, let alone of offense and defense.
Collectives also differ in the character of their actions depending on their
unifying principle. Thus, a crowd is united by its mood, which is the sole
motive power of all its actions, and whose influence makes these actions
seem automatic.
While carrying out collective activity, gatherings engaged in intellectual
pursuits almost invariably reveal considerable caution and foresight in their
actions.
Yet even at this type of gathering some conditions can cause the mood to
rise to a certain level. Then the decisions and the actions of the gathering will
show the effect of this particular mood.
Irrespective of the complexity of various collectives, their activity is
defined by the resultant force of all their constituent units. The same is true
for complex collectives consisting of a number of smaller collectives which
send their representatives to the larger one. The only difference is that the
resultant force of behavior is shaped first in the smaller collectives and then
in the complex ones.
Collective reflexology should examine collective activity from the
viewpoint of both the present moment and the past events. Just as in the
development of an individual personality, its past circumstances and
experience are reflected in its character and its aspirations, so a social
collective cannot be studied without a historical perspective.
Thus, for example, one cannot examine the collective activity of the
European nations drawn into the Great War without turning to the past, to the
old struggle for national sovereignty, which essentially affected the whole
history of the nineteenth century. The Spanish war for independence, the
Holy Alliance of 1815, the restoration of Greece, the unification of Italy and
of Germany, the federal structure of Austro-Hungary, the Russo-Turkish war,
the Russo-Japanese war and the later Balkan war basically represent different
stages of the narrowly nationalist aspirations of various nations. At the same
time, the revolutions that occurred in different countries, as well as the shift
of social forces in favor of the rule by the people indicate the gains of
socialism. The Great War was perceived as the struggle of right against
might, but it, too, concealed the nationalist aspirations of both Germany and
the Allies.
The Great War divided the nations, and everywhere socialist tendencies
gave way to nationalist ones. The idea of the International seemed to have
died during the initial stage of the war. But the more obvious its protracted
nature became, the stronger the idea of internationalism grew, and appeals for
unity were issued by all the Socialist parties of the world. This was first
manifested in a particularly acute form in the Russian Revolution, which, on
the one hand, ended the struggle for national independence by advancing its
slogan of national self-determination, while on the other hand, it made a
decisive move towards internationalism.
Notes
1. See his "Représentation individuelle et représentation collective," Revue metaphisique et de Morale
1898, and Les formes élémentaires de la vie religieuse, Paris, Alcan 1913.
2. Dragichesko, "The Relationship between Psychology and Sociology," New Ideas in Sociology, Issue
2,1914.
3. Us antinomies entre Vindividu et la société, Paris, Alcan, 1914.
4. V. Bekhterev, Personality and the Social Conditions of its Development and Health (in Russian). A
separate edition. St PB.
5. Le Bon, Us lois psychologiques de l'évolution des peuples, Alcan, 1884, pp. 131132.
6. G. Le Bon, ibid., p. 144.
7. G. Le Bon, ibid., p. 148.
8. H. Taine, Us Origines de la France Contemporaine (in Russian), St. Petersburg, 1880, p. 19.
9. Dr. Cabanis, Marat inconnu.
0. G. Le Bon, Psychologie des foules, p. 252.
1. A.S. Rezanov, Le, p. 82.
5
The Collective as a Composite
Personality
Guided by the argument that the property of an entity depends on the
composition of its components, Spencer postulated in his Introduction to
Sociology that the most important goals of human societies correspond to the
principal characteristics of the individual person, society itself being,
according to Spencer, nothing other than an organism.
According to A. Comte (Système de politique positive. Paris), human
society represents an "ever-existing personality," and in this sense there is a
certain similarity between the views of Comte and Spencer. Subsequently, a
number of other authors, including most recent ones, have supported the
comparison between society and the organism. This analogy, however, is of a
purely external nature, and by now it has been essentially abandoned: no one
will agree that sociology should be reduced to biology.
Yet even after discarding the view of society as an organism, it is not as
simple as it initially appears to be to define what a society, or a collective, or
—as I would suggest calling it—a composite personality, is.
According to some authors, we are dealing with a group of individuals who
provide certain services for one another and are in general mutually useful.
This introduces the principle of utilitarianism into the basis of definition.
Tarde, however, although admitting the clarity of this definition, regarded it
erroneous and preferred a juridical interpretation of society, wherein
individual members enjoy certain mutual rights established by custom, law,
or rules of propriety. Finally, according to Tarde, one could develop a purely
political or a purely religious concept of social communion, since all those
who are united by the same political plan and form a single political body, as
well as those who profess the same religion, would have a legitimate right to
be considered members of the same society. In this case, according to Tarde,
the similarity of political goals and of religious belief is due to imitation.
Therefore, he defines a social group as an "assembly of creatures who are
prepared to imitate one another, or who are similar without imitating one
another, since their common features are ancient copies of the same original."
Many sociologists see the most typical characteristic of society as the
interaction of its members. "The long-term, continuous, many-sided and
necessary interaction established in any permanent, rather than accidental,
aggregation of living beings" is, according to De Roberti,1 characteristic of
"community." This opinion is shared by Simmel, Novikov, Sorokin, and in
part by Gumplowiez (group interaction), Giddings, Draghicesco, Bouglé ,
Fouillée, Grasserie, Ward and others.2
It is not enough, however, to talk about interaction: one should define what
kind of interaction is meant. Most sociologists mean the "psychological"
nature of interaction in a social phenomenon. But "psychological" interaction,
too, can be different. For example, it can be determined by mutual benefit, by
a common goal, by a common interest, by common origins, by common links
on the basis of imitation (Tarde) or by mutual strife and persecution.
In this respect we encounter a considerable divergence of opinion amongst
authors, but we shall not discuss these views in detail. My point of view is
that we should recognize as a society any association of interacting
individuals brought together by any common interests whatsoever—political,
economic, religious, business or any other. Tarde's view that society
presupposes similarity based on imitation strikes me as being both too
general and too one-sided: although imitation can indeed create a society, it is
also true that everybody imitates everybody else, including those who do not
form a society in the present sense of the word. On the other hand, society is
based not only on imitation but also on the division of labor which, too,
creates society, for the division of labor presupposes society just as imitation
does, and essentially society cannot exist without the division of labor, just as
it cannot exist without imitation.
A chance conglomeration of numerous individuals in a given period of
time in a particular place cannot be described as a composite personality, a
society or a collective. This kind of gathering is a crowd without any unifying
principle, and since each individual is here on his own and is alienated from
the rest, it is clear that in this case one cannot speak of a collective at all. It is
a different matter when the same mass of people gathers somewhere, not by
chance, but being attracted by a particular goal or mood. Here we have a
mass of people united by a single stimulus that leads to the emergence of a
collective, or a composite personality. One should not forget, however, that
under appropriate conditions any chance gathering can easily be transformed
into a composite personality, or a collective. It is enough for someone to
address a chance gathering and, by generating in it a single attitude toward a
particular public matter or fact, to suffuse it with a certain mood or aspiration,
in order to transform a chance gathering into a composite personality, or a
collective, capable of expressing itself as a whole.
This is how a crowd comes into being. Numerous authors both in this
country and in the West—particularly Tarde, Le Bon, Sighele, Mikhailovskii
and many others—made the crowd the object of their investigation.
A simple conglomeration of individuals does not form a society, nor does
interpersonal interaction necessarily ensure unity. On the contrary, interaction
can lead to hostility, division and even to the complete rupture of all bonds,
as happens in time of war, for example. Thus, collective unity can be
achieved by such forms of interaction that bring individuals together, rather
than divide them.
When collective unity evolves together with the emergence of society, the
question arises whether we are dealing with something real or with a mere
name. A number of authors (without taking into account the old
representatives of the organic school)—Durkheim, Gumplowiez, de Roberti,
Espinas, Fouillée, Gierke and many others—give a rather positive answer to
this question. Durkheim says that "there is a society above the individual. It is
not an imaginary and nominal entity, but a system of actual forces" (The
Method of Sociology).
Tarde, on the contrary, does not believe in the reality of society. "I
confess," says this author,
I find it very difficult to understand how it can possibly happen that after
dispensing with individuals we shall still be left with society. If we
dispense with professors, I cannot clearly imagine what will be left of a
university, except its name that has no meaning if nobody knows it in the
context of all the traditions associated with it. Are we not going back to
the realism of the Middle Ages? (Social Logic, St. Petersburg, 1901, p. 2)
Some other authors, for example, Duprat, closely approximate this view.
Among the Russian authors, it is supported to a certain degree by Lavrov,
Mikhailovskii, and Kareyev. This Russian school, while adopting the
subjective point of view, put forward the importance of personality as the
highest ethical value, and recognized it, according to Mikhailovskii, as true
social individuality. P. Sorokin analyzes this question in detail in his book
System of Sociology (vol. 1, p. 243 ff.). First of all he disproves realism by
saying that "a specific object cannot exist outside and independent of its
elements." On the other hand, expressions like "the soul of society," "the soul
of the nation," "the national spirit," etc., are in constant use. But does this
mean that there is a "public consciousness" or a "soul of society" that exist
independently of individual consciousness or outside individual souls? The
answer is provided by the method of subtraction. Subtract from this collective
"soul of society" the "souls" of all the individuals that make up the society,
subtract from the "public consciousness" the consciousness of all its past and
present members—and there will be nothing left.
Giddings, too, speaks out against the "self-consciousness" of society,
which attributes to the latter a process of thinking that differs from individual
mentality.3
On the other hand, P. Sorokin rebels against Tarde and refuses to accept
his postulation that there is nothing in society that would not be present in
individuals. The author perceives "society as the sum total of interacting
individuals," and claims that this interaction is precisely the new condition
that transforms a sum of individuals into collective unity. In this case, there
appear "a number of attributes, phenomena and processes, which are not—
and can never be— present in a sum of isolated individuals." Society,
however, exists not "outside" and "independently" of individuals, but only as
a system of interacting units. We have already mentioned that interaction is
not unity and that hostile interaction results in division rather than union. We
can describe interaction as the essence of the social process, but not as a
prerequisite for generating the unity on which society is based. It is true that
every society is beset by strife, but as long as this strife does not cause one
side to break up with the other, unity can still be maintained in some respects.
Consequently, one can conceive of a society where two parties confront each
other within the same collective, but one cannot conceive of a unity wherein
one side is completely cut off from the other, their mode of interaction being
a fight to the death.
Some authors (Tarde, Le Bon, Sighele, Mikhailovskii, Rezanov and others)
tend to extend the concept of the "crowd," or the "psychological crowd," to
all types of human gatherings held together one way or another.
The description of various collectives by one common name, "crowd," as
practiced by the above authors, is unfortunate, as it contradicts the established
terms and related concepts. Thus, for example, A.S. Rezanov, the author of
the article, "The Army and the Crowd," says, "the psychological crowd may
be represented by a herd, a meeting, an army regiment, a theatre, a congress,
a parliament, etc."
I would like to mention here that Tarde's division of the crowd into the
male, the female, the young and the old, as well as—according to its
orientation towards the surrounding reality—into the expecting, the curious,
the attentive, the enchanted, the manifesting, the loving, the hating, the idle,
the sad, etc., does not hold any water either, for, excluding gender and age,
the same collective can successively undergo transformations into all of the
above types of crowd within a few hours and without losing any basic
principles of its organization.
As a result of describing any collective by this general term "crowd," we
end up in such a muddle that it is extremely difficult to get out of it at all.
Thus, for example, the same Tarde accords a special place to parliamentary
assemblies, for, in his opinion, they are "complex and contradictory crowds—
two-headed crowds, so to speak, just as there are two-headed freaks of
nature." On the other hand, the same author believes that academies "are
collectives of individuals who mutually influence one another, rather than
corporations based on cooperation." Furthermore, a theatre audience is, in
Tarde's view, "not a permanent crowd, but only half a crowd due to the
sedentary position of its individual members."
In general, despite all his achievements, Tarde is rather confused when it
comes to definitions, and it would be a mistake to follow him in this regard.
We are not going to discuss other authors' classifications of collectives. I
shall only say that I basically disagree on this matter with Tarde, Le Bon,
Sighele, and other authors. Besides, one can find critiques of their
classifications in the works of more recent authors (see, for example, A.S.
Rezanov's "The Army and the Crowd"). I think it more correct to describe
individual collectives by using terms that would indicate the distinct nature of
various collectives.
In this regard, we can describe a crowd proper as a gathering of people
brought together by a temporary cause.
This crowd may not be sufficiently organized if there is no established
order for the purpose of coordinating its actions. The crowd may become
organized when there evolves a more or less strict subordination to one
person as its leader, or even to several individuals who form a presidium, as
in the case of political gatherings, meetings, etc. The leaders of a street crowd
remain in charge only temporarily, for a change of leadership may occur even
while a crowd is engaged in one and the same action. Occasionally, a crowd
might commence something under the influence of one leader and conclude it
under the influence of another. The truth is that every leader gets ahead in a
crowd by using its unity under a particular slogan.
Clearly, the very acceptance of a leader indicates the active role of the
crowd that asserts, as it were, its choice of leader.
In other cases a gathering of people takes place with a particular purpose;
this purpose, however, predetermines the passive role of the gathering itself
that may listen to, or watch, something, while expressing its attitude to what
it is shown or forced to hear by signs of approval or disapproval at the most.
We describe this type of gathering that has no internal organization, but is
organized in terms of its purpose as the public that one sees at the theatre,
concerts, lectures, literary soirées, temples of prayer, etc.
A third category also includes gatherings with definite purpose, but these
gatherings, instead of displaying a passive attitude toward the purpose that
has brought them together, reveal their attitude to the subject matter in the
form of a conclusion or a resolution, a sentence or a decree; this enables
individual members of such gatherings to declare their own opinions, for
which purpose a certain order of discussion through elected representatives is
established. We call such gatherings assemblies. They include parliaments,
church councils, congresses, various societies, institutions, councils,
committees, commissions, circles, courts, etc. Every assembly possesses
some distinct characteristics of external and internal nature. Further, we
should mention collectives that are engaged in a coordinated activity: e.g.,
small enterprises, cooperatives, factories, monasteries, financial institutions,
etc.
We can thus distinguish three forms of a composite personality that differ
in their inner content: an unorganized collective (a street crowd) and two
types of organized collectives represented by an audience on the one hand,
and by a workers' cooperative on the other.
However, there are also some special forms of collectives that do not fit
into the above framework. One of them is based on the instruction of the
young by their elders in kindergartens, educational establishments,
workshops, etc., and can be described by the general name, a school.
Another type of collective evolves as a result of training people to act as a
whole collective. It includes troops, gymnastic societies, dance groups,
orchestras, choirs, public games, organized crime, etc. Such gatherings can be
described as organized hordes.
Yet another form of collective is an association of people who are related
through blood or spiritual kinship, even though they may be separated by
distance. This category includes family, clan, tribe, nation, estate, caste, sect,
party, class, profession, readership, church, etc. Such collectives can be
described as tribal alliances in the first case, and as corporate ones in the
second.
Finally, there are collectives unified only through more or less common
views, even in the absence of close contacts. Such a collective may be
described as a world or a milieu. Here we are dealing with collectives that
possess their own more or less commonly shared opinions and even
traditions, and that therefore represent a real, albeit physically elusive,
collective responsible for so-called public opinion. Among such collectives
one can mention the literary world, the military world, the medical world, the
pedagogical world, and the universal human world that unites all of the
above. Finally, large and complex collectives unified through the highest,
common, historically established power structure with certain prerogatives
and organization are called states.
We should therefore distinguish the following types of collectives: the
crowd, the audience, assemblies, workers' cooperatives, the school, organized
hordes, tribal and corporate alliances, the world and the state. A more
detailed description of all these collectives would at this point be superfluous,
since what has already been said is sufficient for understanding their
differences.
The essence of any collective is its unity; as we have seen from the above,
this may not depend on the degree of this unity, which may vary in character.
Thus, unity may find its expression in common mood, common observation,
common discussion and common resolution, common views and common
action. Collective organization is one of the important unifying factors. I
disagree with such authors as Espinas who interpret organization as
subordination. There is no doubt that subordination implies organization, but
conceptually the two are not identical. Subordination is an orientation
concerning 
power, 
whereas 
organization 
is 
the 
establishment 
of
interconnection and order.
Due to this interconnection and order, every collective is a composite
organism, as it were, with its own history, for under certain conditions it is
conceived, grows, develops and then, after a more or less long or short life, it
disintegrates and dies, often transforming into other forms of collectives. A
collective as a composite organism has its own mood, its own powers of
observation and perception, its views, judgment, and actions, which by their
nature are often at odds with the same manifestations of individual
personalities that make up the collective.
In conclusion, we should say that the collective as a composite organism
exists and functions at the expense of the individual organism, since the
personality is always, to a greater or lesser degree, suppressed by a crowd, an
assembly, a corporation and by any public or state organization; the more
united the collective is, the less room it leaves for individual expression.
Thus, the suppression of a personality is at its highest in disciplined troops,
and seems to be at its lowest in scientific assemblies, since the issues of
science are not subject to collective decision-making by vote.
Like a living organism, society represents a dynamic equilibrium rather
than something static; it continuously moves—whether slowly or rapidly—in
one direction or another. And Tarde has some reason to claim that "societies
live as long as they progress."4
At the same time, society generates such social phenomena as language,
abstract concepts, religion, traditions, codes, etc.
However, the notion of society as an organism is nothing other than a
metaphor, for, as we have mentioned before, most authors no longer
subscribe to this theory.
According to Tarde,
the metaphor of the organic society—discussed in great detail in the
second volume of Herbert Spencer's writings, accepted only provisionally
and rejected subsequently— was taken up again, analyzed and apparently
corroborated by René Worms in his doctoral thesis, Organism and Society.
By taking this proposition to its extremes, and to the summwn of its clarity
and precision, as well as by reporting faithfully every criticism that was
levelled at it, he—like Novikov in his highly interesting book Social
Consciousness and Will—helps to rid science of it.5
In general, any society, being a composite personality, possesses its own
individuality that depends on the intellectual level of its members, their
occupation and career status, on the particular "cement" that unites individual
members into an association, and, finally, it depends on tribal characteristics.
It is apparent from the above that, depending on their common unifying
goal, various public and national assemblies generate different composite
personalities.
Irrespective of their form and membership, assemblies that constitute the
object of investigation for collective reflexology are invariably engaged in a
joint activity, without which they are a mere gathering of individuals who
have nothing in common.
Thus, a composite personality can be represented by any crowd
whatsoever, by a public assembly, a council meeting, a theatre audience, etc.
—in other words, by any kind of gathering united by a certain mood, a
particular goal or action, irrespective of whether they are determined by a
chance happening, or whether the goal or the action are prescribed by law or
established by tradition or by other means.
A vivid example of a composite personality is a street crowd brought
together by chance.
Any crowd at all represents a gathering of people who had previously
nothing in common with one another, and who have come together on a
chance occasion that provokes the same response in many persons. Due to the
unity of its members, a crowd always represents a single whole, a composite
personality in which individuals seem to retire to the background. This
cohesiveness is not achieved immediately, but nearly always with
extraordinary speed.
At political meetings, besides the external causes that provoke a particular
response, the very subject of discussion functions as a unifying agent.
Therefore, although the content of speeches made at such meetings may vary,
the subject of discussion is of interest to all. This generates the unifying
slogans supported by many, and often maintains the common mood. Those
who do not join the rest at these gatherings, and are strongly opposed to the
latter, usually remain passive or leave the meeting, and consequently do not
interfere with the general course of events at such gatherings.
In public, scientific, and professional assemblies, the joint intellectual
activity is determined by a particular goal: e.g., by the need to analyze a
situation, to search for truth or to regulate governmental procedures. Public
and political associations represent assemblies whose goal is predetermined
and specified in a written code that imposes certain demands on the persons
who are part of this alliance or association. The goal of commercial
assemblies is the success of the undertakings that brought the assembly into
existence, etc.
The limits of "composite personality" may certainly be expanded to
incorporate entire nations and countries whose population is united by
common interests. We can therefore speak of Moscow society, Caucasian
society, Polish society, Russian society or the Russian public. In such large
social units, cohesion is achieved predominantly through the press and via
interaction between separate groups; this does not, however, affect the heart
of the matter, since here, as everywhere, we deal with larger or smaller social
groups cemented by certain common interests or by a particular goal.
A nation represents an even more extended social group.
One might imagine that a nation presents such a motley image that it is
hard even to speak about its unity. There is no doubt, however, that at any
given moment in time a nation lives by common interests, experiences a more
or less common mood, feels the same emotions, is stimulated by more or less
similar social ideals, and lives by the same social aspirations, while
generating one common culture. In any given nation, this unity of spiritual
life evolves historically due to a number of economic, political, and
ethnographic conditions, and is determined by cultural unity, by the unity of
socioeconomic interests, and by sharing the same historical destiny in the
course of communal existence.
Finally, the most extended group is represented by the whole of civilized
humanity, whose interests are common to the entire population of the globe,
and who follow the universal human guiding principles.
One may undoubtedly ask the question whether such large groups as
populations of countries or states, or even the whole of civilized mankind,
can be the subject matter of collective reflexology. Here we deal with
associations that, although united by the same interests, are deprived of any
means of mutual communication except the press.
Obviously, the conditions that exist in ordinary gatherings cannot exist
here, and, consequently, not everything that is related to the associations and
gatherings dominated by verbal discourse can be applied here. Nevertheless,
there is no doubt that these large associations represent groups that display a
certain public mood revealed in the press as well as in individual assemblies.
Likewise, through the press and individual assemblies, these large groups
discuss and criticize various events, and pass the resolutions that represent
composite decisions of numerous groups or individual societies of a given
country or state. Thus, in this case, too, we are dealing with the subject matter
of collective reflexology, although this subject matter differs somewhat from
that of ordinary gatherings.
If the state is an association of people bound by certain rules, then one can
apply to it the same laws as one applies to smaller gatherings. This may
easily be demonstrated in smaller states with homogeneous populations
untouched by tribal hostilities. When the represents a conglomerate of
nations, it is the tribal groups', which live by the same interests, that function
as collectives proper; however, when it comes to the solution of general
issues that are relevant to the whole heterogeneous population of such states,
we are dealing with the subject matter of collective reflexology.
Among other things, a characteristic feature of large groups is the slow
pace of all the processes of associative activity, for, in general, huge masses
of people are more conservative; besides, the means of unifying their activity
are more complex (the press, separate assemblies, etc.), although, with
sophisticated forms of self-government and with freedom of assembly, the
cohesiveness of such masses grows faster than it does under different
conditions.
Nevertheless, here, too, we encounter a mass rise in the mood of the active
elements of society; their oppression; public indignation, outrage and
protests; the collective mentality of entire nationalities as it is reflected in the
daily press and literature, as well as in the solutions to public issues; and,
finally, we encounter active manifestations expressed in declarations or
demands of individual groups that may or may not be supported by the
representatives of the whole nation.
Thus, all the elements of collective correlative activity are present and
should, therefore, serve as the material for collective reflexology.
We all know, however, that there are forms of social activity that involve
auditory and visual stimulation, which provoke a vigorous response in the
listeners and viewers who are not meant to take an active part in these
gatherings. The latter include public readings, concerts, theatres, shows, etc.
Here we deal with a collective impression and with the emergence of a
certain mood in a human mass, just as in an individual life a book may
generate a number of impressions and produce a particular mood in the
person who is reading or being read to. A question naturally arises whether
these phenomena belong to the subject matter of collective reflexology.
The above gatherings involve an audience united by a spectacle, and
although communication between the members is here only indirect, it is easy
to see that the cohesiveness of a theatre audience watching a play or listening
to a concert is established due to the universal impact of the spectacle or the
concert, and due to the stimulation of the same responses. Therefore, even
though listeners and viewers are, in these cases, passive participants of such
gatherings, they are actually united through one common interest, display
similar reactions, are stimulated by more or less identical influences, and,
finally, they collectively declare the same attitude to what is taking place on
stage by the signs of approval or disapproval. In view of the above, there can
be no doubt that these gatherings, too, provide material for collective
reflexology.
A public reading with debates represents a gathering that not only
perceives but also reasons, and, consequently, displays not only its common
mood but also its collective intellect.
The gatherings that make certain decisions based on what has been read
and discussed manifest not only collective concentration and collective mood
but also collective intellect in the form of collective resolutions.
As we know, every individual in a composite personality becomes more or
less inconspicuous in a mass, unless he represents the opinions of a particular
group of people or is the leader of the whole gathering.
However, the society, or the collective, while always acting as a composite
whole, does not eliminate the role of each constituent unit, since the latter
affects the resultant force that determines the movement of the collective. The
stronger the social impact of a personality is, the greater is its influence on
the direction of the resultant force. This may continue until the personality
and the collective come into conflict, which may lead either to mutual
concessions and a reconciliation or to a rupture. Incidentally, this is how
public figures are replaced in the course of a revolution, when the collective
moves spontaneously in a particular direction that no longer satisfies the
aspirations of the leaders; as a result of this, the latter gradually lose ground,
and are forced to step aside. However, this phenomenon can be observed not
only during revolutions but also in ordinary life; in such cases the conflict is
generated not only by the collective itself but also by certain circumstances
associated with the personality of the leader. This point of view reconciles
various discordant opinions on this matter found in literature.
It is clear from the above that, in order to influence a crowd or a collective,
one should deal first with its leaders, and it is only with their consent and
cooperation that one can influence the crowd in the desired direction.
As we have already mentioned, every collective possesses its own
individuality reflected in the distinct nature of its aspirations that,
incidentally, change in the course of time.
According to Tarde,
Some nations imbued with moral and religious aspirations are essentially
peace-loving, and take up arms very unwillingly, whereas others display a
true military fanaticism in the interests of their religious or moral
proselytizing, or, to put it better, one and the same society, while
remaining faithful to its aspirations in a particular direction, may in turn be
engaged in war or in production, in militarism or in industry. Egypt, which
was traditionally mystical and industrial, became from time to time—as
during the Ramses dynasty—militant and expansionist, while losing none
of its mysticism. Presently peaceful Islam used to be profoundly militant.
Athenians as a predominantly aesthetically minded nation had for a long
time been fond of war from an artistic point of view, so to speak; later on,
while still remaining artists, they, unfortunately, became too fond of peace
at all costs. There has never been a more trading and, at present, a more
peace-loving nation than Holland; in the XVII century it was the most
bellicose of nations.6
If we now pose the question of what exactly leads to the development and
establishment of a collective, we should first mention the social instinct that
causes even animals of the same species to lead a herd-like existence.
People of more or less similar views naturally come together, and, in the
same manner, people with similar individualities and temperaments
experience mutual attraction. It goes without saying that belonging to the
same profession generates a sufficient number of common interests that bring
people closer together.
Another factor that stimulates establishment of collectives is the mutual
need of individual persons—irrespective of their dissimilarities—for one
another, as well as Darwin's natural selection that conditions the need to join
forces for the purposes of attack and defense, since only those individuals
