benefit who prefer to join a group for the above purposes, whereas the rest
are doomed to perish. As the third stimulus, I have postulated social
selection, which consists of public support for those members who, even
though they may be physically weak, conform to collective interests. This
promotes the development and cultivation of social tendencies in individual
person and maintains the social cohesiveness of a collective, which is passed
on to the next generation via social heredity.7
We would like to point out that every society survives for as long as it is
justified by the existing conditions. In this respect one should bear in mind
Aristotle's law of the self-sufficiency of any political association.8
Regarding its associative activity, a collective cannot be identified with a
simple gathering of individual persons; it always possesses an integrated
physiognomy that represents the collective as a whole. A street crowd
demonstrates this best of all.
Le Bon says in The Psychology of Socialism (pp. 51-52):
The psychology of a crowd is not at all the same as the psychology of the
individual elements that make it up... The soul of the crowd generates
aspirations as ideas that are born in the upper strata of the nation, undergo
a number of arbitrary evolutions, eventually take root in the soul of the
crowd and lead there a secret, vegetal and powerful life.
Then and only then do they become conventional opinions and almost
unshakable convictions, the major factors of religion, revolutions, reforms
and civilization. A crowd is the inherited soil in which all our metaphysical,
religious, political and social perceptions of the world are rooted, and we
should know it very well.
A crowd as a collective is characterized by "monoideism," by considerable
suggestibility, by an extraordinary excitability that often turns into cruelty, by
gullibility, extreme recklessness, reflex actions, by lack of steadfastness, by
striking changeability, relatively small differentiation and by herd-like
submissiveness to its leader resulting from its hypertrophied suggestibility
and predilections for imitation:
Among the characteristic attributes of a crowd we come across such
features as impulsiveness, irritability, inability to think through, an
absence of reasoning and critical thinking, hypertrophied sensitivity, etc.
that are typical of the lower forms of evolution such as women, children
and savages (The Psychology of Nations and Masses, p. 173).
A crowd easily becomes an executioner, and just as easily it chooses
martyrdom. In order for some faith to triumph, it lets rivers ofblood flow
from its own body. (Ibid., p. 175)
Its sheer numbers make a crowd confident in its own power and impunity.
According to Le Bon, a crowd's suggestibility and gullibility are due to its
expectant attention and its lack of critical thinking.
In this connection, habitual distortions intrinsic to human imagination may
explain the origins of legends, while contagion plays a part in their
dissemination.
The following example from Le Bon's Psychology of Nations and Masses
(p. 181) illustrates the gullibility of a crowd: some time ago,
all the newspapers were bursting with stories about two drowned little
girls pulled out of the Seine. At least a dozen witnesses recognized the
children in the most categorical terms. All their testimonies were so
concordant that the coroner could entertain no doubts and filled out the
death certificate, but just as the supposed victims were to be buried, they
were found to be alive, their appearance being only slightly reminiscent of
that of the drowned.
The testimony of the first witness apparently generated the same illusion in
others, and then spread to quite a number of witnesses.
In another case, even the child's mother was prey to this kind of
suggestibility. This is what Eclair reported on 25 April 1895:
A child mistakenly identified a dead body as that of his playmate. This
mistake immediately caused quite a few similar mistakes including the
following remarkable episode: one woman who saw the corpse of the
child exclaimed: "Oh dear, it is my child!" On closer examination she
noticed a scar on his forehead and said: "Oh yes, this is my poor little son
who disappeared in July. He was kidnapped and killed." The woman was a
concierge from Rue Dufour. When her son-in-law appeared on the scene
he declared without hesitation: "This is little Philibert." Several inhabitants
of this street also recognized Philibert in the dead child, and even his
school teacher, on seeing the medal, recognized his former pupil in the
dead boy. Well, what do you know! The neighbors, the son-in-law, the
school teacher, as well as the mother—they were all mistaken. Six weeks
later the identity of the child was definitively established: he was from
Bordeaux, was murdered there and brought to Paris in a stage coach.
The excessive suggestibility of a crowd is clear from the following
example:
In one national theatre where a serious drama was regularly performed "an
actor who played the role of a traitor was in constant danger and had to be
protected, for the spectators, infuriated by his fictional crimes, were ready
to tear him apart "9
In his Psychology of Nations and Masses, G. Le Bon examines in
succession the following aspects of a crowd: (1) impulsiveness,
changeability, and irritability; (2) pliability, suggestibility, and gullibility; (3)
exaggeration and emotional bias; (4) intolerance, authoritarian and
conservative ideas, reasoning, imagination, the influence of race, traditions,
education, and upbringing. There is no need, however, to continue quoting
this extremely subtle observer lest the reader falls under the spell of his ideas,
which are not always completely acceptable.
Numerous authors mention cruelty as a characteristic attribute of the
crowd, perceived by them as a dangerous many-headed beast. Sighele, for
example, says in no uncertain terms that
heroism, valor and kindness can be characteristic of an individual person;
but they can never, or hardly ever, appear as the distinguishing features of
a large gathering. ...A crowd is a substratum in which the germ of evil
spreads very easily, while the germ of good nearly always dies for lack of
the necessary conditions for survival.
The same view is held by Tarde, according to whom, the soul of the crowd
is "murder, robbery or arson."
In his well-known work Origines de la France contemporaine (vol. 2,
Anarchy) Taine says (with respect to the crowd during the French
Revolution) that a crowd's mode of action is violence, which is quite in
keeping with its nature: "everything that offers resistance to a crowd is
destroyed by it."
A crowd that gathers by chance in response to an appeal or an alarm is an
awesome and destructive force. An individual who joins a crowd is suddenly
transformed into a barbarian or worse—into a prehistoric beast, a grimacing
ape, bloodthirsty and lustful.
Sluchevskii, too, is close to this view when he speaks of "high" excitability
and cruelty displayed in action" as the general attributes of a crowd.10
A thirst for revenge makes a crowd particularly violent if the resistance it
meets results in the loss of lives on the part of the crowd. When it gains the
upper hand, no one can tell where the limits of its blind and unrestrained fury
will be.
One can cite as an example the destruction of the cadets (who were
supporting the Provisional Government) on 28 October 1917 in Petrograd
during the second revolution. The newspaper descriptions of this event were
so depressing that we do not dare to reproduce them here.
Without denying the facts on which the above interpretation of the crowd
is based, we must point out its one-sidedness, for the attitude of a crowd is
determined by its mood. In some cases, a crowd acts as a cruel beast, while in
others it is capable of worshipping and defending its idol to the last, and is
ready to die for his sake.
In this respect, G. Le Bon is closer to the truth than other authors, when he
says that "there exists not only a criminal crowd but also a virtuous crowd, a
heroic crowd and many others." When discussing further the destructive
power of a crowd, he comments that only a crowd is capable of manifesting
"the greatest selflessness and the greatest devotion."
And, indeed, there are countless examples of such devotion, including the
self-sacrifice of many when their leader is in mortal danger. All revolutions
have been fought by crowds that at times had to face certain death in order to
defend the rights of the people.
In Le Bon's words (I.e., p. 52), psychologists always saw in a crowd only
"a wild beast never sated with plunder and blood." However, if we look into
this question more carefully, we shall soon discover that the worst instances
of crowd behavior were often motivated by very noble and selfless ideas, and
that in the final analysis a crowd is a victim rather than an executioner.
A book entitled The Virtuous Crowd would be incomparably more
appropriate than a book entitled The Criminal Crowd. According to the
author, the main underlying distinction between an individual and a crowd is
that the former is always motivated by personal interests, whereas a crowd
obeys shared, rather than unstable and selfish, interests:
Heroism and self-abnegation motivate crowds far more frequently than
they do an individual person; beneath collective cruelty one most often
finds a certain conviction, a concept of justice, a need for moral
satisfaction, a total disregard for personal interests, and self-sacrifice for
the sake of common interests—in a word, the exact opposite of egotism.
However, a crowd as such has no proclivity towards either crime or virtue:
it can generate both, depending on the cause that brought it together and on
the direction into which its aspirations are channeled by its leaders; a hungry
crowd is as egotistic as any individual who demands a piece of bread, while a
political crowd crushes everything in the path of the social principles of the
new order it has adopted, and eulogizes its heroes. That is why G. Le Bon is
wrong when he says (I.e., p. 53) that "Egotism—so deeply rooted in an
individual personality—is totally alien to a crowd, precisely because of its
inability to either contemplate or rationalize."
A crowd is not devoid of the self-preservation instinct, and, consequently,
neither is it devoid of egotistic aspirations. One thing is true: when we deal
with a criminal crowd, the motives of the perpetrated crime often lie in the
correctly or falsely interpreted common weal, whereas individual crime is
fairly seldom socially motivated: here egoistic aspirations are far more
common as the source of motivation.
In this respect, a crowd seems to have a leveling effect on individual
persons who, being unable to lead, have no opportunity for self-expression
save, perhaps, by casting their ballots.
Each individual person may be rational, but in a crowd he is often deprived
of the opportunity to protest; at worst he gives in to the influence of the
crowd, participates in its actions and even in crime.
It goes without saying that the noble aspects of the individual personality
in a crowd, including its leaders, cannot find favorable conditions for their
expression, and, consequently, a crowd functions on a far lower level than
many of its members.
Besides, one should bear in mind that an individual person in a crowd
invariably conforms in a greater or lesser degree to the collective view
inculcated in the crowd by the demagogical methods of its leaders.
I happened to talk to some army officers who survived the Russian
Revolution and who knew their detachments well. All of them claimed that
when they would talk to an individual soldier it was easy to convince him to
adopt a sensible view of things, but as soon as this soldier found himself in a
crowd at a political meeting, he would accept the collective slogan together
with the resolution that had been drafted in advance, and would express his
agreement by shouting "Right! Right!"
In this case, the collective suppresses a personality not only through the
power of suggestion applied by its leaders but also because the collective is
stronger and more influential than any individual personality. An individual
would never dare to accomplish what a collective would. In folk reflexology
this attitude is expressed in the well-known saying, "two in distress make
sorrow less."
Irrespective of the above, any collective whatsoever suppresses with its
sheer mass all individual self-expression.
If the mood of the majority or its attitude to a particular fact has become
apparent, few people would be brave enough to go against the stream. Even
meetings that are specifically convened to discuss a certain subject, and
where discussion is the purpose of the meeting, an individual may find it
difficult to speak out against the opinion of the majority after this opinion has
been established. It is even more difficult to do this in a street crowd, where
there is no discussion, but only a leader who holds the crowd in almost blind
obedience, particularly if he has previously won its favor.
The more cohesive and homogeneous a collective is, the greater its moral
power and its activity are, and vice versa. The truth of the former is
exemplified by communist parties that have always been known for their
strict discipline. The opposite case is represented by the Socialist-
Revolutionary Party that played an extremely important role in the February
coup d'état in Russia. However, when after this coup a mass of new members
(who essentially had little in common with socialism) joined the party, the
latter—despite the enormous number of members—could no longer play an
appropriate role either in the Democratic Assembly or in the Preliminary
Parliament. In the end, a perfectly natural split occurred in this party,
whereby one group assumed a more rightist position, while the other—the
more leftist one.
Another example is the Moscow Assembly that proved to be fruitless
precisely because, being convened at the most critical moment of the Russian
Revolution, it had to face two public movements: the census group and the
democratic group. It was counteracted by the Democratic Assembly in
Petrograd which adopted a far more radical point of view, but again failed to
succeed due to the heterogeneity of its membership. As we know, this
Assembly ended in an indeterminate vote on the distribution of power:
whether to include the census supporters in the coalition or not. It was clear
that neither Assembly possessed the moral power, and that the Provisional
Government could not follow their legislations.
The role of the Preliminary Parliament, from which the Bolshevik faction
immediately split off, proved to be equally fruitless at the time of the Russian
Revolution. Obviously, there are moments when a collective is unable to
come to any decision, when party proposals are rejected one after another,
and no common decision can be agreed upon—in other words, no single
general formula can be worked out. Such a situation may develop
predominantly during periods of difficult social crises and public dissent.
This is what happened in the Soviet of the Republic, where the debates on
state defense generated so much discord between the parties that none of the
proposed resolutions gathered a majority of votes in the assembly.
In this case, assemblies themselves undergo a crisis due to the
fragmentation of votes and become ineffective. They are either sabotaged by
one particular party that wants to cause their downfall, or they make suicidal
decisions as was the case with the Convention Nationale during the Great
French Revolution. To illustrate this we shall quote from the well-known
historian H. Taine, who thus describes the crisis of the Convention:
They approve and prescribe what they themselves find reprehensible—not
only absurdities and madness but also crime and the murder of the
innocent. Unanimously and to the sound of thundering applause, the left
joins forces with the right, and sends to the scaffold Danton, its natural
leader, the great organizer and standard-bearer of the Revolution.
Unanimously and, again, to the clamor of applause, the right joins forces
with the left to vote for the worst decrees of the revolutionary government.
Unanimously and to the accompaniment of enthusiastic cheering and open
sympathy for Collot d'Herbois, Couthon, and Robespierre, the Convention,
through arbitrary and multiple re-elections, maintains a homicidal
government which is hated by some for its murders and by others for
trying to annihilate them. The Plain and the Mountain, the majority and
the minority, ended up by agreeing to cooperate in their own suicide.11
It goes without saying that greater homogeneity enables an assembly to
arrive at more clear-cut decisions and to exercise a greater moral power,
which affects both the majority group and its opponents: the former find in
the assembly the most vivid reflection of their own aspirations, while the
latter must respect its decision as the most clear-cut and definitive one that
dots the i's and crosses the t's.
During the most critical periods of human history, half-way policies fail to
gain any moral power, while neutrality of behavior breeds discontent and
condemnation—and occasionally even resentment— not only in one
particular group but in all; as a rule, half-way policies prove fatal for their
practitioners.
One can cite as examples the role of the Girondins during the Great French
Revolution, the role of the Constitutional Democrats in the first Russian
Revolution, and the role of VIKZheV12 in the second, October, Revolution.
Although the VIKZheVs neutrality was to the advantage of the Bolsheviks,
neither the Bolsheviks nor the more moderate elements were happy with its
role.
In the final analysis, the actions of a collective are determined by its
composition, level of development, and dominant mood.
As for the composition of a collective, this concept must include national
characteristics interpreted not so much as the natural peculiarities in the
organization of its members, but rather as the conditions of social heredity
inherited from their ancestors via social traditions, views, customs and,
finally, the moral dimensions of the environment in which the collective is
formed. But, above everything else, the actions of a collective are determined
by its dominant mood, which is conditioned by the given circumstances.
Finally, the last determining factor of a collective action is a cause that acts as
a challenge.
Thus, the behavior of a collective expressed through its actions, gestures,
and declarations depends on the totality of the above conditions, and in this
respect it should be recognized as natural as individual behavior.
Notes
1. E. de Roberti, The New Formulation of Issues in Sociology (in Russian), Sytin Publishing House.
2. See P. Sorokin, "The Boundaries and Subject of Sociology" (in Russian), in New Ideas in Sociology,
Issue 1.
3. Giddings, The Elements, p. 119.
4. Tarde, La logique sociale (in Russian), p. 171.
5. Ibid., p. 153.
6. G. Tarde, La logique sociale (in Russian), p. 246.
7. V. Bekhterev, "Social selection and its biological significance," Vestnikpsikhologii, 1912. "The
Importance of Hormonism and Social Selection in the evolution of organisms," Priroda, October
1916 (in Russian).
8. The essence of this law, known as antarchy, is as follows: every political association is viable only as
long as it satisfies the needs of its members for whose sake it has been created (more about this
elsewhere).
9. G. LeBon, The Psychology of Nations and Masses, p. 202.
0. Sluchevskii, "The crowd and its psychology" (in Russian), Knizhki nedeli, 1893, Nos. 4 and 5.
1. H. Taine, Us Origines de la France Contemporaine, vol. 7, pp. 284-285.
2. The abbreviation of the All-Russian Executive Committee of the Railway Employees' Union (ed.).
6
On Collectivity
Natural sciences have not yet offered an adequate interpretation of sociality—
the tendency to form collectives—in the animal world. The best-known
theory in this respect is that of natural selection, according to which
individuals who live in societies have certain advantages over those who do
not, and therefore the former survive, while the latter die out.
However, this theory is insufficiently comprehensive, for it applies only to
those animals who lead a social life on a permanent basis—following an
inborn instinct, as it were—e.g., bees, ants, termites, etc. It cannot explain the
existence of temporary social groups, although we do know that social groups
in the animal kingdom are often formed on a seasonal basis and as a result of
particular living conditions—e.g., for the purposes of attack and defense.
When these conditions undergo changes, social groups, too, cease existing
until other circumstances arise and force new alliances into being.
In my view, the above theory should be supplemented with the theory of
social selection.1 The latter secures the survival of a newly formed
community by supporting its most socially oriented members—even those
who are physically weak—by providing for them appropriate living
conditions and better protection, which to a large degree ensures their
survival in defiance of natural selection.
We should point out that some authors (e.g., McDougall and others), who
find the concept of natural selection insufficient, put forward a theory of
social instinct, which interprets the latter as the motivating factor in adopting
a communal existence. Yet, if we consider the above-mentioned association
between communal living in the animal world on the one hand and the
seasonal variations and certain living conditions on the other, it will become
clear that instinct alone cannot explain sociality, nor can it explain some
symbiotic phenomena.
Generally speaking, the concept of instinct has undoubtedly been abused,
for it is always brought in when there is no other suitable explanation. In any
case, if a social instinct does exist, it should be decoded. In the meantime,
associative reflex theory can offer quite a satisfactory explanation of
communal living.
We know that various external conditions force individuals to congregate
in certain locations—e.g., in places that offer protection from cold and
inclement weather, or plentiful nourishment—which, naturally, promote
close contact and interaction. This closeness and interaction, repeated many
times and accompanied by a positive mimico-somatic (emotional) reaction,
soon becomes a need reinforced by the appropriate response during
encounters with other individuals under different circumstances. This
generates mutual attraction among creatures of the same species. Even
different species who live together definitely become attracted to one another
due to the above condition. Observe a dog that meets its owner by displaying
signs of affection. Its animation and visible affection reproduce a mimico-
somatic reaction that occurred previously. This reaction, which undoubtedly
quickens vital processes, is certainly beneficial for the animal, which is why
it seeks contact with its owner, maintaining and enhancing thereby its
attachment to him.
It is therefore clear that sociality, or the tendency to form associations, is a
result of individual experience; it is based on the emergence of positive
mimico-somatic reflexes invariably associated with an approach reaction
resulting in closer contact.
The same holds true for human society:
It has long been known that similar individuals become attracted to one
another. We feel close to those who share our language, worldview and
ideals, and who look like ourselves—in short, to those who are similar to
us in external appearance and inner qualities. This similarity acts as a
force that attracts socially similar individuals to one another. Children
seek the company of other children like themselves. Adults seek the
company of other adults like themselves; the attraction between two
Europeans is far greater than between a European and an Asian or an
African. People of the same social status understand one another better
than individuals who belong to different social classes, groups, estates,
professions, etc.2
In general, this thesis is advocated by a number of authors. As we have
already mentioned, McDougall explains it as a manifestation of the herd
instinct. According to him,
the herd instinct of an animal is satisfied only in the presence of similar
creatures, and the greater this similarity the greater the satisfaction. One
observes exactly the same phenomenon among humans: similar elements
come together in a society precisely because we do not feel equally
attracted to all social aggregates, and our herd impulse is best satisfied in
the company of the people who are most similar to ourselves. Uncultured
nations usually live in tribal communities, with tribes closely related
through blood ties inhabiting adjacent territories....The same tendency was
at work in the United States, where immigrants from various European
countries formed their own large communities within certain territorial
limits. It is manifested in our big cities in the segregation of people
belonging to similar race, occupation and social standing.3
The same phenomenon is mentioned by Durkheim (The Division of Social
Labour (in Russian), p. 83 ff.), Waxweiler (Esquisse, pp. 16-57), Pareto
(Trattoto di sociologia generate, Vol. 1, pp. 11151133), Giddings (Principles
of Sociology [in Russian], pp. 19-20), and others.
Another unifying factor is represented by individual differences
("opposites attract"), but only of the kind when the qualities and
idiosyncrasies of one individual complement those of another individual. In
this case, it is mutual need that acts as a unifying impulse. What one
individual possesses can benefit another person, and, conversely, what the
latter has to offer may prove useful to the former. Here the connecting
element is the principle of division of labor, which makes itself felt not only
in public affairs but also, as we know, in industry. Individual insufficiency in
a particular sphere provides an impulse, as it were, for an individual to seek
compensation through another person.
It is easy to see that in both cases the decisive motive for establishing
contact is its social benefit. In the first case, this benefit results from higher
productivity achieved through the combined efforts of similar forces; in the
second case, the same benefit is achieved via mutual compensation of
insufficiency in two or more individuals on the basis of the division of labor.
The benefit is the end result of coming together, the primary impulse being
the favorable mimico-somatic reaction in mutual encounters, which has been
discussed above.
As we know, P. Kropotkin (Mutual Assistance as a Factor of Evolution)
regarded mutual assistance as the basis of associations. P. Sorokin, however,
believes that mutual assistance is only one of the unifying factors. When
elaborating on his objections, he says that animals, as well as humans, come
in contact not only for mutual assistance but also
for mutual or unilateral aggression and for causing harm. A lion looks for
prey and makes contact with it; parasites interact with the organism they
live on, and this interaction may last for long periods of time. People, too,
communicate and interact with one another not only for the sake of mutual
assistance but also for the purpose of mutual annihilation and oppression
(wars, fights, the interaction between the capitalist and the worker,
between the slave-owner and the slave, between the oppressors and the
oppressed, etc.)4
All of this is true with respect to contact and interaction, but unity and
association are completely out of the question when it comes to fighting and
wars. For one should emphasize that interaction and unity are not all identical
concepts.
"Ideological antagonism," says P. Sorokin (I.e., p. 311),
frequently leads to more acute forms of antagonistic interaction, which
bring individuals together in a lasting antagonistic collective unity. The
simplest example of this kind of phenomenon is an argument between two
opponents that ends up in a fight, or a squabble between members of
different parties in a parliament. Other examples of extensive and lasting
collective unities generated and maintained by the antagonism of ideas and
convictions include the antagonistic collective unity (interaction) of the
Nikonians and the Schismatics in Russia, their long struggle accompanied
by the imprisonment and exile of the Schismatics. Another and even more
extensive and prolonged interaction caused and maintained by antagonistic
ideas and beliefs was the collective unity of Catholics and heretics
persecuted by the former (the interaction between the "Holy Inquisition"
and the Waldensians, Albigensians, Cathars and others as manifested in
wars, persecutions, burnings of heretics, etc.). Religious disagreements
and wars locked opponents into a lasting collective unity (e.g., the struggle
between Catholicism and Protestantism). (I.e., pp. 311-312)
P. Sorokin elaborates this point in a footnote to this text: "I emphasize that
from my non-normative point of view a group of interacting Schismatics and
Nikonians represents a collective unity, a real sum total and an association."
It is clear from the above that, on the one hand, Sorokin substitutes the
term interaction for the term unity, implying thereby that these concepts are
of the same order in conventional use. On the other hand, he interprets
collective unity as an actual sum total of individual members, as an
antagonistic association—consequently, as something that has nothing in
common with what we mean by the "spiritual" unity of a crowd or any other
gathering.
It goes without saying that individual behavior in this kind of antagonistic
interaction is governed by causality, and one cannot but agree with Sorokin
that these interactions should become an object of study for sociology as well
as for social reflexology.
We should, nevertheless, distinguish between collective unity, on the one
hand, and antagonism between two or more interacting individuals, on the
other, for the nature and consequences of these relationships are radically
different: it is mutual assistance in one case, and struggle and mutual
annihilation in the other. The statement that "mutual attraction can be caused
not only by altruistic motives but also by hostile ones" is undoubtedly true.
This does not imply, however, that both harmony and antagonism fit into the
concept of "unity." By removing this confusion we shall free ourselves from
the ambiguity it naturally engenders in various discussions of collective
manifestations.
Further, there is no doubt that antagonistic and extremely hostile groups
may form a cohesive unity, but this is achieved by subordinating one group to
another by force, as well as by enforcing punitive measures for separatism
and rewards for good performance of appropriate undertakings. In general,
unity is often established on the basis of collective subordination that
gradually becomes habitual and no longer incites protest in the subordinated
party as it did initially. In other instances, a prolonged struggle exhausts the
energy of the opponents, who make peace for this reason and subsequently
coexist in solidarity. Here we are dealing with natural internal inhibition,
whereas in the first case one group is subjected to external inhibition.
Nevertheless, there exists in both cases a social relationship between the two
groups, which in time may lead to a more or less real, or internal, unity
generated either by a seeming fictive unity or by an enforced unity.
Thus, unity can be of two kinds—internal unity, or unity in the true sense
of the word, and external unity imposed by force or through necessity.
The first may develop from common interests, social similarities, and the
division of labor, when the groups that are part of a given unity do not appear
self-sufficient; the second kind emerges from the confrontation between the
strong and the weak and from the oppression of the latter.
A crowd presupposes a direct association of a predominantly emotional—
or, objectively speaking, a mimico-somatic nature. Therefore, the more
homogeneous individuals are, in terms of their associative activity, the sooner
this amalgamation occurs and the faster a crowd is moved into action. An
assembly expected to solve a complicated problem and united by this
common task, on the contrary, often benefits from the presence of
heterogeneous individuals, since the objective here is not so much the speed
of problem solving as the quality of the solution itself, which is usually of a
multifaceted nature. Therefore, in an assembly, where one finds
representatives of different schools and movements as well as divergence of
opinion, debates proceed in a more animated fashion compared to a society
where everyone more or less agrees with everyone else.
In any case, every collective presupposes the existence of common
interests in one or another respect, which is precisely what maintains the
unity among the members of a collective.5 Some collectives—as, for
example, a crowd—are united by a common mood generated by a certain
event; other collectives, such as a theatre audience, are united by a
performance that arouses particular emotions; a third type, such as trade
unions, are united through a common occupation; a fourth type—e.g.,
financial and other cooperatives, syndicates, trusts, etc.—are bound together
by various economic interests; a fifth type of collectives, such as religious
communities, are united through religious goals; a sixth type of collectives
are united by shared political views (e.g., political parties) or by a common
danger (e.g., military collectives). In short, a collective as such is
inconceivable without some kind of group unity.
So what conditions promote collective unity?
Discourse in the broad sense of the term—i.e., the spoken and printed, or
written, word—is undoubtedly the most important and the most convenient
means of communication for exchange of opinions in human society.
Unquestionably, the spoken word that enables us to engage in personal
exchange of opinion, accompanied by such powerful stimulants of emotions
as gestures and expressive movements, is the most important means of
exchange and communication between people. Its influence, however, is
severely restricted by certain spatial conditions. Nevertheless, in some cases,
the spoken word may function as a connecting link, not only in interpersonal
communication within a given large group, but also in contacts between
various social groups, for the spoken word can establish communication even
between spatially separated groups of people through intermediaries, envoys,
or delegates.
This method of mediated verbal communication, commonly used in olden
days and occasionally practiced today (e.g., when sending representatives
from one group to another), is significantly inferior in practical terms to the
written or printed word. For although the latter lacks such important
concomitant elements as gestures and expressive movements, it can evoke
them in the reader, and its main advantage is the ability to travel across vast
distances.
However, discourse is not the only mediator in social-group cohesion.
Expressive movements and gestures undoubtedly play a significant role in
this respect. By acting directly on a person and by stimulative imitation, the
language of gestures may sometimes have greater communicative power than
discourse: one can observe this in an excited crowd.
It is also beyond any doubt that there are forms of social-group cohesion in
which action rather than words functions as a cohesive agent and produces an
affective state, as, for example, in an audience watching a theatrical
performance. An analogous phenomenon occurs in places of prayer and
occasionally in a crowd, where action often stimulates imitation.
The cohesive factor in professional groups is to a certain degree
represented by the uniform activity pursued by the majority of the group.
Complex coordinated forms of movement, such as gymnastics performed
by many individuals or choir singing that produces a coordinated vocal
effect, are in themselves important means of social cohesion. An equally
important cohesive function is performed by those complex forms of
movement in which the division of various roles (as in the theatre) or of labor
(as in industry) are in pursuit of a particular goal: to convey basic meaning in
the first case and to achieve a joint result in the second.
Evidently, there are thousands of other forms of movements, or stimuli,
that help to establish interaction between two and more individuals. When
this interaction proves to be useful, it naturally brings the interacting
individuals together. If it happens to be harmful for one side or both sides, it
leads to separation or repulsion. This kind of interaction can be maintained
only by some form of coercion. Consequently, when the latter is abandoned,
the union naturally disintegrates. Examples from international relations are
Russia and Finland, Russia and Poland, England and Ireland, etc.
Among the stimuli that promote interaction and often cohesion there are
objects that may possess an emblematic and, occasionally, symbolic
significance. These include various dress codes and insignia that indicate
one's belonging to a particular level of bureaucracy, to a particular circle of
people, etc. Other objects of material culture such as monuments, palaces,
temples, even household utensils and furnishings—in short, all objects of
material culture and everything produced by human creativity—function as
mediators of interaction and unity. They
are the true intermediaries between people: for some they provide an
opportunity to record the results of their labor and actions, for others—
whom they affect as stimuli— they make it possible to perceive these
results and respond to them. From this point of view, human mediators
who transmit stimulation from one person to another may be regarded as
being of the same order as material mediators. Their difference from the
latter lies in their ability to move. One can therefore describe them as
dynamic-material conductors.6
There is hardly any need to add that the mediating role of people is far
more significant than that of mere "dynamic-material conductors." Letting
alone the unsuitable term "conductors" applied to a stimulus, one must say
that human language as an interpersonal mediating agent is far more valuable
than man's role as a moving object. When describing people as points of
contact in a chain of conductors, P. Sorokin gives some vivid examples of the
mediating role of people in the sphere of interaction (I.e., pp. 142 ff.).
Stimulation generated by a particular individual is first transmitted by one
conductor, then it is picked up by another conductor, then by the third and so
on, until it reaches the addressee.
One should not overlook the fact that in these cases the very effect of the
stimulus depends to a significant degree on the personality that functions as a
conductor—on his past experience, views, and predilections, as a result of
which each conductor perceives and interprets material stimuli in his "own"
unique way. This is how the actual fact of stimulation may become distorted
beyond all recognition. Such distortions explain the genealogy of rumors and
legends that sometimes acquire a truly fantastic and even monstrous
character.
Among all commodities, the most active stimulant of interpersonal
cohesion and interaction is undoubtedly money as an established measure of
value attributed to various objects. It may replace them when necessary or
restore them through purchase. P. Sorokin is right, however, when he
emphasizes the special significance of relics belonging to the family, the
church, or the state:
a family relic, such as a lock of Mother's hair, is a sacred object: by
passing it from generation to generation they pass on a stimulus that will
speak of the family glory and honor, will stir guilt in the soul of an
undeserving descendant and pride in the soul of a deserving one; in short,
this lock of hair is a true conductor that links the past with the present, and
the present with the future. The same mutato mutandis may be applied to
all other material conductors.7, 8
Interpersonal communication and interaction can be immediate and
mediated. When people communicate without any mediator, by using their
organs of movement on the one hand and their organs of perception on the
other, one can speak of immediate interaction and communication. All other
cases involve mediated interaction and communication established through
letters, telegrams, sign language, colors, or other objects. Acoustic
equipment, such as the telephone, musical instruments, etc., is just as
important in mediating interaction and communication. Tactile mediators
include the alphabet for the blind, various forms of touching, kisses and other
caresses, sexual intercourse, beatings and other kinds of mechanical impact,
thermal and electrical stimulation, etc. Olfactory mediators include various
aromatic substances, while gustatory ones—foodstuffs and other edible
substances.
It is noteworthy that mediators can establish contacts not only between
individuals separated by vast distances but also between different epochs.
Parchments, monuments of antiquity and archeo-logical finds provide our
link with the ancients and even with prehistoric people! In the same way
monuments of art, architecture, and engineering can mediate interaction and
communication between people of different nations and epochs.
Mediation expands the limits of interaction and cohesion to an incredible
degree: "I am interacting with a friend who lives in America," P. Sorokin
writes in The System of Sociology:
Today I received a letter from him; this stimulus made me perform a
number of actions: I wrote a reply, went to buy abook he needed and
visited a post office. Besides, his letter made me terribly happy. In short,
my friend who lives in America has affected in a particular way my
emotions and actions. As we know, there is an infinite number of such
events happening every hour. Scientists, journalists, newspapermen,
industrialists, heads of states, etc. separated by enormous distances interact
and communicate with one another. We know from our everyday life that
people can participate in the process of interaction irrespective of the time
that separates them. A caretaker paid me a visit yesterday and, since I was
out at the time, left a message with my servant requesting me to provide
proof of my residence permit. Today I have spent almost all day attending
to this business. Hence, the caretaker determined my behavior and
emotions, even though I did not see him and came home several hours
after his visit.
Furthermore, there may be interaction between the living and the dead.
Although Spencer and Marx are no longer alive, they continue to influence
many of our contemporaries, and this influence is not likely to cease in the
near future. Through their works that have reached us in the form of
books, they stimulate a number of emotional responses and actions in
many persons. The will of a deceased Mr. X generates a number of
emotional responses and actions in many individuals—his heirs,
executors, notaries, judges, faculty members of the university to which he
bequeathed his library, charges of the asylum to which he left some
money, etc.
It is clear from the above that in fact neither space nor time are obstacles
to human interaction* due to the involvement of the various mediators P.
Sorokin calls "conductors" (a rather unfortunate term from our point of view).
A letter, a book, a telegram, a will, an ancient statue as a relic of antiquity,
various symbolic signs, etc.— all these are mediators of interaction rather
than its conductors, the latter referring to environment in general and two
certain types of energy in particular: e.g., that of sound, electricity, etc.
Three main organs of perception—touch, vision, and hearing-play an
important role in unifying popular masses into a single social group.
According to Guyau, "touch is the most primitive as well as the surest
means to achieve communication, harmony and socialization between two
nervous systems, two consciences and two lives."9
Tarde, too, recognizes the importance of mutual contact or friction
("collision," to use Sidis's term) in a crowd, which has an electrifying effect.
The unifying significance of touch is corroborated by a state of mutual love
whose manifestation inevitably demands mutual contact. The latter has a
stimulating effect on those preparing for a dangerous attack, it encourages
defense in moments of uncertainty, and provides comfort when misfortune or
calamity strikes. On the other hand, mutual contact facilitates coordination of
movement, as in walking, running, holding hands, dancing and acrobatic
exercises. It is clear from the above that the denser a crowd, the easier its
unity evolves, which is manifested in uniform movements and shouts. While
providing relative freedom of movement, a standing position is more
conducive to closer physical contact and promotes a greater cohesiveness
among the masses.
Vision is another unifying factor. According to Guyau,
the perception of movement or sentiment in others evokes an echo within
ourselves. Here the principle of imitation is particularly apparent. The
affective facial expression of one individual generates the same affective
state in another person. In some cases a gesture by the leader may produce
a magical effect.
Finally, everybody is familiar with the contagious influence of movements.
How many dancers can practice self-restraint at the sight of someone in the
crowd performing an exciting dance?
We all know that the rhythmical marching of troops carries in its wake
gawkers and street urchins who march along with the same speed and rhythm
of movement. And, finally, the movements of a concert-master, as we know,
coordinate the performance of a whole orchestra or choir.
In the animal world, too, movement provides guidance for the whole herd,
as certain movements of the leader are imitated by other members of the herd.
It is also common knowledge that members of the same family may pass on
to each other characteristic speech patterns, gestures, handwriting, habits, etc.
Finally, movement attracts and focuses one's attention. Observe a young
kitten concentrating on and watching every movement of a folded piece of
paper. He becomes completely indifferent to the same piece of paper as soon
as it stops moving.
According to Morgan, newborn birdlings "instinctively grab a worm not
because it is a worm but because it is a small moving object," for, to quote
the same author, "if one moves food before newborn birdlings, they are
quicker to notice it."
One can deduce from the above that the movements, gestures, facial
expressions and actions of a crowd's leaders and heroes in some cases
stimulate concentration and thereby increase collective impressionability,
while in other cases they provoke blind imitation.
The power of contagion in a crowd is well known. An individual who
attracts the wrath of a crowd might be spared if the person standing next to
him would not strike him. This serves as a signal for the rest to fall upon him,
and the lynch mob has its victim.
Hearing plays an even greater role in uniting popular masses. First of all, it
is the ability to hear that makes uniformity of movement possible. One need
mention only the role of music in marches, dances, and gymnastic exercises
involving large numbers of people. Singing performs the same function in
workmen's teams.
The unifying role of music is also evident in gatherings involved in mere
listening to music without actively participating in the performance. When a
musician plays a composition by Musorgskii or Skriabin, the physical nature
of this process can be reduced to the following phenomena: the performer's
fingers, by moving the bow against the strings of the violin, cause their
vibration, which through the airwaves reaches the organ of hearing, where it
generates vibrations ui the form of a neural wave that arrives at the auricular
regions on the cerebral cortex. The latter sends waves back to the periphery
and to the internal organs, which stimulates a certain mimico-so-matic
response and a number of other mental reactions. It is precisely these neural
waves generated in the listeners by the same mediating agent that unite a
mass of individuals listening to a musician. They receive the same charge of
physical energy, and although its effects may vary from one person to the
next due to individual differences, the fact that their mimico-somatic
responses have a common denominator promotes general unity and
interaction among the listeners, as well as between the audience and the
composers who died a long time ago.
Human voice is the most significant means of stimulating concentration. A
whisper in a complete silence is enough to rivet everybody's eyes on its
source.
Even in a noisy crowd a scream or an exclamation will attract general
attention and increase the impressionability of the crowd.
On the other hand, voice stimulates imitation by having the same effect as
contagion. We all know how difficult it is to resist joining a sing-along.
When a crowd's emotions are on the rise, shouts and slogans produce a
contagious effect and are repeated by all or most of those present.
The contagious influence of the voice on a crowd and its unifying power
appear to be particularly important. In the leader's voice, intonation alone
plays a significant role in terms of its impact on a crowd.
As a unifying factor, however, discourse is far more influential. I have
discussed its suggestive function at length in my book, Suggestion and Its
Role in Social Life (St. Petersburg, 3rd edition).10-11 We know that in human
society it is primarily through discourse that gatherings are organized. On the
other hand, the so-called inflammatory speeches delivered at these gatherings
by the leaders produce not only a stimulating effect on the crowd but a
unifying one as well, for it is the general mood that functions as a unifying
factor. The role of discourse in this aspect will be discussed later.
In addition, the process of organizing the masses, which leads to their unity
and to the proper coordination of their activity, occurs through the mediation
of the spoken or written word.
All of the above makes it clear why communal existence, work, and
activity, as well as collectively experienced sorrows and joys, promote by
their very nature the unity of a collective. The enormous importance of
schools, institutions, communal residences, etc., in this regard is universally
recognized.
One should emphasize that collective experiences—especially those of
adversity—have a particularly powerful unifying effect on a collective.
Generally speaking, shared misfortune unites people better than shared
prosperity.
It is evident from the above that a true collective, i.e., a united whole, is
such a collective where the influence of some members on others results in
their unity in one respect or another.
Notes
1. See V. Bekhterev, "Social selection and its biological significance" (in Russian), Vestnikznaniia,
1912. Also by the same author "Hormonism and social selection" (in Russian), Priroda, 1914.
2. P. Sorokin, The System of Sociology (in Russian), part 1, p. 777.
3. McDougall, The Main Issues in Social Psychology (in Russian), Moscow, 1916, pp. 220 ff. [In
English—Introduction to Social Psychology, London, Methuen, 1908.]
4. P. Sorokin, System of Sociology (in Russian), vol. 1, p. 283.
5. According to Tocqueville, "no society can prosper or even merely exist without common beliefs, for
without common ideas there is no common action. People can exist without common action, but a
social organism cannot."
6. P. Sorokin, System of Sociology (in Russian), vol. 1, p. 139.
7. P. Sorokin, The System of Sociology, vol. 1, pp. 140-141.
8. The System of Sociology, vol. 1, pp. 116-117.
9. Guyau, L'art du point de vue sociologique, Paris, Alcan, 1923, p. 3.
0. It is available in German: Suggestion und seine Rolle in Sozialleben, Wiessbaden.
1. Editor's note: It is now available in English: Suggestion and Its Role in Social Life (L.H. Strickland,
ed.), New Brunswick, NJ: Transaction Publishers.
7
Mutual Suggestion, Mutual Imitation,
and Mutual Induction as Unifying
Factors
Among the factors of a unifying nature, we should examine in greater detail
mutual suggestion, mutual imitation, and mutual induction. As far as mutual
suggestion is concerned, I have discussed it in a separate work.1 Therefore, I
am not going to dwell on this subject now, but shall return to it later in this
book.
Imitation has been extensively discussed by such authors as Mikhailovskii,
Tarde, Rossi, Le Bon, Gardner, Sighele, Sidis, myself, Vigouroux, Juquelier
and others.
The general conclusion common to all of these studies is that the mere
gathering of individuals into a crowd is conducive to mutual contagion,
which is precisely what leads to collective action.
According to Binet, who conducted some experimental studies with
children, "the very fact of bringing children together in one group makes
them more susceptible to suggestion."2
According to Vigouroux and Juquelier, "the mere perception of yawning
induces the repetition of this act in others. Yawning is the most contagious
reflex...We happened to witness a passenger in an omnibus start a veritable
epidemic of yawning."3
Our experiments on so-called mental suggestion4 prove that, besides
contagion, there also exists direct induction.
In this case we are dealing with those forms of impact within a crowd
where individual persons act as mutual resonators, as it were.
As we know, every movement that does not provoke defensive
counteraction stimulates imitation, which in itself unites the masses. There is
something spontaneously contagious in movements that are more or less
familiar and that we find easy to perform. Such, for example, are the
movements of yawning, laughter, dancing, etc.
Tarde thoroughly examines the phenomenon of imitation in his works. He
interprets the term not only as individual imitation in the form of so-called
"mental" contagion, but expands it to include social classes and nations. The
heart of the matter lies in the unifying significance of interrelationships
established in a collective, for the latter cannot be an integral whole unless its
individual members accept the general characteristics of the collective, while,
on the other hand, individual achievements are recognized as beneficial to all
and become collective achievements.
This is precisely the source of the "leveling" effect produced by any more
or less organized collective, whether it represents a professional society, a
nation, or a state.
We are dealing here with the extension of individual achievements to the
whole collective through their appropriation and assimilation by other
members of the same collective. This appropriation and assimilation occurs
through simple induction, imitation, suggestion, and persuasion.
Thus, the above factors play an enormous role in the social environment,
for it is due to them that interpersonal relations and collective unity can be
established, or, in other words, it is due to them that a human collective, or a
composite personality, can come into existence. Out of these factors, the
earliest to develop (with the exception of induction) is the ability to imitate,
which is also characteristic of a large class of animals, as we know. One can
observe it in newborn infants: it probably helps them to establish their first
social contacts. The child's first imitative sound, his first smile in response to
the mother's smile or his imitation of a movement once observed indicate the
beginnings of sociality in the first weeks and months of his life.
In general, imitation should be understood in a broader sense than the one
we are used to. Imitation may depend on reproduction, in which case it can
be viewed from a historical perspective.
Cicero's manuscript on the republic is discovered 2,000 years after it was
written, it is reprinted, it provides inspiration; this posthumous imitation
could not have occurred if the molecules of the parchment had stopped
existing and vibrating, and if human reproduction from Cicero to our days
had been disrupted.
Reproduction is a free oscillation whose waves form individual worlds.
Imitation goes even further: its action is transmitted not only over very
large distances but also over enormous intervals of time. It establishes a
relationship pregnant with consequences between the inventor and the
imitator, separated from each other by thousands of years—between
Licurgus and a member of Convent Nationale, between a Roman artist
who painted a fresco on the walls of Pompeii and the modern painters
inspired by it. Imitation is long-distance reproduction.5
It is clear from the above that imitation can become a cause of epidemics
and uprisings when a revolutionary movement spreads from city to city, or it
may cause a reproduction of historical events. Was it not imitation that
revived the ancient Olympic games, javelin throwing and marathon running,
rhythmic gymnastics and the ancient prosodic forms such as the hexameter?
Tarde, who interprets the whole of social life in terms of imitation, thus
describes its significance:
The laws formulated by Malthus and Darwin regarding the geometric
progression of individual reproduction within a given species are the true
laws of radiating reproduction of living individuals. In the same manner—
due to imitation—a local dialect used by a few families is gradually
transformed into a national language. In prehistoric societies, the art of
chiseling stones, taming dogs, making arrows and bows, and, somewhat
later, of baking bread, producing bronze and iron, etc.—must have spread
through contagious imitation. Each arrow, each piece of bread, each
bronze hook and each chiseled stone represented both acopy and a model.
This is how dissemination of diverse useful information propagates
nowadays, too, the only difference being that the increased population
density and the overall progress that has occurred in the course of time
accelerate this dissemination in a most amazing manner, just as the
velocity of sound is directly proportional to the density of the medium.6
The special significance of imitation as the outstanding factor of social life,
so brilliantly analyzed by Tarde in his book, is beyond any doubt, but one
should not forget that society consists not only of imitators but also of
creators—hence the existence of competing individuals. Imitation, therefore,
does not always achieve its goal because of interpersonal competition. Thus,
the life of the social organism consists of imitation and competition, or
rivalry, which interact in a particular way that leads to a dynamic equilibrium.
We have mentioned before that models for imitation are provided by
persons in a position of power. Thus, children imitate parents, pupils imitate
teachers, the younger imitate the older, etc. The same holds true for a
collective environment, where fashion always originates with the upper
classes and then trickles down to the lower classes. The reverse tendency is
certainly not out of the question, but it invariably occurs on a lesser scale and
only in certain periods, as well as under special circumstances. Such is, for
example, the preaching of the simple life that gains popularity from time to
time with its emphasis on simple dress, plain food, hard work, etc.
Usually, the "plebeians" imitate the "patricians," rather than vice versa; the
village imitates the city, and country dwellers imitate city dwellers more
readily than the latter imitate the former; the vanquished imitate the victors,
and those who love imitate their loved ones; the aristocracy imitates the
monarch or the president and the courtiers, rather than vice versa, etc. A
language of high literary merits and rich phraseology—particularly if it
happens to be the language of a victorious nation—spreads through imitation
among the uncultured nation to a greater extent than the language of a less
cultured nation may spread among the highly civilized nations.
According to Tarde,
all similarities of social origin observed in the social world represent a
direct or indirect consequence of imitation in its diverse manifestations:
imitation-tradition, imitation-fashion, imitation-attraction or obedience,
imitation-education orupbringing, blind imitation or conscious imitation,
etc.7
Naturally, Tarde recognizes language as the "great transmitter" of all these
varieties of imitation.
Language itself is imitation. The origins of the European languages in
Sanscrit indicate boundless imitation, where each deviation or transformation
represents the discovery or invention of an unknown author, which, in turn,
stimulates imitation:
The extraordinary proliferation of all kinds of fashion—in dress, food,
housing, needs, ideas, institutions and arts—results in the transformation
of the entire European population into the kind of people who remind one
of a typeset edition printed in millions of copies.8
One should not forget, however, that imitation in a collective encounters
certain inhibiting circumstances that represent obstacles arising from the life
experience of individual members of the collective. Indeed, if the Russians
are imitating the Germans, the English, and the French, it is clear that their
imitation is confined to those phenomena that do not contradict their life-style
and is in their interests and of benefit to them at this particular point in time.
On the other hand, everything that goes against their life-style and interests
encounters natural inhibitions and is rejected. The same holds true for the
Japanese who imitate the West-European countries and Russia. Thus,
imitation is to a certain degree restricted by the collective inhibitions
determined by life experience and life-style, as well as by resistance to
everything that disagrees with national tastes, customs, and established
traditions.
As we know, imitation is inhibited by individual experience; therefore,
imitation is always more prominent in children than in adults. The same
phenomenon is evident in collectives. Younger collectives follow the
example of older ones; younger nations imitate older nations to a greater
extent than vice versa. Thus, Russians in many ways imitate the culturally
older Western countries that borrow from the Russians on a far lesser scale, if
at all.
As a nation that entered the arena of public life after the Russians, the
Japanese imitate to a significant degree both the West-Europeans and the
Russians, whereas the latter two imitate the Japanese hardly at all.
One should bear in mind that imitation is an important aspect of progress,
for any forward movement of society, as well as of individuals, is based on
the imitation of improvement wherever it occurs. The achieved improvement
assimilated by a collective or an individual leads to further improvement,
which is transmitted through imitation to a third collective or individual and
results in further improvement.
In addition, imitation reduces and facilitates the imitator's effort. When
someone achieves improvement through one's personal experience, those
who imitate him do not have to go through the same experience: they
assimilate the result, while omitting all preliminary work. Besides, imitation
occurs at all stages of development and improvement, so that an undertaking
begun by one person can be passed on to another individual while still in its
early formative stage. This leaves room for the original contributions to the
project, which yet again may prove fruitful.
In social life, the law of imitation is manifested in the fact that no invention
ever remains in the exclusive possession of one individual, but sooner or later
becomes the common property of a collective. This is why alongside national
differentiation there occurs—albeit gradually—a leveling process among all
nations as a token of their unity, for no nation created its culture single-
handedly, but borrowed some of its elements from other nations. This
borrowing through direct or indirect imitation is inevitable for any collective
and any nation, however unique its creative potential might be. One may only
say that a nation imitated by others most often is more original in its
creativity, compared to the nations that are not as readily imitated. On the
other hand, one may say that a nation capable of borrowing the best from
other nations has advantages over other nations. Naturally, everything
original affects others one way or another, stimulating their desire to borrow
and imitate. In fact, dissemination of myths, fairytales, legends, certain forms
of art, scientific discoveries and inventions is based on borrowing and
imitation. Myth borrowing that results in a wide dissemination of myths is
particularly remarkable.
Similarly, national art forms are essentially borrowed from other, older,
forms of art, and are, in turn, borrowed by other nations.
Science is also subject to the law of social leveling. However impressive
the development of science in some countries may be, its achievements
inevitably tend to spread wherever conditions are favorable. Quite often we
are dealing with simple borrowing, but scientific creativity as such does not
remain isolated either and tends to spread—again through borrowing—which
brings about a certain degree of social equalization.
The same is true for technology and industry.
If one nation achieves a certain level of technological and industrial
progress, the benefits of this progress necessarily spread among other nations,
equalizing them to a greater or lesser degree in the utilization of the achieved
improvements.
It goes without saying that this tendency towards leveling may unfold
faster or slower, depending on the conditions of the social environment and
other circumstances, but no collective remains in isolation, since all nations,
societies, and circles are in contact with one another. Although local
conditions, a greater or lesser geographic isolation, as well as routine—
typical of all societies as a manifestation of the law of inertia—offer a certain
resistance to this process of leveling, the latter occurs wherever it encounters
favorable conditions.
Thus, in ancient history the Romans appropriated the highest
manifestations of culture from the Greeks, while the barbarians they
conquered borrowed culture from the Romans and subsequently became so
successful in the martial arts that they were able to break the resistance of the
Roman army.
In modern times, Japan as a naval power quickly adopted the benefits of
European culture and within a short time became one of the civilized
countries of the East.
Any invention capable of improving human existence and of providing
mankind with such material benefits, such as agriculture, the domestication
of animals. The invention of gun-powder, book-printing, the discovery of
steam power, the knowledge of electricity, etc., as well as all the
achievements of the human genius in the spheres of literature, art, and
science, after making their appearance in a particular place, are quickly
appropriated by the masses and become universally accepted.
As a result, every civilization represents a product of appropriations by the
human genius in various spheres, adopted by a given nation that, in turn,
makes its own creative contribution to civilization.
It is worth mentioning that borrowing yields permanent results only when
it proceeds from the general to the particular, for one nation borrows from
another first the established principles and only then the institutions that
embody these principles. Thus, Renaissance in Western Europe was preceded
by the preliminary study of Greek literature; familiarity with the works of the
English thinkers paved the way for the adoption of English institutions by
other countries; and the popularity of the French political doctrines prepared
the ground for the revolutions in other countries.
On the other hand, if minds are not sufficiently prepared for particular
principles, then the institutions introduced simultaneously with these
principles or shortly thereafter will prove unstable. This is why
parliamentarism failed in Turkey before the Great War. It also explains the
instability of the parliamentary system in South American republics. For the
same reason, borrowings in every nation are usually disseminated first among
the higher or the educated classes, gradually spreading to the lower ones,
since the former are quicker to adopt abstract principles and new ideas
developed in other countries and to transplant them into their native soil.
Besides, due to special circumstances, it is easier for the higher classes of
society to become familiar with the innovations introduced in other countries.
Let us take fashion. It is first adopted by the higher classes and then
gradually spreads to the lower ones, after which the former discard it in order
to borrow and adopt a new fashion. Here one also observes the influence of
the dominant class that enjoys a certain prestige and authority among the
populace and stimulates imitation among the other classes.
In a large collective, nationalism is a manifestation of its unity, and
everything that sustains national characteristics and traditions in the life of a
given nation also sustains its unity. Even the components of class—these
guardians of caste differences—support the nationalism that manifests the
unity of a given nation, although by their very nature they tend to isolate
themselves from other collective groups that belong to the same nation.
When discussing imitation, our main concern is not the fact that it is
determined by sociality or that social dynamism is an imitational dynamism,
but why it is so. What is the reason for two creatures or two groups of
creatures to begin imitating each other?
According to Bordier, every human being is predisposed to imitation, but
this ability reaches its apogee in human gatherings; to prove this point it
suffices to mention public assemblies where an applause or a whistle is
enough to stimulate the audience in one direction or another.9
While recognizing imitation as an inborn ability, Sighele notes that not
only does it double but it increases a hundredfold in a crowd, where
everyone's imagination is easily stimulated and where the unity of time and
space in a remarkable way—with the speed of a lightning—facilitates the
exchange of impressions and emotions.10
Solly, too, speaks of a predisposition to imitation.11 The essence of this
predisposition, however, remains unclear.
Tarde, as well as a number of other authors, reduces social contagion to the
phenomena of hypnotic suggestion.
When discussing the influence of one personality on another, which
underlies social life and determines imitation—a fact already known to
Espinas and even Cabanis—Tarde writes extensively on hypnotic influence,
trying to convince the reader with the help of several examples and
comparisons that this influence does not essentially differ from a hypnotic
one and is similar to somnambulant state induced by hypnosis. "Imagine a
somnambulist," writes the author on page 86,
who imitates his medium to such a degree that he himself becomes a
medium for a third person who, in turn, imitates the somnambulist, etc. Is
not this what social life is all about? Such a cascade of consecutive,
mutually linked magnetizations is the general rule, while the mutual
magnetization I have just discussed is only an exception. Usually, a
charismatic individual provides an impulse, which is immediately
reflected by thousands of others who copy him in everything and
appropriate his charisma that enables them to affect millions of other
individuals further removed from the center. Only after this impact
(descending from above) has been exhausted can one observe in
democratic times the emergence of new actions: millions of people begin
to manipulate their old mediums and make them obey...It is neither fear
nor the violence of the victor but amazement and resplendence of the
perceived powerful superiority that produce social somnambulism; society
is imitation, and imitation is a kind of hypnotism...
Such is the author's final conclusion.
Had this been a figure of speech, we would not object to it. If we bear in
mind, however, that suggestion in the waking state is a far more widespread
and extensive phenomenon than hypnotic suggestion, it becomes obvious that
the impact of one person on another can never be identified with a hypnotic
impact. Can we agree with the statement that "a social person is a true
somnambulist" and that "a social state, just like a hypnotic state, is nothing
other than a sleep induced by an order—a sleep in an active state"? We
regard this as overstatement typical of this author in general, and of his
interpretation of the "laws of imitation" in particular. Tarde, however, is
followed by Siddis who recognizes that the "self of a crowd consists of
subconscious 
individual 
"selves," 
which 
explains 
the 
heightened
suggestibility of the crowd. Prior to both these authors, our Mikhailovskii—
who is undoubtedly the founder of this hypothesis—discussed this subject in
the same vein.
The above authors are followed by a number of others who treat this issue
from a purely subjective point of view. They see the main condition for
crowd suggestibility in the restriction of free movement, as well as in
narrowed consciousness and monoideism, or mental preoccupation with one
idea. We shall not follow the subjec-tivists, but shall try to discover the
objective conditions of crowd suggestibility. They can be reduced to three
principal conditions: first, confinement to the same position for long periods
of time, which besides restricting active movement leads to physical
exhaustion; second, prolonged concentration on the same subject (usually on
the leader and his speech) undermines one's ability to concentrate; third, the
leader's demagogical methods, accompanied by appropriate gestures and
facial expressions, determine the uniformity of the mood, which, in turn,
defines the direction of the active attitude of the crowd, since a rise in mood
is associated with readiness for action. As we know, under these conditions a
single word or even a gesture that acts as an order may prove sufficient for
the crowd to accomplish a certain act. A fall in mood, on the contrary,
provides a fertile soil for panic, which may sometimes erupt immediately
after a scream or a declaration, no matter how absurd the latter may be.
One should not forget, however, that imitation can be observed not only in
the crowd, but wherever there is human contact of any kind and where
hypnosis is out of the question. Since we know that suggestion also takes
place in the waking state,12 this circumstance should undoubtedly be taken
into consideration and, hence, the hypnosis theory should be regarded as one-
sided at the very least. However, one should not overlook the fact that verbal
suggestion cannot totally explain imitation, for in many cases there is no trace
of verbal suggestion at all. Besides, instances of imitation among animals are
well documented.
Espinas came early to the conclusion that the only reason wasps guard and
protect the entrances to their nest and warn others in case of danger is the
sight of an enraged individual, for there is a "general law in the sphere of
intellectual life according to which the sight of an excited state stimulates the
same state in those who witness it." This law, however, is not new. It is
mentioned by Cabanis,13and Sighele claims that even Horace was familiar
with it.
Here we are dealing with direct imitation, or contagion, in the true sense of
the word, as described by Vigouroux and Juquelier in their work, Le
contagion mentale.
According to Rambasson (Phenomenes nerveux intellectuels et mentaux,
leur transmission par contagion), imitation is based on the fact that for every
"psychic" phenomenon there is a corresponding movement of a cerebral
nature, manifested externally through facial expression, gestures, and posture.
This movement is transmitted in space to other people, stimulating them to
perform the same actions. It is due to this transmission of movement across
distances that laughter, yawning, sadness, and other phenomena—the simple
as well as the complex—occur.14 Here, too, the issue in question is nothing
other than direct contagion, but its cause remains unexplained.
From our point of view, the source of imitation in the philogenetic process
of evolution lies in the competition and confrontation of social life. When one
of a community of animals finds a source of food and attacks it, all the rest
assault the same source of nourishment, reproducing the offensive reflex,
whereas those animals who have not developed this reflex of direct imitation
lose in the battle for nourishment and survival and are destined to die,
according to the law of natural selection. In this case, we are dealing with an
imitative reflex of an offensive nature. In other cases, an animal that
encounters its natural enemy responds to being attacked with an analogous
act of offense or with an act of passive or active defense, since any animal
that would not perform this act would perish, which is why defensive
imitation is vitally important for any living creature that exists under
conditions of rivalry and confrontation. Other defensive imitative responses
in the animal kingdom are of the same origin, for when all means of attack
are exhausted and the only alternative is to run for one's life, any animal who
is less competent in this respect than its neighbors will inevitably perish. The
truth of this observation is corroborated by the mimicry of the lower animals,
who perform the imitative act in relation to inanimate objects. This, in turn,
reveals the vital necessity of the imitative response in general.
Further, one should take into consideration the fact that any imitative act is
facilitated by virtue of its being an act of replication and therefore easily
executed, just as a copy is easier to execute than an act of creation. Since
collective actions always require a certain coordination they are possible only
due to imitation. Hence, a collective experience would be altogether
impossible without imitation. This makes the biological significance of the
ability to imitate particularly obvious.
Is it possible to transmit directly the energy of the neural centers from one
individual to another? To date, there has been no positive answer to this
question, for, as far as I know, all the attempts to find scientific proof of
telepathic transmission across distances—so artistically represented in
Lermontov's poem, "A Dream,"—have not yet yielded any convincing
results.
One should bear in mind that quite a few scientists—mathematicians,
physicists, physiologists, psychologists, neuro-pathologists and psychiatrists
—have tried various approaches to this issue, but all the available data have
not yet provided the final solution to the problem. All the experiments in this
field have so far focused mainly on guessing chosen objects or identifying
their location, on performing a certain action, etc. If this ability to guess is
observed at all, it usually occurs in nervous and rather impressionable
individuals in a hypnoid state—e.g., in mediums who are able to get into a
