trance or a special hypnoid state induced by autosuggestion.
However, such direct effect of one individual on another can also be
observed in the waking state in people of a nervous disposition who are
capable of so-called direct perception.
Incidentally, special experiments in this area were conducted abroad by
Charles Richet, Lehmann, and many others, and in this country by Khvorin,
Zhuk, Kotik, and others. Not all of these experiments are of equal value, and
in any case they have not yielded sufficiently convincing results regarding
transmission of thought over distance. As we know, Richet's experiments
have failed to convince others. Dr. Zhuk's experiments might have been used
to better effect towards a positive solution of this problem. As for Dr. Kotik's
experiments, even though he firmly believes in and defends transmission of
thought over distance, his experiments involving Sophia Shtarker were,
undoubtedly, a result of inadequate caution on his part regarding the tricks of
the subject and her father, as I showed in one of my papers.15 Dr. Kotik's
other experiments suggest some conclusions that could satisfy the most fiery
imagination, but one can discuss them only after they are confirmed by other
authors.
At this stage, we are interested in a simple fact: is direct induction of one
individual by another possible, i.e., can one person influence another without
using signs or any other types of mediation? It seems to me that a question
posed in this manner could be positively resolved on the basis of some
specially designed experiments with dogs, as well as with humans.
I conducted my first experiments with dogs trained by V. Durov in my
own apartment in Petersburg and then in Moscow—both in Durov's
apartment and in his so-called "Little Corner." These experiments were
conducted by me personally, with or without Durov, and in the presence of
several 
assistants 
(Vorobiova, 
Nikonova-Bekhtereva, 
Shchelovanova,
Fleksor, Trirodova-Kazachenko, Professor Feldberg, I. Lev, and others).
These experiments were subsequently continued by my former students
Drs. Perepel and Karmanov, then by Dr. Fleksor together with Dr. Einhorn,
and by Dr. Ivanov-Smolenskii together with Dr. Fleksor. They were carried
out according to the plans I had devised, and were later repeated by me under
different conditions.
The overall number of the experiments conducted with dogs at various
times is between fifty and seventy-five. Three dogs named Lord, Nickie, and
Daisy served as subjects. All these dogs had been previously trained by
Durov to obey ("domestication"), so that during the experiment the dogs
would remain calm and ready to perform the required task. Thus, Lord, a
Saint-Bernard, barked as many times as the experimenter mentally suggested.
The number, however, should not be large (no higher than nine), otherwise
the dog would become confused due to the developing automatism in
barking. I discovered the same ability (to bark as many times as suggested) in
the bulldog, Daisy, with which I conducted some successful experiments on
mental suggestion, or induction, even from another room with closed doors.
A fox-terrier, Nickie, performed some rather complicated actions, mentally
suggested by the experimenter: e.g., she would jump on a chair that stood
near the wall, then jump from this chair onto a little table, stand on her hind
legs and scratch a portrait on the wall; or she would jump on the piano stool
and strike the right side of the keyboard; or run into another room and pick
out one of the many paper balls scattered around the room.
These experiments have been described in detail by me and my former
students (Drs. Ivanov-Smolenskii and Fleksor) and presented at the 1920
Conference at the Institute of Brain Research in a number of papers.16 I
would like to mention at this stage that in the experiments with Nickie and
Daisy every precaution was taken to prevent a situation in which the
experimenter could—albeit unconsciously—provide clues for the animals.
The later experiments were designed so that the assignment was known only
to the experimenter (a doctor) who was separated from the dog, i.e., did not
touch it, but made eye contact only while mentally concentrating on the task
that the dog was supposed to perform; then the experimenter would isolate
himself from the dog by closing the doors behind him, while the actions of
the dog were observed by an assistant who was not familiar with the
assignment. The results were that mental suggestion was followed with a
sufficient degree of accuracy even under these conditions.
The experiments with the bulldog Daisy were designed to test whether the
dog would bark as many times as mentally suggested by the experimenter.
These experiments were conducted in such a way that the suggestion, or
induction, was directed from another room with closed doors, and even so the
results were positive.
As for experiments with people, our first subject was an eighteen-year-old
girl who had never been involved in such experiments before, but who was
highly impressionable and possessed an extremely sharp visual memory (she
could reproduce up to seventy-five words and twenty-five two-digit
numbers), as well as motor memory (she was a fine dancer). The
experimental procedures were as follows: on the table in front of her a
number of most common objects were laid out—e.g., a match, a little box, a
cigarette, a comb, a sea-shell, etc. The number of the objects varied from
seven in some experiments to eight or twelve in others. The experimenters—
myself and Durov—were sitting on two chairs next to each other, with our
backs to the girl, at a distance of about 6.5 metres away from her. With the
exception of the first six experiments we were separated from her by high,
thick screens.
The names of all the objects on the table were written down on small
pieces of paper (tickets) that were folded and put in a hat from which one of
the doctors who observed our experimental procedures would pick one out
without unfolding it and hand it over to me. Having unfolded the ticket, I
would either conduct the experiment myself, without mentioning to anyone
the name of the object, or would pass the ticket on to my neighbor who
would then carry out the experiment. Thus, one of us would concentrate on
the visual image of the object mentioned in the ticket, while the girl was
supposed to indicate which object was being suggested. Here are some of the
results: seven simple objects were laid out on the table; Durov acted as an
experimenter. He conducted six consecutive experiments, all of which were
quite successful, i.e., every object was correctly guessed by the girl, and only
in one case was the object guessed on the second, rather than on the first,
attempt.
In another case, we selected twelve objects as common as those in the
above experiment (a match, a pencil, a hook, a toothbrush, a piece of paper,
an ashtray, a key, a seashell, a cigarette, a box, an electric bulb, and a button).
Durov conducted six new experiments: in the first three he was separated
from the subject by screens in the same way as before, while in the other
three experiments he was separated from the girl by a large metal screen.
During both sets of experiments I was sitting next to him. Folded tickets with
the names of the objects were placed in a hat, then our assistant, A. Lev,
would take one of them and pass it on to me without reading it. After
glancing at the note, I would silently give it to Durov, who would concentrate
on it by intensely visualizing the object and the girl's intention to pick it up.
The results were as follows: the objects selected in the first three experiments
were a box, a piece of paper and a toothbrush; the box and the paper were
guessed at once, while the brush was picked out on second try after an
initially wrong supposition. In the other three experiments, all the objects—a
seashell, a toothbrush, and a key—were guessed correctly immediately.
My next five experiments were equally convincing. The experimental
procedure was the same, the only difference being that after the experiment I
handed the note over to a third party for control. Out of the above five
experiments, two failed and the remaining three proved successful, i.e., the
girl guessed correctly in one case on the first attempt and the other two on the
second.
In other experiments with the same girl, eight objects were placed on the
table, and the experimenter was ten feet away from her in another room,
behind closed doors. Out of the four experiments conducted by Durov two
were successful—with a bulb and a cigarette. The latter was not guessed at
once: after the girl asked "Is it a bulb?" and received a negative reply she
stated with certainty "Then it is a cigarette." Out of my two experiments one
proved successful and the other did not. I must say that in these experiments
we avoided repeating those attempts that initially failed. Thus, approximately
half of the experiments on suggestion from another room with closed doors
yielded positive results, which cannot be explained either by the theory of
probability or by anything else.
Interestingly, when the girl was asked why she would pick out a particular
object during the experiments, she explained that she visualized very clearly
the object in question; on another occasion she said that she visualized half of
all the objects and one of them particularly clearly. This was the object she
felt compelled to pick out. Another interesting fact is that when the
experimenter initially thought of one object and then concentrated on another,
the girl often visualized the first object.
Analogous experiments conducted later with other subjects under similar
circumstances also produced positive results. They will be published
elsewhere.
All of the above brings us to the conclusion that experiments on so-called
"mental" suggestion, or—more precisely—direct induction, are successful
when conducted with excitable people, as well as with animals. This
convinces us that in a crowd, besides mutual suggestion and the so-called
contagion that stimulates direct imitation, there must also exist a special
factor in the form of direct impact via the immediate transmission of
excitation from the brain centers of one individual to the corresponding
centers of another individual. At this point, a detailed discussion of the mode
of this transmission would transcend the limits of our present goal; however,
one can already see the paths to follow in search of the correct explanation of
the above transmission in the form of direct impact.17
Thus, verbal persuasion, mutual suggestion, contagion through imitation,
as well as direct induction are the factors that operate in every gathering as in
a collective united by a common mood or a slogan.
In conclusion, we shall discuss several other factors that play a particularly
significant role in the formation of a collective.
It is commonly known that biological factors—such as the need for
nourishment—lead to socialization for the purpose of obtaining food. The
same bonding role is performed by the need for self-preservation, which
unites different people in various groups with the purpose of cooperation and
in the interests of self-defense and attack. In the same way, sexual function
based on the carnal relationship leads to a "spiritual" relationship, which
usually even precedes the physical one. A marital union is followed by the
family that forms a natural unity due to the material, as well as "spiritual,"
interdependence of family members. For children this interdependence
remains primarily unilateral until they become of age. Subsequently, we are
dealing with blood or tribal ties that maintain "spiritual" kinship and form a
familial or tribal collective.
One should not forget that the habit of communicating with others,
fostered by life itself, becomes an organic need, which from time to time
draws us into society for the mutual exchange of reflexes. "Any idea—no
matter how primitive it might be— moves a person to communicate it to a
fellow human being," says P. Sorokin:
The feelings of joy, grief, sorrow and fear demand a response from others.
Joy must be shared, grief demands consolation, fear and indecision require
sympathy and approval, while hatred calls for revenge. A desire to
accomplish something forces an individual to approach other people for
the purpose of involving them in the effort towards achieving the goal in
question. All these emotions tend to "spill over" the bounds of an
individual soul, making this interpersonal exchange indispensable, and the
more intense these emotions are, the stronger attraction to fellow human
beings they generate. These needs unite people by invisible but effective
ties and attract them to one another—just like the invisible yet effective
force of a magnet attracts iron.18
One should bear in mind that all members of a collective interact with one
another either directly or indirectly. This means that individual A acts on an
individual B as a stimulating agent, while B in turn acts on A as a stimulus.
Naturally, this interaction may prove— and does often prove—unequal, since
one individual may have a greater influence over another individual than the
latter over the former.
Interaction is also established between the leader and the crowd, the actors
and the public, between the organizer of a gathering and the gathering itself,
or the chairman of a meeting and its participants. Here, too, the interaction
can be direct and indirect.
Due to mediators, a collective association may evolve irrespective of
spatial and temporal conditions: a society may be dispersed all over the world
and still preserve a certain unity of its members. Similarly, a "spiritual" unity
maintained through traditions and other mediators, such as writings and oral
communication, may exist in a collective of individuals living in different
periods of time. Such are religious communities, states, and other collectives.
In general, favorable circumstances for promoting interaction and unity are
represented not only by the spatial proximity that brings people together
mechanically, as it were (i.e., stimulates mutual exchanges), but also by the
existence of mediators that include all the objects that may be of interest to
many individuals; historical monuments, literary and scientific works,
technological structures, various inventions, methods of teaching and
upbringing, various events and, finally, the common interests aroused by one
or another phenomenon—these are the principal mediators of human unity.
Notes
1. V. Bekhterev, Suggestion and Its Role in Social Life, (in Russian), 3d edition, St. Petersburg, K.
Rikker.
2. P. Binet, La suggestibilite, Paris, 1900, p. 209 ff. 6. Ibid., p. 16.
3. A Vogouroix et P. Juquelier, La contagion mentale. Paris, 1905, p. 27.
4. See the papers presented at the 1919-20 Conference sponsored by the Institute of Brain Research.
Issues in the Study and Education of the Personality. Issue II, 1920.
5. Tarde. The Laws of Imitation, pp. 34-35.
6. Ibid., p. 16.
7. Ibid., p. 13.
8. Tarde, Op. cit., p. 15.
9. D. Bordier, La vie des sociétés, Paris, 1888.
0. Sighele,l.c.,p.31.
1. See Union mèdec, 1869, vol. 8, p. 369.
2. SeeV. Bekhterev, Suggestion andIts Role in Social Life, 3dedition. St. Petersburg, K. Rikker.
3. Oeuvres complètes, Paris, 1824, vol. 3; see introduction, p. 14.
4. Sighele,l.c.,p.50.
5. V. Bekhterev, "Concerning Experiments on the So-Called 'Clairvoyance' on Stage" (in Russian),
Russkii vrach, 1911.
6. See "Issues in the Study and Education of the Personality," Issue 2,1920.
7. I mean the latest research of academician E. Lazarev on the ionic theory of excitation (Recherche sur
la théorie ionique de Vexcitation ), as well as the electric currents generated by the functioning
cortex (see Dr. Trivus's dissertation and Dr. Larionov's work—both from my laboratory). The waves
of electric energy produced by the excitation of the cortex and the conductors are inevitably
accompanied by the Herz rays, which, as we know, are transmitted in various directions.
8. P. Sorokin, System of Sociology (in Russian), vol. 1, pp. 310-311. See also Ross, Soc. control, p. 14.
8
Language as a Unifying Factor
The deeper we go into the study of the social world, the more we realize that
large collectives only appear to be homogeneous, and that any collective,
once it has been formed, breaks into groups; this fact had been overlooked by
the first sociologists (Compte and others). Ross is right when he says,
The moment the sociologist undertakes to "explain phenomena," he is
staggered by the variety of reactions, the dissimilarity of responses to like
stimuli, exhibited in a given group. Here are contrasts of devout with
undevout, of backward-lookers (traditionalists) with forward-lookers...of
risk-lovers with risk-shunners.1
A population may look gray from a distance, but from close up it is seen
to be made up of multicolored particles which, when grouped like with
like, give rise to all manner of contrasts and effects.2
Ross is wrong, however, when he explains various social groupings in
terms of "innate individual differences." These innate differences are
certainly meaningful (e.g., the significance of gender, race, etc.), but in this
case a far greater significance belongs to the environment that produces
persons with certain views, beliefs, and tendencies often absorbed with their
mother's milk. Since, as we know, language itself is not an innate
phenomenon, views, and tendencies of a social nature are even less likely to
be so.
It goes without saying that collective unity is established more easily in
homogeneous than heterogeneous groups, but everything depends here on the
unifying stimulus. When the unifying stimulus acquires a particular
significance for everyone, then most dissimilar individuals—even those in
opposition to one another—exhibit similar reactions. This tends to happen in
times of national catastrophes, such as wars, natural disasters, etc. Thus, the
Great World War united all parliamentary parties within the warring
countries, as well as bringing together such nations, dissimilar in their tribal
and cultural characteristics, as Bulgaria, Turkey, Austria, and Germany on
one side and England, France, Italy and Russia on the other.
One of the most important conditions that lead to and maintain collective
unity is undoubtedly the common character of symbolic reflexes in the form
of one common language, which, in itself, is a result of collective unity. We
should dwell on this subject in greater detail due to its special importance.
The unifying significance of language is so apparent that our discussion of
this matter can be quite brief. Even the language of facial expression plays an
enormous role in this respect, serving as an important unifying factor in the
whole of the animal kingdom. In the human world, too, facial expression is
not devoid of unifying significance for, as we know, it often functions as a
most important unifying factor, (e.g., in a crowd that produces the same
exclamations, and same gestures and other expressive movements). Facial
expression and gestures are also an important auxiliary means for a public
speaker who is trying to unite his listeners by a common goal or bring them
together in one aspiration. An incomparably more significant role in this
respect is played by sound language, or discourse, that acts over distance as
far as sound can travel and, consequently, can play a unifying role even when
people have no opportunity to see one another.
P. Sorokin writes the following about language:
People who speak the same language are members of the least costly and
most popular telephone network in the world. They can easily connect
with one another at any moment and without any assistance from a
telephone operator. Those who do not know the language are not part of
this network; they have no way of connecting with people who speak other
languages.3
If one also takes into consideration written discourse that permits people to
communicate by mail and telegraph, it becomes clear that discourse is
capable of manifesting its unifying significance irrespective of any spatial
circumstances.
Compared to the primitive language of gestures, sound language has the
advantage of being a means of communication that is not as closely tied to
spatial circumstances as the language of gestures, which is generally a rather
primitive mode of communication.
In the course of social progress, language departs further from being a
primitive reaction that replaces the language of gestures and becomes a more
versatile form of communication in which gestures and intonation are only
supplementary; written language, being symbolic and devoid of gesture and
intonation—these most important tools of self-expression that provided the
primary base for the development of oral language—is the next consequence
of sociality:
The role of language as a symbolic human reflex is enormous.
Our words are similar to an electric current that arises between people,
enables them to influence one another and to condition their interpersonal
relations. Their effect can sometimes be astounding. "A word can kill." In
some religions, as for example in Brahmanism, the word has become the
magic force that rules the world and even the will of the gods.
Significantly, the apostle says in the Gospel: "In the beginning was the
Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God."4
In any collective, language is involved in generating its unity. After many
arguments and conflicts, a collective reaches a state of relative consensus;
this achievement due mostly to the dominant role of language. A social
person submits naturally to the power of persuasion exercised by his fellow
human beings, which results in a collective consensus.
Persuasion is the usual mode of influencing those who participate in
collective assemblies. In essence, any discussion of a particular issue by an
assembly is aimed at persuading the assembly to accept as correct the points
made by the speakers, which in the end enables the assembly to arrive at a
certain decision.
The press uses persuasion to influence a collective in a similar fashion.
In any case, some of the most effective means of uniting a collective
through persuasion are the press and public speaking. Oral speech has a
stronger effect than the printed word, for it affects the listeners with the
emotional impact of the speaker, arousing in them an appropriate reaction
that is further intensified by the common mood of the assembly. The impact
of the press on the members of a collective can never be as strong, but its
sphere of influence is considerably larger, for in one way or another it affects
nearly the whole literate human collective that speaks the same language and,
through translation, the educated portion of all humanity.
How persuasion can affect a mass of people in confusion was exemplified
by Kerensky who, after assuming the post of War Minister at a critical
moment in recent Russian history, at a time when our troops were
fraternizing with the Germans while we had to show our allies whether we
were with the Germans or against them, succeeded by the sheer power of
persuasion in rousing the troops from their passive state and leading them in
the attack of 18 July 1917, which was highly successful. It was, however, the
last spark of energy displayed by the demoralized collective of our huge
army.
Verbal suggestion, which is also realized through language, plays an
enormous role in uniting the masses. Naturally, we are speaking about
suggestion in the waking state, or what we call "personal influence."
"As we know, the personal influence of one individual upon another is an
elementary social phenomenon that, except for its scale, does not differ from
the influence of the hypnotist on the hypnotized."5
Since I discussed this influence in more detail in my work, Suggestion and
Its Role in Social Life, there is no need to dwell too much on this
phenomenon here.
We shall only point out the role of suggestion in collective illusions and
hallucinations, common mood, collective actions, etc.
Examples of collective illusions and hallucinations based on mutual
suggestion were cited in my above-mentioned book. Suggestion may produce
mirages similar to the illusions and hallucinations of the organs of perception
in higher levels of associative activity. This diminishes somewhat the
unifying role of suggestion in collectives in comparison with persuasion,
despite its advantage with respect to speed of action.
Everyone knows that one emotional speech delivered at a gathering is
enough to charge the whole assembly with the same emotion, when the very
words and gestures of the speaker are, as it were, directly absorbed by the
audience.
We also know that to scream "Fire!" in a crowded room is enough to start a
panic. A lynch mob often begins in the same way, with someone
thoughtlessly inciting the crowd. In another case, a single call "Get him!" acts
like a wave of an invisible hand, sending the crowd after its victim.
The epidemic scale of some social phenomena is based to a significant
degree on suggestion.
Suggestion has always played an enormous role in social life. G. Le Bon's
opinion is significant in this respect:
If we were guided in the process of adopting our political, social or
religious views by at least a small dose of the lucid and consistent
reasoning applied by the humblest shopkeeper to his business affairs, then
in the sphere of political and religious issues we would not depend—as we
do now—on fashion, environment, and emotions, and would not be swept
along by current events and opinions.6
In society, all propaganda is in general aimed at suggestion rather than
persuasion. The following example of organized propaganda that placed a
special four-minute limit on its agents' public speaking has been borrowed
from the press:
Americans stationed in Petrograd have received quite definite information
concerning the United States' intention to continue the war in close
alliance with England, France, and Italy on condition of the strict
coordination of plans of military operations and genuinely mutual control
over all measures and undertakings related to the issues of war and peace.
It is impossible to conceal that there exists a certain "defeatist" movement
in some American circles. Specifically, some groups believe that it is not
in the interests of the United States to assist England in defeating
Germany.
These "defeatists" are devising all kinds of strategies to end the war in
such a way that America alone—and not Western Europe—would be the
victor.
President Wilson has turned his attention to this kind of defeatist
propaganda and has set up counter-propaganda units in the United States.
The most curious aspect of this matter is the four-minute limit imposed on
each of these speakers.
The nickname for these 15,000 propagandists is "four-minute people."
They do not frequent large meetings that, as we know, attract people with
definite party views.
Every day they visit theatres, cinemas, railway stations, etc., where they
address their "four-minute" speeches to a chance audience.
They also take streetcars, trains and steamboats where they can address
passengers with their short speeches.
To quickly produce the desired mood in their audience, speakers from the
"four-minute" units use a method of comparison we find rather interesting.
"Some people say that America's victory over Germany is desirable, while
England's victory over Germany is not," begins the speaker, "yet what
sensible man could wish that the thieves who broke into his home would
steal all of his wife's belongings on condition they would not touch his
own things? Or who would wish that his shop would make good sales and
order the cashier to daily throw the contents of his till into the fire?"
This kind of propaganda was quite successful with the Americans.
Naturally, the role of language in a collective is not reduced to its unifying
significance, for due to language a given collective develops its own
worldview. We owe to language the evolution of religion and morals, of
science and arts and even technology, for in the final analysis both religion
and science, literature (including fiction) and technology are the products of
the evolution and richness of language.
Obviously, none of the above could exist without language, and even
culture, in the modern sense of the word, would have been impossible.
In the cultural life of all nations, language functions as a tool that is used to
satisfy various needs arising during the evolution of the composite
personality, including the success of its literature, that reflects the totality of
its intellectual acquisitions. It is due to language that the evolution of
knowledge in human society has become possible.
The wizard—the primeval scientist who, at the dawn of civilization, was
trying to disclose nature's secrets and to subject its mysterious forces to his
own will—uses primarily language in his craft. Whether his purpose is to
cope with a disaster, to punish a thief, or to find a lost object, he is dealing
with verbalization or some other symbolization related to the event or its
participants, and here one finds primitive reasoning by analogy.
When, chanting a spell, he throws wood shavings in the fire, it symbolizes
the death and scorching of the enemy or thief. When the wizard pierces the
heart of the enemy's effigy, it symbolizes the suffering and impending death
of the person thus represented. These methods also depend on the power of
suggestion, for the intended victim is bound to hear of the magician's spell.
With time, the wizard is replaced by the medicine man, who appears at the
stage of social evolution when faith in the power of identification and
symbolization is on the decline, while faith in mysterious forces of nature,
which are revealed to those who devoted themselves to their study, is still
alive.
The medicine man is followed by primitive natural philosophers, such as
astrologists and alchemists, who are striving to understand the essence of
things and to achieve universal scientific values through the discovery of the
philosopher's stone.
It is only after these natural philosophers that the true scientist arrives with
his analytical methods of investigation.
However, the scientist, too, when recognizing the mysterious forces of
nature, created by dialectics and speculative thinking, initially pays homage
to the precocious natural philosophy, in order to explain phenomena he does
not understand.
It is very slowly that science begins to discard these useless fetishes and to
advance the value and role of positive knowledge based on the strict logic
that results from the observation of surrounding nature. With the
development of logical thinking, the role of language is therefore invaluable
in the evolution of knowledge.
We also owe to language the evolution of public opinion.
With the emergence of family hearth, clan, and tribe, there began the
establishment of corresponding positions of a social nature. Before these
alliances came into being, human actions were guided solely by an egoism
that at most permitted mercy for one's enemy; now, for the first time ever,
human actions began to be regulated in the interests of an alliance. This
regulation is everywhere guided by so-called public opinion, which defines
good and evil from the viewpoint of the public interest, rather than from the
viewpoint of individual aspirations alone. In addition, special court
procedures have been developed in the course of time, and punishment for
antisocial behavior, as well as rewards for civic-minded actions, have been
instituted. The court system, however, did not decrease the power and
importance of public opinion, which plays a role in the most primitive
societies as well as in later—and more developed—social organizations.
Public opinion invariably turns out to be a more perceptive judge of human
behavior: it qualifies even those moral actions that fall outside the court's
province and passes its own unequivocal verdict on them. This public opinion
is related to the phenomenon we call popularity, fame, or celebrity, which
stimulates many people to become engaged in actions of a social nature and
social interest.
When a Spartan or an Athenian hero sacrificed his life so easily in order to
win fame in a small country, he was convinced that the whole world with
all its invisible forces was watching him, that the great spectators of the
whole world were applauding him, and that the echo of this applause
would resound forever in the infinity of time and space.7
In all these cases, the role of language is indubitable, for only language
makes the existence of public opinion possible and functions as its means of
expression. It is only due to language that one can become popular, well
known, or famous.
According to Le Bon, one
has to take this into consideration even in politics, where the language of a
defeated nation facilitates the introduction of new ideas, laws, religion,
beliefs literature and institutions that are being imposed on it, and which it
could probably assimilate without the psychological resistance produced
by its dialect.
The Low Bretonne dialect probably did more to prevent Bretagne's
assimilation by France than what Christianity did to achieve it; similarly,
the Arabic language represents no lesser obstacle to the French domination
of Algiers than Osmanism itself.
Therefore, all state governments prefer one state language for their
citizens, so that commands to soldiers can be given in one language—
otherwise the state would no longer be united and its army would fall apart.
Moreover, without language there can be neither sociality nor political
associations and collectives in the true sense of the word.
Tarde is wrong, however, when he claims in his Social Logic (p. 210) that
a child enters social life only when he begins to speak.
Even before acquiring verbal language, the child uses facial expression and
gestures to make contact with the environment.
In exactly the same way, animals, being deprived of language in the human
sense, form communities and achieve concord by using their facial
expressions. Thus, language in the form of articulate speech is only an
improved tool for reaching unity in a human collective.
According to Drummond, the study of the content of language will take us
back not only to what people used to produce with their own hands, but also
to what they used to tell one another while working, and to what they used to
think while talking:
The word is the abacus for the brain, the palpable manifestation of a
mental state, an inherited estate of the cultural wealth of the race. An
ancient word, like an ancient coin, tells us about a bygone mentality, while
by its image and inscription it reveals the mental life and aspirations of
those who minted it.
We had the opportunity to discuss the origins of primal language
elsewhere;8 but let us imagine that a language has already evolved— what
next? It develops further, through differentiation and expansion of its parts,
roots, prefixes, flexions, etc., and through the formation of compound and
composite words by combining different roots. Finally, the language
branches out into different dialects or even several languages, which depend,
on the one hand, on the geographical and economic isolation of the
population and, on the other, on various local influences, such as the
influence of neighbors speaking a different language, of immigration, of
assimilation of nationalities, etc.
Irrespective of the above, linguistic evolution is a legitimate phenomenon;
this evolution, however, does not have to follow the same pattern even under
the same external conditions. One population may live through a period of
political upheavals, another may be a successful trading nation, while a third
one is quietly engaged in agriculture. Such conditions must necessarily affect
the language of these nations. These and other circumstances explain how,
after the fall of the Roman Empire, the Latin language gave birth to several
Romance languages and numerous dialects, just as Sanskrit in the past
provided the basis for many modern languages. Linguistic differentiation,
however, occurs parallel to linguistic assimilation that, in turn, leads to an
amalgamation of languages, which separates them even more from the basic
para-language.
So many languages have already disappeared from the face of the earth. If
the language of a cultured nation—such as Greek or Latin-has been preserved
to this day, even though no longer in use, this is due only to the surviving
literature written in these languages. Conversely, why did a great many
languages sink into oblivion? Simply because they could not compete with
other languages that gained currency in their country, whether due to the
conquest and rule of victors who spoke a different language, or due to
cultural amalgamation with another nation. The fact is, however, that a
language does not vanish without a trace. The disappearing language
introduces into the language that absorbs it some local words, expressions,
accents, etc., which make it possible to speak of linguistic fusion and
amalgamation, rather than the destruction of one language by another. And,
finally, amalgamation of languages is manifest in the universal aspiration to
use a common language in international relations, for which purpose artificial
languages have been created. Thus, after the unsuccessful Volapiuk, we have
recently seen increasing use of Esperanto, so that one can foresee the time in
the distant future when all the nations of the globe will join together, using a
language common to all humanity.
Since language is, generally speaking, the most powerful and most
indispensable means of interpersonal communication, it is no exaggeration to
say that such social changes as the assimilation of individuals and social
groups, which occurred in a given territory due to the replacement of horse-
drawn vehicles by locomotives, are nothing compared to similar
transformations caused by the victory of a major dialect over a minor jargon,
or of a language over dialects.
Linguistic similarity is a sine qua non of all other social similarities and,
consequently, of all the great and glorious forms of human activity that
presuppose existence of these similarities, which provide the framework for
their work.
According to Tarde (The Laws of Imitation, p. 121),
It is due to language that a very strict order is established in the chaos of
contradictory visions and hallucinations that assault the ears of the
primitive man.
Language represents a logical arrangement given to the social human
being, just as time and space are given to the individual.
There is no need to analyze in detail the erroneous nature of this statement,
for language evolves together with society, rather than society evolving due
to language, while space and time are not given to each individual person, but
are the result of the collective experience of the social human being (see
below).
Tarde's interpretation of the origins of language is also rather peculiar. In
his words, languages are born out of religions, although they do subsequently
facilitate the development of the latter:
I am quite willing to believe that words represent the logical consequence
of the fluorescence of myths transmitted from generation to generation.
And, indeed, to speak means essentially to divinely personify and animate
every object whose qualities or actions acquire their own independent
existence and magic power (p. 118). I can interpret the primitive word
only as a peculiar acoustic fetish that emerged by itself, under the
influence of the deification of nature as well as the most extraordinary
human actions, whose number began to increase as religious enthrallment,
or awe, began to be related to new objects and people, losing its
connection to nature (I.e., p. 120).
Thus, in a language, every root is nothing other than an idol still used and
preserved, while language itself is nothing other than an ancient remnant
of prehistoric fetishism and naive, gradually extinguished religions; it is in
a sense the ashes of the ancient sacred fires.
I am quoting these statements for the sole purpose of illustrating the
conceptual chaos that dominates this subject in general.
Our own understanding of linguistic evolution is quite different: language
originally evolved as a result of collective experience, as a supplement to
facial expression in the form of sound imitation and sound reflexes,
conditioned by external and internal stimulation: e.g., being suddenly hurt or
annoyed produced such expletives as "Ouch," "Oh," "Ah," etc., that were
subsequently, according to the law of associative reflexes, transformed into
interjections 
from 
which 
language 
then 
developed 
via 
doubling,
differentiation and combination, prefixes, flexions., etc. The original sounds
that provided the basis for future human language came from the animal
world, and now, due to Professor Garner's research in this field, we have
acquired some more or less definite ideas on this subject. In any case, it is
clear that primates undoubtedly possess a number of acoustic signs for
identifying objects that are a part of their simple everyday existence.
Generic definitions develop subsequently from specific ones. For example,
the word devu that used to refer to a fir-tree, a conifer, became a generic term,
since conifers happened to be the most common trees. On the other hand, in
Iceland, where there are no trees at all, it is the word eik (oak) that is used as
the generic name for tree. God was originally identified with the sky, the sun
or the sunshine (Sanskrit deva) or lightening, and it was only gradually that
man arrived at the concept of God as the creator of the Universe, by
discarding the physical attributes of the object defined by the symbol, so that
God came to signify a superhuman being.
How words lose their original meaning is exemplified by the Russian
Spasibo (meaning "Thank you," but literally "God save you") and the French
Adieu, that are nowadays completely devoid of all religious significance. In
general, all religious terms originate in the everyday world, which is exactly
the opposite of what Tarde states in the above quotations.
Notes
1. E.A. Ross (1905), Foundations of Sociology, New York, Macmillan, p. 291.
2. Ibid., p. 293.
3. P. Sorokin, The System of Sociology, vol. 1, p. 227.
4. Ibid., The System of Sociology, vol. I, p. 127.
5. See Tarde, Social Logic, p. 93.
6. The Psychology of Socialism, p. 44.
7. Tarde, SocialLogic, p. 303.
8. See V. Bekhterev, Objective Psychology (in Russian), St. Petersburg, 1912. Also General Principles
of Human Reflexology (in Russian), 1918, by the same author.
9
On Collective Reflexes in General
Since there is no doubt that some collective reflexes belong to the order of
hereditary organic reflexes, while others to the order of higher associative
reflexes acquired through life experience (we shall exemplify this below),
then a question arises whether a collective can manifest reflexes of a simpler
nature, ones which we call "ordinary" in general human reflexology and
which, as we know, occur in a machine-like fashion. It seems that under
certain conditions a collective, too, can manifest ordinary reflexes. The latter,
in my opinion, include such collective reflexes as panic in a crowd and
unmotivated assault—i.e., collective reflexes of a defensive and offensive
nature that develop through contagion.
Panic is an unconscious defensive reflex of flight displayed by a whole
collective; it is unrelated to external circumstances and often occurs without a
legitimate cause or for an insignificant reason. Such panics are also known to
occur among animals—e.g., so-called stampedes in herds of horses, pigs, etc.
The Gospels mention a herd of pigs possessed by an evil spirit and driven
into the sea, where they perished.
More recently herds of sheep were observed to throw themselves off a
precipice and die under the influence of panic.
We all know how easily an unexpected event can spark off an extremely
dangerous panic among a crowd of people. It is enough to shout "Fire!" in a
dense crowd that is listening to an exciting speech, and a fatal panic that will
claim many lives will break out.
There have been cases when some ill-intentioned individuals used this
device to enrich themselves at the expense of those who were panic-stricken.
At this point there is no need to elaborate on the development of panic, as
the reader can learn more about it from my book Suggestion and Its Role in
Social Life, where I give a detailed description of panics partially based on
my own observations. A characteristic feature of all these panic attacks—
besides their standard and purely automatic manifestation in the form of
uncontrollable flight—is the unconscious nature of the flight reflex itself, for
after the panic no one can usually explain why they ran and how it all
happened.
As for the automatic reflex of collective attack developed through
contagion, this phenomenon, too, is known to occur both in the animal world
and among the humans. Thus, when we encounter a pack of dogs, it is
enough to accidentally provoke one dog for all the rest to join in the attack.
There is no need to elaborate on the danger of such attacks. Likewise, if
someone in an angry crowd assaults another person for the most trivial
reason, the whole crowd will attack the same unfortunate individual without
even knowing what it is all about.
Hereditary-organic reflexes and individual reflexes of the same type have
an identical basis, the only difference being in the collective nature of their
manifestations. Thus, the looting of food stores and markets during a famine
or unrestrained sex orgies exemplify the hereditary-organic reflexes of self-
preservation in the first case and those of sexual nature in the second.
As for collective reflexes of a higher order, they, too, may be observed
under certain conditions. Thus, collective reflexes of concentration are easy
to observe in any concert hall, theatre, or auditorium.
Symbolic collective reflexes—verbal ones in particular—can be observed
in the gestures and exclamations of a crowd, in choral singing, etc.
The emergence of individual or collective purposive reflexes that, like
other associative reflexes, evolve on the basis of experience needs further
elaboration. An individual reflex evolves in the following manner: stimulus A
evokes in individual B a particular reaction of concentration, attack or
defense, or in some cases it may suppress a reaction, depending on whether A
affects the given individual beneficially or adversely. A beneficial effect is
accompanied by a positive mimico-somatic reaction on the part of the
individual, while an adverse effect by a negative one. Both, however, depend
not only on the stimulus alone, but also on the state of the individual. A
repeated action of the same stimulus produces the habit of reacting to this
stimulus in a certain manner, while the habit itself creates a specific need in
the form of internal stimulation caused by the absence of the mimico-somatic
reaction that followed the external stimulation. This results in the internal
stimulation emerging independently, i.e., without any external stimulation,
whose presence always increases this need to the maximum.
When the external stimulus produces an adverse effect, the process of
internal stimulation occurs along the same lines, albeit with the opposite
result—i.e., with a defensive reaction or with the suppression of all reaction.
In other words, one develops an inner need for defensive action or avoids the
given stimulation altogether.
The same result is achieved by mediated stimulation. Thus, a certain
stimulus may affect a given individual not immediately, but following the
impact of a preceding or accompanying stimulus.
Thus, a food object may be hidden inside an inedible shell or under the
leaves of a certain plant or in particular places in the soil, etc. On the other
hand, a harmful stimulus concealed behind some external obstacles may
begin to produce its adverse effect after they are removed. In these cases, the
acting stimulus is accompanied by another, and the impact of the latter is
preceded by that of the former.
However, in accordance with the law of associative reflexes, the
accompanying stimulus begins to act like the primary stimulus after the
repeated action of the latter. For this reason, the stimuli that do not produce
either beneficial or adverse effects directly on an organism provoke a specific
reaction that attracts or repulses the individual, depending on what kind of
primary stimuli accompanied these originally neutral stimuli.
What is true for individual persons is also true for a social group as a
collective entity, which is why the group response to external stimulation is
in certain cases exactly the same as the individual one, the only difference
being that here the individual reflexes are combined due to mutual
communication and suggestion.
Thus, for example, one can interpret the development of an uprising in the
following manner: through one's own individual experience every person
develops a sense of protest as an associative reflex of defense that evolves
from the ordinary defensive reflex under unfavorable physical stimulation.
When enough external reasons for protest are accumulated by many
individuals, manifestations of this protest in a collective are combined, and
due to interaction and contagion a certain collective unity evolves, which, in
turn, enhances the manifestation of this protest. When this protesting unity
emerges in a collective, the most insignificant cause provides a sufficient
stimulus for the collective protest to reach the level of social unrest that keeps
growing to the point of collective explosion in the form of an uprising.
The development of the collective reflex of attack in the form of pogroms,
wars, etc., proceeds in an analogous fashion.
According to the above law of associative reflexes, not only the goal itself
but also the means of achieving this goal—i.e., the preceding stimulus—must
be sufficient for triggering the reflex. Thus, food stimulates not only every
individual but a collective as well. Since it is the acquisition of money that
ensures the acquisition of necessary household objects and food products, it
is clear that, due to experience and according to the law of associative
reflexes, money, too, becomes a stimulus that triggers the same reaction as
the main goal, i.e., the necessary objects and food products, as a result of
which the money itself becomes an immediate goal. All processes of
exchange in collectives, banks, and other organizations are based on this
process of development of associative reflexes in which not only the goal
itself provides stimulation as a result of experience, but, as well, all the means
of achieving this goal.
One should not overlook the fact that any social movement to a significant
degree owes its existence to special propaganda that often consolidates, as
well as stimulates, collective action. This propaganda is usually carried out
by agitators who have no scruples about using every means at their disposal
to electrify a crowd.
One of the classic examples of this kind of agitation is Anthony's speech
after the death of Julius Caesar in Shakespeare's play.
In any case, agitators know from experience when and where to address a
crowd with their inflammatory speeches and to achieve the desired effect by
the appropriate means.
Agitators themselves—these temporary leaders of the crowd-are often far
from courageous, but those who sometimes skillfully hide behind the crowd.
When arrested, they usually show no signs of repentance and at times behave
in an extraordinarily cowardly manner.
Thus, according to newspaper reports, an agitator who during the
Kronshtadt uprising of July 4 and 5 did not waver to agitate for shooting
defenseless women and children in the streets behaved in the most
disgraceful, cowardly fashion when arrested:
He was trembling with fear like a leaf, terrified of being executed, and
begged the military authorities tearfully not to let the Kronshtadt sailors
and soldiers escort him to Petrograd.
"They are angry with me and will shoot me," this agitator and crowd
leader kept babbling.
A social movement as such is a result of the combined activities, struggle,
and cooperation of individual persons, groups, and associations or unions.
Like any social process, it must produce either a borrowing based on
imitation or a creative act. In the latter case, there appears in the collective
life of the society a relatively new and original phenomenon, even though it
may prove similar to one that was once experienced in the past.
Every social movement usually develops until it exhausts all of its
potential power generated by the corresponding stimulation in a given
environment, or until it encounters the corresponding counteraction in
another social movement or in some external force; in other words, a
collective reflex—as any associative reflex—is terminated by internal or
external inhibition. While developing in the form of a collective reflex, a
social movement at a certain point reaches its climax, after which the wave of
the movement begins to ebb and finally disappears, just like an associative
reflex of an individual person. In such cases one can speak about a social
shift at the initial stage of the social movement, about a turning point in the
mood of the popular masses at the peak of the social movement, and about its
decline when the movement begins to weaken and is then replaced by another
social movement.
Obviously, the shift itself, as well as the turning point, is conditioned by
some specific causes that force the masses to change direction.
Thus, during the Great Russian Revolution, when at the innumerable
political meetings in the streets and in public buildings one heard the appeals
to destroy the bourgeoisie, to rob the banks, few of the authorities and of the
bourgeois population realized the danger of this kind of propaganda. They
believed that an ideological sermon of murder and undisguised robbery had
as much legal right to exist as any other, and should not therefore encounter
any legal counteraction in the name of the proclaimed freedom of speech.
Similarly, newspapers and leaflets that were disseminated among the
troops and called for the disobedience to their superiors were not
counteracted, in the name of the same freedom of the printed word.
Then masses of armed workers appeared in the streets of Petrograd, the
shooting began, and we ended up with the July uprising that demanded
military intervention. Those in power woke up to reality, and, at the same
time as the uprising was suppressed, a radical shift occurred among the large
segments of society that turned against Bolshevism.
As a result, a considerable part of society and the authorities moved to the
right, and the freedoms of assembly and speech were curtailed.
Administrative measures against individuals were introduced. Some
newspapers were prohibited, and the Bolshevik movement was temporarily
suppressed.
For at least some time, the Bolshevik leaders did not dare to make
speeches at the meetings where they were usually ridiculed; for a period of
time, the unfolding social movement was suppressed or temporarily inhibited.
All of this notwithstanding, the propaganda among workers and soldiers
was doing its bit, and on the 25th of October of the same year the Bolshevik
coup took place, the Provisional Government was arrested, its head Kerensky
fled, the cadets and the women's battalions that offered resistance were
disarmed and—in the case of the cadets—shot. Thus, the wave of the
Bolshevik movement reached its apogee.
The first months after October 25 were a period when the Bolshevik
movement was still on the rise and, finally, its wave reached its highest point;
this wave, however, can never stay at its peak and must gradually ebb. It is
already on the wane, for the Bolshevism of today is in many ways different
from what it used to be in the first months after October 25.
The same processes occur in all other collective reflexes. Some barely
noticeable symptoms signal the appearance of a new social movement that
grows, develops, reaches its highest point, then gradually weakens and enters
the quiet flow of social life.
10
The Development of Collective
Movements as Associative Reflexes
We know from reflexology that an associative reflex evolves on the basis of
an ordinary reflex and is essentially its reproduction. The same is true of
collective reflexes. Let us take a crowd pacified by a police detachment and
fleeing in panic when shooting begins— this is an ordinary collective reflex.
When a collective is subjected to a violent assault and one can hear swearing,
groans, and heartrending cries from its midst, this is also an ordinary reflex.
But when one hears the same swearing, groans, and cries from a collective at
the time when it is perturbed by an emotional account of the atrocities
inflicted on those it holds dear, then we have an associative reflex. When a
crowd disperses under the influence of a simple threat, this, too, is an
associative collective reflex.
It is clear from the above that there exists a permanent analogy between the
development of a collective associative reflex and that of an individual
associative reflex, for it is evident that in both cases the associative reflex—
whether individual or collective-evolves from an ordinary reflex as its
reproduction under the impact of the stimulus that has accompanied the
principal reflexo-genic stimulation.
One should point out that the stimulus that evokes an ordinary reflex can
be replaced by its symbol—a word or another sign—and the end result will
be an associative reflex of the same type. For example, a person who has
never experienced the impact of firearms himself and has never even seen it
in action, will, nevertheless, turn away from it in fright exclusively under the
influence of stories about its lethal effect. In this case the sight of the firearms
alone or even its mention is enough to stimulate an associative reflex of
defense. The fact is that the symbol is inevitably associated with its meaning
as a weapon whose threat one can avoid only by removing oneself from its
sphere of action.
The same is true of a collective. In the above example of crowd
pacification one does not even have to attack the crowd. Everybody knows
about the lethal power of firearms or side-arms, if not from one's own
experience, then from the verbally communicated experience of others.
Consequently, in the course of one's life, everyone develops a reflex of
defense at the sight of a weapon and when being threatened by it, which is
enough to induce a crowd threatened by weapons to disperse.
If this does not happen, even a single blank-fire shot is enough to put the
crowd to flight.
In this case, too, we are dealing with an associative collective reflex that
follows a symbolic stimulus in the form of a simple threat, thus reproducing
the reflex caused by real stimulation under different circumstances. It is
obvious from the above that for a collective associative reflex to develop it is
not at all necessary to have the entire collective subjected in advance to the
kind of external stimulation that would lead to the development of an
ordinary reflex. The fact is that an associative reflex can occur without such
collective experience, due exclusively to the influence of secondhand
accounts of the consequences of the above impact, as well as due to the
previous experience of some individual members of the given collective. In
the latter case, the experience of certain individuals is disseminated
throughout the entire collective through discourse and becomes its common
property. This is clear from the fact that the quality of a collective and its
relationship to the surrounding world depend on its individual members who
can be subjected to appropriate training. It is therefore natural that the
experience of certain individuals has some effect on the activity of the entire
collective.
Every social movement has a cause that acts as a stimulus. The Swiss
uprising developed on prepared ground after the well-known incident with
the humiliating demand to shoot at the apple on the son's head. The Russian
Revolution of 1905 was started by the 9th of January shootings at the mass
demonstrations. The revolution of 1917 started with the dissolution of the
State Duma instead of the setting up of the responsible ministry demanded by
public opinion. The Spartacists' uprising in Berlin on 5 January 1919 started
with the dismissal of the Berlin prefect Eighorn, who had been elected by the
workers and who decided not to abandon his post as given to him by the
workers.
It is clear that every social movement develops as a mass associative reflex
under the influence of a particular stimulus that acts as the final impetus to a
previously prepared situation.
To establish an analogy between the manifestations of social or collective
activity and the associative reflexes of an individual personality, one has to
analyze a particular social movement.
In this respect there is no better example than an army where genuine
collective associative reflexes are developed through training and discipline
reminiscent of those used to train performing animals. On hearing a single
word "Form!" a mass of people assume formation. The phrase "Stand easy!"
frees the troops from the constraints of the previous command. At the
command "Dress to the right!" one and all turn to the right; at the command
"Dress to the left!" everybody turns to the left. At the command "Ready!" all
rifles take aim; at the word "Fire!" everybody fires simultaneously. Here the
words of the command act as an associative stimulus, while the main
stimulus is the mandatory nature of army drill.
If we take mass gymnastic exercises, we shall come across the same
learned associative collective movement when an instructor's command
causes an entire mass of people to perform simultaneously, one and all, the
same movements. The command in this case, as well as in others, can with
the proper training be replaced by any sign-by a wave of a hand or a scarf, by
a flag, a shot, by the sound of a horn or a drum, etc.—which results in the
performance of the same movements. Finally, after appropriate training, the
complex gymnastic exercises, consisting of a particular sequence of
movements, can be performed to music.
Those who ever attended the so-called Sokolskii rallies had a chance to see
for themselves how an enormous mass of people, numbering tens of
thousands, performed complicated gymnastic exercises to the sounds of
music. All of them would simultaneously raise their arms to a particular
sound of music, lower them to another sound, kneel with the third sound,
then rise with the same tempo, turn sideways, etc.
The same occurs in dancing. Here again music is an associative stimulus,
while the need to move is the principal one, just as in the above example.
Here, too, a mass of people moves to the rhythm of music that acts as a
familiar associative stimulus, while the words of the instructor provide a
particular signal.
Another example is a large orchestra. All members of the orchestra have
before them a musical score from which they have already learned the piece
to be performed. A sign from the conductor focuses everybody's attention on
him, and then, to the movements of his baton, the musicians begin the
prepared and previously coordinated performance on a number of
instruments, some of which are supposed to start at one moment, others at
another and so on. Finally, the last wave of the conductor's baton and
everybody stops playing instantaneously. Is this not a conditioned associative
collective reflex?
The same phenomenon occurs in choral singing, as well as in theatrical
performance, where the function of a conductor's baton is to a certain extent
assumed by the prompter.
Further, the actions of firemen performing a certain task or the coordinated
actions of workers engaged in a collective effort provide still more examples
of the artificially conditioned collective associative reflexes that are perfectly
similar to the associative reflexes of an individual personality.
It goes without saying that in everyday life the same kinds of collective
associative reflexes are conditioned quite naturally.
Thus, to avoid misfortune a crowd is immediately put to flight by a scream
like "Fire" or "Run for your life"; on hearing "Get this thief it beats him up.
The first sounds of gunfire send a crowd into a chaotic flight and so on.
All of these are examples of natural associative collective reflexes
cultivated or conditioned by life itself.
When a collective is subjected to a particular stimulus leading to a certain
reaction, any concomitant circumstance becomes capable of producing the
same reaction.
Let us presume that one nation is engaged in a war against another nation.
It is common knowledge that defensive and offensive actions by the troops
are determined by enemy actions. The enemy tries to conceal these actions.
If, however, its offensives were frequently accompanied by a particular
signal, it is natural that the defenders will be alarmed by this signal, even
though it may not signify the enemy's attack. If the mounted police often
fired at a crowd, the latter will immediately disperse at the mere sight of a
few mounted patrols, even unarmed ones, or on hearing the clatter of the
horses' hooves.
Even artificially cultivated collective associative reflexes adapt to various
natural circumstances. Thus, the sounds of a march induce a crowd to start
walking to the beat of the music, and the sounds of a song accompanying
collective work cause workers to move in rhythm with the song.
When a theatre audience applauds an actor and displays other signs of
approval, it reproduces ordinary gesticulatory reflexes and facial expressions,
doing this as a collective. When a crowd laughs together with an actor on
stage, it is infected by his laughter, reproducing the same mimic reflex. When
listening to an orchestra, it manifests collective concentration just like any
individual person.
When a crowd flees from a firing squad or attacks someone, perpetrating a
mob lynching, it performs a collective defensive or offensive associative
reflex.
When it goes on a rampage, looting bakeries or butchers' shops in time of
famine, it follows the instinct of self-preservation. When a choir sings or a
crowd screams, we are dealing with a collective symbolic or verbal reflex,
etc.
Clearly, all of the above cases provide valid reasons for comparing the
activity of a collective to that of an individual.
The situation is much more complicated when we deal not with a crowd
that performs a particular movement or action but with a mass of individuals
in an assembly, when after prolonged discussions these persons arrive at a
certain decision. There seems to be little analogy with individual human
activity. But let us take a case when a person is given a certain task that he is
supposed to accomplish in one way or another. In this case, the main stimulus
is the goal of providing a certain solution as a result of necessity or need; the
performance of the task is realized through complex, previously cultivated
forms of judgment, considerations pro and contra, evaluation of all available
information and various events from one's own life experience.
It is not difficult to perceive in the performance of this task a certain
analogy with collective work in assemblies.
In assemblies, we deal essentially with the same kind of activity that is
pursued by an individual person, but here the work is accomplished jointly by
an entire collective, as a result of which the resources of individual
experience are replaced by the experience of the entire collective. Here, the
artificially cultivated need for solving a problem and the performance of duty,
or a sense of responsibility that guides collective and individual actions in
such cases, may and should be perceived as the major stimuli that accompany
all collective intellectual activity performed in an assembly and leading to
particular solutions.
Thus, in all the diverse forms of the manifestation of collective activity one
can observe a complete analogy with individual associative activity; and just
as the latter consists of individual associative reflexes, one encounters in a
collective associative reflexes of a collective nature, i.e., performed jointly by
the entire collective.
Every social movement always follows a path that delineates the behavior
of the entire collective as an entity.
Every major collective, however, consists of a number of minor collectives
that, in turn, fall into smaller collectives, etc., so that every collective,
irrespective of its size, follows a distinctive line of behavior that represents
the resultant force of all the aspirations pursued by the individual members of
a given collective. Thus, the line of behavior of any large collective
represents a resultant force of the resultant forces of the smaller collectives
that make it up.
Since the smallest collective consists of two individual persons whose
associative activities differ in character and content, and since all collectives,
in general, have a heterogeneous membership and therefore display different
lines of behavior, it is obvious that every collective is either supported by
others when their interests coincide or counteracted and inhibited in its
actions when there is discord and disagreement. Perfect accord between all
collectives, just like perfect accord among individual members within every
collective, is not possible.
In general, one should bear in mind that every collective, like any
organism, lives, i.e., moves, develops and evolves, and its movement is
determined either by its needs or by external stimuli. The former constitute an
inalienable part of any living collective, for only a dying and disintegrating
collective would lose its needs. A need, however, arises either in the form of
hereditary-organic reflexes determined by biological conditions, or from a
well-established habit conditioned by the numerous recurrences of particular
associative reflexes.
In the first case, the requirements of a collective arise from its past
experience of satisfying the needs that are inherent in the very essence of the
organization itself, just as in an individual organism some needs in the form
of the so-called instincts are determined by the nature of the organization. In
collectives these include conjugal needs, economic needs, needs for
movement, and so forth. In the second case, the needs arise from existential
experience, e.g., the need for education, the need for play, entertainment, and
so on. The latter result from existential experience under conditions that lead
to the consumption or utilization by a collective of a particular material, and
from the concomitant sthenic reaction characterized by corresponding
mimico-somatic and offensive reflexes aimed at a possible prolongation of
the sthenic reaction.1 When the latter is frequently reproduced, it becomes
habitual to such a degree that its insufficient reproduction turns into a
stimulus, generating the need for utilizing the same material. Excessive
gratification, on the contrary, induces a sthenic reaction accompanied by
defensive or offensive reflexes that put an end to the further gratification of
the need.
In collectives one encounters various needs born of existential experience.
These needs lead to the same consequences and are satisfied correspondingly.
Thus, the need to realize manufactured commodities brings about measures
that encourage export; an overflow of imported goods on the market leads to
the introduction of protectionist duties; an increased immigration of
foreigners results in counteractive measures against it, etc.
Needs as a source of motivation for activity and offensive reflexes are the
most important stimuli of progress, for they stimulate inventiveness where
every success is accompanied by a positive sthenic reaction that provides
motivation for further discoveries and inventions; it is the need for
innovations that sets in motion the human genius. The latter satisfies this
need when favorable conditions and appropriate prerequisites become
available. That is why one can state with certainty that genius makes its
appearance in the life of a nation only when its time comes.
Even those phenomena that we perceive as being of an economic nature
such as exchange in the form of buying and selling, services, usage, property,
securities, inheritance, rent, prescriptive right, etc., are, in fact, nothing other
than the result of established social needs entrenched in law; consequently,
they, too, are primarily associative reflexes in the economic sphere—a sphere
that satisfies the established needs of a collective. The market itself, with its
exchange and monetary units, is on the one hand a result of demand, or need,
and on the other of supply as a need to dispose of the products of one's labor
when they are in abundance in order to exchange them for others that are in
short supply; a need, as we have seen, is nothing other than an associative
reflex. It is therefore clear that the whole economic aspect of social life
develops along the lines of associative reflexes, while society, as a living
organism, is in continuous motion, as a result of which the correlation
between various social groups within it is constantly in a state of dynamic
equilibrium.
A collective need can be stimulated by any kind of external influence, due
to which one collective, in the presence of competition, may enter into a
confrontation with another collective and exercise the right of might. Thus,
the once disfranchised Jewry, when liberated by the February Revolution
from its former social slavery, found itself on the crest of the revolutionary
wave in the persons of many prominent revolutionary leaders; yet, despite the
declared freedoms and equality of nationalities, the very first months of the
revolution were followed by pogroms, even though the country was enjoying
all kinds of freedoms and was in the process of preparation for the first
elections based on the universal, equal, direct, and secret ballot. Another
example: the horrific despotism of the tsarist regime resulted in the most
tumultuous revolution in the world since the Great French Revolution and
brought the proletariat, previously engaged in a hopeless struggle against
capitalism, to the forefront.
A third example: the advancement of the proletariat during the coup of
October 25 led to Kornilov's counterattack and stimulated in various strata of
society—among its bourgeois elements in particular—an attraction towards
monarchism, which forced the ruling classes to take severe repressive
measures. The same collision of social aspirations is apparent in the reaction
of the so-called democratic elements of the country to the Bolshevism that
was trying to deepen the revolution. This is what we read on this account in
one of the newspapers of 19 October 1917:
The more furious and sweeping Bolshevism grows, the more tightly those
elements of the country who think in terms of state interests close their
ranks. Slowly but surely the mood of the broad masses of the population is
undergoing an evolution: the democratic Soviets of Workers' and Peasants'
Deputies are moving to the left, while the rest of the country is moving to
the right.
This process began a long time ago, almost from the first ecstatic days of
democratic creativity unleashed by the revolution. The early demarcation
between the state and the class mentality became clearly apparent during
the April days when the left-wing democracy had its first experience of
overthrowing a "bourgeois" government. That was the beginning of
society's move to the right.
This process of reaction-formation, resulting from a collision of interests,
proceeds more or less evenly only when it does not encounter any new
attendant circumstances in its way. For example, in that same movement to
the right among the democratic elements of the country, which was
associated with the growth of Bolshevism, the role of such new attendant
circumstance that slowed down this movement to the right was played by the
Kornilov affair. In the same paper we read the following regarding the above-
mentioned evolution in the popular movement:
The Komilov days broke the thread of this evolution. The democrats
forsook the "bourgeoisie" yet again, having made arash evaluation of the
Komilov affair. The "counterrevolutionary" nature of the bourgeoisie has
again become a bugbear even for the responsible and state-minded
democrats who were only recently agreeing to concessions for the sake of
the unity of all national forces in the name of the country's defense and
freedom. The chasm between the classes has again deepened and widened,
the two sides are now further apart than before the August days, and both
of them are further away from the common goal. The ones who reaped the
fruits of this false storm were the Bolsheviks.
In the democratic country, the Bolsheviks started seizing power, and the
state-minded democratic elements began to lose power among the masses
corrupted and deceived by demagoguery.
When two mutually incompatible processes of social evolution reach their
climax, this leads to a social crisis that results in a mutual inhibition followed
by the turn of the popular movement in one or another direction.
In the Russian Revolution this kind of crisis occurred in the Democratic
Assembly that ended in a completely discordant and fruitless vote.
Like any crisis, a social crisis must certainly lend itself to a resolution, and
this resolution may occur rapidly through an open confrontation in which a
more powerful popular movement suppresses the weaker one by force. In
other cases, a crisis is resolved slowly, and the gradually ebbing tide of the
popular movement outlives itself little by little.
Whether the resolution of a crisis proceeds stormily, "critically," or slowly
and gently, depends on the nature—whether it is more active or less so—of
the popular movement, as well as on some other conditions (e.g., the speed of
the movement's expansion, etc.).
Every social collective may at a particular moment in time undergo its own
crisis.
Thus, in every party, a difference of opinion between two camps or two
movements may lead to the kind of crisis that will be followed by the victory
of one trend or the other.
Incidentally, the debates on defense in the Soviet of the Republic ended in
a crisis and a rejection of the proposed formulae for the resolution.
As for the form of the collective reflex, it may differ, depending on the
nature of the gathering or the collective. Thus, in parliamentary assemblies,
protests are mostly limited to vehement rebuttal or, at the most, to an
eccentric action, altercation, exit from the assembly during somebody's
speech, etc.—greater excesses are rare; in a crowd, we deal with more
extreme manifestations (e.g., mob lynching) that often lead to fatal
consequences.
Associative reflexes2 can also explain the striking phenomenon whereby a
means becomes an end in itself, substituting the latter, as it were. As we
know, the means precedes and even accompanies the end; yet, although
initially the end as a stimulus provokes the reflex, with time the means, as a
phenomenon preceding and accompanying the end, begins to act as a
