stimulus that provokes the associative reflex. Thus, in times of great
revolution, the sermon of liberty and equality as a means of achieving the
common weal becomes, in the long run, an end in itself, initially leading to
the destruction of the public welfare.
As we have mentioned above, the instinct of self-preservation stimulates a
desire to achieve optimal well being under all possible living conditions.
Consequently, everything related to obtaining appropriate nourishment and a
better position in life results from stimulation of a special need associated
with this instinct. Since money and profit are, in general, a means of
satisfying these needs, the acquisition of money is associated with the latter
as a stimulus that invariably precedes it. As a result, money itself, as a means
of satisfying one's needs, is capable of arousing the same reactions that are
stimulated by the satisfaction of the corresponding needs. Thus, here, too, the
means as such becomes the end.
In those individuals whose needs stimulated by the instinct of self-
preservation are not inhibited by other circumstances, they are naturally
maintained and enhanced by various living conditions, which engenders the
attraction to profit and acquisition manifested through the appropriate
reflexes.
The passion for acquisition, however, is in certain cases best satisfied
through joint efforts, which naturally gives rise to all kinds of economic
organizations that exist in human society and satisfy the instinct of self-
preservation in collective life.
One can prove that the oldest habits survive longer in a collective than
those acquired later on, which provides yet another analogy with individual
reflexes. Established ritual, etiquette, and custom are examples of old social
habits that survive throughout centuries without the slightest changes,
whereas fashion, failing to become a habit, is replaced in time by a new
fashion, which, too, will have but a transitory existence.
We would also like to note that as the relationship of an individual to a
collective evolves, there unfolds a process of social identification that reaches
such a degree that not only do individual aspirations become subordinated to
public interests, but the interests of the collective are adopted by the
individual as his own; in other words, they become identified with his
personal, or individual, interests. These are the origins of the line of
individual aspirations called patriotism. Such collective interests as, for
example, the prosperity of the state, constitute an object of identification with
one's own interests to the extent that everything favoring this prosperity
stimulates in every individual a positive sthenic reaction and vice versa.
As we know, a sthenic reaction in individual life accompanies not only the
satisfaction of a physical need, but also the activity of the relating apparatus,
or the so-called mental work, unless it leads to exhaustion. Here, too, what
we call creative mental activity and even the very acquisition of knowledge
as such is usually accompanied by a sthenic reaction, which phenomenon is
reflected in the language itself in such words denoting attraction to
knowledge as liuboznatelnost (literally, love of knowledge) meaning
"curiosity" 
and 
liubopytstvo 
(literally, 
love 
of 
inquiry) 
meaning
"inquisitiveness." Thus, a sthenic reaction that accompanies mental activity
until it becomes tiring appears to act as a reliable stimulus towards the
intellectual growth and self-improvement that occur according to
evolutionary laws. This is also the basis for the role of initiative in associative
human activity.
The same processes take place in a collective where inquisitiveness and
curiosity are enhanced through example and imitation. A crowd of
busybodies gathered in the street around a gawker is a good example. It is
enough for someone in the street to start gazing at the sky, and many of the
passers-by would do the same. Let someone in a big city stop on a bridge and
start peering into the water of the river flowing underneath—within a short
time he will, surely, be surrounded by a curious crowd.
Collective curiosity is behind every new social movement; no matter how
vast the blind faith in the authorities, fascination with the glorious past and
conformity to the traditions of forebears might be, a collective is, due to
curiosity, gradually penetrated by a new current, by one or another
innovation, that through imitation gradually spreads among a considerable
number of people and then among the entire collective. Thus, what one may
call a social shift begins to occur at a faster or slower pace, depending on the
circumstances.
Here one has to note that the longer social stagnation based on imitation of
the past lasts, the greater the thirst for innovation becomes; this is why
epochs of stagnation in society are usually followed by epochs of
rejuvenation and upheavals, the latter occurring the more vigorously the
longer has stagnation lasted and the more atavistic has been the old order.
Habit and mechanization associated with acceleration of various processes
operate in every social phenomenon, just as in any individual action. Thus,
many industrial processes, such as the manufacture of fabrics, clocks and
watches, various commodities, agricultural machinery, etc., occur at a much
higher speed than in the past. Besides, every recurrent social phenomenon
becomes established and turns into a habitual associative reflex, as it were.
For example, a foreign language used instead of one's mother-tongue
becomes habitual with time, replaces the mother-tongue, and, due to habit,
even its alien structure is assimilated as the national one, as it were. We
know, for instance, how rapidly the characteristics of the Greek language
with its periods, meters, and other attributes became the flesh and blood of
the Latin language and formed the solid heritage of Roman culture. The same
is true of other social phenomena. Thus, an introduction of any new social
reform— whether educational, judicial, or administrative—initially proceeds
with great difficulties and must overcome various hurdles and frictions; with
time, however, these same reforms, as it were rendered automatic, become
part of the tradition and are adopted as permanent cultural heritage.
In exactly the same way the generally accepted gift-giving in the East that
initially used to be voluntary, turned first into a custom and then into a
mandatory favor, virtually becoming a kind of obligation. Charitable
donations that used to be collected for victims of fire were with time replaced
by insurance companies. Religious rites were initially performed by the
clergy free of charge, and the parishioners used to reward them with
voluntary donations; with time, however, these voluntary donations turned
into a duty and became compulsory. Special honors bestowed on the powers
that be gave rise to flattery and politeness, the former becoming obligatory in
the East, while the latter became compulsory in the European high society.
The old practice of prostrating oneself before the ruler assumed with time the
form of bowing and taking one's hat off when meeting any person one wishes
to pay attention to, primarily one's acquaintances. According to Tarde (Laws
of Imitation, p. 359), "the widespread need to hear flattery, receive visits and
bows in imitation of high officials and gentry served as a hidden cause of the
gradual and universal spread of politeness in France and other countries."
Due to the same circumstances, the long-term exposure of the popular
masses to theatrical performances in ancient Rome generated a need for them,
just like the need for bread. It is therefore understandable why the popular
masses in Rome began to demand both, shouting out "Panem et circenses."
Every innovation or discovery is generated by a social need, i.e., an
associative collective stimulus that, in turn, arose from the appropriate
recurrent circumstances. Let us take the following example: the human need
for communication is based on the development of culture and leads to the
improved means of communication. Introduction of these means results in the
intensification of exchange, which with time becomes a new social need, etc.
If we look in retrospect at the whole process of the cultural evolution of
mankind with its inventions and needs, we shall reach back to the primal
foundations of communal living, where individual persons, due to imitation,
experience the need to form a collective, while imitation itself also becomes a
need in virtue of its inevitable recurrence under conditions of communal
existence.
Imitation, however, inevitable as it is in social life, implies the loss of
novelty. At the same time it involves a reduction of the imitated process.
Hence, the "necessity of new inventions and fresh sources of imitation, which
could resuscitate the depleting social energy" (Tarde, Laws of Imitation, p.
216). Thus, art gradually degenerates, religious beliefs lose their power and
intensity, discourse becomes shorter and idioms fade. "Honorary titles
(seigneur reduced to seur), greetings (feudal genuflection replaced by a slight
nod of the head), compliments and manners are increasingly reduced,
weakened and simplified as they become more widespread." The same was
proved earlier by Spencer.
On the other hand, no social movement ever starts abruptly, but develops
gradually: it first goes through a latent stage when, due to social conservatism
or to inertia, it slowly—sometimes over a period of many years—forces its
way through and enters the public arena. Before art began to degenerate it
had to flower. Every religious doctrine goes through an initial phase of
development and only gradually gains a wide popularity. Before losing its
meaning, every title had to assume a particular form required by social need.
Before fading away, every term and every expression had to come into
existence and occupy their place in the public vocabulary, affecting the minds
with their novelty and characteristic flavor; every manner of greeting and
parting is a vestige of social relations that used to have a far more significant
and real meaning.
In a word, a collective reflex in this respect evolves according to the model
of individual associative reflexes. Initially, after a latent period, it grows more
or less rapidly. While growing, it gains ground, differentiates through
imitation during its increasing dissemination, and then, having become
habitual, it loses its novelty, weakens and fades, i.e., loses its role as a social
process. It revives, however, with each new stimulus. Thus, in a collective
reflex too, one can distinguish its own developmental stages completely
analogous to those one observes in an individual associative reflex. A latent
period, a more or less rapid growth at the beginning that leads to a climax,
followed by a gradual weakening and final decline (retaining the ability,
however, to reappear under favorable and stimulating conditions)—these are
the stages of a social movement as a collective reflex.
We know that a sthenic reaction associated with a particular stimulation is
subject to the general law of associative reflexes. Initially, therefore, this
reaction accompanies the satisfaction of an organism's immediate needs, but
in the course of one's life experience it begins to accompany those means that
are not immediately connected with the satisfaction of existential needs.
Thus, we have already mentioned that, for instance, money by itself—as
pieces of paper—would be unable to provoke a sthenic reaction, but as a
means of possible acquisition of vitally indispensable objects it stimulates a
sthenic reaction in anyone in need of the latter. On the other hand, achieving
a goal while, for example, obtaining food supplies through one's own or other
people's experience stimulates a sthenic reaction resulting from the
satisfaction of one's existential needs; with time, however, the process of
achieving any goal whatsoever becomes a means of stimulating a sthenic
reaction, even though the goal itself under different circumstances would not
directly lead to the satisfaction of existential needs.
In this respect, what is true for individual persons has the same
significance for a collective. Such, for example, is the development of a
collective sthenic reaction that accompanies the satisfaction of patriotic goals
by the state collective when securing the means that make a future
satisfaction of patriotic goals possible. A victory that guarantees a favorable
outcome of a country's military operations, or an alliance that inspires hope
for satisfying a country's existential needs in a more or less remote future
already stimulate a collective sthenic reaction.
As we have seen, one should include imitation among collective
associative reflexes. It is a reflex that ensures attainment of collective
successes achieved by individual persons. If at the lower levels of the animal
kingdom imitation in the form of mimicry serves primarily for the
preservation of the species, at the highest evolutionary levels of the animal
kingdom imitation serves social goals and provides the basis of sociality. In
the final analysis, the whole of culture as we know it is based on imitation
that permeates the life of every creature whatsoever from its earliest days to
its particular level of development, as well as social life starting from the
primitive peoples, the infant nations, and ending with the nations of the
highest culture.
According to Féré's experiments and some other findings, imitation is not
only conducive to acquiring reflexes but it also facilitates their execution. As
we know, imitation in collective work has a stimulating effect and facilitates
the work effort itself.
We have already mentioned elsewhere that seeing a dancing person makes
one start dancing himself, that a laughing person causes another person laugh,
that someone who yawns makes his neighbor yawn and so on. It is thus
evident that imitation, being a lighter reaction by its very nature, on repetition
easily turns into a need that motivates an organism towards communicating
with other individuals.
That is why imitation is, on the one hand, a stimulus for social life and, on
the other, an inevitable consequence of contacts between two and more
individuals leading a social existence.
One may positively claim that sociality emerges not only due to its benefits
for the species but also due to the immediate, i.e., biological, individual
benefits of imitation itself, which acquires particular significance as a means
of the borrowing by one individual every new success and invention from
another.
If, as we begin to see, repeated imitation easily turns into a need, then
contact with one's own kind as a prerequisite for imitation also becomes a
need, and this resolves the issue of the whole social problem. We realize why
contact with one's own kind emerges in the organic world. It is generated by
the urge to imitate, which turns into a need whose satisfaction is inevitably
accompanied by a sthenic reaction. This is the main prerequisite for a social
instinct.
Any game in general is based on imitation and associated sthenic reaction.
Observe how excited two puppies are by their encounter, as a result of
which they immediately start playing with, and imitating, each other. What
stimulated the previously dormant energy in both of them? It was the
opportunity to indulge in contact and imitation as a stimulating agent
accompanied by a sthenic reaction that leads to play and is in itself inevitably
associated with imitation. And, indeed, if we look carefully at any play, we
shall see that it consists of mutual imitation—particularly by the less
experienced of the more experienced—which is the social meaning of play as
a method of natural learning for an organism in the process of development.
It becomes clear from the above that, as an inevitable result of imitation,
one of the vitally useful conditions for an organism is communication; this is
a natural stimulating agent needed by the organism under all circumstances
when it is in its prime and not suffering from fatigue. This need appears to be
as organic as the need for light, warmth, nourishment, etc.
It is worthy of attention that repetitiveness of collective action causes the
masses to adopt a particular disposition regarding coordination of individual
actions, and this disposition is to a significant degree determined by
imitation. Everyone knows how much training is needed to achieve the
properly coordinated actions of army detachments, how many rehearsals are
needed for actors to achieve coordination in their actions on stage, how many
sessions of singing practice precede a public performance of a choir, or how
many rehearsals are held by an orchestra preparing to show its program on
stage. And yet, this coordination is invariably achieved as a result of practice.
It is therefore clear that experience and imitation have an enormous
significance for a composite personality, not only with respect to reproducing
an action performed on a single occasion, but also with respect to the
collective coordination of actions performed by a mass of people in a
particular order and sequence.
Notes
1 On the sthenic reaction, see V. Bekhterev, Objective Reflexology (in Russian), 2nd edition.
2 See V. Bekhterev, General Principles of Human Reflexology (in Russian), Petrograd, 1918.
11
Collective Hereditary-Organic
Reflexes
As we have mentioned before, one may describe all manifestations of
collective activity as collective reflexes or collective reactions.
Manifestations of these collective reflexes, however, differ from one case
to the next. To achieve a better understanding of various manifestations of
collective reflexes, we are going to examine in sequential order collective
hereditary-organic reflexes (instincts), collective mood and collective
mimico-somatic reflexes, collective concentration, collective observation,
collective judgment, and collective actions.
Let us begin with collective hereditary-organic reflexes.
As in individual life we distinguish hereditary-organic reflexes, so in social
life one has to be aware of the manifestations of the same type of collective
reflexes. 
Their 
immediate 
goal 
is 
self-preservation—particularly
nourishment, satisfaction of sexual needs, attraction to communal living, etc.1
Satisfaction of natural needs with respect to food, drink, protection from
inclement weather and cold is usually accompanied by the corresponding
positive, or sthenic, mimico-somatic reaction on the part of the vegetative
functions, manifested among other things by increased cardiac and
respiratory activity, as well as by movements and actions of an offensive
nature that lead, or are supposed to lead, to the achievement of the most
complete satisfaction of the corresponding needs (the process of eating is
associated with obtaining food products, processing them appropriately and
consuming them with the purpose of satiation; protection from inclement
weather is associated with using clothes, setting up a dwelling, etc.).
Let us assume that a certain country or region is suffering from famine, as
was the case during the revolution in Russia—particularly in its capitals,
Moscow and Petersburg. As a result of the social conditions of our existence,
the problems of food supply move into the foreground. They fill the pages of
newspapers and magazines, and wherever you go the main subject of
conversation is our daily bread. Whether one is visiting a friend or runs into
someone in the street, the conversation will sooner or later turn to food
rations, food products, and eating. Naturally, while standing in long lines in
front of the bakeries, people not only talk about bread but also argue about
the supposed perpetrators of famine. The same is true for individual and
group actions: here one deals with the major influence of the unsatisfied need
for nourishment that resulted in innumerable cases of theft and robbery. In a
word, the whole society, formerly preoccupied with the most diverse
interests, is now transformed: its most important interests concern
nourishment and food. The rest seems to recede into the background before
the interests of the stomach, which dominate everything else.
In all cases, the actual stimulus of an offensive reaction is a need that arises
with particular force when it is not satisfied, whereas total satisfaction—
oversatiation in particular—is accompanied by a defensive reflex.
The nature of the reaction itself is determined by the effect of external
influences on the collective. Beneficial external influences stimulate a sthenic
reaction and lead to offensive actions aimed at intensifying and prolonging
these influences, whereas adverse external influences are accompanied by an
asthenic reaction that stimulates a desire to be free of them or, in other words,
creates an impulse towards movements and actions of a defensive nature.
Since natural needs or attractions underlie the very makeup of individual
mentality and are caused by organic conditions that are inherited rather than
acquired like other needs, we classify them as hereditary-organic reflexes
leading to actions that ensure satisfaction of natural needs.
Due to the fact that satisfaction of various hereditary-organic reflexes, such
as defense from unfavorable conditions, is in many cases easier to achieve
through collective rather than individual efforts, this circumstance often
serves as an impulse for joint action. Every socially raised individual who is a
member of a community strengthens the latter by virtue of his or her
membership, since every community provides protection and defense of its
individual members. It is the socialization of this hereditary-organic reflex
that led to the emergence of the norms of communal living and to the
establishment of power as an organizing principle that provides internal and
external protection of the community itself.
Hereditary-organic reflexes are subjected to the appropriate regulation by
the same concomitant sthenic reaction mentioned above. When hereditary-
organic reflexes—whether in animals or in humans —lead to results that
satisfy the needs of the collective, the latter, as we know, develops a sthenic
reaction that maintains the reflex and its further development until its
completion; on the other hand, when the reflex is incompatible with the needs
of the collective at the given moment, no sthenic reaction is stimulated and
the reflex itself is inhibited. In the end, the goal achieved by this reflex
becomes, due to the previous experience, a more or less permanent stimulus
and as such stimulates, along with the hereditary-organic reflexes, personal
ones as well, which, in this case, assist the hereditary-organic reflexes and
develop in the same direction. In this way, due to past experience, a habit is
eventually formed, which contributes to the achievement of the goal even
before the impulse that develops under the influence of organic stimuli
reaches the appropriate tension. Furthermore, for the purpose of achieving a
goal a collective experience can develop a means to which any individual—
irrespective of the goal to be achieved in accordance with the reflex—would
be totally indifferent; with the development of the collective experience,
these means, due to an associative reflex, begin to act as the stimulating
agents of a sthenic reaction and, consequently, of the corresponding actions.
Such are the above-mentioned money and other signs and documents (as, for
example, treaties etc.), which evolved as a result of the collective experience
of exchanging commodities and which led to the satisfaction of needs for
food, acquisition of clothes and other goods that enhance one's prosperity.
One should also bear in mind that a collective organism can draw on the
support of the individual experience of its members, which under certain
conditions is socialized through imitation. Let us presume that a given
locality is suffering from famine. It stimulates the population to engage in
active behavior, which under the influence of acute hunger and malnutrition
may assume the form of pogroms. In such cases the perpetration of pogroms
and pogrom behavior as such are initiated by the individual members of the
crowd. When a crowd stimulated by malnutrition behaves aggressively, it is
enough to point out the culprit of the people's tribulations for the crowd to
perpetrate its mob law, without bothering to find out whether he is, indeed,
guilty. Under the same conditions, a crowd that has approached a baker's
shop stops its actions when coming against an obstacle deemed
unsurpassable. But then someone brings a crowbar or an axe and strikes the
first blow to the door, and the crowd joins in breaking the door in order to get
inside the shop and loot it. In this case, individual initiative and actions meet
with collective support and involve other elements of the composite
personality in joint participation through imitation, which results in
socialization. Therefore, individual experience and individual initiative are of
colossal importance in collective actions. It is due to them that a given person
usually becomes the leader of the crowd that follows him blindly.
Thus, it is not necessary for the whole mass of people who form the crowd
to possess any previous experience of achieving a particular goal. It is enough
for at least one individual in the whole crowd to possess such experience, and
the crowd is capable of a collective action under his leadership, as long as we
are dealing with means and ways accessible to everyone. This certainly does
not mean that a composite personality needs no experience whatsoever or that
an action performed only once leaves no traces. On the contrary, in a mass of
people, experience is always valuable as it generates a collective habit. A
successful robbery encourages a band of robbers to engage in the same kind
of robbery henceforth. A successful pogrom encourages a crowd to perpetrate
pogroms in the future. Thus, collective habits come into existence and
encourage future collective actions in the form of reproductive reflexes of a
collective nature.
A collective inclination towards self-preservation also underlies the basis
of party tactics and state politics. It guides the actions of the crowd when it
flees from shooting soldiers. The same inclination directs the actions of
organized masses—e.g., troops—when they are threatened with one or
another kind of danger. And finally, the same inclination guides the actions
of state authorities in defense of their international rights and their position in
the country. This is why any power, under the influence of its inclination to
self-preservation, defends its own existence, even though this may go against
the interests of the popular masses. The best, though not perfect, remedy
against this phenomenon is re-elections of the authorities after a certain
period of time, as soon as the majority of the elected representatives forms an
opposition regarding some important issue of government. One should not
forget, however, that frequent change of power has its disadvantages in other
respects, while during the periods of such popular crises as wars, for
example, this is totally undesirable, for it is difficult to set in motion the
mechanism of power that would possess sufficient experience and authority
at a time when the country is in danger and the house is, so to speak, on fire.
The same inclination to self-preservation in a collective organism leads to
preparatory work on defense in the form of constructing fortresses,
manufacturing the means of defense and attack, etc., to the exploitation of
natural resources such as fuel, and finally to the exchange of commodities
and the institute of trade and commerce.
In all of these cases, cooperation facilitates the satisfaction of the collective
inclination towards self-preservation, which naturally results in this
inclination leading to a particular type of cooperation and reinforcing the
communal principle. The same inclination, however, lies at the root of all
wars and plundering raids that divide national collectives into hostile camps
opposing one another. Inclination towards self-preservation also explains the
emergence of religious doctrines, for they reveal human need for protection
against the unknown and mysterious forces of nature and the surrounding
enemies.
One should note, incidentally, that the inclination to self-preservation also
manifests itself in an antisocial manner in all those cases when the whole
collective or the majority of its members face mortal danger. This will be
discussed below.
Attraction to nourishment in social life is at the basis of cooperation in
hunting and fishing; it led to the domestication of animals, to poultry
farming, the exploitation of plant and animal food products, the development
of farming and cultivation of cereals, vegetables, fruit trees, and fodder crops,
as well as to the industrial production of food products. Inasmuch as the
procurement of food products and the exploitation of the environment for this
purpose require cooperation, this attraction maintains and stimulates sociality.
In this respect a significant role is played by the cooperative protection of
the products of labor associated with the procurement of food.
It is common knowledge that farming became possible only after reliable
protection against predators had become feasible.
It goes without saying that an unsatisfied attraction to nourishment is a
frequent cause of social movements against predators or even against
authorities that are incapable of organizing proper food supplies. This is why
a shortage of food products is the principal organizer of the so-called revolts
of the hungry.
The collective inclination towards self-preservation led among other things
to the establishment of the right to own land for the purpose of procuring
food products, to building houses for protection against inclement weather,
etc. Both tasks usually require collective efforts achieved through the
interactions of an economic nature, which underlie the living conditions of a
collective. The same interactions led to the establishment of civil law.
In the course of time, due to the abuse of the right of property by certain
individuals and the exploitation of others for one's own benefit as its
consequence, this right evolves in the direction of socialist tendencies.
However, in this case, too, the heart of the matter in terms of providing for
the needs of every member of a given collective remains unchanged, the only
difference being a more equitable distribution of commodities that provide
for the living conditions of the populace.
Out of the hereditary-organic reflexes of self-protection against attack and
enemies there emerged in collectives the institution of criminal law wherein
the protection of individual life in society is entrusted to a specially elected
organ in the form of a judicial tribunal and executive power.
As the longevity of a family collective is not measured by the lives of its
individual members but includes posterity and the transition from the older
generations to the younger ones, it is natural that in capitalist countries the
right of property inheritance by descendants comes into existence.
This right of inheritance presupposes the continuity of the personality as a
unit of the collective through one's descendants or close relations, and
inheritance therefore assumes the form of the property of one's descendants
or closest relatives. With the changed views on the relationships between
individual members of a collective, it is also possible to change the right of
property inheritance in a socialist direction, i.e., to make inherited property
the common possession of the collective, recognizing it as state or public
property, which, in this case, is the same.
The manifestations of the same hereditary-organic reflexes of self-
preservation also generate in collectives other economic institutions, such as,
for example, the processes of exchange and commercial relations, the
invention of banknotes as the compensation for commodities, etc., as well as
the proliferation of stealing.
As for another hereditary-organic reflex characterized by sexual attraction,
in civilized societies it provides the foundation of the family —the small cell
of peaceful cohabitation, which is at the basis of family life, family traditions,
and institutions that underlie family law. It was the family that originally
introduced cultivation of cereal crops and exploitation of domestic animals.
The family was also responsible for the emergence of clans and tribes, which
led to the appearance and defense of national principles that give rise to tribal
and national strife and wars.
The significance of sexual attraction responsible for reproduction has
become particularly clear since Malthus published his work. It was he who
pointed out that if this attraction did not encounter any inhibition in the social
environment, the population growth was particularly fast; in civilized
countries, however, reproduction is much lower than its maximum potential,
which implies that these countries have certain correctives with respect to the
given attraction. In the final analysis, they are reduced to its inhibition by
placing natural limits on it or by taking artificial measures against
reproduction. In this respect, a relatively slow increase in food products is of
particular importance. Because of this, the population of most European
countries was growing extremely slowly over many centuries, with the
exception of the latest historical period, starting from the nineteenth century,
when the necessary means of subsistence began to increase with the invention
of the machines that are so important in every industry. Economic reasons
among other factors undoubtedly exercise a particular influence on the state
of this instinct. Thus, statistics positively established that the number of
marriages and births in different countries is directly dependent on the prices
of vital products, as well as on the conditions in agriculture and commerce.
As we know, due to the same conditions, the population of modern France
stopped growing except for immigration.
The limitation of sexual attraction in civilized countries is manifested
through the establishment of the "two-children system," maintained by means
of miscarriages and "incorrect" sexual relations, voluntary self-restraint,
infanticide, etc., which in the long run results in a quantitative decrease in
population growth and in the declining role of the family in social life.
Although infanticide assumes enormous proportions among the uncivilized
peoples, it is not even regarded as a crime.
The parental "instinct," which is closely connected to sexual attraction, is
another pillar of the family community. The family exists among all higher
animals whose males and females take care of their offspring on the basis of
cooperative parenting.
When turning our attention to modem humanity, one should bear in mind
that ethnography does not know of a single tribe where the family does not
exist in one form or another. Only among the Negro tribes is the joint
parenting of the offspring restricted to the period during which the child is
breast-fed and unable to walk. Even here, however, we are dealing with the
family, albeit in a more limited form.
In the civilized countries of the European and Asian continent, the stability
of the family is recognized as a prerequisite of the healthy condition of the
society and the state.
The family as a consequence of the sexual and the parental "instincts" is
the progenitor of motherhood that ensures the upbringing of young children,
and since both are essential in the existence of modem society, the latter
supports and protects the family principle in every possible way through the
appropriate laws.
Due to the particular importance of the family with respect to the
replenishment of the population, any state collective supports the institution
of the family, as well as the institution of family law associated with it.
For this reason both the institute of marriage and the parental duties are
protected in all civilized countries by civic and religious sanction, through a
special dispensation that became part of tradition, religious ritual, and state
laws. This protection and support of marriage is the stronger the more
conservative are the state structure and society composition.
One may presume that those societies that did not maintain and improve
this protection in due measure were doomed to degeneration and extinction.
The weakening of this social protection in the form of the parental "instinct"
played a considerable role in the disintegration of the highly civilized
societies of ancient Greece and Rome.
Incidentally, enormous support of the family structure is provided by the
veneration of ancestors, which leads to the stability of the nation. Ancestor
worship greatly contributed to the stability of ancient Rome, as nowadays the
same ancestor worship contributes to the stability and power of Japan and
China.
It goes without saying that the sexual attraction responsible for
reproduction has as its secondary consequence the development of closer
social bonds among the members of a clan or a tribe, as well as the
improvement of moral principles in the social milieu, for maternal upbringing
establishes the norms of social structure, eliminating rough treatment of
subordinates. Regardless of economic and political reasons, the abolition of
slavery and serfdom, too, occurs to a significant degree due to the gentler
conditions of upbringing itself. Further, the emergence of charity in society
and humanitarian regulation of the most cruel manifestations of state egoism
such as war, for example, is also to a significant extent a product of the
parental "instinct" and of the gentler conditions of upbringing associated with
it. Even the demands of the popular masses meet with greater responsiveness
on the part of society's ruling classes due to the same parental "instinct" and
to a gentler upbringing.
However, one should bear in mind that society did not evolve out of the
family, as some presume, and that sociality in the animal kingdom emerged
far earlier than the family did. Society emerged primarily from natural
cooperation established for the purposes of obtaining food by hunting, of
protection from enemies and the pursuit of predators. These joint efforts are
based on the inclination towards imitation and sociality, which brings
together individuals who possess the means of communication and cohesion
in the form of appropriate facial expression and sound language. Little by
little, interpersonal communication becomes a need, due to the mutual
exchange of individual experience and the ability to imitate, which is also
useful as a skill. This is mainly what lies at the basis of social life maintained
through natural and social selection.2, 3
Biological data leave no doubt that social attraction is characteristic of all
animals and even plants that exist in communities. In the human world,
however, communities assume the form of political societies.
Admittedly, not all biologists recognize the "social instinct" as such, but
the real issue concerns the facts, rather than the words. We know, however,
that chickens, hatched artificially away from their family, keep together from
the very start and follow one another without any preliminary training.
The "social instinct" provided the foundation of the human language—this
important means of sociality, which unites people to a greater degree than
even religious or economic conditions.
If individual hereditary-organic reflexes caused by the imperative internal
stimuli (hunger, sexual needs, etc.) in some cases overwhelm all other
desires, it is quite obvious that collective hereditary-organic reflexes can be
manifested even more prominently due to the fact that in a crowd, in addition
to all the other conditions, there is an element of contagion, while at the same
time there are no sufficient conditions for restraining the desires, and as a
result once these reflexes manifest themselves there is no restraining or
inhibiting them. There are more than enough examples of this both from the
sphere of inclination to self-preservation—food in particular— and from the
sphere of sexual attraction.
Inclination towards self-preservation manifests itself particularly strongly
when a mass of people face the issues of life and death as, for example,
during major disasters that affect great numbers of people.
Alongside what one can describe as heroic actions of relatively few
individuals who sacrifice themselves for the sake of saving others, the
majority in these cases manifest the inclination towards self-preservation in
all its crudeness and nakedness when people destroy others in order to save
their own lives. This is what happens during big fires that break out in
crowded public buildings. This is what happens during shipwrecks. This is
what happens at the time of any other mass disaster. Those who have read the
description of a fire in one of the summer theatres in Paris, or who have read
the details of the destruction of the ocean liner Titanic, will easily recall the
striking and at the same time repulsive manifestations of the reflex of self-
preservation in a collective environment under the above conditions.
One observes a different type of manifestations of collective inclinations
towards self-preservation in cases of food shortages. Here we usually deal
with riots and pogroms that are often accompanied with extraordinary
violence. The following account of a riot at the Apraksin Market in Petrograd
during the revolution illustrates how this kind of unrest arises from the
shortages of vital products:
Barrels with merchandise that have just arrived from the railway station
were being unloaded from a cart into the Apraksin Lane fabric store
owned by Mr. Yagodkin. Some passers-by noticed that the merchandise
was packed in barrels rather than canvas sacks, as it is usually done.
Some ten busy-bodies gathered around and approached the carter, asking
him what was in the barrels. One of the shop-assistants overheard the
questions and shouted irritably:
"It's none of your business. This is our merchandise and that's that."
At this moment one of the barrels unexpectedly slipped out of the carter's
hands, fell on the ground and broke into pieces. Sweet tea-biscuits spilled
out of the barrel.
Within a minute the shop was surrounded by a crowd of common people
swearing and shouting:
"You damned marauders! Hiding food! Kill them!"
The crowd was growing by the minute. Its mood was extremely volatile.
A few people started breaking open other barrels from which candies,
crackers and various metal objects—horse-shoes, cramp-irons, files—
spilled out.
Edged on by some individuals calling for reprisal, the crowd stormed into
the shop. The frightened employees ran away. The owner of the shop, Mr.
Yagodkin, escaped from mob law by hiding in the Commissariat.
One person who suffered grievously, however, was an innocent Jew who
happened to be in the crowd by chance.
Ignorant of what was going on, he asked someone:
"Why has a crowd gathered? Did they catch a thief?"
Instead of an answer a hooligan yelled "Kill theYids!" and struck him on
the head, after which the blows fell thick and fast on the unfortunate man.
He was barely ali ve when some militiamen managed to save him from
being lynched. Due to the extremely militant mood of the crowd, troops
were called in to assist the militia. The soldiers of the Pavlovskiy
Semionovskii and the 1st Machine-Gun regiments arrived, as well as a
military detachment.
When they heard the crowd shouting "The marauders are hiding the food,
we should search all the warehouses," some soldiers supported this
demand.
Sokolovskii's warehouse and Ginzburg's shop were immediately cordoned
off. The crowd and the soldiers carried out the search and found large
amounts of sugar, biscuits, sweets, as well as rolls of various fabrics.
All the discovered goods were loaded on carts and sent to the
Commissariat.
The next day the unrest recurred on an even larger scale.
The owner of a fabric store, Mr. Anter, was in the process of dispatching
several rolls of merchandise to the station to be transported to Pskov,
Yuriev and Revel.
At this moment a soldier approached him and with the words "Aha, you
are going to send off some foodstuffs, aren't you?" hit him in the face with
his fist. A crowd gathered immediately and rushed off, shouting threats,
into several shops in search of the allegedly hidden food.
Rolls of merchandise were thrown onto the ground, boxes were broken
open spilling various goods—galoshes, shoes, etc. All in all the search was
thus carried out in 15 warehouses and shops—Ivanov's, Yeliseev's,
Ponizovskii's, Tiukov's and others. The rest avoided the search by closing
down their shops in a hurry.
They say that in Tiukov's store they discovered such incompatible goods
as boots and almonds. Under the crowd's pressure, the militia removed
from Yeliseev's store five cartloads of textiles.
All day long carts with the requisitioned merchandise and food were
coming out of the Apraksin Market.
The crowd rushed to every shop from which the owners or shoppers were
carrying out merchandise and demanded that the wholesalers should sell
by retail only. The crowd and the soldiers prohibited the sending of
merchandise to Finland and to other cities, threatening to loot those shops
whose owners would not comply.
Many frightened merchants ran to the Commissariat, threw in the keys
from their shops and declared: "Do what you like, we are not going to
work under these conditions."
Another case we are going to mention here concerns mob rioting in a meat
line:
Due to the food shortages, a confrontation between the crowd on the one
hand and militia and the army patrol on the other took place at the corner
of Degtiarnaia and Rozhdestvenskaia in front of a city meat shop.
On that day the shop was selling the usual meat rations.
Some time later the meat sales were stopped, and the shop-assistants
declared that everything was sold out.
The crowd did not believe them and began to push into the shop. Shouts
were heard: "They are lying. They are deceiving us. Beat up the crooks."
A militiaman tried to calm the crowd down and explained that the meat
was, indeed, sold out. The crowd did not take any notice and began looting
the shop.
Another militiaman and a military patrol from the Pavlovsky regiment
were summoned for assistance. All attempts to prevent mob violence were
in vain.
The enraged crowd attacked one of the militiamen, Mr. Grinberg, who
was trying to stop the looting of the shop, and beat him up mercilessly.
Seeing the futility of verbal appeals, the military patrol started firing in the
air. The gun-shots to a certain extent brought the rioting crowd under
control. The military detachments that arrived soon after dispersed the
mass of people.
The militiaman Grinberg, beaten up and bleeding, had to be sent to
hospital for medical help.
And here are some telegraph communications about the revolts of the
hungry in the German city of Stettin; brief as they are, these new items paint
a vivid picture of the bloody consequences of this kind of riot:
Stockholm. 13th (26th) June. A Swede, who arrived from Goteborg and
who had been in Stettin during the latest unrest, conveyed to the
employees of the Danish newspapers the following details:
The riots started on Monday, 5th of June. They were provoked by the
rumours that food products were being transported out of the city, even
though the city was suffering from food shortages. The crowd destroyed
and looted several shops, breaking the windows. Police were acting with
extreme cruelty. One young woman sustained sabre wounds. Seven people
were killed near the Ganse bridge. Machine-guns were positioned in the
centre of the city, and the soldiers behaved as if they were on a battlefield.
Machine-gun fire continued through the night. Ambulances were
constantly driving all over the city, transporting the wounded to hospitals.
The next day there were soldiers with machine-guns posted on many street
corners. The city appeared to be dead. Only in front of the shop run by the
commission on food supplies a crowd gathered, even though the sales
were not to start until the next day. On Wednesday the factories resumed
their work.
To further illustrate the manifestations of crowd animosity caused by the
shortages of vital products one can cite recent instances of unrest that took
place at the Sennaya Square in Petrograd:
In the morning, a crowd of women standing in line for bread in front of
the city shop at 6 Zabalkanskii Lane noticed that boxes with soap were
being carried out of G. E. Dolgov's fish shop. The women asked the
drayman where he was supposed to take this soap to. The drayman said he
was taking it to the Baltic Railway Station.
The women began to protest and called in a militiaman on duty. The
owner of the shop produced a permit for transporting the soap out of
Petrogad; this failed to convince the women, however, and they insisted
on dispatching the cartload of soap to the city general store at the corner of
Sadovaia Street and Tairov Lane, where the administration was ordered by
the crowd to sell it for 52 kopecks a pound.
At the same time two carts drove up to A. I. Znesin's meat shop at 42
Sadovaia Street to be loaded with leather for making gloves. Passers-by
were extremely surprised to see this kind of merchandise appear out of a
meat shop.
The Militia was informed, and the Deputy Commissar of the 3rd Spassky
district, Mr. Sharikov, and six militiamen arrived. When Sharikov began
to defend the owner of the meat shop and to explain to the crowd that
Znesin possessed all the documents permitting him to send the leather to
the Novgorod province, the crowd became even more agitated.
"This leather belongs to the commissars themselves—they have been
hiding it here," someone in the crowd said, and they assaulted the Deputy
Commissar Sharikov. He was knocked down and beaten mercilessly all
over. When the Deputy Commissar lost consciousness, they bound him
with strips of leather and hung a poster on his chest with the inscription
"This Commissar is a marauder." A passer-by wanted to intercede on
behalf of the victim, but the crowd attacked him as well; trying to escape
he ran into the district judge's chambers, but the crowd stormed into the
chambers, dragged the defender of the militia out into the street and beat
him up so severely that he had to be sent to the Obukhov hospital.
The crowd stopped a cab that was riding by and put the beaten-up Deputy
Commissar on it under the escort of three soldiers.
A cart was loaded with several boxes to make a platform on which they
put the beaten-up shop-assistant, also covered with pieces of leather and a
poster on his chest saying "A marauder merchant."
The cab and the cart accompanied by a part of the crowd moved along
Sadovaia Street towards Nevsky Prospekt, while the other part of the
crowd went to the Commissariat to demand explanations.
The appearance of two thousand people caused a panic in Gorstkina Street
where the offices of the 3rd Spasskii District Commissariat are situated.
Having realized that the crowd was in a militant mood, Commissar Ozol
decided to summon a military detachment or a fire brigade to protect the
Commissariat, but neither arrived. Some of the crowd broke into the
Commissariat, shouting threats against the Commissar and militia in
general. Nearly all the militiamen had recently been disarmed, but
fortunately two militiamen happened to have revolvers, and the
Commissar ordered them to fire in the air. These two shots produced such
a magic effect on the crowd that it started leaving the commissariat in a
hurry. It was only at 2 p.m., when life in Gorstkina Street had already
returned to normal, that the military detachment arrived.
In other cases, the inclination towards self-preservation assumes the form
of collective actions aimed at protecting property and national wealth from
any infringement and at defending religious beliefs. These include mob law
applied to thieves and robbers, lynching in America, national conflicts and
wars.
In general, any society—just like an individual personality—is interested
in its own welfare under the influence of its inherent inclination towards self-
preservation. Everything that sustains the welfare of society, stimulating in it
a sthenic reaction, generates the desire to possess, whereas everything that
acts adversely, stimulating an asthenic reaction, generates resistance and
counteraction. The longing for freedom and for defending one's rights also
arises from the inclination towards self-preservation, since damage to
freedom suppresses the vital activity of the composite personality.
People generally do not put up with the heavy fetters imposed, for
instance, by the capitalist state on the working masses, and underneath these
chains of economic slavery there always smolders a desire to break them and
escape from bondage. When the state regime and the oppression of the capital
reach a degree that the national collective can no longer tolerate, a coup d'état
called revolution ensues.
A state regime can gradually increase repressive measures to a
considerable degree until they reach a certain limit followed by an explosion
of popular discontent manifested in a riot or an uprising. The explosion of
wrath and revenge occurs sooner or later, depending on the living conditions
of the national collective itself, on whether the nation is disciplined, civilized,
industrious, rich or poor, etc., but it takes place only under certain conditions
as an inevitable effect of particular causes.
The collective attraction to self-preservation and freedom is also
manifested in the fact that when the masses win their rights through a
revolutionary upheaval, they first of all try to liberate their jailed brothers in
prisons and penitentiaries. This recurs during all revolutions, as a rule and a
law. We observed the same during the last Russian Revolution, and this
assault of the liberated people on jails occurred not only in the main centers
of the Russian Revolution-Moscow and Petrograd—but in all Russian cities.
The very seats of judgment, the so-called temples of Themis, were subjected
to destruction and arson, as we saw in the case of the Petrograd Regional
Court burned down by the crowd.
Some reprehensible manifestations of collective sexual desire have been
observed in certain sectarian gatherings, special get-togethers in the form of
Athenian evenings and even in the organized youth societies (ogarochnye
and others),4 which were active here during the period of the first revolution.
One comes across the same phenomena, however, even in uncivilized
countries. This is, for instance, what we read in Ch. Letourneau:
On the Society Islands, as well as many other archipelagoes there even
existed a famous religious fraternity—the Asroy (Areoi in French —E. L.)
association whose purpose was the licentious satisfaction of sexual needs.
It was a religious society. Their patron was the god Oro. All members of
this Polynesian Freemasonry were regarded as superior beings irrespective
of their gender. They were protected by special divine patronage, and after
their death their shadows were taken to a special paradise. The society
recognized total equality of its members; naturally, most of them belonged
to the aristocracy, but commoners, too, were accepted. The only difference
was that for the nobles the formalities were made simpler. This fact is
extremely curious, for in these tribes the nobles regarded themselves as
being made out of a far superior material than other mortals. The
requirements for being admitted to the fraternity were very strict. First, in
a fit of religious ecstasy, they had to prove that they possessed the divine
spirit; then they were accepted as novices. Months or even years later a
novice was subjected to another trial and had to take a great vow to kill
any children he may have from then on. He thus entered the seventh and
last rank of Asroy hierarchy; he was initiated into the sacred rites, songs,
dances and special mimic movements. Ascending all the steps of this
ladder was an extremely slow process; one had to go through new trials
and to reveal his abilities in oratory, singing or poetry. Finally, each new
stage of initiation was marked by a special tattoo.
The aim of the society was stimulation and unbridled satisfaction of erotic
passions. All female members of the society belonged to all male ones,
and couples came together for no more than two or three days. The life led
by the Asroys was an eternal festivity. It consisted of endless feasts, songs
and competitions. They even took trips together to other islands and were
met everywhere with great respect. Women would perform for the
members of the society an extremely sensual dance called Timoradia,
which is accompanied by appropriate singing and is very popular in
Polynesia. The sexual desires stimulated by it were satisfied immediately
and publicly, but children were not admitted and infanticide was enforced
as a strict duty. To be able to save her newborn, a woman from the Asroy
society had to find for the child a foster father among its members, but
then both of them were expelled from the society.
To belong to the Asroy fraternity was considered a great honour. One
Tahitian brought to England by Cook declared that he regarded himself
equal to the King of England because he was an Asroy.
One should note, however, that although we are dealing with the country
where chastity is totally unknown, this society established an erotic religious
cult similar to our sects of khlysty and others.
Sexual desire in a crowd is usually manifested in a crude and ugly form
that at times exceeds all likelihood. When the Austrian troops occupied
Lorraine during the Great French Revolution, the Croatians burned down the
monastery of Saint Nicholas, and many nuns fled to Chalons. Most of them
were raped by the enemy soldiers. They called it suffering martyrdom.
According to the nuns, some of them suffered this kind of martyrdom two,
three and even four times, while one nun suffered eight. This is what we read
in the article "The Defenceless" published in a Saratov newspaper:
To the noise of the events—both momentous and disturbing—some
nightmarish phenomena occur in our city.
I am talking about the unfortunate residents of Petin Street.
What is going on there turns one's heart to ice and paralyses one's mind.
Here are some quotations from Dr. I.V. Vyazemsky's report recently
presented in the Saratov Society of Physicians: "According to the
commander of the N division of the NN reserve artillery brigade, since
early in the morning long lines of soldiers wait their turn in front of the
houses of assignation in Petin Street: the number of soldiers per woman
reaches 40; there were cases when girls ran out of the houses crying "Save
me, I can't take it any more."
Having discussed this statement and having come to the conclusion that in
the centre of the city, before everybody's eyes, women are being tortured,
the physicians' committee arrived at the unequivocal decision to have the
houses of assignation closed.
A. Kalmanovich adds to this description:
The houses of assignation have not yet been closed, and some truly
horrible things continue to go on there: three days ago the drunken crowd
was rioting, breaking the windows, wrecking the furniture and beating the
women, while the "tortures," mentioned in the decision of the physicians'
committee, in some cases assumed unimaginable proportions when the
number of unrestrained men per woman reached one hundred.
One can no longer procrastinate with closing down the houses of
assignation. Protective measures have no effect whatsoever. Although
Petin Street has been closed off from both ends by chains, men get there
through one of the houses in the adjoining street: they climb up on the roof
and from there they climb down the drain-pipes.
One could also cite other examples of striking sexual licentiousness
particularly in a crowd, where people are not embarrassed to perform in front
of everybody the indecencies that individual persons out of propriety conceal
from the public eye.
It goes without saying that a crowd does not know—and has no desire to
know—any restraint, and often leaves nothing but corpses in the wake of its
violence, as it was the case in Kalushcha during the war after the start of the
revolution. We often observe it in other cases, when a crowd commits
violence upon unfortunate young women.
At this point there is no need to dwell on the manifestations of the "social
instinct" in collectives, but there is no doubt that it, too, is expressed in an
imperative manner. This is confirmed by a more than cruel attitude of a
collective to telltales, informers, spies, stool-pigeons, strike-breakers and, in
general, all those who one way or another violate the rules of collective life.
It goes without saying that under conditions of combat life the "social
instinct" is expressed through some exceedingly striking manifestations, and
its violation often leads to tragic and bloody events; law, as we know,
imposes capital punishment even for attempted treason in the army.
Notes
1 The available literature offers a wide variety of opinion on the number of "instincts," or biological
needs, in man. Thus, James, as well as Ellwood and McDougall, distinguish a great number of
instincts, while Darwin recognizes only a limited number of instincts in man. According to Wagner,
there are only three of them: nourishment, reproduction, and self-preservation. We regard
nourishment as a particular kind of the instinct of self-preservation. Attraction to community,
however, should also be included in the category of instincts.
2 V. Bekhterev, "Social Selection and Its Biological Significance" (in Russian), Vestnik znaniya, 1912.
"Individual and Social Factors of Progress and Sociality as aPrereq-uisite of Progress" (in Russian),
Vestnik psikhologii, 1913. "The Significance of Hormonism and Social Selection in the Evolution of
Organisms" (in Russian), Priroda, 1916.
3 See V. Bekhterev, Suggestion and Its Role in Social Life (in Russian), 3rd edition, K. Rikker.
4 Ch. Letourneau. Evolution de la morale, 2nd edition, p. 142.
12
Collective Mood and Collective
Mimico-Somatic Reflexes*
When dealing with an individual person, one can evaluate his mood from a
purely subjective point of view, as is done by a psychologist, by analogy with
his own self, guided by the evidence provided by the person who experiences
the given mood. Reflexology, however, does not need this subjective
evaluation, and collective reflexology has an even lesser need for this kind of
subjective evaluation of such states, for even if it does discuss collective
mood, it assesses this mood, or tonus, exclusively from a strictly objective
standpoint, taking into consideration facial expressions of the members of the
collective, their gestures, the nature of their exclamations, intonation, signs of
approval or disapproval, a particular attitude to a certain fact, etc.—in a word,
everything we describe by the term mimico-somatic reflexes.
As in individual reflexology, the mood of a collective may be lively in one
case, apathetic and depressed in another, or friendly, unfriendly, angry,
patriotic, religious, etc.
It is common knowledge that a friendly mood is characterized by a
collective's benevolent attitude toward certain persons and phenomena, all of
this occurring in predominantly calm tones and without any particularly
intense manifestations. Occasionally, however, it is characterized by an
outburst of a friendly disposition and touch in the form of embraces, kisses,
etc.
An unfriendly mood of a collective is characterized by an inimical attitude
expressed in a milder degree through cautious actions, avoidance of contact,
etc., while in a more pronounced degree this mood is characterized by the
facial expression of hatred, by threats and the desire to engage in verbal
abuse and fighting.
A collective patriotic mood, typical of most people living in an organized
society, is characterized by the manifestations of attachment to one's
collective and by the desire to support and defend it when necessary through
all possible means. Patriotic excitement represents the apogee of patriotism,
as it were, characterized by appropriate exclamations, gestures, and threats
directed at the enemies, etc.
The religious mood of a collective can be observed in any temple, where it
is characterized by a calm, silent concentration on the words of the prayer and
on the external manifestations of worshipping the deity. A higher degree of
religious mood is characterized, as well, by the appropriate facial expression,
by emotional declarations of profound repentance of one's sins, by wringing
one's hands, beating one's chest, and similar manifestations of self-torture (in
some sectarian rituals).
During certain periods, a collective mood, or tonus, can reach an
extraordinary degree of intensity and change into an acute mimico-somatic
reflex of a particular kind in the form of anger, for example, or depression
and fright.
One should not forget that an animated mood and the corresponding
mimico-somatic reflexes are invariably accompanied by a sthenic collective
reaction characterized by a certain readiness for offensive actions, whereas a
depressed mood and the corresponding mimico-somatic reflexes are
accompanied by an asthenic reaction leading to defensive actions and
religiosity, or to total confusion in its more severe manifestations.
Thus, mood prepares the ground for particular collective decisions and
actions, just as in individual reflexology.
An animated mood together with a sthenic reaction always raises the
energy of a collective, whereas a depressed mood leads to the depletion of its
energy.
Therefore, at the beginning of any meeting, in order to stimulate a mass of
people for action, inflammatory or stimulating speeches are made to electrify
the audience, elevate its mood and encourage thereby the adoption of a
particular decision that will lead to the appropriate action.
To provide a vivid example of an inflammatory speech let us dwell for a
moment on a description of the concert-meeting "The Loan of Freedom" that
took place in Pavlovsk during the period of the February Russian Revolution.
The Committee of the Employees of State and Private Credit Institutions
that organized 'three days of the Loan of Freedom' moved the centre of its
energetic activity to the Pavlovsk railway station last night...
"Buy the bonds of Freedom," one hears everywhere. "Fulfil your duty to
the Motherland!" "Exchange your rubles for bonds." "The Motherland
expects everyone to fulfil his duty."
The beautiful summer evening contributed to the success of the concert-
meeting. The public turned out in huge numbers, as if it were the opening
of a concert season.
At this meeting it was the speech by the sailor Fedor Batkin, who was
greeted by a storm of applause, that attracted the greatest interest.
"We are now living through the most tragic, the most horrific and at the
same time the most glorious period of our history," begins the speaker,
Yesterday we were slaves, today we are free citizens. Yesterday we were
people without the right to think, today we are a nation that won the right
to demand.
What can be happier and greater than this? But at the same time what can
be more terrible for a noble and great nation that has always responded
generously to the needs of her neighbors than to become pathetic and
ostracised, and perhaps even to lose the right to be called a state?
"Much has been said about the causes of this decline," continues the
speaker,
but where do we seek salvation—this is the burning question. We must
get at the root of the danger that threatens us. So let us ask ourselves
where this danger is? It is not in a handful of traitors in khaki greatcoats
deserting from the front and shamefully abandoning their positions to the
attacking enemy; nor is it in the worm that has been eating away at the
core of the state organism, for a grassroots explosion of self-renewal and
self-purification can easily get rid of it. The danger is in the indifference
and passivity of Russian society, which is enveloping its large segments
with an ever-growing power and persistence.
"Shame," energetically exclaims the speaker,
shame to a great country, which is unable to achieve unity even at the
most crucial turning point in its history. Shame to all those who are to
blame for the present destruction. Shame to all those who do not bother to
think about the fate of the motherland and who have to be reminded of the
example set by the tiny, heroic Belgian nation of six million who showed
how to die for the honour of one's country.
Do the Russian people know what the honour of the Motherland is? No,
they do not, but if there are some who know this, they—those few—will
be able to speak out about it.
"We are 'resolutionaries' rather than revolutionaries," says the speaker.
Are we indeed incapable of doing and fulfilling even a part of what we
need—desperately need—to fulfil?
Now we are facing this part, this small portion of what is urgently needed
to be done, which is the obligation to give our state a loan of money for
saving the motherland. If Russian citizens fail to comprehend the sacred
duty towards the fatherland, which compels one to make sacrifices for its
welfare without thinking, let those whose conscience is paralysed with the
deadly and heavy sleep of lethargy understand that they should do the
same from sheer egoism.
If they do not wish to be the architects of the temple of freedom, let them
realize that they are building with their own hands a grim prison for
themselves and their children.
"Our motherland is in danger," concludes the speaker, "your mother is
dying. Is there indeed a son who would refuse her the means of recovery!"
After Batkin's speech, drowned in prolonged applause, they opened a
subscription for the bonds, which continued throughout the whole evening
and produced results that exceeded the organizers' expectations.
Mood also determines the attitude of a gathering towards the speeches and
actions of individual persons or toward certain phenomena of social life in
general. That is why, when making his speech, an adept speaker who wants
to sway his audience always takes into account its general mood. When he
disagrees with the general orientation of the gathering, he should express it
rather cautiously and gradually.
Everyone knows how difficult it is to go against the general mood of a
gathering and how relatively easy it is to speak in tune with the general
mood.
To prove this point, I shall take an example from the history of our
revolution. At the night session of the Congress of the Party for National
Freedom, one of the delegates who participated in the debates on P. N.
Miliukov's speech protested against the inclusion of party members in the
Government and attacked A. F. Kerensky when the latter's star was still
shining brightly:
This man is represented here as a victim of the Soviet of Workers' and
Peasants' Deputies. And we—the Party for National Freedom—are
supposed to save this victim at any cost. So we have come here and are
trying to save him, and this is what Nekrasov is magnanimously praising
us for. But is A. F. Kerensky, indeed, a victim? Was he protesting against
the destruction perpetrated by the Soviets?
Then the speaker declares unexpectedly:
"The author of Decree No. 1 was N. D. Sokolov, but we are persistently
told that A. F. Kerensky himself participated in drafting it."
"Rubbish," shouts a voice from the audience, and a pandemonium breaks
out. "Shut up! Down with them! Nonsense..."
One can hear feeble applause here and there.
The speaker tries to continue his speech but a storm breaks out in the
audience once again.
A particular mood in a gathering may cause some characteristic incidents.
Here is one example. During the days of the Russian "Bonds of Freedom"
campaign, when these bonds were being sold in the atmosphere of general
support for this campaign, a big scandal broke out at the Luna Park theatre
when the actor Koshevskii appealed to the public to buy the "Bonds of
Freedom." As soon as the actor Koshevskii started talking about the goals of
the "Bonds of Freedom" campaign, someone shouted from a box:
"Go to hell, liar."
Pandemonium broke out. The whole crowd, one and all, rushed to the box,
intending to lynch the insolent fellow. Order, however, was soon regained,
and the culprit escorted by several people from the audience was sent to the
Commissariat.
Singing is occasionally used for raising the spirits, although just as often
singing itself serves as an expression of the mood. During the same "Bonds
of Freedom" campaign, the sounds of the Marseillaise resounded from the
open-air stage of the Summer Bouffe Theatre during the first intermission.
"Allons enfants de la patrie..." Kachenovskii was singing.
The singer's powerful bass attracted all the public who was asked to buy
the "Bonds of Freedom."
The public, one and all, rushed to the tables and bought nearly all the
available bonds.
In other cases, even external circumstances enhance the mood. Thus,
during the "Bonds of Freedom" days, a decorated automobile with
improvised bank booths arrived at the Zoological Gardens.
The arrival of the automobile created a furor, and the subscription was
highly successful.
In fact, not a single social movement can be launched without the
appropriate mood.
On the other hand, the nature of the mood determines not only the
corresponding energy of the action but also its direction.
One can observe in every gathering a particular mood that may change
even during the course of the gathering, depending on the particular
circumstances and on the speeches of the participants, i.e., it can either grow
in intensity or become weaker and depressed. It is invariably characterized by
the prevalent facial expression, gestures, exclamations, signs of approval
regarding particular expressions and theses, and finally, by the prevalent
content of the speeches and by the relative intolerance of those speeches that
are incompatible with the common mood.
Here is one of the numerous examples. At the time of general disarray
during the first period of the Russian Revolution, an officers' council held a
tense meeting:
The first speaker was the chairman, general Amosov. He painted a horrific
picture of disarray at the battle-fields and on the home front.
Other speakers, most of whom arrived from the front-line, confirmed the
dangerous situation in the army.
Officer Malevanov declared that no one seemed to be in charge and used a
highly inappropriate expression regarding our Government.
It caused an uproar in the audience.
People were shouting "Get out of here, down with him, this is disrespect
of authority, this is intolerable!"
The chairman ordered the speaker from the floor.
Collective mood also depends to a significant extent on the composition of
the gathering itself, on its greater or lesser homogeneity, on the subject of
discussion and its greater or lesser relevance to the interests of the
participants, on the given external circumstances, i.e., social environment,
and even on the greater or lesser importance of the decision to be made by a
gathering. Finally, the mood of a gathering reflects the disagreements
existing in the gathering itself, for they exacerbate the passions of individual
speakers, due to which this passionate attitude spreads across the whole
gathering by means of contagion. Therefore, the fewer disagreements there
are in a gathering the more tranquil the course of its activity. And on the
contrary, the more heterogeneous a gathering is the more tumultuous its
proceedings are.
Occasionally, there may be not one but two or more issues whose
discussion raises the mood of a gathering to the highest degree.
This happens particularly in those cases when a gathering whose
participants represent several different parties is preoccupied with a complex
subject. Thus, at the former State Assembly in Moscow, the first and highest
rise in mood appeared on the second day of the session, during the
confrontation between two opposing views on the ways of revitalizing the
state held by the socialist parties on the one hand and by the Party for
National Freedom on the other. A second rise in mood, albeit of a lesser
intensity, occurred on the third day of the session during which the issue of
improving the fighting efficiency of the Russian army was discussed.
The mood of any gathering is seldom determined from the start, unless it
has been prepared in advance by certain events. Usually, it is only after
several speakers voice their opinions that the nature of the collective mood
begins to become apparent and reaches a certain level when, just before the
final decision is made, the most intense confrontation of opinions takes place.
Therefore, the highest rise of the mood in a gathering usually coincides with
the moment of the sharpest discord. At this moment, one may always fear
certain excesses on the part of different members of the assembly.
After the mood of a gathering has reached its apogee, it tends to drop more
or less quickly, and the gathering calms down.
The greater the size of a collective the more difficult it is to create a
common mood. It is natural, for example, that, because of the heterogeneity
of the individuals who make up a particular state collective, it is impossible
for the whole country to share the same common mood; in the final analysis
the latter represents the mood of individual circles, parties, associations,
classes and nationalities that populate a given country, and depending on the
degree to which certain events or undertakings correspond or fail to
correspond to the goals and aspirations of individual collectives, they
generate a depressed mood in some quarters and high spirits in others. Thus,
the term "common mood in a country" should actually be interpreted as the
prevalent mood or the mood in the majority of individual collectives in the
country; this mood may differ—and very radically—from one collective to
the next, yet one can still perceive in the total mass of the collectives a certain
tendency towards a particular direction exhibited by the majority of them.
Incidentally, a common mood is invariably reflected in election results,
although here much depends on the activity of one or another party. In any
case, the mood manifested at pre-election meetings and gatherings finds its
expression in the voting-papers dropped into the ballot boxes. Thus, the
outcome of the elections determines numerically, as it were, the nature and
the direction of public support for certain parties and persons; the final choice
seems to represent the resultant force of the preferences and aspirations of a
given society.
It stands to reason that the mood of a collective is, generally speaking,
unstable—just as is the mood of an individual person. Regardless of how
high the mood may rise as a result of a particular event, it inevitably goes
down with time. The degree to which public mood determines the attitude of
a collective to a particular event is exemplified by the cases when one and the
same speech provokes different responses depending on the nature of public
mood. Thus, there was a time when Kerensky's belligerent speeches were
