enthusiastically received by the population; but could they possibly provoke a
similar response during the subsequent period of the Russian Revolution
when a well-defined decidedly negative attitude to the external war prevailed
in the army? Certainly not.
When laying down the main principles on which peace should be
established, President Wilson made the following statement:
We believe so sincerely in the inevitable triumph of these ideas that I am
convinced I am expressing the mood and the will of the American people
when I declare that the United States are prepared to participate in any
possible coalition of nations aimed at achieving these ideals and at
protecting them against any violence.
This militant speech received the clamorous approval of the audience. This
was in May 1916, when the Great War had already lasted for nearly two
years, by which time the underwater war was threatening to violate seriously
the interests of the United States, and the public opinion of the American
nation learned much during this long world war. If, however, President
Wilson had made this speech before the Great War broke out, it would have
undoubtedly provoked a storm of protests. It goes without saying that the
mood does not appear to be the same in various social circles of the country,
as a result of which, for example, at the time when Kerensky was making his
speeches that were received with stormy approval at the front before the
offensive on the 18th of July 1918, one could hear all over Petrograd
speeches against the war that received no less approval by the popular masses
at meetings.
The story of Louis the Beloved illustrates the changes in the mood of the
popular masses: "The name Bien-Aimé (the beloved)," reports Carlyle,
quoting Hénault,
was given to Louis XV on the following occasion. When in 1744 this
monarch was rushing from one end of his kingdom to another, having
interrupted his conquest of Flanders in order to render assistance to
Alsace, he was overtaken in Metz by a malady that threatened to cut his
life short. When this news reached Paris, the city stricken with horror
resembled a city stormed by enemy troops. Supplications and moans
resounded in churches, the prayers of the clergy and the people were
constantly interrupted by sobs, so that this loving and tender interest
naturally produced the name Bien-Aimé—an honorary title that surpassed
all the others acquired by this great sovereign.
This is what was written in memory of the year 1744.
Another 30 years have gone by and the "great sovereign" is again
confined to bed by another illness, but what a change in the circumstances:
Paris is stoically calm; church services are not disrupted by sobbing
because no services are held except those ordered by the state for a fixed
fee per hour, and this kind of service is not usually interrupted by
anything. The pastor of his people whose heart is heavy with grief has
been transported from the small Trianon to his Château de Versailles and
put to bed. His flock knows about it but remains indifferent. At the most, a
mention of the royal illness appears among other news once in a while in
the endless stream of French conversation (that flows without interruption
day in, day out and comes to a halt only during the brief hours of the
night). One cannot help betting on this subject after all.. ..Moreover, some
people even dare to speak loudly in the streets; the May sun is shining
over the green field and over the city's bell-towers, the May sun is setting,
and the people are going about their own useful or useless business as if
no Louis were in mortal danger.1, 2
Such was the change that occurred over the period of thirty years in the
Frenchmen's attitude toward their king, but during the periods of
revolutionary movements, when popular masses experience in weeks and
months what they normally experience in years, this change in the mood and
in the attitude to their heroes does not take as long and occurs much faster.
An edifying example in this respect is provided by Kerensky, the hero of the
February Revolution in Russia. Destiny pushed this man—a former barrister's
assistant and subsequently the leader of the labor party in the State Duma—
into the front ranks of the revolutionary leaders immediately after the
overthrow of the monarchy. First he was the Minister of Justice, then
Chairman of the Council of Ministers and the Minister of War and Navy, and
then Head of the Provisional Government with unlimited powers as
Chairman of the Council of Ministers and the Supreme Commander-in-Chief.
Having joined the first revolutionary ministry as the Minister of Justice
from the Social-Revolutionary party, Kerensky immediately assumed in the
new ministry the status of a person who became the focus of attention and
hope for all the forces of revolutionary democracy. Subsequently his
popularity started growing by the hour, and his every step was discussed in
public. His handshake with a watchman and his condescending attitude
toward the highest bureaucrats in the ministry when he assumed his post
made him universally popular with the revolutionary masses. His remarkable
oratorical talent rapidly reinforced his popularity, for during the revolution
the masses needed speeches, more speeches and still more speeches.
At the same time, being the Deputy Chairman of the Soldiers' and
Workers' Soviet, his status in that milieu was such that any leader would envy
his popularity. That is why, when the first Chairman of the Council of
Ministers, Mr. Lvov, resigned, his post was given by right—unwritten but
universally accepted—to none other than Kerensky.
At the critical moment for the Government, when army discipline among
the troops began to disintegrate after the Declaration of Soldier's Rights was
published by the then Minister of War, Mr. Guchkov, who resigned from his
post with the hopeless statement that only a miracle could save Russia, all
eyes turned on Kerensky. He was perceived as the only person who could
lead Russia out of the dead-end and save the country. Then all the ministers
agreed that the War Minister's portfolio, as the most important one at the
time, should be given to Kerensky. His acceptance of this post while
retaining the title of the Chairman of the Council of Ministers made Kerensky
not only the ruler of the ministry, where he was worshipped to such a degree
that at any governmental crisis he was given the portfolios of all the
ministers, but also the ruler of all Russia. His presence among the troops and
his propaganda about revolutionary discipline sparked hope among the
Russian democratic forces that the revolutionary army, like the army during
the French Revolution, would enthusiastically assume the offensive and show
the world what heroic deeds the Russian revolutionary people could perform.
And, indeed, the Russian army that at the time was more involved in political
meetings than in fighting, that refused to obey officers and even dismissed
them arbitrarily, seemed to have come to its senses for a while and on June
18 mounted a successful offensive on the South-Western front, advancing
towards Lemberg in Galicia. The enemy troops began to sustain one defeat
after another. The day of June 18 turned out to be the triumph of none other
than Kerensky. Even though he did not participate in planning the military
operations, he won the glory, and the troops presented him with the Cross of
Saint George. At this point, his glory reached its zenith. His name began to
have a magical effect on the crowd. Thus, during my speech at a concert-
meeting in one of our large theatres, the mere mention of Kerensky's name in
connection with the presentation to him of the Cross of Saint George caused
the whole audience, as if it were charged with an electric spark, to jump to
their feet and start a loud and long ovation in his honor.
It goes without saying that ministers were mere pawns in his hands. Some
of them fawned on him in the full sense of the word, others barely managed
to remain within the limits of decent obedience. He manipulated everybody
as he pleased and fired the most deserving and prominent generals with a
wave of his hand. Thus he dismissed one after another such prominent
military commanders of our army as Alekseev, Brusilov, Kornilov, and
Yudenich. General Ruzskii was unfairly discharged even earlier, when the
post of War Minister was occupied by Guchkov and when the Council of
Ministers was headed by that same Kerensky. Finally, during the initial
period of the revolution, all the papers were full of news about Kerensky. His
every word was devoured and printed. The papers did not stint their praise
and glorification of Kerensky, who was sometimes described as the "pearl of
the revolution." But only a few months later his undecisive and maneuvering
policies brought about his downfall. His lucky star already began to set after
the shameful retreat of our troops in Galicia. Everybody realized that
speeches alone, even those of Kerensky, were unable to stop the
disintegration at the front and that the role of the "Persuader-in-Chief' of the
army was of no help either. His perpetual tendency to adopt a line of conduct
between the Soviet of Workers' and Soldiers' deputies on the one hand and
the so-called democratic strata on the other repulsed all bourgeois elements,
while the Soviets' gravitation towards bolshevism resulted in the left wing of
the democratic parties breaking up with him.
It was the Kornilov "revolt," however, in which, according to the bourgeois
elements, he played the role of an agent provocateur that sharply exacerbated
the attitude of the bourgeoisie towards him, while the introduction of capital
punishment and his constant hesitations and indecisiveness undermined the
trust he had previously enjoyed among the democratic elements.
It goes without saying that the Bolsheviks did not trust him from the very
start, and, after their first insurrection of 3-5 July, he became their actual
enemy.
As a result of all of the above, at the most critical moment in its history—
during the 25 October coup—the Provisional Government of which he was
the head failed to find any defenders for itself in the entire, numerous
Petrograd garrison, except for a few hundred cadets and a detachment of the
women's shock battalion. These forces were, naturally, defeated, the
Government was arrested, and the cadets were massacred in a bloodbath.
After that no one spoke with regret about Kerensky, who managed to escape.
He was, however, remembered, as the leader of the counterrevolutionary
troops he was bringing to Petrograd. But the headquarters focused their
attention on the troops and the outcome of future developments, rather than
on Kerensky.
Here yet again Kerensky demonstrated his indecisiveness, and his military
adventure ended up with the capitulation of the troops and his escape. Now
he was called a criminal by the very same people who had worshipped him
before. Soon they stopped talking about him altogether. No one cared any
more where he was and what he was doing. He was simply forgotten, and
forgotten forever.3 Thus, over the period of several months, the mood of the
popular masses had been changing with developments of historical events.
Similarly, the Great World War at the very beginning awakened patriotic
sentiments in every country; but after several years of warfare, the mood
gradually deteriorated even among those who had the opportunity to boost it
with victories. In the final analysis, Hindenburg, who declared that victory
would be granted to the side with the strongest nerves, was proved right.
However, irrespective of the natural decline or internal inhibition of
collective mood, the latter may also change in the opposite direction under
the influence of events. This kind of change in the mood is called its turning
point.
During a turning point, the mood of the masses completely changes its
direction due to the current circumstances. Thus, in the course of the Russian
Revolution, the Bolsheviks' insurrection in July was followed by a turning
point in the mood of Russian society's democratic circles, expressed in their
highly negative attitude towards the movements on the extreme left, which
used to attract many sympathizers. That did not last long, however, for after
the Kornilov affair on the 27th of August the public mood swung in the
direction of bolshevism yet again.
During the year 1905, people were clearly sympathetic to the revolutionary
movement, but following the subsequent excesses of a revolutionary nature,
and the so-called expropriations, the mood began to change rapidly: its final
turning point in the opposite direction occurred after the onset of the "bomb-
throwing" movement.
A vivid example of this kind of change in the mood of the popular masses
is furnished by the replacement of the militant aspirations of the Russian
army by its "fraternizing" with the former enemies during the initial period of
the Russian Revolution.
Here is another example dealing with the change in the mood of the
rebellious troops after the suppression of the Bolshevik insurrection of July 3-
5 in Petrograd. General Polovtsev, who had suppressed the insurrection and
had been working hard during those days, was relating in his hoarse, tired
voice how he was being thanked by the very commanders he had suspended.
The way they formulated their gratitude was also quite original: "For
demonstrating your authority!" Some may claim that only army-drilled
people, conditioned by fear and subordination, can feel gratitude for being
punished, offended, and suspended from their duties. But gratitude was
expressed even by the soldiers and sailors who had been rioting the day
before.
They say that when K—v was visiting the Commander-in-Chief, soldiers
of the Preobrazhenskii division were enraged and demanded that K—v be
handed over to them; Polovtsev had to convince the soldiers that K—v would
not be allowed to leave without their permission; as soon as comrade Gots
persuaded the soldiers to agree, K—v was spirited off down the backstairs.
Subsequently, after they cooled off and recovered from their rage, the
soldiers were thanking those who were around for having prevented them
from committing an act of insanity.
Here is another example of a rapid change in the mood of a collective.
According to the newspapers, some time after shooting their officers, the
soldiers of the ship Sebastopol started holding funeral services for them.
A turning point in the public mood completely changes the attitude
towards former idols and institutions that used to represent sources of
national pride. We saw this in the cases of Louis the Beloved and Kerensky,
but let us take another example: General Kornilov, the hero of the successful
offensive of 18 July 1917 and subsequently Supreme Commander-in-Chief of
the Russian army, was surrounded by an extraordinary aura of glory and
esteem. But when he led the so-called "revolt" against the Provisionary
Government, everybody or nearly everybody turned away from him, and his
former admirers became his most vehement detractors.
Another example: the former Chairman of the State Duma, Mr. Rodzianko,
was surrounded with no lesser aura of esteem and respect not only during the
period of existence of the Duma itself but also during the first days of the
revolution in which he participated to a certain extent. But what followed
afterwards? When the revolution gave power to the proletariat, he was
showered with all kinds of accusations, and resolutions of protest were
adopted all over the place. These kinds of examples abounded during the
Great French Revolution, as well as during all revolutions in general, and
even in all social movements of any significance.
Another example: during the Maillard tribunal, when aristocrats were
massacred under the pretense of the purely external legality of the tribunal,
very few escaped from the guillotine. But then an aristocrat, Jourgniac de
Saint-Meard presented the judges with evidence of his civic virtues,
mentioning that his soldiers loved him. He was pardoned, and as soon as the
verdict was pronounced, the crowd broke out into the jubilant: "Long live the
nation!" and the prisoner was enthusiastically accompanied from the
courtroom by the same crowd that was ready to gloat over every death
sentence.
This is how the volatile mood of the crowd was described by Marie-
Antoinette's foster brother who happened to be among the fortunate few who
had been spared the lot of the majority to be guillotined:
As soon as the guards raised their hats on the tips of their sabres and cried
out "Long live the nation," the crowd broke into furious applause; having
noticed that I was wearing white silk stockings, some women rudely
stopped the two soldiers who were holding me by the arms and said to
them: "Look out, you are making this gentleman walk in the gutter." They
were right: the gutter was full of blood. I was particularly struck by this
kind of attention on the part of those shrews, since they had just been
applauding violently the massacre of my fellow prisoners.4
Under normal circumstances, the faster and the higher the mood wave
rises, the faster and the lower it then drops, and vice versa, the slower it rises,
the slower it goes down. One should not forget, however, that the very rise in
mood can be caused by certain external stimuli outside the given society, in
which case the above rule cannot operate. Thus, for example, a declaration of
war by another country can immediately stimulate a tremendous upsurge of
the patriotic mood that will subside only gradually, as the developments
unfold. In the same manner, an unexpected military disaster can lead to an
explosion of patriotism in a given country, as occurred, for example, in
October 1917 in Italy where the population was fatigued by the war and the
disintegration of the army—similar to ours—had already begun. However,
the military catastrophe that resulted from the German offensive turned
socialists into so-called social-patriots, while the general patriotic mood
manifested in the slogan "Save the Fatherland" became dominant. Besides,
foreign troops quickly came to Italy's assistance, while her own wavering
detachments were moved behind the lines, which saved the situation.
How quickly society's mood can change is also illustrated by the bourgeois
sabotage after the October coup in Russia in 1917. Initially, bourgeoisie
represented by the Union of Unions declared a total boycott of the Bolsheviks
and rejected any cooperation with them, assuming an implacable position in
this regard. A few months later, however, the same Union of Unions was
forced to announce its repudiation of sabotage. The same was done by the
representatives of higher educational establishments, and then the mood of
the majority became oriented towards the possibility of cooperation, which
gradually began to develop. Some accepted it because of the necessity to earn
a living, others considered sabotage to have been a major tactical error, and
some others believed that the country was in need of work and that politics
should step aside, etc., but the truth of the matter is that sabotage came to be
disapproved by nearly everybody.
The Civil War that broke out simultaneously with the Russian Revolution
also provides numerous examples of changes in the mood of the popular
masses. While the Reds were in power, the popular masses, depressed by the
state of general disarray, showed signs of discontent, greeting the White
troops with great pomp; but within a few months or even weeks the White
regime would cause general discontent, and people would be awaiting the
Reds as their saviors. This kind of change in the mood during the Civil War
would repeatedly occur in both directions.
Another illustration of changes in the mood of the masses, particularly
during the periods of tumultuous political coups, is provided by Germany:
after the impressive victories achieved by that country during the Great War,
when the population was under maximum military strain, it proved
impossible as the revolution unfolded to draft into compulsory military
service any number of recruits. Thus, in the 25 February newspaper dispatch
from Berlin we read that during the discussion of state defense a Social
Democrat, Mr. Scheflin, made the following statement:
It is impossible at the present moment to build a military force by drafting
recruits; the state that has until very recently been one of the greatest
military powers in the world is now forced to recruit volunteers through
newspaper ads. It is necessary that the army of national defence respect
the Constitution, obey the Government and protect the laws of the land. It
is also necessary that the volunteers obey their superiors and adhere to
strict discipline.
As we know, various moods are manifested through corresponding
mimico-somatic reflexes. Thus, a more pronounced manifestation of
depression induced by certain circumstances is expressed in individual life
through sighing, moaning, weeping, sobbing, lamenting, wailing, and
complaining, as well as through other phenomena.
Through a gradual evolution, a depressed mood is transformed into a
wailing song reminiscent of moaning, sighing, and weeping.
On the other hand, a lively mood is characterized by chuckles, laughter,
exclamations of delight, excited speech, as well as sounds and statements of
joy, and then through a gradual evolution it is transformed into a lively song
of a cheerful nature.
In both cases, to enhance its effect, the song is accompanied by the
appropriate sounds of music, with the music itself functioning as a
manifestation of one or another mood and, through contagion, as the stimulus
of the corresponding mood in others.
As we know, mood is manifested not only through voice but also through
body movements: a depressed tonus suppresses movements, whereas a lively
tonus stimulates them, provoking a whole range of various body movements
in the form of a discharge of nervous energy, as it were. Therefore, a lively
tonus at the peak of its evolution leads to a dance.
Naturally, all collective moods are accompanied by this variety of
phenomena. Thus, a depressed state during a funeral is characterized by
mournful singing, funeral marches, silent standing in assemblies to honor the
memory of those, who died, etc.; on the other hand, a collective celebration in
cases of a friendly meeting, a victory, etc., is accompanied by collective
feasting, joyous and triumphant music, cheerful marches, lively speeches,
greetings, solemn ceremonies, balls, etc.
Collective moods tend to spread and in certain cases can even hold sway
over a number of collectives.
Symbolism supplements the evolution of collective moods, enhancing
them to an even greater degree. For example, black flags on days of national
mourning, national flags on solemn occasions of a patriotic nature,
appropriate speeches, toasts, etc., undoubtedly have an enhancing effect on
the general mood of a collective, acting at the same time as further
manifestations of mimico-somatic reflexes.
When a cheerful collective mood is accompanied by the desire to
accomplish appropriate actions and reaches its climax, we speak about the
enthusiasm of the popular masses.
This enthusiasm, however, is manifested under the influence of more or
less strong external causes. It is usually encountered together with the general
rise in mood conditioned by a certain cause.
Thus, just as in individual life, where an insult or some other cause can
provoke irritation, so in collectives, under the influence of certain events that
hurt the honor of a collective and its independence, a hostile mood, an
explosion of enthusiasm and militant energy directed against the perpetrators
of these events may develop. There are many examples of this phenomenon,
particularly during wars. Incidentally, the Americans provided a vivid
example of this during the present war when German submarines started
sinking American ships near the Atlantic coastline. The fact itself was
certainly inevitable in the course of the war, but at the same time the news
about this fact, which indicated the arrival of war in America, immediately
resulted in a great rush of army volunteers and stimulated the increase of
patriotism and the rise in combat spirit among the Americans, which is
evident from a considerable number of newspaper items. We shall quote from
those American sources that deserve our attention. The New York Times
makes the following comment: "Germans could not have chosen a better way
of enhancing military ambitions in the hearts of all Americans and of driving
patriotic fervour to fever temperatures." When discussing the increase in
recruitment, the New York Herald says among other things:
Soon after special issues of the evening papers hit the streets people
thronged to the recruiting stations and demanded to be drafted into the
army. As the dusk approached, ever-increasing numbers of would-be
recruits flowed into the offices and inundated them with their applications
for joining one or another branch of Uncle Sam's armed forces.
At the naval recruiting station by 3 p.m. about 500 young people were
trying to join the navy, and more of them were arriving every minute. One
of the officers at the station said: "The office was inundated by the crowd,
and it was impossible to attend to every recruit immediately on his arrival,
even though our numerous staff are working very hard."
The same story was repeated at all the recruiting stations. Masses of
people thronged there in the hope of joining the army, and their patriotism
and bellicose spirit had been reinforced by the news of the Huns' pirate
raids. Ever since the United States entered the World War there has been
no comparable drive to join the army and participate in the conflict.
Women displayed an equally strong patriotic spirit manifested in their
flocking to the Red Cross in great numbers for the purpose of joining the staff
of the American military and naval hospitals. The Evening Mail writes the
following on this account:
At six in the morning those who wished to join the navy arrived at the
naval recruiting station in such huge numbers that the queue stretched
from the third floor where the recruiting station was situated down to the
street and reached the next block. At all the other recruiting stations
people had been waiting since early morning for the doors to open.
One should add that, according to the dispatches from the coastline cities,
the actions of the submarines did not produce any panic. The lights are not
extinguished anywhere except in two cities, which in compliance with
their mayors' orders are submerged in darkness in the evenings.
All of the above indicates that the mood of a collective can be measured
numerically. And indeed, if we consider a precise measure of collective
mood, there is hardly a more correct method than counting the votes during
an election. Just like the counting of votes in cases of controversial issues
gives preference to one or another decision, so during elections the counting
of votes always determines as accurately as possible the political mood of the
country. However, voting can never be complete, as there is always a number
of those who fail to vote. If the latter cannot be explained by certain reasons
that complicate the process of voting, it should be attributed to a greater or
lesser degree of an indifferent attitude towards political issues and in
particular to the population's attitude towards the issue that is supposed to be
resolved by these elections. It is thus clear that correctly gathered statistics is,
generally speaking, the best and most accurate indicator of the country's
collective mood and the changes it undergoes in the course of time. It is
noteworthy that, just as in individual life, together with the change in the
mood of a group or a nation the whole nature of public views is changed
accordingly.
I can cite an example from French life when a change in public mood was
followed by the change in the general outlook of the whole nation:
A quarter of a century ago, not long before the Great War, reminders of
her past military glory were considered a crime against France. In those
days, Jacob, the well-known professor and philosopher, who was teaching
ethics in école normale for girls in Sèvres, said the following: "If our
motherland is destined to play a brilliant role in the world, it will be
through science, arts, fine crafts, good taste, magnanimity of comportment
—in a word, due to our hospitable nature; I do not see anything in the
future except peaceful means." The supreme representatives of
intellectualism in France in the latter half of the last century were Renan
and Taine who preached a true cult of reason and science.
Reaction against this trend was developing imperceptibly and gradually.
Many were encouraged by the prospect of support from the allies.
Achievements in technology, the automobile industry, and especially
aviation provided new stimuli for reawakening the French bellicose
temperament. The ground had been prepared when the crude excesses of
the Eastern neighbours provoked loud protests. The expedition of a naval
ship to Agadir was the last straw, and the whole of France started talking
about war.
In the intellectual sphere this manifestation of nationalism found a very
radical expression. According to the well-known critic Emile Faguet,
"reaction against Kant, Taine and Renan was manifested with far greater
force than one could have expected. When they are mentioned, it is only
with utmost contempt....The general trend is a strongly pronounced anti-
intellectualism.5
Under such conditions it is not surprising that everything which is
conducive to expanding one's horizons and promotes peacefulness is
nowadays regarded by many Frenchmen as humiliating and indecent....One
English author, troubled by the bellicose mood of young Frenchmen,
expressed herself in the following manner:
Those of my friends whom I used to perceive as champions of peace,
enemies of militarism and restrictive nationalism, and as admirers of
Goethe, Nietzsche and Wagner, have undergone a striking transformation.
They only whisper their former words of peace and progress, while their
every word, every emphasis and every glance makes one aware of their
desire for war.
Similar to the simultaneous changes in the public mood and in the outlook
of French intellectuals, we, too, experienced the same transformation of our
worldview between the 1860s and the late 1870s when the Russo-Turkish
war broke out. Statistics concerning the book market and the books borrowed
from the libraries would yield some particularly edifying findings in this
respect.
Notes
1 In subjective psychology, the term "mood" is used to describe feelings or emotional disposition, but
the word "mood" also represents an objective term, and we use it to describe a positive, negative or
neutral tonus of the higher nervous centers.
2 Thomas Carlyle, The French Revolution, Russian translation, p. 5.
3 Although on 22 November 1917 he himself reminded people of his existence by publishing in the
papers a letter under his signature, the letter itself was more like a cry of despair. Needless to say, it
failed to make the slightest impression on society. His former friends could only regret that the letter
ever appeared in the press.
4 Cabanis and Nass, La névrose révolutionnaire, Paris, 1906, pp. 26-28.
5 I. Mechnikov, 40years, etc., p. 30 (in Russian).
13
Collective Attention and Collective
Observation
With the emergence of a collective, its attention—irrespective of whether it is
directed towards defense, attack, or towards anything that excites its curiosity
—is increased in direct proportion to the number of its members.
Naturally, a collective always has an advantage over an individual with
respect to observation and ability to notice, which is corroborated by
numerous examples, particularly from animal life.
To illustrate this point it is suffice to cite one example from H.
Drummond:1
Here is a herd of deer spread out, as they are fond of doing, over a quarter-
mile distance; each animal in the herd participates not only in the physical
strength of the rest but also in their observational abilities; its caution in
case of possible danger is the caution of the whole herd; it has as many
eyes, ears and organs of smell as the whole herd; its nervous system
embraces the whole space occupied by the line: in a word, its environment
consists not only of what it hears, sees, smells, feels and tastes, but also of
what every individual member of the herd hears, sees, smells, feels and
tastes.
Attention as a kind of collective reflex does not differ essentially in
anything from the individual reflex of observation in the form of visual
concentration or looking, auditory concentration or listening, etc., the only
difference being that here the act of looking and listening is performed by
thousands of eyes focused on one and the same object and by thousands of
pricked-up ears. Here one finds the same forms of muscular contractions as
one observes in acts of individual looking and listening, but these forms of
muscular contractions are performed simultaneously by a multitude of
people. However, there is a big difference in the results of looking, i.e., in the
observational ability, or the ability to notice.
Thousands of eyes see better than a pair of eyes. The minutest weakness in
performance is immediately picked up by a watching collective, whereas a
single spectator may not have paid any attention to it. One may say that a
collective's ability to notice is as many times greater as the collective is
numerically larger than a single individual. In a concert hall one can observe
a listening collective in the particular poses that characterize a certain tension
of the audial apparatus. And again, a listening collective displays caeteris
paribus a sharper observation of sound effects than any individual person
does, for even the smallest fault in the performance will be noticed in a
collective by a certain number of people, whereas a single individual—no
matter how good his musical ear might be—may fail to notice it, if only due
to a temporary distraction.
Collective attention exists in any crowd listening to its speaker, in any
theatre audience watching a performance or difficult acrobatic feats, in any
concert hall where an audience is listening to musical pieces or to singing, in
any school where students are concentrating on the words of their teacher, in
any difficult collective work where people are involved in a joint project and
focus their attention on their supervisor in order to coordinate their
movements, on which the correct result of their work depends.
During certain periods of time, a collective concentrates on a particular
event and displays a heightened sensitivity towards this event. Thus, in
wartime, collective attention is directed at the news from the scene of battle.
In times of famine, collective attention is focused on issues related to the
delivery of food supplies. During a sensational court case, collective attention
is directed at the process of criminal investigation and trial. In the case of a
devastating social disaster, collective attention is directed at the various
details of this event, etc.
Thus, public attention in social life is attracted by any sensational news,
such as the declaration of war, a grandiose undertaking, a new discovery or
invention, even by an outstanding book—generally, by everything out of the
ordinary.
The above-mentioned greater effect of collective attention and the greater
result of collective observation ability are achieved in animal and human
collectives by nothing other than the unifying principle of the collective, due
to which a characteristic observed by one member is immediately
communicated to others by means of signs or language. This is enough to
make it a communal possession and an object of further joint discussion and
identification, which due to the increased concentration by many on the same
object leads to an even greater observational ability in the given direction.
As a result of all this, the evaluation of a particular object of concentration
—whether a painting, a theatrical performance, a musical piece, etc.—turns
out to be more accurate on the part of the collective than on the part of each
of its individual members.
Let us take an example from life. At the beginning of the February
Revolution, P. M—ov, one of the most popular leaders of that time elected by
the popular will to the Provisional Government, during one of his speeches
that followed the coup mentioned in passing the name of a possible future
monarch of Russia. This was enough to provoke an almost universal
condemnation of the speech and to cause an immediate loss of the speaker's
credibility among the leftist political circles of the country, whereas an
individual person exposed to the same speech may have failed to pay any
attention to this place in the speech or during an exchange of opinion the
speaker could have improved the impression from his speech one way or
another.
It is clear from the above why collective, or public, supervision has an
advantage over individual supervision.
Therein lies the importance of public control, which is, generally speaking,
significantly more attentive and strict than individual control.
However, all of the above is true in the case when the watching collective
has an opportunity to display its unifying principle through open criticism
and exchange of opinions. In the case when the whole matter is reduced to
collective observation alone and the collective can at the most express only
its approval or disapproval—as is the case with a theatre audience—the
collective loses a significant degree of the intensity of its power to observe,
so that the end result of the latter is reduced to the impression produced by a
given performance on the majority of observers. But since here, too, the
unifying element in the collective is expressed through signs of approval or
disapproval, a collective observational ability obeys to a certain degree the
law of imitation or contagion. Everyone knows that when a group of people
begins to applaud at a certain place in a play, this applause is picked up by
others and frequently by the entire audience. This phenomenon, as we know,
is used in the theatres by claques hired specifically to support a certain play.
The same condition of imitation and suggestion plays a certain role in the
emergence of collective illusions and hallucinations, which will be described
below.
Without going into detail on this subject, I would only like to draw your
attention to the fact that every collective hallucination begins with a
collective illusion or even represents an illusion based on collective
concentration and oriented in a particular direction by the appropriate mood
and the expectation associated with it. Thus, when a family is struck by grief
as a result of the death of one of its members, it sometimes happens that other
family members see the dead person under certain circumstances or
conditions that give rise to a mass illusion. When troops are in a state of
constant anxiety they are often struck by collective illusions and
hallucinations in the form of an advancing enemy. Such illusions are
particularly common at dusk or at nighttime. Here again, the emergence of
collective visions is generated by one or another external cause that initially
produces an illusion in an individual person, but subsequently this illusion is
passed on to others through contagion and, while spreading, it is transformed
into a collective illusion and sometimes a hallucination.2
As in the case of individual attention, collective attention is characterized
by the fact that every small detail of the event that has attracted collective
concentration provokes a collective mimico-so-matic reaction and numerous
discussions in society. The event itself becomes the object of common
judgment and a topic of fashionable conversation everywhere.
It goes without saying that collective attention is distinguished by the same
characteristics as individual attention.
Thus, when its object is monotonous it is subject to fatigue; on the
contrary, a variety of external impressions, particularly after an interval,
revives it. A single event, no matter how important it might be, is unable to
sustain collective attention for long. The latter is gradually blunted or
inhibited, subsequently switching to another event.
In general, as in the case of individual attention, the change of events that
serve as objects of concentration represents an extremely important factor in
maintaining collective attention.
The latter becomes particularly receptive towards an event if, over a certain
period of time, collective attention has been completely unoccupied, in other
words has been idle and resting, as it were. Naturally, the intensity of
concentration is determined by the social importance of a particular event,
which, in turn, depends on the extent to which this event touches upon the
interests of individual members of a given community or collective.
One is struck by the specific attitude of a crowd, predisposed in a
particular way, towards external influences of one or another kind. It turns
out that, just like the individual person, the crowd remains indifferent to all
influences that fail to match its mood, and, on the contrary, it is highly
sensitive towards the influences whose nature is in one way or another in tune
with its general mood. In this respect, its observational ability and
concentration become heightened. This is completely analogous to the mood
of an individual person. When one is in a low or depressed mood, one is
partially or totally unreceptive to anything that contradicts his mood or can
provide some diversion and raise his spirits. Conversely, when one is in high
spirits, one is unreceptive to other people's grief.
On the other hand, during the periods of political coups, disturbances, and
uprisings, political tensions generate collective fears and collective suspicion.
During the Great French Revolution, as well as during the Russian
Revolution, the fear of a possible counterrevolution persisted in society
during nearly the whole period of the revolution, which provided a breeding-
ground for collective suspicion, and as a result almost any person who did not
belong to the ruling parties was perceived as a counterrevolutionary and was
called to account under the most trifling pretexts. In the same manner, a
heightened collective suspiciousness with respect to spies develops in
wartime. This becomes particularly pronounced in countries like Germany,
where military espionage is practiced on an extraordinarily high level. One
may say that the extent of "spy mania" depends on the extent of espionage,
for the better espionage is developed in one's own country, the greater one's
suspicions are that the enemy state practices espionage on an approximately
similar scale, and it is therefore natural to suspect the existence of enemy
espionage all around you. This is why in Germany, for example, more than in
any other country during the last war, one was struck by the abundance of
posters with warnings against the danger of espionage in the streets, squares,
railway stations, shops, restaurants, cafés, and other public places.
Naturally, German "spy mania" was directed first of all against those
foreigners, including Russians, who had not managed to leave Germany
before the declaration of war. The brunt of this spy mania was then borne by
the prisoners of war. In this respect, we are more familiar with the facts that
refer to Russian prisoners of war. In this case, it is undoubtedly the prisoners
of war themselves who provide the most valuable testimony, which is
reproduced below from N. Vavulin's words:3
Germans were capable of seeing a spy in every prisoner. Searches were a
common occurrence in the camps for prisoners of war. Our physicians,
officers, soldiers and civilians were subjected to searches. It was nearly
always unexpectedly that guards would appear in the POW camps and
hospitals, cordon off the huts and block all the entrances and exits. The
camp authorities would arrive together with detectives and occasionally
even with police dogs. The fuss would start right away, and German
officers and sergeant majors, brandishing their weapons and red from the
strain, would yell at their own soldiers as well as at ours. This swearing
directed at us and the shrill orders mixed with threats unnerved us in the
extreme. Physicians and officers were subjected to more humiliating
search procedures than soldiers. They were usually led out of their own
premises into an empty hut where they were told to strip naked. While the
physicians and officers were undressing and the German officers were
rummaging in their underwear, majors or superintendents would go into
the living quarters of those being searched where with the enthusiasm of
treasure-hunters they would dig into the dirty linen, crawl under the beds,
tap the walls and open the bedside tables.
Soldiers were searched in a less elaborate manner but more frequently: a
block sergeant major, as one responsible for his block, would usually
order the prisoners to line up on the drill-ground with their belongings.
And when the gray ragged mass lined up with their equally ragged sacks, a
sergeant major would appear with interpreters and sometimes with a pack
of police dogs; they were always on the look-out for something decidedly
criminal, but all they would ever find were rags, occasionally notes,
diaries, books and newspapers, which were immediately confiscated and
sent to the commandant's office.
"Spy mania" terrorized not only the German population but their army as
well. The Germans' cruel treatment of us was quite understandable, for it
was rooted in their fear that their humane treatment of the prisoners of war
might give rise to the accusation of being in illegal contact with us, which
might allegedly endanger the security of the German empire.
Germany guards its military secrets extremely jealously. Searches,
censorship and interrogations were all auxiliary means for determining the
"loyalty" of a person under suspicion. Those who expressed any
disapproval of the German empire were subjected to harsh punishment.
Prisoners of war who for reasons of health qualified for exchange but
aroused some suspicions remained in the camps for ever.
When discussing the POW group that returned from the fortification works
to the hospital of the Altdamm camp, the author notes,
They were half-dead, withered men, skeletons in the full sense of the
word, their yellowed skin dry and taut. Most of them were in the last
stages of tuberculosis, and some were crippled. So only because these
people had been driven to exhaustion while working for the state, the
German Ministry of War issued an order not to exchange them, since they
might give away military secrets. And such prisoners were ruthlessly
detained to their dying day.
For the purposes of preventing espionage, all the correspondence from
prisoners is kept in quarantine for 10 days and longer before undergoing
censorship, and most of it is destroyed altogether. For the same reasons,
no outsiders have the right to visit the POW camps, and prisoners
themselves are forbidden not only to live in private houses but also to go
from one camp block to another.
The author does not even touch upon the special protective measures that
were applied to anyone leaving German borders, and a description of those
measures would add a great deal to the general picture of the German spy
mania.
According to Dr. Kreslin, who personally inspected the camps of the
Russian prisoners of war in Germany, the picture appears to have been even
gloomier, but we shall not dwell on this subject, so as not to re-open the
wounds of the recent past.
Let us note in conclusion that depending on its mood—particularly in the
periods of anxiety or anticipation of a certain event—a collective, just like an
individual person, develops an increased sensitivity of its organs of
perception to the point of generating collective illusions and hallucinations.
To illustrate the above statement I shall limit myself to citing a case of
collective hallucination that occurred, according to the description in
Razvedchik, on the night of June 2-3 in the Andizhan garrison soon after the
well-known uprising of 18 May:
The Andizhan garrison was languishing under the yoke of the ever-
increasing problems of army life. The number of prisoners was growing
by the hour; the convoy duties and distant reconnaissance raids tied up
daily the whole of the Andizhan cavalry; there were justified fears of
apossible outburst of fanaticism as the time of the execution was
approaching: rumours were afoot that the locals were prepared to rush to
the rescue of their holy ring-leaders and chiefs, and it seemed that the
numerous crowds of daredevils could easily wipe from the face of the
earth a handful of Russians, among them a temporary Commander-in-
Chief with his retinue and the top officials of the military tribunal.
If those who were fully aware of the Andizhan circumstances did not view
the situation through rose-coloured spectacles, just imagine the feelings of
those who received information that was inflated to chimeric proportions
and you will easily imagine the state of the ignorant mass of soldiers.
Batmen were particularly ardent disseminators of all kinds of nonsense
among the soldiers.
The night of the Andizhan alarm happened to be pitch dark and cloudy. It
was preceded by rain and possibly by a thunderstorm.
The newly arrived riflemen were billeted in the barracks and camps. In
those days barracks were very poorly lit inside, and the huts were lit nearly
as sparsely as on the fatal night of 18 May. The rest of the space in both
the city and its environs was drowned in thick darkness. The street-lights
in the city of Andizhan were not lit up that night in accordance with the
glorious Russian custom that prohibited it when the moon was supposed to
be shining, even though it was obscured by the clouds.
It was after midnight. Soldiers in the barracks and in the camps were
sleeping side by side, holding on tightly to their rifles and touching the
cartridges in their sleep. A truly anguished and depressing silence reigned
supreme.
Suddenly there was a muffled noise somewhere in the distance, which
made us all start, a second later feel terrified and then gasp, for the noise
kept growing and growing; then it turned into the hubbub of a crowd in
which one could discern occasional wild screams and then whole sheaves
of screams, squeals and shrieks.
Then all of this was joined by the cries of "Hurrah" that were scattered at
first, then loud and then a thundering victorious "Hurrah," and "Hurrah"
kept expanding and enveloping the camp and the barracks.
Suddenly gunfire started. An eye-witness arrived at the camp when yet
another soldier on duty was firing the last charge in the direction of the
retreating enemy. At that moment all the soldiers were already lined up in
their huts and were carefully examined and calmed down by their
superiors. Everyone was frankly explaining where he was shooting and
how many times, but no one in the camps or on the outposts really knew
where the shooting and the alarm started.
In fact, the false alarm was caused by a wounded soldier who jumped out
of bed in his delirium and started running and screaming; after that all the
other wounded jumped out of beds.
The riflemen detachment closest to the infirmary was woken up, grabbed
their rifles and shouting "Hurrah" dashed to the rescue of their wounded.
This stirred the whole camp! People started jumping to their feet, pulling
on whatever clothes was within their reach, clamouring and yelling. They
shouted the "Hurrah-that grew and reverberated and reached the farthest
corners of the garrison.
Then someone fired his rifle into the darkness and the mass hallucination
was born in all its glory—everybody was seeing, hearing and shooting at
the enemy. For one second people became deaf to the voices of their
superiors and to the signals.
Notes
1 H. Drummond, Progress and Evolution of Man, p. 177.
2 For details and numerous examples of collective illusions and hallucinations see my book, Suggestion
and Its Role in Social Life (in Russian), St. Petersburg, 3rd edition, 1908.
3 N. Vavulin, VestnikZnaniia (Herald of Knowledge), issue 6—"German procedures to secure a
victory."
14
Collective Creativity
The view of collective creativity has recently undergone an essential
transformation. Whereas in the recent past it was believed that creativity was
predominantly a product of individual persons, distinguished by the power of
their intellect, and that with respect to the processes of reasoning and
creativity a collective was unable to produce anything but mediocrity,
nowadays, on the contrary, one has to recognize as a fact that collective
creative activity plays a dominant role in mankind and that reasoning itself
owes its existence to nothing other than collective activity. Incidentally, this
point of view is also shared by the outstanding modern sociologist E.
Durkheim. At this point, we shall not dwell on this fact but merely note that
when it comes to individual reasoning, a great deal, if not everything is
determined by previous individual experience, that defines the value of a
particular judgment. Application of this experience to a new task is
eventually reduced to supplying pieces of evidence "for" and "against" and to
counterbalancing both categories for the purpose of summing up the final
results of this weighing process in the form of a particular conclusion.
A similar phenomenon occurs in collective reasoning, the only difference
being that here one deals with the application of experience generated not by
an individual person but by the many people who make up a given collective.
As a result, collective judgment usually appears to be more comprehensive
than an individual one in the same measure as the experience of many is
more comprehensive than the experience of one. For this reason, a critique of
a particular object or its details in a collective is invariably more
comprehensive and multifaceted than an individual critique, and the
judgments and decisions themselves turn out to be more prudent.
However, due to the participation of many individuals in the discussion,
the effort of summing up the results in a collective is much slower than an
individual effort.
Therefore, in those cases when a decision has to be made quickly and
hastily, a collective effort is out of place, even though it might be better than
an individual effort; and, on the contrary, when there is a need for greater
prudence and caution in the absence of urgency, then a collective effort
should be preferable to an individual one.
The nature of collective judgment is affected by the composition of the
collective that determines the views and interests of the majority of its
individual members.
It is obvious from the above that, depending on the composition of the
collective, its judgments and decisions may in some cases be tendentious and
even somewhat biased. As a general rule, however, collective judgments and
decisions appear to be more impartial than individual judgments and
decisions. In order to ensure a reasonably unbiased collective judgment, the
collective itself should consist of diverse elements.
When tackling a particular problem and being guided by one's own
experience and erudition, individual intellectual activity essentially utilizes
collective experience, synthesizes it, and makes certain conclusions on the
basis of the appropriate material. At the same time, it is distinguished by
creativity, determined by its own experience, erudition, and talent. However,
this activity, fruitful as it may be, always bears the stamp of individuality,
and when the erudition and the breadth of one's views are inadequate, it bears
the stamp of one-sidedness. For this reason, the best results are achieved in
those cases when individual creative activity is discussed in a collective,
where, under cross-criticism from people who work in other areas, the
conclusions and decisions made by an individual person in the quiet of his
study are polished, supplemented, expanded or generalized. Naturally, they
are disseminated and popularized in gatherings.
In this process, however, the work itself is often stripped of its most
idiosyncratic features. In order to preserve them the author should be allowed
to utilize only those comments of the collective that he finds acceptable.
The content of speeches in an assembly usually reflects the personalities of
individual members of the assembly, but one should bear in mind that those
who speak in assemblies unconsciously take into consideration the nature of
the assembly as well as the views of other speakers; consequently, even these
speeches reflect the influence of the collective, but the final collective
judgment results only from mutually opposing and mutually criticizing
opinions and is eventually transformed into a particular decree, decision, or
resolution.
As we know, every assembly may proceed in a sluggish or lively manner,
depending on the importance of the subject, on the composition of the
assembly, and on the interest displayed by its participants.
If there is not total discord of opinion at the assembly and there is a
possibility of finding a common meeting-ground, the decisionmaking follows
the line of the resultant force of two or more opposing opinions, but the more
divergent the views are, the longer it usually takes to produce each result of
the discussion, i.e., a decision.
It goes without saying that the skillful guidance of debates is conducive to
a greater success and a greater restraint of an assembly, as well as to its
progression towards a certain goal. On the other hand, inadequate regulation
of debates may rapidly lead to an incident of one kind or another, while the
inability to resolve an incident in good time occasionally ruins the assembly
altogether. The truth is that any incident that arises from a disagreement in an
assembly brings this disagreement into sharp relief, undermining thereby the
proper work of the collective, if not bringing it to a complete halt in cases of
the total divergence of opinion among various parts of the assembly.
Collective productivity also depends to a certain degree on the size of the
given collective. Usually, the larger a collective is, the slower its work
proceeds, but it provides a better guarantee of satisfaction of the interests of a
greater mass of people. However, the size of a gathering should have some
limits for the purposes of a successful collective discussion of a subject, and
assemblies with more than 500-1,000 participants make it very difficult to
guide the discussion properly.
When the subject matter becomes clear to the majority of people and when
the process of collective work and exchange of opinions reveals the heart of
the matter, then one may speak about the collective elucidation of the subject.
When we talk about a collective analysis of a subject, about a collective
critique, a collective view of things, etc., all of these are in essence only
particular stages of collective associative activity, which finally assume the
form of collective judgment.
The result of the latter is a resolution or a decision, a decree or a sentence
approved by the majority of the assembly. These decisions, decrees, or
sentences are essentially a summary of the whole discussion and the final
conclusion at which the collective arrives as a result of its work, and their
formula is proposed by one or another side, or by any participant in the
debates.
As to the verbal collective statements in the proper sense of the term, there
is essentially only one case when a collective articulates a speech, all parts of
which are well coordinated. This occurs in choral singing, but here the
coordination is aimed at producing a harmony of sounds, as well as verbal
symbolization.
On the other hand, collective statements may be more or less coordinated
in the form of exclamations and cries in a crowd; in other cases, however,
these statements are made by each person individually, even though their
content may be more or less coordinated. Such are statements and demands
put forward by a mass of people who have gathered for a particular reason or
are united by common interests.
In other cases—and this is what happens most often—a collective
expresses its opinion by using one common formula (it makes no difference
whether it is written or verbal) transmitted through an elected person. This
common formula—a sentence, a resolution or a decision—may be very
laconic, but before it is generated it always demands some preliminary work
in the form of a collective discussion of a given issue.
The press is a form of collective discussion that involves a whole nation
and is therefore broader in the sense of a wider spectrum of people who are
attracted to the discussion and wield a skilful pen; besides, due to the nature
of the press, the discussion itself is devoid of the specific expressiveness
possessed by the live word.
Naturally, even though such discussion may be particularly valuable in the
sense of elucidating various aspects of the issue, its very nature makes it even
more time-consuming and eliminates the possibility of arriving at a particular
decision by direct vote, with the exception of the questionnaire method.
We have already mentioned that numerous authors claimed that only
individual activity could be of a creative nature and that collective activity
was incapable of creativity or, in any case, could not be compared to
individual creativity in terms of its quality.
Thus, many authors consider a crowd to be dumb and its mental processes
to be inferior to those of individual persons, for a crowd's decision is
allegedly confined within the bounds of mediocrity.
Incidentally, Gabelli illustrates this idea with respect to the process of
election—for example, the elections of a university's president:
They elect a person who is least threatening to their self-esteem, who does
not stand out and is totally unprepossessing. They often wish to see in this
position the most tolerant, amenable and agreeable person of minimal
importance—in a word, the least energetic and decisive man of little
influence. Thus, the person who is elected is not trusted by either his
supporters or his detractors, each of whom is very well aware of the
reasons behind his own vote. Occasionally there were even cases when
after the elections the supporters of the chosen candidate were less
inclined in his favor than his adversaries.
The above views are not exceptional. They are shared by other authors
who wrote on the same subject. Max Nordau:
Bring together twenty or thirty Goethes, Kants, Helmholtzs, Shakespeares,
Newtons, etc. and ask them to pass a decision or a judgement on some
practical issues of the day. Their reasoning might differ radically from the
judgements of an ordinary gathering (although I cannot guarantee this),
but when it comes to conclusions, they will not differ in any way from the
conclusions of an ordinary meeting. Why is that? Because each of the 20
or 30 participants, besides his inherent originality that makes him unique,
possesses the inherited characteristics of the species; the latter make him
similar not only to his fellow participant but also to any stranger he may
have passed in the street. One may say that all normal people possess
certain characteristics which represent a certain homogeneous value X, to
which, in personalities who are above the average, another value is added
which varies depending on the individuality and which should be defined
in various ways—<s.g., b, c, d, etc. Once we accept this proposition, we
shall have in a gathering of 20 people who are presumably all geniuses 20
X and only 1 b, 1 c, 1 d, etc. There is no doubt that 20 X will prevail over
the individual b, c, d, etc. This means that the generic characteristics of the
human kind will prevail over the individual personality and that a worker's
cap will cover a doctor's or a philosopher's hat.1
E. Ferri expresses the same idea in a different way but just as vividly: "A
gathering of sensible people may result in an assembly that lacks common
sense, just as in chemistry a combination of two gases may produce a
liquid."2
According to Sighele,
Collective psychology is full of surprises: hundreds or thousands of
people are collectively capable of committing actions that not a single
person out of a hundred or a thousand would ever commit individually.
These surprises are nearly always of a regrettable nature. A gathering of
good people hardly ever produces a fine result, occasionally a mediocre
one and in some cases a very poor one.3
While looking for an answer to this question, the author comes to the
conclusion that "in a crowd the good qualities of certain individuals are not
added to one another but subtracted from one another." This means that the
best qualities of some people are overshadowed by the lower qualities of
most members of the crowd. It is thus clear that the latter should prevail over
the former. Besides, the influence of mutual suggestion in a crowd should
have a similar effect. Finally, the level of morality in a crowd is relatively
low. The same phenomenon is observed in assemblies with respect to
intellectual powers, which are, in the final analysis, inferior to the most
moderate talent, even though a crowd that most often behaves like a
bloodthirsty beast is in some cases capable of displaying a degree of nobility
and heroism.
The unity of a crowd as a social personality is, as we know, a prerequisite
for mass actions. This unity is often established independently of membership
composition and under the influence of emotional states generated by a
particular fact.
However, once this unity is achieved, a crowd as a social personality is no
longer the average element of its individual members but possesses some
totally distinctive characteristics. This is presumably caused by the fact that
higher products of associative activity of various individuals are subtracted
from one another, as it were, and follow the middle line, while everything
valuable and heterogeneous is effaced. Conversely, everything which is
associated with the lower forms of associative activity, such as impulsiveness
and selfishness, and is suppressed in individual persons, is here accumulated,
as it were, and overshadows the higher manifestations of associative activity.
This is why, according to G. Le Bon,
it is stupidity and not intelligence that is accumulated in a crowd. What
they call "the whole world" cannot be more intelligent than Voltaire, but
quite the opposite, it is Voltaire who is more intelligent than "the whole
world," if the latter phrase means a crowd.4
On the other hand, due to their considerable numbers, the members of a
crowd develop a greater confidence in their power and authority which no
individual person possesses in the same measure. This is associated with the
belief in the impunity and infallibility of a collective decision. Then a crowd
develops an extraordinarily high susceptibility to suggestion and a
predisposition towards contagion.
All of this results in a predisposition towards impulsive behavior and in the
inability to assess one's actions critically.
The above leads to what one may recognize as a general phenomenon—on
the one hand a crowd displays a diminished intelligence, and on the other an
increase in mimico-somatic reflexes. All of this, naturally, refers to a street
crowd and under no circumstances may be applicable to all assemblies,
particularly those of a special nature.
According to G. Le Bon, "the activity of a crowd is always and everywhere
inferior to the activity of an individual person."5 This statement, however, is
disputable, for even a crowd that is unorganized but united by a particular
mood is capable of heroic deeds and actions that embellish the pages of
history up to the present. On the other hand, while listening to the reports
prepared in advance by specialists, assemblies smooth over certain biases and
overstatements, and the usefulness of this process is corroborated by the fact
that the specialists-speakers themselves often agree with the suggested
corrections and even renounce their own statements.
Much has been said in general about the greater stupidity of a collective in
comparison with an individual person without a thorough analysis of all
circumstances. First, one should not confuse street crowds united solely by
their mood with other collectives. On the other hand, actions of an individual
person often represent the fruit of long searches and efforts based on the
synthesis of collective work, whereas a crowd is for the most part forced to
make decisions that cannot be postponed for any length of time. At the same
time, a crowd's decision is the resultant force or, more precisely, the
arithmetical mean, of the opinions of many people who make up the crowd,
therefore the nature of the decision is determined by the composition of the
crowd, so that when it is dominated by mediocrity its opinion will also be
mediocre, but it has not at all been proved that a crowd of intelligent people
will produce an opinion that will be inferior to those expressed under the
same conditions by each individual separately.
Incidentally, Bentham points out the following difference between the
decrees of political societies of a permanent nature and identical societies of
an accidental and short-term nature: the former find it easier to arrive at
decisions that are in the true interests of their members than do the latter
ones.
According to G. Le Bon, even parliamentary assemblies represent a dual
danger of "unproductive waste of finances and a progressive restriction of
individual freedom." However, this statement, too, is in need of correction. It
is indisputable that parliaments regulate finances better than a minister's
individual power. Public control alone immediately cuts off from the national
budget those expenditures that cannot be justified from the standpoint of
public interest. Even though parliaments do not spare money for needs
ignored by the crown, they also discover the sources of payment for new
expenditures regarded as productive.
As to the right to personal freedom, it is a debatable question who
promulgates more edicts that restrict personal freedom—a parliament or an
administrator who on busy days churns out a cornucopia of mandatory
decrees that are for the most part completely inane.
Nor can one accept without reservations Le Bon's statement regarding the
one-sidedness of opinions voiced by a crowd, even if it is a parliamentary
crowd. If in the interests of its elected representatives a party votes
unanimously in compliance with party discipline, one should not forget that
prior to voting in parliament the issue was thoroughly discussed at party
meetings where many opposing opinions intersected and where the adopted
decision reflected the median line of behavior, which happened to be the
most expedient one in the given situation of party politics. This
predetermined voting explains the indifferent attitude of the audience to the
speeches of lesser known speakers, whereas the speeches of the main party
leaders always appear to be significant and are received with due attention.
One should note that until now a crowd has been described as a more or
less independent community of people, whereas an active crowd cannot exist
without its leaders; the crowd and the leaders mutually complement and
influence each other. The leader is guided by the mood of the crowd and by
the effect of his speech on the crowd, whereas the crowd listens to its leader
and obeys him in its actions.
Any assembly whatsoever represents a combination of individual intellect
and the collective one. Undoubtedly, not everybody can lead a crowd, and
then again no two crowds are alike. Not every leader of a parliamentary party
can lead a street crowd and vice versa.
For this reason, characterization of the crowd by various psychologists has
suffered from a one-sidedness of one kind or another, since they failed to take
into account, as they should have, a close interrelationship between the leader
and the crowd, to which subject our Mikhailovskii devoted some interesting
pages and which was subsequently discussed by such prominent Western
sociologists as, for example, Tarde and some others.
As for collective creativity in the narrow sense of the word, we do not
share the accepted view that one cannot compare collective work and
individual work in terms of their quality. Sighele, G. Le Bon, and others
claim that with respect to the quality of work a crowd can never reach even
the average level of individual work. This opinion, however, should be
regarded as extremely one-sided. Although it is true that initiative and
inventions are more often generated by an individual rather than by a group,
one should not forget that an individual genius himself is a product of
society, one who personifies a synthesis of collective work and is inspired by
the crowd, that the implementation of an initiative requires participation of
the crowd, that inventions are produced for the crowd and are totally
meaningless without it, and that an individual impulse is often completely
powerless unless it receives the support and the enthusiasm of the crowd. It is
clear that the individual without a crowd, or a composite personality, is
nothing—just as a crowd without the individual is nothing. It is only the joint
effort of an individual and a crowd, or a composite personality, that ensures
progress. Under these circumstances, each side is destined to play its own
part in the progress of humanity.
In order to express itself, the individual mind needs the assistance of the
collective, for it creates for the collective, it invents what is useful for and
needed by the collective, it gathers the material that has been prepared before
it by the collective, it synthesizes the facts that have already been prepared by
the collective, it carries out a particular task in the interests of the collective
and follows all the instructions provided by the collective. On the other hand,
the collective mind makes use of the products of the individual mind, while
smoothing over all the sharp angles in its creative activity, eliminating the
superfluous details and rough spots that may be of some significance to an
individual but of no general importance. It generalizes the activity of the
individual mind and renders it universally acceptable and meaningful. It is
therefore completely wrong to claim that only an individual mind can create
properly, whereas the mind of a crowd or of an assembly is incapable of
creativity or at least of the same measure of creativity as an individual mind,
and that the latter should in this respect be regarded superior to the collective
mind. I am more inclined to the idea that better creative activity is achieved
through joint work in which both the individual mind and the collective mind
participate and where the former takes upon itself to prepare and process the
material, while the latter criticizes, supplements, and generalizes it. Do we
not know the cases when even creations of geniuses were corrected as a result
of criticism and evaluation meted out by the collective that was supposed to
judge it? And if this is the case, it is obvious that the collective mind will
always play its role in appropriately correcting and smoothing over the
shortcomings and one-sidedness of individual work.
Collective or public opinion is the result of discussion of a particular fact
or event by individual members of a collective or by its various circles, and in
large collectives it is individual parties and societies that are involved in this
discussion disseminated by word of mouth and in an educated society by the
press, which generally plays an enormous role in all collectives without
exception. In cases of particularly important events in countries that enjoy
political freedoms, a certain role in shaping the public opinion is played by
grassroots meetings and partly by theatres, cinemas, fair-booths, etc.
In general, any task which is delegated to an assembly is in any case
performed as a collective exchange of opinions that are subsequently
synthesized in the form of a conclusion by the collective mind, or more often
by an individual mind which, in this case, reflects the effort of the collective
mind.
In general, as I have already said, one should not overlook the fact that
individual creativity makes use of the accumulated riches of the collective,
that every discovery is the fruit of the accumulated facts and that even a
generalization of these facts, which is needed for making a new discovery, is
often prepared to such an extent that the only thing left to do is to summarize
everything previously accomplished; the discovery itself is in the air, as it
were, and an individual mind is nothing other than a vivid manifestation of
this generalization.
When posing the question of how the process of discovery occurs, Tarde
says, among other things, the following:
First we notice the emergence of all kinds of inventions and mutually
opposing ideas and fantasies that keep appearing and disappearing until
the arrival of lucid formulae, and some useful machines make us forget
everything else and from then on serve as the foundation on which further
improvements are built for future development. This process therefore
approximates collective thinking without a collective brain, which is
possibledue to the solidarity (as a consequence of imitation) of many
brains of inventors and scientists who exchange information about their
discoveries.6
However, we also know some forms of true collective creativity. They
include language, common law, beliefs, myths and, to some extent, folk
epics. Even artistic creativity can be pursued by a collective, the evidence of
which one can see on the walls of some old Russian churches that were
painted by groups of artisits, such as "Rublev and fellow-painters"; some
works of literature, too, were created collectively. For example, a well-known
Russian literary work, Kuzma Prutkov, belongs here. It is thus indisputable
that to deny the role of the collective in creative activity means to go from
one extreme to the other.
In the final analysis, creativity in human history is achieved by the joint
efforts of a collective and of individual persons. The lot of the former is for
the most part to accumulate experience and facts and to bring together the
results of individual experience, in the interests of the whole entity, whereas
the lot of the latter is to synthesize these facts from a personal point of view.
It is clear that a creative act is not possible without the first process, nor is it
possible to complete a creative act without the second process. But both
together, through mutual enrichment, lead to creativity, progress, and
improvement.
Here we shall discuss only the most important collective invention—the
human language. P. Collet says that the emergence of language necessarily
presupposes an already existing community and is therefore explained as an
exclusively social fact:
Modern linguistics.. .deduces the formation of language from gestures,
cries and exclamations through which the savage expressed and
communicated to others his sensations and sentiments; then also from the
