consonances (onomatopoeia) with which he tried to imitate cries of
animals and sounds of nature, which is still done by the most backward
savages and by our own children in the first months of their lives.. ..Renan
and Max Muller deduce the roots of the words from these imitative
consonances - the primitive monosyllabic sounds that formed the first
words by joining together in various combinations. Thus, language must
have been a spontaneous and unconscious product of the collective, rather
than the individual, mind.
At the same time, thought could not have emerged, let alone develop,
without this indispensable tool.
The origin of writing was also of an exclusively social nature. Initially
ideographic and subsequenüy phonetic writing signifies a transition from
animal existence to intellectual life which, together with language, brought
about the most important factor of civilization—the very condition that
made the evolution of tradition and upbringing possible and that enabled
each generation to make use of the progress achieved by the previous
generations.7
In his paper, "Sociology and Cognitive Theory" (New Ideas in Sociology,
issue 2), E. Durkheim derives the origins of logical judgment and,
consequently, of thought itself from social life. He begins by pointing out that
the content of logical thought consists of general notions (concepts) and
poses the question, "What enables society to participate in concept
formation?"
He claims that as long as one perceives a concept as a general idea this
question remains insoluble, for an individual is capable of comparing his own
perceptions and images and of distilling something common from them. The
author defines concepts in a different manner. Sense perceptions are fluid:
they may appear and disappear while undergoing changes each time; these
transformations may result not only from a change in the perceived object but
also from a change in the perception itself; whereas a general notion remains
"outside time and evolution," being isolated from these fluctuations, as it
were. A general idea is a mode of thinking, "which is fixed and crystallized at
every point in time." The system of ideas is already provided with one's
mother tongue, for each word signifies a concept, while the language itself is
more or less fixed and if it undergoes any changes at all their pace is slow.
Any logical notion, even if it is "not universal, it is in any case capable of
becoming such." An idea is not my exclusive possession —it can be
generalized, which is impossible to achieve with "perceptions," as "any
intellectual communication between people consists precisely of exchanging
concepts." Thus, by its very nature, a concept is not something individual, but
is by origin a result of the collectivity, just as language itself, which consists
of concepts, is a result of that same collectivity. In the final analysis, ideas are
nothing other than objects of collective experience; they are "collective
representations" in a form that has been established by language, and they are
shared by the entire group.
Thus, logical judgment becomes essentially possible when an individual
acquires in addition to his individual ideas the concepts that are shared by
many intellects and are of an impersonal and stable nature. In the end,
impersonal thought evolved from collective thought.
Furthermore, the most recent neopositivist school of sociology recognizes
that not only concepts, or ideas, but also the principal intellectual categories,
are nothing other than products of society, which is perceived not as an
"imagined and nominal entity but as a system of actual forces." Such
intellectual categories as the concepts of time, space, genus, number, cause,
substance, personality, etc., which reflect the most common attributes of
objects, are in the end a product of society. According to E. Durkheim, one
encounters the most fundamental of these categories when analyzing
religious beliefs. "Religion is an. essentially social phenomenon." On the
other hand, one cannot represent time "without taking into consideration the
devices we use to divide, measure and express it through particular signs."
Time is impossible to understand unless "one is able to distinguish its various
heterogeneous elements." And so, according to Durkheim, a personal, i.e.,
individual, experience is inadequate for "generating the concept or category
of time, as time is an abstract and impersonal frame that encloses not only our
own individual existence but also that of the whole humanity." But if time is
not only my time, but the time that belongs to all people as something
objectively conceived, it is apparent that it can only be a result of collective
experience. In reality, the order of phenomena distribution in time is
generated by social life. Days, weeks, months, years, etc., correspond to the
periodicity of rituals, festivals, and public ceremonies. According to the
author, perceptions and representations of the sequence of events are a result
of individual experience, whereas the category of time shared by the whole
group is a national creation. The same holds true for space and causality.
With respect to space, one should first of all renounce Kant's view of it as
something pure and absolutely homogeneous that does not serve any purpose.
As a result of individual experience, spatial representation consists of the
primary distribution of findings provided by this experience, so that some of
them are located to the right and others to the left, some are up and others are
down, still others are situated in the north, south, west, east and so on—
similar to the distribution of time according to dates. It is the identical
division of space that proves the social origins of these differences. There
also exist more direct indications pointing to the social origins of spatial
distributions. Some Australian and American societies are of a circular shape
and their space is divided in the same way as the tribe's camping ground.
Thus, the nation of Zuni (Pueblo) consists of seven groups and their space
consists of seven parts of the world closely connected with the corresponding
group. And since each tribal group has its own symbolic color, each part of
the world has the same color. It is thus clear that spatial organization is
directly dependent on social organization, and even the distinction between
right and left is associated with religious beliefs.
The concepts of genus, force, personality, and action are also of the same
social origin.
Even logic with its concepts of identity and contradiction is apparently of
social origin, for we know that in mythology a part may equal the whole, or a
certain object may exist before the beginning of time—in other words, we
encounter irreconcilable logical contradictions and "are constantly faced with
the problem of existence that possesses the most contradictory attributes,
such as unity and multiplicity, matter and spirit, ability to be divided to
infinity without losing integrity and so on." Therefore, the principle of
identity is far from eternal and depends at least in part on historical—and
consequently on social—facts. Clearly, the claim that categories are not
derived from experience but precede it and make it possible, as well as
Spencer's view of categories as originating in individual experience are
equally unacceptable. If empiricism had some foundation, categories—these
most universal concepts—would have to part with their common
characteristics that determine their application to everything real and at the
same time establish their independence of each individual experience,
whereas perceptions and images are both "individual and subjective":
These are the two types of knowledge that represent two poles of intellect,
as it were. To deduce reason from experience under these conditions
means to make it disappear, for to arrive at such a conclusion means to
reduce the universality and necessity that characterize reason to mere
appearances and illusions that may be convenient in practice yet lack any
real foundation; it also means to recognize the objective reality of logical
life whose orderliness and organization are precisely the main functions of
categories.
On the other hand, to view categories as a product of a particular type of
mind perceived as an "emanation," as it were, of a supreme or divine
intellect means to adopt a hypothesis that cannot be scientifically
corroborated. Besides, the categories of human thought have never been
set in a single unchangeable form. They have been continuously created,
destroyed and re-created; they have been changing depending on time and
place. Divine intellect, however, is endowed with the opposite quality.8
In a word, "knowledge consists of two types of mutually irreducible
elements." Empirical knowledge is generated under the direct influence of an
object and, consequently, here we are dealing exclusively with individual
states, whereas categories are a product of collective experience.
We believe that this issue appears much more obscure when the object of
investigation is discussed from a subjective point of view, for in this case one
has to deal with collective notions, impersonal thought, etc. The issue is
significantly simplified when one discusses it from the objective point of
view that perceives language as a tool of logical thought. And indeed, we can
definitely say that judgment is impossible without language, for in a
subjective world notions in the form of judgments do not and cannot exist.
There can only exist an associative interaction of notions based on contiguity,
similitude, belonging, etc., whereas a connection that we call a judgment
becomes possible only in the presence of verbal and other symbolic
combinations. However, words, gestures, and written signs represent, as we
know, symbols that are a product of collective experience—hence, judgment,
too, is nothing other than a similar product of collective experience. It is thus
clear that logic evolved together with language, and when language is at a
primitive stage of its development, logical thinking cannot achieve its full
potential; this is corroborated by primeval mythology.
When pursuing logical thinking, a person goes beyond his sensual
experience, enters the realm of abstraction and acquires an insight into the
true nature of the outside world, but he is able to do this only with the help of
a symbol as a product of collectivity and as the first step towards abstraction
characterized by impersonality. It is only through the mediation of collective
experience that a given symbol acquires a certain stability and becomes an
impersonal sign for denoting particular objects, as well as the first
generalization of all analogous objects of many individual experiences.
Obviously, it is only through this kind of generalization that people reached
the level of intellectual interaction, which is therefore a product of
collectivity.
It is beyond any doubt that without the collective experience that resulted
in the symbolic identification of various phenomena of the external and the
inner world, man would have remained in the position of an inferior being,
whose inner world would have been limited to images and sense experiences,
which would have precluded his communication with others, as well as have
prevented him from creating generalizations in the form of concepts. It was
only through language that man acquired the ability to form concepts that
belonged to all and whose meaning was verified by all, and at the same time
every individual acquired the ability to engage in further juxtapositions of
concepts and new generalizations and conclusions, which was destined to
result in the emergence of logical discourse. It is only under conditions of
collectivity that the universality of certain symbols-concepts is generated, for
each individual mind uses its own personal experience to verify to a certain
extent the given symbols-concepts. At the same time, a given symbol is
correlated in a collective with all the other symbols through a mutual
exchange of these symbols, so that symbols in general and concepts in
particular acquire a certain significance, impersonality, and stability in the
furnace of joint collective work, for these symbols become a creation of the
whole society, rather than of a single individual. It is clear from the above
that our logic, too, is a product of collectivity, and as the latter was evolving,
so was the logic whose evolution is still going on; this does not mean,
however, that the individual plays no part in this process.
As we know, categories have been and are recognized as absolutely
meaningful and invariable forms of any judgment. This may indicate that
from the very beginning of intellectual exchange between those who existed
and worked in a human collective there was total and unconditional
unanimity regarding categories, and this unanimity became part of the
character, so to say, of every individual who grew up and was brought up in a
collective. Durkheim is right in recognizing that not only are categories of
social origin but that they also signify various aspects of social existence:
Thus, the category of clan was initially inseparable from the notion of the
human soul; the category of time is based on the rhythm of communal life;
the category of space was modelled on the space occupied by the group;
collective force served as the prototype for the notion of active force—an
essential element of the category of causality.
We believe, however, that categories, as well as concepts, are products of
both individual and collective activities. It is precisely here that one observes
complete accord between the results of personal experience and those of
collective experience, which developed and consolidated them as the
immutable forms of human intellectual exchange. An individual person exists
in space, and the very structure of his organs (semicircular canals, etc.)
enables him to orient himself in it. He exists in time, being endowed with the
faculty of perceiving a sequence of stimulations (acoustic ones and others),
and is capable of measuring the sequence of external phenomena with the
help of his breathing rate, the number of completed movements, such as
steps, etc. He acts, being the cause of changes in the world, using his
extremities as if they were tools like levers; he observes similar objects
reproduced with a particular ease when encountering other similar objects,
and through juxtaposition he establishes kinship between them; he perceives
a group of objects that are interconnected and that form an integrated whole,
etc. However, even though all these products of individual experience are
based on the organic foundation of the perceiving apparatus, they would have
remained underdeveloped and discordant in their various parts without
collective experience. An individual whose perceiving organs possess the
organic structure that enables them to form categories would not have been
able to create them as universally meaningful phenomena without a mutual
exchange in this respect with other individuals and without the collective
experience that alone is capable of establishing categories as an indisputable
basis of any judgment, which, in turn, results from the mutual exchange of
personal experience between individual persons.
Categories reflect the collective mind, but the latter became part of them
on the basis of individual intellectual activity. It is essentially the individual
mind that records the succession of the seasons, of day and night, respiratory
movements, etc. But only the collective mind could make this succession
universally meaningful by establishing further necessary subdivisions of this
succession in the form of particular periods. In the same manner, through
experience, the individual mind determines spatial dimensions with the help
of its movements for which purpose it uses the dimensions of its body or
movements, such as, for example, the thickness of one's fingers, vershok (a
Russian equivalent of an inch), a quarter, a foot, arshin (one step), etc., as
well as the body movements to one or the other side, forward, backward, etc.
However, these definitions, too, acquire universal significance only through
collective experience, which correlates all the necessary results of individual
experience, supplements them with the necessary details, and creates thereby
universal categories, distilling them, as it were, from the sphere of individual
experience because of their universal significance. Being a product of
individual-collective experience, general categories nevertheless preserve
their theoretical value, for they cannot but reflect the very nature of things.
We already know that abstract thinking is possible only with language, and
since language is a collective creation it is clear that categories, like all
general notions, could not have been generated without collective experience.
This, however, does not at all exclude the possibility that with the emergence
of language one or several individuals may have initially created symbols for
some general notions in response to a practical need, after which these
symbols became universally popular through their generalization. One can
provide some proof of this, which amounts to experimental data, from the
sphere of inventiveness of the gesticulatory language of the deaf-mute, which
can be verified by any observer.
We are dealing with a group of deaf-mute children aged ten to twelve. In
the evening of 27 June, I came into their dormitory together with Dr. Sh. The
children were in their beds though not yet asleep. They were gesticulating
with one another in a very lively manner, as all deaf-mute children of this age
do. Incidentally, every adult is identified in their language by a gesture that
indicates a particular characteristic. For example, a lady with full cheeks is
described by an index finger pointing to a cheek; a moustached gentleman is
indicated by a movement of both hands from under the nose, as if smoothing
out one's moustache; a doctor who wears glasses is described by forming a
circle with the fingers of the right hand and placing it in front of the right eye,
etc. I was warned that it would be extremely interesting to see what gesture
the children would invent specially for me, as I was a new person they were
seeing for the first time in their life. So what did they do? Within a few
seconds one child used his right hand to make a gesture showing my beard
descending down my chest and indicating thereby the characteristic external
attribute of my face, which was sufficient for the gesture to be adopted by the
children for referring to my personality among themselves.
Thus, here an appropriate gesture made by one child and rapidly adopted
by other children is generalized through collective exchange and is
transformed into a universally accepted gesture that has become a communal
possession understood by one and all. Do we not perceive the same in the
oral language of the hearing population? Someone attaches an appropriate
term to a new object and this term is accepted by others, entering general
usage. Thus, not so long ago such words as "hooligan" and others entered
general usage to be joined somewhat later, due to their popularization by the
press, by such new scientific terms as "X-rays" introduced by Roentgen and
"reflexology" suggested by me as the name for a new discipline I was
developing that focused on the objective study of human personality.
It is evident from the above that one cannot exclude creative participation
of an individual person. There is even more reason to recognize this with
respect to the formation of categories that depend in certain measure on the
organic conditions of functioning of each individual's organs of perception.
All of the above, however, does not diminish the creative role of society,
and E. Durkheim is a thousand times right when he says that
it is precisely the society that possesses a creative power that no other
creature possesses. In reality all creation, except the mystical operation
that eludes intellect and science, is a product of synthesis, and if the
syntheses of separate representations occurring in the depth of each
individual consciousness can create something new, then how much more
effective are the vast syntheses of a multitude of individual
consciousnesses that are societies. Society is the most powerful focus of
physical and moral forces that exists in the world.9
Notes
1 Max Nordau, chapter VII, quoted from Sighele.
2 E. Ferri, Nouveaux Horizons, 2nd edition, p. 484.
3 I.e., p. 54.
4 I.e., p. 168.
5 I.e., p. 322.
6 Tarde, Laws of Imitation, p. 153.
7 P. Collet, Elements de sociologie, Paris, Riviere, 1908. Quoted from the Russian publication New
Ideas in Sociology, Issue 2, pp. 84-85.
8 E. Durkheim, Sociology and Cognitive Theory. Quoted from the Russian publication New Ideas in
Sociology, 1914, Issue 2, p. 33. See also Windelband, Zeitschrift f Volkerpsychologie VIII, p. 166 ff.;
Steinthal, ibid., p. 178 ff.
9 E. Durkheim, Sociology and Cognitive Theory. Quoted from the Russian publication New Ideas in
Sociology, 1914, Issue 2, pp. 65-66.
15
Coordinated Collective Actions
Much has already been said about a crowd's actions when we were discussing
the characteristics of the crowd and hereditary organic reflexes. In those
cases, however, we described mostly the actions of a street crowd, which is a
chance gathering of people united by the same mood. It is therefore natural
that this kind of crowd will always be impulsive and in some cases extremely
cruel, while in others, noble, depending on its leaders and on the cause of the
gathering.
At this point, we would like to draw your attention to another aspect of
collective actions when, due to the coordinated efforts of many individuals,
they often achieve the results that are unfeasible for individual persons. In
this case, the joint result of collective actions depends on the greater or lesser
coordination of actions and movements of the members who make up a given
collective.
Examples of such coordinated collective actions are an orchestra, a theatre,
joint collective work of drafting resolutions, dances, acrobatic and gymnastic
exercises, movements of troops, actions of a crowd under someone's
leadership, etc.
Owing to collective activity, modern civilized society generates creative
work that assists the appropriately directed collective efforts in producing
some marvelous creations of architecture, that before our very eyes works
wonders of modern technology in the form of various structures of public and
private nature, and that attracts collective labor to mines, factories, plants and
to any undertaking that is beyond the powers of an individual person.
It has been known for a long time that there is a direct relationship between
any kind of work and mood. This has been proved with precise figures in the
case of individual work.
This is no less true with respect to collective work. Here, too, general
mood affects the intensity of labor in a most dramatic manner.
There are grounds to believe that musical sounds, too, can influence
collective work in terms of its increase or decrease, as is the case with
individual work. This underlies the significance of songs and music for
performing certain forms of collective work.
When discussing the relationship between the successfulness of the work
itself and its stimuli, one might compare the productivity of collective effort
undertaken in the interests of a common cause and the productivity of the
same work effort accomplished in one's own interests. Experience shows that
comparison should favor the latter, and the reason for this is that the
satisfaction of one's own interests provides a stronger stimulus for mimico-
somatic (emotive) reflexes and raises the energy level higher than the
stimulation provided by the synthetic complex of common interests if the
person is not sufficiently pervaded by sociality. Work for the sake of the
common interest often weakens the desire to do more than one's neighbor and
thus weakens individual work effort. Uniform evaluation of work and
uniform distribution of the products of work among all frequently eliminates
competition, an important stimulus for work in general. It goes without
saying that establishing universal control over distribution not only diverts
masses of people from productive labor but also, by requiring a special kind
of guardianship over the personality, oppresses it and simultaneously inhibits
its energy.
At the same time, improvement of production processes is inhibited by the
establishment of mandatory social conditions of work in a certain form.
Elimination of individual ways of production and procurement weakens
private initiative and personal interest in improving one's work. On the other
hand, restriction of needs to a universal standard leads to the suppression of
impulses to activity and inventiveness. Finally, the inability to use the
products of one's labor at one's own discretion suppresses the impulse to
economize and accumulate the products of labor in the interests of the future.
It is thus apparent that, while recognizing the particularly important value for
society of work on collective principles, one has to bear in mind that for the
sake of greater success in improving the speed of production and its quality,
as well as in utilizing the products more economically, individuals should not
be deprived of encouragement measures such as bonuses and other forms of
satisfaction of one's personal interests.
The actions of a collective, however, are expressed not only through
physical labor in the true sense of the word.
Thus, the creation of spiritual and cultural institutions should also be
regarded as collective actions that pursue a particular goal.
One should not forget that stability of energy level in a collective depends
on the circumstances of its manifestation. Thus, in a struggle, a passive
defense is always exhausting, as it does not generate energy, provide training
or skill, nor does it temper the fighting elements in the struggle; the enemy's
unexpected blows produce the oppressive effect of anxiety and perpetual
danger, leading to a fruitless expenditure of energy consumed by the
anticipation of this danger. The situation is quite different in the case of so-
called active defense or attack, in which fighters become experienced and
where maximum energy is generated with each success, which invariably
stimulates a sthenic reaction.
One should also bear in mind that it is not only collective undertakings of
an active nature that can be successful but that in certain cases passive
measures may prove no less effective than active ones. Let us take a strike as
an example of a collective work stoppage. In a certain sense it is often more
effective than any other active measures.
The planned nature of collective work presupposes a degree of organized
behavior in the collective itself, which is achieved with the help of an
executive bureau or authority elected by the collective.
It is apparent that the planned nature of collective activity is directly
related to the degree of organized behavior of the collective itself, while this
latter organization is, in turn, a direct relationship with what is represented by
the executive bureau or authority and with the degree of ability of these
organs to exercise their rights and achieve their goals.
It goes without saying that coordination of movements not only determines
the productivity of collective actions but also provides them with power that
is beyond the reach of individual persons. If you remove this coordination
you will be left with the chaotic movements that hold no hope of overcoming
considerable obstacles.
Thus, every army is based on the coordination of its movements learned
throughout the period of preliminary discipline training.
One may say that the energy of a collective is directly proportional to the
coordination among its individual members established through organized
behavior, and inversely proportional to their disunity.
On the other hand, collectives that lack the appropriate organization more
often display a destructive, rather than creative, force, as exemplified by the
unrestrained actions of a crowd. This is so mostly due to the particular
importance acquired by the demagogical element represented by the leaders.
Social aspirations and social undertakings, as well as collective work, are
directly related to the mood of the community, while the nature of the actions
of the given community depends on the moral qualities and the level of
advancement of its popular masses. Thus, an elevated mood is associated
with the revival of social activities that may occasionally assume an
expansive and even aggressive nature; whereas a low mood is associated with
its passivity, lack of vitality, and a limited scope of action.
Literature—this mirror of public moods—also provides a vivid
manifestation of this rule. In some cases, when the mood is low, one is struck
by the public whining and inaction; under other circumstances the aspirations
of the national heroes and of society itself acquire a wide scope.
With respect to the Russian reality one could observe an animated mood
during the 1860s, while the period of reaction during the 1870-1880s was
associated with a depressed mood, when public whining and Weltschmerz
were in vogue, particularly among the young, which forced the healthy forces
of society to fight against it.
Just as the mood of individual persons is reflected in all spheres of their
activity and is expressed through its suppression and through reactions of a
negative or defensive nature, so the mood of a collective is reflected in its
activity. Thus a low public mood is reflected in visual art and its colors, in
literature, in collective activity, in the sluggishness of external reaction, and
in the negative direction of foreign policies. In a word, everything acquires a
coloring that corresponds to the mood.
On the other hand, we know that victory, by raising the mood in troops,
increases their combat energy, while a defeat, on the contrary, paralyses it.
That is why it is so much more difficult to enter a battle with troops that have
once been defeated than to do this with victorious troops.
It seems, however, that nothing reflects the nature of the popular mood
better than a nation's creative activity and, incidentally, its mythology and
beliefs.
During the periods of suppression of national spirit, religion and mysticism
have always acquired a particular vitality among the popular masses. This
was the case in pre-revolutionary France, and also here in Russia, when the
first open revolutionary act on the 9th of January was even led by the priest
Gapon, with a cross in his hands and gonfalons and icons carried in the front
rows of the demonstration.
It is quite natural that when all political movements are suppressed, the
masses reveal a proclivity towards mysticism, which provides a fertile soil
for religious fervor. Thus, during the eighteenth century in France, this was
manifested in Svedenborg's theosophy, Saint-Madard phenomena, and
Cagliostro's mystical charlatanism.
In Russia, this was manifested in the activities of various sects, discoveries
of a great number of new relics of newly proclaimed saints, and in recent
times, in the rapid dissemination of occultism and "Rasputinism," which was
nourished by religious mysticism as well as by the unsatisfied sexual needs of
aristocratic ladies.
Revolution does not immediately sweep mysticism away, and besides,
during a revolutionary period a certain segment of society that has been
depressed by the events searches for the solution to its problems in religious
beliefs. In addition, as reasoning ability declines during the periods of
popular movements, the impressionability of the crowd, on the contrary,
increases, which nourishes the tendency towards mysticism expressed in the
heightened attraction to everything supernatural and miraculous.
At the very height of the French Revolution, the mysticism of the popular
masses did not disappear but was merely transformed. This is what we read
on this subject in the book by Cabanis and L. Nass (The Revolutionary
Neurosis, p. 328):
Under the influence of the popular festivals that replaced church
ceremonies there was a considerable spread of mysticism among the
masses. On memorial days, when the virtues of a particular hero were
publicly glorified or the triumph of an abstract principle was celebrated, a
kind of prophetic exaltation transformed these people, who aspired to the
new ideals with all their heart and soul. Only newly-born religions are
capable of generating such lofty impulses.
We must admit that, in the process of transition to the ceremonies of the
cult of reason and then of the supreme being, the terrorized society was at
the time obsessed with a true religious delirium that was very close to
madness and that kept growing with the successes of Robespierre's
dictatorship.
In Russia, during the Revolution we observed huge crowds of worshippers
in the temples and even greater crowds flooding the streets during religious
processions—all of this happening at the time when some of the zealous
revolutionaries were preaching condemnation of religion in the crudest
manner from the pulpit and were publicly denying the existence of the
divinity and mocking popular beliefs.
Part 2
Editor 's Foreword—1999
It has been five years since the publication of the first English translation of
Part 1 of V.M. Bekhterev's Collective Reflexology (Strickland, 1994), an
interval during which the frustration involved in a job half-completed was
counterbalanced by satisfaction from seeing that this great Russian scientist's
views were at last attracting positive interest. This is not to mention the sheer
pleasure gained from exploring his ideas in other contexts (e.g., Strickland,
1997), and consequently heightened respect for the achievements of his far-
ranging intellect. The review by Duane Rumbaugh (1996) of the first
translation of Part 1 was, as far as I am aware, the first informed treatment of
Bekhterev's social psychological contribution in English beyond those of Jaan
Valsiner (Valsiner, 1994; Van der Veer, R. & Valsiner, J., 1991) and
ourselves, and it produced an immediate reduction in our feelings of
isolation. We have little doubt that Valsiner's and Rumbaugh's commentaries
made it easier for Tzvetanka Dobreva-Martinova and me to gain support for
and to publish during this interval a translation of Suggestion and Its Role in
Social Life (Bekhterev, 1908/1998), also with Transaction Publishers.
During this half decade, colleagues and I have been discussing Bekhterev's
contributions before a variety of sympathetic audiences (Dobreva-Martinova
and Strickland, 1995; 1996; Strickland, 1997; Strickland and Dobreva-
Martinova, 1996). These efforts also left time to appreciate the enormous
gaps in our picture of this great scientist. Perhaps especially, we attempted to
become more comfortable with the scope of his central concept, "reflex,"
than we had been on the basis of those social-psychological (or, in his terms,
"biosociological") writings on which we had concentrated in Part 1.
An early firsthand introduction to and description in English of Bekhterev's
scientific extension of the reflex concept was provided by the latter's younger
colleague, Prof. A. Gerver (1933), who, toward the end of his commentary on
General Principles of Human Reflexology, stated that Bekhterev's
scientific activity is displayed with peculiar vividness in his creation of a
new and autonomous science which he calls "reflexology." According to
Bekhterev's definition, reflexology is a branch of science which studies the
human personality from the stricdy objective biosocial standpoint.
Reflexology, as Bekhterev teaches, investigates all the manifestations of
the "so-called psychic activity," or, in other words, "the spiritual sphere,"
from the objective standpoint and confines itself to the external
peculiarities of the activity of man: his facial expressions, his gestures,
voice and speech, as a coherent integration of signs, in correlation with the
exciting external influences—physical, biological, and, above all, social—
but also with the internal, regardless of whether either of these two types
of influence is referable to the present or the past. From the standpoint of
reflexology, man is not only a living organism but a "bio-social being"
acting in dependence not only on the natural but also on the social
environment. The divisions of the earlier psychology dealing with
intellect, feeling, memory, will and attention Bekhterev replaces in
reflexology by divisions into higher and personal reflexes, mimico-
somatic reflexes, reflexes of concentration, of reproduction, etc. (Gerver,
1933, pp. 11-12)
The reader may startle at the presumption involved in broadening the
notion of "reflex" with its traditional, restricted, typically Pav-lovian
construal, to facial expressions and gestures, as mentioned above, to the
realm of mob action and inter-army conflict, as discussed in Part 1 of
Collective Reflexology, and to major social movements, as evidenced in Part
2. Hence, it seems useful to review the notion here, extending the
introductory commentary of Jaan Valsiner to Part 1. Some of the following
paragraphs are saturated with quotes from other of Bekhterev's works,
particularly from the rarely noted General Principles of Human Reflexology
(Bekhterev, 1933), to indicate both the extent and insistent nature of
Bekhterev's conviction about the scope of his reflex formulation.
The concept "reflex," referring to a "reflection" and transformation of
energy, was seen by Bekhterev as applicable to every variety and form of life
—from one-celled organisms reacting to bright lights to complex human
groups under conditions of stress and panic, and to societal upheaval.
In his General Principles of Human Reflexology (1933), Bekhterev
painstakingly traced the route by which the reflex notion could be extended:
As we have reason to suppose that the existence of all living beings
depends on the irritability which characterizes their protoplasm, an
irritability which is an energic manifestation at the basis of which
processes of ionization lie, we must keep in mind that the nervous current
which develops in the nervous system of the higher animals and man is
only a derived phenomenon which is a qualified kind of irritability.
But if the nervous current isaqualified kind of irritability of cellular
protoplasm, the question arises: What is the source of that energy which is
manifested in the form of nervous current as a special kind of cell
irritability?
It is obvious that the primary source of this energy lies outside the
organism, and is to be found in food and those stimuli which exert their
influence on the organism through its internal surfaces, the cutaneous and
mucous coats, the movements of the limbs and the whole body in space
(muscular stimuli, semicircular canals) and through special receptive
organs (eye, ear, nose, and mouth).
As early as 1896,1 advanced the hypothesis that our receptive organs are
merely transformers of external energies, so that these energies produce
the nervous current when they impinge on the receptive organs. The
nervous current must be regarded as a manifestation of one kind of the
universal energy. (Bekhterev, 1933,p. 174)
If one were to accept Bekhterev's view of the generality of the reflex, i.e.,
of an event sequence involving "energy in—energy transformed—energy
reflected" in the lives of both lower and higher organisms, then one could
also, by extension of the laws applicable to energy, accept his argument that
the behavior of individuals and groups becomes explainable in the same
terms that applied to external energy and its transfer (e.g., in mechanics) in
the universe-at-large. Thus, with this concession, one could reasonably
explain of the activities of persons and groups in terms of the law of
conservation of energy, the law of gravity, of equal and opposite reactions, of
inertia, of relativity, etc. This explanatory effort reaches its zenith in
Collective Reflexology (with groups and collectives in Part 1, with larger
social units in Part 2), but, at the level of the person, it is in the rarely cited
(but here frequently quoted) General Principles of Human Reflexology
(Bekhterev, 1933) that the derivation is at its most detailed in English.
Inhibited Reflexes and the Experience of "Mind"
If the social events of general interest to psychologists and sociologists are
acknowledged to be in some manner the products of thinking minds, one may
wonder how Bekhterev proposed to get from reflected energy to mental
phenomena? This question follows from the common but erroneous
assumption that such a materialistic scheme as reflexology could offer no
place to the "spiritual" event. Bekhterev seems to have evaded the mind-body
problem by asserting that it simply could not pose a challenge for an energic/
reflexological analysis. Psychic or conscious experience was but a
qualitatively different variety of reflection. The private conscious event was
itself generated by the inhibition of a behavioral reflex, but one property
which made it amenable to objective scientific analysis was that it was
publicly accountable: one could observe and tell another, or otherwise reveal
to him/her, what "was on one's mind." As a consequence of the
communicability of accounts, scientists could analyze them, not necessarily
assuming their validity, just as they could any other data. The scientist could
also tap unconscious psychic activity through other associative reflex routes.
Bekhterev had first outlined a model of mind with special reference to issues
of the conscious and the unconscious (for him, the "personal sphere" and the
"general sphere") in Suggestion and Its Role in Social Life (1908/1998).
These concepts were modified as he developed his reflexological doctrine,
and in his General Principles of Human Reflexology, after a discussion of
creativity during sleep, Bekhterev (1933) said:
In the subjective world of our thought processes, we are, as you know, by
no means conscious of everything, but rather of only the final result of our
thinking, while the process itself by which we come to the given
conclusion usually takes place to a great extent in the sphere of the
unconscious or unaccountable processes.
Taking into consideration certain processes of the unconscious, i.e., direct
knowledge in the form of intuition, and also unconscious "intuitive"
creativeness, we must, on the contrary, come to the conclusion that the
conscious is not at all an indispensable factor in establishing of the
appropriate correlations between the individual and the environment.
On our part, we do not think of denying "psychic" reality, but we are
justified in maintaining that it is not something isolated from the brain
processes. We say that the psychical process is inseparable from brain
process and, therefore, when studying brain processes in their higher
manifestations, we study along with them the course and development of
psychical processes, (p. 44)
It is significant, as noted above, that one of Bekhterev's criteria for the
occurrence of a conscious mental event was that it be communicable (in his
terms above, "accountable"). This turns out to be a social psychological
standard for what we nowadays consider to be the subject matter of
"personality." Bekhterev (1933) addressed the issue as follows:
As we have already stated, we regard the physical and the psychical as a
unitary phenomenon in one process of nervous current, as energy passing
through the higher ranges of the nervous system. From experience, we
know that this phenomenon as such can reveal itself in man in its complete
form—with the participation of the psychic ingredient—only by means of
active concentration, which is usually closely associated with the stimuli
which issue from the somatic sphere. If we call one reflex conscious, and
other subconscious or unconscious, this does not mean that the latter are
not accompanied by the element of the psychic, or by consciousness,
during their activity, but only when they are not reproduced as are other
activities, and, consequently, are not "accountable." To the category of the
accountable belong all reflexes which occur when active concentration
participates, (p. 104)
Prior to attempting to compare his "objective" formulations about the
psyche with leading "subjective" formulations, Bekhterev characteristically
reiterated his basic position, perhaps so often because he felt he had to
remind his audience that it was neither possible nor necessary to define a link
between a material event and a parallel, immaterial "soul":
Everything said above leads to the conclusion that psychic or subjective
processes are, as we have already mentioned, a result of tension of energy
or of nervous current in certain areas of the cerebral cortex, while there is
simultaneous inhibition of other areas. In cases in which the conditions of
nervous conductivity are facilitated and the nervous current moves
unimpeded, the subjective process or consciousness is weakened and even
disappears. We have already said that, for instance, every action
performed for the first time is mostly conscious, while every habitual
action is unconscious. But even an habitual action may become conscious,
if it is performed slowly and with concentration on each component
movement.
Concentration, as a process of excitation of a definite center, is invariably
bound up with impediment or inhibition of other centers; hence it happens
that everything that produces concentration is accompanied by conscious
perception; everything that is not accompanied by concentration goes
unperceived 
and, 
consequently, 
occurs 
without 
participation 
of
consciousness. (Bekhterev, 1933, p. 401)
To again reinforce his view that the mental event has ties to something
ultimately observable objectively, Bekhterev (1933) stated:
Finally, what are we to call ideas and thoughts, by which we understand
the subjective process which has attained a certain degree of completeness
and development? Is this process distinct from other manifestations of
association-reflex activity? As a matter of fact every idea is accompanied
either by weakly expressed external movements, in the form of words or
actions, or by internal movements and secretions, (p. 402)
With this line of thought, Bekhterev certainly anticipated subsequent
conjectures that thinking was a variety of internal speech, but his
preoccupation 
with 
objectivity 
was 
doubtless 
directed 
at 
those
contemporaries who construed thought as a purely "spiritual" phenomenon,
and he felt (probably correctly, in tsarist Russia for one reason and in
communist Russia for another) that the materialistic/ energic argument
needed insistent repetition at every opportunity:
It is clear from this that thought is also a higher association reflex, but a
reflex inhibited in its external manifestation, as we have already said.
Consequently, there is but one process, in which the inner phenomena, in
the form of thought, are merely inhibited reflexes in which the subjective
process is more marked in comparison with the external objective process.
(Bekhterev, 1933, p. 403)
The talk of "concentration" (today we would frequently substitute the term,
"attention") and "ideas" may sound as if Bekhterev's accountable psychic
phenomena involved only cognitions in relation to one another and to
behavioral events. However, as Gerver (1933) indicates, he took a similar
line with respect to feelings: were "feelings" primary or secondary
phenomena? He said,
for us, who hold the energic view, which we make the basis of
reflexology, this question does not arise, for, as a result, of the
manifestation of the nervous current, we have simultaneously, in the given
case, both external or objective phenomena manifested in the sympathico-
somatic sphere and inner or subjective phenomena, the origin of which
may be, in one case, a result of a higher association reflex, in another, a
result of lower reflexes arising in the somatic sphere....Let us take the
sphere of feeling....when we weep over the coffin of a dear person, the
primary impulse arises under the influence of an external stimulus and, by
establishing an association reflex, excites a reaction in the form a general
physical state which leads to tears. In this case, the beginning was a higher
or association reflex, which evoked the same cardio-vascular reaction as
appears in a physical state conditioned by strong, external stimulations, for
instance, intense destructive processes. (Bekhterev, 1933, p. 405)
Thus, feelings as "mimico-somatic" reflexes were central for Bekhterev as
of course they were for his better-known European contemporaries, the
psychoanalysts, who were also transforming the view of thinking persons.
Bekhterev and the Psychoanalytic Movement
Most of one's thoughts, these inhibited behavioral reflexes, might
reasonably be expected to be shared with the rest of one's culture; however,
some would become problematic for the individual, might not "fit" with the
rest of the person's world. Bekhterev's psychiatric work on interpretation of
these troublesome psychic reflexes inevitably brought him into contact with
contemporaneous formulations concerning the human personality, in
particular those of Freud, Jung, and Adler, and he compared his own
approach with theirs in surprising detail (see Bekhterev, 1933, Ch. 51-53).
With respect to Freud, he said:
We cannot help seeing the correlation of Reflexology with Freud's
doctrine, known as psycho-analysis, first of all in so-called catharsis
(purification by confession) which is equivalent to discharge of a
"strangulated" affect or of an inhibited mimico-somatic drive. Is this not
the discharge of a reflex which, when inhibited, oppresses the personality,
shackles and diseases it, while, when there is a discharge of the reflex
(catharsis), naturally the pathological condition disappears? Is not the
weeping out of a sorrow a discharge of an impeded reflex? (Bekhterev,
1933, p. 413)
Psychic trauma as a cause of neurosis is referred by Freud chiefly to the
sexual sphere. This, however, cannot be accepted without very extensive
reservations and has met with much opposition in scientific literature.
(Bekhterev, 1933, p. 414)
Freud sets out from the supposition that associations are grouped into
complexes, and, consequently, neither the individual's words nor his
gestures are casual when his thoughts are concentrating on anything, but
refer to the complex to which concentration is directed. (Bekhterev, 1933,
pp. 414-415)
Of the "reflexes" which were the subject matter of early investigations of
Breuer and Freud, Bekhterev (1933) said:
These reflexes are abnormal partly because they are a pathological
response to external impressions and partly because they are not a direct
response to an impression received, but are a response only through the
mediation of pathological memories. These latter, isolating themselves
from the patient's own observation, or his ego, give hysterical symptoms
their apparent arbitrariness and this, by the way, distinguishes hysteria
from traumatic neuroses.
Dissociation of the ego is by no means unusual. On the contrary, even in
the normal state, this splitting may be observed to a certain extent. We
may look without seeing, hear without being accountable to ourselves in
respect of what we hear. Needless to say, the splitting is much deeper and
more considerable in pathological states. In hypnosis and suggestion, we
have obviously a similar, only deepened, process of dissociation of the
subject's ego or personality. (Bekhterev, 1933, p. 414)
To clarify his disagreement with Freud, Bekhterev turned to the issue of
sexual energy, and he expressed qualified support for Adler:
It must be noted here that Freud's method, since it is exclusively
subjective, has led its initiator to the gravely erroneous conclusion that
sexual trauma is the basis of the common neurosis. Such error frequently
results when investigations are given a subjective basis. The Great War
produced an enormous number of neurotics, and here the traumata were of
a different kind, and rarely sexual. Thereupon, Freud's disciple, Adler,
advanced the doctrine, not of sexual trauma, propounded by his master as
the cause of the common neuroses, but of social conflict, in which the
neurotic manifests an active attitude toward the illness ("flight into
illness"), which for him is something desired as an inevitable escape from
the given situation. Adler's theory also, though considerably wider in
fundamental principles than Freud's, is based to a considerable extent on
subjective analysis, and therein lies its weakness. (Bekhterev, 1933, p.
415)
Bekhterev seemed most favorably disposed to Jung's approach:
Let us point out that Jung's investigations (mentioned above) in respect of
the association method, which takes the form of stimulus-words, have
afforded an important objective indication of what has been experienced
and done, and this indication may be used in revealing both the causes of
neuroses and of criminal acts, fields in which it is already beginning to be
applied. For this purpose, a special list may be compiled in which words
that relate to what has been experienced are inserted among indifferent
words, and it transpires that, when a word is presented to the subject in
order to stimulate free association with other words, a more or less marked
delay results in respect of the reaction, when words bearing reference to
grievous experiences are presented. (Bekhterev, 1933, p.415)
After reminding us that interpretation of such delays using subjective
states, like "guilt," is "exceedingly relative," Bekhterev adds that
the objective understanding of phenomena from the reflexological
standpoint throws a light, hitherto unavailable, on Freud's theory, with its
processes of repression (inhibition), conversion (substitution), etc., and at
the same time, through the scheme of abnormal reflexes, supplements
those processes, which were regarded as purely subjective states.
(Bekhterev, 1933, p. 415)
In closing his general comparison with psychoanalytic theories, Bekhterev
(1933) said,
It is true that psycho-analysis as presented by Breuer, and more so as
presented by Freud, who developed it into a system, is, as we have said,
teeming with a subjectivism often too involved and difficult to be
explained even from the viewpoint of contemporary subjective
psychology. Nevertheless, it is clear from all that we have said above that
the psycho-analytic method of treatment is easily understood by means of
the reflexological doctrine of association-reflex activity.
It is unnecessary to enter here into the details of this problem. We merely
state that, from the viewpoint of reflexology, a strangulated affect is just
an inhibited, that is, externally unexpressed, mimico-somatic reflex, which
is revived by the psycho-analytic method, that is verbal stimuli, is
ultimately released from inhibition, and is liberated, so that there results a
cure, which presupposes the liberation of other reflexes also which have
been suppressed in inhibition of the fundamental reflex. (Bekhterev, 1933,
pp. 415-416)
Dreams and the Reflexological Approach
It will not come as a surprise that Bekhterev went even further with his
reflexological explanation of mental phenomena to the point of examining
dreams, which he judged to be so vivid precisely because the person was not
overtly acting at the time of the dream's occurrence:
There is no doubt that dreams also, the detailed psychology of which has
been elucidated by psycho-analysis, may be harmonized with the data of
reflexology. Freud himself, when outlining the scheme of dream
development, remarks that the psychic apparatus is constructed after the
model of a reflex. It is true that he intends this comparison to be taken
figuratively, but, clearly, in discussing complex psychic phenomena, even
he cannot dispense with the scheme of the reflex. (Bekhterev, 1933, p.
417)
But Bekhterev disagreed with what he took as Freud's position on the
source of the dream:
According to Freud's view, in dreams there is a process which consists in
a regression of the nervous current to the mechanism of perception. The
phenomenon of regression he regards as nothing new, for thought in
general often regresses along the path of memory, but usually does not
transgress the limits separating it from perception, for memory never
presents such vivid pictures as does perception. In dreams, on the contrary,
regression extends much farther, almost as far as hallucinations.
(Bekhterev, 1933, p. 417)
Bekhterev's alternative to the hypothesized regression of mental processes
to the perceptual level was an ingenious and provocative formulation:
Dreams are, like thoughts, reflexes inhibited in their external
manifestation, but, in contradistinction to thoughts, dreams are
distinguished by special vividness, because the suppressing influence of
the active part of the personality—that part which controls internal
stimulations—is removed. (Bekhterev, 1933, p. 418)
But psychologists are mistaken in thinking that we have, in dreams, a
process regressive in respect of the sequence of the thinking process, for
they maintain that here there is a regression to processes of perception.
We have referred above to the fact that processes freely discharged in
motor reactions in the waking state occur without intense subjective
images, while all inhibition of motor acts produces extreme tension and
intensity of the subjective state. Therefore, there is no necessity to have
recourse to the hypothesis of a regressive movement of the current in order
to explain dreams, but we have only to recognize that, during sleep, there
are conditions inhibiting external discharge in the form of movement, and
this, under the influence of inflowing stimulations, enormously raises the
intensity of subjective states. (Bekhterev, 1933, p. 418)
And, indeed, in sleep we have obviously inhibition of all motor reactions,
for, notwithstanding even vivid pictures of movement, conversation, etc.,
all the sleeper's motor organs remain motionless. We shall not here enter
into an explanation of this phenomenon, which is obviously associated
with suppression of the active part of the personality, this part being
centered in the frontal areas. But a fact is a fact, and this fact explains the
existence of dreams, for according to the thesis propounded by us,
inhibition of an external reaction, when this inhibition is accompanied by
simultaneous excitation of a certain area, always raises somewhat the
intensity of the inner or subjective experiences. (Bekhterev, 1933, p. 419)
Bekhterev (1933) extended his analyses to other of Freud's basic concepts
(e.g., "censorship" by the super-ego being construed as inhibitory associative
reflexes that stand in opposition to a given complex; phantasy; poetic
creativity, etc.). Likewise, he developed a reflexological analysis of affective
phenomena such as moods, motives, tendencies, etc. But it really is not
necessary to "dilate further on this here," as Bekhterev himself would often
say. It is enough for present purposes to acknowledge the nearly super-human
feat that he executed in developing such a "grand theory." One can only
speculate about what the immediate future of his efforts might have been,
perhaps in correspondence with Freud and other contemporaries, had he not
left the scene so suddenly.
From Individual to Group Reflexological Processes
If Bekhterev's group psychology, i.e., collective reflexology, had been
called "biosociology" from the start, perhaps its general reception would have
been a bit more sympathetic, but even then one might have anticipated some
reluctance to accept the following paragraphs from Part 1—"In Place of a
Foreword"—(p. 27, present revised translation):
As we shall see later, a strictly objective examination of the subject leads
inevitably to the conclusion that manifestations of the composite
personality revealed through the analysis of social life are ruled by the
same law that is revealed through a strictly objective reflexological study
of manifestations of the individual personality. The very forms of this law
turn out to be the same for both the individual personality and the
composite one; this should certainly be recognized as an advance in
knowledge, since the theory of the opposition between the personality and
the crowd accepted by many authors who wrote about the crowd is to a
considerable degree exaggerated.
As will be shown below, one encounters among the manifestations of
social life essentially the same reflexes, in the form of universal, eternal
movements following the same path and evolution, as one finds in
individual activity. This should be quite clear if we take into consideration
the fact that a collective is nothing other than a group of individuals inter-
connected through their various interests and representing a single entity
in the form of a collective, or composite, personality.
In his Introduction to Part 1, Bekhterev restated his position:
While reflexology studies the manifestations of individual associative
activity, collective reflexology studies the same activity of the collective.
It reveals, therefore, how this activity is manifested in the case of social
affiliation between several (two, three, or more) individuals and to what
extent the characteristics of this associative activity depend on the nature
of the social bond established between individual persons, (p. 40, present
translation)
At the close of his Introduction to Part 1, he rephrases his argument once
again:
The opportunity to reveal the regular development of individual
expression and actions presented itself when we started investigating
human behaviour not from the standpoint of subjective causality but from
a strictly objective point of view, recognizing the need to regard all acts of
human personality as manifestations of higher, or associative, reflexes.
The latter are based on energy, and whether one calls it neurological or
neuropsychological does not really matter, for in any case it is energy
similar in its nature to other kinds of energy. It is thus evident that there
must also be a certain developmental regularity in the manifestations of
popular masses who represent a composite personality.
In this respect, observation and experience lead us to infer that the basic
laws of associative activity of the composite personality are the same as
those operating throughout the whole of animate and inanimate nature.
Here we disclose through analysis the same universal cosmic laws such as
the law of energy conservation, of gravity, of repulsion, the law of equal
and opposite reactions, of similarity, rhythm, entropy, evolution,
differentiation, integration or synthesis, the law of adaptation, of selection,
inertia, etc.
We shall see that the world is ruled by the same basic laws which are
common to all phenomena in general, both inorganic and organic as well
as supra-organic, or social, which will be discussed in more detail below.
It cannot be otherwise. If the world of animate nature is the result of the
transformation and complication of inanimate nature through evolution—
the natural sciences give us no grounds whatsoever to doubt it—then why
should there be one set of laws for the inorganic world, another for the
organic and a third for the supraorganic world? (pp. 42, present
translation)
The Fate of Bekhterev's Social-Reflexological Theory
It would be satisfying to be able to complete this Foreword with a
comment on the reception which the social science community gave to this
"grand theory," but in introductions to Part 1 and to Suggestion and Its Role
in Social Life we have discussed how Bekhterev's efforts in the area of social
psychology remained hidden for at least a half century. In the ordinary course
of events for our field, Bekhterev's reflexological doctrine would most
certainly have received more attention—in social psychology, at the very
least, by Gordon Allport, (who did acknowledge one Bekhterev paper
published in German and one book (Suggestion and Its Role in Social Life, in
Russian) in his discussion of "simple and sovereign theories" in the
influential chapter, "The Historical Background of Modern Social
Psychology" (Allport, 1954). In this widely cited source, Allport compared
with one another the positions of all those (Western) theorists with whom
Bekhterev compared himself (e.g., Spencer, Tarde, Binet, McDougall, Le
Bon, Sighele, etc.). As one possible consequence of more detailed
consideration by Allport, Bekhterev's heroic efforts would have been at least
acknowledged in subsequent literature concerning theories of social behavior.
Alas, this has happened almost not at all.
On the other hand, it is also possible that, by now, whatever the attraction
such a general theory of reflexology might have held in the 1920s, or even
the 1950s, it would have faded with the evolution of criteria to which a "good
scientific theory" should hold. One is reminded of a provocative paper by
Warren Thorngate (1976), wherein he pointed out in his discussion of
"postulates of impotence" that, while psychologists have been long taught to
value most highly a theory that can boast simplicity of formulation, accuracy
of prediction, and generality of application, it is the unfortunate case that it is
impossible to generate such a theory: the theory that is general and simple
will not be able to make accurate predictions; the theory that is simple and
accurate will have limited generality; the theory that is general and accurate
will not be a simple one. Thorngate cites a similar contention by Levins
(1968), applicable to biological systems, to the effect that "it is not possible to
maximize simultaneously generality, realism, and precision" (p. 7). The
reflexological theory of Bekhterev, which began in biological systems and
was elaborated to explain social systems, would doubtless have proved to be
as vulnerable in the one system as in the other. But it is not timely to criticize
reflexology's vulnerabilities in terms of philoso-phy-of-science formulations
developed a half century after the theory was first presented. It is perhaps
enough to admire the genius and bravery of the person who would have
attempted such a venture in his own time, and to regret that his ideas did not
receive the attention they deserved.
References

Foreword
Once we have analyzed the various facets of associative activity, it becomes
essential to investigate the laws that apply to the activity of the collective,
since no science should be satisfied with only a description of its phenomena.
To the contrary, it must explore the dependence of these postulates on
external factors of some form or other. This aspect of scientific discipline is
especially important, since in discovering the fixed regularities between two
phenomena and the law-determined reliance of some phenomena on others,
we gain the potential to predict the course of phenomena or to foresee the
future. In these two facts lies the true value of scientific knowledge.
If a mathematician predicts the divisibility or indivisibility of a number,
then he does so employing a previously ascertained dependence. If an
astronomer forecasts solar or lunar eclipses, he can do so only thanks to the
discovery that the movement of planets depends on the laws of gravity,
established by Newton.
If a chemist can foretell the atomic mass of an existent, but not necessarily
located, body and, to a point, its characteristics, then he also does this only
because of the discovery of law-determined dependence, which is, in turn,
explained by Mendeleyev's periodic system. If a mechanic builds a new car,
having planned in advance how it will work, he achieves this through the
knowledge of a certain dependence of phenomena on the correlation between
one lever and another. In the same manner, a physicist can calculate the
number of joules needed to warm a body of a certain magnitude, by
employing a certain dependence. We have the same ability, to some extent, in
biological investigations, although the extent of this ability does not come
close to that of astronomy, chemistry, mechanics, and physics.
Nevertheless, Mendel's laws provide us with the opportunity to anticipate
the characteristics of offspring, and Darwin's teachings help clarify the
growth and evolution of organisms and hypothesize on the nature of species
whose extinct forms are discovered in geological strata.
Thus, a paleontologist can, using the same concept of dependence,
reproduce from one or more parts of a skeleton an extinct species. The
humanitarian sciences should obviously be pursuing the same end; indeed,
some of them have already established a certain relationship between
phenomena which is sufficient to postulate the course of phenomena in the
future.
They were able to establish this dependence thanks to a strictly objective
method, such as the one which is employed in political economics but which
is nonexistent in sociology. The sociological law of historical illusionism
shows, among other things, how the subjective understanding of phenomena
differs from objective reality. For example, it is proven that the participants in
the French Revolution felt that they were fighting for freedom, equality, and
fraternity when in truth the battle was fought for the release of the bourgeois
from the domination of feudalism; in other words, the conflict forwarded the
interests of capitalism. The same can be observed in Christianity. Did it stand
for the teachings of Christ, as its proponents stated, or for the scholastics, for
the hierarchic organization of the Church, and for the rule of Christian
materialism, as is revealed today? There is a large discrepancy between the
ideas promoted by the proponents of Christianity and the objective facts of its
historic development, which even the leaders of the movement failed to
notice. It is only necessary to remember the Inquisition in order to prove this
point. Another example of illusionism is the recent World War, at the root of
which lay the economic rivalry between England and Germany.
Unquestionably, each side in the conflict strove to vanquish the other in order
to leave it in economic ruin, which, in the end, served to undermine the
capitalist ideal and instigate a general trend towards socialism.
For the above reasons, all subjectivity must be excluded from sociology
and from the study of popular mass movements in general, and only objective
study can provide the opportunity to establish certain correlations and laws.
We must strive for the same thing in collective reflexology. The fact that in
individual associative activity we have been able to confirm certain
dependences instills the hope that in collective associative activity these
dependences will also be found.
In addition, the analysis of associative phenomena displayed by the
collective brings us to the conclusion that the dependences here are the same
as those observed in relation to the associative activity of the individual.1
Each of these factors, then, serves in the given manner as a confirmation
and reference point for the other.
In the end, it must be acknowledged that if the reflexology of an individual
is based on energic science, which recognizes all the external characteristics
and manifestations of an individual as being the result of the energy he
accumulates with the processes of birth, nourishment and the influence of
external factors, then collective reflexology can only become a science—with
the ability to outline the laws of social life—when it takes an energic point of
view and explores the vital manifestations of each collective in relation to the
circumstances which led to, and the reasons for, its creation; in relation to the
characteristics of the individuals which make it up; and finally in relation to
the external factors that have affected the collective in the past and the
present. This will enable us to look at the results of collective activity and the
factors that influence them, as well as to investigate the correlations or laws
that govern the actions of the collective.
In the text that follows, we will analyze these correlations under separate
categories, defined as laws. As was previously mentioned, the basic
principles or laws of the supraorganic world, in its objective study, are the
same as those of the organic and inorganic worlds.2 This is the best proof that
reflexology, as a strictly objective science that studies individual and
collective associative activity, comes closer to being natural science than
does any subjective discipline such as general or experimental psychology,
which employs a method of self-observation that cannot be applied to the
sciences of living and nonliving nature.
Notes
1 See V. Bekhterev, General Principles of Human Reflexology (in Russian), Petrograd, 1918.
2 On the same topic, see my investigation (1920/21): On the basic laws of the world (in Russian), and
others in Issues in the Study and Education of the Personality, issues 2,3 and 4.
1
The Law of Conservation of Energy
The Law of Conservation of Energy, discovered by R. Mayer, widened in
scope by the work of the English physicist and technician Joule, and finalized
by Helmholtz, needs no explanation in the present day because it is widely
accepted and understood. Basically, this law stipulates that the amount of
energy in a closed system does not diminish or increase, and that all forms of
energy in nature are, in relation to each other, equivalent. As for the Principle
of Conservation of Matter, it is simply a subsection of the principle of
conservation of energy, since matter is nothing more than a bundle of
(restricted) energy. Besides, in the present day it is difficult to speak of the
conservation of matter without making exceptions for the discovery of
radioactive bodies.
In my book, General Principles of Human Reflexology, I prove that since
our sensory organs are, in essence, transformers of external energy (which
was already stated by me in 1896 in a separate work1), any activity a person
executes is subject to the law of conservation of energy.
The social process is dependent on the collective energy of individuals and
is always manifested in some activity, work, and the like. This, in turn, can
only be done with the consumption or expenditure of energy.
It goes without saying that very rarely does the collective perform a task as
a whole; more often, the initial work is done by one or several people, while
the others are onlookers, listeners, or even imitators. However, this can also
be called collective work—granted, it is unequally distributed—since some
expend stored energy, while others transform the byproducts (external
stimuli) into reserves of energy, or release their own energy under the
influence of a stimulus that causes imitative behavior, i.e., do so very
economically. Nevertheless, in one way or another, that the whole collective
participates in a single activity is indisputable.
In this way, social or collective energy is made up of the combined energy
reserves of all the participants in a single task. It is important to keep in mind,
however, that the energy expended by these participants is not all converted
into useful output or work; some of it is wasted on overcoming the inertia of
the collective, on internal friction between workers, on overcoming various
external obstacles, etc.
Thus, only a fraction of the originally available energy is channeled into
useful work, as is the case during the execution of any mechanical work.
It is possible to apply to the performance of collective work the
parallelogram of force used in mechanics, namely: the result of action is
manifested in a certain direction, and is equal to all efforts made by the
separate individuals participating in the given collective work.
To social energy are also applicable the processes of suppression or
inhibition, and those of augmentation, which are dependent on the application
of new strength or on the release of individuals' energy reserves; this will be
discussed later in more detail. A social movement that encounters opposition
either becomes part of another movement or is temporarily inhibited, but it
does not disappear.
Already, these facts support the assumption that the law of conservation of
energy can be applied to the activity of the collective as well as to the
individual person. However, up until now the application to mankind of the
principle of conservation of energy has been hindered by subjective outlooks
on the human personality, and as a result this understanding could not be
established.
The strictly objective point of view, taken by reflexology and eliminating
subjective phenomena, acknowledges that every individual is basically an
accumulator of energy, which has already begun to be collected at conception
and continues to be accumulated with the ingestion of food and with the help
of sensory organs functioning as transformers of energy. In its turn, reserved
energy is used to perform kinetic and molecular work in the flexing of
muscles and in glandular secretion. There can be no doubt that the law of
conservation of energy can be applied to humans, as to any living organism,
since otherwise it would lose its status as a universal law.
Today, we know that the energy reserves of our brain centers are held,
primarily, in the granular section of neurons, since cellular grains disintegrate
as a result of continuous function and fatigue. In this way, the very substrate
of nervous energy is known to us.
Recently, it has even become possible to determine the percent of useful
work the human organism can do in relation to the amount of energy it
receives with food. Specialized research has shown that useful work executed
by human muscle has an efficiency of approximately 21 percent, whereas a
steam engine gives only 13 percent useful work for all the energy it uses.
Moreover, fitter individuals efficiently use up to 36 percent of the energy
they accumulate. Of course, in all these situations, the expenditure of energy
on the functioning of internal glands and other somatic organs (sweat
production, faster blood circulation, etc.) is not taken into account. The fact
that humans utilize energy more efficiently than steam engines is explained in
this way: the human "engine" replenishes, to a certain extent, its deficit with
the stored energy that is always in the body's centers, thanks not only to the
absorption of nutrients, but also to the transformation of different forms of
energy in the external environment, which affect our external sensory
devices.
In my article, "The immortality of the human personality from a scientific
point of view" (in Russian), Vestnik Znaniia, 1918, and other publications, I
discuss in detail the fact that the creativity of human associative activity lives
forever.
"A person's 'spiritual' self," I write in that paper,
in possessing self-contained value, can never vanish without trace. In this
way, every human individual who combines the experience of his
ancestors with his own does not terminate his existence with the
termination of his life. On the contrary, he continues to exist in all those
people whose lives he affected and, eventually, is preserved in posterity. It
can be said that during his lifetime, an individual distributes his energy
among those who surround him, who in turn transfer this energy to others,
