continuing this cycle to the farthest reaches of human communication and
interaction. In a composite human personality, everyone is connected in an
intricate relationship, such that no single event can occur without having
widespread repercussions. One heroic deed breeds another, just as one
crime begets another.
But if the action of one individual cannot be erased, then conversely there
is nothing that can produce energy without the related external influences; in
other words, no extra human energy arises outside certain mutual
correlations. While on this subject, it is impossible not to mention the
observations that L. Tolstoy made, quite correctly, in his famous work War
and Peace (p. 272), and which I have already stated in reflexology:
The recognition of individual freedom as a force that is able to influence
historical events, i.e., as one not subject to any laws, is to history what the
recognition of the total freedom of planetary movement would be to
astronomy.
The above admissions eliminate all possibility of the existence of laws,
and thus the existence of any knowledge. If even one freely moving planet
exists, then the laws of Kepler and Newton are nullified, as is our
understanding of the movements of heavenly bodies. If one free human
action exists, then there are no historical laws and we have no
understanding of historical events.
From here, naturally, stems the conclusion that human energy, like any
other, can neither be created nor destroyed and simply changes from one
form of work or energy to another.
It is evident, then, that when analyzing the collective personality or the
collective we must keep in mind the same law of conservation of energy.
Here, too, this law should be considered of equal importance, since if it is
possible to apply it to the individual, then it is equally possible to apply it to
the large number of individuals that make up a collective and are connected
to each other in one way or another. A crowd, in the same way as a solitary
individual, is stimulated by its surroundings thanks to its sensory detectors,
sight and hearing; it is likewise an accumulator of energy that is transformed
into kinetic energy or work, and emitted as a reaction to external stimulants
of one form or another.
Of course, we cannot hope to test this law in practice, since it is not
possible to take into account the amount of stored energy in every individual,
nor can we allow for the way in which this energy is combined when
individuals are brought together in a crowd. However, arbitrarily designating
a certain quantity of energy to both, it can be said that the energy of the
collective personality is expended depending on the amount of its stored
energy, and depending on the strength of the stimuli causing the latter's
release; and that the direction and power of these releases of energy,
manifested as work, are subject to a certain regularity, as statistics show. This
last fact leaves no doubt as to the fact that all phenomena of social life show a
relation between given causes and their respective effects.
In any case, it is erroneous to assume that the individual mind controls
occurrences. Only the above conditions can serve to bring forward one or
another individual, and allow him to become the spokesperson for social
moods and collective actions. Naturally, it follows that in social matters the
role of the majority is limited by individual attributes, and, as a result, an
individual representing a certain view can influence a crowd and even crush
the said majority. This is, in a nutshell, the role of the individual in popular
movements.
In times of social upheaval the role of the crowd becomes so much greater
that its so-called instincts, or inherited/organic reflexes take the upper hand,
and the intellectual control of those individuals who act as leaders of the
crowd is reduced; whereas in calmer periods of social life, the role of the
director of the crowd's actions is more significant.
In any case, not only do the actions of separate people not disappear
without trace, but a crowd's creativity is also preserved through its
transformation, over time, into various forms of energy.
Does a national collective, having created a culture, ever "spiritually" die?
It can fall apart, separate, or be subjected to foreign rule. But in all these
cases, the results of a nation's creativity are passed on to, and used by, other
nations who mold that creativity into new forms, as was done by the peoples
that inherited the "spiritual" riches of ancient Greece and Rome.
In any case, for those who maintain a strictly objective view of the human
individual in accordance with the teachings of reflexology, i.e., reject the
subjectivity of contemporary psychology and observe in humans only
associative activity, there can be no doubt that the law of conservation of
energy must be applied to the individual and his actions. In concluding, I
must mention that the reflexology of humans has been studied by myself only
from an énergie2 point of view, which I have already stated in the book,
Psyche and Life,3 and analyzed in more minute detail in the French and
German editions of the same name.4
Notes
1 V. Bekhterev, Obozrenie Psikhiatrii and Neurologisches Centmlblatt, 1896.
2 See V. Bekhterev, General Principles of Human Reflexology (inRussian), Petrograd, 1918.
3 V. Bekhterev, Psyche and Life (in Russian), St. Petersburg. SPB.
4 W. Bechterew, Psyche und Leben, Wiesbaden; also by Bechterew, L'Activité psychique et /a vie,
Paris.
2
The Law of the Proportional
Relationship between Speed of Motion
and Motive Power
In mechanics, there is a long-known law which states that any increase in the
speed of an object's movement is determined by its motive power, which is
proportional to and of the same direction as the movement. This law is a
general one, and can be applied not only to the inanimate but also to the
organic and supraorganic worlds. In the organic world, every function can be
subject to acceleration, depending on certain stimuli, and this increase in
speed is proportional to the strength of the stimulus and the direction of an
organism's actions. However, since organisms are made up of essentially
closed systems in which the extent of development and the direction of most
functions is predetermined, the application of this law is limited.
In associative activity, as we know, a stimulus is proportional to the force
of its agent until functions are exhausted and the system begins to break
down. As for the orientation of the stimulus, it is determined by the structure
of motor organs and therefore can only be in one direction. However, in this
case the stimulus can function as such only if it has the same orientation as
the organs, and so conforms to the law.
In the social world we find confirmations of the above law in many
different situations.
Society is in continuous mobile equilibrium as a consequence of the
interaction of stimulating and inhibiting conditions; social movements take
on a form that is determined by the dominance of the latter over the former
and flow in the direction of the resultant force. In view of their significance, it
is important that we investigate both of the aforementioned stimulating and
inhibiting influences in greater detail.
When a social movement is born, it is dependent on the dominant
stimulating, facilitating, or vitalizing conditions. This last condition can be
either natural or artificial. Natural vitalizing conditions encompass, first of
all, the forces that are in direct correspondence with the main stimulus, the
one that initiated the social movement. Let us assume, for instance, that we
are dealing with a patriotic movement that was instigated by wartime
conditions. It is quite natural that in such a situation all instances of apparent
military success would increase and strengthen national patriotic feelings in
one way or another.
The development of a religious movement is strengthened by the execution
of various religious rites and ceremonies. On the other hand, the growth of an
antigovernmental social movement is vitalized with every event that makes
the success of the movement more likely, etc. Furthermore, natural vitalizing
conditions of a social movement include all those conditions that, although
they are not in direct contact with the movement, are in harmony with its
objectives.
Thus, movements of a moral nature are encouraged by religious circles;
sports or gymnastics movements receive support from military spheres, since
physical exercises help in the military preparation of the population, etc. This
also explains the fact that the more extremist parties receive support in
revolutionary times, while during calmer periods moderate parties are given
primary consideration.
If we are presented with a military collective in the form of troops, it is
evident that every increase in strength results in the more rapid fulfillment of
goals, the speed of fulfillment being proportional to the force that was added.
If we observe a working collective, we can see that the augmentation of
strength proportionally accelerates the work being done.
However, social life manifests itself not only in manual labor.
Revitalization of social activity occurs, in esse, with every new discovery or
invention. These findings act as a kind of force, in the form of an impetus for
social activity, in the sense that they uplift the mood of, and arouse interest
within, the social body.
On the basis of what has been said, it becomes clear that if there exists a
general trend in society towards a certain profession, then there must be a
stimulus that drives people to choose that profession. We can say with
certainty that the attraction to that profession would be directly proportional
to the size of its stimulus.
If immigration occurs, then it is also controlled by the stimulus that
encourages people to come to a given country, and the more enticing the
stimulus, the greater the number of immigrants. Conversely, emigration from
a given country is stimulated by unfavorable conditions in that area, and this
emigration again increases as conditions worsen in relation to those in other
countries.
If a collective, while spreading and growing, funnels its actions in a certain
direction, then we can say without a doubt that the interest drawing it in that
direction does so with a rapidity that increases in proportion to the increase in
interest.
The advances of science are governed by the same law. Any discovery,
like a revitalizing force, turns minds in a certain direction; the greater the
discovery, the more intensive the work related to it. Something new in the
arts will attract artists, and cause them to work in a style that imitates the
innovation.
Finally, we can make the same observations in the economic/fiscal world.
Every industry develops in the direction of, and with the strength needed to
meet, the demand for its product. This is also true for trade, which is always
conducted in connection with the demand on communication routes: the
lesser the latter, the greater the growth of trade.
It should be added that trade is also stimulated by manufacture and by the
amount of money in a country, which complicates the usual simple
relationship. However, this does not affect the crux of the matter, since in this
case we are dealing with conditions of growth of capital and competition,
which are also subject to the law of proportional correlation between the
speed of motion and motive power.
When a relatively intense conflict between parties is underway and the
balance of their strengths seems insufficiently steady, it becomes very risky
for one of the parties to make a sharp deviation in its own favor without first
deciding whether or not it is strong enough to stand up to opposition;
otherwise, the matter can come to provocative events and to the stimulation
of a defensive reaction from the opposing party, and the situation may have
unfortunate consequences for the instigating party. In fact, this is exactly
what happened in Russia with the decision to dissolve the fourth State Duma,
after which revolutionary movements openly developed both in the Duma
itself and in the nation. A similar progression of events led to the second
March revolution in Germany (1920) and to the seizure of power by the
military party with Lustwitz at its head.
In general, during periods of imbalanced social forces, every careless step
on the part of either side of the conflict can function as a challenge,
provocation, or excitant for a widespread national movement. History gives
many, many examples of this. However, closest to ourselves would be the
events of January 9, 1905, which gave a substantial boost to the revolutionary
movement in Russia and greatly precipitated the first Russian revolution,
which had already been instigated by Alexander the Third's regime and by
the unfortunate Russo-Japanese war. Another example is the Lena executions
of 1912. These events, which took place far off in the taiga of Eastern
Siberia, should seemingly have had no effect on the masses of inner Russia.
However, they served as the first of several blows that led to the birth of the
proletariat in Russia. Finally, the agonizing death of the workers of Lena was
reflected in a renewal of the revolutionary movement.
A wave of strikes swept across Russia, workers began to listen more
attentively to revolutionary slogans and to unite in the name of a fight for
their rights. Thanks to this, by the time war commenced in 1914, the workers'
movement in St. Petersburg had reached the level of a barricade rebellion,
and it was only the sudden war that, for a time, suspended the revolutionary
movement. However, it arose with renewed strength two and a half years
later in connection with our failures at the front and the stubbornness of the
tsar, who turned everyone against him except his most deferent bootlickers as
a result of the Rasputin scandal.
But the bourgeoisie, in siding with the revolution, did not take into account
the fact that it had always served as a support for capitalism. The no longer
restrainable worker's movement naturally had to take its course, and almost
all of the bourgeoisie died in the fire of the revolution, while the intelligentsia
affiliated with it was subjected to all kinds of persecution after its pointless
decision to sabotage bolshevism.
Temporary rest or suspension of action is another revitalizing condition of
social movements.
No social movement can maintain high levels of energy without a break. It
generally quiets down of its own accord, due to internal inhibiting conditions
that are manifested in the growing fatigue of the followers of the movement.
However, rest or a temporary break in the movement give it new life and new
impetus to proceed.
Artificial revitalizing conditions encompass all those that are created by the
collective itself. Stimulating conditions, such as praise, rewards, and other
encouraging measures taken by social and class establishments, religious
communities, etc., fall under this category. On the other hand, repressions
such as punishment, isolation, arrest, etc., are artificial inhibitors set up by the
collective to deal with people who disrupt the normal living conditions and
activity of the collective.
When a social movement is well established, usually only a very small,
ordinary spark is required to vitalize it, while during the initial stages of its
development achieving the same vitality would have demanded great effort.
This is why, when the conditions are right, even the most insignificant
impetus can create turmoil in a given movement.
As we already stated, the contemporary Russian Revolution started only
with the edict on the dissolution of the State Duma, though the conditions for
a revolution had long been favorable.
The Swiss revolution—which, likewise, had been brewing for a long time
—began with the ridicule of a prominent Swiss citizen. In every social
gathering or crowd, when the prevailing mood reaches its peak, it suffices to
shout one slogan, to drop one incautious word, and the crowd or gathering
becomes active.
An example of this occurrence can be found in the depiction by L. Tolstoy,
in War and Peace, of the lynching of a petty criminal on Count Rastopchin's
porch. Naturally, the transition from inaction to action comes more easily to a
crowd than to a gathering, since a crowd acts much more impulsively, more
automatically, than any organized meeting.
Finally, it is important to keep in mind that the revitalization of a social
movement can also occur in the form of a reaction to another, opposing,
social movement or state measure.
In this way, the persecution of Christians in the first centuries of that
religion's existence did not weaken the Christian doctrine in any way; to the
contrary, because it gave Christians the appearance of martyrs, this ill-
treatment supported their cause.
The same is true for the persecution of religious renegades (for example,
the raskol'niki in Russia) and the harassment of revolutionaries. In general,
we can say that every new social movement that wants to change the existing
way of life, and yet does not represent the majority, must in the course of its
establishment make sacrifices and be mistreated.
The battle with cannibalism cost many human lives during the European
expeditions to and conquering of distant countries. The fight to provide
international aid to those maimed by war was stimulated by the suffering of
many thousands of injured people. The struggle for political freedom claimed
a great many lives of so-called political prisoners. The fight for freedom of
one nation or another was expiated by that nation's protests, which were often
put down by force.
The battle for equality of nations was accompanied by an array of wars
fought against imperialism, which brought innumerable deaths. We need only
remember the Napoleonic wars, the Sebastopol war, and the Great European
War to see the costs of fighting for state equality.
The effectuation of permanent peace and the disarmament of nations also
requires sacrifices, and the struggle of the Internationale must bring with it
many losses before this admirable and, shamefully, as yet unrealized goal can
be attained.
A huge revitalizing force in the progression of social movements is rivalry,
a result of the competition between two, three, or more social movements that
are more or less similar in character and goals. Here competition serves as an
impulse for the collective to exert its internal forces, adding to the strength of
the social movement. If it then demonstrates viability, the movement will be
revitalized through competition and will consequently receive a mighty
stimulus to its development. So many important social movements owe their
existence to competition, and so many have developed thanks to it! It would
be difficult to enumerate all the examples that support this observation, since
they are visible to everyone. However, we must not forget that competition is
only a revitalizing factor when it is not overwhelming, since in the latter case
competition has the totally opposite effect, slowing development in
movements not able to stand up to rivalry. Among other things, stimulating
or encouraging conditions that aid the distribution of an innovation
throughout the collective include, on the one hand, all subsidiary discoveries
which are congruous with the innovation, and, on the other, the improvement
of living conditions in the collective.
It is important to mention that the frequent revival of any reaction is
irrevocably connected to its acceleration, as is the case in mechanics. The
same can be observed in social movements. In any collective, social
movements that are frequently repeated and that become habitual always
develop more quickly than those that did not have an equivalent in the past.
The leaders of revolutions know very well that people must be made
accustomed to revolutionary demonstrations if an organized insurrection is to
take place. Any military leader knows that seasoned troops—that is, those
who have had experience under fire—are stronger in battle than those who
are new and unaccustomed to fighting.
If factors furthering a certain phenomenon are repeated one after another,
then they seemingly build on each other and, all together, act with greater
force in a certain direction. It would be easiest to observe this in relation to a
social phenomenon. For example, let us look at an incident of great social
importance, such as the entrance of the United States of America into the
Great European War despite the fact that the interests of America, it would
seem, were not affected by that conflict. America, which not long before the
war had been promoting the idea of international arbitration, could not have
entered the conflict without the influence of social and national movement.
Let us try and determine how, in the end, this seemingly improbable event
occurred.
We must first of all say that, at the dawn of the war, the international
position of the United States boiled down to an adherence to the following
principles: (1) the Monroe Doctrine, which guided the American government
to take upon itself the protection of the New World from attacks by European
nations and at the same time to distance itself from European transactions; (2)
the defense of the concept of freedom of the seas; and (3) an overwhelming
preoccupation with international arbitration, which would provide the
opportunity of preventing the recurrence of war.
The eruption of the European war in 1914 came unexpectedly for America
because the latter could detect no visible signs of the pending events. Also,
every warring European country had many expatriates in America, and their
respective arguments confused the scenario and made it difficult to decide on
whose side the truth lay. From the very beginning of the war, America
declared neutrality, which was accepted as natural and necessary. Then,
because of the diversity of opinion in the States, the president was forced to
make a speech in which he cautioned that the differences in views held by
Americans could inhibit the fulfillment of the responsibilities of that great,
peace-loving nation, which consisted of giving advice to, pacifying, and
negotiating between nations "not as a follower of one warring faction or
another, but as a friend." This is how the president expressed himself on the
twentieth of April, 1915:
We are the negotiators for all nations of the world; since our nation was
made from the mixing of all nations, we are, naturally, the intermediary
between their blood, their traditions, their interests, tastes, and desires. We
are able to understand them all as a whole, not each individually, for we
are not on the side of one nation or another but rather understand, bring
together, and represent all nations. In this sense, I name the American
people the negotiators.
American neutrality, then, had every reason to be complete and infallible—
and it was, in the beginning. As the war wore on, and as the atrocious
behavior of the Germans after their invasion of Belgium became known,
American public opinion turned against Germany. The commission of the
Belgian government, which arrived in America in September of 1914 with
complaints about the conduct of the Germans, did not achieve all the results it
intended; however, the president, in his response, in order to prevent the
necessity of the United States entering the European conflict, retained the
right to state his decision on the subject should the need arise. This was, of
course, a purely diplomatic move, an attempt to get out of a difficult
situation; the sentiments of most Americans were already on the Belgian side,
and they sent money, food, and clothing to that country. This aside, the
necessity of sustaining American neutrality was nationally supported, and the
political actions of the president were judged unanimously as being proper.
Things began to change only when it became apparent that America was
seriously threatened by the war at sea. In August of 1914, the American
Minister of Foreign Affairs sent a memorandum to each of the warring
countries which referred to the serious danger facing neutral countries as a
result of their uncertainty in the guarantee of their rights at sea. England
agreed to follow, with some exceptions, the stipulations of the London
Conference, whose declaration it later rejected. The diplomatic controversy
which arose over the subject between America and England revealed that the
latter was not inclined to support the American view that the oceans were not
sole property of the Old World. England's stance came down to the fact that it
would not agree to the reduction of its naval fleet until its neighbors agreed to
the reduction of their armies.
Further into the conflict, America's primary goal became bringing about
the end of the war as quickly as possible. On 27 May 1916, the president
spoke to the League of Peace and outlined the principles on whose basis
peace would be established. At this point, it became obvious that America
was not disinterested in the conditions of the future peace; this is revealed
especially in the following section of the president's speech:
We so honestly believe in the necessity of the triumph of these ideas, that
I am certain I express the thoughts and wishes of the people of America
when I declare the willingness of the United States to participate in every
possible alliance of nations, to attain these ideals, and to safeguard nations
against any abuse.
It goes without saying that this speech was greeted with enthusiastic
approval by the American people, who had decided, under the influence of
certain events which demonstrated to them that without participating in the
war on land it would be impossible to protect America's rights at sea, that it
would be best to fight for these rights. However, the point of America's sword
was not directed at Great Britain (as could be expected in lieu of the above-
mentioned diplomatic polemic), but at Germany, and this is why: there was
no agreement between the States and Germany about arbitration, which was
Germany's own fault; it was very vague on the matter, as it was in the
rejection of the offers of the Hague conference. Moreover, a conflict with
Germany had already occurred when that country had protested that America,
in the role of a neutral nation, should not have been providing weapons to
warring countries, although Germany itself had given arms to countries in the
Balkans not long before. The righteousness of America's conduct in this case
could not be questioned, not only because countries in question, not being
prepared for war, would find themselves in an unfavorable position, but also
because America, in the event of war, would not be without support. Of
course, in the end, this state of affairs would allow the nation with the
greatest number of arms-manufacturing plants to rule the globe.
The establishment by Germany of a self-declared zone within the
boundaries of which it could sink any commercial ships had also served as a
provocation to the Americans. When the Lusitania was sunk, causing a large
number of deaths, the indignation of the American people knew no bounds,
and yet this terrible incident also blew over at no cost to Germany. However,
further incidents, including derisive and negligent treatment by the German
government; conspiracies and improper relations with German diplomatic
agents within America; Germany's intrigues in Japan and South Africa, which
were meant to turn those countries against the States; and even at tempts to
instigate revolt in Cuba, Haiti, and San Domingo, had distinctly provocative
characteristics and greatly tested the patience of the United States.
At the ceremony of presentations in appreciation for the maneuvers of the
Austro-Hungarian and German troops and navy, there were neither apologies
from the German government, nor punishments for the participants; indeed,
the commander of the submarine that sank the Lusitania received, from that
crowned degenerate Wilhelm, the highly prestigious medal Pour le Mérite.
Meanwhile, the actions of submarines against American citizens further
aggravated the situation, and on the 2 February 1916, the president of the
United States stated, in a speech given at Topeka, that if the rights of
American citizens were contravened it would become necessary to revert to
forceful action.1 Negotiations that were taking place as a result of the sinking
of the ship Sussex partially appeased the United States, and war was
temporarily prevented.
Near the end of 1916, President Wilson planned to draw up a note in which
he would request all warring countries to outline the purpose of the war.
Germany discovered his intention, and, along with its confederates (Austro-
Hungary, Bulgaria, and Turkey) addressed itself to the Allies with a request
to begin peace negotiations. In reality, this was hardly a peaceful offer; it had
the tone of a conqueror asking a favor of the conquered. Moreover, neutral
states found themselves threatened by Germany, for they did not participate
in the peace process and so could not prevent Germany and its allies from
declaring themselves free from all responsibilities pertaining to the protection
of the interests of neutral countries. This meant that German submarines
would have no scruples about sinking neutral ships. The distribution of
President Wilson's note addressing the purpose of the war clarified the
situation. Germany gave a rather indefinite reply, but the responses of the
Allied Nations were honest and to the point, and agreed with the stance taken
by the American government.
Then came Wilson's famous speech to the Senate, which was addressed to
both the American people and the citizens of the world, and in which he
outlined the conditions of the future peace accord, as approved by the United
States. The essence of the speech was as follows:
A pact cannot be stable unless it is in accordance with the principle which
states that governments are directed by the people and express their
desires, and that the transfer of people from one governor to another, in
the manner of a material possession, is equivalent to the deprivation of
civil rights.
What were the consequences of this address? The media in France and
Great Britain, as a reflection of public opinion, approved of the points that
had been brought up in Wilson's speech. In Germany, on the other hand, there
were either very few who actually supported Wilson, or else they did not
have the opportunity to express their views. Besides, on 16 January 1917, the
German Minister of Foreign Affairs sent an order to the German envoy in
Mexico, in which he wrote of Germany's refusal to be involved in any
discussions of the sinking of the Sussex, and offered New Mexico and
Arizona to the Mexican government if, along with Japan, it would attack the
United States.
It cannot be doubted that these fresh incidents turned the Americans more
and more against Germany, and set the stage for cooperation between
America and the Allies. Indignation naturally grew in the States over
Germany's naval piracy and cruel conduct in occupied territory. Things could
perhaps have still been resolved without war, but it so happened that in
January 1917, Count Bernstorf sent the American Minister of Foreign Affairs
a memo with the declaration that the German government planned to increase
submarine action, contrary to the wishes of America. German/American
diplomatic relations were subsequently suspended. Even then, the president
was reluctant to start a war, and told the Senate: "We are true friends of the
German people, and wish to remain on peaceful terms with the government
that speaks in their name." This statement, however, had no effect; America's
demands were not met. In the beginning of March, it became evident that the
German government planned to put its threat into practice, and so the
president asked the Congress to approve America's becoming an armed
neutrality. Five hundred of the 531 members of both houses backed this
measure, and on the twelfth of March there followed an order to arm
American trade ships. But Germany did not relax its piratic politics in
relation to the trade ships of all nations. As a result, on 2 April 1917, Wilson
spoke to the Congress once more with the proposal to declare war with
Germany, and on the sixth of the same month the lower chamber voted in
favor of a decision which had earlier been accepted by the Senate:
Taking into account the fact that the German imperial government has
made many openly hostile movements towards the government and people
of the United States of America, the Senate and the House of
Representatives of the United States of America have decided, upon
meeting in a general sitting of the Congress, to formally declare war—
which Germany has thrust upon America—between the German
government and that of the United States; this gives the president full
authority to use the naval and armed forces of the United States, as well as
the other resources available to the government, in waging war against the
German imperial government. The Congress of the United States
guarantees, by this very act, that the collective strength of the country will
be directed towards the victorious resolution of this conflict.2
It is obvious that, in order for the outlook of the peace-loving American
nation to change, and for the relations between it and autocratic Germany to
be determined by a collective militant reflex on the side of the American
people, there must have been a systematic reinforcement of the mood in
America through constant aggravation on the part of Germany.
Thus, this example clearly proves how a social movement is sustained and
developed in a certain direction under the influence of the incentives and
stimuli which support it, and eventually cause the destruction of all
restraining factors; the law of inertia is conquered, and a peace-loving nation,
excited by military feeling, sends its sons to a bloody battle fought on the
distant European continent.
Notes
1 See How Did America Become Involved in the War?, June 1918. (Brochure in Russian.)
2 How Did America Become Involved in the War?, July [sic] 1918. (Brochure in Russian.)
3
The Law of Gravitational Attraction
In my work, On the Basic Laws of the World,1 I prove that laws such as that
of gravitational attraction are not only valid for the mechanics of heavenly
bodies and inanimate nature, but also for the animal and plant kingdoms and,
likewise, for the social world.
In biotic nature, we meet with the law of gravitational attraction in
phenomena such as positive taxis and positive tropism. These phenomena are
so well known in biology that there is no need to go into detail about them
here. However, it is important to understand gravity in the biological sense as
more than simply mechanical gravitational pull, since any cooperation or
symbiosis is a special kind of biological gravitational attraction.
Symbiosis is also quite well known, and needs no detailed explanation.
Some facts must nonetheless be touched upon. Careful, in-depth research has
proved that the transformation of nitrogen into nitrates is aided by root
nodule bacteria. There are also many investigations into the mutually
beneficial relationships between various species of mushrooms and woody
forest plants. Thus, mycelia, weaving themselves around the roots of woody
plants, protect them from dehydration, cold temperatures, and parasites.
Moreover, they help the root system in that they extend it further and increase
the rate of its absorption of nutrients. In growing together with the thin,
secondary roots and in entering their tissue, the threads of the mycelia play
the role of root hairs which absorb water and associated minerals into the root
system.
Bernard proved another important fact in this area: that the mycelia of
mushrooms which are cohabiting with a plant are essential to the latter for the
germination of its seeds. These do not germinate, even in the most favorable
conditions, in the absence of the mycelia whose fungi are subsequently
parasitical to the plant. Especially good examples of this can be found when
observing orchidae. Similar phenomena are known of the lycopodia, which
reproduce via spores (Traube). Aitkinson's, Metenius', and Grevilius'
observations lead to the same conclusions about blackthorns, sometimes
classified as ferns.
All mushrooms are essentially parasitical plants. They live and breed on
the body of one plant or another because they can only survive with its help.
From this naturally stems the mutual attraction of the two plants. They are
connected by their living conditions in the same manner as two stars or two
planets are connected in the solar system.
Not less known are the symbiotic relationships in the animal kingdom. But
here there are different conditions defining the mutual gravitational pull, or
attraction, between organisms. These are the cooperation and mutual help that
animals give each other. Interesting details on the subject can be found in
Kropotkin's work, Mutual Aid as a Factor in Evolution. It would be
impossible to imagine the animal world without cooperation or mutual aid,
for this is what determines the mutual attraction of one individual to another.
In this way, the law of gravitational attraction is also applicable to the
social world.
The instinct of self-preservation leads to the development of socialization,
bringing individuals closer not only mechanically, but also binding them in a
collective, in which the mutual attraction between individuals is expressed.
Some, such as Waksweiler, call this attraction "social affinity" (l'affinité
sociale). However, no matter how we interpret that "social affinity," relations
between people, which are inevitably formed by nature during child-rearing,
lead to the development of an attraction to those similar to oneself and to a
habit of reacting to their influence in a particular manner. And since the
influence of those similar to ourselves is most often expressed in mutual
need, then it is clear why, in any place where there are mutual interests, social
units are formed very easily. Moreover, unity is brought on by a need for
protection from the unfavorable influence of an aggregate of forces:
Travelers under threat of attack draw closer, band together, and fend off
the attackers with combined forces; in the case of danger, many formerly
unacquainted people come together to form a single group. The joining of
people in permanent offensive and defensive unions, starting with the
agreements of two or three individuals, and ending with the conjunction of
cities, regions, and kingdoms, is a process that is caused by certain
biological conditions. These not only drove people together, but also
created a certain order and organization in human relations.2
In general, individuals who have similar internal and external
characteristics are mutually attracted; on the other hand, it can be said that
dissimilar people, who are, nonetheless, mutually complementary, attract.
(This category encompasses the mutual attraction of opposites.) In the above
cases, it seems that the useful outcome of a union is somehow instinctively
foreseen—in the first case from the union of identical forces, and in the
second from the combination of mutually complementary forces.
Thus, the collective truly owes its conception to the law of gravitational
attraction, with the unification of the collective occurring, in one case, thanks
to the similarity in character, opinion, or goals of its component individuals,
and in the other thanks to the union of unlike, but mutually complementary,
personalities. However, no matter what the main conditions influencing the
education of a collective are, it cannot be doubted that the basis of the above-
mentioned phenomena is the mutual attraction (pull) of individuals to one
another.
The application of the law of gravitational attraction to the collective
personality is characterized by the fact that in social life we encounter certain
gravitational centers, which can be large or small. The latter also experience
an attraction to each other, with the strength of pull depending on the size of
the centers, as can be observed in the gravitational attraction of heavenly
bodies. However, here the size of the center is less important than its social
significance. Obviously, the attraction between these centers is dependent on
the distance and ease of communication between them; here, also, is
applicable the formula which states that the gravitational pull between two
bodies is inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them.
What, then, defines the strength of attraction of certain centers in the social
world? There can be no single answer, since even the attraction itself can be
determined by such diverse factors as the geographical, economic, political,
or scientific/literary conditions in a given area.
Obviously, the geographical conditions influencing gravitational attraction
are ones such as the convenience of transport systems leading to a given
center, the proximity of a center to areas of greater development in extractive
industry, etc. Economic conditions of attraction include the location of a
place in relation to trade routes and its nearness to areas encompassing
manufacturing industries. Political conditions would encompass the location
of internal government and of political leaders' living quarters. Finally,
scientific and literary attractions are comprised of conglomeration of
scientific or literary heritage, as well as scientific discoveries and inventions.
Indeed, every discovery or invention immediately attracts individuals,
including those who wish to learn about it or discuss it, and the more
important the invention, the greater its power of attraction. If a scientific
discovery is of an experimental nature, it attracts people with the same
interests who wish to look over and make observations on the discovery,
listen to lectures, etc. If the discovery is technical, plans for its exploitation
are made immediately, and people interested in this new concept gather to
work on it. If a new theory is devised and taught in one of the higher
institutions of learning, individuals flock to that school. If there is a new trade
venture taking place, which is in itself a form of discovery, it similarly
amasses a number of participants and supporters. Furthermore, any factory or
plant attracts a range of people who function either as organizers or as
workers. Any school, as a discovery of an imitational nature, becomes an
attractive force for young people. Because of this, a large city, which contains
many of the above factors, acts as a strongly gravitationally attractive center.
Thanks to these conditions, centers of gravitational attraction are those
places on the globe which collect the most institutions that cultivate
discoveries and inventions in various areas of human activity, from the
economic to the scientific and artistic. It is due to this law of gravitational
attraction that world centers of civilization are created.
Depending on the creative activity of a nation, its living conditions, and its
cultural level, various centers of civilization have been created through the
ages. Thus, at one time, the centers of civilization were at Babylon and
Nineveh, at another time at Alexandria, later in Athens, and finally Rome.
In the middle ages, many cities—but primarily Paris—could be considered
such centers. However, every country has its own centers of civilization,
which attract its population.
Notes
1 See issues in the Study and Education of the Personality (in Russian), issues 2 and 3,1920-21.
2 P. Sorokin, System of Sociology (in Russian), Part I, p. 269.
4
The Law of Repulsion
As well as being influenced by the law of gravitational attraction and
cooperation, social life is subject to the law of repulsion and confrontation.
Every society, from the moment of its conception, begins to differentiate or
break up into various parts; one form of differentiation fosters the division of
labor and therefore promotes cooperation and rapprochement, while the other
form is characterized by the diverse ambitions of individuals in a society. The
consequences of the last type of differentiation include mutual repulsion and
dissociation, and later rivalry, competition, and confrontation. This is why no
society can evade rivalry, competition, or confrontation; however, even these
conditions can eventually lead to its success and improvement. In a
developed, mature society the very structure of social life brings about
confrontations between parties, in which each one strives to hold the
dominant position.
Thus, every social enterprise breeds many individuals who aspire to be at
its head and, to that end, endeavor to display their most favorable
characteristics. This, in turn, spurs other candidates into action.
The same can be observed in all areas of manufacturing work, where we
often meet with repulsion and dissociation, leading to rivalry and
competition. This is what makes every company try to improve the quality of
manufacture, or at least increase the distribution of its product, to attract the
attention of consumers. Furthermore, rivalry and competition in the work
force of industrialized nations bring about not only the intensification of
manufacture, but also the development of a proletariat—thanks to the
increased number of would-be workers—and then the devaluing and
thoughtless exploitation of these laborers.
An identical process of mutual dissociation and repulsion, followed by
rivalry and competition, can be found in any line of work, including the
pedagogical and scientific, since the followers of these disciplines strive to
achieve a higher standing and better results than the others of their
profession. Working alongside discoveries and inventions, the rivalry and
competition have—in accordance with the law of repulsion—decentralizing
influence on the collective personality. This influence is dependent on the
necessity of propagandizing and popularizing every discovery and invention.
Propaganda diverts some of the populace from the larger centers, spreading it
throughout the surrounding areas. This is accompanied by the dissemination
of the given invention in the interests of making it accessible to rural sections
of the population, which allows for the creation of smaller, secondary centers.
The location of these centers depends on certain regional conditions, patterns
of communication routes, etc.
In this way, along with the law of gravitational attraction, inventions and
discoveries themselves exert an attractive or repellent force, and consequently
every composite personality attains a certain equilibrium between both
forces. This, in turn, determines a population's distribution over the earth's
surface and its grouping in one area or another:
All the aspects of society that we now consider to be accepted, proven,
habitual, were once the subject of heated debate. There is not one peaceful
organization which did not originate from discord. Grammar, poetry,
predominant industry, the legal code the constitution, the catechism, all
this, which makes up the basic foundation of society, was slowly
developed through societal dialectics. Every grammar rule is in essence
nothing more than the expression of a habit of speech that was able to
triumph over all others. Every article of the code is an agreement, a
peaceful treaty following bloody civil conflict, aggressive polemic in the
press, and oratorial tempests in parliament. Every principle embodied in
the constitution triumphed only after many revolutions, etc. (Tarde, The
Laws of Imitation, pp. 173-174)
Dissociation and rivalry, based on the very narrow political interests of
individuals, can be traced through all of human history. Confrontations
between families and tribes; battles fought in the initial stages of social
development by the younger members of society to overturn domination by
the father, or head, of the family; struggles of the individual against caste or
class rule; constant confrontations over economics; the struggle of the
individual against assimilation into mass society; battles for liberating the
individual from slavery, then from economic and religious servitude;
conflicts between parties in every caste, class, and organized society; internal
strife in families, artels, communes, co-operatives, parties, parishes, indeed in
any social group—these are many, but far from all, of the types of mutual
repulsion, rivalry, and confrontation that inevitably develop with the mighty
strength of an immutable law in the conditions of social life. Yet at the same
time, the law of repulsion and dissociation, acting as a stimulus of
competition and confrontation, serves as an important factor in the
development and improvement of the human personality.
Thus, although imitation and borrowing, which determine the degree of
similarity, that is, the basic condition of mutual attraction and conjunction
between peoples, encourage nations to unite, this alliance is hindered by
dissociation, competition, national prejudice, historical traditions, national,
class, or party pride, etc., all of which correspond to the force of repulsion in
mechanics. The basic reason for this repulsion and dissociation is the natural
and social inequality between people, of which the latter can be eliminated by
the spread of culture.
In the words of C. Letourneau,
man can be called a hierarchical animal. With the exception, perhaps, of
the utterly primitive hordes of Tierra del Fuego, inequality exists in all
human societies. Everywhere, the weak are dominated by the strong;
however, in societies with a complicated structure and organization that
depend on the inherited transferal of privileges, the power of the strong is
replaced by the power of the rich. In this manner, the function of the fight
for survival, as it exists in the rest of the organic world, is distorted, and
often the weaker but more privileged individual conquers a stronger and
more intelligent adversary. This situation is especially evident in societies
with strict divisions into castes, but our contemporary states are, to a
considerable extent, still rife with similar instances, even the more
democratic ones. (C. Letourneau, Morality, p. 347)
From the above, it can be seen that, in the case of humans, intellect—or the
higher forms of correlative activity, which lead to wealth as a result of
innovation—is the best weapon to employ in the battle for survival, and has
replaced strong teeth, sharp claws, and healthy muscles. However, the
evolution of intellect, a result not of individual, but of social life, since only
interaction and the exchange of personal experience encourages the
development specifically of intellect, does not exclude the possibility of
physical conflict. This is proved by the countless wars humanity has waged
throughout its entire history, a practice which it, sadly, cannot seem to
abandon. To humanity's shame, it has not yet produced the most important
innovation, one which would forever terminate wars between nations and
conflicts between individuals.
Competition and rivalry occur not only between individuals, but also
between various institutions. Every government institution competes with and
rivals other, analogous government institutions. Every industrial or trade
firm, every undertaking, every factory is the competitor or rival of similar
establishments within a given country. Likewise, institutions involved in
intellectual work compete with each other and strive to prove themselves
better than their rivals.
Different schools, such as academies, universities, institutes, etc., compete
with and rival each other in the same way as do all scientific and pedagogical
institutions, not to mention the scientists and academics themselves.
In a word, no matter which branch of work we look at, we inevitably meet
with competition and rivalry between institutions and their representatives.
However, in this competition or confrontation, in this seemingly antisocial
act, lies the guarantee of social improvement.
The fact of the matter is that, thanks to this confrontation, there occurs a
social selection that protects and provides for the development of socially
valuable institutions, and precipitates the extinction of institutions that do not
answer to social needs. This social selection, in contrast to Darwin's natural
selection, does not depend on any mutation within organizations and is not
based on adaptation to the natural conditions of existence, but rather has as its
object one principle: social usefulness, which determines the fate of any
social institution. This institution is preserved for posterity not as a result of
the biological inheritance that is so important in Darwin's natural selection,
but as a result of social inheritance, which I elaborate on in another text.1
The above proves that the law of repulsion, brought about by competition,
rivalry, and conflict, is one of the basic laws of social improvement, and if
without social rapprochement and cooperation there can be no realization of
social goals, then without competition, rivalry, and conflict, which are the
basis of the law of repulsion, there can be no improvement in the social
world.
Notes
1 V. Bekhterev, "Social selection and its biological significance" (in Russian), Vestnik Znaniia, Nord u.
Sud, 1912. V. Bekhterev, "The significance of hormonism in the evolution of organisms and of social
selection" (in Russian), Priroda, 1916.
5
The Law of Equal and Opposite
Reaction
The subject here is a law in agreement with which social movements, led by
various social groups, interact in such a way that the growth of one social
movement stimulates opposition from other, contrary movements. Thus, the
vigorous growth of nationalism instigates the growth of socialism, and
increasingly powerful authority is accompanied by the strengthening of
opposition. With the development of anarchy comes a desire in other groups
to uphold the governing power and establish unity.
In all situations of this kind, opposition gains momentum at the same rate
as the given social movement grows.
Sociopolitical life is defined by the aspirations of various parties or circles
and usually follows a zigzag path, leaning first towards one party, then
towards another, depending on the party's influence. There have been
moments in history when inter-party conflict has led to a social crisis.
Every new social movement meets with some form of opposition from
certain social groups. But when one movement gains the upper hand, the
combined forces of other parties begin to act against it. Thus, despotic rule is
the lead-up to revolution, and a revolution, in turn, brings about a
counterrevolution.
Any discovery or invention in general, once made, strives to be
disseminated in its immediate environment; then it gradually spreads further
and further, meeting with inhibition in the form of the conservatism of
society and opposition from the established inventions that it encounters. Let
us say that the invention in question is the electric light. It is known that its
distribution was, and still is, inhibited by the conservative public, and also by
opposition from representatives and supporters of gas- and Auer-lighting. In
the end, the analysis of any analogous case will show that reaction is equal to
action, and opposite to it in direction.
If there is pressure exerted by the government on certain cross sections of
society, there is also opposition from the social circles involved in the
conflict, which grows in proportion to the extent of the reaction displayed by
the governing power, and as a result the inertia of the masses plus the action
of the opposition is balanced by the reaction of the government. Over and
above the inertia of the masses, every revolution meets with resistance from
counterrevolutionary, less radical elements; as a result, reaction must be equal
to action and opposite to it in direction.
In conflict between parties, the power or dominance of one party is resisted
by others, and the first will continue to expand its influence until the
opposition of the other parties becomes equal to its power. Similarly, in war
the action of one side will outweigh the reaction of opposing forces until this
reaction becomes equal to the action.
Socialist tendencies arise in society as a reaction against the bourgeois;
internationalism and pacifism develop as a reaction against war.
If, with the passage of time, patriotism is weakened in some sections of the
population under the influence of internationalist views, then it is inevitably
strengthened in other sections. Thus, when Russian soldiers refused to fight
under the influence of Bolshevist internationalist propaganda, death
battalions, officers' battalions of honor, and women's battalions arose as a
reaction against Bolshevism.
In general, with any reinforcement of a social movement, another opposing
movement develops as a counterweight. In this way, every time the
equilibrium between separate sectors is disturbed, a movement is established
in society which attempts to correct the disturbance through reaction.
Conflict between parties is also governed by this law, and whenever one
party gains the upper hand, other parties rise up against it and, if necessary,
form a bloc. On the same basis, the governments of constitutional countries
must accede to the views of the parliamentary majority, which is formed
either of one governing party or of a bloc of several parties, which are, in
turn, in conflict with the opposition.
Here, all efforts of the opposition are directed at resisting the majority and
gaining compensation in certain areas.
But there are times in history when the state as a whole is threatened, as for
example during war; in such instances, all parties seem to merge in order to
avert the given danger, and even the parties that would otherwise make up the
opposition support the government.
In other words, in order to ward off impending danger, all powers in the
state merge into one; there is no disagreement either between parties or
between representatives of the people and even governments as to the
military mobilization of the country. Moreover, the governing spheres usually
reorganize themselves into a so-called ministerial coalition to attain even
greater combined power whereby to oppose the external danger.
In accordance with the given law, when a single group has insufficient
strength, coalitions and blocs are formed for its aid. The same law is
applicable to large collectives such as nations and states. When France
became powerful and militant in Napoleon's time, coalitions of other
countries rose against it. When Russia gained strength in the first half of the
nineteenth century, English, French, Sardinian, and Turkish coalitions
opposed it.
When Germany emerged as the next great power, Russia and France united
to counterbalance its alliance with Austria and Italy; when the Great War
began, Russia, France, England, Italy, Japan, and, later, America consolidated
to oppose Germany's four-way alliance with Austria, Bulgaria, and Turkey.
All this conforms to the law of action and equal reaction.
As another example of the embodiment of this law, let us take the
contemporary Russian social movement known as subbotnichestvo, which
lasted from 1919 to 1920. The following was written by myself for the
magazine 
Zhelezno-Dorozhnaiia 
Tekhnika 
i 
Ekonomika 
(Railway
Technology and Economics), when the given movement was in the early
stages of its development:
Subbotniki, or subbotnichestvo, is a social movement that cannot be
passed over in silence. Indeed, is it not surprising that, at a time of such
instability in Russia, when everyone is subject to already habitual
undernourishment and weakness, and complains of the shortage of bread,
resources, and all other necessities, masses of people go out on Saturdays
—ostensibly a time of rest after six days of work—to perform hard labour,
doing so not for their own benefit, as would be expected, but for the
benefit of others and of society. Until now, such occurrences, if they
existed, were only singular happenings, brought about by extraordinary
circumstances such as sudden catastrophes, which necessitate the input of
combined social powers. This is that so-called community aid, which has
survived from ancient times in "communal" Russia. But here we are
dealing not with a single phenomenon, brought on by the sudden advent of
some unforeseen incident, but with a phenomenon that has taken on a
systematic form and spread throughout Soviet Russia. What, then, could
be its cause? Undoubtedly, the movement is a reaction by the healthy
sections of society to the destruction and lack of peaceful constructive
labour that we are experiencing.
Subbotnichestvo first arose in the environment which felt the greatest need
to demonstrate, by example, how work should be conducted in the "new"
Russia; but it is spreading throughout society independently of any party
or view. The centuries-long slavery and ignorance of the Russian people,
as determined by the previous regime, is the reason why, when the
labourers received freedom and even a position of some power, a totally
unforeseen event occurred. The fact of the matter is that, upon gaining
liberty and rights, those workers who had previously executed unbearably
hard forced labour who had so to speak toiled beneath a yoke, began to
free themselves from work altogether. This added a more serious type of
ruin to the general disintegration that was inevitable after a complete
overthrow of traditional social life: a lack of discipline, or "slovenliness,"
on the one hand, and an evasion of work, or "mischievousness," on the
other. This destruction of the labour force, the only guarantee of the
progress and creativity of the masses thanks to its ability to overcome any
obstacle, was the most dangerous phenomenon of our society at the time,
caused by past sins and present conditions. It was leading us to certain
death, a fact which there seemed to be no way to avoid; for where a
fountain of force and life should have sprung up to replace the old,
stagnant ways, there gaped an awful abyss, ready to swallow even the
greatest efforts and cloud over the sunniest hopes for a new social life.
However, social life is subject to certain laws or regularities, which are as
immutable as the movement of celestial bodies, though not well known or
understood. And so, while the widespread ruin in our country was
inevitable in the world of social phenomena, so was the reaction to this
ruin, in the form of that amazing and unique social movement,
subbotnichestvo. In the mechanics of inorganic nature there is, as we
know, a law which states that reaction is equal to action, and also opposite
to it in direction. That same law is applicable to the "social" mechanics of
mankind. Thus the aforementioned lack of discipline has been replaced by
the coordination of social forces, which should eventually lead to the
evolution of structured social forms. Presently, healthy and productive
collective work, subbotnichestvo, has replaced evasion of labour. The
most intriguing aspect of the situation is this: notwithstanding the
widespread evasion of forced or involuntary, and therefore difficult,
labour at the beginning of the revolution, we are now, during the
formation of a new social life, witness to the rise of freely chosen,
collective, selfless, almost heroic, and in any case honourable labour;
honourable not in the old, false sense of the word, but according to its true
meaning. This collective labour in the form of subbotnichestvo is being
disseminated throughout Soviet Russia, permeating all healthy elements of
society. This is nothing short of initiative, the "great initiative," in Lenin's
words, within the productive work of our new life. What makes this social
phenomenon amazing, albeit short-lived, is that, contrary to the
democratic principle that every labour must have its recompense, this
movement demands no payment. Here there is only selfless work,
dedication to the well-being of society. Subbotnichestvo is both a social
impulse and an example of the best way to serve society.
It is interesting to trace the development of the given "epidemic" of labor.
This is discussed in various newspaper reports, excerpts of which are
presented in an article written by an observer (Railway Technology and
Economics, August 1919):
It all began with communist railwaymen of the Moscow railway junction
on the Kazan' line. At first, a resolution was made which stipulated that
every Communist must work two extra hours without pay. By the tenth of
May, 182 people on that line were working six hours overtime, and with
incredible efficiency; they performed work in those few hours which
would otherwise have taken one week to complete. The productivity of
labour reached 270%, with some groups working even on Sundays.
The Kazan' example was followed by the Moscow-Alexandrovsk railway
line, where a resolution on subbotniks for Communists and sympathizers
was made in a general meeting of Communists and affiliated causes, and
already, on the 17 May, the first subbotnik raised the productivity of
labour to three times its original number. Next came the decision to create,
as of the 22 May, an additional four-hour working day for Communists;
then the movement in favour of subbotnichestvo spread to the countryside,
first in railway service shops in Saratov, then in Ruzaevo, later in railroad
service shops in Tula, still later in Sormovo, Voronezh (8 and 15 June)
and at the Maloyaroslavets station (13 and 15 June), etc., etc.
In this way, in the course of one month this epidemic of labour infected
many Moscow organizations and spread to many provincial cities, and has
presently become an almost universal phenomenon. In relation to this, we
cannot overlook the importance of uplift in mood, which not only greatly
increases the productivity of labour, but also stimulates people to continue
that labour on Sunday. It is obvious that in every type of work there is an
honourable impetus, and this case is no exception: the honourable impetus
has become infectious, attracting many people and uniting in the name of
labour all those who have not lost their will to live. Moreover, when
reading reports of the previous week's subbotnik, of the increased number
of those going out to work, of the participation not only of the Communist
initiators, but also of non-Party people, of the greater amount of labour
executed—one comes to the conclusion that the framework of a renewed
industrious life is being established, and one seems to distinguish the first
rays of the dawn of a promising future on the horizon.
This is how we see subbotnichestvo from the domestic aspect. But from the
point of view of social life, the phenomenon was, as we mentioned, a reaction
to destruction or ruin in the form of a possible counterbalance. Thus,
subbotnichestvo, combined with measures taken by the government to
prevent ruin, will develop until the period of decline has been overcome, at
which point reaction will finally equal action.
The law of equal and opposite reaction is also supported by incident of
collective inhibition, which are comparable to the effect of friction in
mechanics and physics.
When speaking of instances of inhibition in social movements, it is
necessary to keep in mind that they are as inevitable in social life as
revitalizing conditions; inhibitors act as regulators of social movement—
bringing it into line, so to speak. What would follow, for example, if a large
collective was left without government, that most important inhibitor within
any social collective? The answer is that absolute freedom, whose extreme is
anarchy and disorganization, which, in turn, inevitably leads back to
organization and limited freedom, would result. This is why mankind's
aspiration towards ideal and absolute freedom is a "Blue Bird," a dream
whose realization can never last longer than a brief moment, and can only
occur during periods of social turmoil; this was achieved, for example, under
Kerensky at the time of the Russian social revolution. As there is no
absolutely free "will" of separate individuals, so there cannot be absolutely
free "will" of collectives, since it is in its turn limited by pressure from
neighbors, organizations, and the conditions of life in general.
All so-called acts of abstention, endurance, and silence, in other words, all
forms of passive and active inertia, which are devoid of or else have
insignificant external influence, are part of inhibition in individual life. The
latter also encompasses restraint upon receiving insult; abstention from
participation in any given undertaking, spectacle, or from the fulfillment of
instincts; endurance upon confiscation of belongings; silence when provoked
through taunting or personal affront to join a conversation, etc., etc. In
collectives we encounter the same phenomena, though on a mass scale.
In the following, we will focus on the development of acts of inhibition in
the collective as a manifestation of the law of equal and opposite reaction.
In any society, we have, on the one hand, agreement, cooperation, and
assistance and, on the other, disagreement, opposition, and inhibition. The
first set of conditions is one which supports and revitalizes social progress,
while the second inhibits it or holds it back. Equilibrium between these two
factors causes social stagnation; domination by inhibiting conditions leads to
decline; and the predominance of helpful conditions leads to progress. And,
since at any given moment some forms, being more complete, receive
support, and less complete ones meet with opposition, progress is
characterized by the perfection of social forms and the accumulation of social
abilities.
The very development of social movements does not escape acts of
inhibition.
According to Tarde (The Laws of Imitation, p. 130),
Every belief, every need always comes to light with great difficulty, since
it must first conquer old habits and established beliefs; then, having
overcome these obstacles, it can only be disseminated until its unbridled
success breeds a new opponent, which blocks its path and introduces an
apparently insurmountable obstacle. New opponents of a given need
consist primarily of habits caused directly or indirectly by the need itself.
Thus, for a given belief, which is always in some measure false,
adversaries are opposing ideas that had originally been encompassed by it,
or that had been discovered separately with its assistance. Examples
include heresy, or teachings that arose from a certain dogma but are
opposite to it and strive to terminate its victorious procession across the
globe, as well as scientific theories or industrial applications that sprang
from previous theories, and that now disprove the latter or hinder their
progress.
The great amount of effort which Christianity expended in order to carve a
niche for itself in ancient countries is widely acknowledged. The new religion
took precedence over older ones very slowly and eventually reached its
zenith during the middle ages, after which its influence and importance in
Europe gradually declined, while being replaced by scientific and
philosophical ideas in the intellectual segments of the population.
As an example of an episode of conflict between religion and science, one
can point out that in Galileo's time the religious viewpoint collided with the
new scientific theory of the rotation of the earth; the latter met with great
opposition on the part of the former, until it was able to finally and
completely eliminate religious superstition.
The same can be seen in the development of revolutionary movements
under monarchies. At first, the revolutionary movement encounters strong
repulsion from the greater part of the population, but gradually accumulates
more and more followers, until it finally triumphs. In Russia, for example, it
is possible to observe a complete reversal of outlook and values in the span of
one or two generations. During the hanging of the murderers of Alexander II,
the people of Petersburg threw clods of dirt at the twisted, jerking bodies of
the condemned Perovskaia, Zheliabov, and others; at the time of the
revolution of 1917, the names Perovskaia and Zheliabov were practically
idolized, and the populace swarmed with red socialist flags onto the same
square on which they were hanged.
It can be said, as a rule, that every new social movement instigates an
opposing reaction, whose magnitude is proportional to the extent to which the
movement is more radically different from the traditional social structure. If
the new movement, notwithstanding the obstacles placed in its path, forces its
way forward, then—as with the collision of physical objects—the force of
opposition is equal to that of action; however, the opposing mass is usually
smaller than the acting one, and so must forfeit its position.
The existing social system is in itself a kind of internal inhibiting factor,
which is gradually overcome by a new movement, which acts as a social
reflex; and here, too, we observe once again the law of equal and opposite
reaction.
In this way, the spread of any innovation in a social environment meets
with opposition on the part of any other innovation that has a similar
propensity to distribution. The end result could be that two innovations are
mutually balanced and both receive equal—though limited—distribution, or
else that one of the innovations gains precedence over the others and is most
popularized.
Tarde represents the "collision" of innovations as their interference, and
compares their dissemination through imitation to radiation (rays). This
interference, however, does not always cause mutual resistance to
dissemination, but in certain cases leads to the merging of rays and so to their
amplification and acceleration. When the interaction is simultaneous, it leads
to conflict and mutual weakening or destruction; however, if the interaction is
sequential it results in rhythmical progression.
In science, one theory replaces another. Thus, for example, Copernicus'
system displaced that of Ptolemy, and the latter, being less successful than
the former, was inhibited by it. However, all scientific facts, whether they are
eventually found to be applicable to real life or not, are nonetheless kept and
collected, since they make up material for evaluation—a reference point, if
you will— and are not directly related to human activity. The same occurs in
other areas of human activity—in literature, in the arts, and in industry. One
trend replaces another. Romanticism is pushed aside by realism, the classical
art of one school is replaced by that of another, and finally all classical
schools give way to modernism, while the accumulated wealth of preceding
works becomes simply examples of art. In technology and industry there are
also certain styles, tastes, and views that replace one another without
destroying any of the antecedent, gradually accumulating discoveries.
In the evolutionary process of the above areas, the inhibition of some
phenomena and the arousal or revitalization of others can likewise be seen:
The accepted philosophical system survives all the blows of critics until
another, rival system arises. No matter how excellent a famous school of
art is, it only exists until another comes to replace it. The Gothic style
eliminated that of the Romanesque period; the Renaissance style was
needed to eradicate Gothicism. Similarly, in spite of criticism, classical
drama would have survived had not Romantic drama arisen (a hybrid
form, as a matter of fact). An industrial product disappears from the
market only after the advent of a new product that answers the same need,
or after the lessening of demand in accordance with a change in fashion or
tradition, of which the last circumstance could simply be the result of the
popularization of a new style (and not necessarily distaste towards the old
or new principle) and not only opposition to the former principle. (Tarde,
The Laws of Imitation, pp. 167-168)
Any invention is difficult to conceive; however, once it is complete, it
immediately enters into competition with the tools that perform the same
tasks as the invention but do so less efficiently. It gradually replaces these
tools, at the same time encountering—from them and from conservatism in
the environment—reaction, equal to the action of the invention, but opposite
to it in direction.
In this way, initiative, imitation, and competition determine the
development and direction of any social movement. Moreover, imitation is
directly dependent on the homogeneity of the environment, and is itself a
contributor to uniformity, whereas competition is determined by the diversity
of its environment, and initiative on the activity of its supporters. Because of
this, imitation is easily spread in the living conditions of closed societies,
parties, etc., while competition is strongest when one social factor or another
goes outside the bounds of a given society, party, or any more or less
homogeneous environment.
The analysis of rivalry does not fall under the scope of our task, since the
subject has already been dissected by a large number of eminent sociologists,
the first being Marx. We will simply mention competition as a factor of a
correlative nature, one which counterbalances imitation and which is no less
important in the social environment than imitation. Only thanks to rivalry do
social forms change, since imitation on its own could only lead to a single
form, which would bring us to question the very existence of society, its life.
It seems Tarde also faced this question, but did not resolve it, leaving it open
to discussion. On page 33 of his work, he points out the difficulties
encountered in resolving the social question:
Would it be good or bad [he writes] if someday there was established
complete unanimity of human outlook, attained via the exile, or forcible
conversion, of discordant minorities, and will this type of structure ever be
realized? Would it be good or bad if commercial, professional, individual,
political, and national rivalries were eliminated by organization of labour
or by state socialism, by a worldwide federation or by European stability
—the first step towards the union of the European states—and is this in
store for us in the future? Would it be good or bad, if, once it had removed
all control and opposition, a free and strong social power emerged, one
which was completely autocratic and had the potential for great deeds, one
which had absolute power in the face of a certain Caesar, party, or nation,
a government which was the most humanitarian, the most sensible
imaginable, and is it even advisable to establish such a system? This is a
dangerous question.
The author goes on to say that mankind is heading in the direction of the
greatest truth and ultimate power, but the method by which this apogee will
be attained is uncertain—whether "by the development of contradiction,
competition, and criticism, or by the elimination of all these and by the
infinite dissemination (through imitation) of a single will, a single idea which
will root itself more and more in the masses" (Tarde, The Laws of Imitation,
pp. 33-34). Can it be doubted that neither method can either lead to the
proposed result, or be realized in practice, since each of them complements
the other and leads to the maximum attainable well-being?
