It is clear, then, that to the list of inhibiting conditions must be added, in
certain cases, competition, which does, indeed, act as a very strong inhibitor
for any unstable social phenomenon. Because of this, Tarde is wrong in
trying to reduce social life to imitation, since competition, as we know, is far
from always an inhibiting force, and is often a stimulus, which was discussed
previously.1
An example of competition in the area of culture could be the
displacement, near the beginning of our era, of the cuneiform writing system
in Asia Minor by the Phoenician alphabet that had spread throughout the
Mediterranean coastal regions. Undoubtedly, this displacement occurred with
the military and political successes of the people settling the shores of the
Mediterranean, but we know that even conquerors sometimes have an inferior
culture to the vanquished, and the subject must consequently be analyzed, for
the most part, as the competition or rivalry of two cultures that history has
brought together in conflict.
The same can be observed with respect to language. The languages of less
cultured nations are gradually supplanted by the languages of more culturally
developed nations, since the latter inevitably come into close contact with the
former in the role of conquerors.
Furthermore, in languages there is likewise rivalry between different
linguistic forms, and, consequently, mutual inhibition, under which one form
survives the competition and becomes established and popularized through
imitation, while the other is inhibited and disappears completely. Of course,
the result of the conflict or competition is tied to possible simplifications of
the language, and to the form of its expression.
Inhibiting conditions in the collective are created by the government. In the
end, all power—which is most often the result of the predominance of one
class over another—is a form of coercion, and without constraint there can be
no power. It is nonetheless true that in some cases government is the result of
an agreement between two or more conflicting parties. However, in the latter
case, it would still suppress the elements that do not partake in the agreement.
To realize its plans, a power cannot do without certain instruments, which
become a part of it and at the same time are executors of its will.
Whether the power is the result of agreement between parties or classes, or
of the predominance of one of them, it has the opportunity to act on behalf of
society as its representative. But it is enough for the main instrument of a
government—its military—to forfeit its loyalty to the given power, as
happens in most revolutions, and the power is deprived of its basic support
and collapses.
Every social movement, once commenced, has a tendency to continue
developing in its original direction in accordance with the law of inertia. If, in
reality, this does not occur, then this is caused by certain obstacles, both
internal and external, that inhibit the movement. The inhibition of collective
labor can depend on any new stimulus that attracts social concentration.
Take, for example, a collective that is busily executing its usual, day-to-day
labor. Someone new arrives, bringing with him an interesting piece of
information, and the work of the collective is suspended while he speaks.
Let us next imagine a meeting whose object is the discussion of a certain
issue, and the attention or concentration of the collective is focused on the
speech of the orator, who is developing, step-by-step, his theme. But
suddenly an alarm bell is sounded, or an unusual noise is heard outside, and
the collective's concentration, formerly directed at the orator, is immediately
transferred to whatever has interrupted him.
Or let us say that a crowd in the street is displaying collective anger
towards an individual. However, if the object of the anger is adroit enough to
amuse the crowd, to provoke its laughter, then that is often sufficient to calm
the collective. In a different scenario, another person might distract the crowd
with his exhortations, and redirect the anger of the collective at himself.
In another scenario, we may be dealing with an enraged crowd that under
the influence of whatever provocation or rumor begins to commit excesses.
The advent of armed forces is enough to disperse the crowd or assuage its
rowdiness.
Or perhaps it is a hungry crowd that begins protesting against a person
selling produce. It often suffices to find out that the merchant does not even
have any groceries, since he has not received them, for the crowd, originally
geared for a pogrom, to disperse.
Let us look at another example: a collective is beating someone. As soon
as armed forces arrive on the scene, the person, who would certainly have
fallen victim to the crowd's fury, is saved.
On the other hand, the primary factor inhibiting a social movement is its
encounter with movements of a different character. These are, once again,
external inhibitors for the social movement.
When any social movement is developing or growing, it does not usually
meet with opposition from other social forces until it reaches the level of
development at which it clashes with the interests of other movements.
At this point, social circles of the "enemy" camp begin preparing
themselves for a suitable reaction, which eventually confines the social
movement within certain boundaries.
All conditions of social and state imposition can be considered more or
less constant or, to be more specific, long-term inhibiting factors in social
life.
The above include laws, customs, morals, traditions (even so-called
prejudices), class establishments, generally accepted conditions of social
morality or propriety, religious demands, established decorum in speech,
dress, and manner, the demands of fashion, etc.
Temporarily inhibiting conditions include all those that are consequences
of current events.
A social movement can be artificially inhibited through diversion or
through direct opposition. Thus, when war breaks out, the opposition, in view
of the patriotic mood of the nation and of the imminent danger, terminates its
conflict with the government. To this end, governments have more than once
resorted to war, guided by the principle that the prospective victories will
distract or temporarily suppress a revolutionary movement.
The thirteenth century was notoriously replete with the persecution of the
Jews—who were often accused of ritual killings—and was so because the
lower classes began displaying acute discontent, pushing the clergy and the
nobility to redirect, out of trepidation over the possibility of any independent
step being made by the masses, the mutinous thoughts of the people at
religious persecution of the Jews. Mythical fables, telling of Jews using the
blood of Christian infants for ritualistic purposes, were invented to serve as a
diversion of national discontent into a channel that would not be threatening
to the clergy and nobility. Judaism was to be the scapegoat for the sins of the
governors and ecclesiastics, and so the massacre of Jews began, with murder
and robbery stemming from the most insignificant occurrence. Another way
of heightening the religious fervor of the medieval citizen was the burning of
witches and sorcerers, and the persecution of heretics. An analogous
endeavor was embodied, though of course on a larger scale, by the Crusades.
Similarly, an inhibiting factor for national movements is any new social
event that implicates the whole of society. This could be a war, an unusual
discovery, or even the organization of public entertainment on a massive
scale. No wonder the hungry Roman crowd, which was diverted, in the
interests of the government, by various spectacles, cried for "bread and
circuses."
We will take for the illustration of our point a quotation from Tarde (Social
Logic, p. 233), which discusses the achievement of social unanimity:
When, in one region or another, parties or classes are dying of starvation
and are at each other's throats, the discovery of a coal mine, an asset for
all, establishes accord and good feeling. When a country is divided by
internal strife, the establishment or conquering— for a military nation, this
is equivalent to establishment—of a colony generates a great amount of
energy, which is both pacifying and enriching. Columbus' incredible
discovery eliminated or postponed for several centuries many European
wars and revolutions; how insignificant, in comparison, seem all the petty
colonial disputes that it caused. It provided a vent for the spirit of
disagreement, bitterness, and inhumanity which, finding satisfaction in the
maltreatment of savages, no longer plagued Europe.
In the realm of inhibition must be placed the following factors: the
limitations placed on the opinion of one party when it encounters the
opposing view of another party, and also the opposition that is organized
against the selfish plans of one representative of power or another; the
constitutions which limit the functions of power; and finally the laws that
create barriers to the various desires that oppose the basic requirements of
communal life.
The suppression of a social movement through direct opposition by, for
example, the government or the military, is also a form of external inhibition.
In these cases, we are usually dealing with temporary inhibition, since when
the power is weakened the movement once again develops.
Thus, we must not forget that taking drastic measures against one social
movement or another can have only a short-term effect. Such measures are
inevitably followed by more rational ones, and even these do not always
result in permanent inhibition, since when the inhibiting conditions are taken
away the original movement can be revitalized.
No less valid examples of enforced, i.e., external inhibition of the social
outlook are easy to pinpoint in the Russian Civil War (after the Revolution).
Areas that were previously under the control of the "Red" regime jubilantly
celebrated the advent of the "Whites," even showing a certain amount of
enthusiasm. However, as time passed, the populace began to despise the new,
harsher regime, and welcomed the return of the "Reds." Some regions were
transferred from power to power, and underwent this change of affiliation,
many times.
Aside from external inhibition, it is equally important to look at internal
inhibition, which is made up of regional conservatism, of habits and customs,
and of routines that hinder the development of any social novelty.
Any collective associative reflex develops on the same ground as a regular
reflex and, appearing as the result of an accessory aggravation, is easily
subject to internal inhibition.
Let us say that, under the influence of one condition or another, an
associative collective reflex is established, satisfying certain individuals. Let
this reflex be in the form of the right-wing organizations that were so
powerful—and active—in Russia during the period preceding the Great War.
With the advent of war and the "call from above" requiring all parties to
unite, those organizations became more subdued, and when the revolution
developed they disappeared completely, since they no longer had a sensible
reason for existence, and no longer felt a corresponding gratification.
What happened was, in large part, internal inhibition, as opposed to
external inhibition which would be, for example, the result of a prohibiting
law or of limitation by administration or force.
Internal inhibitors in collectives are not rare. Thus, in history we often
come across conditions that necessitate the manifestation of energy and
enthusiasm, which is nonetheless rendered impossible for reasons of internal
organization, class disagreement, etc. Such events include the delay of a
cultural movement whose followers are torn apart by civil dissension, civil
war, disparity of religious beliefs, with skepticism on one side and religious
fanaticism on the other, or constant conflict between conservatives and their
opposition. It is due to these same conditions that the very struggle of a
nation for its historic existence is often unrealizable, since the products of its
military initiatives collapse and are lost under conditions which it would be
pointless or impossible to withstand. All these situations usually lead to the
stifling, and limitation or weakening, of national impetus, and consequently
to inactivity and paralysis.
In general, wherever there is action between opposing forces, there is
suppression of social movements as well as a prevalence of social deviance.
Even a simple disagreement between individuals paralyzes both of them to a
greater or lesser extent.
As a matter of fact, the so-called collective frame of mind or "mood" is
also subject to internal inhibition, one sentiment being replaced by another.
Let us take an example from the history of Russian society. In the beginning
of the Great War, all of society followed even the most insignificant events of
the war: every success at the front excited the masses and provided reasons
for triumphant speeches at social meetings, whereas military failure was
considered a national misfortune, and plunged the majority into depression.
However, a period consisting solely of military failures gradually dulled the
social reaction to these events.
Even during the first stages of the Revolution, the July 18 offensive gave
powerful incentive to the development of the patriotic mood in the country—
since the Revolution itself began under a patriotic flag—but that was to be
the last burst of patriotism. The following military failures finally uprooted
any belief in the successful continuation of the war and provided favorable
conditions for internationalistic teachings. This bred an attitude of
indifference towards the war, and when the Bolsheviks seized power this
attitude reached such proportions that the secession (from Russia) of Poland,
Lithuania, Latvia, and then of Ukraine, Finland, the Caucasus, and the Baltic
area, passed over with no specific reaction from Russian society, as though it
was not the dismemberment of a living social body that was taking place, but
rather that of a lifeless corpse.
A phenomenon of collective reflexology deserving particular attention is
the natural inhibition caused by executions and shootings, found in the
mimico-somatic reflex of a crowd. The shocking effect of a murder on the
nerves under peaceful circumstances, and the contrasting gradual
accustomization to death which develops during war, is well known. A
soldier who is accustomed to battle no longer feels afraid or conscience-
stricken; he is excited in conflict and seems almost drunk, disregarding
danger. A mimico-somatic reaction, in this way, is gradually dulled through
constant repetition, and, in the end, the reaction—even in the face of an
individual's own death—is completely inhibited. The constant expectation of
death makes people indifferent to real hazards.
Here we are speaking of a more or less total suppression of a mimico-
somatic response, resulting from over-stimulation. In support of the
aforementioned, we find the following passage in the memoirs of Cattelain, a
member of the Paris Commune and the chief of the Criminal Investigation
Department:
In the end, as people must tire of anything oft-repeated, so the soldiers no
longer destroyed or annihilated captives, but opened fire on them without
fury, without wild howlings, rather in perfect order and with wise
economy of cartridges.
Two or three shots from a rifle, sometimes only one, directed without
hesitation into the skull, and it was over. Eventually even the victims
allowed themselves to be placed against the wall with calm hopelessness.
There were only a few modest complaints:
"My poor boy, what is it you want to do, you didn't even give me time to
kiss my children."2
Greater indifference in the face of death, on the part of both sides, is
difficult to imagine.
Even simple people, seeing themselves surrounded with executions,
become as accustomed to the idea of their own death as to any everyday
action. Thus, in Orange, the peasants danced upon the scaffold and a fifteen-
year-old boy asked the executioner: "Will you hurt me very much?"
The endurance and courage of the leaders of revolutionary movements,
who usually die with heroic calmness, go without saying. It is known with
what indifference Danton went to the scaffold: "You will show my head to
the people, it is worthy of such an honor" were his last words. There were
even jesters at the gibbet. Thus, one jokingly wished the onlookers greater
success than he had attained; another, at the guillotine, taunted the spectators
as well as the executioner.
It is known with what stoicism conspirators die during periods of bloody
revolution. Thus, the victims of the "red shirt" conspiracy went to the
scaffold, one by one, without revealing their secrets. An even better example
in this respect is the Girondins:
The heroic death of the Girondins makes them deserving of immediate
immortality. Every measure was taken to prevent them from drinking
anything "invigorating" before the execution. This did not prevent the
Girondins from singing the Marseillaise, not as a funerary psalm, but as a
victory march, as a symbol of hope for their mother-land. They sang it
with enthusiasm, loudly and clearly, on the cart taking them to their doom,
and going up the steps of the scaffold. Only the heavy knife of the
guillotine silenced their voices forever. (Cabanis and L. Nass, p. 98)
The crowd which witnessed so many executions was no less indifferent.
Sacrifices to the guillotine were inculcated upon society more and more;
they began to be habitual, became a national necessity, and the crowd,
accustomed to the sight of blood, no longer felt disgust; soon it could not
do without such stimulus as the spectacle provided. Mothers brought their
children to the scene of the execution, fathers hoisted them to their
shoulders, "nurturing in them a feeling of hate towards the tyrants and a
sense of courage." (Cabanis and L. Nass, p. 101)
Analogous phenomena can be observed in a crowd on the attack.
In the beginning, the crowd still feels negatively, or at least
apprehensively, towards blood, but as soon as the first blood is spilled, the
fury of the crowd knows no bounds; blood ceases to arouse disgust: to the
contrary, it means absolutely nothing to the crowd, and even excites it, which
explains the ridiculing and maltreatment of the corpses of those murdered.
There are many more examples, which, however, need not be given since
they are known to everyone from history. It is clear from the above that the
accustomization to something which previously aroused a mimico-somatic
reflex is in itself an act of internal inhibition.
For the same reason (the surmounting of internal obstacles) any social
innovation is not immediately accepted, but enters life gradually through the
re-education of society. If an innovation drastically changes the usual tenor of
social life, then it always meets with more or less strong opposition, because
of habits and routines that are expressions of the law of inertia. So, the
greater the expected break in social tradition, the greater the counteraction on
the part of society, and the greater the internal inhibition, which for the most
part can be stifled only with force. The opposition, briefly mentioned
previously, which followed the introduction of the steam engine, the
construction of railways, the first attempt at cultivating potatoes, the
enforcement of reforms, etc., can serve as an example of this form of
inhibition.
National disturbances and rebellions find a favorable environment in
identical encounters between established social conditions and innovations
for which society is unprepared.
To avoid this, all more or less radical social innovations are most easily
introduced through younger generations, which have become neither
entrenched in the rut of old traditions nor accustomed to them; consequently,
in these cases a certain role must be assigned to the re-education of youth.
In general, every innovation in a social environment that affects the
interests of the majority must encounter opposition, whose magnitude
depends on how deeply those interests are affected. With the passage of time,
the given innovation becomes habitual, and is later defended as something
acquired by and belonging to society, despite the fact that it was at one point
forcibly thrust upon the latter. Thus, Roman law was once violently enforced
among the GalloRoman people, who then recognized the defense of Roman
law as their sacred obligation.
Obstacles to the popularization of one innovation or another in the
collective include contrary opinions rooted in society, as well as various
predispositions, and even national, tribal, or class pride based on the past life
of the collective. Depending on these conditions, one collective accepts an
innovation more easily than another, in which the popularization of the same
innovation meets with much greater adversity. It goes without saying that the
popularization of an innovation in the collective meets greater opposition if it
does not originate in an authoritative environment, and, conversely, it is
accelerated if the innovation comes from prominent and trusted spheres,
which stimulate imitation in their surroundings. Similarly, the spread of an
innovation from one collective to another is facilitated if it originates in a
collective of higher status, as compared to one which spreads from a less
important collective, since the former has greater influence than the latter.
In general, internal inhibition is facilitated by frequent repetition of social
facts. "As a result of frequent use [says Tarde (The Laws of Imitation, p.
216)], a word is shortened, faded, worn, like a pebble bathed by waves;
religious belief loses its intensity, art degenerates, etc." The same can be
observed in every social movement which, with the passage of time, wanes,
losing its novelty, and neither attracts the interest of others nor retains the
attention of its own followers.
Because of this, a new stimulus is needed to revitalize the social
movement. Even a break in the movement may turn out to be beneficial,
thanks to the fact that its followers have time to accumulate energy, and the
movement may eventually excite public interest again.
The previously mentioned collective concentration, like its individual
counterpart, is subject to fatigue and internal inhibition. In view of this,
collective labor, which demands strenuous concentration, must be
accompanied by certain breaks, after which it can continue with renewed
energy, thanks to the resultant revitalization. In this case, the point at which
inhibition begins depends on the nature of the collective labor, and on its
greater or lesser monotony.
Analogously, an orator who speaks unenthusiastically and monotonously
tires his audience in a very short time, whereas an orator who is able to
diversify his speech in expression and content can entertain a crowd, without
exhausting it, for two hours or more. So, to sustain concentration during
continuous mental effort, both interruptions and changes in subject matter are
employed. When these factors are taken into account, collective pursuits can
continue, as is known, for five or six hours without excessive fatigue, and
various other collective activities can continue for a much longer time. It is
acknowledged that eight hours of physical labor, coupled with appropriate
rest periods, is the optimum duration of work; in most cases, this schedule
prevents undue fatigue and exhaustion under conditions of continued physical
effort. However, overwork depends not only on the number of hours of labor
and length of breaks, but also on the strenuousness of the labor and even on
the interest in the work.3
If an inhibitor of social life is something which hinders society's natural
course of development, then it should not be forgotten that in collective, and
similarly in individual reflexology, the same factor can be both inhibiting or
stimulating, depending on the conditions in which it is present. Thus, the use
of force, such as military execution, often leads to the suppression of a
national movement in the first stages of development; however, when
national aggravation has reached a higher level, executions can only instigate
a renewal of national displeasure and strengthen the national movement.
Thus, during the historic strike of February 25, when the Cossacks refused to
shoot the populace and shouted, with the people, "Down with autocracy,"
police were introduced into the army, and the shooting of rioters was
conducted from machine guns arranged on the roofs of houses. But it was too
late, and the political strike quickly turned into a general one. Every day,
renewed slaughter increased national indignation and rooted the national
movement more firmly, so that it eventually gained the upper hand when a
crowd of people in the streets mingled with troops, took control of the
situation, and executed a coup with lightning speed.
Another example: competition, as we saw, is often an inhibitor for
fledgling social movements, while in other cases it is a stimulus. It is known
that competition is often a stimulating factor in the development of one social
movement or another. Only movements that are insufficiently strong and lack
vital capacity can, as was mentioned, be stifled and inhibited, since they are
unable to withstand competition; as a matter of fact, it is on the basis of this
principle that protectionism is established. In all other cases, competition
serves as a revitalizing factor of social movements, allowing for increased
activity of the collective, which can then advance beyond its competitors.
In all the above cases, acts of inhibition in the collective create conditions
that breed opposition to any social movement, and in the end this reaction
always equals action, as represented by the given movement.
In conclusion, let us note that strikes must be added to the ranks of
important collective acts of inhibition, having in some cases political and in
others economic significance. Here we are dealing with an act of social
negativism or with a display of social inhibition that forces previously
vigorous social activity to terminate, sometimes immediately. This inhibition
is usually manifested under the influence of whatever external conditions are
powerful enough to affect the masses, at a time when the latter cannot
otherwise oppose such— usually coercive—influences. In other words, when
active opposition is unrealizable, its place is taken by inhibition. A strike is
an act of great social importance, not only as a defense, but also as an actual
form of counteraction, often attaining positive results. One of the most
significant, in magnitude and results, is the general political strike that broke
out in Russia in 1905. It would be of interest for us to follow in this work the
development of the strike that eventually led to the convocation of Russia's
legislative institutions.
The general political strike in Russia of 1905 has deep roots, whose growth
began long before the strike's eruption. The pointless Japanese war, which
demanded of Russia colossal sacrifices, first aroused the discontent of the
Russian people. Propaganda against the tsarist government spread across the
country. The government would not capitulate, and continued to uphold a
very strict regime. But the long-developing discontent with the tsar's regime
increased rapidly. In order to paralyze the agitators who opposed the tsar's
rule, the latter organized its own agitation among workers; to this end,
"societies" were formed among the workers, at the meetings of which orators,
bribed by the government, proved to the people that it was in their interests to
depend on the tsar, that they should not believe the tsar's adversaries, that the
tsar honestly wished to help his subjects, that the tsar loved his subjects and
wanted only the best for them, and that if there was discontent with the tsar's
regime in Russia, then it was the fault of over-zealous government officials,
etc. One such society was established in Moscow. Another was organized in
1904 in St. Petersburg, on the police's initiative and with the participation of
the priest Georgiy Gapon; one of its goals was to distract workers from
"criminal" propaganda.
Gapon, who was at first trusted by members of the government and clergy,
was an excellent orator, well able to entertain his audience. When he depicted
the plight of the worker, tears came to the eyes of his listeners, and they
displayed childlike joy when he dictated that workers must depend wholly on
the tsar. Needless to say, the tsar's name was, at that time, still associated
with a kind of aureole, handed down from father to son in the manner of an
inheritance. The number of members of societies and followers of Gapon and
other orators grew with incredible speed. Audiences seemed to recognize, in
the words of the speakers, their own hidden hopes, and the crowds of workers
were overcome with a feeling of excitement.
This propaganda, abetted by the police, did have a reverse side, since to
ignite hope was equivalent to letting it burst into flame, and it is quite
understandable that the simple masses made their own deductions to that end.
If the tsar was the "father" of the people, then he had to listen to his
"children," in view of which the idea arose to appeal directly to the tsar, in
order that he hear the "needs" of the people and help them in such dire
circumstances. It must be noted that the people had previously appealed to
the tsar through messengers. In this case, however, it was decided not to
depend on messengers, but to go en masse to the palace, carrying portraits of
the tsar, icons, and gonfalons.
This idea was realized on the ninth of January. The capital spent a worried
night, awaiting grave occurrences, since no one could believe that this direct
appeal to the tsar could pass without unfortunate consequences for the
unarmed crowd. The situation unfolded in the manner that had been foreseen
by those who understood the true state of affairs. The Winter Palace was
surrounded with troops that impeded the advance of the workers; shooting
followed, many workers fell victim, and the ground around the palace was
stained scarlet with blood. In official bulletins, the wounded were counted in
the hundreds, and the dead—in tens; rumors spread of much larger numbers.
Witnesses stated that children, who had climbed trees in the Admiralty park
to watch the goings-on, fell like hunted sparrows when the shots rang out.
The disillusionment among the workers and lower classes was total. Their
last hopes, centered on the tsar and based on the belief in him instilled by the
orators, had yielded bitter fruit. It became clear to everyone that appeals
would not serve their intended purpose, and alternate means of influence on
the tsar had to be used. Almost all of the country was in a state of upheaval,
which increased to greater and greater dimensions; this led to the replacement
of the governmental regime and the outline of a future constitution through
the Duma. But that which could have appeased the people, more or less, in
the spring of 1905, when opposition to the government could not have
reached the corresponding height or tension, was now an insufficient measure
after the increase in social strength following the unsuccessful publication of
the Duma's legislative act. Since the rights of the people were not properly
addressed, whilst the unfortunate Japanese war showed the more unpleasant
aspects of the tsar's regime, the national movement developed more and
more, and at last, in the circles opposing the government, there came the
decision to organize an offensive in the form of a general political strike
among the working classes, which was later joined by railway stations and,
on the twelfth of October, by the postal/ telegraph workers. The country's
heartbeat seemed to have been suspended. Trains were forced to stop when
they encountered the strike. The post and telegraph terminated their activities.
Factories and plants closed. Trams in the cities stopped running, and stood—
or even lay—empty in the streets. Needless to say, the strike threatened the
well-being of the entire country, and the government understood its sinister
meaning; as a result, the act on the powers of the legislative Duma was
quickly drawn up, and on October 17 the corresponding manifesto was
publicized, a manifesto which gave wing to the hopes of the stifled Russian
people.
At that moment, the strike ended. Trains resumed their functions, wheels
turned once more at the factories and plants, trams made the rounds of city
streets once again. In this way, the act of the seventeenth of October served
as a lubricating oil that did away with the inhibition in the wheels of the
social machine, which had ground to a halt when social needs were not met.
From the above, it is clear that the law of reaction being equal to action is
as immutable in social life as in mechanics. In society, it encounters a more
complicated situation and so is not manifested in such simple forms as in
mechanics, but this does not change its essence and lessens neither its
significance nor its importance.
Notes
1 Aside from imitation and rivalry, every new factor requires an initiative or stimulus, without which
no discovery, invention, slogan, or appeal could exist. This is the role of personal initiative in the
social process. But initiative only becomes useful when it encounters imitation by and rivalry from
others. In this way, initiative, imitation, and rivalry all determine the social process or social
movement.
2 P. Cattelain, Mémoires inédites du chef de la Sûreté sous la Commune, Paris, pp. 231-37.
3 See V. Bekhterev, "The main tasks of the reflexology of physical labor (in Russian), Issues in the
Study and Education of the Personality, issue 1,1920.
See also my presentation at the conference on the scientific organization of
industrial labor, which was given in Moscow in February of 1921, and which
can be found in the conference proceedings.
6
The Law of Similarity
There is a theorem in mechanics which states that two geometrically similar
systems, whether they be dimensionally different or not, will always have
geometrically similar movements, so that even after every possible
displacement they will still resemble each other.
This theorem flows naturally out of the law of similarity, which can
likewise be observed throughout the organic world. Thus, we know that,
thanks to the relatively constant basic composition of the living matter of the
protoplasm, all basic functions such as nutrition, breathing, and digestion, are
similar. Everywhere in the organic world there is a commonality of organism
structure, represented by cell colonies and the greater or lesser centralization
of parts of the body. Chemical and mechanical processes, in turn, are
common to all organisms. Even external defense mechanisms, such as the
production of poison, are of a similar character in such dissimilar organisms
as the bee and the snake. Finally, even the ontogenetic development of
organisms proceeds according to one common plan, creating all the forms of
phylogenetic development.
In social life, this law of similarity can be observed in various facets of
collective activity. Thus, all social organization is defined by one and the
same formula. In the collaborative form of social activity, there is always an
elected presidium with a chairman at its head; an autocratic form of
government has an appointed head which delegates people to execute its will,
etc. In different countries, under a wide range of circumstances, we meet with
more or less identical forms of worship, while the concept of God is
universal. It is known that the mythology of the Old and New Worlds has
common aspects which cannot be explained by interaction between nations,
since the similarity between the myths was ascertained upon the discovery of
America. Quite a few common rituals and customs were also found between
nations that historically had no communication among themselves.
While discussing the resemblance between native Mexican religion and the
Catholic one, C Letourneau notes:
This is not the first time we come across the fact that, at all times and in
all places, people belonging to different races have done similar things and
come to similar and sometimes very unique conclusions, which can only
be explained by their common humanity. Recall the ancient Egyptians, the
African negroes and the redskins, who cruelly decided to cut off the noses
of their unfaithful wives; or the lip Botoque of the Botocudos, Nutka-
Columbians, and negroes of central Africa, etc. (C. Letourneau, Morality,
p. 227)
Furthermore, the historical development of civilizations seems to follow a
single plan. The hunting lifestyle of primitive nations was exchanged for a
nomadic one after the domestication of livestock, and this nomadic period
was replaced by agriculture with the advent of increased security. In terms of
the socioeconomic order, the slave-owning system was replaced by the feudal
one, the bourgeois-capitalist regime replaced feudalism, and finally
bourgeois-capitalism was exchanged for socialism.
If we observe the revolutionary movement in Russia from 1905 to 1906,
and especially from 1917-onwards, and compare it to the following
revolution in Germany and to preceding insurrections such as the Great
French Revolution, we inevitably note its conformity to the regularity of
complex human activities and, in turn, the latter's correspondence to the law
of similarity. Who can deny that the Russian Revolution of 1917 was, in large
part, a repetition of the revolution of the late 1700s, notwithstanding the fact
that both revolutions occurred in different historical periods, that one of them
developed at a time of peace and another in the midst of a colossal world war,
that both were realized by nations of different race under different climates
and historical conditions.
As for the German Revolution, it was almost a duplicate of the Russian
one, although it did not reach the level of development of the latter and
advanced at a faster rate than the original. Like its Russian counterpart, the
German Revolution began in the army, with mutiny quickly spreading across
the whole Western Front; the murder of officers, the creation of soldiers' and
peasants' committees, the division of the nation based on the principle of the
self-determination of peoples, and the first coalitional ministry likewise
followed.
The German Revolution even had its own Kornilov in the person of
Hindenburg; its counterrevolutionary opposition in some military sectors, its
Bolsheviks represented by Spartacists; its leader of Bolshevism in the person
of Liebknecht; its July uprising and December days in Berlin, etc., and all
these events unfolded in the same order as they had in Russia, despite the fact
that there were seemingly more than enough reasons for the revolutions to
follow different paths. Although the German Revolution developed during
the same war as the Russian, Germany was a conqueror nation that could be
proud of its military successes and of the extension of its borders; a nation
that led an especially disciplined life, and whose people were more highly
educated than Russia's; a nation that lived under the aureole of a glamorous
military history; a nation that was governed, more prudently than ourselves,
by a dynasty that was not weakened by Rasputinism, but rather that enjoyed
great popularity in the person of Wilhelm II; all this, it would seem, should
have led the revolution in Germany along a different course. And yet the
revolution in Germany, nonetheless, developed along the same lines as that in
Russia; however, it could not, in view of special conditions in its country of
origin, withstand the pressure of the counter-revolutionary movement and so
did not attain its goal.
Obviously, this common resemblance of circumstances can only be
explained by the fact that the same reasons or laws are active in determining
their development. If this is so, then we cannot doubt that this similarity of
phenomena must exist in social movements that develop during more
peaceful periods of social life, since in periods of revolution the resemblance
is simply more apparent, and stands out more obviously than at other times.
Without providing many other proofs of this law in the supraorganic
world, let us now point out the congruence between the phases of the
ontogenetic development of correlative activity and the various phases of
ontegenetic development in general, since a child, throughout its mental
development, undergoes all the changes that humanity has experienced, from
savagery to cultural enlightenment.
Since the mechanism of association, or brain, has essentially the same
structure in individuals of a given type, the so-called associative functions of
these individuals develop along parallel lines. This is the basic explanation
for the ease with which separate species of similar character, upon
communication with each other, are united for joint activity when necessary.
This can be observed most often in the communication of humans. What
conditions, then, explain this union through associative activity in human
society?
Recently, some authors1 have hypothesized that the unconscious activity of
all individuals, which represents the experience of previous generations, is
the same; differences are only noticeable in their conscious aspects. This
would, of course, help elucidate the common actions of the masses in certain
cases. But the fact of the matter is that the inherited experience of preceding
generations is manifested in simple reflexes, and not in those "unconscious"
processes which refer, as do conscious ones, to associative activity that
implies past individual experience. From this it is clear that, no matter how
tempting the above hypothesis is, it cannot be useful in the explanation of the
common associative activity of a mass of individuals.
In our opinion, the unifying factors of the associative activity of
individuals in social groups are the following: direct influence or induction,
imitation, then suggestion, and finally persuasion.
The least studied form of mutual influence in the collective is induction, or
the direct action of one person upon another. Previous investigations in this
field have given, for the most part, unconvincing results. But recently I was
able to conduct an investigation, in the form of a laboratory experiment, both
on animals and on humans. This investigation, which served as the subject of
my talks at the conference on the study of the brain and psychic activity in
1920 (see Issues in the Study and Education of the Personality for 1920-
onwards), leaves no doubt as to the possibility of direct induction from one
individual to another.
If this is so, then it is clear that in a collective, especially one such as a
crowd, there can and doubtless does exist direct mutual influence or
induction, which should always be taken into account.
Imitation is essentially a natural consequence and further development of
the process of reproduction, since any reproduction is self-imitation or
imitation of a once-performed act; muscular stimuli that were experienced at
one point leave an impression which, upon revitalization, instigates new
actions of a similar nature.
The imitation of others has to do with the reproduction of what has been
heard, seen, or felt, with the impulse in this case being an external stimulus
that arouses, thanks to previously acquired individual experience, the muscle
movements needed to imitate. Imitation is the basic social reflex, just as
reproduction is the basic reflex of individual associative activity.
Without speaking of imitation as a means of biological defense in the form
of so-called mimicry, it is necessary to keep in mind that imitation is a
primary form of communication among all living creatures.
This is why, in all inferior creatures, imitation alone is the basis of social
life.
If caterpillars are moving as a clump in one direction, as I have had the
occasion to observe, tumbling over each other with identical movements,
then this is happening only thanks to tactile/muscular imitation.
If we hear, on a quiet summer evening, the chirping of grasshoppers, then
this is also due to imitation. When a swarm of mosquitoes circles our heads,
moving up and down in unison, then this is likewise explicable only by
imitation.
In the kingdom of the bees and ants, we meet with imitation everywhere.
Even when the hive is attacked by foreign bees who begin eating the young
larvae, the bees that inhabit it must perform the same cannibalistic act.
If a frog's croak is heard on a marsh, its friends will invariably make their
voices heard in response. The cry of a rooster is echoed by its feathered
neighbors.
Imitation can also be heard in the chorus of birds. As soon as the "guard"
of a group of birds flaps his wings and flies away, the whole flock follows
suit.
In the world of mammals, imitation has likewise developed to an
incredible extent. Everyone has witnessed how a herd obeys its leader, how
the braying of a donkey or neighing of a horse is reciprocated by others of its
kind, etc. A massive amount of observations have been conducted on the
propensity to imitate among various animals, and the monkey's notoriety in
this respect has even become proverbial.
Imitation, generally, is a phenomenon that has spread among all higher
animals. It includes such actions as the stampede of a herd from danger, or
the hunting down of an animal by the pack. Everyone knows that when one
of the animals of a pack comes upon prey and begins to pursue it, the rest of
the pack soon follows. In herds of sheep and other animals, the leader's
movements are imitated by the other members of the herd. Thus, his
inspection of some object or other incites his confreres to do the same, as
does his leap at a certain place. If a dog begins barking in response to a noise,
its neighbors will follow suit. The cry of one goose stimulates the honking of
the whole gaggle. When a flock of birds settles on a tree, or a group of wild
seagulls rests, from fishing, somewhere on a sandy bank, the cry of one bird
can be heard spreading throughout the flock, giving the impression of a
curious conversation.
All these are examples of imitation in the life of animals, of which many
more could be provided, if it were necessary to do so.
For animals, imitation seems to be an instinctive, natural act. For instance,
let us say we are observing a chick that has just hatched from its egg. Being
separated from its mother, it cannot orient itself among the food, or eggshells,
surrounding it, and dies of starvation because it is not "taught" to eat. But
how is it possible to teach it to do so? To this end, it suffices to tap your
finger, imitating a mother's pecking, and the chick immediately begins
pecking also, imitating the movement of your hand. The first piece of
eggshell to enter its mouth is swallowed thanks to reflex action; after a few of
these imitational ventures, the chick begins executing the pecking motion,
which leads to ingestion, on its own.
Imitation is inevitable in the interaction of two similar individuals because
it is always accompanied by a sthenic reaction; direct experience has proved
that physical labor increases with the observation of synchronous
movements. Féré's experiment can be considered convincing in this respect.
If the subject performs work on an ergo-graph, and at the same time another
person across from him performs the same movements on an ergograph, the
amount of work done increases. This sthenic influence of imitation is
accompanied by a positive emotional, that is mimico-somatic, reaction,
leading to a more or less extended period of reproducing the same
movements. This is why imitation is a universal law in the animal kingdom.
This situation brings us to the conclusion that at the base of sociality lies not
only social selection, but often also a biological or, more specifically,
bioreflexological factor that holds a stimulating role in imitation, causing at
the same time a mimico-somatic reaction of a positive nature.
In the world of humans, imitation can likewise be encountered at every
step, notwithstanding the fact that the process of inhibition plays a much
larger role among humans than in the rest of the animal kingdom. Look at
children, and you will see how they imitate each other and their elders. The
shout of one initiates the shouting of others. If one begins to be naughty, the
others will follow his example.
Imitation is likewise at the basis of the development of human speech,
gesture, and partially mime, since deaf people remain dumb from birth, but
have well-developed mime, while blind people have very limited miming
skills, while their other senses are acutely developed.
The entire upbringing of humans, as that of the majority of animals, is
based on imitation, thanks to which the experience of elders is passed down
to youth. Even in the social life of adults, imitation is found everywhere. If
somebody yawns, especially in the evening, everyone begins yawning. It is
known what vitality is brought into society by one animated and happy
person. A previously bored and listless group begins, under his influence, a
lively conversation. The infectiousness of laughter is also generally
acknowledged. Certain people cannot see someone crying without also
bursting into tears.
On the other hand, imitation through mimicry between people who live
together a long time can be so strong that it leads to similar characteristics, as
can be observed in a married couple.
It is likewise known that imitation causes stuttering, hysterics, and other
nervous conditions.
In some diseases, the ability to imitate reaches its highest level, causing a
given word or motion to be immediately repeated (as in so-called echolalia
and echopraxia). Finally, imitation is manifested in all complex actions,
including work; every student repeats what his teacher dictates, and only
gradually does he free himself from imitation by creating something more or
less original. This kind of imitation is also widely known. But even complex
social phenomena are based, in some measure, on imitation. Thus, the course
of the Russian Revolution was, in large part, an imitation of the Great French
Revolution. Doubtlessly, the reason for this resemblance was the common
conformity to laws of social phenomena by both revolutions; however, the
fact that imitation played a role here shows the similarity even between the
purely external forms of certain social phenomena.
We will not discuss the nature of imitation, one of the most basic reflexes
in biology, for it is only vaguely understood. Let us be content with saying
that the impulses registered by sight and hearing seem to ease the
reproduction of what has been seen or heard, in the same way as the previous
execution of a movement eases its reproduction, and that the facilitated
movement is stimulated by an accompanying sthenic reaction.
A special form of unification by means of conversation is suggestion and
persuasion. Suggestion is, in the true sense of the concept, indirect imitation,
since simple imitation involves the reproduction of one act or another under
the influence of its relative ocular, aural, or tactile impulse, whereas
suggestion is the fulfillment of one act or another by means of its
corresponding symbol (word). The difference, consequently, is that in the
first case an external impression stimulates the coinciding act, and in the
second the same act is reproduced through its corresponding symbol.
Can there be suggestion among animals? Seemingly, yes; at least, among
the more highly developed of them. When a flock of wild birds is busy eating
somewhere, for example in a field or marsh, it assigns to one of its members
the responsibility of being "guard," and as soon as that guard extends his neck
or looks up, the flock is put on the alert; when he gives a worried cry, the
flock quickly flies away. In this way, his voiced warning results in a certain
action by the flock. What is this, if not suggestion? At least, there is nothing
in this case that would isolate this phenomenon from that which we know to
be human suggestion.
In this way, it can be acknowledged that animals employ, depending on
their living conditions and the usefulness of imitation in their fight for
survival, mimicry and other forms of imitation, and later suggestion.
Among humans, we can observe induction, imitation, and suggestion; the
latter can be accomplished not only by a cry or gesture, as in the animal
world, but also, and most often, through the spoken word.
The mechanism of suggestion, in its turn, boils down to the fact that under
certain conditions connected to the interruption of concentration, a word can
stimulate the facilitated reproduction of a movement or condition previously
acquired through experience.
As is known, science has devised a method of suggestion both under
hypnosis and normal conditions. In real life, however, we are dealing with
unpremeditated suggestion in the conscious state, which is imperceptibly
passed from one person to another, differing from imitation, as was
mentioned, in that the latter entails the reproduction of something seen or
heard, whereas the former involves the stimulation of a certain movement or
condition with the help of a word, cry, or gesture.
The significance of suggestion in social life is immense, and arises
everywhere in a manner similar to that of imitation, often becoming
interwoven with the latter by almost imperceptible threads.
I will not set out to discuss the role of suggestion in any greater detail,
since I dedicated an entire book—Suggestion and Its Role in Social Life—to
the subject; it is to that work that I direct the reader.
At this point, we should go on to analyze another process, known as
learning, based on instruction and persuasion. Here, we are dealing with an
even more complicated imitational process, in which, in order to reproduce a
foreign act, an individual neither blindly imitates it nor is directly verbally
stimulated to perform it, but first evaluates its significance and usefulness and
only then, after a positive outcome of this evaluation, begins reproducing that
act. As a result, the act is recognized as having been learned, with the object
of learning being not so much the form of the act, but its substance.
When learning is realized, and the learned action can be reproduced thanks
to training, then the act can be executed with especial speed and ease upon a
certain spoken or written command, or even a certain sign, for example a
gesture playing the role of a suggestion.
It goes without saying that if learning, that form of more complicated
imitation, is inferior to the latter in terms of its speed of execution, then it is
infinitely superior to the same in view of the evaluation that takes place
before what is to be learned is accepted.
There is no need to elaborate on the significance of this form of
complicated imitation in social life. It suffices to say that it lies at the base of
all civilization and culture.
Some symbols doubtlessly owe their existence to metaphorical
assimilation, based on similarity and imitation. This fact is not difficult to
prove. The movements of a conductor's baton are based on the spatial
interpretation of tone and of the extent of the register; colored symbols are
based on the assimilation of forms existing in reality. Thus, a red flag is the
sign of blood and so is appropriated by revolutionary movements. It is
likewise the warning of any danger.
A green flag or banner is the color of growing plant life, and so is
associated with hope for future flowering and prosperity.
Black is likened to the darkness of the grave and is, therefore, the symbol
of death and terror.
White, conversely, signifies glittering brightness and is appropriated by the
monarchy.
Blue, corresponding to the color of the sky, is often a symbol of purity and
innocence, and so is an emblem in some religious decorations and rites.
In all these cases, the symbol is a reproduction of another phenomenon that
has a certain meaning in our surroundings.
The cases in which certain actions of people have an effect on a given
bystander must be categorized as a special kind of influence. Examples of
this include a situation in which an infant, seeing one person beating another,
begins crying, or in which an adult, observing a horrific scene, loses
consciousness. Here there is neither imitation nor suggestion, but nothing less
than direct influence in the form of an associative reflex.
A similar example is provided by the following incident. When
Mendeleyev represented the Petrograd chamber at the hundredth anniversary
of Yuryev University,2 he stumbled on his way to the podium. The audience
rose as one in respect to his person. This was not an ovation that could be
accompanied by public applause. This was silent homage to national pride
and fame.
Another example is the reception of Kerensky among troops at the peak of
his fame. Here is one of these scenes:
At precisely nine o'clock, the orchestra began playing the Marseillaise, the
Cossacks stood at attention, and Kerensky appeared in an open
automobile. A crowd of thousands of people roared "Hurrah."The faces of
the welcomers betrayed excitement and love towards their revolutionary
chief and hero. Kerensky rose to his full height in the vehicle and, holding
his cap high, hailed the people. His sincere, spiritual face attracted
attention by virtue of its expressiveness and inner charm. When
Kerensky's automobile flew through the crowd, everyone, as though
hypnotized, ran after it, constantly shouting "Hurrah" and waving caps
and hats (from newspaper articles).
Influence, as understood in the aforementioned manner, is exerted by
means of associative reflexes, in a way similar to the stimulation of
associative reflexes with the help of symbolic signs, whether this
[stimulation] is caused directly in the form of suggestion, or in the form of
logical persuasion.
This type of associative influence seems to come somewhat later in the
developmental process, with the establishment of certain relations between
people. Thus, if a child sees a fist-fight through the window and begins
crying, then this is undoubtedly an act of induction; but the latter can appear
only after imitation in the cycle of development, since it requires a certain
amount of experience in relation to even such simple acts as the beating of
one person by another. In exactly the same manner, when one person rushes
to help another who has fallen or dropped something, he is executing an act
of induction that once again requires the corresponding life experience and
therefore evolves later than imitation.
As for suggestion, it arises at the point when a child has sufficiently
mastered the meaning of gesture and speech, which occurs after the learning
of the meanings of simple acts, and so suggestion may begin simultaneously
with or, more commonly, after induction.
Finally, persuasion by deduction requires well-refined speech and
completely developed logical premises, and so is manifested at an even more
advanced age, when experience has accumulated the knowledge of causative
correlations between separate phenomena.
In this case, it is of primary interest that imitation and suggestion, as well
as induction, lead to coordinated actions of the collective or of a group of
people in the collective, since this is especially significant to the social
conditions of life. It must be kept in mind that a person's imitation and
suggestion, and likewise induction, never remain at the same level. They are
dependent on his condition at one time or another, on his greater or lesser
concentration, and, partially, on the source of a given influence. Thus, a
condition of passivity and distracted concentration is conducive to the
development of imitation, induction, and suggestion.
On the other hand, a developed person who possesses fresh strength, and
who is consequently not fatigued, can oppose imitation, induction, and
suggestion. An invigorating reaction of a mimico-somatic nature always
promotes the development of imitation, induction, and suggestion in a
direction which is correspondent to the character of the given emotion. It is
known that in a crowd, imitation, induction, and suggestion meet with
especially favorable conditions, and this is because a crowd's emotive state
goes up sharply under conditions of passivity or fatigue.
Also, children and less developed adults are, as is known, more easily
subject to induction, imitation, and suggestion.
However, the source of influence itself is not unimportant in terms of the
realizability of the influence in one form or another.
Thus, influence exerted on a student by the gesture or word of his friend
will be completely different from that exerted by the gesture or word of his
teacher; in exactly the same way, the influence exerted on an uneducated
person by the gestures or words of someone similar to himself will not be the
same as the influence exerted by those of an educated person, etc. Lastly, a
person's character is not insignificant in terms of the strength of his influence.
Experience, in any case, leads us to acknowledge that some people are by
nature predisposed to imitation, induction, and suggestion.
Finally, the physical and abnormal conditions that increase a person's
susceptibility to imitation, induction, and suggestion should be mentioned.
These include such conditions as somnambulistic states, which encompass
hypnosis or artificial sleep and some of the general neuroses, for example
hysteria, psychasthenia, and others.
Countersuggestion is the condition in which a recipient opposes the
influence being exerted on him by suggestion, which explains the habit
people have of protecting themselves from outside influence. This opposition
sometimes occurs in relation to the influence of deductive reasoning, when a
person disagrees, without foundation, with everything of which he would
otherwise be easily convinced. He is told, for example, that it would be
beneficial for him to take a walk, and he, without sufficient logical basis for
his response, says, "There is no need for me to do that," and so on. This habit
of negativism in word and deed is the result of upbringing and, partially, of
mood.
However, we should keep in mind that although countersuggestion and
logical disagreement are not, in the end, insurmountable obstacles to
suggestion and persuasion, they can in some measure limit the influence of
one or the other.
As for imitation and induction, opposition has no place, or at least is not
obviously apparent, in these processes. As a matter of fact, this observation
largely holds true only in relation to imitation in the sense of the mimicry of
actions or words, and not in the broader understanding that Tarde discussed
in his sociological treatise, which included all processes of sympathy,
suggestion, and imitation in general.
Under normal conditions it is not always easy to separate suggestion and
persuasion, since both processes of influence are often put to use at the same
time, in the same situation. We can only say that in one case we are dealing
with direct induction upon another individual and, consequently, the
provocation of certain conditions, actions, or statements due to the speech or
gestures of another person, whereas in the other case we are dealing with the
provocation of actions or statements in an individual by means of reasoning
or of so-called logical assumptions, which support the necessity or usefulness
of the given actions or statements at the present time. These actions, etc., can
only be brought about if the person being influenced, after weighing the
corresponding pros and cons, agrees with the reasoning of the influencer, i.e.,
acknowledges the necessity and usefulness of the suggested actions under the
given circumstances and makes them the governing principles of his conduct.
In view of this, it is clear that logical influence or persuasion through
reasoning requires time, since it is accompanied by criticism, while
suggestion is imparted directly, and therefore rapidly; however, the former
always corresponds to the interests of the individual who carries out the
suggestion, whereas the latter gives the interests of the recipient no
consideration.
The fact that imitation is the basis of community was already suggested by
Tarde. The study of speech, the first and basic weapon of community, shows
that imitation plays such a great role that without it the very development of
speech would be impossible. Likewise, upbringing is based in large part on
imitation. The latter also allows humans to adopt societal conventions and
customs. Thanks only to imitation does mankind become a social creature in
the true meaning of the word.
On the other hand, aside from imitation it is necessary to keep in mind
suggestion, i.e., the propensity to directly obey verbal influence. Here, we are
dealing with mutual influence, by means of the word, of one group of people
on others, in a form of suggestion that can be called social suggestion.
Already, when being taught at school we encounter suggestion at every step.
When a person enters one group, one circle or other, he immediately receives
suggestions concerning external conditions of society, customs, and
traditions, and involuntarily follows them.
The process of persuasion, which leads to learning, acts on collectives in
the same manner as on individuals. When the leader of a crowd explains an
event to it, he is not necessarily acting solely through suggestion. A certain
portion of the influence in such cases must be attributed to persuasion, which
is also an important factor in the unification of the neuropsychic activity of
masses of people. In terms of the significance of persuasion as a unifying
factor, the fact that at school everyone is taught, and hopefully learns, the
same things is especially important, and helps to level the classes of a given
society.
The unification of members of any social group is accomplished with the
help of the aforementioned factors, based on induction, imitation, suggestion,
and learning (as a result of persuasion). The external impulses that lead to
unification are, however, predominantly class and professional conditions
that pull an individual into a given circle of habits and customs. These
conditions, as was already stated, are the determinants of many external
characteristics of one person or another, depending on the group of
individuals or social class he belongs to.
In every social group there are certain rules, customs, and traditions that
are usually adopted by every new member relatively quickly, thanks to the
aforementioned factors that often act in a manner imperceptible to most
individuals; however, as soon as a person transfers from one group to
another, for whatever reason, he realizes to what extent the conventions and
customs of the former group are a part of him, and sees that it will take some
time to become accustomed to the conditions of the group which he is
entering.
Here, too, the law of similarity is at work. Upon the unification of people,
the associative activity of separate individuals has to manifest itself in
correspondence with everyone else's and consequently, when it finds itself
under new conditions, must regain resemblance to fellow members of the
new association.
However, the above facts do not make up the law of similarity in its
entirety, since we know of cases in which there is no evidence of
communication among nations whatsoever, and so there can be no question
of imitation or borrowing between them; nevertheless, we can observe
similarities between some of their social phenomena. Examples of this have
been given already, including similar forms of worship in the Old and New
Worlds, the resemblance between myths of very different nations, etc.
It is clear that in the above cases identical causes lead to identical effects,
which conclusion satisfies the basic principle of the law of similarity.
Notes
1 Vierkandt, Naturvölker u. Kulturvölker, Lamprecht, Moderne Geschichtwissensch, et al.
2 Today's Tartu University in Estonia (translator's note).
7
The Law of Periodicity or Rhythm
The omnipresence of oscillation is well known in physics. In essence, no
form of energy exists without oscillating motion. The movement of celestial
bodies, like the movement of atoms and molecules, has its own cycles;
organic nature likewise displays cyclic functions such as growth and
reproduction. The vascular, respiratory, and metabolic systems, as well as
sleep and wakefulness, are arranged in phases and cycles.
All processes of collective activity are subject to periodicity. Change of
mood, stimulation, depression (as a result of fatigue), and other analogous
phenomena characterize this periodicity in manifestations of the associative
activity of separate personalities. Even the talent of prominent figures is
subject to periods of development, prosperity, and decline, and consequently
has its own cycle as an expression of a rhythmical form of movement. In the
very activity of gifted individuals can be observed periodicity, which is
supported by a detailed analysis of their life.
Similarly, a political or social environment displays cyclicity in its
development and movements. First and foremost, any collective undergoes its
origin, development, then stages of prosperity and decline. Even such a
collective as a national organism, strengthened by a state system, has phases
of development, during which it increases its power, reaches its apogee in
relation to power and prosperity, after which it gradually enters a period of
decline and decay. Thus, one form of state system is outdated over the
centuries, after which a nation begins to live a new state life—which will also
be subject to growth, prosperity, and decline.
In essence, any state that is subject to the law of rhythm undergoes a period
of primary development, then of prosperity, succeeded by a period of decline,
and then, in a renewed form, the state can once again display its energy and
power, attain new prosperity, decline, etc. In this case, the state's military
power is also subject to the same law of development, predominance, and
subsequent weakening.
Even political regimes are subject to periodic fluctuation, since
government reaction is usually replaced by liberal reforms, followed again by
reaction and then by governmental liberalism. It can even be said that any
political measure, brought to its extreme, prepares its own downfall by
inciting an opposing reaction in society.
However, not only a state organism, but any civilization has its periods of
development, prosperity, and decline. In this respect, I will quote G. Le Bon's
work The Psychology of Nations and Masses (pp. 327-29), in which he
characterizes the cycles that the ancient civilizations experienced.
At the dawn of these civilizations we see a handful of people, of varied
descent, who have joined together thanks to chance emigration, invasion,
and conquest. The partial acknowledgement of a single chief is the
unifying factor between these people, who otherwise differ from each
other in language, religion, and origin.. .Gradually, time has its natural
effect. The sameness of the environment, the continual interbreeding, the
demands of social life eventually leave their mark, and the agglomeration
of different individuals begins melding together to form a race, i.e., an
aggregate which has common features and feelings that are fixed more and
more through heredity. A crowd becomes a nation, one which nonetheless
cannot immediately escape from barbarism. Usually, it will rid itself of
barbarity only when, after much effort, constant conflict, and countless
failures, it attains an ideal. The nature of this ideal is of small importance;
it could be the cult of Rome, Athens, or Allah, it could be anything, but
this ideal is enough to create unanimity of thought and sensibility between
all individuals of a race. Only then, can a new civilization be born, with all
its institutions, beliefs, and arts. Carried away by its dreams, a race
acquires all that gives it brilliance, power, and majesty... Having
completed its constructive work, time inevitably begins the process of
destruction, which can be evaded by neither gods nor people. Once a
civilization has attained acertain level of power and complexity, it stops
growing and is condemned to decline. Soon its hour of senility comes,
drawing closer as the ideal that upheld the spirit of the race is weakened.
As the ideal fades, the edifices of politics, society, and religion supported
by it begin to totter.
The final surrender of the ideal causes the race to lose its spirit; it
degrades into a handful of isolated individuals and reverts to what it was at
the very outset of its existence, a crowd.. .Civilization loses its solidarity
and finds itself subject to the sway of every chance occurrence. Rabble
gains power, barbarity arises. A civilization may still seem brilliant,
because its external façade still stands, but in reality the building behind
the façade is undermined, has nothing to uphold it, and collapses in the
first storm . . .To go from barbarity to civilization, by following a dream,
and then to gradually weaken and die as that dream is lost—that is the life
cycle of every nation.
It would have been better to say "the life cycle of every civilization." After
the fall of a civilization, new conditions of a political nature generate a new
culture that carries new ideals, often originates at another point on the globe,
and comes to dominate the nations of the old civilization.
A civilization that has already undergone periods of development and
prosperity, and is at the stage of decline in one part of the world, is often
adopted by other nations and revitalized, though on a different basis and with
a different nature, under the influence of new impulses. From what was said,
it is clear that just as there are encouraging or revitalizing influences in
collective manifestations, there are also inhibiting influences, and these
dominate consecutively, determining the character of the periodicity of social
phenomena.
Even the energy of separate nations is subject to the law of rhythm. After
being manifested in one direction, it weakens while trying to proceed in
another direction.
It is clear that, where in the physical world we have wavelike and
oscillating motion in the form of rhythm, in the world of collective entities
we likewise have evolution through wavelike motion. Any society goes from
stagnation to activity and, conversely, from exaltation to downfall. Observe
the history of any nation, and you will be convinced of the truth of this
statement. In any collective in general, a conservative government is replaced
by a more liberal one, which is, in turn, replaced by another conservative
government; interest in an innovation is displaced by reversion to the past, to
custom, indeed eventually any innovation itself becomes customary or
ancient.
Inhibition plays a certain role in this process of transition from progress to
regress and vice versa. The manifestation of a single trend eventually leads to
a lack of social interest in that trend, which internally inhibits the process of
its social development and forces society to take another, opposite direction.
Once a direction has been taken by society, it is brought, in accordance to the
law of inertia, to those extremes that stimulate opposition and protest in
significant sectors of the population, and in the end create a shift in social
opinion, inducing social movement to proceed in an opposite direction.
As is known, the sociologist Tarde clarified in detail the role of imitation
as an important reflexological element in the life of societies. However, the
objects of imitation are creative acts that are usually attributed to separate
individuals, although we should not forget the existence of collective, or
national, creativity. In this way, the life of society is regulated, on the one
hand, by creativity or discoveries and inventions, and on the other by
imitation; social movement is accompanied by uplift during a period of
discovery and invention and by stagnation during the periods following the
discoveries. During this time, primarily imitational social labor develops and
facts requiring explanation and synthesis accumulate. At some point during
this accumulation, a great mind appears that makes the corresponding
generalization, and a discovery arises once again, stimulating social uplift.
Social and scientific thinking moves in this wavelike manner, as do all areas
of human creative activity.
Any more or less significant social movement develops along an ascending
line, reaches its apogee, and then wanes to the point of extinction, after which
a new social movement arises, most often born of the old one, but having a
new form which, in its turn, develops on an ascending line, reaches an
apogee, and wanes, which is characteristic of the periodicity of social
movements. And so it is clear that social movements never follow a straight
line, but always travel in waves, one replacing another. The increased energy
at the beginning of any social movement is exchanged for its subsequent
decline, after which the same movement can be revitalized. An uplift in social
mood is usually replaced by depression. The aspirations of society in one
direction, meeting with unfavorable or unwanted phenomena, cause social
change, after which society strives in a different direction.
Thus, the revolution in Russia reached extreme bias towards Bolshevism
during the uprising in Petrograd in the beginning of July 1917, causing a
break in the social movement and creating a counterrevolution without
having achieved its goal; but the counterrevolution, in its turn, led the social
movement to support Bolshevism once more.
It should be kept in mind that in revolutionary periods the waves of social
mood flow much more turbulently than under normal conditions. Usually, a
revolution explodes when the oppression of the people reaches its pinnacle,
and so it is all the more violent if it is intended to free the people from
slavery. Everything that is connected in one way or another to the old order is
destroyed or abused.
Let us take, for example, relations with the church and religion. As the
servant and bulwark of the monarchy, during revolutionary coups religion is
subject to abuse and degradation, which increase as the revolutionary
movement develops, once again as stipulated by the law of periodicity. To
convey at least a fraction of the hate that was displayed during the Great
French Revolution towards the objects of Catholicism, we will list here some
of the more characteristic events of 1789, involving so-called iconoclasty.
We will speak neither of the purely barbaric destruction of bell-towers,
monumental church fences, tombs, holy vessels, nor of the utterly pointless
burning of fabric and lace. However, we will pause to cast our eye on scenes
that are even more vivid in this respect.
Starting in August of 1789, fires sprang up across all of France on which,
to the accompaniment of the wild screams and dances of drunken rabble,
thousands of priceless objects were burned: marble tables, fireplaces,
mirrors, windows, stained glass, statues, carved chairs, and suchlike.
