"Igniters" of castles did not even spare the ancient trees growing nearby;
all were cut down and burned on the spot.
Iconoclasts attacked not only images of "former" angels, "former" Christs,
and "former" saints, but also canopies, gonfalons, candlesticks, lamps,
cups, vessels, bowls, and all the decorations used in customs of the
"former" Catholic cult. Local workers were forced to aid the "cause"; they
were made to abandon their work in order to plunder the lands of the
church and nobility.
Thus, here we are dealing with the rage of a frustrated people against the
products of human labor and talent generated over many centuries.
Analogous phenomena during revolutionary periods can be observed in
relation to art. It must be said that one of the more saddening circumstances
of national upheaval is the vandalism of art, when valuable masterpieces are
destroyed to satisfy no real need; many treasured objects have been destroyed
in this manner in the bonfires of revolutions. Does this mean that leaders of
revolutions direct the people against art? No, emphatically not. It is known,
for example, what efforts the Convention made to revive art and in general
give the masses all that had been the sole privilege of the wealthy, holding
grandiose public ceremonies to this end, and yet all this time the people
continued to vandalize objets d'art. During the Russian Revolution, the
Bolsheviks strove to give the proletariat everything that had been the
exclusive prerogative of the upper class, establishing the actual and legal rule
of the proletarian classes and poorest peasants. Some of the latter's leaders
gave lectures on the importance of art, created new museums from former
palaces; but was it not at this time that palace treasures were subject to the
most destruction and plunder? This astonishing vandalism can be explained
by the underdeveloped nature of the people, their lack of proper upbringing
and education. However, this state of affairs does not fully explain the
pointless destruction of art which could have been spared with out detriment
to the revolution. It is clear that the reason for the vandalism lies in
something else: and that is the indifference, even hatred, towards all that was
connected with the aristocracy expressed by the people's thoughtless
pilfering. When a nation is held in the chains of slavery and these chains are
suddenly broken, the nation, left to itself, gives full rein to its instincts, and
here national hatred towards all reminders of the past order destroys
everything, good or evil. The heavier the chains of slavery were, the more
powerfully is national vandalism manifested in times of upheaval.
This is related to another situation.
In all periods of their history, nations have striven to represent their
political and religious/moral beliefs as certain symbols, which are reflected in
art. However, as these beliefs begin to wane or disappear, a nation turns
against the symbols that it previously accepted with a fury whose strength
depends on the degree to which a nation's new political convictions oppose
its former ones. This is why everything that reminds a nation of the preceding
structure of its life, or represents in symbolic images its previous barbarity,
falls victim to its blind hatred. And so, the French Revolution of the
Convention's time was marked by a decree on the destruction of all royal
statues, tombs, and burial-vaults, and during the Bolshevist period of the
Russian Revolution, two months later, a decree was made on the public
removal of statues that offended national sensibilities. The results of these
actions are widely known and there is no need to go over them here. They
can be found in the very history of such periods of social upheavals.
In a word, we also meet with the law of rhythm in consecutive events, in
the sense that one social movement is replaced by another, opposing one. To
a certain extent, a correlation between the power of a social movement and
the following declination in another direction does exist. Thus, the greater the
social uplift in one direction, the greater its consequent fall. Compliance with
this law is even manifested in the greater or lesser acuteness of a given social
movement. In other words, the more turbulent an uplift's development, the
more speedy and abrupt is the consequent decline, and, conversely, the more
gradual the uplift, the slower the following decline.
In political movements, the law of rhythm is manifested in the fact that the
greater the power possessed by political movement of a given orientation, the
stronger the reaction towards it. The excessive oppressiveness of the tsarist
regime in Russia led, in the end, to Bol shevism, which was foreseeable even
at the beginning of the revolution, since on the twentieth and twenty-first of
February cries of "down with Milyukov," and "down with the Provisional
Government" could be heard in the streets of Petrograd.
As a matter of fact, periods of revival, which recur from time to time in the
history of a nation, are classic demonstrations of the periodicity of social
phenomena. Although it might seem that certain nations and their
accompanying cultural valuables have long disappeared, and that even the
inheritors of their culture have become the legacy of history or even
archaeology, that they have gone beyond the reach of the contemporary
world, a period of revival nonetheless comes, during which they are once
more the center of social attention and imitation, having been reproduced
under new conditions.
It must be noted that the same pattern can be observed in other
manifestations of national life. Thus, society may experience a burst of
nationalism, followed by indifference to national issues and even by the
burning of nationalist literature; it may swing from a period of materialism to
idealism and vice versa, sometimes pausing on the middle course. Every
scientific profession undergoes the same wavelike motion in its development,
which is characterized by various trends during different periods.
In general, all manifestations of the associative activity of any collective
are subject to the law of rhythm. Thus, it is first of all important to establish
that the mood of the collective experiences periodical shifts in one direction
or another, which are determined not only by external conditions which could
be favorable or unfavorable to the collective, but also by the instability of the
mood itself, which cannot remain unchanged for long periods of time.
Usually, one social mood is replaced by a calmer or opposing mood, which is
then subject to a similar change, etc., examples of which were put forward in
the first part of this work.
The mood of national masses is not an exception to the general rule.
Today's hero is made the idol of the crowd tomorrow, but the day after
tomorrow the same crowd is indifferent, or even hostile, towards him. So
many former idols have undergone a period of national enthusiasm and
fascination displayed at the simple mention of their name, and ended
ignominiously with national indifference, oblivion, or even degradation and
accusation. Such was the fate of Hannibal, of Julius Caesar, of Napoleon I.
Even the fate of the most recent hero of the Russian Revolution, Kerensky,
may in time prove to be the same. Only the impartial court of history gives
every personality that plays a part in the fate of nations its place, evaluating
its positive features without concealing its negative aspects.
The fluctuation of social mood can occur quickly or slowly, depending on
the period of social life. In revolutionary periods, change of mood occurs
with kaleidoscopic speed. Displeasure with one political regime leads to
rebellion; the conditions of the resulting new regime do not usually appease
the people for long, and soon the voices of dissenters rise once again,
attracting more and more supporters, preparing a retro-insurrection.
In the evolutionary process, we have, aside from the alternation of
opposing social moods, the transfer from one trend in the creative process to
another. It is likewise widely known that, after a turbulent manifestation of
social life in the form of a revolution, there is often a subsequent process of
social reaction.
But even outside these periods of national indignation, every nation goes
from vitality to stagnation, after which, under the influence of some stimulus,
national life is revived and steps are taken towards progress; however,
stagnation eventually takes over again.
Any doctrine in general, after developing, reaches dominance and finally is
subject to transformation. The new doctrine that replaces it is doomed to the
same fate; it must blossom, then wither away and undergo a metamorphosis.
Neither literature nor philosophy can evade a similar displacement of one
trend by another: romanticism is exchanged for naturalism, after which
comes mysticism, then again naturalism, etc.
Any social progress in intellectual life experiences the same fluctuations,
flowing now quickly, now slowly, going from stagnation to revival and so on.
Such is the progress of literature and science. Art moves in much the same
manner, with one trend replacing another, after which the new trend, having
completed its cycle of development, is replaced by a different, often opposite
one that has been previously experienced but has acquired new
characteristics, corresponding to the times and to the progress achieved in art.
Even religion does not escape a similar fate.
Any belief undergoes a cycle of birth, prosperity, and decline, eventually
clearing the way for religious quests of a different nature to arise.
Primitive Christianity had its own period of development and prosperity,
attained in the third century. However, when it took the Emperor Constantine
to its bosom, granting him all its property and privileges, the pure
Christianity of yore signed its own death sentence. Soon, it became a state
religion, gaining special significance in state life and receiving a powerful
impulse to its popularization. In this new form, Christianity reached the
pinnacle of its development in Europe during the Middle Ages, when it
acquired control of state life, and even kings were forced to worship at
Canossa. Later, however, state power triumphed over Christianity, and
gradually, with the evolution of state political life, it began to take on a more
and more secondary role; with the development of science, Christianity is
gradually losing its influence on the minds of the people.
If we turn to economy, we meet with the same cycles. Every nation
develops past a stage of fledgling industry and reaches its economic peak,
after which, thanks to competition or new conditions, it loses what it had
attained at the time of its prosperity. Every branch of industry, every
undertaking suffers the same fate. Some invention or other stimulates a
certain industry, enabling it to reach its full development; but then a new
invention appears and the enterprise falls into decline, forfeiting its place to
new industry.
In this way, the manifestations of collective activity, and likewise the
manifestations of individual life, are subject to the law of rhythm, which
consequently has universal significance.
From the above, it is clear that the social process follows a wavelike path.
After the tsar's regime, after proclamations of war until death or victory, the
Russian Revolution, as a reaction against the tsarist regime and proclamations
of war, brought Bolshevism about very quickly; the latter should, in the not-
too-distant future, lead to an inclination to the right, which may be expressed
as a dictatorship. In the way that tsarism brought itself to the extremes of
despotism during the war and thus destroyed itself, so Bolshevism may
eliminate itself through careless and excessive reform. Only a gradually
agitated social sea can be calmed through the slow diminution of its waves,
until finally it enters quiet shores. This is why individuals and parties taking
the middle line in turbulent times always lose. To become accustomed to
their environment, they must be as changeable as kaleidoscope figures.
"The uprising of the crowd," says G. Le Bon (The Psychology of
Socialism, p. 133),
is a matter of minute-long fury, and under the renewed influence of its
conservative inclinations the crowd soon returns to its former views and
demands the reinstatement of those ideals that it destroyed in a fit of
anger. Our history of the past hundred years repeats this to us on its every
page. A revolution, for example, barely completes its destructive acts, and
already almost all the things that it overthrew, be they religious or political
institutions, are reinstated under a new name.
A good example in this respect is the Russian Revolution. As is known,
mutiny in the troops began with the murder of officers, most of whom
consequently had no choice but to flee; they were degraded and humiliated,
even in uniform they were reduced to the level of simple soldiers, while in
some places they were forced to be cooks. However, with the advance of the
Germans after the Brest peace, in view of the shortage of regular officers in
the Red Army, they were once more called to military activity.
In the course of social evolution, the social process, having begun with a
certain phenomenon, generally becomes complicated by new ingredients and
then returns to a simplicity analogous to the original phenomenon. This fact
can be illustrated by a number of examples.
However, I will outline only one: that of the church. Initially, the church
represented an institution free from state control in the form of a small
commune; then it became closely connected to the state, as a part of its
government; finally, it once again distanced itself from the nation, regaining
its independence and becoming a hierarchically organized institution,
acknowledged by the government and state as a free and self-contained
establishment.1
Note
1 We do not undertake to discuss whether we are dealing with the universal dissemination of energy or
its accumulation under certain conditions, e.g., with the formation of stars.
8
The Law of Inertia
It is important to acknowledge the law of inertia among the other laws
affecting collective life, since it is universal for all nature. Every social trend
is subject to this law, which causes any movement, once it has developed, to
tend to be popularized across the whole collective, attracting more and more
individuals in the manner of a snowball that grows unlimitedly as it rolls
through snow.
"Society could not exist, could not make a step forward without the
priceless treasures known as routine, simian imitation, and totally ovine
herdability that are constantly increasing with every new generation" (Tarde,
The Laws of Imitation, p. 76).
Even conservatism is a manifestation of the law of inertia, as is any routine
in general.
As is known, a crowd is especially conservative. In the words of Le Bon,
(The Psychology of Nations and Masses, p. 191):
The changeability of a crowd is expressed only on a superficial level; in
essence, the crowd's conservative instincts are still active, and are as
indestructible as any human's. The crowd feels a holy respect for tradition
and an unconscious, deep terror of any innovation that has the potential to
alter the conditions of its existence.
The most turbulent revolutions often result only in the re-christening of
time-honored institutions.
The ideas of any society always lag behind those of its scientists and
philosophers by several generations.
A vivid example of the law of inertia is the reinstatement of the Catholic
Church due to popular demand after it had been abolished during the French
Revolution. Here is the speech of a former member of the Convention,
Fourcroix, cited by Taine:
Widespread celebration of the Sabbath, and likewise attendance of church,
indicates that a mass of French people wish to return to former customs,
and this is not a time to oppose the national tendency or disposition.. .The
majority of people displays a need for religion, ritual, and priests. The
mistaken belief of some contemporary philosophers, which I also shared,
was that education, if it attained sufficient popularization throughout the
nation, could destroy religious prejudices. But these prejudices serve as a
source of solace for a huge number of unfortunates, and consequently
priests, altars, and ritual must be left to the national masses.
Try to pass a radical reform in any environment, and you will see how
great the conservatism of people is, since, in the end, the reform will not be
passed due to that very conservatism. Why do great revolutions rarely
achieve their objectives? Once again because the conservatism of the masses
cannot be overcome.
On the other hand, a social movement that has already commenced tends to
experience further development.
Even such artificially created obstacles as persecution can only detain the
movement for a short time, if at all, since a social movement that is deeply
rooted in the external or internal conditions of social life continues to fix
itself in people's minds notwithstanding persecution, and even its sacrifices
are beneficial since they give it an aura of martyrdom. If the movement is for
some reason manifested too early, it can temporarily be vanquished through
inhibiting influences; still, eventually it is revived under more favorable
conditions.
Any social movement, once developed, continues to develop in its original
direction, provided the conditions are favorable.
If this does not occur, and one social movement or other is terminated or
temporarily arrested, then this depends on the internal or external inhibiting
conditions of the environment in which it develops. If, on the other hand, the
direction of the social movement changes, then this is once again dependent
on external phenomena.
The law of inertia leads to the fact that any action that was once performed
is reproduced upon repetition of the original stimulus, leading to the
development of habit and practice.
We are dealing, in the end, with collective reproductive reflexes that are
manifested with greater and greater ease upon repetition. This results in social
habits and tendencies that are based on the imitation of elders by youth and of
older members of a given association by newer elements. Every generation
more or less follows the path of the previous generation, conserving the
latter's institutions and customs, and only new living conditions can lead to
the innovations that mark a new generation.
It goes without saying that the law of inertia acts more powerfully in places
where living conditions lead to national isolation and to the greater unity of
the old and the young, which can be observed, for example, in China, which
is geographically separated from other nations, as well as in the lifestyle of all
nomadic peoples in general. This results in the unusual conservatism of such
nations, which retain for centuries their patriarchal lifestyle and the
corresponding tenor of life; this structure has the same form now as it did at
the dawn of civilization.
Conversely, warlike peoples that often collide with other nations during
periods of conquest are marked by progressiveness, although there are other
factors at work here such as competition with neighbors in everything related
to progress, without which conflict could not be successfully resolved.
The reasons for many customs and habits are lost in the mists of antiquity,
and at the present time even their significance cannot be discovered;
nonetheless, they are recreated from generation to generation through
tradition or routine. Take, for example, fortune-telling: who can say with
certainty why it takes place on Christmas Eve or at a christening, rather than
at Easter?
Likewise, various contemporary establishments are encumbered with a
large number of pointless ceremonies whose origin is murky or unknown, but
which are nonetheless executed in corresponding situations. Finally, many
useless social establishments, including various forms of etiquette, are upheld
in accordance with the law of inertia that lies at the base of social
conservatism.
The suspension of one social movement or another does not depend on its
own loss of strength for no apparent reason, but follows either the desertion
of the movement by its supporters due to the conditions of the time, or the
combination of certain events, or even the temporary inhibition of the
movement itself after it meets opposition in one respect or another.
This is best proved through example.
Take Christianity, for instance. Appearing as a reaction to the crude,
materialistic views of paganism, it attracted more and more people and
reached its acme during the middle ages, when it triumphed completely over
paganism. If, with the passage of time, Christianity has ceased to occupy its
former social role, then this is explained by the new structure of European
social life that came with the development of industry and science. The same
can be observed in other social movements.
Thanks to the above law, any social movement, once developed, tends to
continue in its original direction. This forms a kind of routine in social
activity. In connection with this, the social environment is generally marked
by widespread conservatism. It seems that the less educated a society is, the
greater its conservatism. From this stems the retention of customs, beliefs,
and traditions which have long ago lost their significance.
Any innovation comes into existence only when the corresponding
environment is prepared for it, and when the old structure has already lived—
or outlived—its time. So many laws have remained on paper only because
society was not properly prepared for them. On the other hand, the forceful
introduction of something into social life always meets corresponding
opposition and resistance.
It suffices to remember the potato rebellions in Russia, the opposition to
smallpox vaccination and to the disinfection of communities during cholera
epidemics, etc., in order to gain a clear concept of the latter, forceful,
introduction.
Even the use of steel instead of goose quills stimulated universal
opposition, and only gradually did it acquire popularity.
Finally, tradition and habit in social life can also be traced to the same law
of inertia.
Does not prestige or authority in the eyes of the crowd serve, in its turn, as
an example of the law of inertia? Everyone knows that authority, even after it
is completely exhausted, still has the power to subject the masses, which
follow it due to inertia, finding themselves fascinated.
On the other hand, if a shift in a certain direction occurs in society, it
usually has the tendency to progress further than necessary, and the more
turbulently the shift occurs, the greater the tendency outlined above.
Any social movement, once bom and rooted in social life, continues to
develop, if the conditions are favorable, until it exhausts all available
material; unless it is opposed through whatever measures, it will develop
further until it finally loses its social raison d'être.
In this way, a social movement always grows to a certain limit, after which
it wanes, gradually dying due to lack of supporting material. During this
process, a movement will usually develop to its greatest extent, if it is not
inhibited.
At this point, the movement meets with opposition from groups that have
joined together for a rebuttal, and undergoes its crisis. Thus, a revolution that
has reached its furthest extent usually meets with repulsion from a
counterrevolution.
However, thanks to the previously outlined law, a movement can go
outside the limits where it should have stopped in its wavelike progression.
Thus, the contemporary Russian Revolution, instead of being all-
encompassing, as it was in the very beginning, was subject to a strong bias
towards the rule of the proletariat and became, in essence, party-oriented,
answering only to the interests of the proletarian class.
As is known, the Revolution of 1905 likewise resorted to expropriation and
bomb-throwing that, in turn, stimulated a reaction in most of Russian society,
which was horrified by these wild forms of revolutionary movement. After
the Revolution of 1905, when the period of reaction reached its peak, the
inclination of society toward the right once again attained such heights that it
far from corresponded to the existing correlation of social forces.
For the above reason, intermediate groups hold predominant influence in
times of social stagnation, and lose all their influence during national
upheaval. The first situation is explained by the fact that the masses, if
manifesting discontent, are disposed to pay attention to promises of better
living conditions but do not initially follow extremist movements to the same
extent as moderate ones, since the former seem Utopian and their propaganda
is made difficult by social stagnation and reaction. Because of this, during
periods of national transition or change the first to make history are the more
moderate parties; however, when the real revolution begins and the
conditions for propaganda are more favorable, extremist movements take the
upper hand thanks to the fact that they give greater satisfaction to the
demands of the nation by acting decisively and consequently opening wider
horizons to the future. Finally, they are once again in opposition to moderate
currents and win over their followers.
This can be compared to the movement of a cart. In order to start it on its
way, the efforts of the wheel-horse are most important, and the traces play
only a secondary role. However, when the cart begins moving, the action of
the traces give it such momentum as could not have developed with the help
of the wheel-horse alone.
Moreover, it should not be forgotten that national movements are
frequently joined by the most conservative elements, which previously
constituted stagnant water in the social bog, and consequently strive to pull
themselves out of obscurity by enlisting in the most advanced, i.e., more
extreme, groups.
Meanwhile, the more moderate elements, accustomed to obeying their
leaders, remain loyal to their slogans and resist the social movement. In the
end, these moderate currents lose ground in the large, agitated mass, they are
no longer heeded, and gradually they are abandoned by their followers who
perceive the success of others. Because of this, they enter into agreements
with more radical parties in order to save their reputation, are nonetheless
forced out of the social scene while taunted and pursued by extremist
currents, and often die. Such is the fate of all moderate trends under
conditions of national upheaval. An example could be the fate of the
Girondins at the time of the Great French Revolution or the downfall of the
Russian Cadets during the Bolshevist coup.
It is clear that the law of inertia acts here in two ways. Thanks to it, some
elements lag behind, remaining in one place; others, caught up in the flow of
a movement, climb further and further, reaching the most remote slopes of
social change.
The same law of inertia is responsible for the fact that an established power
remains long after it loses public sympathy and support. It is likewise known
that panic continues for some time after there is no longer a basis for its
existence. Under the influence of a success on the battlefield, troops advance
further than originally planned, and often meet with unforeseen opposition.
When a national movement is growing, it opens new horizons with every
victory, as a result of which the demands of its leaders become greater and
greater. The power opposing the movement, however, takes into account only
recent results and in order to protect itself tries to make as few concessions as
possible, while its actions are guided by reactions to current developments
rather than by forecasts of what is to come. From this stems the common
phenomenon of government concessions coming too late; this circumstance
always aids strongly rooted movements, since they find in belated and
insufficient concessions new stimulus to attack and promote their demands.
Then, when the victor has been established as a given side or party, the latter
usually abuses its position and more or less completely ignores the interests
of the conquered side, in this way giving reason for the growth displeasure
and opposition, or even open indignation and revolt.
Let us take an example from the Russian Revolution. According to the
central organ of the Russian Socio-Democratic Labour Party, its Labour
Newspaper, Commander-in-Chief Kornilov's so-called "mutiny" was brought
on by the mistakes of the revolution. This is what we read on the subject in
Den' (31 August 1917):
The central organ of the Russian Socio-Democratic Labour Party was
brave enough to openly declare, at a time when the mutiny instigated by
Gen. Kornilov was still not liquidated, that the mutiny was prepared, to
some extent, by the mistakes of the revolutionary democracy. The neglect
of national defence, the concessionary attitude towards anarchic groups
and slogans, the policy of isolation of the proletariat from the progressive,
wealthy classes, which separated these classes from the revolution with a
speed that corresponded neither to their interests nor to those of the
democracy itself—all this taken together laid the groundwork for an
eruption of counter-revolutionary forces, in this case in the form of
Kornilov's march on Petrograd.
Compromise with revolutionary adventurism and obstinacy towards the
wealthy classes were equally provoking with respect to a counter-
revolution, and from the unfortunate July Days onward we progressed
irreversibly towards reactionary coup.
Fortunately for Russia, the latter took the form of the badly executed
Komilovsk adventure. However, it would be a grave mistake to think that
the eradication of a badly executed adventure is also the elimination of a
counter-revolution.
It is enough for general living conditions to worsen, and dissatisfaction
with the heads of a nation immediately develops, even if the worsening is not
the fault of the governing party.
Whenever general discontent arises, it first of all takes the form of a protest
against the prominent figures who are assumed to be responsible for the
misfortunes of the people. This is the case during all revolts caused by
starvation.
This is why a kind of instinctive aspiration to create national, rather than
party, government arises during terrible national ordeals, in the hope that all
the elements of society will be represented and that the crisis will
consequently be properly addressed.
This is why it became necessary to create coalitional ministries in such
parliamentary countries as France and England at the time of the Great War.
In Russia, this was echoed in demands for a ministry of trust, and then for
a ministry of responsibility.
Of course, these demands were not met, but the Tsar was punished for his
disregard by the revolution. In the course of the latter, the voices and slogans
of the majority of people once again requested a coalitional ministry made up
of representatives of socialism and the bourgeoisie, in view of the fact that
single-party government cannot be responsible at such important moments in
history.
Finally, the law of inertia is found in the fact that once the unity of a
collective has been attained, it is upheld solely through internal discipline, in
the manner of training; however, it exists only until some stimulus is able to
push the collective out of its precarious balance and thus make it fall apart or
become disorganized.
Likewise dependent on the law of inertia is the impossibility of the
immediate termination of a movement that has just begun. Even forceful
suppression does not suddenly, completely liquidate a movement, which is
most often only temporarily inhibited, and in due course it once again
manifests itself with former or even greater power—provided, of course, that
it is not redirected. Thus, the first Russian Revolution of 1905, extinguished
by the heavy heel of reaction, flared up with even greater strength in 1917
and swept away in no time at all the power that had trod upon it in 1905.
However, inertia is also manifested in another aspect of social life:
A series of socializing acts, repeated many times for one reason or
another, acquire the power of a habit that is subsequently maintained only
out of inertia, independent of any other cause or condition. After a certain
number of repetitions of mutual influence, collective unity that has already
emerged and has existed for a certain time continues its existence through
inertia, up until the moment when a given force creates a new condition
that renews the connection between interacting units. The inertial force
resulting from the repetition of a series of actions is well known. It
suffices to say that the multiple and monotonous mechanical reaction to
one stimulus or another causes that reaction to be spontaneously
reproduced when the corresponding stimulus is given. In other words,
repetition creates habit and habit creates routines of action, which
spontaneously force us to act in agreement with these routines. In this
respect, habit—in the literal sense of the word—can be called biological
inertia.
There are countless examples in social life in which habit/inertia plays a
leading role. Such examples include the preservation of marital unions when
they have lost any real unifying basis; the sus-tainment of trade ties with
certain individuals when thousands of trade correlations with others exist; the
continued participation in one circle, party, society, organization or other
after there is no longer any agreement with their views. Accustomization to
executions, shootings, and similar occurrences should be attributed to social
inertia: although unusual punishment may at first stimulate a sharp social
reaction, with time the attitude towards it becomes indifferent, as that towards
any ordinary occurrence.
In accordance with the law of inertia, when the authority of a separate
individual has begun to grow in the eyes of a crowd, it continues to do so
regardless of subsequent events, and sometimes remains even when the merit
of the individual has long faded away and no further services have been
executed by him or will be in the foreseeable future.
This is not difficult to observe in any context, but it is especially obvious in
the army, where one success or another quickly exalts the originator of the
triumph in the eyes of the crowd; this person's popularity grows until it
reaches its peak at a certain level. Caesare, Caesare, morituri te salutant—
that cry embodies the boundless authority of a military leader over the crowd.
The law of inertia has greatly abetted the union of societies, as well as their
homogeneity. Thus, some of the smaller associations, thanks to the features
of their lifestyle that were passed on in accordance with custom from
generation to generation, remained immune to foreign influence and
preserved their collective attributes throughout the centuries. In this way,
social inheritance is one of the manifestations of the law of inertia.
In France, for example, marriages and funerals have long been known to
proceed without the participation of the church, and the French workers
commonly deride anything related to religion; yet according to G. Le Bon
(The Psychology of Socialism, p. 40), marriages without participation of a
representative of the church are very rare among laborers. A marriage-
contract concluded in the city hall seems insufficient. The situation comes
down to a certain persistence of religious beliefs in the face of many efforts to
eradicate faith and religion. The same can be observed during the second
Russian revolution.
Notwithstanding the decree on the separation of the church from the state
and on the permission of marriages in the commissariat, there were very few
who wished to take advantage of such a simplified form of matrimony.
Thanks to the same law of inertia, forms of government, for example old
monarchies, outlive themselves and so collapse like houses of cards with the
first onslaught of a revolutionary movement.
In terms of politics, we can point to England as an example of the
conservation through inertia of the outer shell of a monarchic state structure,
which, in reality, covers a republican form of government.
An analogous example is the Tsar's rule in Russia during the latter days of
the house of Romanov. In the end, it was the embodiment not of the power of
the tsar, but of the handful of rascals who used the organ of the tsar's power
in their own interests, since in Russia true tsarist power did not exist
throughout the reign of Nicholas II.
In the end, inertia is the conservative force that preserves the national and
class peculiarities, as well as the historic features, of popular character.
Tarde categorizes some phenomena explained by inertia as counter-
imitational, but this counter-imitation is, in the end, the product of his
obsession with the alleged universality of the laws of imitation in collective
life. In fact, his counter-imitation ultimately proves that collective life is
subject not only to the law of imitation, since it contains phenomena which
oppose that law, which cannot be classified under it in any form, and which
were classified as counter-imitation in an utterly unscientific manner.
Generally speaking, the collective is much more conservative than the
separate individual. It is more difficult for the former to accept any
innovation, more difficult to force it to turn its back on the old way of life.
All customs, social traditions, leftovers of antiquity, and remnants of the past
in general must be made part of the law of social inertia.
Look at how contemporary society has sustained the most awful ordeals in
a period of war. In view of recent traumatic events, it would seem that social
life should at certain points have been thrown completely off balance; and yet
the public persisted in its former habits of attending the theatre, café-
chantants, cabarets, and other entertainment-oriented establishments as if
nothing had changed, as if everything remained as it always was. To a
detached viewer, the situation gives the repulsive impression of a feast during
a plague. When foreigners expressed dismay upon observing how, at the time
of the unfortunate Tsushima incident, Petersburg filled the theatres and café-
chantants, they were right to do so, since they were temporary visitors to
Petersburg and did not enter its life, and so did not surrender to the law of
inertia; however, to the common citizen, Tsushima meant nothing. The
former was accustomed to visiting during the day, being at the theatre or
café-chantant in the evening, and was not likely to refuse these pleasures,
since tradition was a powerful force in his actions.
Russia underwent even more terrible ordeals in 1917, when the drawn-out
war resulted in the complete disruption of the internal life of the country and
the reduction of food to minimal hunger rations. But what of that? It would
seem that a national uprising was in order. Not so: throughout the festive
days of Christmas, notwithstanding the hunger bread-rations and the difficult
times, preholiday commotion continued in the usual manner.
Here, for example, is a characteristic description of this pre-holi-day bustle
in Moscow, where the bread rations went down to V4 pound per person with
the warning that even the V4 pound could be reduced. The Moscow
correspondent for Nash Vek (31 December 1917) writes on this subject:
I can confirm that Moscow has fulfilled the expectations placed on it and
on its oft-tested qualities; it accepted the reduction in bread rations and the
prediction of their possible transition into a sweet memory with admirable
calmness and even, somehow, indifference. "Why bother with the ration
when we need to worry about getting the goose?" Not so long ago, we
could cry with pathos: the demands of everyday life are not dead; no, they
are very much alive. These demands are vital and tenacious, they hold
tightly to existence. In Moscow, perhaps, they hold tighter than anywhere
within the boundaries of the Soviet republic. And these demands of
everyday life have managed to inject into the current tragic events all the
grandeur of festive occasions, to retain all of the latter's details and
accoutrements.
From the point of view of, say, an expatriate Russian prisoner of war it
would seem that the Great Hood had begun in his country, and he would not
know where to turn to find the ark in which Russia could save itself:
But upon scrutinizing the situation more closely, his surprise would know
no bounds, for against the background of the flood he would see almost
ordinary festive designs. He would be astounded to see that into the flood
have 
squeezed 
bustling 
streets; 
overflowing 
delicatessens 
and
confectionery shops, out of which pour streams of people carrying parcels,
packets, and boxes; gently glittering Christmas tree lights and happy
evening gatherings; posters made of sheets advertising countless theatres,
cabarets, café-chantants, cinemas with repertoires of day and evening
performances.
The situation in Moscow was echoed in Petrograd, and in the latter even
during the most grueling moments of the political situation, posters flashed
by with headlines such as "Entertaining Ball," stipulating that "Ladies should
not forget to invite their partners, nor gentlemen their ladies," and promising
"continual dancing" with the participation of renowned artists, as well as
confetti, serpentines, snowball-fighting, games of "post-office" and other
ostentatious nonsense, not forgetting that the lady who received the most
cards would be presented with a prize in the form of a silver surprise. And all
this was taking place at a time when old Russia was governmentally and
economically degenerating, when Finland had seceded, when whole areas to
the west were breaking away in Germany's interest, when robberies took
place everywhere, when you could not walk down the street in safety for you
risked being stripped of your clothes.
Furthermore, although during a period of conflict with an established
regime there is no lack of blame attributed to the governing powers, when the
opposition is at long last victorious and takes the upper hand it usually
manifests great severity towards the losing side. Only gradually do these
relations smooth over as general calming occurs; however, if afterwards
another overthrow is enacted, it is once again accompanied by greater
repression than was displayed before the coup.
The same occurs in battles between nations. In the beginning, the attacker
suffers significant losses from the resistance of the enemy, but when this
resistance is broken, the victor always rewards himself with a savage reprisal
of the vanquished. Obviously, for the first while a kind of vengeance plays a
role here, determined by the necessity of giving resistance, the greater the
latter, the greater the animosity that is manifested quite vigorously in the
period immediately after the conqueror emerges victorious from battle. But
the law of inertia is also at work in such situations, and causes the animosity
that had developed during conflict to continue to grow after victory when, it
would seem, there should no longer be a place for bitterness since the
conditions suitable to its development have ceased to exist.
Why do wars that do not bring decisive victories end, for the most part, in
mediation? This is because the national enthusiasm of one side knows no
bounds and cannot terminate its aspirations and desires at a certain limit.
However, the same occurs on the opposing side, since the force of inertia is
so great that national masses cannot limit themselves or stop their movement
at a determined point.
On the other hand, does an agitated national sea calm down all that
quickly? Does not a revolutionary war, once developed, continue to its limits,
only if it does not meet with opposition?
An event as important as a war does not end without the kinetic energy of
the participatory nations flowing into another form. In Russia, after the Great
War, this energy was transformed first of all into a revolution, and then into a
civil war. Of course, with adept direction of the national organism a more
expedient channeling of kinetic energy could have been attained. In any case,
here we are dealing with nothing less than the suppression of kinetic energy
in one direction in order to guide it along another course.
Revolutionary movements also know no bounds or limits during their
development, destroying everything to which they take a dislike or which
sabotages them, and overturning, without basis, things that could seemingly
have been left untouched. When one movement or another develops in art, it
once again goes outside the bounds of permissibility. Examples of this can be
futurism in art and poetry, cubism, and other branches of modernism.
New religious doctrines likewise take forms that break the limits of
acceptability. The development of every fashion eventually reaches such a
level that it becomes obviously ugly and is only then replaced by a new
fashion which, in its turn, grows to incredibly strange forms, after which it is
again exchanged for another. Even science, exact science where, it would
seem, there should be no room for such fads, carries one new doctrine or
another to its furthest limits through inertia. This explains the various
extremes in scientific/practical areas of knowledge, for example in medicine,
pedagogy, etc. Thus, everyone knows to what extent went the humoralists,
the representatives of cellular pathology, the various investigations involving
tuberculin as a remedy for consumption, Erlich's medicine for the treatment
of progressive paralysis, etc.
It is clear that the formulas and concepts that govern people's minds were
devised and developed slowly, gradually, through evolution and are equally
slowly losing their significance. And at a time when these formulas are no
longer ultimate truths for educated individuals, they continue to play the role
of governing concepts for the uneducated masses.
The influence of the Greek and Roman worlds in science and art lasted for
many centuries after both languages died; in essence, Europe is still in the
thrall of Greek art, just as various European establishments are under the
influence of the creations of the Roman mind.
When minds are taken over by a new idea, much time and effort is required
to completely crowd it out with another:
Before finally dying, the idea will for a long time make up part of the
legacy of inherited ideas, which are called prejudices, but which we
respect nonetheless. An old idea, even when it is no more than a word, a
sound, a mirage, has incredible power, and is still able to subject us to its
influence. This explains the tenaciousness of our ancient inheritance of
outlived ideas, opinions, and conventions which we awfully accept,
although they would not be able to stand up to the smallest amount of
criticism if we were to analyze them. (G. Le Bon, The Psychology of
Nations, p. 117)
In general, old, tattered ideas hold on to their influence for a long time,
taking the form of a prejudice.
Let us bow to the force of the idea, says Le Bon (ibid., p. 122):
When it reaches a certain period of its development, there are neither
deductions nor proofs that can conquer it. For nations to come out from
under the yoke of some idea or other, either hundreds of years or violent
revolutions, and sometimes both, are necessary. All that mankind has the
power to do is count the chimeras that it has invented for itself, and whose
victim it has subsequently become.
The law of inertia likewise explains belief in authority and in anything that
relates to social convention and habit or to the will and influence of
ancestors, all of which are so powerfully manifested everywhere. Finally, the
role of tradition and its predominance in life falls under the same law. In the
words of Tarde (The Laws of Imitation, p. 241):
The appeal of traditions bequeathed by ancestors prevails everywhere over
aspiration to innovation. Even our contemporary society does not present
an exception in this respect, so rich is it in all kinds of adoption in
language, ideas, institutions, literature. Let us compare, for example,
"new" words, recently borrowed by the French from the English, German,
Russian languages on the one hand and the old French vocabulary on the
other; or the newest ideas of evolutionism and pessimism with the already
acquired traditional views; or the most recent legal reforms and the entire
French code, which is in essence contemporary to Roman law, etc. In this
way, imitation of a new fashion is quite naturally only a tiny stream next
to the great river of tradition and custom.
Generally speaking, the element of tradition always holds a predominant
role in the collective.
The appeal of antiquity is transferred into manners and even into language.
Thus, in the words of Dostoyevsky (Notes from the House of the Dead), to
please someone in Siberia you tell them that they have an elderly appearance;
the person conversing with you is dubbed "elder brother"; and when
addressing a twenty-year-old youth, you pay your "respects to grandfather so-
and-so." These customs were apparently brought from China. But in ancient
Egypt, in Rome, in all places age was highly respected, as it is even now.
On this basis, national character (the so-called national consciousness of
subjective psychology) results from the acknowledgment of the value of a
peoples' history, and determines the extent of a nation's aspiration to increase
and improve this legacy. The conservation of the traditions of previous
generations gives the opportunity to hold a certain definitiveness and
inherited consistency of views which, reproducing the past, seems to pay the
necessary homage to the dead, as well as to prepare the soil for future
generations. This living heritage preserves the cultural valuables of a self-
sufficient nation, which are, in turn, dependent on the fusion of the
individual's creativity with the collective process, and the consequent alliance
of people into a single unit.
It is thus quite natural that in every social organism conservative parties are
the keepers of the legacy of the past and the customs of the nation. These
parties, subdivided into smaller collectives, are expressions of collective
inertia and are at the same time inhibitors that stand in the way of any social
progress, since while progressive parties strive to attain new rights and
borrow all kinds of innovations from foreigners, conservative parties reject
both rights and innovations, maintaining that inherited customs and laws are
a more certain guarantee of a good life.
Finally, the predominance of the law of inertia in economics is also more
than obvious.
In agreement with that law, the established cost of some item of produce or
other is remarkably stable, independent even from the conditions of supply
and demand. In fact, more or less acute influences are necessary for the price
to fluctuate. Prices easily augment, but do not so easily diminish, which is
understandable because in the first place they receive a stimulus in the form
of the material gain of the merchant determining the cost, and in the second
place, even if such a stimulus is lacking, the law of inertia comes into effect:
it is manifested on the one hand in the accustomization of the populace to the
higher cost, and on the other in the merchant's habit of receiving higher pay.
We also know that, as a result of the same law of inertia, a demand that has
once originated does not soon disappear. This can be proved with many
convincing examples. It is clear, for instance, that the force of habit will
prevent bread from ever being replaced by imported rice in Europe, just as
rice was not replaced by bread in Asia.
All that is related to social inheritance should be placed under the law of
inertia. It is true that Tarde categorized social inheritance under the laws of
imitation, but the name does not change the essence of the matter. In any
case, inheritance is a conservative force that retains social forms for a longer
period than necessary.
Any collective is a living organism, existing thanks to reproduction and
realizing its own reproduction in the form created by history at every given
moment. The cult of ancestor-worship and respect, preserved over the
centuries, determines the passage from generation to generation of social
traditions, customs, and even superstitions, which ensures the continuity of
the descent to posterity of the previous generations' achievements.
Let us recall, says G. Le Bon (The Psychology of Nations and Masses, p.
245),
how tenaciously religious superstitions persisted for many centuries,
contradicting even the most simple logic. In the course of more than two
thousand years the most brilliant geniuses bowed down before their
power, and only in contemporary epochs has there arisen the opportunity
to question their correctness.
In the words of Tarde:
A person is freed (though never completely) from under the yoke of
custom, only to be shackled once again in order to curb and establish the
accomplishments of the period of temporary freedom. If he is sufficiently
vital and talented, then he will once again throw off his chains and make
new conquests, but then he again reverts to captivity, etc. Such is the
historical fate of all great civilized nations. (Tarde, The Laws of Imitation,
p. 245)
The same must be said in relation to rituals. It is known that the pagans
kept their heathen rites long after they were converted to Christianity. When
the Renaissance came, the supporters of new views nonetheless preserved the
habits of feudal life for some time afterwards. In general, it is known that
social forms are conserved long after the very basis of law and order has been
changed. When matriarchy was destroyed, the custom of fictitious maternity
in relation to the father, in the form of a kuvada, stayed on. The bonfires on
Midsummer Night on the eve of St. John's Day remind us of the worship of
pagan gods. The abduction of women ceased long ago, but the memory of it
is retained in certain traditions accompanying wedding ceremonies. A
religion that replaces another retains, to an extent, the ceremonies of latter.
Finally, in language we also meet with the conservation of previous roots
when a given word obtains new meaning.
In general, it is not difficult to find expressions that are apparently devoid
of all meaning and that are passed down as such from generation to
generation. Furthermore, certain features of law are retained through the
generations, notwithstanding the fact that they arose under different
circumstances, at another time, and are an anachronism in the given period.
In art and literature, it is not a challenge to point out many peculiarities that
are simply remnants of the past and have become a part of routine. Finally,
all so-called ceremonials—those "great museums of history," in the words of
Paul Viollet—are manifestations of the law of social inertia, which takes such
an array of unique forms.
Court etiquette, religion, are these not remnants of the past carried through
to our time? In a word, the law of inertia controls all of social life, and so it is
very difficult to shift society from former foundations and mould it to new
forms.
The inevitability of revolutions is also apparently contained in the law of
inertia, which brings a society that exists within the confines of old forms, at
the same time acquiring new unanswerable needs, to a dead end. The only
way out of such a predicament is the violent, forcible casting-off of the old,
long outlived structure of life, and its radical renewal through revolution.
9
The Law of Continual Motion and
Change
One of the ancient Greek philosophers maintained that everything in the
world is moving, changing, flowing. This idea can be found in the later works
of other authors in a more or less definitive form. Thus, for example, in C. T.
Letourneau's Morality, p. 5, we read:
We now know, and are surprised at how we could not previously have
noticed, that everything in the world is constantly changing, everything is
developing in a positive or negative direction, that the doctrine of
immobility is only the dream of metaphysicists. Our forebears despised
changes. We thirst for them.
And yet the principle of continual motion was for some reason not
included among the basic laws of the world, perhaps because it was crowded
out by the widely acknowledged law of evolution. However, the law of
evolution is a law of development, and this is not one and the same as the
principle of continual flow and change.
This principle states that nothing in the world is permanent or
unchangeable. Every thing, in the end, is not eternal, it is created and then
destroyed, as a result of which any constancy is but an illusion. In its initial
forms, this principle was suggested by Heraclitus, and was later reflected in
the metaphysical teachings of Hegel. Even in antiquity, Leucippus and
Democritus put forward the atomic theory of matter; these atoms, and
likewise molecules, are acknowledged to be in continual motion. The
bonding of these atoms creates various substances. Presently, we know that
atoms are divided into elements of energy in the form of negative electrons
and positive nuclei or corpuscles that can separate, leading to the decay of the
substance which they make up (the so-called radioactive phenomena). The
atoms themselves consist more or less of complicated systems of electrons
moving around a positively charged central nucleus. Such is the situation in
the inorganic world, which must be represented as energy continually
transforming from a free to a stored state and vice versa, with even the stored
energy, although in the form of a substance, being in a kind of eternal, albeit
internal, motion.
If we turn to the organic world, we once again encounter movement—and
only movement—everywhere. A plant germinates from a seed, then grows
thanks to processes of nutrition and respiration, to the motion of fluids, and to
chemical transformations instigated by the sun's energy; in time, the plant
produces flowers, fertilization occurs, and seeds ripen; they in turn give rise
to a new plant, and so forth. In a word, we can see here a continuous process
or movement and changeability.
The same can be observed in the animal kingdom. Its processes of birth,
nutrition, respiration, growth, reproduction, and uninterrupted transformation
of external energies, are in essence processes of motion and change. The
human personality is itself an entity in constant flux, which daily enriches its
reserve of knowledge and skills through experience. But if what we have
outlined above is correct in relation to the inorganic and organic worlds, it is
no less applicable to the supraorganic or social domain. There, too, we
encounter motion and change everywhere.
A society is created through the interaction of two or more individuals,
then grows and develops, borrowing the experience of the preceding
collectives and neighboring societies with which it interacts; the society
differentiates as it grows, communicating and uniting with other societies.
The society borrows and imitates, as well as creates of its own accord;
changing with the passage of time, it undergoes the corresponding
transformations. In a word, we are looking at perpetual movement, continual
transformations, and there is neither stoppage of this motion nor rest.
Whatever phenomenon we select from social life, we observe in it, once
again, only motion. Moreover, the relations of an individual with society and
vice versa are not constant, but change first in one aspect, then in another.
Political forms of government in a state vary as the preceding ones cease to
exist. The very form of a state's government does not remain permanent and,
although relatively slowly, one form supercedes another. The political ties of
a nation change depending on the international situation: alliances and
agreements are replaced by discord and war.
Economic life is still more mobile. It is enough to follow the quotations in
the exchange newspapers to understand how quickly the attitude of the public
changes towards valuables and how ceaselessly the money market fluctuates.
As for trade, it is composed entirely of cycles and motion.
If we turn to language, it is hardly necessary to prove that it is not
stationary, that any language lives, continually developing, with some words
falling into disuse and oblivion, while others are reincarnated. Furthermore,
the definitions of words alter, as a result of which their meaning often does
not correspond to their original roots. This continual change of language goes
on to such an extent that, with the passage of time, new languages, whose
origin can only be traced through the comparison of roots, are formed from
the combination of others.
Even meanings do not remain invariable; to the contrary, in due course one
and the same word or definition acquires significance or content completely
different from its original interpretation. One of the numerous examples of
such transformation is the meaning of the Russian word for "autocracy,"
which during the epoch of Ivan III referred to state independence, but has
since changed its meaning to "unlimited tsarist power."
Let us next glance at literature: we know that it is likewise a living
organism, since any style or work of literature goes through an early period
of its existence, a period of further development and maturity, and even a
period of decrepitude. In literature we also have a constant exchange of
trends and even more frequent shift in mood, which reflects the eternally
changing social disposition.
No less is experienced by scientific literature; in this realm, the
accumulation of facts causes one hypothesis to be replaced by another, and
uninterrupted laboratory work gives, each day, new basis for old theories or
lays the groundwork for new scientific theories and disciplines. The same
must be kept in mind in relation to the arts, where artistic creativity takes on
newer and newer forms and where we once again meet with the uninterrupted
exchange of schools, trends, and styles.
In this way, everything in the supraorganic world, as in the organic one, is
subject to change; everything flows, and one can agree with the fact that the
entire universe is, in the end, simply a process of continual change and
creation. As was already stated, this doctrine is old and has its origins at the
time of ancient Greece, although traces of it can be found even earlier.
The very form of universal motion was defined differently by different
people. For some, including Vico, Nietzsche, Le Bon, and Blanqui, all
phenomena could be reduced to eternal and constant circulation, the return of
previous forms, and the reintroduction of one and the same changes; when
the last stage of a given pattern is attained, everything reverts to the first
stage, as a result of which the world experiences a kind of cyclical flow. Even
in Ecclesiastes (ch. 1, lines 4-10), we read:
One generation passeth away, and another generation cometh: but the
earth abideth for ever. The sun also ariseth, and the sun goeth down, and
hasteth to his place where he arose. The wind goeth toward the south, and
turneth about unto the north; it whirleth about continually, and the wind
retumeth again according to his circuits. All the rivers run into the sea; yet
the sea is not full; unto the place from whence the rivers came, thither they
return once again... The thing that hath been, it is that which shall be; and
that which is done is that which shall be done: and there is no new thing
under the sun. Is there any thing whereof it may be said, See, this is new?
It hath been already of old time, which was before us.
If we compared this poetry with the most recent findings, for example
those of G. Le Bon: "The evolution of the world is, according to recent
analysis, composed of two very different phases: the phase of accumulation
of energy in atoms, and the phase of the release of that energy," then we
would observe, in essence, the same idea in both, the latter simply being
expressed in another language in agreement with the most recent
understanding of energy.
In this way, we are dealing with the circulation of energy from a free state
to one tied up in the form of atoms, and with its release into space, after
which, over millions of years, the energy once again is formed into atoms.
According to Le Bon: "The origination of anything is, without a doubt, no
more than a repetition of the past. There is nothing that could allow us to
believe that something exists for the first time, in the same way that we
cannot concede that it disappears forever and never arises again" (G. Le Bon.
[1909]. The Evolution of Matter).
In the supraorganic or social world, this theory was applied by Vico,
according to whom nations in their historic development go from a stage of
deism, through a stage of heroism, to a stage of humanism, after which the
first stage begins again, obviously with some alterations. This doctrine, as is
easy to see, assumes that there is a limited number of forms in the world that
can be translated into inevitable changes.
However, it is also possible to take the diametrically opposite viewpoint,
i.e., that the number of forms that the changes can take is unlimited, and the
process of alteration in the world does not repeat itself and is, consequently,
irreversible. In this case the process of change can be realized in only one
direction, but the way in which this change takes place is unpredictable.
However, there is also an intermediate opinion that maintains that the
process of change, although irreversible, remains more or less parallel to its
initial stages. In this case, the process of universal change can be figuratively
portrayed as a spiral; such is the view of Goethe, Tarde, Mayer, and others. It
is a fact that the world is continually changing, but this variation is not in the
form of a cycle, as was so beautifully stated by Ecclesiastes, since, in reality,
even the circular movement of planets is only an apparent phenomenon. If
planets orbit around the sun and their axes, then they, in essence, do not
repeat one and the same movement, for the sun itself moves in a certain
direction, and the ellipsis of the planets causes continual deviations.
There are many facts supporting the belief that the cyclical development of
phenomena is nonexistent; without speaking for the inorganic world, it can be
said with certainty that the organic and social worlds contain many examples
of the above. In the organic world, we can point to the birth of plants and
animals, their development, growth, and reproduction, the product of which
has, however, a different individuality. In the social world, state structure
initially existed on the basis of democracy in its primitive forms. In due
course, an elected head holding limited power came into being, followed by
the advent of an unlimited monarch; then came limited monarchy once again,
and finally a period of democracy returned, but in the form of parliamentary
republics or even councils. It is clear that in the above case a cycle has
occurred, in which the initial form has been altered but nonetheless
reinstated. This is demonstrative of the aforementioned spiral motion, in
which ceaseless progression creates forms that cannot be retrieved in their
original configuration, but return in a slightly different form. If we turn to the
development of religion, we see, initially, the worship of animals or people,
e.g., of one's ancestors (totemism), then the worship of heroes, after which
came the worship of gods, then once more, with the weakening of religious
views, the worship of people in the form of society or mankind (as in
socialism or internationalism).
If we look at the history of marriage, we observe that at first marriage was
a more or less free institution; then family ties were firmly cemented by
religion, and the breach of familial fidelity was severely punished; later
marriage was liberated from religious shackles and was subject, for the most
part, simply to state marital requirements; then gradually marriage once more
became a relatively free institution, with marriage or divorce needing only
mutual agreement or officially registered agreement of one of the parties.
Societies or tribes themselves go from conditions of discord and conflict to
alliances, eventually merging into one large state organism which once again
divides into factions.
Literature is at first national, in the form of national epic; then, distancing
itself from the people's interests, it becomes a caste institution, fulfilling the
demands of the more intellectual classes, and finally re-emerges as the
literature of the masses and serves the interests of the entire nation rather than
those of a separate class.
Even in language such mutability can be found. Without going into
philological details, let us note that, for example, in communicating with
another person we used primarily only ty (Russian informal "you"); later that
was expressed, depending on the situation, as ty or vy (Russian formal
"you"), while in the languages of more developed nations, such as in French,
the word tu is no longer employed in interaction with others—vous is always
preferable.
Folk wisdom undergoes comparable transformations. In the beginning,
information acquired through experience by primitive nations is replaced by
religious views that, with the passage of time, almost crowd out the facts that
stem from experience and observation and that lie at the basis of science;
however, in due course, science takes the upper hand and experience and
observation reclaim their rights in the life of human societies.
There is no need to multiply these examples. They are sufficiently
convincing for us to determine that the world is continually changing, that
everything in the world is flowing, that the array of newer and newer forms to
which the phenomena of the world are molded are irreversible, but at the
same time there is a constant restoration of conditions and forms that are
similar to those that have previously existed; yet these conditions and forms
inevitably contain something new that has not existed, is not repeated, and
cannot be reverted to in the future. From the above it is clear that the law of
continual motion and change is essentially contained in the concept of the
irreversibility of eternal change.
10
The Law of Energy Dissipation or
Entropy
As is known, the so-called second principle of hydrodynamics, according to
which it is accepted that energy strives towards dissipation or towards the
transition from uneven concentration to even distribution, has been
established as an immutable law of physics since 1824, the time of Sadi
Carnot. This law was later presented in a more precise fashion by Klausius in
Germany and by Thomson in England.
Such transition of energy from uneven to even distribution always occurs
directly, while a transition in the opposite direction requires preparatory
work, i.e., the expenditure of other forms of energy. In other words, as
expressed by Professor Khvol'son, transformation possesses "a kind of
direction."
For example, the performance of work releases a corresponding amount of
heat; however, when heat is transformed back into work only a fraction of its
energy goes to the execution of useful work while the rest, to an extent
depending on the situation, is wasted. Thus, when a rock falls to the ground it
loses kinetic energy, at the same time developing heat which cannot be
transformed into kinetic energy again.
This basic law of physics, which can likewise be proved true in chemistry
—for example, in irreversible reactions—is unquestionably applicable to
organic nature, since all organisms are, essentially, carriers of stored energy
that strives to be disseminated, naturally shifting into one form of mechanical
work or another and being transformed into heat and other more simple forms
of energy during the processes of decay. The support of life itself, i.e., the
nutrition of an organism, cannot occur without a certain amount of work.
Finally, the adaptability of organisms is based on the same law, since
adaptability presupposes the lessening of mechanical and other influences on
an organism through the transformation of mechanical energy, e.g., an
impact, into other less harmful energy forms (heat, electricity, etc.).
But this law of entropy or dissemination of energy also has indisputable
significance in relation to the supraorganic world. In essence, every
individual who makes up part of a collective is the carrier of the stored
energy of his colleagues, which he disseminates directly in the surrounding
environment in the form of motion or actual work. Otherwise, his activity can
be in the form of signs or signals that stimulate work in others; however, the
renewal of the energy spent here always entails work, in that sources for the
restoration of energy must be sought out and acquired.
If the human organism, while performing physical labor, can get up to 33
percent useful work from the energy reserves it acquires primarily from food,
then in the execution of collective work there is likewise a known percentage
of useful work that cannot be employed for the restoration of spent energy in
an amount equal to the latter's expenditure.
Furthermore, we know that the development of culture is realized through
the creative work that is done on discoveries and inventions. These
discoveries and inventions—in other words, the energy spent on them—are
disseminated directly among the masses through learning and imitation,
without the people having to expend the same amount of energy as was used
to make the discovery. The progression of any collective in general, be it a
class, party, or nation, to a certain level requires first of all the expenditure of
one amount of energy or another on the part of the individuals making up the
collective. This progress, however, is inevitably accompanied by the
dissemination of the accumulated energy through the direct transferal of the
results of the work to other collectives. The achievement of whatever results
by one collective or another will again require the expenditure of new energy
by the individuals making up the collective, which will be disseminated in the
aforementioned manner, and so on.
Let us now look at all instances of conflict between nations. Before these
encounters between rival parties occur, widespread preparations are
conducted in the form of armament, of the accumulation of equipment and
food, of the establishment of communication routes, etc., requiring a huge
amount of collective energy. During the conflict, this reserved energy is used
for activity or work, but the latter does not completely replenish the expended
reserves, on whose recovery a corresponding amount of energy must be
spent.
The same can be observed in economics. The manufacture of one product
or another requires a certain amount of energy; the end product, however,
does not provide the means for the recovery of the energy expended on its
manufacture. To this end, the product must be turned into a commodity and
sold, in other words energy must once again be spent on it in the form of its
exchange, through sale, for money.
It is clear, in this way, that collective human energy is turned into work
that cannot be transformed, of its own accord, back into human energy in an
amount equal to the work conducted; this is an expression of the law of
dissemination of energy or entropy in the supraorganic world.1
Note
1 We do not undertake to discuss whether we are dealing with the universal dissemination of energy or
its accumulation under certain conditions, such as the formation of heavenly bodies.
11
The Law of Relativity
We will not touch on the principle of relativity in the world in general as
delineated in Einstein's famous paper,1 but will examine only its
manifestations in collective activity.
The law or principle of relativity in social life is a principle thanks to
which any social movement, like any individual action, has significance not
on its own, taken separately, but in correlation with the given social
conditions.
Thus, in tsarist days, the demand for confidence in the Russian government
was acknowledged as an extremely radical form of social movement, whereas
during the revolution, only a few months later, even the demands for a
republican Russian state were not considered radical. This was because many
social circles already supported the concept of a democratic federal republic,
and some went still further in this respect, reaching out to accept Bolshevist
socialism.
Similarly, the program of the Party for National Liberty was, for the
majority of the Russian population, too radical in tsarist times, and more
moderate elements turned from it, while the party itself was forced into the
underground by the governing power; yet, during the revolution, even the
transformed Constitutional Democratic Party, which leaned quite sharply to
the left, was considered extremely right-wing and was, in its turn, pushed into
the underground by the Bolshevists. A comparable contradiction can be seen
in the fact that during a period of great triumphs, small victories are all but
ignored, whereas when victories are lacking even insignificant military
successes are treated as events of great importance to the state. In a time of
social tranquility, even an insignificant event becomes the main topic for
discussion throughout society and is subjected to social criticism from all
sides, while during large-scale social shifts and crises, important occurrences
are given little or no attention. In the same way, at a calm point in social life
even an ordinary, so to speak mundane, murder creates a sensation. In periods
of national turmoil, when blood flows freely as a result of civil war, there is
no mention of separate murders in the public or the press. Even the
mysterious, nightmarish execution of seven innocent students by unknown
people in Petrograd in the beginning of March 1918, failed to create a furore
in society. The lynchings and murders of blameless people or of petty robbers
that decorated the daily newspapers during the Russian Revolution need not
even be mentioned. They passed by practically unnoticed.
Furthermore, the law of relativity is manifested in the fact that the strength
and nature of any social movement cannot be assigned a single, constant
assessment, since under certain conditions and at a certain time it could be
acknowledged as progressive, and therefore as abetting social development,
while at another time it may be considered retrograde or as hindering healthy
social development.
One and the same social movement or action, depending on the social
situation, can act, in one case, as an inhibitor of progress, and in another as its
stimulus. Thus, inflexible rule is a destructive beginning when it hinders
healthy social currents and, conversely, is essential and beneficial when it is
used for the control of anarchy.
A patriotic mood is advantageous and essential for a healthy state in a
period of war, when national unity is indispensable, whereas that same
patriotic mood, expressed in excess, can be quite harmful in peacetime,
limiting the cultural aspirations of the separate nations that make up the state,
