and consequently inhibiting the development of their unique cultures.
In the end, it can be said that there is not one social inhibitor that, under
different conditions, and even at one and the same time, is not stimulating for
some sectors of society, and limiting for others.
Let us look at more or less constant social inhibitors, such as the demands
of laws, religion, social traditions, morals, decency, etc.
They are all, in essence, inhibitors in some cases and stimuli or incentives
in others. Thus, the demands of the law limit freedom of action in relation to
so-called crimes, but also assist social life in sheltering the population from
theft and violation. Similarly, the demands of religion forbid immoral actions,
since they contradict the service of God, and in doing so aid their suppression
in life, while at the same time inciting in others the will to execute highly
moral deeds.
Social traditions limit the freedom of the individual, but at the same time
exert a stimulating influence in relation to everything that abets the
establishment of social beginnings.
In this way, there is not one social phenomenon that could be
acknowledged as always having absolute meaning; to the contrary, every
social phenomenon must be analyzed as having relative significance.
Finally, one and the same organization, depending on the conditions
surrounding it, reacts to a given event differently at different times.
Thus, the Petrograd Council of soldier and worker deputies was opposed to
the issue of Liberty Bonds at the outset of the Russian Revolution, since
those bonds supported the war; yet at the end of June, during the Liberty
Bond promotion campaign, that same Council appealed to the populace to
purchase bonds.
The law of relativity is generally applicable to all collective movements.
When starvation affects a country, the instinctive reflexes of a crowd are
manifested in numerous thefts and outrages that do not meet with sufficient
counteraction on the part of the governing power or of society, whereas at
another time the same phenomena meet with their natural opposition not only
from the government, but also from all sections of society, and so their
occurrence is rare.
When a crowd is happy, it is difficult to force it to cry and, conversely, it is
not easy to enliven a crowd that is depressed.
When a mass of people is reared in accordance with certain views, it is
extremely difficult or almost impossible to imprint upon it opposing views,
and at the same time equally easy to imprint views that correspond with those
acquired through upbringing.
If collective concentration is directed at a certain quarter, then it is once
again extremely difficult to distract it with other events and, conversely,
concentration is easily transferred to objects related to the subject at hand.
The same can be observed in collective actions.
The tactics of parties in their mutual conflict and in their conflict with the
government change in accordance with the conditions of the day, since at one
time a certain mode of conduct may be suitable, while at another the same
conduct may not only be unsuitable, but can even be harmful to the interests
of the given party, and the latter is forced to employ other tactics. On the
other hand, repressive measures, applied in a country in the form of
inhibitors, can be unsuitable in some instances and acceptable in others.
It must be noted that the power and significance of any influence on the
collective depends on the condition of the collective at any given moment,
since sometimes even powerful influences can have a weak reaction and vice
versa. Thus, at the beginning of a war any event connected to it is greeted by
society with a burst of patriotism, whereas when the war has been overly
long, the same sort of event no longer stimulates an equal reaction in social
circles. This proves that, when evaluating social movements, various social
conditions, which in one way or another are reflected in the force of a social
movement, must be taken into account. Of course, this does not at all lessen
the important and all-encompassing significance of the law of relativity.
Statistics show that more than one thousand steam-powered and sailing
vessels sink yearly at sea. The number of victims must be huge, but this does
not amaze anybody, since each disappearance occurs separately, on diverse
occasions. Yet if we are dealing with the supposed sinking of one large
passenger liner, a tempest of anxiety sweeps through society.
The same is manifested in relation to the significance of other
undertakings.
Thus, when troops did not obey the command of their leader during the
tsarist regime in Russia, their disbandment was considered a drastic measure,
whereas under the conditions of the revolution, with the weakening of
military tradition, the measure was acknowledged to be insufficiently strict
and was replaced by reorganization, reeducation, and mass repression.
Another example: at the time of the Great Russian Revolution, the death
sentence was revoked, but, after a short time, when excesses and traitorous
acts became widespread in the army, it was reinstated by the same individual
—Minister Kerensky—who had originally revoked it.
The very attitude of society to the death sentence changes in relation to its
living conditions. In peaceful times, an execution deeply shocks the social
conscience and often serves as a subject of accusation of the government on
the part of the majority of the population, while at a time of war even mass
shootings do not stimulate a reaction in society. In general, the attitude
towards the execution of convicts is far from identical in cases when the
occurrence of an execution is rare and in ones when executions are conducted
on a massive scale. Earlier, we spoke of the fact that, at the time of the
constant use of the guillotine during the French Revolution, there were those
who, going onto the scaffold, taunted their executioners or made completely
uncalled-for jokes about them. This is reminiscent of the attitude of those
victims that end up on the dinner table in countries where cannibalism is
dominant.
Thus, it is known that the South American Guarajos, upon catching their
human prey, strangle each captive successively. "The victims, who were as
bloodthirsty as their captors," we read in C. Letourneau's Sexual and other
Morality, p. 85, "died in the manner of redskins, singing a death-song in
which they taunted their executioners, reminding the latter of the many
friends and even relatives of theirs that had been eaten by the former."
The principle of relativity is evident in many other manifestations of social
life, and especially in the replacement of one social phenomenon by another.
Thus, the heavier the yoke from which a nation frees itself, the greater the
repression of the former oppressor by revolutionaries and victorious
dissidents. However, that repression is also in direct correspondence with the
extent to which the nation is freed from guardianship. This explains much of
what we observe in the development of the French, Russian, and any other
revolution.
According to G. Le Bon (The Psychology of Socialism, p. 74),
Theend result [of national government] is first anarchy, then the despotic
rule of one of those tyrants that a crowd always turns to when driven by a
slave-like instinct of self-preservation.
Such was quite often the case in the history of democratic disorder. After
SullaMarius and the civil wars came Caesar, Tiberius, and Nero; after the
Convention came Bonaparte; after the year of '48 came Napoleon III, and
all these despots were adored by the masses. How could they have
remained in power, if the nation had not felt sympathy for them?
In the words of the same author, "Socialism may triumph; but it will not
survive for long, and the regime following it will be one of oppression that
for a long time will suppress or reverse the civilization of the country in
which it is based." It is possible to disagree with the author over the substance
of his views on socialism, but his knowledge of social conditions cannot be
refuted. The essence of the issue lies in the fact that an extreme shift of social
life in one direction is usually followed by a shift in the opposing direction.
In this way, the more powerful and damaging is the destruction of former
foundations of society in revolutionary periods, the more furiously is a
reaction manifested—such is the law of history. Similarly, the more street
robberies develop, the more frequently and violently society's self-defense is
manifested in the form of lynch-ings and brutal punishments.
A broken and degraded army's morale always falls, and this is expressed in
various thefts, drunkenness, coercion, and depravity. This is the typical
reaction to despair, similar to the way in which a person drinks and abandons
morality when subject to despondency or grief.
Even external symbols of a military nature are subject in the above case to
disregard and destruction. Weapons are discarded, sold, or plundered and
signs of excellence are removed; outfits are destroyed or thoughtlessly
wasted, etc. The Russian Revolution is a perfect example of this situation,
and the decrees on the removal of shoulder-boards and decorations were only
a reflection of the general views among soldiers on the superfluity of both,
while the decrees against the plunder of government property were publicized
when, unfortunately, hardly any military equipment remained.
During the Russian Revolution, in order to protect against a
counterrevolution, two battering rams were deployed—the declaration of
soldiers' rights and the land question. The first was directed at the
commanding officers of the former army and was reflected in discipline; the
second, directed against landowners, or the bourgeoisie of the countryside,
was meant to stimulate an uncontrollable pull towards the land. However,
both these conditions were fatal not only for the army, but also for the
revolutionary government itself in the question of democratic peace with
Germany. It took incredible effort for the revolutionary government to
reinstate in the army its former principles of command.
We have not listed a large number of those phenomena that are
manifestations of the principle of relativity; but the given examples are
enough to allow us to assess the significance of this universal principle in
social life, in which it is as vividly expressed as in other manifestations of
organic and inorganic nature.
Note
1 Annalen d. Physik, 17, 891,1905.
12
The Law of Evolution
Continual change on its own is not yet evolution, since change is the result of
continual motion, whereas evolution is the development of one form from
another and, consequently, the gradual or rapid transition from one form to
another. It is true that change, although it does not entail the creation of
forms, is usually accompanied by variation in situation and form. From this it
becomes clear that the law of evolution complements the law of change. We,
nonetheless, associate evolution not only with change, to which any
movement in general is related, but also with the transformation/ conception
of new internal or external forms, which represents a certain level of
accumulation or deficiency of a substance, as well as its displacement. In a
word, evolution, representing the development of new forms, is essentially a
creative act, and the law of evolution is in essence a law of creation.
The significance of the discovery of this universal law cannot be overrated.
According to Haeckel, the formation of our contemporary theory of evolution
is superior to all other achievements of the human spirit. Already
foreshadowed one hundred hears ago by Goethe, it was satisfactorily
expressed by Lamarck only in the beginning of the nineteenth century, and
acquired its final form thanks to Darwin in 1859. Darwin's theory of natural
selection filled the blank left in Lamarck's teachings in relation to the
interaction of heredity and adaptation. We now know for certain that the
organic world developed slowly, step by step, according to the same "eternal,
unchanging laws" that Lyell, in 1830, proved applicable to the inorganic body
of the earth; we know that the countless species of animals and plants that
inhabited our planet in the course of millions of years are simply branches of
one family tree; and we know that mankind is the newest, most perfect shoot
of the family tree of vertebrates.1
The first investigations that firmly established the law of evolution (those
of Lamarck, Spencer, Darwin, et al.) led to the conclusion that the creation of
new forms occurs slowly, as a result of which a formula was established that
stated that nature does not advance by leaps and bounds; however, further
investigations showed that the creation of new forms hardly ever proceeds
gradually, and even makes large leaps (de Vries), in which some saw the
revocation of the law of evolution. In essence, this instance is only an
extension of the understanding of evolution as a development of new forms
or as a creative process, only travelling in more rapid shifts or jumps and not
in the small and barely noticeable changes that were earlier acknowledged to
be instrumental to the law of evolution.
The law of evolution is a universal law, scientifically proven in all areas of
physico-biological knowledge, so it is quite natural that it should also be
applicable to social life and, consequently, to the development of collective
reactions:
It must be noted that in the process of evolution we meet with changes in
phenomena that must be considered irreversible. The progression from
fetishism to idolatry is as irreversible as the transfer from animal gods to
human gods, from zoomorphism to anthropomorphism, and even from
creature-gods to gods of domestic animals. (Tarde, 1. c, pp. 205-6)
Examples can also be given of irreversibility in linguistics, such as the
transformation of hard endings of verbs into soft ones in a large number of
Romance languages that have their roots in ancient Latin. To give further
examples would be unnecessary.
The law of evolution can be traced in all social phenomena, beginning with
language, which, coming from one common root, changes into newer and
newer forms through gradual differentiation and integration. Every custom
has its own history in which it is likewise not difficult to find manifestations
of the law of evolution. It is still easier to trace evolution in myths—be they
about Cerberus or Hercules. In both cases we can follow the myth's primary
development and all the further changes to which it is subjected in connection
with the views of various nations. Similarly, does not the development of
religious doctrines, beginning with Vedism, through to Brahmanism and
Zoroastrianism, then from Moses to Christ and Mohammed, and finally to the
various branches and sects of Christianity and Islam, give the opportunity to
establish in full the role of the law of evolution?
Furthermore, evolution can be traced in any social process, even in the
mood of the masses, which, originating as an almost invisible spark,
gradually gains strength and reaches its apogee. The creativity of nations is
also subject to a corresponding evolution; after all, does not artistic, literary,
and scientific creativity evolve, in its turn, as does everything that is the result
of the creativity of the human mind? Is not Theocritus reflected in Virgil,
Menander in Terence, Plato in Cicero, Byron in Lermontov, etc.?
Since the law of evolution is common to all social movements, it is
understandable that every social movement, wherever it is manifested,
undergoes the same stages of development. However, when one collective or
another reproduces a social movement that has already been experienced by a
different collective and which spread to the former collective thanks to
imitation, then the various stages of the movement are not recreated in their
entirety, but are experienced in a more or less condensed version, in a manner
similar to the way in which, in the biological world, an embryo goes through
diverse stages of development. This gives young nations, which must go
through the stages of development of social movements that have already
been undergone by established nations, the opportunity to live through them
in a shorter time, with the many interim stages of social movements passing,
in these cases, in an edited form. This is why our Russian Revolution, which
was in many ways a copy of the French one, went through the various stages
of its development much faster than did the latter. The same can be observed
in relation to all other social movements that have developed on different
areas of the globe. Thanks to this, nations that have attained the level of
development at which cultural achievements become realizable through
initiative and imitation can experience a number of historical steps at a speed
much greater than that which was attained by nations whose cultural success
was achieved primarily through their own efforts. Russia of the seventeenth
and nineteenth centuries, and Japan, which until recently was an isolated,
largely uncivilized country and now enjoys a high level of cultural
development, can serve as such examples.
It is customary to think that social evolution does not make sudden leaps,
although it would seem that this is contradicted by, for example, the
acceptance of new religion by a nation. But here it only appears as though a
rapid displacement takes place:
Everyone knows that all great religions—Brahminism, Buddhism,
Christianity, Islam—stimulated mass conversion among whole races,
which apparently accepted them immediately; but upon entering more
deeply into the analysis of these conversions it can immediately be seen
that, though a nation may have changed its religion, it did not in actual fact
discard it; in reality, the accepted beliefs underwent the alterations
necessary for them to be made a part of the race's former beliefs, and were
only a continuation of the latter. This explains the changes experienced by
a given religion upon its adoption by a nation. Chinese Buddhism, Islam
on the Deccan peninsula, etc. can serve as examples of the above. (Le
Bon, The Psychology of Nations, pp. 79-80)
However, G. Le Bon's opinion cannot be accepted unconditionally. As
there are leaps in the biological world, in the form of the so-called de Vries
mutations, so there are revolutions in the social world, which are quite lawful
phenomena in the course of historical process.
It should also be kept in mind that evolution always goes from the general
to the specific, as for example with the emergence of the proto-language
preceding the development of separate forms and, above all, mythology and
sciences. The development of science also went from more general to more
specific fields, and so it is in all cases.
In general, the evolutionary process in human society is determined by
many factors of a geographic, climatic, or socioeconomic nature and, as a
matter of fact, assigns a very small historic role to separate individuals, be
they geniuses or no. Everything comes down to the evolutionary process of
the society itself, i.e., of national masses. Separate personalities, however,
can nonetheless play a huge role in the latter if they manage to discern
astutely the pulse of national life and the direction of historical process,
thereby applying their energy towards the corresponding development of the
national masses. It is possible, of course, that a powerful personality,
employing its high position, can hinder to some extent the proper evolution of
the social process. However, in such cases the individual must be analyzed
not on his own, but as a personality that has better comprehended the legacy
of past generations and is supported by one collective or another, embodying
its hopes and aspirations, as a result of which the role of that individual is
determined by the social significance and power of the collective he
represents. In this way, the role of a ruler, military or national leader,
demagogue, or representative of a certain class or party is not so dependent
on the qualities of the individual himself, which are obviously not without
significance, as on the importance of the mass of people that is associated
with that individual.
In the manifestation of the law of evolution, the inequality of collective
units and individuals has enormous significance. If nature had given everyone
the same characteristics and abilities, then obviously there could be no
imitation—that mighty factor in social life —since there would be nothing to
imitate. There would likewise be no aspiration to perfection, the other
important factor in evolution, no conflict or rivalry, those still more important
impulses of social movements. In exactly the same way, it would be almost
impossible to realize social division of labor, specialization, or differentiation
of social strengths, which greatly assist the success of a civilization. In a
word, in place of progress we would have the more or less complete
stagnation of social life. The proper organization of social life assumes the
equality of all people in relation to social rights, and only this principle
guarantees fair treatment of individuals. But nature does not treat all people
equally, and the living conditions of separate individuals, as of separate
nations, are not the same. This lies at the basis of individual national
peculiarities, which have enormous significance in the evolution of human
society.
Shall we apply the law of evolution to various social phenomena? Let us
begin with the evolution of power. State power or government arose from the
concept of the family, which is in itself a kind of self-sufficient state. These
states, or families, reproducing and merging into tribes, created tribal states
which, in turn, grew and became nations. States also arose through the
establishment of collectives whose members pursued a shared goal, for
example, plunder. Runaways from surrounding villages would join the horde
or band and, in this way, the pillagers would eventually grow in number and
subject the villages to their rule, which would become lawful upon an
agreement between the attackers and their victims. Such was the origin of
Rome in all its glory, and, subsequently, of states that owe their existence to
Mongol invasions.
First to be established in a developing state is a communal structure; the
latter evolves into a monarchy, which does not arise before the development
of private land-ownership, under which system free landowners can be
stripped of their fields by their more powerful neighbors:
The ability to acquire large land areas and the so-called process of
feudalisation and serfdom transforms formerly free landowners into the
servants of their stronger rivals, and removes any remnants of a free,
communal system based on the concept of a popular assembly (in Russian,
veche). Inks stead, a council arises, made up of the best and noblest men,
who govern their estates and unite under the most powerful landowner—
the prince, who is acknowledged by all of them as their sovereign.
(Takhtarev, Society and State [in Russian], pp. 12-13)
It is clear that with the acquisition of material possessions comes division
into classes, and along with that comes the beginning of class conflict. It must
be noted that the path of political forms was already charted by Aristotle,
who, based on an analysis of the 157 nations known at that time, drew up the
sociological law of creation and displacement of various political forms of
state existence and rule in connection with the correlation between classes
and the conditions of class conflict.
Generally speaking, continual transformation in collectives through
evolution usually leads to the complete restructuring of their organizations,
either towards the centralization of power or towards its decentralization.
Thus, for example, the Catholic and Orthodox church went from being an
evangelical, democratic commune to a hierarchic organization ruled by an
episcopal aristocracy, and then to a monarchy controlled by a patriarch and a
Roman high priest limited by synodal edicts. Once the Pope proclaimed
himself infallible, Catholicism became monarchical absolutism.
In exactly the same way ancient Rus', with its independent princes, evolved
into a limited monarchy, then into an absolute monarchy.
However, that absolute monarchy has once again been exchanged for
democracy, first in the form of a limited monarchy, then in the form of a
republican structure with decentralized local rule and a federational state
organization.
This exchange of one condition for another in the organization of society is
determined by the fact that, according to the law of inertia, centralized power
gradually reaches an absolutism that becomes unbearable for the nation and
instigates a reaction in the form of fierce opposition, leading to the deposition
of the absolute monarch; conversely, democratic power can also bring a
country, in accordance with the law of inertia, to impossible conditions,
sometimes causing civil war and stimulating opposition in society, disposing
the latter towards monarchy again.
Archaeology and ethnography complement still more our knowledge of the
evolution of states:
We can now establish to some extent the embryology of all our social
institutions. The most distant past of our societies has unfolded before our
eyes. It is possible, for example, to study the creation of societies de visu.
These are at first the primordial and anarchic hordes of the early days of
the Quartemary period, such as can be observed in the present day on
Tierra del Fuego. Then these hordes join into classes and tribes, the latter
divide into castes, then become subject to leaders and, finally, create
despotic monarchies, and so on. (Letoumeau, Morality, p. 25)
In general, investigations into the law of evolution of social conditions and
the development of civilization find extremely valuable material in
ethnographic research. Letourneau, in speaking of the evolution of the human
race, remarks (Morality, p. 23):
History, even with the aid of legends, reveals to us only one moment out
of the evolution of the human race. With the help of ethnography, we can
hope to go to its very sources, reconstruct all the missing links, and tie the
beginning to the end. The steam engine is in this way connected with flint,
the steamship with primordial boats, the palace with cave-dwellings,
polysemantic languages (i.e., those in which one word has various
meanings) with simple ones, differential calculations with the primordial
numerations of an aboriginal who futilely attempts to count his own
fingers, the great Aryan religions with the animism of an African Negro
who bestows upon his external surroundings a conscious life similar to his
own. Raphael becomes a distant descendent of the primitive artists of the
Department Lozère, France.
In its turn, the ownership of land arose through the population of certain
areas by members of the tribe that had annexed them in some way or other. In
due course, the land was divided, becoming the property of classes, families,
and finally separate individuals. The way in which language developed leads
us to assume that in the distant past language could have arisen from
onomatopoeic signs, associative reflexes, and ordinary sound reflexes: oh,
ow, ah, hm, etc.2 The associative reflex of speech that arose in this way
gradually differentiated through additions, inflections, reduplication, and
integration by means of figurative expressions or words that denoted general
concepts. Thanks to imitation, language would then have spread among
families of the same race, class, or tribe or in a certain collective, be it a
commune or a horde. In this way, a race that spoke a single language, which
would, however, have its own dialects thanks to the division of the populace
into separate collectives, would gradually have been created.
It can likewise not be doubted that during its development and perfection,
language progressed from gestures to words of concrete meaning, and from
concrete words to abstract meanings. The language of our contemporary
savages or so-called primal nations is still rich in gestures. It is furthermore
established that there are no abstract and comparatively few collective nouns
in primitive languages, since words and gestures express concrete objects.
Because of this, primitive languages are rich in substantive nouns, verbs, and
prepositions (L. Brühl, Les fonctions mentales, p. 175-onwards). The
language of gestures serves to represent concrete phenomena, such as action,
placement, etc., and is not composed of separate gestures, but is made up of
complicated complexes of them which characterize a certain action. These
combinations are a kind of mobile description or imitation. For example,
making a motion as if raising liquid with cupped hands represents water, etc.
The further evolution of language is relatively well traced thanks to the
successes of comparative linguistics. But we will not go into detail on the
subject. We shall limit ourselves to pointing out that a basic language can
change in pronunciation due even to its social surroundings. Thus, for
example,
various Latin words, going from a Roman environment to a Spanish or
Gallic one, underwent regular and characteristic changes, with each letter
being replaced by a certain other letter, so a particular consonant in
German or English is equivalent to another consonant in Sanskrit or
Greek; this essentially means that a root language, going from a primitive
Aryan environment into a Germanic, Greek, or Hindu one changed its
consonants in the manner outlined above, acquiring an aspirate sound
instead of a hard sound, and vice versa. (Tarde, Social Logic, pp. 22-23)
The evolution of views on the causality of phenomena is what we call the
evolution of human thought. As is known, Compte differentiated the three
stages of thought: theological, metaphysical, and positive.
But Stuart Glennie maintains that the basic law of intellectual development
is the law of transition from a quantitatively undefined to a quantitatively
defined conception of the interaction of objects; in other words, that
evolution moves from extremely incomplete views on the causality of
phenomena towards the establishment of more precise views.
In place of the three Compte stages, the author divides the development of
human views on causality into the following steps: magical, supernatural, and
scientific.
The first stage encompasses that period in which mankind, knowing
nothing about the reasons for natural phenomena and finding activity in itself
through self-observation, naively assumes that this activity influences its
surroundings. This is a period of national infancy, throughout which mankind
believes that in order to cause rain it is enough to water the earth to the
accompaniment of certain incantations; that to wreak vengeance on an enemy
it is necessary to pierce his effigy; that to cure a person of an ailment it
suffices to figuratively remove a certain object from his body, etc. Here, we
are dealing with the association of one's actions with a certain magical force,
which supposedly influences external phenomena and live objects.
In the next stage, mankind acknowledges the reason for the phenomena of
the surrounding world to be a certain spiritual, invisible force. This step
arises with the development of an understanding of the spirit as a special
creature that actually exists.
At this stage, mankind populates nature with invisible creatures that have
characteristics similar to his own, and thinks that the phenomena of nature are
a result of their intervention.
Here, we are looking at animism in the form of the integration of one's
subjectively understood activity with natural phenomena, and consequently at
the integration of one's own spiritual conditions, evaluated from the point of
view of a spiritual force.
The third stage acknowledges strictly mechanical causes for all the
phenomena of nature, with the exception of their basic origin in the form of
the Creator of the universe, who controls the world in accordance with
established laws; the supernatural has no place at all in natural phenomena.
We will say, in addition, that a further and final stage of development of
human thought must entail an exclusion of supernatural forces not only from
natural phenomena, but also from the concept of the beginning of the
universe, which would conclude the evolution in our perception of the
surrounding world.
Let us likewise note that we cannot imagine a case in which one of the
above stages is consecutively exchanged for another. Usually, in one and the
same period it is possible to meet with manifestations of the first and second,
or only second, steps in one sector of society, while in other more intellectual
sectors even the third stage can be observed. It is hardly necessary to say that
the latter stage is the result of the application of experience in life and in the
laboratory in order to prove the complete regularity of all natural phenomena.
As for the evolution of scientific knowledge, it goes, as does the evolution
of language, from the general and undefined to the specific and more defined.
The progress of knowledge, uncovering deeper and deeper correlations
between phenomena, also finds many inherent discrepancies that were
previously not noticed. Thanks to the fact that important integrations of the
accumulated facts are achieved by geniuses, it is natural that the evolution of
knowledge advances by leaps and bounds. It is thanks to this powerful
synthetic ability of the human mind that mankind achieved its greatest
discoveries, beginning with Galileo's teachings, Newton's laws, and so on.
Vast collective work of the human intellect was realized in a similar manner,
and expressed in encyclopedias of scientific knowledge, in codes of laws, in
social morals, in artistic creativity, etc.
In art, as in knowledge, evolution proceeds by startlingly rapid progress,
thanks to new ideas and new understandings that seem to refresh faded ideas
and outlived knowledge. Thus, the Romanesque style was transmuted into the
Gothic one thanks to new influences stemming from the Crusades. Later,
Gothic art underwent a transformation into Renaissance art under the
influence of renewed interest in all aspects of Greco-Roman life.
From the above it should be seen that there is nothing that would suggest
or justify the parallel development of science and art. Each branch of
correlative activity develops successfully at a different point in time. Let us
also note that ideals, as symbols of the beautiful, change along with race and
epoch, and so each race creates its own art, just as every era is associated
with a distinct art form.
What, then, is the root impulse of the evolutionary process? In biology, the
process of evolution is determined by the process of fighting for survival,
which is based on the needs of an organism on the one hand and the
inequality of different organisms' resources for attack and defense on the
other. But in our opinion—and likewise in Kropotkin's—cooperation, based
on the profitable achievement of a goal, i.e., the satisfaction of needs, also
plays a role in the evolutionary process and creates conditions that improve
an organism's chances for survival via better offense and defense. The same
can be observed in society, at the foundations of which lie rivalry and
conflict, which are based on the same principles of collective needs and
unequal distribution of resources for offense and defense. Cooperation also
has significance here, since it makes the achievement of goals, and therefore
the satisfaction of needs, more likely and creates better conditions for defense
and aggression in conflict.
Can there really be aspiration toward activity without the stimulus of
needs? Obviously not. But to what conditions are rivalry and conflict subject?
Apparently, only the inequality of strength and resources. What determines
cooperation? The aspiration to strengthen one's resources for rivalry and
conflict.
From the above it is clear that social equality is possible only as an ideal,
since with its advent there would no longer be an impulse to strive for new
social achievements. This is why, if socialism has equality as its ideal, then it
is only equality of social rights; it cannot aspire to level the abilities of
separate individuals. For, if this ever occurred, it would lead to the complete
termination of any social progress.
The evolutionary process is often confused with progress or even
improvement, which is incorrect if only because evolution is development
without distinction, and can go forward, towards progress, or backwards,
towards regression.
However, the understanding of progress requires its own definition. It must
be noted here that the question of what is perfection and what is progress
cannot be resolved without a certain preface, i.e., a clarification of the aspect
from which we should approach the question itself, since that which we call
improvement from one point of view can be considered regression from
another. Let us imagine that we have a machine which is designed to execute
a certain task, for example a flying apparatus. Upon becoming acquainted
with its component parts, we would perhaps find it more complicated in
comparison to other existing systems, but the degree of perfection of the
apparatus itself, independent of its complexity, would be determined by its
greater or lesser suitability to its application, i.e., to aviation. And so, in all
cases involving tools or machines, the degree of perfection is determined by
how well a tool or machine is suited to the purpose for which it was designed.
The concept of progress is still more general. It deals with forward
advancement, but not necessarily with perfection, since progress can occur in
the direction of backward development. Because of this, although perfection
is inextricably tied to progress, progress itself can be accompanied not by
qualitative improvement, but, conversely, by degeneration. Nonetheless, the
word progress is often wrongly used to signify perfection.
Going on to the discussion of the perfection of living nature, it is necessary
to keep in mind that here we must also have two separate fields of study,
depending on whether we look at the subject from a strictly biological point
of view, in terms of the perfection of one individual or another's features, or
from a social point of view, i.e., encompassing life as a whole. With respect
to the former, we have no basis on which to recognize one organ, one part of
the organism as being superior, since an individual is whole and indivisible.
Due to this, the opinion that greater development of the nervous system and
its central organs partially characterizes greater perfection, which some
biologists hold, has no significance here.
An entire organism is a self-sufficient machine, the essence of which
comes down, on the one hand, to the acclimatization of the organism to its
surroundings, and on the other to the exploitation of nature's external forces
through the establishment of multiform interaction of the organism with its
environment. From the above it is clear that the role of the nervous system,
and partially of the central one, in the establishment of interactions is huge,
yet not to the exclusion of everything else, since some parts of the body are
tools through which the nervous system fulfills its role, and therefore the
quality of these tools should be taken into account. In the end, the degree of
an organism's perfection should be evaluated in terms of the extent to which
the organism has established interactions with its surroundings, and not in
terms of the size of its brain.
Furthermore, the exploitation of a given environment, and likewise
acclimatization to it, is ensured by the differentiation of organs and the
coordination of their functions, so we could say that a more advanced
organism would be the one that comprises the greatest differentiation of
organs or the greatest coordination of their functions; however, the final
result of the external manifestation of both these processes is the
establishment of multiform interaction, so the latter is a more reliable
measure of an organism's perfection.
Some think that, in analogy with the artificial machine, acclimatization to
an environment and the exploitation of surrounding conditions are executed
in order to fulfill the goal of self-preservation and so are a manifestation of
perfection; but this would entail holding a teleological point of view, whereas
nature itself knows no goals. This is why we retain a strictly objective
definition. If we were to uphold a teleological definition, which is wrong in
its very essence, then we would have to acknowledge that an organism that is
better equipped to preserve its own life is also more complete, but, in such a
case, we would discover that single-cell organisms, in not knowing death, are
more advanced than ourselves. Ephemeridae would be less perfect than lower
species, and elephants, parrots, crows, and others would, in having longer life
spans, be more perfect than humans.
In this way, the only correct definition and understanding of perfection is
as self-establishment through adaptation to one's environment and through
exploitation of that environment via diverse interactions with it.
The same can be applied to species. It can be said with certainty that every
biological species is the product of its environment and its era. This is why
we cannot speak of the greater or lesser perfection of a species in terms of its
greater or lesser ability for self-preservation. In a certain era, with the
alteration of surrounding living conditions, one species could become better
adapted to the achievement of the above goal than another, and vice versa.
But all species can be compared to one another with respect to the diversity
of their interactions with their surroundings, and so, depending on the extent
of the above, their greater or lesser perfection can be determined. Since the
nervous system is the apparatus that undertakes more complex interactions
with the environment, it is clear why its superior development is considered a
sign of perfection; however, not only the nervous system, which some species
do not even possess, is important here.
If we turn to social progress in the sense of perfection, then here, too, a
teleological explanation is often given. Thus, according to some people,
progress is simply a change in society that brings it closer to its ideal, and
that ideal must appear better than the contemporary situation, even if in
practice it turns out to be worse; in other words, we are dealing with an
evolutionary process that goes from worse to better, no matter what the
understanding of "better" is. Simply speaking, the determination of what is
"best" is based on other theoretical considerations, while the ideal itself is
acknowledged to be a previously established norm. There is no need to say
that this definition of progress does not stand up to criticism.
Another definition of social progress corresponds to our understanding of
social evolution. However, in this delineation it is assumed that the most
recent form is the best one, which is essentially wrong, since evolution is not
necessarily synonymous with change for the better. Evolution is change
connected to the creation of new forms, but these new forms can in reality be
worse than the old ones, which is not a rare occurrence since society can not
only progress, but also regress. Thus, for example, one reform or another is a
manifestation of the evolutionary process, but it can turn out to be a
regressive rather than progressive factor and must lead to the corresponding
consequences.
Every existing theory of social progress can be classified, in essence, as
one of the above views. Thus, the first group would include all authors who
have proposed principles as ideal norms of the social conditions of existence.
The history of science is full of many such norms that were thought to be
ideals. These include:
the principle of the Golden Mean (Aristotle), the principle of happiness or
benefit (Hedonism, Eudemonism, Utilitarianism), the principle of love
(Christianity, Petrazhitskii), the principle of the imperative (Kant), the
principle of life (Nietzsche, Guyau), the principle of community (Wundt),
the principle of unity (Nathorp), the principle of solidarity (M.
Kovalevskii, L. Bourgeois, and almost all contemporary French
sociologists), the principle of growth of knowledge (Comte, Bockle, de
Roberti), the principle of general well-being [success] (Hoeffding), the
principle of conservation of strength, etc.3
Of course, this enumeration does not nearly encompass all existing
principles, and far from exhausts the list of names that accompany one
principle or another. Utilitarianism ("the greatest good for the greatest
number"), for example, has many more disciples, including Mill, Bentham,
Spencer, Tarde, N. K. Mikhailovskii, P. L. Lavrov, and others.
Another trend, analyzing progress as evolution, likewise has a number of
followers, including Condorcet, Comte, Spencer, Ostwald, Novikov, and
others.
The obvious incompleteness of such concepts of progress led to the
complementation of that understanding by E. V. de Roberti. According to
that author, the process of change that occurs in a biological environment is
evolution, and the process of change within a social environment is progress.
However, since teleology is the basic precondition of social existence, E. V.
de Roberti states that "progress is a type of evolution that has, for us, become
a goal" (Qu'est ce que le progrés?, pp. 34-35).
But evolution does apply to the supraorganic world, though it may not
satisfy the requirements of progress. It cannot be limited to a biological
setting, since in the supraorganic world it is obvious that evolution can and
does take place either towards improvement or towards degeneration. If we
include in the understanding of supraorganic evolution the concept of
teleology, then this view becomes very similar to one of the aforementioned
principles. In this way, the preceding theory of progress does not have any
advantage over the others.
We maintain that here, as in other cases, teleological and subjective points
of view must be excluded. And so, as in the biological world, the social
process must be characterized by the establishment of diverse interactions
with the environment in terms of adaptation to it and the exploitation of it
through greater social differentiation (division of labor) and greater
coordination of the separate components that make up the society. The
reverse would characterize social regression. However, the general
understanding of progress can be separated into more specific parts. Thus, the
form of relations between the individual and society, which in the present day
best corresponds to the development of individual and social creative activity,
that leads to the subversion of surrounding nature to the aspirations of
mankind as a social individual and to the aspirations of the collective itself,
should be considered a specific form of progress. This subversion of nature to
the aspirations of the human collective is accompanied by growth in the
productivity of the given society's labor, which leads to the broadening of its
vital interactions.
Social regress, meanwhile, is characterized by the suspension of the
progressive movement of social life and of its creativity, is determined by
certain internal or external inhibiting conditions, and may even result in the
disintegration of the collective itself.
The disintegration or destruction of the collective leads to its complete
ruin, which consists of its dispersion, or more precisely of its transition from
a structured and harmonious state to an unstructured and discordant one, its
transition from social life to socially unstructured life, just as the death of an
organism is a transition from the collective existence of its cells to
disintegration into comparatively structureless molecules and atoms.
In collective reflexology, as in the rest of the world, the general rule is that
creation in the evolutionary process always goes more or less slowly and
sequentially, whereas destruction and disintegration nearly always proceed
relatively quickly. Because of this, social, strictly organized collectives,
whose creation has taken centuries, can be destroyed in a relatively short
time, as is demonstrated by the history of the Roman Empire as well as by
that of many other states, and even by the history of tsarist Russia.
Notes
1 Haeckel, Monism as the Link Between Religion and Science, p. 45.
2 See V. Bekhterev, Objective Psychology (in Russian), 3.
3 P. Sorokin, "What is progress?" (in Russian), Novyie idei v sotsiologii, issue 3, p. 146.
13
The Law of Differentiation
At the present time, the law of differentiation is an established truth in the
organic and inorganic worlds, so we will not delve into its role in those areas.
We will simply provide a quotation from a speech by the well-known
naturalist Haeckel, which will show us how the process of the gradual
differentiation of the world is now perceived:
In the very beginning, there is nothing in the endless space of the universe
except mobile, elastic ether1, 2, 3 and countless, homogeneous particles
dispersed throughout it in the manner of dust in air; these are primary
atoms; it is possible that the latter are in essence only "points of
condensation" of a vacillating substance, the remnants of which form
ether. By combining in certain ratios into groups, these primary atoms, or
atoms of mass, form the elements identified by contemporary chemistry.
In agreement with the popular Kant-Laplace hypothesis, from such a
vacillating "primordial nebula" arise rotating celestial bodies. Among the
many thousands of bodies in the universe is our sun; it gathers planets
around itself that have broken away from its mass thanks to centrifugal
force. Our little earth is only one of the planets in the solar system; all
individual life on that sphere will be the result of the action of sunlight.
When the white-hot planet Earth cools down to a certain degree, water
droplets that are prerequisite for organic life settle on the surface of the
earth's hardened crust. Carbon atoms begin their organic activity and join
with other elements into plasma that is able to expand. A small clump of
this plasma escapes the constraints of cohesion and individual growth and
splits into two identical parts.
This first monera initiates organic life, along with its corresponding
function of succession. In the monera's homogeneous plasma, soft internal
mass coagulates into a solid central nucleus; this differentiation of the
nucleus and the protoplasm creates the first organic cell. For a long time,
our planet is populated solely by such protists or single-cell organisms.
From the union of these protists result the lower histons, multicellular
plants and animals}
We provide this quotation only to illustrate how, in accordance with the
law of differentiation, the diversity of living things is thought to originate; we
do not necessarily consider all of the author's opinions as correct. It is known
that many physicists now reject the hypothesis concerning ether; furthermore,
the author does not touch on the construction, and consequently on the
creation, of atoms from electrons, on spinning nebulae, etc. The essence does
not lie in whether or not everything in the above scheme is true to reality, but
in the outline of that scheme, in how the evolutionary process is thought of as
the differentiation of primitive forms, gradually leading to greater and greater
diversity and, consequently, to the complication of the external world.
We know, moreover, that in the organic world the law of differentiation
can be traced throughout the development of every separate individual,
beginning with the moment of the fertilization of the egg.
However, in the social life of every collective, as in that of mankind, we
must also keep in mind the law of differentiation or of the isolation of
individuals according to certain characteristics, and the law of selective
integration, which leads to the grouping of individuals in relation to their
peculiarities and aspirations.
The historic development of mankind proceeds in accordance with these
two basic laws.
Ribot (The Evolution of General Ideas, p. 31) states, while discussing the
development of a child's mind:
The only suitable formula is the following: the mind goes from being
undefined to being defined. Even if we mean undefinitiveness in the sense
of generality, then we can maintain that it is neither the specific that arises
first of all, nor the general in the exact sense of the word, but the
undefined.
Something similar can be found in relation to society.
Primitive society is undifferentiated or, more exactly, very slightly
differentiated. It is held together only by joint existence, common interests,
and mutual interaction, established thanks to experience. This is the society
of the most primitive savages, who know neither state life, nor division into
castes. In the civilized world, such a society can be represented by a group of
children being raised in a common environment. Only with age is
differentiation and organization originated in this society, which
simultaneously brings with it the integration and coordination of the
differentiated sections of society.
The state as a political organization of society can vary in accordance with
the stage of development and condition of a given society, taking forms
ranging from republican to absolute/limited monarchical. In both cases, there
is a number of further subdivisions, including veche democracies, feudal
states, elected and hereditary monarchies. Even a single state can differentiate
into 
self-governing 
areas 
upon 
decentralization. 
This 
process 
of
differentiation is based on the fact that the satisfaction of physical needs is
better realized when some parts are isolated from others, as is any process of
division of labor.
In this way, the differentiation of the state as a political and social
organization reflects the level of development and condition of the given
society, and so can create self-governing communities under a veche-type
democracy and then a feudal state with an elected or hereditary suzerain at its
head. More complex state forms such as monarchies and democratic or
bourgeois republics are the products of further differentiation in the state
organization of society.
Any viable collective in general, once formed, immediately differentiates.
Already, in the very beginning, a presidium or bureau is formed in the
collective, as well as an executive, a treasury, an organization of special
sections, commissions, etc. In larger collectives, for example in states,
differentiation is much more complicated.
Here we are dealing with the isolation of organs of government, i.e.,
departments, and with the division of the remaining mass of the population
according to descent (differences in nationality), labor (professions and
unions), property (classes), education (intellectual vs. nonintellectual), views,
etc. Further differentiation leads to the formation of separate organizations,
co-operatives, 
commissions, 
organs 
of 
self-government, 
a 
central
government, various ministries, etc., etc. Moreover, differentiation in the
collective occurs in relation to vocation and even to the very tools of
production.
According to Waltman (Darwin's Theory and Socialism, p. 242), the
development of mankind's social life is subject to the biological laws of
differentiation, acclimatization, selection, etc., as is the development of
organic life. The main contrast, however, lies in the fact that humans' organic
forces are complemented by technical tools, i.e., forces separate from the
organism that interfere with the law of organic selection of individuals,
organic succession and perfection.
The law of selection of organs and organisms can also be seen in the realm
of technical tools, in that the tools—thanks to humans, of course—
differentiate, conform to new goals and produce newer and better forms. In
the history of tools there is likewise a constancy of form.
Lafargue is, in this sense, correct in saying that perfection and the fight for
survival are enacted not among people, but among artificial organs.
The law of differentiation in social life encompasses the division of labor
and, in general, the gradual separation of a primarily simple and
homogeneous collective into various parts in the form of separate circles,
parties, etc., and likewise in any manifestation of individualism in society:
In the lower races, says Le Bon (The Psychology of Nations, p. 34), all
individuals, even when they belong to different sexes, are at almost the
same psychic level. Being quite similar to one another, they enjoy that
equality of which contemporary socialists dream. In the higher races, the
inequality of individuals and of the sexes is, conversely, taken for granted.
In this way, whereas savages have few dissimilarities in relation to
personality, qualities of the mind, and correlative functions, historic nations
in general are quite disparate. Take a Russian, an Englishman, a Spaniard, is
it not possible to distinguish one from the other by observing their manner,
their expression, their actions? Does not history show deep-seated differences
between these nations in the entire makeup of their social personality?
One of the main results of civilization, says the author quoted above (I.e.,
p. 35),
is, on the one hand, the differentiation of races through the constantly
increasing mental labour experienced by nations that have reached a high
level of culture, and, on the other, the greater and greater differentiation of
the diverse layers that make up every civilized nation.
It is clear that organization is an inescapable result of the differentiation of
any community. Organization can take various paths, proceeding, for
example, in the following manner:
(1) acts of interaction, (2) their repetition, (3) custom, (4) juridical norm,
(5) juridical institution, as the totality of norms of behaviour in a certain
sphere of relations (family institution, institutions of ownership, power,
inheritance, court, etc.), (6) a social organization as the totality of the
juridical institutions of the given population sector. (P. Sorokin, I.e. Parti,
p. 340)
In another case we could be dealing with a shorter path, consisting simply
of an agreement among members of the collective, of the formulation of its
statute, and of its design and implementation.
Any movement is at first more or less chaotic and general in nature; only
upon development does it gradually acquire a more and more defined shape,
in other words a more specialized form, attaining a definite physiognomy.
In this way, any social movement goes from being slightly defined to, over
time, being more defined.
This process of the internal organization of a social movement, when it
goes from being general and chaotic to being more specific and defined, is
what we call differentiation.
Let us take any crowd that is gathering as a consequence of a given
dissatisfaction. In the beginning, almost nobody knows why he is in the
crowd and what the result of the gathering will be. When the first small
groups are formed, a mutual exchange of opinions takes place within them.
The basic reasons for unrest become more and more clear upon the above
exchange of opinions in separate parts of the crowd and later within the entire
body, and gradually this general dissatisfaction takes on a concrete form,
following which definite accusations are directed at certain individuals.
In this way, the animosity that was at first very vague and undefined
differentiates into a concrete dissatisfaction aroused by the actions of certain
individuals and by the order that they established. It is obvious that this
differentiation of a social movement, in the given case of crowd movement,
goes from undefined and chaotic enmity to concrete dissatisfaction with
certain personalities, and can even lead to violence.
However, all that was presented in the above example can only take place
if the crowd's mood is firmly established against certain individuals, which
may in fact not occur. Let us take any meeting at which some sort of question
is being discussed. At the outset, no one can predict the course of discussion
or what conclusion the meeting will reach, since at first everything is chaotic,
uncertain. But now the discussions begin. At first, the question is gone over
from various points of view, and still it cannot be said what path the
discussion will take; however, in due course the essence of the question is
more or less clarified. The views of various parties seem to become more
focused, guaranteeing the differentiation of diverse opinions, which is
nowhere more easily observable than in the light of collective criticism.
Along with this, the resultant majority of opinions is established, and in this
way the point of view of the majority is determined. Finally, a resolution is
suggested in accordance with the opinion of the majority, which is accepted
by the gathering after a vote.
This ends the collective labor that was at first chaotic and undefined, but
which through gradual differentiation attained greater clarity and became a
single, concise formula.
Let us take, furthermore, the development of a large social movement,
such as an uprising. First of all, the matter commences with the silent
dissatisfaction of the populace, which from time to time is manifested in the
statements of separate parties, the actions of separate groups, and then of
whole bands, but in the beginning nothing is specifically determined and the
path the uprising will take is unknown.
Then, an organization arises in the dissatisfied nation that attracts like-
minded rebels, and the uprising gradually becomes more ordered, changing
into a national movement with slogans and a list of demands.
In this way, the national movement that began as hollow, almost totally
undefined dissatisfaction gradually achieves complete organization, in other
words differentiates into an armed uprising that has a certain program of
action and all the necessary resources at its disposal.
Let us take military operations as another example. First to be developed
are plans of offensive action, but not one officer is misled as to the fact that in
practice the plan is almost never followed to the letter.
The prepared plan is thought of more as a goal towards which it is
necessary to strive than anything else, but in the end the achievement of that
goal will depend on the various conditions that are encountered. Even when
the maneuvers themselves are developed, usually no one can predict how
their execution will proceed, since much of what will happen is unexpected,
uncertain, and only with the passage of time does the situation and,
consequently, the mode of achievement of goals become more or less
defined.
It must furthermore be kept in mind that when a collective encounters
external influences of one kind or another for the first time, its reaction is, in
the beginning, undefined and chaotic and only later does it take on a certain
form.
In the above cases, the law of differentiation, common to all social
movements in general, is once again manifested.
Let us say that a government, for purposes of self-defense, undertakes
certain measures in order to crush one social movement or another. These
measures incur widespread indignation in opposing circles. As a result of
this, a reaction begins in the latter which is at first expressed in an undefined
form, serving only to sharpen the antagonistic mood, and then, upon
discussion of the state of affairs in various circles and party gatherings, takes
on a defined form of opposition, having at its disposal a corresponding
program of action and the resources necessary for the achievement of its goal.
The very government endeavors against social movements, which serve as
inhibitors for the latter, are at first little defined and chaotic, and only
gradually acquire a definitive form.
In the same way, any social opposition to one movement or another starts
out insufficiently defined and only takes on a more or less concrete form with
time.
Thus, every social movement, and even the conditions inhibiting it,
undergoes from beginning to end the various stages of differentiation, living
through a preparatory, and most often undefined, stage and then through the
primary stages of a relatively well-established social movement.
This movement, however, is at first far too general and only gradually
acquires a more and more precise character, obtaining, in the end, a more or
less concrete goal.
The same law finds support even in collective concentration, since the
object of attention is primarily one event or another as a whole, while later
collective concentration is directed at various details of the event.
Furthermore, if we are dealing with a collective mimico-somatic reaction,
then at first it is undefined in the extreme, manifested as more or less general
dissatisfaction, but then it takes on a certain form, as a result of which the
mimico-somatic reaction acquires a concrete, specific nature, which is
evident from the preceding examples.
All other manifestations of the collective are likewise subject to the law of
differentiation. As a matter of fact, this law can quite clearly be traced in the
development of language.
According to A. Schleicher:
The point of departure in the development of all languages consisted of
simple sounds that served to illustrate views, ideas, or concepts, and could
function in any context, i.e., as any grammatical form, although no
specific phonetic expression existed to represent these functions. In this
way, at this primitive stage of language development there were no
especial phonetic differences between verbs, nouns, conjugations,
declensions, etc. Let us attempt to represent this in at least one example. In
place of words that in contemporary German are pronounced tat, getan,
Töter, tätig, there existed, at the time of the birth of the primary indo-
germanic language, the word dha, since that word (meaning to suppose or
do, and pronounced dha in Sanskrit, da in ancient Bactrian, de in Greek,
de in Lithuanian and Slavonic, da in Goth, ta in High German) is the root
of all those stated above. At a later stage in the development of the Indo-
Germanic language, roots were employed twice to express certain
relations, joining one word or root to another, but each of the elements
remained independent (at that time, roots were used as words in their own
right). Dha, da, orta, for example, was used to indicate the first person.
Upon further development of the language, when elements fused into a
single whole and roots could be adapted, dhadhami was formed from the
above three words (dhadhami in Sanskrit, dadhami in ancient Bactrian,
tdm+tuom to form tetomi in Old High German, tue in New High German).
The original dha contained various undistinguished and undeveloped
grammatical forms, including verbs and nouns, along with all the above
derivatives. The same relationship can be observed even now in languages
that have remained at the lowest stage of development. (Ravenarius,
Philosophy as a World View in Agreement with the Principle of the Least
Measure of Force, 1912, p. 70)
The differentiation of words proceeds in another direction. L. Geiger gives
historical examples of such differentiation, in which one and the same word,
sometimes with a few variations, serves to indicate different objects, for
example Der See and die See, Belt and Belt?
It is hardly necessary to prove that the development of language proceeds
via differentiation and integration. It suffices to remember that all Indo-
European languages are descended from a single source, Sanskrit.
The very process of differentiation of languages depends on the migratory
patterns of nations and on the creation of new words that, little by little, add
complexity to a primitive language which is at the same time filled out with
borrowed words. It is also known for certain that climate is not without
influence on language.
Thus, for example, in a warm climate there are many words resplendent in
open vowels, whereas in cold countries aspirated vowels predominate.
Similarly, racial peculiarities are reflected in the character of a language in
the pronunciation of words, which proves the relation of race to language
development.
If we were to analyze any scientific movement, then we would be
convinced that it develops through differentiation and subsequent selective
integration or synthesis. In science, new facts are constantly accumulated and
the material, sometimes colossal amounts of it, begins to differentiate;
however, at the same time synthesis is taking place, with facts being
integrated through categorization into theories. The same goes for literature
and the arts. Here, we first have new works that stand out from the rest thanks
to their peculiar qualities, while from the aggregate of these peculiarities
grows a new trend, a certain governing system.
Even the acquisition of skills and knowledge is subject to the law of
differentiation. What does a person do in order to learn how to play an
instrument? He must first study separate notes, i.e., be able to differentiate
between sounds when playing them on the given instrument, and can only
then integrate them, making the transition to melodies. What does a person
do in becoming literate? In the beginning, he studies the alphabet, in other
words differentiates grammar, and then learns to put together separate letters,
syllables, and whole words.
In fact, the same general law of differentiation can even be applied to
demand, since any need arises from experience and, consequently, develops
only after a discovery or invention provides its source. Thus, the need for
narcotics such as tobacco is the result of the discovery of their anaesthetic
qualities and of a mode in which these could be exploited. Of course, even
prior to this discovery there had been a demand for the stimulation of nervous
activity in general, which could have been satisfied in another manner, for
example with alcohol; but this demand could not have taken the form of a
need specifically for tobacco before the latter's discovery. It is likewise
correct to assume that the necessity to cover certain parts of the body arose
not before clothing, but as a consequence of it, since the requirement for it
came about as a result of climatic influence.
If we now glance at the development of demand within a society, we must
take into account the fact that the development of any social need also
undergoes differentiation. Let us take, for example, the aforementioned need
for sources of stimulation. In the beginning, it was an undefined and more or
less general social requirement, which arose with the growing complexity of
civilization, and it found no satisfaction except, perhaps, in the consumption
of certain fruits; however, mankind very soon discovered how to make wine
from grapes, and from then on the demand for stimulating substances became
more defined in terms of the need for wine. With time, new inventions came
in the form of various types of wines and of the preparation of alcohols from
grain, and the same demand developed into a need for a certain quantity of
wine or vodka, thus making the original, uncertain demand more defined and
differentiated, divided into a number of needs. With the discovery of the
effeet of opium, morphine, cocaine, and other narcotic substances, that same
demand for stimulation differentiated still farther.
Thus, all of the above information leaves no doubt that any social
manifestation must develop via differentiation. This principle is as universal
for social movements as it is for associative reflexes in individual life.
Notes
1 Provided this substance even exists, we might add.
2 Haeckel, Lectures on Natural Science and Philosophy, p. 46. (Russ. trans.)
3 L. Geiger, Urspung d. Sprache, Stuttgart. S. 58.
14
The Law of Reproduction
The law of reproduction is applicable not only to the organic world. Already,
in the law of rhythm or periodicity, we have encountered the fact that once-
existent processes are eventually reproduced, even if in a somewhat modified
form. It is impossible not to be reminded of Ecclesiastes' beautiful prose in
this respect. On the other hand, in the inorganic world we have one body
dividing to create another that retains the definitive characteristics of its
source. An example: a planetary world whose progenitor is the sun. Another
example— the creation of crystals.
In the organic world, the law of reproduction is manifested in the
production of offspring, or in biological heredity. It can also be seen in the
basic characteristic of cellular tissue, i.e., the way the latter responds to
stimuli. Once the initial response has taken place, any later response to a
similar stimulus is greatly facilitated. The response is often reproduced even
following a very insignificant external stimulus.
As for correlative activity in the form of higher or associative reflexes,
reproductive processes permeate the very foundation of its manifestations,
characterized by the reproduction of corresponding reactions, including the
motile, cardio-vascular and secretory, on which there is no need to tarry.
Let us now go on to the evaluation of the law of reproduction in the
supraorganic world; we shall be brief, in view of the clarity of the theme
itself.
There is considerable evidence indicating that "spiritual" heredity,
alongside biological and individual heredity, plays a prominent role in the life
of society. Spirituality is inherited by society through upbringing and
ancestral values, and is transferred to one social or ganization or another from
the past in the form of established forms of social life.
The above is related first of all to collective "spiritual" wealth, in particular
language, customs, legends, general concepts, etc., that are passed down to
descendants from preceding generations, and likewise all that is known as
established tradition and the structure of social life in general.
Often it is impossible to know when and under what conditions a custom
was formed, or to recall its original purpose, but it nonetheless retains its
force and influence, even if the conditions that caused its creation no longer
exist. Examples include the burning of bonfires on Midsummer Night, which
dates from pagan times; fortune-telling at yuletide; Christmas trees, etc.
No less tenacious are the traditions of intellectual society, which withstand
any influence of fashion. These include the wearing of singular clothing such
as a suit or top-hat for official occasions, and all the so-called worldly
customs that indisputably govern intellectual circles, notwithstanding their
widely acknowledged absurdity.
It is necessary, furthermore, to keep in mind that all the products of the
creative activity of separate personalities are reflected in the "spiritual"
structure of social organizations and become the legacy of society. These
include such results of human creativity as science, literature, and the arts.
Without a doubt, all these products of the creative activity of separate
individuals, as we already stated, reflect social moods and views, and in this
respect they serve as indicators of the creative activity of a nation. But at the
same time science, literature and the arts are factors whose existence and
inheritance must be reflected in the social life of nations, since the results of
the past mental work of every social group are not without influence on its
activity.
It is likewise necessary to bear in mind that the activity of societies reflects
their spiritual property, which consists of the acquisitions made by humanity
with every step it makes in its progress in the realm of industry, and which is
directly related to aspects of social life such as the perfection of methods of
communication between people across long distances, the improvement of
transportation, etc.
The above clearly demonstrates what significance social heredity holds in
the collective life of nations and social organizations.
Without a doubt, this social heredity is made possible thanks to the
transfer, via the printed or spoken word, of the products of human creativity
from one generation to another. A special role in the above relation is played
by the upbringing of youth; however, simple successive transfer of
information from person to person, which occurs within every social
organization and serves as a foundation on which to strengthen social
traditions, is of no lesser significance.
Because of this, nothing in social life is done without former influence, and
nothing disappears without trace.1 Even that which seems to originate solely
through personal initiative is found, upon closer scrutiny, to arise from social
demand and is doubtless related to many other factors of social life. In a
word, there is nothing in social life which is utterly new and does not have its
roots in past social life. Even genius, that powerful initiator in human society,
is the product of preceding sociobiological conditions, and realizes its
creative activity thanks to the accumulation of material suitable for its
synthesizing work. In general, any social development is accompanied by the
accumulation of collective experience as a result of the corresponding
expenditure of collective energy.
Conversely, backward development or social decay is the result of the
insufficiency or impossibility of collective experience, following the
inhibition of social energies. This corresponds to Spencer's biological law
which states that any development is accompanied by the accumulation of a
substance, and that decay goes hand in hand with the reverse.
In this way, any step forward in the development of society is the result of
past collective experience. It is clear that the law of social heredity
encompasses the establishment of dependent relations between past and
present social events. The latter fact consists of the certainty that if one event
or another occurs, it will be followed, provided no impediments arise, by a
certain consequence which represents a new social form.
The most convincing example of social heredity is custom. "There is no
government," says Tarde, (The Laws of Imitation, p. 312),
more mercenary and despotic, no legislative power more strictly obeyed
or more severe, than custom. By this word I denote the coundess old and
new habits that regulate private life not abstractly or from on high, as it is
controlled by law, but directly and in all areas, encompassing all artificial
needs, all sympathies, antipathies, peculiarities of manner and morals that
are attributes at a given time of one person or another.
Custom, in a way similar to government and legislation, is tied to religion;
it is an off-shoot of ceremony.
At one time, predispositions thought to be contained in large social groups
were often defined as the genius of the people. The predispositions that were
attributed to nations included grammatical forms, certain forms of
government, religious beliefs, etc. It was accepted that certain social
phenomena, and not others, were peculiar to the character of one nation or
another. Thus, for example, China and Japan were considered countries in
