which European institutions were not natural, while polytheism was believed
specific to Greek thought.
Yet, although it is indisputable that one nation or another has its own
temperament and a certain level of efficiency that depends on climatic,
economic, and other conditions, it is likewise indisputable that all else which
characterizes a nation depends on its social life and structure, established over
the ages. Because of this, the introduction of new forms into social life via
enlightenment of the masses can radically change a nation's predisposition or
character, as may be observed in Japan.
Every social individual has his own physiognomy and personal qualities
that are handed down from generation to generation in the form of dominant
signs. That is the genius of the people, the genius of their language, the
genius of religion. That is the essence of Party spirit, that is what defines the
character of a certain area, its customs and habits. In a word, wherever there
is a certain collective there is likewise collective expression of the former's
individual characteristics. Thus, all of the above, with the exception of
temperament and mental development, are not inborn biological peculiarities,
but rather ones influenced by the living conditions of a given community and
so anchored in it that they have come to be considered symptoms of its
character, and are studied with especial consistency.
Note
1 For specifics, see my work, The Immortality of the Human Personality, as a Scientific Question (in
Russian), Sep. pub.
15
The Law of Selective Integration
Once again, we meet here with a universal law—the law of selective
integration. The latter can easily be observed in inorganic nature wherever
systems of bodies are formed, wherever we deal with the mobile equilibrium
of forces, and wherever complex chemical bonds are created. But the organic
world is likewise subject to the law of integration. Is not an organism a
synthesis or integration of cells in the form of tissues, a chemical integration
through the media of blood and bodily fluids, and, finally, an integration of
energy achieved by the nervous system? Is not the above synthesis of various
functions performed in the interests of the whole organism, of its vital
activity and self-preservation?
We will not linger on the above manifestations, since it is not our object to
discuss the organic world, but in passing let us note that the same law is
applicable to society. We have already seen that, alongside differentiation and
collective activity, we meet with the law of integration or synthesis in the
collective. The development of collective associative reflexes does not
proceed without selective integration.
In the associative reflexes of separate individuals, the above law is
manifested in the fact that if we create, along with an excitation that
stimulates a reflex, a secondary stimulus, then the reaction to the given
excitant will always also be accompanied by a reaction to the secondary
stimulus. The latter's occurrence is due to the nature of the phenomenon
itself, and so is impossible to avoid in most cases. In life experience, this
dependence is in one way or another taken into account, with the
consequences of a certain action later becoming the causes of action in
similar instances. For example: the hunger a newborn infant feels is sated by
its mother's or wet-nurse's breast. In this first life experience, we are dealing
with lack of nourishment or hunger as an organic irritant, and with the
satisfaction of the given need by breast milk. Until life experience suggests
another way in which to alleviate hunger, the infant will satisfy its demand
for food by suckling. As a result, the elimination of hunger as a stimulant or
irritant is so closely associated with the consequent well being of the
organism that a certain need for the latter is established, and nourishment
becomes the necessary goal for satisfying the given need.
In this way, a goal is formed as a result of a certain experience, and it
controls conduct in some situations, i.e., when similar conditions arise.
However, not every goal is established through individual experience, and
in such cases it is the result of the collective experience of others, an
inevitable consequence of the law of associative reflexes. Thus, life
experience states that all people die sooner or later. A person himself, in this
instance, does not and cannot have experience in relation to death, but the
collective life experience of the death of others acquires a governing
significance, and a goal is established— on the one hand, to postpone one's
own death, and on other to be prepared for its advent.
Another example: experience shows that people who come in contact with
a highly infectious disease also contract it; this leads to prudence and
precaution when encountering those stricken by the infection. A given person
may not have personal experience in this respect, but he nonetheless
integrates the encounters of others into his own knowledge, and in taking the
corresponding measures creates a personal goal to evade infection.
In this way, we are dealing with the creation of a defensive reflex that
develops through the experience of people in general, and is engraved on a
given individual not through personal experience, but through the natural
cultivation of a collective reflex and the application of foreign experience to
himself, thanks to synthesis or integration.
In general, in any collective the process of selective integration goes on,
alongside differentiation, in a manner comparable to the way a heterogeneous
crowd is quickly homogenized, drawn into a unit around a single, common
goal. Similarly, the individual sections of any collective band together thanks
to the establishment of organization. In every organized crowd, agreement or
harmony inevitably arise among its component parts and a common goal is
established, which is evidence of the beginning of integration.
In agreement with the law of selective integration, organization is realized
in any collective in order to consolidate its parts for common activity.
Thanks to the above law, any long-lasting collective unity, depending on
the duration of its existence, goes from being indefinite to defined, from
irregular to regular, from structureless to structured, in short, from
disorganized to organized (P. Sorokin, The System of Sociology, Volume 1, p.
33).
In this way, the initially fractionated and differentiated sections of society
are subject, in due course, to amalgamation, under the influence of common
language, custom, religion, and other institutions or causes that play the role
of stimuli. Thus, the ancient Greek states were united thanks to the Olympic
games and the Delphic oracle. Nations of the Roman empire were allied not
only by force, but also by a shared language and a common religion that was
able to accommodate countless new gods, and likewise by the dissemination
of a single culture. Christianity and a common culture unite the civilized
nations of our time.
State integration, with its accompanying political organization, always
goes hand in hand with the differentiation of the population into class and
tribe structure. Nations, in borrowing cultural innovations from one another,
are already becoming united in some measure. Even wars, which, it would
appear, must separate nations, since they are born of rifts between them, in
reality lead to unions and alliances, and do so thanks not only to the
conquering of one nation by another, but also to the appropriations that a less
civilized nation gains from a more culturally developed one, especially if the
latter is victorious. This integration is facilitated by state organization that
similarly joins culturally and religiously dissimilar nations into one powerful
unit.
Furthermore, 
economic 
and 
cultural 
propinquity 
can 
develop
notwithstanding the political separation of nations. The same occurs in
relation to smaller collectives, such as castes, which unite on the grounds of
common cultural and economic interests and, through imitation of and
borrowing from one another, gradually merge, eliminating class and caste
divisions.
In general, differentiation in a society's political organization is always
accompanied by integration. In the words of Takhtarev:
Even the more complex forms of political society and state are created
simply, through integration. Thus, tribes merge into peoples, and peoples
take on the form of alliances of self-governing nations.
Feudal principalities, upon losing self-sufficiency and consequently
becoming dependent on more powerful estates, fall under the control of
their rulers and are molded into a more complicated feudal state. When
urbanized republics of an autonomous nature can no longer be
independent in terms of the ensurance of their external safety, they must
inevitably unite in order to fight external enemies. By forming alliances
and federations, they lay the groundwork for more involved republican
forms. Basic state forms, becoming similarly dependent on one another,
give rise to more complex forms of the nation-state. Finally, even nation-
states and nation/class societies, under the influence of international
communication and the loss of self-sufficiency, form unions and become
international states which, in their turn, strive to unite and be transformed
into an all-encompassing world republic. (Takhtarev, Society and State [in
Russian], p. 10)
The organization of power always has a certain connection to the
interrelations of the social classes and groups that make up a nation, while the
manifestation of power as a force, in accordance with H. Jephson (The
Platform: Part II, 1901, r.p. St. Petersburg.) and Arthur Bentley (The Process
of Government), depends on pressure from one large social group or another.
In the end, power inevitably falls into the hands of the ruling social group,
becoming a tool used in the achievement of its goals.
In this way, it is obvious that integration goes hand in hand with
differentiation in the political life of nations. The process of integration goes
still farther than described above, so that when a state can no longer satisfy
the requirements of its existence, they are supplemented through the
necessary agreements, alliances, and mergers.
The process of selective integration likewise accompanies the realization
of any collective activity. Collective actions are at first insufficiently
organized and undefined; with time, they take on the form of better
coordinated actions, geared towards the achievement of a certain goal.
Thus, if troops are learning maneuvers, their movements at the outset are
uncoordinated, and only gradually do they acquire the requisite harmony, and
consequently become defined or precise.
The same process of transition from discord and chaos to harmony can be
found in orchestra practice, in choral singing, and in other similar collective
movements. Here, too, we are dealing with integration.
Examples of integration can also be found in industrial alliances, in various
financial unions, in co-operatives, etc.
It is hardly necessary to prove the importance of integration or synthesis in
scientific thought, as in any creative act, since it is obvious that the
accumulation of facts is followed by their union into a single system, their
integration into one principle or under one law. Any creativity in general, be
it scientific or artistic, is in large part synthesis and integration. According to
Tarde (Social Laws, p. 106), it is absolutely indisputable that the sciences
were composed at first solely of small, separate discoveries that had no
common ties; then these discoveries were amalgamated into groups (where
every separate grouping was a discovery in its own right) and theories that, in
their turn, were united into broader theories, supported or corrected by a
number of other discoveries. Finally, all this was connected by bridges of
hypotheses.
The life of every society is characterized by creativity, which in a
collective is not manifested identically in all individuals, but more or less
unequally, being present to a greater extent in some than in others. Creativity
is the work which, in the form of impulses, instigates any social process.
Creativity is expressed through an invention or discovery that sooner or later
leads to change in social correlations. Thus, the discovery of fire totally
altered human existence by allowing for the use of artificially prepared food,
the employment of malleable metals instead of stone, the occupation of
heated enclosures in much colder climates, the illumination of darkness, etc.
The discovery of explosives gave the opportunity to use firearms, to more
easily alter the earth's surface, to better exploit its underground resources; the
invention of steam power speeded up routes of communication and changed
the organization of human work, replacing manual labor with factory and
plant industry; the discovery of electricity made it possible to contact a
person over long distances in a written or spoken form (via telegraph or
telephone), as well as making a large number of improvements in various
areas of technology.
In the above cases, we are dealing with inventions and discoveries that
have to do with improvements in the sphere of technology which originated
new eras in human history. Discoveries in the area of higher manifestations
of human correlative activity are no less important. Such discoveries were
made by motivators of science and art, by founders of religion, who laid new
paths to greater knowledge and moral perfection. This, in turn, led to the
establishment of better living conditions for mankind, as a result of the ability
to predict events, the creation of perfected tools that facilitate human work,
the abolition of slave labor, the termination of feudalism and serfdom, etc.
It goes without saying that alongside these important discoveries and
inventions we meet minor ones at every step, whose magnitude is
correspondingly less; however, their impact in terms of the character of their
influence on social activity is significant. Take, for example, an invention like
the safety pin. Did it not economize human activity by eliminating the
necessity of sewing those areas of material that could be pinned?
What, then, contains the force and significance of creation? Where does
that novelty, which is found in every discovery and invention, come from? It
cannot be doubted that creativity is the result of the synthesis or combination
by a powerful mind of existent phenomena and lesser discoveries; this
opinion can be supported by the analysis of any important invention or
discovery.
It can be said with certainty that any discovery or invention is based on the
synthesis of natural phenomena. Separate facts are discovered by many, but
their combination is not evident to all; it is that combination which creates
something new, heretofore unknown. Let us take Newton's discovery, for
example. Who did not know that apples fall from the tree to the ground; yet
that obvious fact, in combination with others no less commonly known to
astronomers, led to a discovery which surprised the world.
Thanks to such integration, scientific prediction becomes possible, as is
proved by many widely acknowledged cases.
The significance of large numbers, as it is revealed in statistics and in the
theory of probability, also has at its basis the law of integration.
Not only science itself, but also various views, assumptions, erroneous
theories, fantastic suppositions and outlooks, beginning with primitive
humans and ending with civilized races, in a word, all animistic, fetishistic,
totemic, and other inane views and theories, as well as religious conceptions
and fantastic explanations of natural phenomena given by uneducated people,
are products of synthesis —fallacious, it is true, and originating in incorrect
beliefs, but synthesis nonetheless as an integrating principle.
The same should be said of the arts. Thus, for example, the original art of
the Hindus owes its creation to the merging of ancient Egyptian and Assyrian
art. A similar trend can be traced in the development of European art, and in
that of other nations.
The same process of integration, as we saw, lies at the base of the
development of categories and general notions.
Finally, the development of language itself is affected both by
differentiation and integration. How, for example, were the present-day
Romance languages derived? Obviously, through the commingling of Latin
with local tongues; and in that process of combination, synthesis or selective
integration played a role.
Even the spread of a language does not occur without the participation of
the law of integration, since a new language appropriated by one people or
another is altered as it merges with the local dialect.
Thus, as a consequence of two centuries of Roman occupation, the Gauls
adopted Latin, but that language was gradually adapted to their needs and
their linguistic forms, resulting in the creation of French.
In the end, any social phenomenon arises due to integration. Thus,
countless poor, minor languages of separate peoples unite via integration into
one great and powerful language that satisfies everyone.
Even social morality is gradually established thanks to the evaluation of
individual conduct under various conditions by individuals and groups, as a
result of which public opinion evolves in the form of social morality.
The assimilation and union of nations is also relegated to the ranks of
integration, when we are dealing with the internal affinity of one to another.
According to G. Le Bon (The Psychology of Nations and Masses, p. 15),
"When a nation has achieved this unity, an instinctive agreement of all its
members on all important questions is established, and serious differences of
opinion can no longer arise within it."
Finally, how many architectural styles are products of the process of
integration? A true connoisseur of art can speak of this better than the author
of this book. Even civilizations owe their development, in the end, to
integration, since there are no pure civilizations made up of a single people,
only mixed ones in which a thin coating of one civilization covers another,
more ancient culture.
If we turn to the question of what factors lead to social synthesis, it is
necessary to keep in mind that in organized societies, the goals of collective
union or synthesis are forwarded by meetings, conferences, discussions, etc.
The same purpose is served by all factories with their distribution of labor,
corporations, banks, government institutions, scientific societies and other
collectives in which representatives of different specialties work towards a
common goal.
One of the most important forces leading to the manifestation of
integration, agreement, and alliance by separate individuals is imitation and
appropriation. Notwithstanding the differentiating role of various factors, in
the form of geographic, economic, and other influences that distinguish
nations from one another and divide societies, imitation, based on adoption,
establishes a similarity between the differentiated sections of nations and
societies, which leads to their union and integration.
The difference between the civilized nations of Europe and, for example,
the Albanians is so great that it is difficult to even contemplate the possibility
of their alliance, whereas between Switzerland and other West European
nations there are already many forms of cultural attachment, thanks to a long
history of mutual imitation of a cultural nature. In exactly the same manner,
the lowest class of a society, thanks to imitation in the area of education,
gradually merges with the higher classes in cultural and economic respects,
which leads to greater integration and union of those groups. Imitation in
language, i.e., the borrowing of a language by one nation from another,
likewise serves as an important stimulus to integration, leading to the
merging of nations. In general, imitation plays an integratory and unifying
role everywhere. Social beliefs and aspirations, thanks to mutual
communicability and imitation, grow in force and significance even in
relation to the number of members of a collective.
The discovery of one or another compromising situation in the life of any
public figure is sufficient to arouse society's mistrust of his every action.
Selective integration always arises when a certain influence on society is
treated with a similar attitude in different social groups.
Thus, opposition to one act of the government or another can develop in
both right and left circles. When a calamity such as war or rebellion occurs,
the various sectors of the population strive to show their patriotism. One and
the same goal can excite different social collectives. For example, the
exploration of tropical countries on the African continent and of the North
and South polar regions spurred the activity of many scientific collectives and
merchant classes of different nations, which joined forces on that basis.
Those cases in which different movements can originate from and be upheld
by a single source fall under the same category of phenomena. Thus, the
Russian Revolution of 1917 led at first to the union of all opposing elements
in the country, due to the fact that the preceding tsarist rule had been disliked
by all socialist and liberal-bourgeois sectors of the population, as by the rural
populace. Finally, a great social misfortune usually motivates aid-giving
movements in diverse social circles.
All these are examples of the law of selective integration, in accordance
with which various social movements are inspired by one and the same
stimulus.
Selective integration can be observed in relation to any collective activity.
Take, for example, a meeting at which a question of great importance is being
discussed. Notwithstanding the disparity of views of different political
parties, some of those parties can agree on a single decision.
In the case of the development of collective disorder on the basis of a food
shortage, we often see, through integration due to collective associative
reflex, looting not only of shops containing provisions, but also of shops
which display goods of a totally different nature.
In general, collective arousal, as well as collective concentration, that has
been generated by a certain object is later directed at all objects which have a
certain relation to the first.
Finally, conflict with power sometimes integrates even the most extreme
social movements. There is no need to search for examples abroad; we can
simply point out the joint activity in Russia of extreme left-wing parties, of
the notorious Union of the Russian People, and of former police chiefs and
gendarmes. Here is what can be read on the subject in the newspapers:
A few minutes before the arrival of the special train bearing the minister,
in the gilt hall where tsars had rested on voyages from Tsarskoe Selo to
Petersburg and back, commander-in-chief of the military district Gen.
Polovtsov indignantly handed me a card, found in the palace seized by the
maximalists, painted in two colours—black and red.
Caricatured in black were long-nosed, grey-haired, skull-capped Jews, and
in red— a sea of blood, pouring into a vase held by a Jewess from the
throat of a fourteen year old Hungarian girl who had been "slaughtered" in
Tissa-Eslar.
"Well, you see," said the general curtly.
"I see," I shrugged. It was as in a good detective story: all the threads were
tangled. Extreme left, extreme right—everything boils down to a ritual
pogrom.
In this episode there is perhaps something exaggerated, even accidental,
but there is no doubt that many former monarchists manifested a certain
inclination to work with the extreme left, to the exclusion of all other
revolutionary parties.
In all the above cases we are dealing with selective rather than simple
integration, for it occurs between social movements of different natures
which have a certain relationship to a single phenomenon that plays the role
of a common stimulus. However, true merger is also possible when two or
more related organizations, pursuing similar goals, unite into one whole,
while preserving their individual physiognomy. In this instance, the
individual response of each organization is lost, and from the moment of
merging it is the new, united organization that displays a reaction to external
events.
On the other hand, it is known that for the purpose of mutual support, two
or more social movements, striving to coordinate forces, can form a bloc.
In this way, we are looking here at cases in which a few different social
currents, in order to better attain their objectives, integrate with one another
without losing their social physiognomy. Often, this integration goes no
further than the definite goal, the achievement of which is acknowledged to
be the only activity necessitating a combined effort, while other activities
entering into the scope of the united organizations follow diverging
directions. Thus, for example, political parties unite into blocs to combine
action, pursue certain political goals, form trusts, etc. There is no need to look
far for examples of the preceding in the political history of nations.
K. Marx, discussing the civil war in France, points out that the downfall of
the Paris Commune was facilitated by the traitorous agreement between
former revolutionaries and socialists headed by Louis Blanc, who chose to
support the forces of order at the Versailles Assembly. Here is what the
historian of the Paris Commune, Lissagaray, writes on the subject:
The votes of the rural deputies and Thiers' past too obviously contradicted
his republican declamations; the former heroes of the front likewise
instilled little trust. Thiers sensed this and turned to the purest of the pure,
the revolutionary aristocracy that had returned from exile. Its prestige was
not damaged in the eyes of provincial democrats. Thiers pursued
aristocrats in couloirs, told them that the fate of the republic was in their
hands, flattered their senile vanity and so skillfully handled them that they
became a submissive tool in his control, and he could telegraph that they
applauded the speeches of March 21 that anathematized Paris.
Louis Blanc, who had innocently grumbled against the foundations of
French society all his life, now bared his teeth at the members of the
Commune, who, in his words, sentenced him to death. With whom, he
asked the delegates, with whom can one negotiate in Paris? The nobles
who are already disputing each others' power are fanatics, idiots or
swindlers, not to mention those involved in Napoleonic and Prussian
intrigues.
These or similar forms of the selective integration of different parties, for
the purpose of conflict with others, are also encountered in the contemporary
conditions of a revolutionary movement. Examples are there for everyone to
see.
In exactly the same way, scientific societies that pursue the same end often
work together in order to attain the best possible results. Thus, for example,
national and international associations of scientific institutions represent such
integration of scientific collectives. The same can be observed in relation to
social organizations of an economic or professional nature.
Their coming together in unions for the combined protection of their
interests is yet another example of selective integration.
The first step in the gathering of a crowd is the arrival of separate groups
of people who are attracted by the same situation, after which these separate
groups along with newly arriving individuals aggregate into a single dense
crowd.
In its primary stages, a rebellion is likewise composed of a few bands that
later unite, forming one common organization. The latter then directs the
actions of its component parts toward a definite goal.
When common effort, such as collective labor, is executed by
inexperienced individuals, there is at first insufficient coordination of work;
often, one person's work inhibits another's. Little by little, however, the work
of many people reaches complete coordination and so allies the participants
in the common labor.
Orchestral or theatrical performances can serve as suitable examples in this
respect. Every musician or actor must begin by rehearsing his role separately,
which could be seen as supporting the law of differentiation, but later these
separate roles will be complemented in a single body, which corresponds to
the law of selective integration. Finally, choral singing is another example of
the same phenomenon.
In all these cases we are dealing with the same phenomenon as it is
observed in the reflexology of individual persons.1
Thus, one and the same goal, as a basic stimulus, can motivate a large
number of individuals, who consequently draw closer together and eventually
unite, realizing the principle of social synthesis or selective integration.
Note
1 SeeV. Bekhterev, General Principles ofHuman Reflexology (inRussian), p. 19 and ff.
16
The Law of Historical Succession
While observing the phenomena of social life, we must come to the important
thesis that no social movement can arise before the necessary prerequisites
are in place. This law is immutable to such an extent that, if a genius predicts
one discovery or another several centuries before its realization, that
discovery will, nonetheless, not come into being until the preconditions are
established that make it a natural and indispensable link in the common chain
of historical events. Even firsthand observation of a phenomenon is
sometimes unable to shift scientific thought from an established position
before the groundwork is laid for the phenomenon's acceptance. A classic
example of this is the reaction of the Paris Academy of Sciences to the first
human record of a meteorite reaching the Earth's surface:
A "heavenly rock" fell near the town of Jouillac in 1790, and the mayor of
the town, astounded by the occurrence, decided to send a notification of
the fact along with the signatures of 300 witnesses to the Academy of
Sciences, and what happened? The speaker, having read this notification
to the members of the Academy, expressed the following opinion: "How
unfortunate it is that an entire municipality supports popular superstitions,
whose very existence is regrettable, in a formal report. What can I say of
this? Only that any philosophically educated person, having read this
certification of an obviously false fact, of a physically impossible
phenomenon, can himself make all the necessary conclusions. '1
It is obvious that the unpreparedness of minds for a new, apparently
unbelievable phenomenon led to the lamentable rejection of a fact witnessed
by 300 people—a fact which, given the absence of prejudice, it would be
easy to verify in situ, and thus dispel all doubt; but even that most simple and
natural method of confirmation would not be resorted to in the given case,
due to the inacceptability of the fact itself to unprepared minds.
A no less amazing example is the stance the same Academy took on the
impossibility of human flight in view of the fact that it contradicted human
nature, while the Medical Academy of Paris found nothing extraordinary,
outside the phenomena of imagination, in Mesmer's experiments with
hypnosis. In both cases, it was necessary for mankind to live another few
decades before these previously inconceivable facts and discoveries were
acknowledged.
Because of this, there is no doubt that the development of science is a
gradual process of preparing social thought. Even Mendeleev stated this: "In
attempting to know the infinite, science itself has no end, and, being
universal, inevitably acquires a national character, even more or less personal
nuances" (The Foundations of Chemistry IV, 1905-1906). The author,
comparing science and art, goes on to say:
In essence, science and art pursue the same goals; alongside the arousal of
a sense of pleasure, science and art are occupied with the study and
description of the real world, expanding our mental scope and developing
our knowledge of nature and life. And so to the psychological similarity
between the two fields is added a biological resemblance, since both have
an independent significance in practical life, in the relationship of a person
with his surroundings, and lead to the employment of the products of
human creativity.
Academician P. I. Valden notes on the subject:
But the scientist and inventor, artist and poet, is a living part of nature; his
will is a part of the peoples' will, his imagination—a part of the fantasy of
the masses, his creative force—part of the hidden spiritual force of the
people. This is why the roots of an individual's creativity are embedded in
the imagination and will of the masses. This is why the analysis of
scientific creativity must touch on collective, national imagination.
(Priroda, Sept.-Oct. 1917, p. 90)
From the aforementioned it is clear that scientific and artistic creativity is
based on the preexisting spiritual "forces" of a nation.
On the other hand, it can be said with certainty that no invention can be
realized before the corresponding factual knowledge is obtained through
previous investigation. Thus, the invention of fire could not be realized
before man made use of tools and could prepare material for the maintenance
of fire. The cart could not be invented before the wheel and the domestication
of large animals such as the horse or ox. The steam engine appeared only
after better acquaintance with the function of the piston and lever and the
action of steam, etc. In these cases the role of the genius is to unite the
disparate components in such a way that they form a tool which performs a
certain task:
One discovery, writes Tarde,
always has in it the potential for other discoveries, but it is uncertain
whether the latter will be realized, and if they are, when and in what order.
The discovery of the surveying compass contained in itself the discovery
of America and Oceania, in the sense that the practically impossible
detection of that continent or archipelago without a magnetic indicator
became very narrowly probable after its discovery, more or less probable
after 50 years, slightly more probable after two or three centuries and
highly probable or even certain after 1000 years.
In any case, it is clear that though of these two discoveries the first had to
precede the second, Florida's existence could have been known before or
after that of Brazil, and New Caledonia's before or after New Holland's.
The discovery of writing obviously had to occur prior to the invention of
the printing press; the discovery of Arabic numerals was essential to the
success of our mathematicians. Before all else, the discovery of articulate
speech is the sine qua non condition for all other discoveries. (Social
Logic, p. 124)
The more prepared the national organized masses are, the more easily and
painlessly any upheaval proceeds. Thus, the February Revolution in Russia
was hailed in that country as a deliverance from the terrible oppression and
traitorousness of war, and caused an immense emotional uplift in the
populace, to the extent that people on the streets embraced and congratulated
each other on the advent of the "rebirth." In a word, there were no protestors,
since the remaining handful of monarchists hid from the common exultation.
Troops voluntarily shifted to the side of the new government, exchanging
their former imperial pennants for red flags and marching to revolutionary
songs.
The October Revolution, organized by the Bolsheviks, who staged a new
coup thanks to their seizure of power, presents a different picture altogether.
Its slogans—immediate peace, immediate division of land, and the rule of the
proletariat—attracted the troops and laboring masses, but the remainder of
the working democracy and bourgeoisie, not to mention the intellectual
elements, saw in this coup unfavorable consequences for itself and so entered
the ranks of enemies of the new revolution, supporting the already almost
nonexistent (thanks to arrest and displacement) Provisional Government; as a
result, the masses were instantly organized into committees dedicated to
saving the motherland and the revolution, the city Duma became the center of
new organizations that opposed the latest power, and employees of ministries
and other government institutions announced a boycott and would no longer
serve under the new government. Inevitably, a civil war broke out, ending
with the victory of the Bolshevist forces at Petrograd, followed by the
execution of the cadets, the dreadful events in Moscow and widespread
pillaging in other towns, not to mention countless arrests.
Notwithstanding its military success and the gradual elimination of all
bourgeois publications, including socialist newspapers, the new government
could not fully master the situation, which led to a drawn-out civil war, and
all because the population of the country was not and could not be prepared
for Communism.
The development of the cultural life of nations is subject to the same law
of historical succession. The stone age was replaced by the bronze age, in
turn supplanted by the iron age, the steam age, and the age of electricity, but
this succession was determined by corresponding discoveries and inventions,
before which none of these periods of human cultural development could
have been realized.
In exactly the same manner, the hunter-gatherer way of life does not
become a nomadic or agricultural existence before the safety of the
population from aggressive neighbors is guaranteed, while the industrial
structure of a country is dependent on an excess of people unable to support
themselves by working the land, as on a few other conditions, of course.
The political structure of the state itself is the result of the previous
preparation of the masses for a certain form of government.
A social phenomenon of such huge importance as the establishment of a
new political system, in the form of, say, a socialist republic, is not fully
realizable without the corresponding prerequisites, as is discussed by R. P.
Pinkevich in his article (see Vecherniaia Zvezda, 25). These prerequisites are
that the proletariat be literate and have a sufficiently wide scope of
knowledge, a certain moral fortitude, that a significant majority of it be
consciously socialist, i.e., know what socialism is and how it should be
approached, and, finally, that it understands the harm to the common interests
of work and the whole caused by the satisfaction of private interests of the
working class, etc. In other words, the proletariat should be sufficiently
intelligent to be able to achieve a given minimum level of mental and moral
development, if not become the brains of the country, and be educated in a
corresponding manner; if this is not the case, any socialist restructuring of the
state ultimately ends in failure, as is portrayed by G. Le Bon in his book, The
Psychology of Socialism.
And so, when there is a lack of historical prerequisites, the law of historical
succession leads to disastrous consequences.
Note
1 Dr. M. Kemmerich, Kulturcuriosa, Munchen. Quoted from K. Krall's Thinking Animals, p. 233.
17
The Law of Economy
We owe the principle of least expenditure of resources in nonliving nature to
Zellner (Ueber d. Natur. d. Cometen, 1872, Leipzig). The law of adaptation,
to be discussed later, inevitably leads to the principle of economy in the
organic world. There is no need, however, to discuss this aspect of the
principle in detail in this work, since we are concerned primarily with the
supraorganic world.
The philosophy of Avenarius (Philosophy as a World View According to
the Principle of the Least Effort, 1913, St. Petersburg) maintains, as is
known, that the "spiritual" sphere achieves its goals through the least possible
expenditure of strength; however, it does not achieve this immediately, and
does so through practice and habit.
This principle can be considered as also holding true for correlative
activity. The creation of concepts, the search for regularity of phenomena,
numeration, and mathematical calculations all correspond to the principle of
least expenditure of strength. Whether we analyze correlative activity as a
result of nervous or other energy, it is clear that the principle of economy
must be acknowledged as immutable in relation to any exercise, and
consequently any repetition, that is accompanied by the economy of energy.
We will not go into detail on the application of this principle to the
correlative activity of separate individuals. Let us simply note that the
principle of economy lies at the basis of any adaptation, since the process of
adaptation essentially involves the economy of expended strength as a
consequence of the more precise definition of the work being done.
Obviously, the law of economy must also have significance in a social
environment. Here, the process of adaptation strives to achieve the greatest
satisfaction of needs with the least expenditure of effort and resources. All
socioeconomic life is based on the principle of the development of greatest
productivity with the least possible expenditure of effort and resources.
Moreover, we owe even the inception of collectives themselves to the
principle of economy, since the assimilation of people into societies can only
be justified by that principle. Where separate individuals cannot execute a
given task, a collective is needed to unite those individuals and allow them to
achieve the necessary result through joint effort. This is why any type of
collective is, in essence, a realization of the principle of economy of strength,
as is any manifestation of social life. Avenarius is right in saying:
The influence of the principle of least effort in practical life is not only
obviously manifested in any aspiration to freedom, to division of labour,
to the union of administrative, commercial, legal, state, and social life, but
is likewise apparent in the institutions of trade, political economy,
legislation, government, politics, etc. (I.e., p. 95)
Any symbolism can also be explained by the principle of economy, for the
former's purpose is to replace complex phenomena with eyecatching or, in
any case, expressive and easily understood signs. Language itself, a product
of social creativity, is a realization of the principle of economy. With the help
of words, a number of objects and the relationship between them can be
easily defined, and at the same time the whole process of interaction between
us and surrounding individuals is facilitated, as are all processes of
correlative activity in general.
The establishment of common concepts and classifications, as a result of
social living conditions, also satisfies the law of economy of strength.
The principle of economy is manifested even in the creation of words that
are abbreviated to an astonishing extent. The Psycho-Neurological Institute is
often contracted to "Psycho-Neuro"; the Institute for the Study of the Brain
and Psychic Activity is called the "Institute of the Brain": for example, "Let's
go to the Institute of the Brain," or even just "the Brain." Instead of The
Soviet of Worker and Peasant Deputees, people say "the Sovdep" or
"Smol'ny." All complicated names are contracted in agreement with the law
of economy: "Ponto," "Mono," "Santrud," "Glavsanupr," "Nkps," etc.
Similarly, commonly known and frequently used terms are often abbreviated
in writing, for example "etc.," "e.g.," "i.e." Telegrams necessitate still further
abbreviation of words and names, while stenography replaces words with
certain signs. The use of numeration and mathematical calculations in the
interests of economy was already noted.
Furthermore, the symbolism of signs is no less vividly expressed in, for
example, 
cockades, 
shoulder-boards, 
clothing, 
various 
conventional
designations and badges, even in the attributes of callings and professions
(the wearing of corresponding regalia, e.g., the official's cockade, the judge's
chain). In some cases, symbolism comes down to the reproduction of one
characteristic quality or function of an object, which replaces the object itself.
This is manifested in onomatopoeic words (whistle, buzz, cluck, bang, etc.)
All these are diverse forms of economization in correlative activity.
In essence, the development of all science or knowledge, and its practical
achievements, pursues the same goal of the realization of one aim or another
with the least expenditure of effort.
The principle of economy is realized in the process of scientific creativity
with every generalization, every establishment of one dependence or another,
and every foundation of a law that encompasses a number of dependences
and phenomena.
Here is what we read, for example, in A. A. Chuprov's book (Essays on the
Theory of Statistics [in Russian], 1909, pp. 9-11) on the subject of the law of
gravitation:
The discovery of this law signals an upheaval in the theory of gravitation.
Why? Even before Newton it was acknowledged that the sun attracted the
planets; the inverse proportionality of attraction to the square of the
distance between bodies was like wise given consideration. Newton
simply made the transition from the analysis of forces acting between
bodies of finite proportions to the study of forces that attract infinitely
small parts. This transition is attended by such economy of mental energy
that it rightly deserves the place given it in the history of the study of
gravity. If memory had to retain every single fact established in this area,
then with the growth of knowledge the complexity of the problem would
go outside the limits of the capacity of human intelligence.
Meanwhile, with Newton's discovery of this law "we immediately get out
of our difficulty: the variegated mass of separate facts instantly becomes
easily surveyable. The abundant accumulation of observations is summarized
in a compact formula."
Even art is subject to the same principle of economy, discarding, in its
aspiration to stimulate uplifted emotions, all that is spurious and cumbersome
in order to achieve the same result with the least expenditure of effort.
Any social movement develops in the direction of least resistance as long
as this does not contradict the basic object of that movement, which also
satisfies the principle of economy.
The achievement of greatest results with the least expenditure of resources
is the guiding principle of any conflict in general, including military activity
and the triumph over obstacles.
In the end, all social life is permeated by the principle of economy. The
ideal of individual and social life is the achievement of greatest results with
least effort. It can be said that the satisfaction of the most demands with the
least possible expenditure of resources is the basic rule behind all
socioeconomic politics. Any collective activity tends to take the path of least
resistance, which also conforms to the principle of economy of effort. Thus,
any new institution gains the potential of future development only when it is
needed to fill a void in society and when its realization does not require
excessive resources; in other words, when it conforms to the principle of
satisfaction of the greatest number of needs with the least expenditure of
effort and resources. An established institution always develops, in relation to
its goals, in the direction in which it encounters the least resistance.
The development of national culture once again is subject to the law of
economy of effort, since it is determined by the existence of available
material on the one hand, and by the corresponding preparation of a work
force on the other. The same can be observed in relation to the development
of mining and refining industry and to the development of various
professions and arts.
The development and teaching of sciences is no exception in this respect,
since it is similarly directly dependent on favorable conditions and on the
preparation of the corresponding personnel, which creates the possibility of
economizing resources and efforts.
It is obvious that when an undertaking comes in contact with one obstacle
or another, it cuts through the impediment where the latter is weakest.
Naturally, this corresponds to the law of economy of effort. For this reason,
the path along which national dissatisfaction develops is always in the
direction of least resistance.
The same principle of economy explains the compromises reached when,
for example, an established custom collides with a sensible innovation.
In this case the expediency of the innovation conquers routine; the essence
of the latter becomes a new doctrine, while the form remains the same. In this
way, only the form of the previous structure remains. Thus, Christianity,
while spreading among the European nations, left the organization of the
state untouched, only altering social life.
All vestiges of the past in general are partially explained from this point of
view.
In many cases a new religion, new political form, or new law limits itself
to the introduction of a new norm, leaving the essence of its former
equivalent applied to new conditions.
The settling of a nation once again follows the path along which it meets
with the fewest obstacles and where people find the greatest number of
conveniences and corresponding resources. Emigration usually takes place in
the direction of least resistance, least competition, and greatest number of
fundamental comforts. If we are dealing with the movement of a rioting
crowd, it always occurs in the direction of least resistance and in a direction
favorable in terms of the achievement of the given goal.
The same can be seen in relation to a military collective during battle. It
commonly moves in the direction of least enemy resistance, i.e., proceeds
where the opposition is weakest. A mission may require that the point of
greatest resistance of the opposing side be crushed, but this is likewise done
while bearing in mind the eventual achievement of the best results with the
least expenditure of resources.
The governing principle of usefulness (utilitarianism), no matter what its
objective, also agrees with the law of economy, since a useful thing often not
only satisfies one demand or another, but also does so with the least
expenditure of effort. And the actions of any collective in general correspond
to the principle of utility only if, satisfying one need or another of the
collective, they are executed with the least use of energy and resources.
It is necessary to point out the role of training and habit in the realization
of this law, since in the life of social institutions and in the collective activity
of any society, former experience and acquired habit play a significant role in
terms of the least expenditure of energy resulting in the greatest achievable
results. The fact is that in collective and individual activity, training leads to
facilitated activity in a certain field. In other words, the multiple reproduction
of a certain collective action creates habits that, in leading to the facilitated
activity of the collective in a certain direction, realize this law in practice
under the conditions of collective activity.
From all that has been outlined above, it is clear that in the life and activity
of a collective the law of economy has the significance of a basic principle,
and it can be said that there is no manifestation in the life of a collective in
which this principle does not have its place. In any case, this is one of those
principles that must be taken into account when discussing all correlations in
the life and activity of the collective.
18
The Law of Adaptation
The law of adaptation is applicable not only to organic nature, but should be
extended to encompass all of the inorganic world as well. Is the evolution of
the inorganic world not based on the mutual adaptation of interacting bodies
and energies? Is not the movement of heavenly bodies, in its turn, mutual
adaptation of a kind? On the other hand, when a rock is smoothed by the
ocean, when a river or stream chooses to flow through the most pliable soil,
is this not once again adaptation in inorganic nature?
But we are interested primarily in the organic and supraorganic worlds.
As for the former, the formula of life, given by Spencer and consisting of
"continual maintenance of the adaptation of internal interactions to external
ones" (The Foundations of Biology: Part I. 8 I and ff., par. 30), cannot be
acknowledged as satisfactory. We are not dealing solely with the adaptation
of internal interactions to external ones, but likewise with the use of external
interactions for the sus-tainment of internal ones. If the first, Spencerian part
of the formula reveals the passive role of a living creature, then the second,
added by ourselves, exposes its active role. The higher the species of animal,
the greater the extent to which the second part of the new formula, i.e., the
use of external interactions in order to support internal ones, is manifested.
In the plant world, we seem to encounter primarily the first part of this
formula; however, there is some exploitation of external interactions, if we
take into account a flower's rotation with the movement of the sun, the
turning of leaves in order to best make use of solar energy, the guiding of the
root system in order to most efficiently absorb the nutrients in the soil, etc. If
the adaptation of internal inter actions to external ones depends on the
changes in internal processes brought on by a change in external conditions,
e.g., the increased production of heat and so less significant loss of it in cold
external temperatures, the protection of essential organs from weight loss
upon scarcity of nourishment, etc., then the use of external interactions is
expressed in external movement, tropism, and taxis, and in the development
of free movement for the procurement of food, of protection from bad
weather, of shelter from the elements in a burrow or cave, and, among the
higher species, in the construction of burrows or dens in preparation for
winter, or of nests in the mating season, etc.1
Finally, in the human world all material culture is distinguished by the
exploitation of various natural forces, for the purpose of sustaining the
internal interactions of the organism at a corresponding level.
One of the vital apparati for the execution of this adaptation of internal
interactions to external ones and the use of these in the interests of the
organism is the nervous system and its receptors, or transformers. The latter
select stimuli from the external world that are significant to the organism and,
transforming them into electrical impulses, connect the influence exerted by
those stimuli to the corresponding motor and secretory apparati (muscles,
vessels, and glands) thanks to the associative system in the skull and spinal
cord. It is especially important that some receptors, such as the retina, the
Corti spiral organ, the Schneider membrane and the heat receptors on the
skin, give the opportunity to sense external excitants from a distance and thus
allow the organism, with the help of experience, to prepare for the
corresponding reaction. Thanks to the higher centers of the nervous system,
diverse interactions between the organism and its environment are
established, depending on individual experience and in connection with the
changing conditions of the individual and the surrounding environment.
Thanks to this act of adaptation, a number of higher or associative reflexes
is created, through which the complex and multifac-eted adaptation of every
individual to the conditions surrounding him, and likewise his exploitation of
the latter in order to survive, is established.
Moreover, both adaptations are improved thanks to the fact that the
nervous system separates excitants into their component parts. Acquiring the
ability to react, depending on experience, to one part or another, the nervous
system can at the same time determine the relationship in space and time
between interdependent excitants, which makes it possible in many cases to
prevent subsequent irritants.
If for the first, i.e., passive section of the aforementioned formula, the
analysis of external irritants was sufficient to work out the adaptations to
them of internal interactions, then it is not in the least sufficient for the
second, active section of the life formula, where synthesis is of especial
significance. This is why the areas of gray matter in the nervous system, by
means of which associative reflexes are carried out, cannot be studied only as
analyzers, as they have recently been branded by physiologists of the I.
Pavlov school. They are, in my opinion, not only analyzers, but also
synthesizing apparati or associators, which is proved 
by 
direct
experimentation.2
The fact of the matter is that in living conditions all forms of complex
adaptation of an organism to its environment and its exploitation of the latter
require not only analysis, but also the selective integration of external
influences, as well as the coordination of corresponding responses. It cannot
be doubted that, among higher animals and humans, both the one and the
other are realized with the participation of the cortex as a superior organ
which establishes all the complex correlations of an organism with its
environment, and alters them in correspondence with changes in the latter.
Not only does a given external influence cause the corresponding reaction
to it, but the latter is also able to reproduce, to a certain extent, a
corresponding influence. In laboratory experiments, this is demonstrated
when people who are especially impressionable under regular conditions of
arousal of associative reflexes are subjected to electrical stimulation of the
skin applied simultaneously with a sound or light stimulus. After the
establishment of an associative reflex, the examinee quickly withdraws his
hand or foot whenever the sound or light signal is given because, in his
words, he experiences an electrical shock that in reality does not exist. In
other, more common cases, although there is no such hallucination, the test
subject reproduces the possibility of an electrical shock to which the trained
associative reflex conforms.
On the other hand, a reflex itself is always associated in a certain manner
with a given stimulus, since a harsh and unusual stimulus incites a defensive
reflex, whereas a moderate stimulus having a favorable effect on the
organism arouses an offensive reflex, adapted to the stimulus in a way that
employs the latter to the utmost. This continuous relation established between
the reflex and its external influence, becoming more and more entrenched,
leads to the mechanization of actions whenever the reflex is repeated multiple
times. Thanks to this mechanization, reflexive action is executed more easily,
speedily, and without excess movement. Concentration, as a preparatory
reflex on the part of receptive organs, is likewise associated with a certain
external influence, and that is sufficient to arouse concentration upon the
reproduction of a certain influence, or to reproduce a given external influence
as a result of concentration.
These conditions are at the basis of the facilitated reproduction of a
previously experienced reaction, which has exceptional significance not only
in view of the fact that facilitated reproduction satisfies the law of economy,
but also in that, through multiple repetition of one reflex act or another under
the corresponding influence, certain habits are created and new
"supraorganic" demands of the individual arise, guiding his conduct.
That which relates to the individual is also applicable to the collective
personality. Here, adaptation follows the same path as described above, and
is manifested not only in the establishment and maintenance of continual
adaptation of the collective's internal interactions to its external ones, but also
in the corresponding use of external interactions in order to preserve the
collective. The collective, like the individual, needs to satisfy its vital
demands while it develops and grows. The adaptation here is realized by
means of associative reflexes of a collective nature, forming under various
external influences, both of nature and of other collectives or individuals.
In this way, the collective fights for its existence, adapting to the
conditions of locality and climate, to surrounding living and nonliving nature,
and to those collectives with which it interacts, at the same time employing
all three components of its environment in its interests. The development of
collective associative reflexes occurs in correspondence with the collective's
living conditions and surroundings; these reflexes include concentration,
imitation, symbolism, and offensive and defensive acts that satisfy demands
of an inherited/organic nature as well as those acquired through experience of
repeated external influences of one kind or another, in the form of customary
collective actions.
There is no need to examine such adaptational activity of the collective in
detail, since its nature is clear from what has already been stated. Let us
simply underline the fact that any existential manifestation of the collective in
terms of one reflex or another is, in essence, an act of adaptation to
surrounding conditions or of their use in the interest of self-preservation.
Notes
1 See V. Bekhterev, Psyche and Life (in Russian), St. Petersburg.
2 SeeV. Bekhterev, General Principles ofHuman Reflexology (inRussian), 1918.
19
The Law of Selection
The collective, as any phenomenon, must have its basis in the common laws
of nature; otherwise, its existence would have no justification in the
surrounding reality.
It cannot be doubted that, in application to living conditions, only the
principle of usefulness can justify one phenomenon or another, and in this
respect the followers of Darwin's doctrine study sociality in the animal
kingdom as a condition conducive to success in the struggle for existence.
Thanks to it, animals that lead a social form of life gain precedence in the
struggle for existence over others and, by virtue of natural selection, increase
the probability of the survival of their species.
So, from the point of view of Darwinism, we owe the development of
associations to the struggle for existence, i.e., to external reasons, since the
struggle of communities with external foes and with nature is more successful
than that of individuals. But external conditions alone are insufficient for the
formation of communities, since in the animal world they are determined not
only by struggle, but also by the conditions resulting from the organization
itself. Not to mention the fact that the struggle itself arises as a consequence
of an organism's living conditions, in connection with the need for
nourishment. There must be other reasons for the formation of groups or
societies.
When individuals interact, they exert an influence on each other, thanks to
which any feeling—for example, that of satisfaction—is augmented if
achieved communally, since the effect of one's own satisfaction,
complemented by the impression of the same satisfaction in others, reaches
the highest possible level thanks to the action of direct induction or infection.
People eat with greater appetite when seated at a table with others. In exactly
the same way, dancers enjoy dancing in a group much more than
individually.
The aspiration to interact with others, in its turn, develops with the increase
in energy that accompanies greater satisfaction of vital needs, whereas a
depressed condition is attended by a desire for solitude and the evasion of
society. The communication between individuals, leading to imitation which,
in turn, facilitates the acquisition of social habits, is also of great significance.
This is why the reason for the creation of collectives is by no means confined
to their usefulness in the struggle for existence, but is connected to the
conditions outlined above. In a word, struggle alone is not the incentive for
the formation of a collective, but also individuals' needs for society, which
can originate on a basis having nothing in common with struggle.
The law of associative reflexes also has significance in this instance. The
latter law, as is known, states that any secondary stimulus that accompanies
the principal one becomes, upon frequent repetition, an independent stimulus
of the same reflex. Let us say that in the struggle with nature, in hunting, and
in other undertakings primitive man met with more success when in a group.
This led to the creation of communal living conditions in accordance not only
with the law of natural selection, as regards the survival of the fittest, but also
with the law of associative reflexes. By virtue of that law, the better results
achieved as a community, arousing the corresponding sthenic reaction in
separate individuals, were accompanied for each person by secondary
stimulants following association with other people. The latter was in itself a
favorable stimulant and so attracted individuals.
But since communal living conditions must have their own laws, society
establishes corresponding conditions of sociality, connected to the inhibition
of the aspirations of the individual and the limitation of his rights for the
benefit of society, along with his acquisition of special comforts and
advantages (e.g., better protection). From this it is clear that the conditions of
social life are indirect results of the law of associative reflexes.
It should be kept in mind that the unquestionable prevalence of the
principle of the struggle for existence has lately been more and more
disputed, especially since Kropotkin published his well-known work, Mutual
Aid as a Factor in Evolution, which contains abundant factual material on the
given subject. We can acknowledge with Kropotkin that the principle of
solidarity and mutual assistance is as widespread in organic nature as the
struggle for existence. This explains the development of sociality and the
formation of collectives better than does the struggle for existence principle.
Collective attacks, although they guarantee success, also necessitate the
division of the spoils into as many parts as there are members of the
collective; consequently, the majority of predators prefer to hunt alone.
Collective defense is an altogether different matter. Here there is no need
to share something throughout the collective. Of course, it is quite obvious
that the collective is not assaulted by solitary individuals, but, conversely,
any attacker corresponds the strength of his forces to the potential of
resistance on the part of the attacked, which is why collective defense usually
meets its match in collective offense.
Thus, it is obvious that the existence of collectives is not sufficiently
justified either by conditions of offense or by those of defense.
No, it is mutual assistance under general living conditions—not only those
of defense or attack—which accounts for the existence of collectives, since
mutual assistance is unrealizable without the collective, and vice versa. In
any case, mutual aid as a factor of evolution is definitely manifested in the
collective, at the same time justifying the existence of the collective itself.
In separate works, I tarry on the subject of solidarity and mutual assistance
as natural phenomena that proceed alongside the struggle for existence,1 and
as phenomena that guarantee improvement and progress.2 In those
compositions, I prove that in social life we meet with a particular law of
nature called "social selection" which, to counterbalance natural selection,
acts not in terms of the survival of the strong and physically capable, but
rather of the weak and less adapted to the conditions of life, yet more sociable
or, in general, answering more to the interests of society.3
Thus, the support of the elderly, of the sick or handicapped, of helpless
infants—all this is executed in the collective as something proper and
unavoidable, and yet it clearly contradicts the law of struggle for existence in
the sense of a struggle against everyone, solely for self-preservation.
It is surprising that the acknowledgement of the universality of the latter
law for a long time forced biologists to study cooperation and mutual
assistance, which are expressed in the human collective as well as
everywhere in nature, as principles subject to the law of struggle for
existence, and consequently having no apparent justification in the conditions
of organic nature. On the basis of what was outlined above, we cannot agree
with the majority of authors who analyze the formation of the collective as an
inevitable result of the law of struggle for existence.
Grant-Allen (Die Neue Zeit, 29, 1893-94, p. 71) accuses the defenders of
unlimited natural selection of discussing it not in a broad biological sense, but
in a very narrow one. They focus only on the individual, not on a group or
species. Yet biology should adopt the view that a race enters the struggle for
existence as a separate unit.
In the words of G. Le Bon, association replaces powerless personal egoism
with mighty collective egoism that benefits everyone. With the exception of
groups held together by the ties of religion, blood, or politics—ties whose
influence weakens from day-to-day —solidarity of interests can unite people
quite firmly:
At the present time, the word solidarity is used far more frequently than
the old terms liberty, equality and fraternity, and soon it will supplant
them altogether.
Yet it in no way symbolizes those terms. Since the end object of any great
coalition of interests is struggle with other interests, it is obvious that
solidarity is but a partial form of the universal battle of organisms and
classes. Solidarity, in its contemporary understanding, inevitably excludes
liberty and confines our former dreams of fraternity in the very narrow
frame of association. (Le Bon, The Psychology of Socialism)
Conflict is without a doubt one of the important stimuli in the
establishment of sociality and of the collective; however, if it were the sole
stimulus, the creation of collectives with aesthetic or moral goals would be
incomprehensible.
How, for example, are we to understand the reason for the formation of
orchestral, choral, literate, or artistic collectives? It is obvious that life is
controlled not only by struggle, but also by other guiding motives, the
significance of which, for some reason, has been obscured without sufficient
basis.
According to N. Ferri, Marx's doctrine is a further development of the
theory of evolution. It should be noted, though, that if this can be
acknowledged at all, then certainly not from a historical point of view, since
Marx preceded Darwin and came from a different background. However this
may be, the doctrine of pure socialism, which presupposes not the equality of
people, which does not exist, but the equality of rights to a human existence,
should be taken into account alongside the struggle between classes. The
division of labor is unavoidable owing to the disparity between people's
natural or acquired talents. However, the situation should never arise in
which some are idle, while others are overworked like pack animals.
Science acknowledges the vitality of mutual assistance based on solidarity
of interests. "As for solidarity," writes G. Le Bon, that ardent antagonist of
socialism, "no observation can deny its existence, because such solidarity can
be seen in relation to all creatures. Some animal societies, especially lower
(i.e., weaker) ones, only exist thanks to the narrow solidarity which makes
possible their protection from enemies." But solidarity, in our opinion, should
not be analyzed only as a means of protection from enemies. Solidarity is a
factor of evolution of the same significance as struggle, so its roots are deeply
embedded in living nature, and if in certain cases it depends on struggle
between individuals, then struggle is in the same measure determined by
individuals' solidarity. Most importantly, solidarity is a factor conducive to
and supportive of sociality.
Of course, the law of struggle for existence, along with solidarity and
social selection, does have a place in the human world, and only the
interaction of these laws determines human progress. What would happen to
humanity if competition and rivalry did not exist in its midst? Without rivalry
between individuals, tribes, and classes, in a word, "without universal
struggle, man would never have departed from his primitive barbarity. He
would even now be living in caves, guarding his meager spoils from wild
animals, and would never have known the benefits of civilization" (G. Le
Bon, The Psychology of Socialism, p. 77).
According to the above author, the doctrine of equality and fraternity
received a significant setback in the form of
the doctrine of evolution, which proved that a continuous struggle, always
resulting in the death of the weakest, is enacted in nature—a law that is
doubtlessly bloody, but that is the source of all progress, and without
which humanity would never have come out of its primitive barbarity,
while civilization would never have existed (Le Bon, p. 61).
But the struggle for survival, whose motto is "Death to the weak and
unfit," leads in the human world, as in that of many animals, to the
development of conflict between societies in which solidarity must govern;
consequently, the latter must be acknowledged an important factor in
evolution in general. V. Bittner recently expressed this final deduction from
Darwinism:
If in the first stages of its development the law of struggle for existence
entailed "death to the weak," then human struggle, leading to the
development of social instincts, forms the basis for protection not only of
the emotionally connected, but also of the distant and weak.
It is necessary to keep in mind that it is not so much the struggle for
existence that plays a role in the development of the organic world as the
survival of the adapted, while adaptation is the correspondence of the
structure and function of an organism to its environment, and is realized not
only through struggle.
Generally speaking, adaptation is not backed by a certain principle. It does
not matter to nature by what means this correspondence is achieved—
whether through an organism's progress or through its regress, as is the case
in the parasite world; moreover, correspondence can be achieved, as was
mentioned, via the adaptation of the environment to an organism. The latter
condition, almost nonexistent among the lower organisms, is manifested to
greater and greater extents with the progress of organization, and reaches it
highest level in the human world.4 Thanks to this, man is the only higher
animal that has populated the entire earth.
As a matter of course, the mighty source of the adaptation of nature to
one's own interests is creativity, which in turn is based, in large part, on the
conditions of social life.
According to A. Parshin,5 the adaptation of physical surroundings to the
interests of the human organism proceeds in two directions: (1) "the creation
of clothes, tools, optical instruments, modes of transport, etc., in order to
sustain the human organism, and (2) the re-arrangement of the physical
environment in the form of houses, roads, cultivation, etc."
All this is, in the end, material culture that can be attained only by means
of cooperation. However, sociality arose neither from these conditions, as A.
Parshin maintains, since it can be found in the animal world, nor from the
Darwinist struggle for existence. It has a self-contained basis in the principle
of best satisfaction of demands as a community and in the principle of
protection of any member of that community so long as this is in the interests
of the group. The support and protection of children, of the sick and of the
elderly could not be accounted for from the point of view of the law of
struggle for existence or from that of natural selection.
Only gradually, according to A. Parshin, did "familial-cooperative groups"
replace individually egotistical hunters. The family was a cell, and for the
adaptation of nature to its needs animals were domesticated and the first
attempts to cultivate grain were made.
Later, cooperation resulted in the creation of clans and tribes, which
represented the first groupings of humans and which were formed under the
influence of ancestral ties. With the growth of the family into a tribe,
ancestral ties became insufficient, and necessitated the intermediation of a
religious element that arose solely from an instinct of self-preservation.
Religion led to the conception of a strict tribal god, who protected the family
and punished parasitism and rapaciousness within the tribe.
The further development of peaceful cooperation was the consequence of
the development of state, national, and religious principles.
However, it should be noted that the family, clan, or tribe did not lead to
the development of extensive cooperation in labor; the latter was the product
of the expedient combination of energies of individuals who pursued the
satisfaction of the same demands. This is why sociality is manifested in the
animal kingdom long before family. The family and sociality are factors of
the evolution of social cooperation in the human world: the first of tribal and
national, and the second of political and partly of religious cooperation.
Under contemporary conditions, humankind lives in nation-state or simply
state forms, in which parasitism and rapaciousness are restrained by legal
norms. States are the cooperations that, in protecting themselves,
unfortunately manifest animal instincts in the form of rapaciousness and
parasitism towards other nations and states, which inevitably leads to wars.
As for religion, having appeared, as many maintain, out of an instinct of
self-preservation, or having been, according to others, the fruit of social
living conditions, it assisted the family, clan, and tribe, and then also served
the state. However, religious doctrines from the time of ancient Greece began
to reject tribal and state origins, which is proved in the Iliad's description of
Zeus' chagrin at Achilles' persecution of Hector; ever since the emergence of
Christianity, religion has promoted the peaceful coexistence of humankind in
its entirety, condemning rapaciousness and parasitism as evils common to all
of humanity.
Religion, however, being the support and stimulus of the uneducated
masses, will not be the decisive factor in the union of humanity; it is more
likely that the development of governmental systems as well as the
elimination of economic slavery and of the so-called capitalist structure will
play a significant role. Humanity would undergo a union of states, with state
isolation being eliminated and states becoming autonomous areas
federatively connected into a single whole under conditions of mutual
assistance and cooperation.
The development of so-called moral principles that go alongside the
development of collaboration in the human world is likewise of assistance in
the establishment of unity, but the development solely of moral principles,
without the establishment of expedient conditions of communal living, is not
enough to bring mankind closer to unity.
There exists an opinion that the development of collective and altruistic
motives arises on the basis of human aspiration to personal gain. This view is
upheld by N. Shavrov:6
Man is egotistic, as are all creatures.
