Altruistic motives arose only as a consequence and on the grounds of
human aspiration to personal gain....
The struggle with nature and unfavourable external conditions, the
procurement of food, the hunt, self-defence from attacks by animals, etc.
were significantly more successful when executed in a group. This left a
lasting impression on the human psyche, and originated certain ideas that
were partly conscious, and partly remained in the subconscious. Being
repeated acountless number of times over thousands of years, this left such
deep impressions in the neuro-psychic area that it originated notions which
seemed original and peculiar to humankind from the very beginning.
The author precedes his views with facts derived from the law of
associative reflexes, in agreement with which any accessory irritant that
accompanies the main stimulus, after a number of reproductions, is able to
incite the same reflex as that stimulus.
While applying that law to collectivism, the author notes:
Mankind's first motives were egotistic; collectivism was an accessory
phenomenon in the life of primitive humans, a means of achieving
objectives such as the satisfaction of elementary demands. Later, it
became an independent stimulus.
Altruistic concepts, which also became independent motives for humans,
came as a result of collectivism. This was a natural occurrence, since
without the former all sociality and collective forms of struggle would fall
apart like a building made of sand. Accessory phenomena and resources
for the achievement of goals gained the significance of independent
stimuli, (l.c.p. 18)
In essence, this definition of collectivism corresponds in part to Darwin's
doctrine, since both explain collectivism by the principle of struggle for
existence, placing egotism, as an embodiment of the struggle against
everyone, at its foundation. However, the principle of struggle for existence
leads to survival by means of natural selection. How can collectivism, which
arose from egotism, play a role in evolution?
Obviously, we cannot do without inheritance. In order to save his theory,
the author allows for such inheritance: "Undoubtedly, the higher psychic
reflexes leave quite stable impressions on the organization of the brain's gray
matter, and can be transferred by one method or another to posterity" (p. 40).
It is understood that the basis on which these psychoreflexes can develop is
inherited, for example in the form of connections between psychic centers of
molecular balance, etc.
However, no matter how attractive the hypothesis of physiological
inheritance may seem, there are as yet no facts that would allow us to discuss
with certainty the heredity of acquired characteristics and influences,
excepting those which act upon the embryonic plasma; in any case,
experiments with animals, especially lower ones, are not convincing in this
respect.
If we do not acknowledge the hereditary transmission of collectivism, then
it must be a phenomenon in the existence of every species that depends on
the circumstances and conditions of the organism's environment, as do all
associative reflexes.
From my point of view, both external and personal factors are significant
for the development of collectivism; in other words, it is contributed to by the
law of associative reflexes, by imitation—which is also an associative reflex
—and by the law of natural selection. The transmission of the inclination to
collectivism must occur through the upbringing of each successive
generation, which is supported by the fact that the conception and nurture of
offspring is impossible in the life of higher animals without a family
collective.
Moreover, the collective itself maintains conditions that make it a self-
sufficient institution, since it is upheld not only by the success of collective
labor, which leads to mutual encouragement, competition, and to the division
of labor through specialization, but also by the factor that I call "social
selection."
The latter ensures common mutual support within the collective —support
not of the most adapted to the conditions of the surrounding environment, but
of the most adapted to the collective, for though they may be the least strong
in the physical sense, they are strongest in the social respect.
Notes
1 V. Bekhterev, "Social selection and its biological significance" (in Russian), Vestn. Znaniia,
NordundSud, 1912.
2 V. Bekhterev, "Individual and sociological factors in the development of organisms and sociality as
the condition of progress" (in Russian), Vestn. Psikholog, 1913.
3 See V. Bekhterev, "The significance of hormonism in biology and social selection" (in Russian),
Priroda, 1916.
4 V. Bekhterev, Psyche and Life (in Russian), St. Petersburg.
5 A. Parshin, Scientific Religion (in Russian).
6 Dr. N. Shavrov, Thoughts on Man and the State: A Fighting Army (in Russian), 1917, p. 15.
20
The Law of Interaction
Among the other laws which govern the existence and activity of the
collective, it is necessary to analyze the law of interaction. This law can be
expressed in the following formula: if two systems have a certain relation to
each other, the influence they exert on each other is directly proportionate to
their force. This law can first of all find support in the movement of celestial
bodies. The interaction of physical forces is, as is known, the subject of study
in mechanics; the phenomena of molecular and chemical interactions are
studied in physics and chemistry. But the same law belongs in the organic
world, and includes the correlative activity of separate individuals.
The organic world consists entirely of interactions. Presently a new
scientific discipline has even been isolated under the name of "ecology,"1
which is occupied with the study of interactions between the organism and its
environment and, in part, of interactions between plants and animals. In the
latter instances this science essentially represents phyto- and zoosociology,2
and is crowned by homo-sociology.3
From the above it is clear that the law of interaction has especial
significance in human collective existence, since the creation of the collective
itself, requiring the union of individuals, presupposes their interaction. This
interaction occurs not only between individuals of the same collective, but
also between every individual and society as a whole, and to an even greater
extent between society and its directors, or between a crowd and its leader.
On the other hand, all government is based on interaction between the ruled
and the rulers.
All relations, says P. Sorokin (The System of Sociology: Book 1, p. 81),
from manufacturing and economic to aesthetic, religious, and scientific ones,
can fall under the category of interactive relations. Simply speaking, the
interaction of two or more individuals is the fundamental concept of social
phenomena; it can serve as a model of the latter. In studying these models,
we can discover the structure of all social phenomena. Having decomposed
interaction into its component parts, we will have decomposed the most
complex social phenomena.
Lasalle was correct in saying that a constitution is worthy and acceptable
when it is a proper expression of the relationship between forces acting in a
state at any given moment, since any social existence presupposes the
conflict of classes and parties and consequently the interaction between them,
and, in essence, every collective interacts with all the other collectives it
encounters during its existence, and even with the entire surrounding world.
In order to clarify the principle of interaction, let us take the most primitive
collective—the street crowd—and see how this principle is manifested.
There have been a number of investigations into the crowd, but they,
generally speaking, contain much one-sidedness and exaggeration. Some see
in the crowd only the tendency to commit crimes, not noticing that it can be
heroic in the fullest sense of the word, and forgetting that history owes many
of the most noble human impulses to the crowd. Others do not sufficiently
distinguish between the characteristic peculiarities of a street crowd and those
of a crowd presiding in courts in the form of a jury, in parliaments, in
committees, etc. That which is correct in relation to a street crowd is not
applicable to an organized meeting, and vice versa. Thus, for example,
among the characteristic qualities of the crowd, authors name impulsiveness;
irritability; lack of reasoning, analytical, or critical ability; excessive
emotionality; extraordinary suggestibility, etc. G. Le Bon, as a matter of fact,
compares these qualities to those observed among such lower specimens of
evolution as women, children, and savages.
However, can it be said that the form of reasoning of councils, committees,
or parliaments is comparable to that of women, savages, or children? If this
were the case, could it be conceivable that where the greatest impartiality,
prudence of judgment and circumspection in decision-making is required,
humankind would allow the collective mind to act? How could we resolve
with this view the fact that the foremost nations of Europe leave the most
important questions of their existence, often deciding the fate of nations, to
be resolved by the collective mind in the form of parliaments and
international tribunals?
It is obvious that this is not the way things stand, and that which relates to
the disorganized crowd cannot be carried over to the organized meeting,
where different outlooks and deductions, cultivated by various thinkers and
accepted by individual minds from a certain viewpoint, are presented and
evaluated.
We can acknowledge the fact that the decisions of meetings cannot lay
claim to genius, although that too depends, in essence, on the significance of
the leaders' minds and on their ability to defend their opinion; but at least the
decisions of meetings are relatively rarely one-sided, which is more an
attribute of the individual mind.
Similarly, we cannot agree with those authors who maintain that reason
cannot play a role with respect to crowds, and may even have a negative
connotation to them. In the words of the same G. Le Bon, "it is impossible to
influence a crowd with reasoning because only a crude association of ideas is
accessible to it. This is why orators who can make an impression on a crowd
always appeal to its emotions, not its reason. The laws of logic have no effect
upon it" (The Psychology of Nations and Masses, p. 243). In relation to the
disorganized crowd this opinion may be considered just, but only in the case
when the crowd is already in the grip of a certain mood, which occurs quite
often. But if the crowd is calm, such as one that has collected not under the
influence of dissatisfaction or indignation, but simply to hear an orator, then
how can it be said that the laws of logic have no effect upon it? The orator
who would take Le Bon's advice and, throwing logical argument to the
winds, put emphasis on impact in an attempt to arouse the crowd's emotional
side would gain little respect. In any case, a calm crowd listens to arguments
which must be clear, simple, and convincing, and better still accompanied by
corresponding examples or images; emotional overtones are important only
when the crowd must be motivated to act.
As for the organized crowd, such as jurors, I have attended famous trials
and have several times encountered the fact that the lawyer who presents
irrefutable arguments has a greater influence on the jury than the one who
emphasizes sensation and attempts to arouse an emotional reaction in his
audience. At one point G. Le Bon (I.e., p. 293) writes:
Jurors succumb as easily as any crowd to the influence of compassion,
and very little to the influence of reasoning... .They cannot, says one
lawyer, withstand the sight of a woman breastfeeding her child, or a
procession of orphans... .In order to gain the favour of the judges, it is
enough for a woman to be attractive, according to desGlayeux.
All this is true, but it is equally true in relation to the individual mind, and
there is no great difference between a separate individual and a crowd of
jurors. The latter, in any case, yields to arguments as well as to the arousal of
emotions. I once knew a famous lawyer from Petrograd who was able, thanks
to his talent, to excite the emotions of the public and the jury, but who in
celebrated trials always allowed his assistant, who based his speech
exclusively on analysis and argument, to speak first.
It was this combination of analysis and argument in one speech and
emotional overtones in the other that had the profoundest effect on the jury.
That the crowd's inclination towards emotion is a far cry from commonplace
phenomenon is proved by the well-known fact that where a single person
could easily relent under the influence of a petitioner's tears, an aggregation
or crowd would not be affected by those same tears, which may even create
the opposite impression to what was intended.
In relation to an electoral crowd, G. Le Bon once more notes an inability to
reason, a lack of critical thinking, irritability, bias, and gullibility (I.e., p.
229). But it is the spirit of parties that acts at electoral meetings, and party
politics is not based on emotions or feelings, but on interests supported by
certain arguments; no method of arousing emotion would be sufficient to
conquer the interests of a party, expressed in its platform.
We can at least partially agree with the stand taken by G. Le Bon (I.e., p.
307) that in a crowd people are always equal and, if we are dealing with
"general issues, the vote of forty academicians is no better than the vote of
forty water-bearers." But we can in no way accept his arguments and
assumptions pertaining to universal suffrage, which he condescendingly
allows to remain in its present form only because it is a kind of contemporary
religious dogma; besides, he concedes that limited voting would have no
better results because it would be accompanied by the "tyranny of castes."
Although G. Le Bon's opinion that the "arithmetical median of all elections in
any country is an expression of the race's soul" (I.e., p 308) is quite correct,
he is not right in complementing this view with the phrase: "This soul
remains almost identical from generation to generation." The diverse
electoral results of various epochs in one and the same country prove that,
notwithstanding its immense stability, the change of a nation's "soul" is a far
from exceptional phenomenon.
Furthermore, it is worthy of attention that authors who wrote of the crowd
were inclined to maintain that a collective resolution, thanks to the interaction
of separate members of the crowd, will always be of lower quality and less
intelligent than the decision of an individual. But this is a significant
misconception. The fact is that we cannot analyze a congregation as an
exclusively collective mind, since we are usually dealing with the union of
the latter with the work of the individual mind. Any question is commonly
prepared by a specialized speaker for a meeting, where, through criticism and
discussion, the rough points of the presentation—such as overesti-mation of
facts, exaggeration, or one-sidedness—are smoothed over and eliminated, in
the manner of a lathe rounding the sharp edges of a block of wood.
Sometimes, gaps in the presentation are filled out with new arguments or
facts. Under such circumstances, can it be doubted that the results of this
work would be better and more valuable than those of the work of an
individual mind? And who does not know that the decision of a board, for
example that of the supreme court or court of arbitration, is acknowledged to
be far more authoritative than the opinion or command of an individual
representative of one administrative power or another. It goes without saying
that a board can also make erroneous dicta, which occurred, for example,
when the French Academy of Sciences rejected the possibility of man's flight
as "contrary to his nature," and when the Paris Academy of Medicine saw
nothing more exceptional than plays of the imagination in mesmerization,
which was later acknowledged by science to be the product of hypnosis, a
very real phenomenon. We can likewise mention as another example the
latest dictum of the Petrograd Medical Council, which requires that the same
procedures and measures as are taken during operations be followed during
the hypnotism of patients, which is almost equivalent to completely
prohibiting the use of hypnosis in Russia. Examples of mistakes in court that
have led to the conviction of innocent people are also widely known.
However, these errors of the collective mind are not, in essence, related to its
work, but rather to the errors and inadequate knowledge of those specialists
who spoke out on the above issues, and who, thanks to their authority, were
able to convince their audience of that which was the product of their own
ignorance.
Thus, the blame for these collective faults falls not on the work of the
collective mind, but on the work of the individual mind that in the given case
chaired a meeting and overpowered it with its authority.
It is often forgotten, when the work of individual and collective minds is
juxtaposed, that the types are essentially incomparable. The fact is that when
we are dealing with the work of the individual mind, we are first of all
dealing with a specialist who often has excellent erudition, has the
opportunity to concentrate on the given task, to make the necessary
investigations, and who can spend days, months or even years on this
process. Naturally, the resulting work is substantial and more or less exact,
although sometimes one-sided.
On the other hand, in the collective mind we are not always dealing with
such specialists, and there is no opportunity to concentrate, make
investigations over long periods of time, etc. However, meetings usually
contain versatile specialists, if not experts, in the given issue, as well as
representatives of related fields, who analyze and discuss the issue from
different viewpoints and who are usually more disinterested than separate
individuals. Collective work is distinguished by a more careful attitude to the
task at hand and a more comprehensive study of the subject; nevertheless, it
cannot have the same depth as that of the individual mind.
In this way, it is clear that the probability of a correct decision is greatest in
those cases when the work of the collective mind is combined with that of the
individual mind, whose role is to prepare the issue and present it for common
discussion or criticism.
This is what should be analyzed in the interaction of minds in collective
meetings. It should be noted at this point that the work of the individual mind
also cannot be analyzed exclusively as such. It is known that geniuses employ
the results achieved by thousands of preceding minds, in the way that a good
tradesman uses the knowledge and skills of a large number of past tradesmen.
This is why great inventions and discoveries, which seem to be the product of
an individual mind, are really the result of the activity of the collective mind
of prior generations. As we know, even general concepts owe their existence
to the creativity of the collective mind, i.e., to that of the crowd, not of the
individual.
The results of the interaction of classes and other collectives are shown not
only in the gradual mitigation of differences, but also in the level of influence
of more significant collectives on the general course of events. Even the
government of a country is in direct correspondence with the significance and
strength of a certain class or party.
The law of class conflict states that if a given class establishes its rule in
one way or another over a country, it creates its own government. If two rival
classes cannot decide which of them is superior, the government can only be
coalitional if there is the possibility of agreement between them. If there is no
agreement, and both classes are weakened by mutual conflict, the way is
cleared for the development of a dictatorship. This is what happened at the
outset of Napoleon Ill's reign in France, when Cavaignac had to step down,
thus giving the former the position of dictator.
When we are dealing with the government of a parliamentary country, the
governing organ—the ministry—is upheld by the parliamentary majority, and
if for some reason such a majority is not formed in its favor, the government
makes compromises, attracting various parties in order to gain the support of
most of the nation's representatives.
But the interaction of collectives is not limited to politics; it is manifested
to no lesser extent in the areas of science and art, since here we also have
separate parties of scientists or trends in art, in a word, separate camps that
interact with one another, continually smoothing over their differences,
sometimes even entering agreements for combined work. Once again, highest
significance is achieved by the party or trend that has greater real force.
In economics we also meet with the interaction of collective groups at
every step.
Interaction is first of all established between vendors and consumers or
purchasers, thanks to which the setting of prices (supply and demand) occurs.
The very character of the objects used by a given collective depends on the
established tastes, habits, and other conditions in which the consumers live.
Furthermore, interaction is manifested between rivals who each strive to
place themselves ahead of the competition. In the same way, interaction
between various groups of consumers is established, especially when there is
a lack of goods, etc.
Finally, in international relations we are likewise dealing with the
interaction of every national collective with its neighbor and with all
collectives in general that it encounters in any way. Here, as in every other
case, the result of interaction is the authority of one or several collectives
over all others. But in the end, the course of international life is determined
by the force resultant from the interaction of all national collectives that come
in contact with one another in order to establish international ties.
Notes
1 See Haeckel, Generelle Morphologie d Organismen, 1866, p. 286. Berlin. Rd.
2 See Sukachev, An Introduction to the Study of Plant Communities. Morozov, The Forest as a Plant
Community. Vagner, "Sociology in Botanies" (in Russian), Priroda, 1912. Henslow, "L'écologie au
point de vue de l'évolution des végétaux, Scientia t.XIII, Annex 87-107.
3 Waxveiler, Esquisse d'une sociologie, pp. 1-87; P. Sorokin, System of Sociology (in Russian), p. 10
and ff.
21
The Law of Compensation or
Substitution
In this case we are dealing with a law in accordance with which any motion
or any other form of energy, upon encountering an obstacle to its
manifestation, is replaced by another motion or energy (also essentially a
kind of movement). There is no need to dwell on the fact that this principle is
the direct consequence of the principle of conservation of energy and is
supported by countless examples of phenomena in the physical environment.
Moreover, it is the basis of the so-called principle of the unity of energy. In
chemical reactions, whenever one substance is replaced by another, we
likewise meet with this law.
In the organic world, the above principle is also manifested everywhere,
since, for example, the artificial inhibition of the development of roots and
stems in any vegetation is compensated by the growth of new roots and stems
from other parts of the plant. In the animal kingdom, the absence of an organ
is compensated by the intensified function of other organs. In the functions of
the nervous system we meet with the substitution of destroyed or degenerate
systems and fibers by healthy systems and filaments.
In correlative activity, we also have the substitution of one action, one
forgotten word, one unsatisfied demand by another. Thus, sexual needs are
often replaced by artistic or religious quests and vice versa. As a matter of
fact, the method of sublimation in the treatment of neuroses is based on this
principle.
The same process of substitution can be observed in the activity of the
collective. When its mental development is hindered, as it was, for example,
in Russia under the tsarist regime, it is replaced by an astonishing rise in the
popularity of mystical doctrines, gambling, and alcoholism. When the
government forbids open agitation, the latter finds expression in underground
propaganda. In the interests of inhibiting the latter, governments often
attempt to divert a national movement by, for example, participating in a war.
In order to distract a conquered nation from aggressive acts towards its
conqueror, deft diplomats strive to redirect the energy of the vanquished
nation by supporting its attempts to capture other nations. Thus, Bismarck,
having taken Alsace and Lorraine from France, encouraged its colonial
politics. Wilhelm II, not having understood the good sense of these tactics
and having made a false step in provoking the Agadir incident, set the stage
for the world war that would prove unfavorable for Germany, as well as for
himself.
The law of compensation played a large role in the dissemination of
Christianity at the end of the second and beginning of the third century. As is
known, under the Antonines and especially under the Severi an astounding
awakening of religious thought was manifested in Roman society, along with
a thirst for belief in the exalted, spiritual, and immortal.
This was really a reaction, in the form of the replacement of social
philosophy by a new doctrine, to the indulgence in sensual pleasures that was
so widespread at the time. Moreover, the Roman Empire accepted into its
pantheon the gods of all conquered nations, and naturally the existence of
countless gods undermined belief in them and weakened the social
significance of pagan religion.
Attempts had been made, in connection to the above aspiration of society
towards the exalted and spiritual, to moralize ancient myths and inspire pagan
religion, but these did not satisfy society. It was at this time that Christianity,
with its doctrine of a single God in heaven and spirit of love and forgiveness,
saw that it could make use of the reaction and quickly established its rights.
The heroism of the first Christian martyrs, their unshakable belief in the
righteousness of their doctrine and the incompatibility of Christianity with
other religions, from which stemmed bloody persecution, completed its
victory over paganism.
The fact that the realization of every great thing in social life is often
connected to terrible trials, and that only sometimes, thanks to these trials and
to the suffering of its defendants, does it acquire final acknowledgement, is
based on the same law of compensation.
This law also explains the remarkable contradictions that are manifested,
especially during periods of insurrection, in the collective life of nations.
Thus, in the most tragic days of the Terror, filled with almost incessant
executions, when thousands of people died on the scaffold, all places of
entertainment were full to the overflowing. Such was the situation during the
Great French Revolution and during the Russian Revolution. The reason lies
partly in the fact that the populace, previously deprived of all forms of
pleasure as a result of poverty and oppression, having attained freedom,
pursued the various entertainments that were previously inaccessible to it,
and partly in the need to relieve the oppressive conditions of life. This is why
all forms of diversion enjoyed full attendance while the guillotine reigned in
Paris, and why the dancing-halls were full during the Russian Revolution at a
time of ceaseless executions and looting.
Something analogous can be seen in the acts of the Parisian prisoners who,
despite the Sword of Damocles—the guillotine—that already hung over
them, indulged without any sense of moderation in amorous escapades. Here,
for example, is how Cabanis and L. Nass describe life at the Conciergerie
prison of Paris, based on contemporary accounts:
Having passed the first grille, of which there were at least four, you
reached an area girdled by iron chains. Here, meetings with visitors were
allowed; usually only women came to see the prisoners, and were greeted,
of course, with the greatest affability... Here, in the words of one
contemporary, husbands became lovers, and lovers redoubled their
affection. It was decided without any formal agreement to no longer heed
the laws of social propriety, which were "valid only when it was possible
to postpone expressions of fondness to another, more convenient place and
time." The tenderest of kisses were recklessly dispensed right and left.
Under cover of darkness or of a commodious skirt, intimacies gratifying
even the fieriest impulses were brazenly exchanged. At times, these
ecstasies were interrupted by the advent of the unfortunates who had just
been sentenced to execution, and who were led through that area from the
hall in which the tribunal presided. For a moment, a death-like silence
would reign, frightened looks would be exchanged, and then .. .then the
tender words, the kisses, the fervid declarations would recommence,
everything would acquire its former aspect and take its course.
The same could be observed at the main entrance. Husbands, wives, and
lovers filled the long benches that lined the walls, embracing and caressing
one another with the same happiness and tranquility as if they lay on a bed
of roses. Rarely, some wept and bemoaned their fate.
No less intense love affairs unfolded in other wings of the prison. During
the day, inmates occupied themselves with their toilet, dressing and
adorning their bodies, and in the evening love came into its own. All was
employed in its favour: the evening dusk, the shadow of a passing cloud,
the tiredness of the guard, of the overseers, the modesty and sleep of the
other prisoners. (I.e., p. 132)
The process of communication by signs and symbols is also based on the
principle of compensation or substitution. It is necessary to keep in mind that
any sociality allows for the development of individuals' abilities to signal one
another. Primitive signaling is the language of mime. The effect of fright
experienced by one individual is a signal of danger for another, while
exclamations of despair are cries for help, etc. The mime of sexual arousal is
once again a sign of a certain type, leading to the corresponding stimulation
of the opposite sex. The grasshopper's chirp, the bird's twitter or song, the
dog's bark, the sheep's bleat, the rabbit's thump, the cow's low, the horse's
neigh, the macaque's squeals and other noises—all these are essentially sound
signals, lying at the foundation of animal language, completely
understandable to animals of the same species and attaining, among humans,
the level of complex articulate speech.
These signals are, of course, not always sound. They can also be photic,
such as the glow of a firefly and the change in color of some animals' skin;
mechanical, such as the movement of an ant's antennae and the expressive
poses or movements of many animals, human gestures; olfactory, in the form
of animals' secretions when in heat, etc. But they always serve as
characteristic indicators of the animal's condition—a signal or sign of its
condition to others:
When a deer throws up its head, all other deer do the same. This is a sign,
which means "Listen." If the first deer cannot see the object that attracted
its attention and seemed suspicious, it emits a low sound. This is a word
that means "Caution." If the deer then finds that the object is not only
suspicious but also dangerous, it employs a further form of language—
intonation: in place of the low sound "Caution," it makes a harsh, loud cry,
signifying "Run, in order to save your life." This is the nature of language:
sign or gesture, sound or word, and intonation (H. Drummond, I.e., p.
181).
It goes without saying that the higher an animal is in terms of
development, the more complex its signal is. Let us take the dog as an
example. C. Darwin noted from four to five variations in the barking of a
dog. But that animal also expresses its condition through growling, whining,
and also through the movement of its tail, ears, or entire body, its expression,
etc. Here, language is already so mul-tifaceted that it exposes many aspects of
the dog's internal condition, which could not be manifested by, for example, a
bird. Monkeys have up to six vocal variations and a primitive language that
serves to signify certain objects, as well as a diverse body language.
Human speech, which arose through the evolution of the primitive
language of sound reflexes, gestures, and mimed movements, is the product
of sociality and developed thanks to the continuous communication between
people, without which signals in the form of speech could never have existed.
In any case, a number of facts support the presumption that this ability to
signal is not inherited, and consequently not inborn, but is acquired by
experience in the form of higher or associative reflexes.1 The fact that a
person deaf from birth is also always mute but replaces the lack of speech
with a profusion of gestures upholds this standpoint.2
On the other hand, someone who is blind from birth has poor gestures and
mime, but excellent intonation. A person deprived from birth of
communication with his kind, as was Hasper Hans, has no developed speech.
Finally, someone who is born to parents of one nation and is taken in infancy
to another country acquires the latter's language without retaining any trace of
his parent's speech.
It should be noted that the language of gestures and mime develops under
the control of sight, and the language of sounds under the control of hearing.
Actually, a significant similarity is observed between the gestures and mime
of deaf-mutes and those of primitive peoples, which is due to the fact that the
limited sounds produced by the former and the latter force them to highly
develop gesture and movement.
Even the gestures employed for abbreviation are common to various
nations. All this would be puzzling, if it were not explained by the fact that
those gestures and mimed movements are reproductions of illustrative,
indicative, and other movements which cannot differ from nation to nation
because they correspond to the same external object.
"Mallery brought together a few foreigners from the Utah territory and one
deaf-mute. The latter recounted to them the long story of a certain robbery,
which contained dialogue; it turned out that they understood each other
perfectly" (Ribot, The Evolution of General Ideas, pp. 48-49).
As was mentioned, deaf-mutes develop mime and gesture to the highest
possible level, thanks to which they easily understand each other and, without
being taught, use more or less identical illustrative and indicative gestures.
These form a terminology and simple syntax. The latter consists of the order
or sequence of the gestures one after another and their combination.
For example, let us say that the gesture for a child is the indication of small
stature, and that an infant is designated by an imitation of breastfeeding,
carrying or rocking. A dog is represented by barking accompanied by
movements of the head and mouth. Loss is represented by the futile search
for a dropped object, "forget" by a hand drawn across the forehead and a
shrug of the shoulders. A hand placed on the heart demonstrates love, while
hate requires the same gesture complemented by negative shaking of the
head, etc.
As for syntax, according to the investigations of several authors (Scott,
Taylor, Romanes, and others) it does not contain parts of speech or terms of a
certain linguistic significance, but is based exclusively on the sequence of
signs/gestures, with signs arranged in the order of their relative importance.
To be precise, the subject comes before the predicate, the object before the
verb, and the passive before the active; all unnecessary parts are eliminated.
For example, the gestures for the expression "I beat somebody with a stick"
would come in the following order: I-somebody-beat-stick, and for the
expression "I was beaten by somebody with a stick," the sequence of gestures
would be as follows: somebody-I-beat-stick. This syntax is, of course,
extremely poor and can hardly be compared even to the limited syntax of the
feeble-minded. Moreover, in most cases religious, moral, cosmic, and
metaphysical concepts are inaccessible to such uneducated deaf-mutes.
However, the latter are not bereft of creativity (Ribot, p. 43).
Why exactly did humanity develop a primarily spoken language? I have
written about the anatomical conditions that assist the development of a
language of sounds in another work.3 Nonetheless, we cannot overlook the
especial advantages of a spoken language over one of gestures, and the fact
that the former gave a certain direction to human symbolism.
There is reason to think that, at first, man had the opportunity to use both
forms of language, but that spoken language gained precedence due to the
following conditions. A language of gestures requires the use of the hands,
hindering their application to other employments; it is not effective in the
dark, or across large distances. Furthermore, gesture and mime are of an
exclusively imitative origin, as a result of which they are connected to
concrete stimuli and are ill adapted to generalizations, although it cannot be
denied that further development of the language of gestures may lead to the
creation of generalizing signs. Support for this possibility can be found in the
existing gestures for abbreviation. On the other hand, speech allows for
development and subtle differentiation with respect to external sound stimuli
and to the manifestation of the voice or sounds, in relation both to intonation
and to the complexity of sounds, and all this with minimal expenditure of
energy.
The depiction of written speech was at first derived from the language of
gestures (the so-called ideographic script). M. Müller provides a number of
examples that are quite edifying in this respect. The similarity to gestures is
found in the isolation of terms, in their order, and in the concrete illustration
of actions. He cites a letter from a citizen of the Caroline Islands to the
captain of a Spanish ship: a person with outstretched hands is depicted at the
top (request), below and to the left of whom are drawn the proffered objects:
five large and seven smaller shells; directly to the right of this are the objects
required in exchange: three large and four small fishing hooks, two pieces of
iron and two axes.
Signaling has a huge significance not only in human collective life, but
also in that of animals. A flock of birds calmly feeds until its sentry cries out
and, raising its wings, gives the signal for all to fly from the approaching
enemy.
The same is known in relation to quadrupeds that live in herds. The entire
herd obeys the directions of its leader, who signals how it should behave.
Human symbolism, other than writing and drawing, takes on the following
forms:
1. inarticulate sounds in the form of simple reflexes;
2. inarticulate sounds in the form of associative reflexes;
3. articulate sounds as a further development of associative reflexes, in the
form of sound imitation;
4. articulate sounds as a further development of associative reflexes
through prefixes, complications, and suffixes (speech);
5. reflexive mime and gesture, such as crying, laughter, etc., as a result of
external excitants; defensive movements of the hands to cover the face
and eyes; and removal of the hands from acute irritants;
6. mime and gesture in the form of associative reflexes;
7. pantomimical gestures as areproduction of actions (indicative,
illustrative, etc.).
The significance of signal in the social conditions of human life is
acquired, as in individual life, through the experience and training of
members of the collective.
A cry of despair, according to general experience, signifies misfortune,
while shooting, once again in accordance with the experience of all those
who have come in contact with firearms, is assumed to be accompanied by
grave casualties. This is enough for both one and the other, even in the
absence of true misfortune or of any casualties, to have the usual
consequences in a crowd, i.e., panic in the first case and answering shots in
the second.
An alarm bell, as is known, is sounded in case of fire to summon
assistance, whereas a church's bells call its congregation to attend the service.
Thus, both serve as a signal to leave one's house—in one case, to go to the
scene of a fire, and in the other to attend church. Analogous conditions can be
observed in all other cases.
In human life, signals are encountered at every step.
Let us say that the training of troops is taking place. All await a certain
signal. The command is given, and the military collective comes into motion.
Battle also commences upon an agreed signal, in the form of a command, and
all further developments are likewise in accord with signals.
Take a team of firefighters. A signal given from the tower mobilizes a
mass of firefighters, and each sets about performing his task. In an instant, all
is ready for departure; when the fire is reached, the words of the team,
playing the role of signals, activate a mass of people. Having completed their
mission, the firefighters rest until they once again hear the fire alarm, calling
them to duty.
Another example is a mass of people, say, guests at a ball. For a while they
wander up and down the room. But now the music, serving as a signal,
commences and you see first one pair, then another separate from the crowd
and begin dancing.
You are in the theatre, awaiting the commencement of the play. The public
is gradually arriving, and at the appointed time the conductor appears in the
orchestra. He lifts his baton, which is a signal for the musicians, and a
moment of tense concentration for the audience. But now the curtain rises,
the play begins. Characters come onto the stage, speak to each other and to
the public. There are signals for this mass of people on the stage that are not
noticeable to the audience. These signals are given by the prompter, and
consist of phrases that should be pronounced at certain times, and reminders
of actions that should accompany the given scene.
Observe, furthermore, how a choir sings. First of all, the precentor gives a
signal in the form of a tuning fork's sound; then, at a gesture of his hand, the
choir begins singing. Otherwise, the choir may sing after the wave of a
conductor's baton, with the initiation of music, or, finally, upon the
termination of an introductory tune. All these are essentially signals that
bring on a certain form of collective action.
Let us go on to collective gymnastic exercise. What guarantees the
coordination of the actions of the many people who participate in
complicated gymnastics? Once again, it is a command or signal that
determines the beginning of activity.
Suppose you are in a school where a mass of students are employed in
collective mental labor. During break, they wander through the corridors
shouting and making noise. But now the bell rings and everyone rushes to
their class to take their places. Little more than three-quarters of an hour pass,
and another bell announces the beginning of the next break. In the class itself,
the teacher may signal the commencement of collective work such as a
dictation.
While attending communal games, or even horse-races, you can once again
observe that collective action is initiated by a certain signal.
If you study the collective actions of a workers' co-operative, you will
likewise see that coordination of common labor is achieved by a command or
by the singing of a workers' song.
In large collectives, a bell serves to arouse everyone, to summon them to
meals, to their studies, and to bed. In a word, signal plays an important role in
all cases that require coordinated collective activity.
In all the above instances, we are dealing with an organized collective, in
which the signal itself is a previously appointed and habitual sign. Even in
unorganized collectives, the signal holds an analogous significance. Thus, for
a crowd that is confined to a small area, the cry of "Fire!" can serve as a
signal to the development of panic. At large conferences it is often difficult to
hear anything over the din of conversation, but as soon as the chairman of the
meeting rises from the table to pick up the bell, everyone quietens.
Likewise, in a crowd it is enough to shout: "Stop that thief!" for a few
people to take on pursuit of the said wrongdoer.
A provocational shot often serves to begin a street fight and lead to civil
carnage.
The alarm-bell sends people to the scene of a fire. Church bells call them
to church and prayer.
A cry of desperation sets a crowd in motion.
At large meetings, where a certain decision must be made, in other words
when the collective must express a certain attitude to whatever occurrence,
one word is pronounced by the chairperson and a majority of raised hands
determines the nature of the meeting's resolution.
As can be seen from the given examples, signal is an indispensable
condition in various instances of collective activity. It is in some cases a
warning sign that stimulates collective activity, whether offensive or
defensive. In other cases, it can serve as a source of coordination of collective
activity, without which the latter would never be realized. Finally, it
determines the termination point of common action.
This clearly outlines the significance of signal in collective activity, where
it has, perhaps, even greater importance than in individual existence, since
without it the lives of many people would naturally be endangered, and
collective activity itself would in many cases be unrealizable.
Let us note at this point that the development of sign communication is
related to the question of social heredity, thanks to which the experience of
individuals of one generation is passed on to those of the next, establishing
the social succession that has determined the historical development of social
origins.4
Furthermore, the sign or signal in social life plays the role of a symbol;
gestures and the spoken word are essentially symbols of surrounding
phenomena or of certain interactions and actions. In the life of civilized
nations, symbolism plays a very broad role, giving rise to a number of
artificial symbols, often having a specific historic origin.
Thus, the regalia of tsarist rule, insignia, coats of arms, etc.—all this is
nothing more than an expression of social symbol. Symbolism is manifested
everywhere in social life, as, for example, in customs, in religious
ceremonies, in the so-called language of flowers, and even in the style of
dress or jewelry.
Some phenomena of symbolism are transferred from generation to
generation in the form of habit, having lost their initial meaning.
Thus, the burning of bonfires on Midsummer's Night, being a vestige of
pagan times, has lost all of its original significance. On the other hand, some
remaining marriage customs have survived and retained their symbolic aspect
to this day.
The ceremonial side of religion, carried out during communal worship, is
full of symbolic meaning, and there is not one movement of the priest during
a church service that does not symbolize one event or another from the life of
Jesus and from his doctrine.
The same should be said of all communal ceremonies and rites in general,
such as communal celebrations, dances, funerals, games, etc. Everywhere, we
meet with social symbolism in its most variegated manifestations. As a
matter of fact, court etiquette is also resplendent in symbolism, being based
on the deification of the ruler and his elevation to the state of idol. N. Kareev
says in a newspaper article:
Such idols, to whom divine homage was paid, incense burned, and
sacrifices made— the incense of glorification and flattery, and sacrifices
of reason and conscience, human pride and honour—have at one time held
the highest positions in all countries.
From the times of ancient Egypt, where the pharaoh was "king and God,"
this tradition of pharaohism, of the deification of the ruler, spans the ages;
it can be seen in the hellenistic kingdom of the Ptolemies, in the Roman
Empire, and in a more recent form in Christian Europe during the period
of absolutism.
Was not the monarch made the representative of God on earth? Was not
his rule ascribed to God's will? Were not kings named earthly Gods? Did
not Bossuet exclaim: "Kings—you are gods!" and call them "Christs" in
the sense of the Lord's Anointed? Was not the official epithet "adored"
applied to monarchs? Was not their person "holy"? Were not many
generations raised with such attitudes towards the ruling power, and did
this not become a deeply rooted tradition? (N. Kareev, Nash Vek, 1917)
But this deification of the ruler can also be translated to the collective
power of the nation, to which J. Rousseau assigned absoluteness and
infallibility in his "Social Contract." National government is likewise treated
to the servility that was once given rulers of the monarchial era.
Here is how, in one newspaper article from the beginning of the Russian
Revolution, the new national leadership was glorified (I take this quotation
from Kareev's article):
To her majesty the Nation, proud and triumphant, wise and powerful, the
heroic nation, the selfless nation, the righteous nation, the nation of clear
conscience, the nation-prophet, the teacher of all nations, etc.
The Russian people were then praised as
pure and righteous, on the path to God's kingdom. Honor to you, honor to
the Russian nation, mysterious and radiant as He whose blessing casts
light on all your future enterprises. Honor to the nation-Saviour, honour to
the nation that harbours in its womb shining revelation for the whole
world. Honor to the nation that will teach and be loved by all that is
disturbed, restless, and without peace or happiness on this Earth.
In the national intellect the author, as a matter of fact, observed "something
approaching the image and likeness of that perfection which has been the
goal of spiritual aspiration from the day of the Creation."
It could be countered that this deification represents the view of only one
person. No, this text was printed in a widely read newspaper, apparently for
the enlightenment of a mass of subscribers. Speeches in the same vein were
made at meetings, deifying the national government or at least establishing its
infallibility.
Lectures, entitled "What does 'a great noise from a northern country'
signify?" were even given on the prophetic prediction in biblical times of the
Russian Revolution.
This theme was addressed numerous times by one member of the order of
the "Eastern Star," and attracted a huge audience. Yet the deification of
national power was not limited to newspaper and lectoral eulogies.
Kerensky, embodying power as the national favorite in the first part of the
Russian Revolution, did, in fact, take on the role of highest, autonomous
leader of the people, before whom the same ceremonies had to be enacted as
before the true, hereditary monarch who had ruled "by the grace of God." All
the prerogatives of absolute power belonged to him, for at critical moments
of the Russian Revolution all ministers handed their portfolios over to him,
giving him the unqualified right to control their fate. It goes without saying
that accommodation for the new ruler had to be the same (the Winter Palace)
as that occupied by the tsars before the revolution.
Symbolism also penetrates our everyday life in the form of the admiration
and flattery of those holding powerful positions, and likewise in the form of
politeness towards all individuals, be they of lower social standing or higher,
with the exception of family members, with whom there is no need for either
etiquette or conventional politeness in view of its utter pointlessness.
The significance of symbols in social life, however, is not limited to the
above, for symbolism is manifested in such reflections of social life as
sculpture, architecture, art, literature, and poetry. Moreover, mime and
mannerisms are full of symbolism, which is not difficult to discover through
even a very superficial analysis of these phenomena.
Social unity, as any complex object, can be symbolized, usually by a
banner, sign, or emblem, e.g., uniform, flag, etc. The role of the symbol may
be played by a person or even a group of people, e.g., a presidium, military
leadership, etc., that is an expression or representation of the given collective.
That same symbol is often a factor conducive to unity, cementing ties
between many members of the community by reminding them of their
association to the given collective and binding them to a certain solidarity
and code of behavior, in agreement with the interests of the community. This
cementing role of symbols is discussed by P. Sorokin in his System of
Sociology (Part I, pp. 322-323).
Furthermore, are not various badges, signs, and orders, heraldry, religious
and state ceremonies, dances, even games and celebrations, social symbols?
In view of the fact that symbolic signs, thanks to established customs,
embody historic facts, they have so powerful an influence over people's
minds as to be fetishes. The regimental banner can serve as an example. I
remember how, during the World War, one of the Russian nurses happened
to find, on a battlefield already taken by the Germans, a banner belonging to
a regiment that had been defeated along with the other divisions under
General Samsonov's command in Eastern Prussia. She kept that banner with
her throughout the duration of her captivity, and, being subsequently
released, smuggled it with the greatest precautions through Germany and
other countries. And at the moment when she, released along with other
nurses thanks to a recent agreement, crossed our border from Finland, I
(being accidentally on the same train) could myself witness the effect of this
fetish on the minds of the soldiers. When the nurses unfurled the rescued
banner from the window of the carriage, the few soldiers who were on the
platform, and who had no relation whatsoever to the destroyed regiment,
reacted to the pennant as to a holy object. Some of them crossed themselves,
others cried, still others kissed the ragged cloth in veneration.
It goes without saying that the nurse thought herself a heroine, and felt she
should personally present the banner to the sovereign. Such is the power of a
symbol that embodies the strength of a regiment as a military unit, in its
historic past and in the present. The banner ceases to be an object, it is not
even a simple symbol, it is something holy for which life is sacrificed without
second thought, and which is an honor to carry during battle despite the fact
that doing so places the standard-bearer in great danger, since he has no
ability to protect himself.
A similar deification of objects can be found in religion, for example, with
respect to pieces of wood (as among the Australian aboriginals), rocks,
mountains, plants, creatures (e.g., totemic animals, worship of bulls,
crocodiles, and of the ibis in Egypt). No less significant is the regard shown
for objects such as coats-of-arms, symbols of the judiciary, medals, amulets,
uniforms (symbolizing military honor), icons crosses, statues of the saints,
the Papal slipper, countless relics; and this type of worship takes place in all
areas of life (Sorokin, System of Sociology: Part I, p. 186).
The extent to which the crowd is attracted to symbolism can be seen in the
establishment of new celebrations by the Convention during the great
revolution in France, as a result of the appearance at the same time of the new
Cult of Reason. It is interesting to note that these celebrations were seemingly
a copy of ancient church ceremonies, replete in allegory, proving just how
much symbolism is a necessity of the masses.
Here, for example, is the manner in which a typical celebration of the
Triumph of Reason was held at the Cathedral of Notre Dame on the 20
Brumaire, year 11, or rather on the 10 December, 1793 (see Cabanis and L.
Nass, I.e., pp. 332-333):
A mountain was erected at the centre of the church, covering the choir. A
round portico in the Grecian style was located at its peak, bearing the
inscription "philosophy" on its façade. On either side of this were busts of
philosophy's apostles: Voltaire, Rousseau, Franklin, and Montesquieu. A
sacred Fire of Truth burned on the slope of the mountain. To the
accompaniment of music, two groups of girls in tricolour sashes, crowned
with flowers and holding torches, climbed the mountain and met at the
altar, where each payed homage to the holy flames. Then a beautiful
woman, clothed in a white garment and red head-dress, came out of the
temple. This was the embodiment of freedom, before whom all the
republicans bowed to the strains of a laudatory cantata.
All customs pertaining to various social institutions are once again proof of
the significance of symbolism to the masses. Even during the period of
revolutionary persecution of the church, the state funerals at Petrograd on 7
May did not much differ from religious funerals. Besides, are not celebrations
for whatever reason, not to mention respect for rank and various military
ceremonies at times of peace, permeated by symbolism from beginning to
end?
In a word, the life of the collective is full of symbolism due to the fact that
the symbol facilitates the mastering of complex interactions by replacing
them with comparatively simple and easily reproducible signs, often having a
historical significance.
Finally, the most important actions of individual historic figures become
symbols for the following generations. Prometheus' heroic act became the
symbol of martyrdom for truth; Jesus' heroism is the symbol of gentleness
and endurance; Svyatoslav's bravery is the symbol of courage; while the
conquests of Napoleon, Suvorov, and others are symbols of military strategy.
The ancients' stoicism, the epicureans' doctrine, the Cynic Diogenes' behavior
—all this has turned into contemporary symbolism.
In this way, all significant heroic deeds of individuals, having outlived
themselves in the history of nations, become nothing more than symbolic
forms to subsequent generations. Even important events take on the status of
symbols, e.g., "Sedan," "Tsushima," "Panama," etc.
We also meet with the phenomenon of compensation in national creativity
in the form of so-called personification, observable in relation to one
abstraction or another. In religion, we meet everywhere with personification
of gods, in place of whatever abstraction. The same, obviously, can be
observed in many traditional legends.
In Pareto's words:
The highest level of personification exists where a name is given to
abstraction, uniformity, or emotion, transforming the latter into objective
entities. Then personification progresses, turning into anthropomorphism.
Through the acquisition of gender, personification becomes more
concrete. Such personifications originate of their own accord, aside from
the participation of consciousness.
In this way, symbolic definitions are inevitably sources of personification,
and it can be enough just to name a certain abstract concept for it to be
personified 
and 
anthropomorphized 
via 
substitution. 
Such 
is 
the
personification in traditional folktales of Miserable Sorrow, Jealousy, etc. In
the ancient world, the same process of personification led to the creation of
such gods as Fortuna and Victoria. And according to Boudain (Les cuites
paiens dans I'empire romain: Part /, pp. 415-416), there were many other,
less famous gods and goddesses in Rome who all embodied some
metaphysical concept, e.g., Aequitas, Bonus eventus, Concordia, Copia,
Disciplina, Fama, Felicitas, Fides publica, Gloria, Chronos, Juventus,
Libertas, Mens, Pax, Pietas, Prosperitas, Providentia, Salus generis humani,
Sanctitas, Vertus.5 It goes without saying that the personification of one
abstraction or another achieves the pinnacle of its development when a
legend is created that confines the abstraction not only to a certain
personality, but also to various actions.
The fetishization and deification of people is widely known. Here, a role is
played not only by position, with which an aureole of fame, power, etc. is
already historically associated (e.g., the deification of tsars), but also by
success in actions (e.g., the deification of an of ficer, ruler, demagogue, etc.).
Examples of such deification are numerous, including the cult of Roman
emperors, the cult of the holy pope, etc. There is even an example of the
Zulus' deification of their king. In a letter addressed to His Majesty, the
following can be read:
O Pedzuln, king of kings, king of the heavens! What are glorious lands in
comparison to our great king? What comes of the mighty forests before
the great Elephant? His trunk hews the branches of trees. His breath in the
face of enemies is as fire falling on dried earth. Enemies are devoured by
fire before the person of the king of kings. Being himself the king of fire,
he rises to the azure heavens; he sends his lightning to the clouds and
forces the rain to fall from them. Mountains, forests, and you, the green
fields! Hear the voice of the son of Maggoban, king of the heavens.6
We could give many other analogous examples of the deification of
oriental kings, but the above is sufficient to prove how personalities achieve,
under certain conditions, in the eyes of one collective or another, a position
on the same level as that of a god.
Notes
1 See V. Bekhterev, "The biological development of mimicry;' Vestnik Znaniia and sep. pub.(in
Russian), St. Petersburg.
2 There are also experimentally ascertained facts which demonstrate that deaf animals are unable to
produce [vocal] sounds. In experiments on dogs performed in our laboratory by Dr. Larionov,
animals whose hearing was lost after their temporal lobes had been removed eventually lost the
ability to bark.
3 V. Bekhterev, Objective Psychology (in Russian), St. Petersburg.
4 For details see the following of my works:
"The significance of social selection to the development of organisms" (in
Russian), VestnikZnaniia, 1912; "Hormonism and its significance in the
development of organisms" (in Russian), Priroda, 1916; and others.
5 Pareto, Trattato di sociologia generala, Part I.
6 M. Kovalevskii, Sociology (in Russian), Part II, pp. 194-95.
22
The Law of Dependent Relations
We will not tarry here on the significance of the law of dependent relations in
the inorganic and organic worlds, where all things obviously have a certain
relation to each other. This relationship is defined in the following manner:
An effect can be traced to its direct cause, which is in turn an effect, and
so on. This chain of consecutive causes and effects of indefinite length—a
chain in which a given phenomenon forms but one link—is in essence a
linear sequence. An infinitely large number of such sequences can coexist,
and these sequences may cross, so that one and the same phenomenon, if it
is the product of several different phenomena, becomes an effect among a
number of sequences of effects; though these may share a common origin,
they will in the future remain disparate and completely disconnected.1
We will only be occupied here with events of the social order, which make
up the substantial part of our analysis.
There is not a single social movement that has not been the consequence of
preceding events. The fact of the matter is that the development of social life
always follows a particular sequence, just as cause generates effect.
The justification of establishing this law in the supraorganic world can be
seen in the fact that wherever a social movement is delayed, external or
internal reasons for this delay can be determined, as can the reasons for a
developed social movement's gradual change in direction.
If the actions of separate personalities are "causal" and so determined by
the successively transmitted influence of certain people on others, then it is
clear that all historical events also have a similar "causality" and succession.
In fact, it is easy to be convinced by historical analysis that contemporary
events have their origins in past human actions; going successively from
present events to past ones, we will quickly reach prehistoric times, and even
be forced to trace contemporary events back to the most primeval epoch of
human life and still farther into the depths of the centuries.
As is known, a number of authors, from Aristotle to Marx, Engels, and the
latest sociologists and economists, have already discussed the question of the
correlation of various phenomena in social life and have attempted to clarify
the regularity and conditionality of diverse social phenomena.
One of the more productive and exact methods in this respect is the
statistical method, thanks to which a number of social correlations have been
determined, such as the correlation between the development of crime and
social inequality, cost of living, and many other conditions; between the cost
of produce and the magnitude of salaries; between the extent of dispossession
of land and the growth of the working class; between birth and death rates,
etc.
Other scientific methods of determining the regularity of social phenomena
include the facts provided by genetic sociology, which trace the development
of social life and outline the displacement of some phenomena by others.
This course enables us to ascertain a particular regularity in relation to the
self-sufficiency of every independent society, to the development of marriage
and the family, and to the development of certain aspects of the economic life
of organizations of power and legal regulations, as well as the regularities
between means of subsistence and population size established by Condorcet
and Malthus, between the division of labor and the stratification of society
into groups and classes, between the ruling class and the organization of
power. Both the statistical method and the method of genetic sociology give
the opportunity to anticipate events in social life and establish the dependence
of some phenomena on others.
Let us take once again the example of famine, when the behavior of the
masses is subject to all the consequences inevitably following this factor. In
this case, all the endeavors of the masses are directed towards the
procurement of nourishment. We are dealing first of all with the possible
economization of produce, with its more uniform distribution, and with the
intensification of production of foodstuffs through increased development of
agriculture or market-gardening and the invention of food surrogates. Since
collective concentration is determined by the same factor, famine is
manifested not only in actions, but also in all conversations; everywhere, the
dominant topic of discussion is the question of nutrition, the size of the food
rations, etc. This preoccupation is reflected in speech reflexes, with the
spoken definitions of famine, rations, and foodstuffs being discussed in all
forms. Moreover, the government itself, as the leader of the collective, is
subject to the aforementioned factor and interferes in personal life, imposing
upon it the interests of the whole collective, for the most part with respect to
the question of nourishment. Thanks to this, limiting measures are
implemented on the export of produce, and means of acquiring extra
foodstuffs are developed; state or social allotment of produce is implemented;
communalization of farming is established; incentive measures are taken
towards the intensification of agriculture and livestock raising, etc.
If all these measures prove to be insufficient, the natural consequences are
the emigration of a fraction of the population and the organization of attacks
on neighboring territories in the form of raids, wars, and marches, in order to
seize foodstuffs in exchange for other produce and to obtain new colonies.
Finally, the equal distribution of property takes place within the country not
only through the forceful communalization of foodstuffs, but also through the
rise of theft and burglary, and lastly through revolts by the deprived classes
who wish to attain power and gain better food products. It goes without
saying that all these inescapable consequences of famine can be exposed on
the basis of many documented facts and depicted as numbers on the grounds
of statistical material. As is known on the basis of statistics, periods of famine
are always associated with limited population growth due to a lower birth and
higher death rate-that unpleasant proof of social calamity—regardless of
whether this heightened death rate is the result of epidemics and greater
susceptibility to illness, or of an increase in the number of the murders and
suicides that usually accompany the development of starvation.2
If we look at a factor of another nature, for example the sexual
dissatisfaction of the collective as a result of, say, an imbalance of the sexes,
then we will once again have the total dependence of the behavior of the
masses on this factor, namely: intensified discussion of sexual themes among
the dissatisfied segments of the population; the limitation, within reason, of
sexual encounters; their replacement by surrogates such as onanism,
homosexuality, and the movement of the masses in the direction of possible
regulation of marital relations, e.g., polygamy in the case of an excess of
women, polyandry with an excess of men; the implementation of various
measures controlling internal order, e.g., compulsory marital cohabitation, as
was stipulated sometimes among convicts on lengthy prison labor, or those
exiled to uninhabited areas (Sakhalin Island); the abduction of women from
neighboring areas, even mass assault to this end on neighbors (witness the
abduction of Sabine women in Roman history); mass rape of women during
attacks on defenseless towns and villages, etc. Intellectual dissatisfaction, in
its turn, also has significant social consequences, characterized by a higher
demand for books in libraries and in the market; a rise in the amount of
original and translated works; a greater number of research organizations
(laboratories, institutes, scientific expeditions, etc.); a larger number of
people entering learning institutions, etc.
Obviously, periods of great social upheaval, requiring a heightened pace of
life, lead to an increased popularity of mental stimulants in the form of
narcotics (alcohol, tobacco, opium, morphine, ether, cocaine, etc.)
It is evident that the existence of societies, and likewise the life of separate
individuals, is determined by needs that are partly hereditary/organic, or
common to all people (attraction to food, to the opposite sex, to society), and
partly acquired through habit in the course of life (attraction to mental
nourishment, to stimulating substances). An insufficient satisfaction of these
needs decides the direction of social aspirations, which, depending on the
magnitude of dissatisfaction, vary in their intensity and extent. This situation
can be expressed by a particular mathematical formula. If we designate the
quantity of material that completely satisfies the given demand as a, and the
insufficiency of material for the satisfaction of the given demand as b, then
the level of dissatisfaction should be indicated by a - b. The consequences of
this discontent should also be numerical, and so can be similarly represented
by variables. Now we can express the changes in collective reflexes under the
influence of certain forces, in the form of dissatisfaction, by the
corresponding mathematical formula: a - b = ±x ±y ±z . . . Since lack of
satisfaction, being an irritant, can be signified by a particular number through
the accumulation of certain facts and through the given calculation, and since
numerical material on the consequences of this irritant can be obtained from
statistics, we can define more precisely, with the help of mathematical
computations, the interaction between the irritant and its consequences in
form of collective reflexes.
Even Spencer acknowledged the regularity of phenomena in social life,
comparing, as did Comte, human society to an organism, explaining why it is
subject to the laws of integration and differentiation, the laws of rhythm and
equilibrium, the law of struggle for survival and the law of cooperation and
division of social labor. The process of evolution of any society is contained
in the manifestation of these laws; as a matter of fact, Spencer studied social
phenomena in terms of their development, in the manner of Aristotle. After
Spencer came many other authors who supported the evolution theory,
including Espinas, Letourneau, Worms, Charlet, Lilienfeld, et al.
It should be noted that Aristotle, the true founder of sociology, in his
discussion of various political forms in Politics, elucidates the regularity of
their exchange under the influence of class conflict and of the interaction
between social groups. Aristotle's doctrine on society and government, as
self-sufficient communication between people, is itself a very important law,
on whose basis the existence of any society or state is founded. Later, the
concept of the regularity of social phenomena was supported by Machiavelli
and a number of more recent authors, the most important of whom are Vico,
Montesquieu, Adam Smith, Turgot, Condorcet, Malthus, Saint-Simon,
Comte, Quetlet, Spencer, Marx, Engels, et al.
Upon investigating the development of human societies, we meet,
generally speaking, with an amazing regularity in the progression of social
forms. "This regular recurrence found in the most varied forms of social life,
observable in both the evolutionary and revolutionary social development of
all nations, gives sociology the opportunity to discuss the regularity of the
development of social life" (Takhtarev, Sociology, its Concise History, p. 40).
At the present time, there is no need to prove the existence of a successive
tie between historical events. We must keep in mind, however, that behind
historical events there are always national movements, or manifestations of
the collective reflexes of the masses. In other words, historical succession
presupposes the succession of national movements. But the exchange of
social movements can even be seen independently from the succession of
historical events in the fact that one movement is always connected to a
number of preceding ones, which, in turn, are related to other, even earlier
ones, etc.
Let us take the progression of social moods during the war with Germany.
When the war began, a patriotic uplift occurred, uniting for a time all parties
and classes in a country [Russia] that for a long period had been divided by
party conflict. Gradually, this primary impulse was replaced by a calmer,
nationalist mood, which, not finding the necessary satisfaction due to a lack
of victories, vented itself in a revolution that had long been anticipated in
Russia, but which had been quelled through political repression. This is why
the nationalist mood of the country, not having found a corresponding outlet,
was released upon the disbandment of the State Duma (in which the people
saw further diminution of their rights) in the form of harsh opposition to the
government. Opposition cleared the way for revolution, which, having begun
under the flag of bourgeois patriotism, led to the liberation of the proletariat;
as a result, the class struggle of the latter was greatly facilitated, ending in
domination by the proletariat and in the rise of socialist aspirations, leading to
the rebirth of the International.
In this way, everything is in one common chain, all is logically connected,
one social movement is the inevitable consequence of another, and is itself
the cause of following movements. There is not one social movement that is
not directly related to preceding ones as their effect, or to following ones as
their cause, or that would stand on its own, disconnected from any other
phenomena.
Just as there is logic in a chain of oral reflexes, there is logic in the
sequence of actions. He who has said a should be prepared to say b as well.
That same logic is manifested with immutable regularity in relation to
collective aspirations and movements. In this respect, the logic of historical
events is subject to the same regularity and cannot differ from the logic of
language or of individual actions. Here, effect stems from cause in the same
measure as a logical deduction springs from preceding premises.
All this is clarified if we recall that new social movements are, in essence,
a development of previous social movements.
There is not one social movement that does not have its roots in the past, or
roots that are not the inevitable consequence of even earlier social
movements. This is the essence of the law of dependent relations.
Let us take as an example a large-scale social movement, for instance the
Great War.
Why did such a terrifying event develop, and what brought the nations of
the world into conflict?
The external aspect of this colossal social event is not complicated and can
be summarized in the following way: in the summer of 1914, an Austrian
archduke was assassinated at Sarajevo.
Austria, which had long extended the tendrils of its political desire over
Serbia, seeing that the roots of this crime appeared to be embedded in Serbian
propaganda, pronounced the famous ultimatum to the State of Serbia, which
was almost equivalent to a removal of its independence.
Serbia, in its turn, applied to Russia for help through tribal and political
ties.
Guided by the response received from Russia, Serbia agreed to most
Austrian conditions, with the exception of those points that were
incompatible with the understanding of an independent Serbia. The
presentation of the ultimatum was, however, the work not only of Austria, but
also of Germany. Notwithstanding the diplomatic attempts of Russia,
England, and America to find a route to reconciliation, Berlin provoked
Russia by publishing a decree on mobilization in the newspapers. The
Russian ambassador immediately telegraphed this fact to Petersburg.
The order turned out to be apocryphal, and was consequently officially
recalled. News of the correction was likewise telegraphed to Petersburg.
However, the transfer of this notice was delayed by twelve hours.
The "publicized" German order to mobilize was followed by Russian
mobilization directed against Austria, which had meanwhile declared war on
Serbia and had mobilized its own troops to that end.
Then came the German ultimatum to Russia on demobilization, which, not
having any effect, was succeeded by German declaration of war on Russia.
The Franco-German war was declared soon after, on account of the Russo-
French treaty; then England joined France and Russia as a result of the
violation of the Belgian neutrality. Later, Japan, Italy, Portugal and America,
in that order, united forces with the allies; Turkey and Bulgaria took the part
of Austria and Germany. But all this is only the external and consequently
immaterial aspect of the conflict. Far more significant is the fact that German
culture had for a long time been at an extraordinary level of development, as
a result of a number of conditions that there is no need to discuss here. Then,
