after the military conquests of the 1860s, which led to the rise of Prussia in
the German alliance, and especially after the war of 1870-71, Germany's
internal politics were directed towards appeasing the interests of the military
caste, or the so-called Prussian cadets, and of the German bourgeoisie. This
led to an augmentation of Germany's imperialist interests, to economic and
political rivalry with England, and to the sustainment of armed peace in
Europe.
In the end, the tension endured by European countries for half a century
could not but erupt into a terrible war, for which Germany had systematically
prepared itself in the course of no less than forty-five years.
In this way, the Great War was the predictable result of the imperialistic
militarism that arose in Europe after the war of 1870; hence the murder of an
archduke in Serbia was enough to cause the spontaneous outbreak of a world
war. It was impossible, in view of the natural logic of events, to localize the
military conflagration through any diplomatic mediation, and states were
drawn into the war one after another.
This, of course, is not all.
From the time of the Franco-Prussian war, the psychology of the German
people had completely changed. The country, made great through victory,
carried this triumphant mood over to its ideology, which released itself in an
uncontrollable stream of aspiration to world control in politics, industry,
science, etc. Colonies were rapidly seized, world markets were secured for
German industry, and at the same time a number of institutions were
established in Germany that answered to its dreams of world politics and its
aspiration to extend its trade influence over the whole world, which could not
but instigate firm and systematic opposition from England. Austria,
Germany's neighbor and ally, was the first to come under the scope of
German politics; Russia was to have been the next, but she defended herself
in good time through an alliance with France. Then German influence spread
via Austria to the Balkan peninsula, after which Kaiser Wilhelm, representing
the Prussian military acquired economic and political control of Turkey, both
its European and Asian territories from Asia Minor to the Persian Gulf.
Finally, thanks to the Saunders mission, Germany was able to implement its
own agenda at Constantinople.
This very situation contained the origin of the Great European war, for it
compromised the political interests of Russia, Italy, England and France.
Moreover, the humiliation of France by Germany in 1870-71 and the
increasing political rivalry between Germany and England were substantial
factors leading towards the confrontation between nations.
It is necessary to keep in mind that so-called interests are once again the
fruit of national aspirations, prepared for decades and even centuries, and
having deep roots in preceding national movements of a political, economic,
and religious nature.
It should also be noted that almost the entire nineteenth century, especially
its latter half, was spent in the conflict between nationalism and socialism or
internationalism, and there was probably no country where nationalism was
so firmly established as in Germany.
Nationalism is collective egotism. Aside from certain tribal peculiarities,
nationalism is sustained by habitual lifestyle and original development of
intellectual and spiritual culture.
Patriotism, a display of state egotism, arises on the basis of nationalism.
The concepts of internationalism and cosmopolitanism are in opposition to
the latter, as manifestations of international and interstate altruism.
Economic dependence of one nation on another, international intellectual
exchange, world trade, international industry and other ties between civilized
peoples 
contribute 
to 
the 
development 
of 
internationalism 
and
cosmopolitanism. In this respect, a large role is also played by the proletariat,
which, being bereft of property and living only on salary, is not tied to a
certain area and changes its living quarters depending on the location of its
work. Moreover, the working class' struggle with the bourgeois and the
wealthy unites the proletariat of all countries under a common objective.
Here is what Laufenberg says on the subject in his work The Great War, p.
6:
No matter how often France, Russia and England call this conflict with
Germany a defensive war, a war that nobody wanted, no matter what
purpose is assigned to it, be it the defense of threatened national domain or
independence, no matter how frequendy it is asserted that its goal was the
well-being of nations and the guarantee of future peace, the fact that all
states contrived this war with every resource at their disposal, and that
capitalist development led to world conflict in incontrovertible sequence,
is indisputable. World war was the inevitable consequence of a capitalist
system of global economy, and involved the revolt of productive forces
against the predatory capitalist and nationalist forms that had governed up
to that point. Now, the near future belongs to a new economic system, one
that was born of terrible suffering and upheaval.
A struggle between nationalism and internationalism was going on
simultaneously with the conflict outlined above, and although that struggle is
not yet concluded, it is certain that, in the end, internationalism will triumph.
Meanwhile, those states that have developed extensively but have not yet
assimilated their citizens or united them culturally and economically in the
manner of Russia, Austria, and Germany are destined for a reformation, in
the sense of an isolation of the nations that form them.
From the aforesaid it is clear that such an immense national movement as
the Great War has its roots in the distant past and even in early historic times;
if we analyze these roots, then once again we will find they are related to
preceding national and social movements, and so on to infinity. In a word, we
are dealing with the immutable phenomenon of dependent relations in the
form of historical succession, without which not one social movement could
be understood, regardless of its magnitude.
But the logic of historical events extends still farther.
As a result of the colossal expenditure and sacrifice that must accompany
success in contemporary warfare, it so happened that nations could not
sustain the tension connected to victories, and consequently the momentous
war that had induced a remarkable effort on the part of national forces began
to stimulate greater patriotism in some segments of the population and
dissatisfaction in others, especially in the masses, as well as the development
of internationalist aspirations. The longer the war lasted, the more repression,
unstable in its political forms, developed within states, and finally the masses
lost patience. An underground reaction first developed in the country subject
to the greatest tyranny and administrational repression, in which the masses
were least accustomed to self-restraint and discipline, and which was poorer
and generally not as well equipped in comparison to other countries: Russia.
Moreover, its administratory mechanism had displayed obvious inadequacy
prior to the war and, unable to sustain the pressure of social aspiration to
democracy, was crushed with the initiation of the February Revolution. The
latter soon adopted the slogan of protest against military repression and
against the war itself, as a result of which the army began to deteriorate;
hence Order N° 1 was simply an expression of this irrepressible social protest
against military control.
In other countries, thanks to differently structured government, better
systems of conducting war, and other related conditions, mass protests arose
later. But they did commence, both in Italy and elsewhere. Of course, in Italy
the situation was temporarily rectified thanks to active support of the allies
and to the development of patriotism in the country under the influence of
defeats and of a deep-seated rivalry with Austria. Even in America, a strike
movement began early in 1918. Central European countries were last to be
affected by unrest, for they were momentarily the conquerors, and the noise
of victory covers the murmur of the people; nevertheless, mass protests in the
form of general strikes took place in 1918 first in Austro-Hungary, and then
in Germany. Obviously, in this case, notwithstanding military success and
acquisition of territory, a general reaction arose in the form of struggle for
immediate peace, as a consequence of both famine and fatigue due to war
which resulted in a catastrophe.
If we specifically analyze the development of the Russian Revolution, we
also meet with complete historical regularity.
In order to understand the social process that created the Russian
Revolution, it is necessary to take into account the continual drain on a
country of drawn-out, unsuccessful battle, the discovery of all the sore points
of past social life, the lack of technical education, the almost total absence of
industry, the illiteracy of the masses, the incompetence and decay of the
administration, and the utter depletion of the state's economic life.
Naturally, under such circumstances the people protested against the old
form of government, being generally dissatisfied with the old order.
Moreover, during the national conflict all classes and parties-except the
socio-democrats and internationalists, who were in the minority—desired and
aspired towards one goal: the defense of the country from the external enemy.
Yet confidence in the fact that all of the country's resources were employed
for this defense, and that the latter was led by honest administrators, wavered
more and more under the influence of both obvious factors and rumors that
frequently grew to enormous dimensions, implicating even Defense Minister
Sukhomlinov in treasonous acts. This is what led to the overthrow of the old
government, as represented by a weak-willed monarch surrounded by
similarly wretched minions and political charlatans. The possibility and hope
of conquering the external enemy, of terminating economic ruin, and of
revitalizing the country was originally embodied in the State Duma because it
had, over the past ten years, led an open battle against the decaying tsarist
autocracy, and so had acquired, in the eyes of many, or at least of the so-
called bourgeois, the status of an organizing factor of the masses.
Of course, the lower classes were silent on the matter; but political
propaganda could be found in the army, in rural areas, and at the factory, and
although the dissatisfaction may have stemmed from different causes, all
agreed on one point—life could not continue in the same manner.
For those classes that included the bourgeoisie and part of the
intelligentsia, the deposition of the monarchy was a reaction to the inability
of the old order to organize the defense of the country, and consequently
signified Russian rejuvenation and the beginnings of citizens' rights and
freedoms. For the proletariat, which was subject to social trends, the demise
of the old order meant release from centuries-long oppression, and so the
strengthening of the revolution was more valuable to it than any nationalist
aspirations, which in any case were not easily understood by the uneducated
masses.
The overthrow of the monarchy occurred with incredible ease, which is
explained by the fact that it had almost no supporters; it had lost all defenders
in the army, which was composed primarily of fresh troops that had been
infected by the same socialist propaganda as had the entire urban proletariat.
However, during the first days of the revolution, upon the formation of the
Provisional Government, an argument arose between representatives of the
bourgeoisie and of democracy—an argument that nearly brought about a total
rift between the groups.
This shows that, at least with respect to the Provisional Government, rot
had begun to eat away at the issue of social unity.
Consequently, soon after the onset of the February Revolution, the masses
were split into two different camps—supporters of the bourgeoisie on the one
hand, and supporters of democracy on the other—which were not reconciled
at the Moscow State Assembly.
It should be kept in mind that the people, having remained in slavery and
illiteracy for centuries and being bereft of independence in social life, as well
as never having known politics, could not be expected to resolve important
state questions; on the other hand, a revolutionary intelligentsia had arisen
over the long period of tsarist repression, and led the struggle against the
patriotic and nationalist tendencies of the bourgeoisie. Thus, any further so-
called "deepening" of the revolution was really the result of conflict between
one force and another. It goes without saying that the turbulent sea of
impoverished, exhausted, and underdeveloped masses, whose patriotism was
eradicated by a complete exclusion from matters of state and social life,
followed the demagogues of an internationalist inclination, which meant that
a social movement directed against the war, the bourgeoisie, and capitalism
in general arose.
This is the explanation for the denationalization of the Russian Revolution,
in which all socialist parties took part, and which evolved so quickly that
neither the first, primarily bourgeois government, nor the following
coalitional one could improve the military situation or increase material well-
being with respect to land and grain, while the imperialist aims of the war
naturally stimulated the people to oppose its continuation.
The famous Order N° 1; the activity of the Soviet of Worker and Soldier
Deputees that had the government wrapped around its little finger;
Zimmerwald's triumphant march into Russia; and above all the struggle with
the bourgeois classes' imperialistic tendencies— these are the main stages of
the first period of the Russian Revolution, which led it to Bolshevism and
transformed it from a national uprising to a party revolution of the proletarian
classes.
Alongside this process of denationalization was the gradual process of
state decay and disintegration, the reasons for which lay partially in the
instability of the former tsarist order. An especially large role was played in
this respect by Bolshevism, which had, as the purveyor of proletariat
internationalist tendencies, converted the revolution into a struggle of the
working and military masses against all other classes of the population.
This Bolshevist Revolution, initiated by the coup of 25 October 1917, led
the country to proletarian dictatorship and civil war. The isolation of Russia
from its allies, which were branded representatives of bourgeois imperialism,
the separate armistice with Germany, and subsequent separate peace talks
were natural consequences of the coup.
With time, however, the masses were disillusioned even by Bolshevism,
partly as a result of its failure to achieve the promised peace, but especially in
view of the insufficiency of produce, of the widespread industrial and
economic ruin, and the impending famine.
Obviously, at the outset of the revolution there was no one in the huge
organism of the old Russian government system who could have effectuated
constructive labor on the necessary scale, primarily due to the fact that the
former government, as was mentioned, stifled any manifestation of sociality,
making society a passive herd in which the beginnings of statehood and
healthy nationalism were simultaneously extinguished.
The Russian Revolution is commonly compared to the Great French
Revolution, and there are, in truth, a number of similarities between the two.
There is also, however, a significant difference that has apparently been
overlooked by those who closely analyzed the French and Russian
revolutionary movements, namely: the former originated in peaceful times
and so could manifest colossal energy in the military respect, while the latter
began in a period of taxing and prolonged warfare, which had sapped the
strength of the nation, and consequently eliminated any call to arms and led,
in the end, to severe protest against the war. All parties, as well as their
representatives, that had supported the concept of conducting war until
victory or death were immediately cast away, and even those that had spoken
of continuing war, if only with democratic considerations, suffered a fiasco,
since at the beginning of the revolution the pull of the land was strongest, and
therefore the attraction of peace at all costs was also greatest.
Bolshevism, which had been the first to announce "peace at all costs" as its
objective, naturally attracted the people's interest. At the same time, our army
began disintegrating with astounding rapidity. Factors accounting for this,
aside from Order N° 1, included the lack of patriotism of the Russian people.
It could even be said that patriotism was systematically eradicated in the
nation. The slogan of the tsarist regime was "defense of the tsar, the faith, and
the fatherland," from which it is clear that the fatherland was always the last
consideration. Moreover, our schools were distinguished by an absence of
courses pertaining to knowledge of the fatherland; self-government was
withheld from the people, and political judgment was forbidden, contributing
to the development of passivity in the general public towards the fate of the
country. The majority was illiterate, crushed by poverty, and deprived of a
unifying exchange of ideas because of the persecution of any discussion on
political grounds.
As for the intellectuals, it should be noted that, in view of their opposition
to the ruling power, the word "patriot" was to them almost equivalent to
blasphemy, due to the fact that patriotic slogans were usurped by the tsar's
toadies, lackeys, and their vile minions, who called themselves true patriots
and true Russians. The very understanding of patriotism was embodied in the
support of an absolute monarchy, which was despicable to most of the
intelligentsia. As well, the vastness of the land did not suggest any immediate
danger from the enemy, and so soothed the populace by providing apparent
security.
In view of these factors, it is not surprising that the army did not
experience particular fervor. At the beginning of the war, a dislike of the
Germans was incited by media baiting, which stimulated a patriotic uplift in
the country for a time; however, inept management of the war, general chaos,
lack of arms and ammunition, unjustifiable rapaciousness and unceremonious
theft, and the incompetence of or betrayal by such leaders as former Defense
Minister Sukhomlinov, generated an even greater decrease in the patriotic
mood.
The revolution developed as a protest against the given situation, and it
appeared as though the country's defense would consequently be improved.
However, since the revolution arose in an area debilitated by war, it could
only lead to fraternization at the front; to the slogan "Peace at all costs,"
without annexation on the grounds of the self-determinating ability of
nations; to the homily that the Germans were in fact friends, while the enemy
was the bourgeoisie and "counterrevolutionary" officers, as well as the
capitalists of the world, including our allies—England, France, and America.
At the same time, the slogans of international law, equality, and justice were
inaccessible to the understanding of the Russian people. Besides, the pull of
the land was heightened under the influence of rumors of the division of
property.
"What do I need with land and freedom, if I am to be killed?" was the
characteristic question put to Minister Kerensky when the latter toured the
front lines, pronouncing his patriotic speeches. In this query can be seen the
crude, obtuse egotism that accompanies utter disinterest in the fate of one's
country.
In social life, all is linked together, thanks to which every element is part
of an indissoluble fabric; it is enough to yank out one thread, without having
bridged the resultant fissure or rearranged the elements dependent on the lost
thread, for the structured harmony of the collective to falter, leading to
complete disorder. This is what causes social ruin, which is always more
difficult to remedy than to prevent.
G. Le Bon, while discussing the mental preconditions to the Great French
Revolution, notes (p. 134):
Complaisant humanitarianism, beginning with pastoral idylls and
philosophical reasoning and ending with the guillotine, is an excellent
representative of those apparently innocent ideas that quickly led the
ruling classes to weakness and disorganization. They no longer believed in
their own purpose and were even, as Michelet so justly observed, the
enemies of their cause.
When, on the night of 4 August 1789, the nobility renounced its centuries-
old rights and privileges, the revolution was already prepared. The people had
but to follow the directions they were given, and, as usual, the latter were
taken to extremes.
It goes without saying that the reasons for the Great French Revolution lie
significantly deeper than G. Le Bon supposes, but he is right in saying that a
step, once taken, inevitably has further consequences that, as the given
example proves, can take on the magnitude of an event of great importance,
such as a revolution.
There is a view that society's evolution, under normal conditions, is quite
slow. On social spheres, G. Le Bon writes (p. 58):
Excepting those cases in which civilizations were destroyed by invaders,
they have always changed very gradually. Many institutions have
collapsed, many gods have fallen from their pedestals, but only after long
periods of senility. Great states have disintegrated, but only following a
period of gradual decline that neither society nor individual people could
prevent.
It should be kept in mind, however, that the development of social life does
not often proceed unimpeded. For this reason, when the evolution of state life
is hindered by artificial barriers, a revolution unfolds, which causes far more
drastic changes than those that would have been achieved through peaceful
evolution. The history of the French and Russian revolutions provides
edifying examples in this respect. The same holds true in relation to uprisings
and wars.
The correlation discussed above is not limited to the behavior of certain
social groups towards others, but exists between social groups and the
surrounding conditions of nature, which even Bockle acknowledged. It can
be said with certainty that not only products of national creativity, such as
language, custom, and myth, but also any literature, art, and even social and
state institutions reflect the influence of surrounding nature. Moreover, life
itself and even the fate of nations correlates not only with other social
collectives but also with the natural environment, which there is no need to
prove. Finally, even the personality of a given people, its "spiritual"
physiognomy, and partially its physical appearance, is in direct correlation
with nature. Inhabitants of the north and south, of the continental and coastal
areas differ in many respects, as do natives of mountainous and flat regions.
But this by itself is insufficient proof of the influence of nature. There are
facts that leave no doubt about the existence of a correlation between the
behavior of large collectives and the condition of the sun, and since the
condition of the sun is, in its turn, related to the condition of similar stars, it is
clear that the fate of humanity is bound to that of the entire universe.
Here, we will limit ourselves to facts on the correlation of major collective
movements in the human world with meteorological phenomena, the causes
of which are related to the changes sustained by our Sun when it is covered
by a greater or smaller number of sun-spots.
It has long been known that the periodicity of the maximum number of
sunspots appearing on the sun, measured in eleven-year intervals,
corresponds to the maximum magnetic storms and aurorae occurring on the
earth, also every eleven years. The coincidence in this case is so complete
that the section of the sun occupied by spots can be determined from the
position of a magnetic needle. Aside from this, sunspots affect the average
temperature on the earth's surface, precipitation, and consequently, according
to the abbot Moret, are reflected on world grain and grape crops, on the
flowering of lilacs and return of swallows to France.
The same periodicity, in relation to the pulsation of the sun, controls the
number of tropical cyclones and polar icebergs. Obviously, all this is a
product of the heat and electrical energy of the sun. If a connection can be
made between the periodicity of sunspots and harvests on Earth, then the
former should also be related to the origination of economic and financial
crises (Prof. Jung). As a matter of fact, Moret is presently establishing the
dependence of social and political phenomena on the periodicity of sunspots.
In the economic life of nations, we have the same constancy of known
correlations. Trade and currency depend on one event or another in a country
that affects the harvest. War in one part of a continent is reflected on world
trade, etc. On the other hand, the condition of the world market can depend
on an agreement between two nations, or even between two large trade firms.
"World trade is such a delicate organization, that at the right moment it is
enough to place a feather on the balance of the scales in order to change the
flow of trade and credit and induce an unprecedented boom or crash."
It goes without saying that the status of world trade is in direct correlation
with meteorological conditions, since the latter affect harvest; and since
meteorological conditions are, in turn, determined by the sun, the dependence
of trade on earth must be carried over to the depths of the cosmos.
Not only trade, but also various human interactions should be placed in
correlation with the condition of the sun. In this respect, the facts given by
the French author Moret in his article "The Sun and Ourselves" are of
particular interest.
In the words of Ya. Perelman:
The material collected in D. O. Sviatsky's article deals with this question
on a broader level. He compares the chronological dates of large-scale
revolutions with the years of maximum solar activity. The resultant picture
of their relationship is astounding. Indeed, it cannot be passed off as
simple coincidence that the years of especially high sunspot generational
activity—1830,1848,1860,1870,1905 and 1917—registered on Earth not
only in the form of magnetic storms, but also in the form of widespread
social upheaval: the July revolution (1830); the February revolution
(1848); the revolution in Italy (1860); the Paris Commune (1870); the first
and second Russian revolutions (1905 and 1917).
If all this is not accidental, if the pulse of humanity really throbs in unison
with the beating of the cosmic heart of our planetary system, then we can
attempt to create on this basis a kind of political horoscope for the coming
years.
To the question of whether the political storm currently raging in our
country will spread still further, as the supporters of permanent revolution
assert, or whether it will fall into decline, the astronomer can answer: 'The
latter is more likely than the former. The pinnacle of solar activity has
passed, the thermal and electrical heart of our planetary system should
soon settle down."
Our body is incomparably more sensitive than was suspected by ancient
medics: we cannot see electricity, yet we can feel it when a storm is
approaching.
Examples? I have collected hundreds of them throughout the period that I
have been making observations. A significant part of my life has been
spent in teaching, and, consequently, I have been in continual contact with
students, both young and adult. And I have found, as a general rule, that
the number of punishments increases on days of strong electromagnetic
disturbances. This means that sunspots stimulate not only the magnetic
iron strip of a compass, but also the complex organism of a child, sensitive
to external forces; unable to withstand sudden influence, the youth
surrenders to various excesses in moments of general excitement.
But this is only one of many examples. All of us, young and old, must
admit that the electrical condition of the atmosphere, connected to the Sun,
influences our character and disposition, our mood and, in all probability,
accord within our families and our parliamentary sittings, as well as the
making of laws, the aggravation of relations between states, and even the
declaration of wars.
What is true for separate individuals is likewise applicable to the masses,
where individual will weakens and behaviour is subject to the law of large
numbers.
If this is so, periods of lesser solar activity should coincide with periods of
peace and tranquility within nations. Glance at the curve of the Sun's
activity, and you will be convinced of the extent to which astronomy has
the potential to point out dangerous shoals and deeps to the helmsman of
the state.
It has long been noted that world exhibitions take place in times of
decreased solar activity. Then as solar activity augments, humanity seems
to succumb to the grip of a fever — conflicts are born, wars arise. It is as
though a gust of insanity blows through peoples' minds.
Here is chronological reference for the preceding lines. The years of
minimum solar activity—1867, 1878, 1889, 1900, and 1910— correspond to
the years of world exhibitions in Paris (the first four) and in Ghent (1910). By
contrast, the years of maximum solar activity coincide with periods of
political turmoil and military conflict in France: 1870—Franco-Prussian war;
1881-83—Tunis, Annam, Tonkin; 1894-96—Madagascar; 1905—Morocco;
1914-17—First World War.
It is still uncertain whether we are dealing with the direct or indirect
influence of the Sun's activity. D. Sviatsky notes, however, that if solar
corpuscles, which according to Arrhenius bombard our atmosphere more
strongly during periods of increased solar activity, creating magnetic storms
and lighting the polar aurorae, affect our meteorology and-even our grain
prices, as was shown by Hershen, then should not humanity's collective
technology also experience the powerful influence of our sun, thanks to
which "we live, move, and exist"?3
We have quoted this interesting section in order to awaken in the reader's
mind the thought that dependent relations in a social environment are not
limited to the natural environment on our earth, but are significantly more
extensive, reaching into the depths of the universe and tapping into its infinite
amount of energy.
Notes
1 Coumot, Essai sur lesfondements de nos connaissances, Paris, 1851, p. 49 and ff.
2 Addendum upon reading the manuscript proofs. Recently, P. Sorokin made a presentation at the
scientific conference hosted by the Institute on the Study of the Brain and Psychic Activity, entitled
"The influence of hunger on people's behavior and social processes," in which the author approached
the question in a manner similar to my own, but developed his thoughts in significantly greater
detail. He defined the role of hunger in human behavior as "nutritaxis," which in my opinion should
be replaced by "attraction to food [nutrients]." Having characterized the physiological and correlative
manifestations of "nutritaxis" among separate individuals, the author went on to outline the
consequences of mass starvation. The most important of these were (1) the change in the average
import and export of foodstuffs into and out of the affected region; (2) the invention and alteration of
means of obtaining food; (3) emigration (in peacetime and wartime); (4) aggravation of social
struggle in the form of crime; (5) hungerriots, disorders and revolutions; (6) the introduction of
centralization and "communalization" in the socio-political structure of society; (7) an increased
death rate; (8) a decreased birth rate; (9) a change in the society's "morality, customs, and
ideologies." The number of and extent to which these conditions develop in a society affected by
famine depends on several other conditions (for example, whether the lacking products are
supplemented by import). The general conclusion: "At the heart of many of the most significant
social phenomena that, at first glance, seem to have nothing to do with nutrition, lies the independent
variable in question—in other words, the amount and quality of chemical elements that enter the
organisms of the members of a given society:'
3 Bulletin of the Russian society for the promotion of natural and social science (in Russian), Book VI,
6,1917, p. 310 and ff.
23
The Law of Individuality
The law of individuality must be placed among the most important conditions
of social life, for it is in essence a universal law. Every atom, every molecule
is a kind of individual in the form of a closed system of a certain type, while
chemical bonding creates a colony of individuals. In this way, the inorganic
world is itself development according to an individual type.
In the organic world, which includes both plant and animal kingdoms, the
individual is at the foundation of the evolutionary process and of any
development in general. In this way, the organic and inorganic worlds evolve
in accordance with the law of individuality, in the sense that every new
transformation does not destroy former individuality as a more or less closed
system, but only changes its aspect.
In a word, in the constant variability of forms there is something that,
rather than being destroyed, is preserved in the form of basic attributes that
characterize a certain individual. For example, no matter how much a person
changes throughout the aging process, he retains a definite physiognomy with
its characteristic features. The same can be seen in the supraorganic world.
Any profession, any party, and any society in general has its own
characteristic peculiarities, which are preserved in the face of all possible
alterations; similarly, no matter how much one nation or another changes, it
remains a distinct collective individual. Their way of life, their form of state
government could change, but the Russian people will remain Russian, with
their own peculiarities; French, Italian, and other nations will continue being
French, Italian, etc. It is clear, in this way, that the supraorganic and social
world is subject to a certain regularity, developing in agreement with the
principle of individuality.
This thesis is also important for other reasons, since the conditions of the
development of phenomena in closed systems are applicable to any
individual. It is in these closed systems that, due to the interrelation of
various processes, the principle of equilibrium predominates. When the
process of combustion develops in a physically closed system, it is
terminated of its own accord, thanks to the accumulation of carbon dioxide
and a lack of oxygen. A fever developing in an organism is regulated by the
production of antitoxins in the blood, on condition, of course, that the heart
can withstand the higher body temperature. A parallel phenomenon can be
observed among supraorganic individuals or collectives. After discontent
arises for whatever reason in a social environment, it gradually subsides
thanks to the opposition of various elements. There is no need to discuss this
important fact in detail. It is self-explanatory.
The cornerstone of any society is the family; a number of families forms a
class; several classes form a community; several communities form a tribe
with one or another social structure; then come alliances of tribes, etc. Social
groups in the form of clubs are created on the basis of political views; then
some parties may make alliances; the latter can be united into a bloc, etc. All
these are social individualities of a kind, with their own characteristic traits.
In the area of economics we have artels, cooperatives, syndicates, trusts,
etc. In administrational spheres we likewise have special organizations that
gradually broaden the scope of their activities. The same can be observed in
the military, where troops are divided into smaller organized units. Any
society, in its turn, is divisible into classes, guilds, professional unions, clubs,
etc. In a word, any social environment that is undergoing the process of
organization separates certain parts from itself, as a consequence of one
demand or another. These parts have their own relatively isolated existence,
pursuing their corporate interests; each segment obeys its own code of
conduct, consequently creating its own views, habits, manners, and
sometimes fashion of dress or special badge, etc. All this leads to the fact that
each part of society acquires its own individual traits and form of
organization; it can and must be acknowledged as being social or collective
individual.
The same can be said of larger collectives, such as races.
"One of the more vivid impressions," says G. Le Bon, (The Psychology of
Nations and Masses, p. 5),
that I have gleaned from extensive travel through various countries is that
every nation possesses a spiritual structure just as invariable as its physical
peculiarities, and which generates the nation's feelings, thoughts,
institutions, beliefs, and arts.
The commonality of feelings, ideas, beliefs and interests, created through
gradual inherited accumulation, gives the psyche of a nation great unity
and stability, and at the same time guarantees immense strength, (p. 15)
Qualities of character are the inalterable basic elements that allow us to
define the psychic composition of higher nations. Discoveries made by the
human mind are the common property of mankind; strengths or
weaknesses of character are the sole inheritance of a given nation, (p. 29)
Moral and intellectual peculiarities, whose aggregate expresses of national
soul, are essentially the synthesis of agiven nation's past, of the legacy of
its ancestors and of the motives of its actions... This aggregate of common
psychological features comprises that which is rightly called national
character, (p. 10)
In the end, every Englishman, Frenchman, and German has common traits,
characteristic of his nation.
The question of to what extent institutions hold educational significance
for the nation is not without importance. Of course, this educational
significance of institutions cannot be denied, but it is also beyond doubt that
they are more often a by-product of national culture.
The influence of race can easily be demonstrated by the following
example: a theatrical work that receives widespread acclaim in one country
frequently has little success in another.
When a foreign institution, religion, language or art is adopted by a nation,
it acquires new traits that are related to the latter's character. Much interesting
information can be found pertaining to this fact in two chapters of G. Le
Bon's Psychology of Nations, pp. 77-107. We will cite here only the
concluding lines of the last of those chapters:
No matter how great religious influence is, and it is especially so in the
East, the influence of race is greater still. Racial soul, guiding the fate of
nations, also guides their beliefs, institutions, and art; that soul can be
found in whatever characteristic of a peoples we choose to study. It is an
active force that cannot be dominated by any other. It represents thousands
of generations, and is the synthesis of their thought.
If we supplement what has been said with information on the type and
development of industry present in various nations, on their diverse social
traditions, on customs, on family structure and on peculiarities pertaining to
social life, we will have all the features that characterize the individuality of
every nation.
National individuality vastly facilitates our understanding of national
history:
History can be analyzed as a simple account of results achieved by the
psychological character of races. It develops out of that character just as
fishes' gills developed as a consequence of an exclusively underwater
existence. Without a preliminary knowledge of a nation's spiritual
constitution, its history appears to be a chaos of events governed only by
chance. When the soul of a nation is bared, however, its life is seen to be a
proper and inevitable consequence of its psychological traits. In all the
manifestations of a nation's life, we find that the immutable spirit of the
race weaves its own fate. The power of racial soul is most obviously
manifested in political institutions. (I.e., p. 48)
It is acknowledged, not without foundation, that every nation has the form
of government most natural to it.
Keeping in mind that the existence of nations is manifested in the creation
of sciences, beliefs, arts, material culture and in collective actions, it cannot
be denied that not one of these aspects, taken separately, gives a full
understanding of character; only all of them together can represent the
personality of a given people, since one nation may excel in an area more
than another, or may have a different set of values from another, etc., and that
is what lies at the foundation of the ability to distinguish between nations.
As was already stated, any social environment divides into a number of
parties, corporations, alliances, clubs, and other organizations that, created
under the influence of the demands and interests of their members, stratify
the social environment, becoming separate social cells or individuals.
Any independent collective begins life with self-determination, in other
words it establishes and clarifies its own purpose and goals, separating them
from the objectives of other collectives. Only after that is interaction between
collectives set up. In disorganized collectives, such as street crowds, this
gradual self-determination obviously does not take place, but it nevertheless
is sometimes realized seemingly of its own accord.
Every social cell leads its own life, existing until it satisfies the interests of
its members to a certain extent, or in any case until it achieves its initial
purpose. It is known that Aristotle in his Politics established the principle of
autarchy or self-sufficiency, in accordance with which the state, or any social
organization, be it a party, alliance, club, or other community, exists only as
long as it remains self-sufficient, i.e., satisfies the interests of the majority of
its members.
In this case, we are essentially dealing with individual self-sufficiency,
which lies at the basis of the existence of any society, any collective
personality, for only with the achievement of individual independence does it
acquire its own raison d'être.
If, for example, a party loses its raison d'être, it is no longer self-sufficient
and consequently either falls apart, allowing its members to join other parties,
or undergoes corresponding transformations, supplementing and altering its
program in order to become self-sufficient once again.
An economic organization also subsists only while self-sufficient in the
sense that it serves the interests of its clients. As soon as it sacrifices its
autarchy, however, it loses its clientele and ceases to exist. A labor
organization likewise exists only to the point at which it answers to the
interests of its members, and is extinguished as soon as the interests of the
majority are not satisfied. Any financial organization is subject to the same
principle. While it is in a position to withstand the competition of other
organizations, i.e., is self-sufficient in the aforementioned sense, it continues
its existence, which is terminated only when the organization cannot sustain
competition or insufficiently serves the interests of its members.
A government organization, founded on the principle of protecting the life
and possessions of its fellow citizens and possibly of the better satisfaction of
their needs, ceases to be self-sufficient in the eyes of other states when its
power becomes inadequate to execute the above functions. In order to retain
its position, the organization is forced to make alliances, or else must
succumb to more powerful state organisms and is eventually swallowed up
by them.
In this respect, as in others, we have a perfect analogy with the existence of
any entity in the plant or animal kingdoms, which lives only while it is self-
supporting, i.e., is able to satisfy its needs and withstand rivalry in the fight
for survival. When this self-sufficiency is terminated for whatever reason, the
individual expires. Similarly, an inorganic individual, in the form of a
chemical bond, begins to deteriorate following loss of self-sufficiency.
Radium is not a self-sufficient element under the present conditions of the
earth's crust, and so constantly decays.
It is clear that the law of individuality, as was noted at the beginning of this
chapter, is universal and can be applied not only to organic and inorganic
phenomena, but also to the supraorganic or social world.
24
Conclusion
In the preceding pages, I have attempted to outline the main laws which the
human society or collective, when studied objectively, is found to obey. I
hasten to note that, quite possibly, some principles could have been united
into still more general laws, while others, as yet unknown, will be discovered
upon further investigation. Thus, for example, some prefer to analyze the law
of evolution, the law of differentiation, and the law of selective integration as
one body. As we know, the processes of differentiation and integration have
until now been united in biology by authors such as Spencer under the law of
evolution. I prefer, however, to examine each of these principles
independently in order to arrive at a more detailed analysis of their
phenomena. This in no way diminishes the interconnection of phenomena
and dependences, for it cannot be doubted that when a law of the
development of forms is established, further clarification of the progression
of forms inevitably leads us to deduce that new forms are conceived through
the differentiation of older ones, and through the unification of their parts into
new combinations.
In this way, we seem to be dealing with a single process. But the fact of the
matter is that, if we further clarify the manner in which the evolutionary
process proceeds, we are forced to say that the law of continual motion and
change is itself the origin or basis of the evolutionary process, since
movement is connected to the variability of position—and variability of
position lies at the foundation of the variation of forms, i.e., evolution. On the
other hand, is not the law of historical succession also closely related to the
law of evolution? The law of inertia, thanks to which forms temporarily
retain their original appearance, the law of reproduction, which lies at the
basis of heredity, and the law of similarity, which controls the similar
development of organs under identical conditions—are not these and other
laws closely tied to the law of evolution? Yet no one would insist on their
assimilation into a single law. This is why we deem it necessary to isolate the
laws of differentiation and selective generalization as independent in their
own right.
We consider it best to analyze laws or dependences of phenomena from
their primary source. It is obvious, of course, that all laws are in a certain
correlation with each other, since the world itself is a coherent and
harmonious unit, in which certain phenomena are related to, or more
precisely flow out of others. All that exists in the world is in a state of
interaction and mobile equilibrium.1
Thus, we do not attach especial significance to the question of whether or
not certain laws should be merged into a more general law, since in the end
we are dealing with a single cosmic process. It makes no difference whether
we analyze the latter in greater or lesser detail, since difference of opinion
could not undermine its basic principles; the social or supraorganic world
should be studied just as objectively as the world of biological phenomena,
without any excursions into the area of the elusive and intangible national or
collective soul. When studied from a strictly objective viewpoint, in
agreement with the methods of reflexology, the social world is seen as a
continuation of the organic and inorganic ones, and so the energic point of
view that governs physical phenomena is also applicable to social ones. Just
as the material world, be it inorganic or organic, comes down to various
manifestations of energy, so should the supraorganic or social world be
analyzed as a manifestation of energy in masses of people, united into a
collective personality. Similarly, a separate individual's activity is simply a
manifestation of energy received from without.
When a strictly objective method is applied to the study of the social or
supraorganic worlds, we find that the resultant new scientific discipline—
collective or social reflexology—closely corresponds to my theory of
individual reflexology. In both the latter and the former, human activity is
analyzed from the viewpoint of upbringing and of the development and
coordination of various associative reflexes, which arise due to stimulation of
the sensory organs, i.e., transformers of external energy. The fact that social
reflexology resembles individual is completely natural. If a collective that
unites a group of people through common mood or interest is a collective
personality, then obviously everything that relates to the separate personality
should be applicable to it, with the exception of mutual induction, imitation,
suggestion, and other forms of influence peculiar to the collective
personality.
Naturally, the development of collective reflexes and manifestations of the
collective personality follow the same path as the manifestations of separate
personalities.
The strictly objective study of the personality leads us to conclude that the
individual is the product of the influence of external energies and that he is
himself an amalgam of energies, the primary accumulators of which are
cellular elements and, partially, the nervous system and its central organs.
Thus, all actions of the individual personality are externally determined,
which I discuss in detail in my book General Principles of Human
Reflexology (p. 81 and ff.), and consequently are subject to the action of a
number of external causes. The dependence of the number of crimes,
suicides, births, etc., on the price of grain is indisputable. According to
Prussian statistics, fluctuation in the number of thefts, for example,
corresponds amazingly closely to fluctuation in grain prices. Even the
frequency of seemingly insignificant actions, such as errors in the writing of
addresses on letters, is directly dependent on socioeconomic conditions.
My specific investigations in this area leave no doubt as to the truth of the
above statements. "In the end," I write in a work dedicated to this subject,2
"crime is the fatal consequence of a number of both generally predisposing
and directly responsible or immediate factors." The latter include not only the
character and temperament of the personality itself, which reflect its past life
and the conditions of its development in terms of heredity, conception, and
reproduction, but also all those conditions that act upon the given personality,
such as upbringing and environment (comradeship, loss of class, economic
situation), and finally one direct cause or another. These are the factors that
determine why, under given socioeconomic conditions, one personality rather
than another commits a crime.
But if the regularity of phenomena is established in relation to the separate
personality, then it must also exist in relation to the actions of the collective,
which is an aggregate of personalities as accumulators of energy.
We already know that geographic, climatic, politico-economic and other
conditions determine the character and morals of social institutions.
Historical events likewise inevitably follow certain antecedent conditions; a
good leader should foresee the course of social life and its outcome.
"Gouverner c'est prévoir" goes the French saying, and it is a practical
deduction made from observation of national movement, which, in the end, is
subject to laws as immutable as those that govern the movement of celestial
bodies.
It is also natural that, if the collective is an aggregate personality made up
of individual accumulators of energy, the regularity of its phenomena must be
manifested in the same forms as that of the individual personality. This is
why analysis of external manifestations of individual and collective
personalities shows a more or less complete correspondence between their
respective dependences (laws); these dependences are, in turn, a reproduction
and extension of their counterparts in the organic and inorganic worlds. There
is no other way that matters could stand; and if this has not been determined
until now, then it is solely due to the fact that the external phenomena
affecting human and social activity were analyzed from a subjective
viewpoint, which served as an obstacle to the merging of humanitarian
knowledge with the physical and biological sciences. The cosmic process is
nonetheless a manifestation of a single cosmic energy, and no matter where
or in what forms the latter is found, it is demonstrated in the same
interactions and is subject to the same dependences or laws.
